# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"Why is Khufu's Barge considered a landmark case study in the history of non-ferrous shipbuilding materials?"**

**Model Answer (3-mark structure):**

1. **Zero ferrous metal content** — The entire 43.6 m vessel uses **no iron nails, bolts, or rivets**, relying on wooden tenons, copper-arsenical pins, and rope lashings. *(1 mark)*
2. **Materials-selection sophistication** — Imports Lebanese cedar for the hull (lightweight, rot-resistant), acacia for tenons (high compressive strength), and sycamore for decking — a textbook **functionally-graded material** layout. *(1 mark)*
3. **Load-transfer mechanics** — Demonstrates that **mechanical interlock (mortise-tenon) + tension binder (rope lashing) + non-ferrous pin (copper-arsenical)** can sustain wave-induced hull bending without any ferrous reinforcement — proving the engineering viability of all-non-ferrous ship construction. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"Compare and contrast the materials and joint technology of Khufu's Barge with the Lisht timbers."**

**Model Answer (3-mark structure):**

| Aspect | Khufu | Lisht |
|---|---|---|
| **Plank thickness** | 140 mm (heavy) | 50–80 mm (refined) |
| **Tenon material** | Acacia only | Acacia + tamarisk |
| **Caulking** | Inferred (limited evidence) | Reed-bundle caulking systematic |
| **Copper alloy** | Cu-arsenical (rare) | Cu-arsenical + early tin-bronze |
| **Iron content** | Zero | Zero (continued prohibition) |

**Engineering Interpretation:** The Lisht timbers reflect a **700-year refinement** — lighter planks, tighter joint tolerances, more systematic sealing — without abandoning the non-ferrous doctrine. *(3 marks total, with tabular comparison worth 2 marks and the interpretation worth 1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** and offer **internal choice** (OR option). Each option typically splits into part (a) **7 marks** and part (b) **7 marks**. Time allocation: ~25 minutes per Part B question.

---

### **Question B-A** `[KTU University Exam - Module 1 Past Pattern]`
**"With specific reference to Khufu's Barge, explain the engineering rationale for the choice of non-ferrous joining systems over ferrous fasteners in ancient Egyptian shipbuilding. Discuss (a) the religious and chemical reasons for iron avoidance, and (b) the load-transfer mechanics of the mortise-tenon-lashing system, with appropriate calculations."**

#### Part (a) — Religious & Chemical Rationale (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **Religious Prohibition (3 marks):** In Egyptian theology, iron was metaphorically the **"bones of Set"** (Seth, the god of chaos who murdered Osiris). A vessel intended to carry the pharaoh's *ka* (spirit-soul) to the afterlife could not be contaminated by Seth's bones. This is documented in the *Book of the Dead* and confirmed by the **complete absence of iron artefacts** in Old Kingdom funerary deposits. The religious doctrine was codified and enforced for **> 2,500 years** (Old Kingdom through Late Period), making non-ferrous shipbuilding a theological imperative.

2. **Chemical / Corrosion Rationale (2 marks):** Iron in contact with **tannin-rich cedar** undergoes accelerated galvanic and acidic corrosion (tannic acid attacks iron oxides, releasing Fe²⁺ that catalyses further oxidation). The corrosion product (iron tannate) is black and stains the wood, compromising both **structural integrity** and **ritual purity** (a stained vessel is unfit for the afterlife). By eliminating iron, the wood remains in pristine condition — which is precisely why **Khufu's planks survive 4,600 years later in near-original condition**.

3. **Engineering Pragmatism (2 marks):** Ancient Egyptian copper metallurgy produced **copper-arsenical alloys** (3–5% As) and early **tin-bronze** (8–10% Sn) by c. 2600 BCE. These were **sufficient for stress-concentration pins and staples** at the mast-step and stern-post, but not for the mass fastening a 43.6 m hull would require. Wood-on-wood (M&T) + rope lashing was the only **scalable non-ferrous solution**.

#### Part (b) — Load-Transfer Mechanics of Mortise-Tenon-Lashing (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **Joint Geometry Description (2 marks):** Acacia tenon (30 × 30 × 80 mm) sits in matched mortises cut into adjacent cedar planks. Spacing: **~200 tenons per metre of plank length**. Each strake pair has **8,720 tenons** for the 43.6 m hull length.

2. **Shear Capacity Calculation (2 marks):**

$$F_{shear} = \tau_{allow} \cdot A_{shear} = 8 \text{ MPa} \times (30 \text{ mm} \times 80 \text{ mm}) = 19{,}200 \text{ N} = 19.2 \text{ kN}$$

3. **Bearing Capacity Calculation (1 mark):**

$$F_{bearing} = \sigma_{b,allow} \cdot A_{bearing} = 6 \text{ MPa} \times (30 \times 80) = 14{,}400 \text{ N} = 14.4 \text{ kN}$$

4. **Governing Mode and Total Joint Capacity (1 mark):** $F_{joint,allow} = 14.4$ kN (bearing-controlled). With 8,720 tenons per strake interface, the **total interface capacity** is $14.4 \text{ kN} \times 8{,}720 \approx 125{,}568$ kN ≈ 126 MN — vastly exceeding the demand from a wave-induced bending moment of ~2.3 MN·m.

5. **Role of Rope Lashing (1 mark):** Lashing carries **transverse tension** (perpendicular to plank face) and induces **compressive pre-load** on the joint, increasing friction capacity and preventing plank separation. The lashing is **not** the primary load path for in-plane shear — that role belongs to the tenons.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A common student mistake is to claim the *rope lashing* carries the hull's bending load. **It does not.** Rope carries only transverse tension. The bending moment is carried by **wood-on-wood compression at the tenon faces and by the continuous plank fibres**. Lose 2 marks if you misattribute this. Another common error is to use **mild steel** tensile strength (410 MPa) for the tenon — the acacia allowable is only ~55–85 MPa, depending on the failure mode being analysed.

---

### **Question B-B (OR Option)** `[KTU University Exam - Module 1 Past Pattern]`
**"The Lisht timbers (c. 1900 BCE) provide evidence of evolving non-ferrous shipbuilding practice between the Old Kingdom and the Middle Kingdom. With the aid of comparative analysis, discuss (a) the materials continuity and innovation, and (b) the engineering implications of plank-thickness reduction from 140 mm (Khufu) to 50–80 mm (Lisht)."**

#### Part (a) — Materials Continuity and Innovation (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **Continuity (3 marks):**
   - **Wood species:** Both use Lebanese cedar (when available) and acacia — the same imported/local dichotomy.
   - **Iron avoidance:** Both periods show **zero ferrous metal**, confirming the religious prohibition held for 700+ years.
   - **Joinery type:** Both rely on **mortise-tenon + rope lashing** as the primary non-ferrous fastening system.

2. **Innovation (3 marks):**
   - **Systematic reed caulking** — the Lisht timbers show the first clear evidence of **bundled-reed caulking** between strake seams, a major watertightness improvement.
   - **Tighter tolerances** — Lisht mortise-tenon fits are measured at **< 0.5 mm clearance**, compared to ~1 mm in Khufu, indicating improved tooling and craftsmanship.
   - **Broader use of copper-arsenical pins** — the Lisht inventory shows more extensive use, including at non-structural locations, suggesting **declining cost** or improved supply chains.

3. **Engineering Significance (1 mark):** The Lisht evidence proves the Egyptian shipbuilding tradition was **adaptive**, not static. The 700-year gap was a period of **empirical iteration and optimisation** — a precedent for the modern materials science philosophy of *continuous improvement*.

#### Part (b) — Engineering Implications of Plank-Thickness Reduction (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **Bending Stiffness Analysis (2 marks):** For a plank of width $b = 0.6$ m, the second moment of area scales as $d^3$:

$$\dfrac{I_{Lisht}}{I_{Khufu}} = \dfrac{d_{Lisht}^{3}}{d_{Khufu}^{3}} = \dfrac{65^{3}}{140^{3}} = \dfrac{274{,}625}{2{,}744{,}000} \approx 0.10$$

The Lisht plank has only **10% of the Khufu plank's bending stiffness** per plank.

2. **Compensating Mechanisms (2 marks):**
   - **More tenons per unit length** — Lisht boats use ~280 tenons/m vs. ~200 in Khufu, restoring joint density.
   - **Tighter tolerances** — improved load transfer efficiency.
   - **Internal framing density** — Lisht boats have closer-spaced acacia ribs, providing more transverse support.
   - **Systematic caulking** — enables thinner planks by reducing reliance on wood-swelling for watertightness.

3. **Mass Reduction Benefit (1 mark):** Mass per unit area drops from $77 \text{ kg/m}^{2}$ (Khufu 140 mm cedar) to $36 \text{ kg/m}^{2}$ (Lisht 65 mm cedar) — a **53% reduction**, increasing payload capacity and reducing draft.

4. **Hogging-Sagging Wave Response (2 marks):** With the same wavelength loading, the Lisht hull has a higher deflection $\delta$ per unit moment (proportional to $L^3 / EI$). However, the design compensates by using **deeper hull forms** (Lisht boats were deeper in cross-section relative to beam) and **tighter frame spacing** — essentially a **redesigned structural topology**, not just thinner planks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often claim "thinner planks = weaker boat" — this is **wrong without context**. The Lisht timbers show thinner planks **with compensating design changes** (more tenons, closer ribs, better caulking). The overall structural efficiency is **higher**, not lower. Lose 2 marks if you conclude "Lisht = worse than Khufu".

---

## 5.3 Part C: Numerical Practice (Bonus 3-Mark Items)

### **Numerical C1** `[KTU Practice Module 1]`
A Khufu-style joint uses a **20 mm × 20 mm × 60 mm** acacia tenon. If $\tau_{allow} = 8$ MPa and $\sigma_{b,allow} = 6$ MPa, find the safe load.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{shear} &= 8 \times (20 \times 60) = 9{,}600 \text{ N} \\
F_{bearing} &= 6 \times (20 \times 60) = 7{,}200 \text{ N} \\
F_{allow} &= \min(9{,}600, 7{,}200) = 7.2 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** $F_{allow} = 7.2$ kN, bearing-controlled.

---

### **Numerical C2** `[KTU Practice Module 1]`
Calculate the **buoyant force** on a Khufu-style hull displacing **80 m³** of Nile water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³).

**Solution:**

$$F_b = \rho g V_{disp} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 80 = 784{,}800 \text{ N} \approx 785 \text{ kN}$$

**Answer:** $F_b \approx 785$ kN.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Memorise it before the exam.**

### 📌 Key Facts to Memorise
- [ ] **Khufu date:** c. 2600 BCE, Dynasty 4, length **43.6 m**, beam **5.9 m**
- [ ] **Discovery:** 1954, Kamal el-Mallakh, at the Great Pyramid of Giza
- [ ] **Reconstruction:** 1968–1971 by Hag Ahmed Youssef
- [ ] **Pieces:** 1,224 disassembled parts, currently in **Grand Egyptian Museum**
- [ ] **Lisht date:** c. 1900 BCE, Dynasty 12, Middle Kingdom
- [ ] **Lisht excavation:** 1906–1934, Metropolitan Museum of Art
- [ ] **Zero iron** in both Khufu and Lisht — religious prohibition
- [ ] **Cedar:** $\rho = 550$ kg/m³, $E = 9$ GPa, $MOR = 55$ MPa
- [ ] **Acacia:** $\rho = 760$ kg/m³, $E = 12.5$ GPa, $MOR = 85$ MPa
- [ ] **Tenon size:** 30 × 30 × 80 mm in Khufu; spacing ~200/m
- [ ] **Plank thickness:** Khufu 140 mm; Lisht 50–80 mm

### 📌 Key Formulas to Memorise
- [ ] Archimedes: $F_b = \rho_w g V_{disp}$
- [ ] Floating: $F_b = W_{hull} = m g$
- [ ] Shear in tenon: $F_{shear} = \tau_{allow} \cdot b \cdot L_t$
- [ ] Bearing on mortise: $F_{bearing} = \sigma_{b,allow} \cdot b \cdot L_t$
- [ ] Bending stiffness ratio: $EI$ comparison between cedar and aluminium
- [ ] Second moment: $I = b d^3 / 12$
- [ ] Bending moment in wave: $M_{max} = \rho_w g a B L^2 / 24$

### 📌 Key Engineering Concepts
- [ ] **Mortise-tenon** = primary non-ferrous shear-transfer joint
- [ ] **Rope lashing** = transverse-tension binder, NOT primary load path
- [ ] **Copper-arsenical pin** = local reinforcement at stress concentrations
- [ ] **Reed caulking** = watertightness, no structural role
- [ ] **Functionally-graded materials:** cedar hull + acacia tenons + sycamore deck
- [ ] **Modern parallels:** GRP hulls, bronze fastenings, Cu-Ni sheathing
- [ ] **Religious doctrine:** Iron = "bones of Set" → banned in funerary/barque vessels
- [ ] **Chemical rationale:** No tannic-acid corrosion of iron in cedar
- [ ] **Design evolution:** Lisht timbers show **thinner planks + more tenons + tighter tolerances** = structural optimisation

### 📌 Common Exam Mistakes to AVOID
- ❌ Attributing hull bending load to **rope lashing** (it's wood-on-wood compression)
- ❌ Using **mild steel strength** for wood tenons (wood is 5–10× weaker)
- ❌ Concluding Lisht boats are **"worse"** because planks are thinner (they are **optimised**)
- ❌ Forgetting the **religious rationale** (it's not just corrosion — it's theology)
- ❌ Confusing **mortar-and-tenon** with **dovetail** (Khufu uses square M&T, not dovetail)
- ❌ Missing that **caulking has no load role** (it is sealing only)
- ❌ Forgetting to check **both shear AND bearing** in tenon calculations

### 📌 Examiner's One-Line Conclusions (use in answers for full marks)
- ✍️ *"The Khufu barge demonstrates that a 43.6 m vessel can be engineered to withstand wave-induced bending moments using only non-ferrous joining systems — a proof-of-concept still valid in modern GRP and aluminium shipbuilding."*
- ✍️ *"The Lisht timbers represent 700 years of empirical iteration within a strict non-ferrous materials doctrine, yielding lighter, more efficient vessels without abandoning the original engineering philosophy."*
- ✍️ *"Iron avoidance in ancient Egyptian shipbuilding was simultaneously a religious mandate, a chemical compatibility requirement, and a structural engineering choice — a uniquely integrated materials specification."*

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Planking** in shipbuilding refers to the process of covering the internal skeletal framework (frames, floors, keels, beams, and stringers) of a vessel's hull with a continuous external skin made of wooden planks, metal plates, or, more specifically for this course, **non-ferrous metal sheets/alloys** to form a watertight, structurally sound, and hydrodynamic outer shell.

In the context of **PENST523 (Non-Ferrous Materials in Shipbuilding)**, planking is examined not merely as a traditional carpentry operation, but as a sophisticated **marine cladding engineering process** where non-ferrous metals such as **copper (Cu), aluminium (Al), Muntz metal, naval brass, silicon bronze, and cupro-nickel** are employed either as the primary planking skin (in wooden hulls) or as protective sheathing over the hull.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Planking is a foundational Module 1 topic. The examiner expects students to clearly distinguish between *wooden planking* (traditional) and *non-ferrous planking/sheathing* (modern) along with material selection rationale, fastening methods, and corrosion behavior in marine environments.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **human skeleton** — the bones (ribs, spine) form an internal structure, but they cannot protect the organs or hold the body shape without **skin, muscles, and tissue wrapping them**. In a ship, the **frames and floors act as the skeleton**, and **planking is the skin**.

In traditional wooden ships, this "skin" was made of overlapping or edge-to-edge timber planks. However, when ships sailed for months in **salty, bio-fouling-rich seawater**, the timber and iron fasteners deteriorated rapidly. Shipwrights discovered that **copper sheets** nailed over the wooden planks below the waterline:
- Prevented **bio-fouling** (barnacles, weeds, molluscs).
- Resisted **galvanic and saltwater corrosion**.
- Improved **hydrodynamic efficiency**.

Thus, *planking* in this course essentially covers the **engineering philosophy of selecting a non-ferrous "skin" that survives the marine environment**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Sheathing** | A protective non-ferrous (usually copper) covering applied over the hull. |
| **Carvel Planking** | Edge-to-edge smooth planking where planks meet flush. |
| **Clinker Planking** | Overlapping planking where each plank laps over the one below. |
| **Caulking** | Driving cotton/oakum into plank seams to make them watertight. |
| **Paying** | Applying hot pitch/tar over caulking for sealing. |
| **Scantling** | The prescribed cross-sectional dimensions of a plank. |
| **Bio-fouling** | Accumulation of marine organisms on the wetted hull. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Shipbuilding Metrics to Remember:**
> - Plank thickness for wooden hulls: **25 mm to 75 mm** depending on vessel size.
> - Copper sheathing thickness: **0.5 mm to 1.6 mm** (commonly 16 oz to 28 oz per sq ft).
> - Aluminium alloy hull plating: **4 mm to 12 mm** for small craft; up to **25 mm** for large vessels.
> - Density of seawater: **1025 kg/m³**.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Planking — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The planking operation in a shipyard, particularly when non-ferrous materials are involved, follows a deterministic sequence. Each step is critical and any deviation compromises hull integrity.

**Step 1 — Lofting and Template Preparation**
The full-size hull lines (sheer plan, body plan, half-breadth plan) are drawn on the loft floor. Templates (called *moulds*) are cut for each frame. These templates are used to **shape each plank or metal sheet** to its correct curvature (round of beam, deadrise, tumble-home).

> **Why?** Because every plank is unique — it must follow the compound curvature of the hull.

**Step 2 — Material Selection (Non-Ferrous Focus)**
The designer selects the non-ferrous material based on:
- **Position on hull** (above or below waterline).
- **Expected corrosion environment** (immersed, splash zone, atmospheric).
- **Mechanical loading** (longitudinal, transverse, slamming).
- **Compatibility with adjacent materials** (galvanic series).

**Step 3 — Cutting and Shaping**
Wooden planks are shaped by **steaming or baking** over kilns. Non-ferrous metal sheets are **cold-rolled, hot-formed, or brake-pressed** to required curvature. Aluminium alloys (5083, 5086, 5456) are popular because they are **cold-formable** without cracking.

**Step 4 — Fitting (Raising)**
Planks are progressively fitted from the **keel upward** (called *"raising the bottom"*) or from the **sheer downward**, depending on shipyard tradition. Each plank is held temporarily with **cleats and shores**, then the next plank is fitted and faired.

> **Why this direction?** Planks laid from keel up allow gravity to assist in alignment and prevent internal voids.

**Step 5 — Fastening**
This is where non-ferrous material science becomes critical:
- **Copper nails and rivets** were used historically to fasten wooden planks and copper sheathing.
- **Silicon bronze screws** are used today for fastening non-ferrous sheets to wooden frames.
- **Aluminium rivets** (e.g., 5056, 2117 alloy) join aluminium plating.
- **Muntz metal** (60% Cu, 40% Zn) was developed by George Frederic Muntz in 1832 specifically as a sheathing alloy — cheaper than pure copper, equally anti-fouling.

> **Important engineering principle:** Fasteners must be of the **same galvanic group** as the sheathing to avoid bimetallic corrosion. A copper nail in aluminium plating creates a **galvanic cell** that dissolves the aluminium.

**Step 6 — Caulking and Sealing**
In wooden hulls, cotton or oakum is driven into seams. For metal planking, **welding, brazing, or mechanical seams** ensure watertightness. Modern aluminium hulls use **MIG/TIG welding**.

**Step 7 — Inspection and Testing**
Planking is tested for **watertightness** (hose test, chalk test), **fairness** (visual + template check), and **fastener integrity** (tap test).

### 2.2 Non-Ferrous Materials Commonly Used in Planking

| Material | Composition | Application | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Copper (Cu)** | > 99% Cu | Underwater wooden hull sheathing | Anti-fouling, ductile |
| **Muntz Metal** | 60% Cu, 40% Zn | Sheathing (cheaper than Cu) | Anti-fouling, hot workable |
| **Naval Brass** | 60% Cu, 39% Zn, 1% Sn | Sheathing in polluted waters | Resists dezincification |
| **Aluminium 5083** | Al-Mg-Mn | Modern light craft hull | Excellent sea-water resistance |
| **Aluminium 5086** | Al-Mg | Small boat hull plating | Weldable, corrosion resistant |
| **Aluminium 5456** | Al-Mg-Mn | High strength hulls | Used in naval fast craft |
| **Cupro-Nickel (90/10)** | 90% Cu, 10% Ni | Salt-water piping, sheathing | Resists bio-fouling |
| **Silicon Bronze** | Cu-Sn-Si | Fasteners, screws | High strength, corrosion resistant |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Area of one plank | $A_p = L \times b$ | m² | $L$ = length, $b$ = breadth |
| Number of planks (approx.) | $N = \dfrac{W_{hull}}{b} \times k$ | planks | $k$ = correction for curvature (1.05–1.15) |
| Weight of planking | $W = A \times t \times \rho$ | kg | $\rho$ = density of non-ferrous material |
| Density of Copper | $\rho_{Cu} = 8960$ | kg/m³ | Reference constant |
| Density of Aluminium | $\rho_{Al} = 2700$ | kg/m³ | Reference constant |
| Density of Muntz Metal | $\rho_{M} \approx 8440$ | kg/m³ | Reference constant |
| Plank thickness (empirical) | $t = 0.04L + 6$ | mm | $L$ = rule length of ship in metres |
| Sheathing weight (per m²) | $w = t \times \rho$ | kg/m² | Multiply by hull surface area |
| Galvanic potential difference | $\Delta E = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ | V | Determines corrosion rate |
| Copper sheathing thickness (oz) | $1~\text{oz/ft}^2 = 0.0341$ mm | — | Historical unit used in KTU questions |
| Scantling ratio (plank thickness to width) | $t : b = 1 : 4$ | — | Common rule of thumb |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Engineering Insight:** Always express densities in SI units (**kg/m³**) in KTU answers. Mixing CGS and SI will cost marks.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

- **Copper sheathing** (18th–19th century) increased Royal Navy ship cruising range by 50% by eliminating frequent hull cleaning.
- **Aluminium 5083-H116** is the standard hull material for **INS Vikrant-class** support craft and fast patrol vessels — chosen for weight savings and corrosion resistance.
- **Cupro-nickel** is used in marine heat exchangers and condensers for the same anti-fouling reasons as in planking.
- **Muntz metal** sheathing on the hull of *Cutty Sark* (1869) is still partially intact, demonstrating century-scale marine durability.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Number of Planks Required for a Hull

**Problem Setup:** A wooden fishing trawler has a wetted hull surface area of **$A = 280~\text{m}^2$**. Standard plank breadth is **$b = 0.20~\text{m}$**. Due to hull curvature, an overlap correction factor of **$k = 1.10$** is to be applied. Find the approximate number of planks.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing relationship.**

The total plank area required (accounting for curvature wastage) is:

$$
A_{total} = A \times k
$$

This must equal the number of planks times the area of one plank:

$$
A_{total} = N \times A_p
$$

Where $A_p = L \times b$. Since each plank follows the girth of the hull, $L$ varies per plank. We approximate using the average plank length $\bar{L}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute known values.**

$$
A_{total} = 280 \times 1.10 = 308~\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Express the average plank area.**

For the average plank of length $\bar{L}$ and breadth $b$:

$$
A_p = \bar{L} \times b = \bar{L} \times 0.20
$$

**Step 4 — Number of planks per strake (horizontal row).**

For one complete strake encircling the hull at a given level, the number of planks end-to-end is:

$$
N_{strake} = \dfrac{\text{Girth at that level}}{\bar{L}}
$$

**Step 5 — Approximate total planks (integrated approach).**

A more practical estimate is to use the total hull area:

$$
N = \dfrac{A \times k}{b \times \bar{L}}
$$

Assuming average plank length $\bar{L} = 4~\text{m}$:

$$
N = \dfrac{280 \times 1.10}{0.20 \times 4} = \dfrac{308}{0.80} = 385~\text{planks}
$$

> **Result:** Approximately **385 planks** are required for the wetted hull.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Weight of Copper Sheathing

**Problem:** A ship has a wetted hull surface area of **$1200~\text{m}^2$**. Copper sheathing of **28 oz/ft²** is to be applied. Calculate the **total weight of copper** required. Given: density of copper $\rho_{Cu} = 8960~\text{kg/m}^3$ and $1~\text{oz/ft}^2 = 0.305~\text{kg/m}^2$.

**Step 1 — Convert sheathing specification to mass per unit area.**

$$
w = 28 \times 0.305 = 8.54~\text{kg/m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by total area.**

$$
W_{total} = w \times A = 8.54 \times 1200
$$

$$
W_{total} = 10248~\text{kg} \approx 10.25~\text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification using thickness approach.**

Thickness corresponding to 28 oz/ft²:

$$
t = 28 \times 0.0341 = 0.955~\text{mm} \approx 0.001~\text{m}
$$

Then:

$$
W = A \times t \times \rho = 1200 \times 0.001 \times 8960
$$

$$
W = 10752~\text{kg} \approx 10.75~\text{tonnes}
$$

> **Result:** Approximately **10.25 to 10.75 tonnes** of copper (slight variation due to rounding).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always show both **unit conversions and final substitution** explicitly. Examiners award step marks for unit clarity, not just the final number.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Planking Materials

| Property | Wood (Teak) | Copper | Muntz Metal | Aluminium 5083 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Density (kg/m³)** | 700 | 8960 | 8440 | 2660 |
| **Tensile Strength (MPa)** | 80–100 | 220 | 380 | 290 |
| **Yield Strength (MPa)** | — | 70 | 145 | 215 |
| **Corrosion in Seawater** | Poor (rots) | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| **Anti-fouling** | Poor | Excellent | Good | Moderate (needs paint) |
| **Workability** | Easy | Cold-formable | Hot-workable | Cold-formable, weldable |
| **Relative Cost** | Low–Medium | Very High | High | Medium |
| **Service Life (years)** | 5–15 (with maintenance) | 40–80 | 30–50 | 30–60 |
| **Weight per m² at 5 mm** | 3.5 kg | 44.8 kg | 42.2 kg | 13.3 kg |

### 3.4 Why Aluminium 5083 Dominates Modern Hull Planking

**Step 1 — Weight advantage.**
For identical volume:

$$
\dfrac{\rho_{Cu}}{\rho_{Al}} = \dfrac{8960}{2660} \approx 3.37
$$

Aluminium is **~3.37 times lighter** than copper for the same thickness.

**Step 2 — Strength-to-weight ratio.**

$$
\text{Specific Strength}_{Al} = \dfrac{290}{2.66} = 109
$$

$$
\text{Specific Strength}_{Cu} = \dfrac{220}{8.96} = 24.6
$$

Aluminium 5083 has a **~4.4× better specific strength**, making it ideal for fast vessels and naval craft.

**Step 3 — Corrosion resistance.**
Aluminium forms a self-healing **Al₂O₃ passive layer** that prevents further oxidation — analogous to how copper forms Cu₂O/CuO patina.

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**
Aluminium 5083 is the **dominant non-ferrous planking material** in modern small-to-medium craft.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "planking" in the context of shipbuilding. Distinguish between carvel and clinker planking.**

**Model Answer:**
Planking is the process of covering the skeletal framework of a ship's hull with planks or metal plates to form a watertight outer skin.
- **Carvel Planking:** Planks are laid edge-to-edge, forming a smooth outer surface. Used in large, seagoing wooden ships.
- **Clinker Planking:** Planks overlap each other along their edges, like roof tiles. Used in small, fast boats (e.g., Viking longships, rowing boats).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is copper sheathing? Why is it preferred over iron sheathing for the underwater hull of wooden ships?**

**Model Answer:**
Copper sheathing is the application of thin copper sheets (typically 0.5–1.6 mm thick) over the underwater portion of a ship's hull.
**Reasons for preference over iron:**
1. Copper resists corrosion in seawater (forms protective patina).
2. Iron rusts rapidly, weakening the hull and adding weight.
3. Copper is anti-fouling — prevents marine organism attachment.
4. Copper's patina (Cu₂O/CuCO₃) is self-healing.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

> **Q. (a)** Explain in detail the various **non-ferrous materials** used for ship hull planking and sheathing. Discuss their **composition, properties, and specific applications** in marine environments. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** Describe the **step-by-step process of planking** a wooden ship hull, with emphasis on the **fastening techniques** used for non-ferrous sheathing. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The principal non-ferrous materials used in ship planking and sheathing are:

**1. Copper (Cu)**
- **Composition:** > 99% pure copper.
- **Properties:** Excellent corrosion resistance in seawater, ductile, anti-fouling, high thermal and electrical conductivity.
- **Application:** Sheathing the underwater hull of wooden warships (introduced by Royal Navy in 1761). Thickness: 0.5–1.6 mm.

**2. Muntz Metal**
- **Composition:** 60% Cu, 40% Zn.
- **Properties:** Cheaper than pure copper, can be hot-rolled at lower temperatures (700 °C), good anti-fouling.
- **Application:** Sheathing of merchant vessels in the 19th century. Patented by G.F. Muntz in 1832.

**3. Naval Brass**
- **Composition:** 60% Cu, 39% Zn, 1% Tin.
- **Properties:** Resistant to dezincification (selective leaching of zinc), good in polluted harbours.
- **Application:** Marine hardware, condenser tubes, sheathing in aggressive waters.

**4. Aluminium Alloys (5083, 5086, 5456)**
- **Composition:** Al + Mg (4–5%) + Mn.
- **Properties:** Low density (2660 kg/m³), high specific strength, excellent seawater corrosion resistance, weldable, cold-formable.
- **Application:** Hull plating of fast patrol boats, hydrofoils, fishing vessels, modern naval craft.

**5. Silicon Bronze**
- **Composition:** Cu + Sn + Si.
- **Properties:** High strength, corrosion-resistant, non-magnetic.
- **Application:** Fasteners (screws, bolts, rivets) for joining non-ferrous sheets.

**6. Cupro-Nickel (90/10)**
- **Composition:** 90% Cu, 10% Ni.
- **Properties:** Outstanding resistance to bio-fouling and seawater corrosion.
- **Application:** Piping, heat exchanger tubes, premium sheathing.

> **Conclusion:** Selection depends on exposure zone (immersed/splash/atmospheric), required life, and budget.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

The planking operation proceeds in the following sequence:

**Step 1 — Lofting**
Full-size lines of the hull are drawn on the loft floor. Moulds (templates) are cut for each frame station.

**Step 2 — Frame Erection**
The keel is laid on the building slip. Stem, sternpost, frames, floors, and beams are erected and braced.

**Step 3 — Plank Preparation**
Wooden planks are cut to approximate size, then shaped by **steaming (for transverse curvature)** or **baking over kilns (for compound curvature)**.

**Step 4 — Raising the Bottom**
Planks are fitted starting from the **garboard strake** (next to the keel) and worked upward toward the sheer. Each plank is temporarily cleated, the next fitted, then both are faired and permanently fastened.

**Step 5 — Fastening Techniques for Non-Ferrous Sheathing**

- **Copper Nails:** Used historically to nail copper sheets to wooden hulls. The copper nail is ductile and does not rust in seawater.
- **Silicon Bronze Screws:** Modern replacement for copper nails — better holding power, removable.
- **Aluminium Rivets (Alloy 5056):** Used to fasten aluminium plates to aluminium frames. Cold-driven.
- **MIG/TIG Welding:** Used to seam aluminium hull plates.
- **Mechanical Seaming (Lock-Form):** Used for some non-ferrous sheet joins.

**Step 6 — Caulking and Sealing**
For wooden hulls: cotton/oakum is driven into seams and sealed with hot pitch. For metal planking: welded or mechanically seamed joints are tested with a hose.

**Step 7 — Inspection**
Planking is checked for **fairness**, **fastener tightness**, and **watertightness** before launch.

---

#### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

> **Q. (a)** Discuss the **galvanic corrosion problem** in planking when **copper sheathing** is fitted over an **iron-fastened wooden hull**. What remedial measures were historically adopted? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** A vessel has a wetted hull area of **$850~\text{m}^2$**. Muntz metal sheathing of **22 oz/ft²** is to be applied. Calculate the **(i)** total weight of sheathing and **(ii)** its thickness in mm. Given: density of Muntz metal $= 8440~\text{kg/m}^3$ and $1~\text{oz/ft}^2 = 0.305~\text{kg/m}^2$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**The Problem:**
When copper sheathing is nailed to a wooden hull using **iron nails or bolts**, the assembly forms a **galvanic cell** in the presence of seawater (an electrolyte).

- **Copper** is more **noble** (higher electrode potential, $\approx +0.34~\text{V}$ vs SHE).
- **Iron** is more **active** (lower potential, $\approx -0.44~\text{V}$ vs SHE).
- Potential difference: $\Delta E \approx 0.78~\text{V}$.

The iron acts as the **anode** and corrodes rapidly, while copper is protected (acts as cathode). The iron nails dissolve, leaving **holes in the planking through which water enters**, and the copper sheets may even detach.

**Historical Remedies:**

1. **Replacement of iron nails with copper nails** (introduced by Royal Navy after 1780). Copper nails have similar potential to copper sheets, eliminating the galvanic couple.
2. **Use of yellow metal (Muntz metal) nails** for Muntz sheathing.
3. **Application of "chock pins" or wooden plugs** between iron and copper — an insulating barrier.
4. **Use of composition (copper alloy) bolts** for attaching the sheathing.
5. **Modern: cathodic protection** with sacrificial zinc anodes attached to copper-sheathed hulls.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Wetted hull area, $A = 850~\text{m}^2$
- Sheathing specification: $22~\text{oz/ft}^2$
- Density of Muntz metal, $\rho = 8440~\text{kg/m}^3$
- $1~\text{oz/ft}^2 = 0.305~\text{kg/m}^2$

**Part (i) — Total Weight of Sheathing**

Weight per unit area:

$$
w = 22 \times 0.305 = 6.71~\text{kg/m}^2
$$

Total weight:

$$
W = w \times A = 6.71 \times 850
$$

$$
W = 5703.5~\text{kg} \approx 5.70~\text{tonnes}
$$

**Part (ii) — Thickness in mm**

Using the density relation:

$$
W = A \times t \times \rho
$$

Solving for $t$:

$$
t = \dfrac{W}{A \times \rho} = \dfrac{5703.5}{850 \times 8440}
$$

$$
t = \dfrac{5703.5}{7,174,000} = 7.95 \times 10^{-4}~\text{m}
$$

Converting to mm:

$$
t = 0.795~\text{mm} \approx 0.80~\text{mm}
$$

> **Result:** Total weight $\approx \mathbf{5.70~\text{tonnes}}$ and thickness $\approx \mathbf{0.80~\text{mm}}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency** is the most common error in sheathing problems. Students confuse **oz/ft²** with **oz**. Always multiply by $0.305$ to convert to **kg/m²**.
> 2. Forgetting to convert **mm to m** (or vice versa) when using density leads to answers off by a factor of 1000.
> 3. In **galvanic corrosion questions**, students often write "copper corrodes" — this is wrong. The **more noble metal (copper) is protected; the less noble (iron) corrodes**.
> 4. In descriptive questions on planking, examiners expect **specific plank names** (garboard, sheer strake, etc.) — vague answers like "bottom plank" lose marks.
> 5. Always mention **Muntz metal's inventor and year (G.F. Muntz, 1832)** if asked about its origin — examiners love historical context.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Planking** = covering the hull frame with planks/sheets to form the watertight skin.
- **Carvel planking** = edge-to-edge (smooth). **Clinker planking** = overlapping.
- **Copper sheathing** is the historical non-ferrous planking solution; it prevents bio-fouling and resists seawater corrosion.
- **Muntz metal** (60 Cu / 40 Zn) is a cheaper, hot-workable alternative to pure copper, patented in 1832.
- **Aluminium alloys 5083, 5086, 5456** dominate modern non-ferrous hull plating due to their **low density, high specific strength, weldability, and self-healing oxide layer**.
- **Fastener material must match the planking material** in the galvanic series to avoid bimetallic corrosion.
- **Naval brass** (60 Cu / 39 Zn / 1 Sn) resists dezincification in polluted waters.
- **Silicon bronze** is the preferred fastener for marine non-ferrous sheathing.
- **Copper-nickel (90/10)** is used where extreme anti-fouling and corrosion resistance are required.
- **Galvanic potential difference** $\Delta E = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ drives corrosion — always identify the anode correctly.
- **Standard sheathing weight conversions:** $1~\text{oz/ft}^2 = 0.305~\text{kg/m}^2 = 0.0341~\text{mm}$ thickness of copper.
- **Plank raising sequence:** keel → garboard → bottom → bilge → side → sheer strake.
- **Key densities:** $\rho_{Cu} = 8960~\text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{Al} = 2700~\text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{Muntz} = 8440~\text{kg/m}^3$.
- **Lofting** is the first and most critical step — every plank template originates from the loft floor.
- **Caulking and paying** are unique to wooden planking; metal planking uses welding or mechanical seaming.
- **Specific strength of aluminium 5083 is ~4.4× that of copper** — explain this in comparative questions to score full marks.

> **Final KTU Tip:** When a question asks *"Discuss non-ferrous materials in planking"*, always structure your answer as: **Material → Composition → Key Property → Application → Limitation**. This 5-element structure is what examiners look for and guarantees full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Erections and Joiner Work
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Erections and Joiner Work

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — "Erection" in Shipbuilding

In shipbuilding, **Erection** refers to the systematic process of **lifting, positioning, aligning, and joining prefabricated hull blocks, sub-assemblies, or structural units** (built in the fabrication shop) onto the building berth, slipway, or in dry dock to form a complete ship hull. The term encompasses the *physical act of assembly* and all the temporary and permanent joining operations involved.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1):** *Erection is the assembly phase where pre-fabricated sub-units (panels, blocks, modules) are brought together, aligned against reference lines, temporarily secured, and finally welded, bolted, or riveted using compatible non-ferrous fasteners and welding consumables to form the structural entity of a vessel.*

### 1.2 Formal Definition — "Joiner Work"

**Joiner Work** is the discipline within ship outfitting that covers all **non-structural interior finishing activities** required to make the vessel habitable and operationally functional. This includes:
- Construction and installation of **cabins, mess rooms, pantries, offices, and WCs**
- **Partitions, bulkheads, doors, hatches, and linings**
- **Ceiling, deck covering, and floor finishing**
- **Furniture, bunks, wardrobes, tables, and storage units**
- **Galley fittings, sanitary fittings, and trim**

> [!NOTE]
> Joiner work is governed by the **classification society rules** (e.g., IRS – Indian Register of Shipping, Lloyd's Register, DNV, BV) and ISO 8468 / ISO 12217 standards for marine furniture and outfitting.

## 1.3 Role of Non-Ferrous Materials

| Application Domain | Non-Ferrous Material Used | Reason for Selection |
|--------------------|---------------------------|----------------------|
| Superstructure sheathing | **Aluminium alloys (5083, 5086, 6082)** | Light weight, corrosion resistance in marine atmosphere |
| Interior paneling | **Anodized aluminium, PVC laminates, FRP** | Non-corroding, easy to clean, fire-retardant |
| Fasteners (bolts, nuts, rivets) | **Copper-nickel (Cu-Ni 70/30), Monel, Brass** | Resistance to galvanic corrosion in seawater |
| Plumbing & piping | **Copper, Cu-Ni 90/10, Cu-Ni 70/30** | Biofouling resistance, low maintenance |
| Heat exchangers | **Aluminium-brass, Cupro-nickel** | High thermal conductivity + seawater resistance |
| Sacrificial anodes | **Zinc, Aluminium-Zinc-In alloys** | Cathodic protection of hull |

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

> **Analogy 1 — Erection = "Assembling a Giant 3D Jigsaw Puzzle on a Seismic Bed"**
> Imagine you have thousands of precisely cut, pre-painted, and pre-fitted steel puzzle pieces (ship blocks) that must be lifted by giant cranes and lowered onto a soft, slightly yielding "table" (the building berth). Each piece must be aligned within **±2 mm** tolerance. The "non-ferrous connection hardware" (cathodic anodes, copper-nickel pipes, aluminium superstructure joints) acts like the **specialty corner clips and laminated backing** that hold the puzzle firmly without rusting. One wrong alloy choice and the entire hull could suffer galvanic corrosion — just like a brass clip embedded in a steel frame would slowly eat the steel.

> **Analogy 2 — Joiner Work = "Furnishing a Floating 5-Star Hotel"**
> If erection is the skeleton and walls of a building, **joiner work is the interior design of a luxury apartment**. The non-ferrous materials (aluminium partitions, copper plumbing, brass fittings) play the same role as **stainless steel kitchen fittings and aluminium window frames** in a coastal apartment — they must look good, resist salt-laden air, be fire-retardant, and last for 25+ years with minimal maintenance.

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Standards (Bold Highlight)

- **Density of Marine Aluminium Alloy 5083:** **2.66 g/cm³** (~35% lighter than mild steel)
- **Yield Strength of Al-5083-H116:** **215 MPa (min)**
- **Density of Copper-Nickel 70/30:** **8.94 g/cm³**
- **Design Temperature Range for Joiner Work (SOLAS):** **-10°C to +65°C** ambient
- **Galvanic Series Potential (Aluminium vs. Steel):** Difference of **~0.75 V** (must be isolated)
- **Standard Fire Rating (IMO FTP Code Part 5) for Joiner Panels:** **Class C — 30 min**
- **Maximum Permissible Asbestos Content (post-2002):** **0% (prohibited)**
- **Standard Berth Dimensions (ISO 8468):** **1900 mm × 700 mm (min)**
- **Minimum Aisle Width in Crew Spaces:** **600 mm (SOLAS Ch. II-2)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Non-Ferrous in Erection?** During erection, dissimilar metal contact between **aluminium superstructures and steel hulls** creates **galvanic corrosion cells**. This is mitigated using **zinc or aluminium sacrificial anodes**, **insulating rubber/neoprene gaskets**, and **non-metallic bushings** — all of which are non-ferrous or non-metallic by design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ship Erection Process — Step-by-Step Logic

The erection process is not a single event; it is a **planned, sequenced engineering operation**. Below is the canonical sequence followed in modern shipyards.

### Step 1 — Pre-Erection Planning
- **Why?** To ensure safety, dimensional accuracy, and resource availability.
- **How?** Review of **block erection sequence drawing (EES — Erection Elementary Sheet)**, **lifting study**, **crane capacity verification**, and **scaffold/access plan**.
- Output: Approved **Method Statement** and **Risk Assessment (JSA)**.

### Step 2 — Pre-Erection Survey of Berth & Blocks
- Survey the building berth, reference baseline, and pre-fabricated blocks.
- Check **non-ferrous anodes**, **insulation pads**, and **cathodic protection continuity strips** are in place.

### Step 3 — Rigging & Lifting
- Attach **lifting shackles, slings, spreader beams** to pre-designed pad-eyes on the block.
- Verify **Centre of Gravity (CoG)** of the block against the lifting plan.
- Lift using **gantry crane, tower crane, or strand jacks** depending on block weight.

### Step 4 — Positioning & Alignment
- Block lowered onto **sacrificial support blocks (camber bars, keel blocks)**.
- Adjusted using **hydraulic jacks, dogs, and shores** to align with reference lines (baseline, buttock lines, frame lines).
- Tolerance: **±3 mm** on critical dimensions (per IACS S21).

### Step 5 — Temporary Attachment (Tack Welding / Clamping)
- Blocks held in place with **tack welds, strong-backs, erection brackets**.
- **Non-ferrous aluminium strong-backs** are used to prevent steel contamination on Al superstructure blocks.

### Step 6 — Final Joining
- **Butt welding** of seams using compatible **ferritic / austenitic consumables**.
- **Non-ferrous fasteners (Monel, Cu-Ni, A4-80 stainless)** used where aluminium is joined to steel.
- **Insulating gaskets** (Neoprene, PTFE) inserted at all Al-to-steel interfaces.

### Step 7 — Inspection & Survey
- **NDT (Non-Destructive Testing):** UT, MPI, DPI on welds.
- **Dimensional survey** to confirm hull form within tolerance.
- **Cathodic protection continuity test** for all non-ferrous components.

## 2.2 The Joiner Work Process — Engineering Breakdown

Joiner work is divided into **three hierarchical levels** in modern shipyards:

| Level | Description | Typical Non-Ferrous Materials Involved |
|-------|-------------|---------------------------------------|
| **L1 — Primary Joiner** | Bulkhead panels, ceiling, deck sheathing, linings, doors | Aluminium honeycomb panels, PVC laminates, phenolic-faced plywood |
| **L2 — Secondary Joiner** | Bunks, lockers, wardrobes, desks, shelves, mess tables | Aluminium alloy 6063 profiles, anodized aluminium trim, brass hardware |
| **L3 — Tertiary Joiner / Trim** | Curtain rails, name plates, notice boards, sanitary accessories | Stainless steel (A4-80), brass, anodized aluminium, copper piping |

## 2.3 Classification Society Rules (Governing Standards)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 exam, memorize the following standard references:
> - **IRS Rules & Regulations, Part 3 (Hull)** – Chapter 11: Hull Outfitting
> - **SOLAS Chapter II-2** – Construction (Fire protection, smoke detection)
> - **ISO 8468** – Ships and Marine Technology – Ship's Bridge Layout
> - **ISO 12217** – Furniture – Strength, durability, and safety
> - **IACS S21** – Hull Erection Tolerances
> - **IMO FTP Code** – Fire Test Procedures

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the consolidated formula table for Erection & Joiner Work. All symbols follow standard ISO 80000 conventions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Applicable To |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | $\sigma_{lift} = \dfrac{W_{block}}{A_{pad-eye}} + \dfrac{M_{dyn} \cdot c}{Z_{pad-eye}}$ | Combined static + dynamic stress at lifting pad-eye | MPa | Erection lifting design |
| 2 | $W_{block} = m \cdot g = \rho_{steel} \cdot V_{block} \cdot g$ | Weight of steel block | N | Crane capacity selection |
| 3 | $W_{Al} = \rho_{Al} \cdot V \cdot g = 2.66 \cdot 10^{3} \cdot V \cdot 9.81$ | Weight of aluminium block | N | Lighter superstructure blocks |
| 4 | $F_{dyn} = W \cdot (1 + k_{imp})$ where $k_{imp} = 0.5 \text{ to } 1.0$ | Dynamic amplification during lift | – | Crane hook load design |
| 5 | $\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{n \cdot k}$ | Allowable stress (yield ÷ safety × knock-down) | MPa | Aluminium joint design |
| 6 | $\sigma_{galv} = \dfrac{E_{cathode} - E_{anode}}{R_{circuit}}$ | Galvanic corrosion driving EMF | V | Bi-metallic joint check |
| 7 | $I_{prot} = \dfrac{m_{anode}}{t \cdot I_{design}}$ | Required anode mass for cathodic protection | kg/yr | Hull sacrificial anode |
| 8 | $\delta_{panel} = \dfrac{k \cdot q \cdot a^{4}}{E \cdot t^{3}}$ | Maximum deflection of a flat joiner panel | mm | Partition panel design |
| 9 | $R_{fire} = \dfrac{\text{FRL of material}}{30 \text{ min}}$ | Fire resistance classification ratio | – | SOLAS joiner compliance |
| 10 | $n_{crew} = \dfrac{\sum A_{spaces}}{A_{per\_berth}}$ where $A_{per\_berth} \geq 4.5 \text{ m}^{2}$ | Number of crew berths per area | persons | Accommodation sizing |
| 11 | $\tau_{rivet} = \dfrac{P}{d \cdot t}$ | Shear stress in non-ferrous rivet | MPa | Aluminium joint design |
| 12 | $T_{friction} = \mu \cdot N$ | Friction force in bolted joint | N | Slip-resistant joiner mounting |

> **Where:**
> - $W$ = weight (N), $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $\rho$ = density (kg/m³)
> - $\sigma_{y}$ = yield strength, $n$ = factor of safety (typically **2.0–3.0** for marine), $k$ = knock-down factor
> - $E$ = Young's modulus (Al = **70 GPa**, Cu-Ni = **135 GPa**, Steel = **210 GPa**)
> - $q$ = uniformly distributed load (N/m²), $a$ = panel short span (m), $t$ = thickness (m)
> - $k$ (panel coefficient) = **0.00406** for simply supported plate under UDL
> - $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (Al-Al ≈ **0.45**, Al-Steel ≈ **0.61**)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles learned here directly apply to:

1. **Modular shipbuilding & offshore platforms** — pre-fabricated topside modules are "erected" onto hulls in the same way as ship blocks.
2. **Mega-yacht construction** — heavy use of aluminium joiner work for weight-sensitive luxury vessels.
3. **Naval frigates & destroyers** — aluminium superstructures demand rigorous galvanic isolation during erection.
4. **Cruise ship outfitting** — thousands of cabins require high-quality, lightweight joiner work using non-ferrous trims.
5. **Inland waterway vessels (ISO 8383)** — aluminium joiner work reduces draft and increases payload.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides **exhaustive derivations** of the most important KTU-expected calculations for the topic. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No shortcuts.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Allowable Stress for Aluminium Lifting Pad-Eye

**Problem Setup:** A shipyard is erecting an **aluminium superstructure block** of dimensions **8.0 m × 3.0 m × 2.5 m**, made of **Al-5083-H116** plates. A pad-eye is welded to the top. Calculate the allowable lifting load.

### Step 1 — Determine the Block Mass
The block is hollow; assume **average plate thickness $t = 6$ mm = 0.006 m**.

$$V_{steel-equiv} = 2 \cdot (8.0 \cdot 3.0) \cdot 0.006 + 2 \cdot (8.0 \cdot 2.5) \cdot 0.006 + 2 \cdot (3.0 \cdot 2.5) \cdot 0.006$$

$$V_{steel-equiv} = 0.288 + 0.240 + 0.090 = 0.618 \text{ m}^{3}$$

> **Conversion logic:** We calculate the surface area of all six faces of the rectangular block, multiply by the plate thickness, and double (since each face has an inside and outside plating). The actual volume of Al is approximately the same in this case since we are modelling as plate surfaces.

### Step 2 — Apply Aluminium Density
For **Al-5083-H116**, $\rho_{Al} = 2660 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$.

$$m = \rho_{Al} \cdot V = 2660 \cdot 0.618 = 1643.88 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Static Weight
$$W = m \cdot g = 1643.88 \cdot 9.81 = 16{,}126.5 \text{ N} \approx 16.13 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Dynamic Amplification Factor
Marine lifting standards (e.g., DNV-OS-H205) specify $k_{imp} = 0.5$ for normal crane lifts, and up to **1.0** for extreme conditions (sea-state lifting).

$$W_{dyn} = W \cdot (1 + k_{imp}) = 16{,}126.5 \cdot 1.5 = 24{,}189.8 \text{ N} \approx 24.19 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 5 — Calculate Allowable Pad-Eye Stress
The pad-eye is a **circular boss** of diameter $d = 80$ mm. Its cross-sectional area:

$$A_{pad} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (0.080)^{2}}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

Static stress (assuming centric load):

$$\sigma_{static} = \dfrac{W}{A_{pad}} = \dfrac{16{,}126.5}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.21 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 6 — Apply Factor of Safety
For marine aluminium lifting gear, **Factor of Safety $n = 3.0$** is mandatory.

$$\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{n} = \dfrac{215}{3.0} = 71.67 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 7 — Compare and Conclude
Since $\sigma_{static} = 3.21 \text{ MPa} \ll \sigma_{allow} = 71.67 \text{ MPa}$, the pad-eye is **safe with 96% margin**. ✅

> **Conversion logic check:** The 3.21 MPa is the *actual* stress; the 71.67 MPa is the *allowable*. Their ratio (3.21/71.67 = 4.5%) shows the design is very conservative — typical for marine where safety dominates over material economy.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Galvanic Corrosion Check at Al–Steel Joint

**Problem Setup:** An aluminium deck (Al-5083) is bolted to a steel hull girder. Determine if additional cathodic protection is needed.

### Step 1 — Identify the Galvanic Couple
In the standard **galvanic series in seawater**:
- Steel (mild): $E_{Fe} \approx -0.61$ V (vs. SCE)
- Aluminium 5083: $E_{Al} \approx -0.95$ V (vs. SCE)

Aluminium is **more anodic** → it will corrode preferentially.

### Step 2 — Driving Voltage
$$E_{driving} = \vert E_{cathode} - E_{anode} \vert = \vert -0.61 - (-0.95) \vert = 0.34 \text{ V}$$

### Step 3 — Estimate Circuit Resistance
The joint has an insulating neoprene gasket of **resistivity $\rho = 10^{12} \text{ Ω·m}$** and area **$A = 0.02$ m²**, thickness **$t = 2$ mm = 0.002 m**.

$$R_{gasket} = \rho \cdot \dfrac{t}{A} = 10^{12} \cdot \dfrac{0.002}{0.02} = 10^{11} \text{ Ω}$$

> **Conversion logic:** By Ohm's law for a uniform resistive element, $R = \rho \cdot L / A$, where $L$ is the current path length through the gasket. We plug in $t$ for $L$ because the current must traverse the full gasket thickness.

### Step 4 — Calculate Corrosion Current
By Ohm's law:

$$I_{galv} = \dfrac{E_{driving}}{R_{gasket}} = \dfrac{0.34}{10^{11}} = 3.4 \times 10^{-12} \text{ A}$$

### Step 5 — Interpret Result
The current is **3.4 pico-Amperes** — effectively **negligible**. The neoprene gasket provides adequate isolation. ✅

> **Conclusion:** With the insulating gasket, the Al–steel joint is **galvanically safe** even without a sacrificial anode. The installation is **compliant** with IACS UR Z17 / Z23.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Joiner Panel Deflection Check

**Problem Setup:** A **cabin partition panel** is made of **aluminium honeycomb sandwich** with face sheet thickness $t = 1.0$ mm, core thickness $h = 25$ mm, panel size **2.0 m × 1.0 m**, simply supported on all four edges. A uniform load of **$q = 500$ N/m²** is applied (occupancy + furniture). Check deflection limit per ISO 12217.

### Step 1 — Identify Panel Properties
For aluminium honeycomb sandwich (Al face, Al honeycomb core):
- Face yield strength: $\sigma_{y,face} = 215$ MPa
- Effective flexural rigidity (per IACS): $D = 220$ N·m (typical for 1 mm × 25 mm × 1 mm configuration)

### Step 2 — Apply Plate Deflection Formula (Navier's solution for simply supported rectangular plate)
For aspect ratio $a/b = 2.0/1.0 = 2.0$, the coefficient for UDL is $\alpha = 0.00406$ (from Roark's Formulas for Stress & Strain).

$$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot q \cdot a^{4}}{D}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Values
$$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{0.00406 \cdot 500 \cdot (2.0)^{4}}{220}$$

$$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{0.00406 \cdot 500 \cdot 16}{220}$$

$$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{32.48}{220} = 0.1476 \text{ m} = 147.6 \text{ mm}$$

> **Conversion logic:** The cubic term $a^{4}$ is a hallmark of plate bending — deflection grows rapidly with span, which is why large joiner panels use sandwich construction rather than monolithic plate.

### Step 4 — Apply Deflection Limit
ISO 12217 / IMO FTP Code allow $\delta_{max} \leq a/200$ for non-structural partitions:

$$\delta_{limit} = \dfrac{2.0 \text{ m}}{200} = 0.01 \text{ m} = 10 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 5 — Compare
$\delta_{max} = 147.6$ mm $\gg$ $\delta_{limit} = 10$ mm. ❌ **NOT ACCEPTABLE.**

### Step 6 — Iterate to Find Required $D$
Solving for $D$:

$$D_{req} = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot q \cdot a^{4}}{\delta_{limit}} = \dfrac{0.00406 \cdot 500 \cdot 16}{0.01} = 3248 \text{ N·m}$$

> **Conclusion:** The current panel is **way under-designed**. The shipyard must select a panel with **$D \geq 3248$ N·m**, which corresponds to increasing the face sheet thickness to **$t = 1.5$ mm** and using a higher-density honeycomb core. This is a **standard failure mode** in KTU exam questions — students must catch the unit conversions and span ratio carefully.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Material Selection for Common Joiner Applications

| Component | Function | Material (Non-Ferrous) | Thickness Range | Standard / Code | Cost Index |
|-----------|----------|------------------------|-----------------|------------------|------------|
| **Cabin partition** | Privacy, fire barrier | Al honeycomb sandwich + PVC laminate | 25–50 mm | IMO FTP Pt 5 | 4/10 |
| **Ceiling panel** | Acoustic + fire | Al perforated sheet + rockwool | 50–75 mm | SOLAS Reg. II-2/3.2 | 5/10 |
| **Floor covering** | Anti-slip, durable | Anodized Al tread plate (5-bar) | 3–6 mm | ISO 8468 | 3/10 |
| **Door (fire-rated)** | 60-min fire integrity | Al frame + mineral wool core | 45–55 mm | SOLAS Reg. II-2/9 | 6/10 |
| **Window frame (porthole)** | Watertight + corrosion resistant | Bronze / Cu-Ni / Al-bronze | – | ISO 1751 | 7/10 |
| **Sanitary piping** | Water supply / waste | Cu-Ni 90/10 (DN15–DN100) | – | ISO 17885 | 5/10 |
| **Bunk frame** | Crew sleeping | Al-6082 T6 extruded profile | – | ISO 8468 | 4/10 |
| **Galley counter** | Food safety | Stainless (AISI 316) — *not strictly non-ferrous, but non-corroding* | 1.5 mm | NSF/ANSI 51 | 6/10 |
| **Cable tray** | Power & data | Anodized Al-6063 | 1.5–2.5 mm | IEC 61537 | 4/10 |

## 3.5 KTU Numerical: Total Crew Berth Calculation

**Given:** A cargo ship has the following spaces:
- Officers' cabins: 12 × 12 m²
- Petty officers' cabins: 8 × 8 m²
- Crew cabins: 20 × 6 m²
- Hospital: 1 × 16 m²
- Recreation room: 1 × 30 m²

**Find:** Total number of berths and check if MLC 2006 standard (minimum 4.5 m² per person in crew spaces) is satisfied.

### Step 1 — Total Area
$$A_{total} = 12 \cdot 12 + 8 \cdot 8 + 20 \cdot 6 + 1 \cdot 16 + 1 \cdot 30$$
$$A_{total} = 144 + 64 + 120 + 16 + 30 = 374 \text{ m}^{2}$$

### Step 2 — Apply MLC 2006 Limit
$$N_{berths} = \dfrac{374}{4.5} = 83.1 \Rightarrow \text{Round down to } 83 \text{ persons}$$

### Step 3 — Subtract Non-Berth Spaces
Hospital and recreation are not sleeping spaces (typically excluded from MLC counting).
$$A_{sleep} = 374 - 16 - 30 = 328 \text{ m}^{2}$$
$$N_{berths}^{corr} = \dfrac{328}{4.5} = 72.9 \Rightarrow 72 \text{ berths}$$

### Step 4 — Conclusion
**Maximum 72 crew can be accommodated** under MLC 2006 standards. Any berth addition requires expansion of cabin area or reduction in hospital/recreation space.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below are designed for **Mermaid v10+ compatibility**. All node IDs are alphanumeric, and labels use **clean uppercase alphanumeric text** only (no markdown formatting inside quoted labels).

```

flowchart TD
    top[ALUMINIUM SUPERSTRUCTURE BLOCK]
    pad1[ALUMINIUM STRONG BACK]
    gasket[NEOPRENE OR PTFE GASKET 2 MM]
    pad2[STEEL BRACKET]
    bot[STEEL HULL DECK PLATE]
    anode[ZINC SACRIFICIAL ANODE]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The pink gasket (Neoprene/PTFE) is the **dielectric barrier** that breaks the galvanic circuit. The blue sacrificial anode is the **controlled path** for protective current — it corrodes preferentially, sparing both the aluminium and the steel.
