# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Question)]**
**Define the term "Erection" in shipbuilding. List any three non-ferrous materials used as fasteners in the erection process and justify their selection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Erection in shipbuilding is the process of **lifting, positioning, aligning, and joining prefabricated hull blocks and sub-assemblies** onto the building berth or in dry dock to form the complete hull structure. It involves temporary attachment followed by permanent joining using welding, bolting, or riveting, all in accordance with approved method statements and classification society rules.

**Three non-ferrous fasteners (1.5 marks):**

| # | Material | Justification |
|---|----------|---------------|
| 1 | **Monel (Ni-Cu alloy 400)** | High strength + excellent seawater corrosion resistance; used for superstructure-to-hull bolts |
| 2 | **Copper-Nickel 70/30** | High resistance to biofouling and galvanic corrosion; used for pipe flanges and seawater fasteners |
| 3 | **Aluminium Bronze (Cu-Al-Fe)** | High strength + spark-resistant; used in hazardous zones (tanker decks) where sparks must be avoided |

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Question)]**
**Differentiate between "Joiner Work" and "Outfitting" in shipbuilding. Name the classification society rules that govern joiner work.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Joiner Work | Outfitting (General) |
|--------|-------------|----------------------|
| **Scope** | Interior non-structural carpentry, paneling, furniture | All post-construction activities (piping, electrical, HVAC, safety) |
| **Materials** | Wood, plywood, laminates, aluminium partitions, PVC | Pipes, cables, ducting, pumps, life-saving appliances |
| **Examples** | Cabins, mess rooms, galley joinery, ceiling | Engine alignment, electrical cabling, fire-fighting systems |
| **Standards** | SOLAS Ch. II-2, IMO FTP Code, ISO 12217, ISO 8468 | IACS UR series, SOLAS Ch. III (life-saving), IEC 60092 |

**Governing Rules (1 mark):**
- **IRS Rules Pt. 3 Ch. 11** (Hull Outfitting)
- **SOLAS Chapter II-2** (Fire protection)
- **IMO FTP Code** (Fire test procedures)
- **ISO 12217** (Furniture strength & durability)
- **IACS S21** (Hull erection tolerances)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and typically has internal choice (a) and (b) carrying 7 marks each.

---

### 📘 Question Choice A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the various stages of **ship hull erection** with a neat block diagram. Highlight the role of non-ferrous materials at each stage.

**(b) [7 marks]** A shipyard needs to erect an **aluminium superstructure block** weighing **25 tonnes**. The lifting gear uses **four pad-eyes**, each of cross-sectional area **1500 mm²**, made of **Al-5083-H116** ($\sigma_y = 215$ MPa). Verify if the design is safe under:
- A dynamic amplification factor of $k_{imp} = 0.5$
- A factor of safety of $n = 3.0$

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Stages of Hull Erection [7 marks]

The eleven canonical stages of hull erection are:

**Stage 1: Pre-Erection Planning** [0.5]
- Review of EES drawing, method statement, JSA, lifting study
- Role of non-ferrous: **zinc anodes** pre-positioned in templates

**Stage 2: Berth & Block Survey** [0.5]
- Reference baseline, buttock lines, frame lines verified
- Non-ferrous: **cathodic protection continuity strips** checked

**Stage 3: Rigging** [0.5]
- Slings, shackles, spreader beams attached
- Non-ferrous: **Monel pad-eyes** for aluminium blocks; **galvanised steel** for steel blocks

**Stage 4: Lifting** [1.0]
- Block lifted by gantry or tower crane
- Non-ferrous: **aluminium alloy lifting beam** to avoid magnetic disturbance near compass

**Stage 5: Positioning** [0.5]
- Block lowered onto camber bars, keel blocks
- Non-ferrous: **PTFE slider pads** reduce friction

**Stage 6: Alignment** [0.5]
- Hydraulic jacks, dogs, shores adjust block to ±3 mm tolerance
- Non-ferrous: **brass shims** for fine adjustment

**Stage 7: Temporary Attachment** [0.5]
- Tack welds, strong-backs, erection brackets
- Non-ferrous: **aluminium strong-backs** prevent steel contamination

**Stage 8: Final Joining** [1.0]
- Butt welding of seams
- Non-ferrous: **Al-Si welding consumables (Al-4043, Al-5356)** for aluminium blocks

**Stage 9: NDT** [0.5]
- UT, MPI, DPI on all welds
- Non-ferrous coupons included for weld procedure qualification

**Stage 10: Dimensional Survey** [0.5]
- Confirm hull form within IACS S21 tolerance
- Non-ferrous: **invar tapes with brass fittings** for low thermal expansion measurement

**Stage 11: Cathodic Protection Check** [0.5]
- Verify anode-to-hull electrical continuity
- Non-ferrous: **zinc / Al-Zn-In anodes** tested for capacity

> *Full 7 marks awarded for diagram + clear explanation with non-ferrous annotations.*

### Part (b) — Lifting Pad-Eye Safety Check [7 marks]

**Step 1: Convert block weight to Newtons** [1.0]
$$W = m \cdot g = 25{,}000 \cdot 9.81 = 245{,}250 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Apply dynamic amplification** [1.0]
$$W_{dyn} = W \cdot (1 + k_{imp}) = 245{,}250 \cdot 1.5 = 367{,}875 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Load per pad-eye (4 pad-eyes share equally)** [1.0]
$$P_{eye} = \dfrac{367{,}875}{4} = 91{,}968.75 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Calculate stress in pad-eye** [1.5]
$$A_{eye} = 1500 \text{ mm}^{2} = 1500 \cdot 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{2} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$
$$\sigma_{actual} = \dfrac{P_{eye}}{A_{eye}} = \dfrac{91{,}968.75}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 61.31 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Calculate allowable stress** [1.5]
$$\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{n} = \dfrac{215}{3.0} = 71.67 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6: Compare and conclude** [1.0]
Since $\sigma_{actual} = 61.31 \text{ MPa} < \sigma_{allow} = 71.67 \text{ MPa}$, the design is **SAFE** with a margin of 14.5%. ✅

**Margin of safety:**
$$MS = \dfrac{71.67 - 61.31}{61.31} \cdot 100 = 16.9\%$$

---

### 📗 Question Choice B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **classification of joiner work** in a modern merchant ship. List at least **four non-ferrous materials** commonly used and explain why they are preferred over ferrous materials for joiner applications.

**(b) [7 marks]** A cabin partition panel of dimensions **2.5 m × 1.2 m** is subjected to a uniform load of **$q = 400$ N/m²**. The panel is **simply supported** on all four edges and has a flexural rigidity $D = 450$ N·m. Using the plate deflection formula, determine the maximum deflection and state whether it meets the ISO 12217 limit of $a/200$.

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Classification of Joiner Work [7 marks]

Joiner work is classified into **three hierarchical levels** (1 mark for classification + 4 marks for materials + 2 marks for justification):

**Level 1 — Primary Joiner (1 mark)**
- Bulkhead panels, ceiling panels, deck coverings, doors, linings, insulation
- These define the **habitability envelope** of the ship

**Level 2 — Secondary Joiner (1 mark)**
- Bunks, lockers, wardrobes, desks, shelves, mess tables
- These are **furniture-grade** items installed within primary joiner spaces

**Level 3 — Tertiary Joiner / Trim (1 mark)**
- Name plates, signs, door hardware, curtain rails, sanitary accessories
- These are **finishing touches** that complete the outfit

**Four Non-Ferrous Materials (4 marks):**

| # | Material | Application | Why preferred over ferrous |
|---|----------|-------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Aluminium 5083-H116** | Partition panels, ceiling panels | Lighter (2.66 vs 7.85 g/cm³), corrosion resistant, easy to fabricate |
| 2 | **Copper-Nickel 90/10** | Sanitary piping, water service | Biofouling resistant, no internal corrosion, long life (40+ years) |
| 3 | **Anodized Aluminium 6063** | Trim profiles, window frames, curtain rails | Aesthetic finish, scratch-resistant, does not rust |
| 4 | **Aluminium Bronze (Cu-Al-Fe-Ni)** | Door hardware, hinges, latches | High strength + non-sparking (safe in flammable atmospheres) |

**Justification for non-ferrous preference (1 mark):**
- **Corrosion resistance** in marine atmosphere (salt-laden air)
- **Weight savings** (Al reduces upper-deck weight → improves stability & fuel efficiency)
- **Maintenance-free** (no painting required over 25-year service life)
- **Non-magnetic** (Al & Cu-Ni do not disturb magnetic compasses)
- **Non-sparking** (Al-bronze used in hazardous zones)
- **Aesthetic** (anodized finishes retain appearance for decades)

### Part (b) — Panel Deflection Calculation [7 marks]

**Step 1: Identify aspect ratio** [1.0]
$$\dfrac{a}{b} = \dfrac{2.5}{1.2} = 2.083$$

**Step 2: Look up plate coefficient** [1.0]
For simply supported rectangular plate with UDL, Roark's Table 11.2:
- At $a/b = 2.0$, $\alpha = 0.00406$
- At $a/b = 2.5$, $\alpha = 0.00585$
- Interpolating: $\alpha \approx 0.00406 + 0.4 \cdot (0.00585 - 0.00406) = 0.00478$

**Step 3: Apply plate deflection formula** [2.0]
$$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot q \cdot a^{4}}{D} = \dfrac{0.00478 \cdot 400 \cdot (2.5)^{4}}{450}$$

$$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{0.00478 \cdot 400 \cdot 39.0625}{450}$$

$$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{74.69}{450} = 0.1660 \text{ m} = 166.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Calculate ISO 12217 limit** [1.0]
$$\delta_{limit} = \dfrac{a}{200} = \dfrac{2.5}{200} = 0.0125 \text{ m} = 12.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Compare** [1.0]
$\delta_{max} = 166.0 \text{ mm} \gg \delta_{limit} = 12.5 \text{ mm}$. ❌

**Step 6: Conclude and recommend** [1.0]
The panel **fails** the deflection criterion by a factor of **13.3×**. The joiner designer must:
- Increase $D$ to at least $\dfrac{0.00478 \cdot 400 \cdot 39.0625}{0.0125} = 5973$ N·m
- This can be achieved by **increasing face sheet thickness to 2.0 mm** and using a **higher-density honeycomb core** (e.g., 80 kg/m³ instead of 50 kg/m³)
- Alternatively, add **intermediate stiffeners** at 600 mm spacing

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> **1. Unit Conversion Errors (Most common, -2 to -3 marks):** Students routinely forget to convert mm² to m² or kN to N. Always write: "$A = 1500 \text{ mm}^{2} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$" explicitly. KTU examiners deduct heavily for ambiguous units.
>
> **2. Confusing Static vs. Dynamic Stress (-2 marks):** Always include $k_{imp}$ in lifting calculations. A pure static check is **incomplete** and will be marked down.
>
> **3. Forgetting Factor of Safety in Allowable Stress (-2 marks):** The yield strength is **never** the allowable. Always divide by $n = 2.5$ to $3.0$ for marine aluminium.
>
> **4. Mis-Identifying the Cathode/Anode in Galvanic Corrosion (-1 mark):** The **more anodic** (more negative potential) material **corrodes**. For Al–Steel, the **Al corrodes**, not the steel. Many students write the opposite.
>
> **5. Omitting the Plate Coefficient $\alpha$ in Deflection Calculations (-2 marks):** Using $q \cdot a^4 / D$ directly without $\alpha$ yields an **incorrect answer**. The coefficient depends on aspect ratio $a/b$ and is **mandatory**.
>
> **6. Mixing Ferrous with Non-Ferrous (-1 to -2 marks):** If a question asks for **non-ferrous materials**, do **not** list stainless steel unless the question explicitly says "non-corroding." Stainless is technically iron-based ferrous.
>
> **7. Not Specifying Alloy Temper (-1 mark):** Always state the **temper** (e.g., H116, T6, O). A bare "Al-5083" loses a mark; "Al-5083-H116" is exam-perfect.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🧠 MEMORIZE THIS CHECKLIST BEFORE ENTERING THE EXAM HALL**

### A. Core Concepts
- [ ] **Erection** = assembly of pre-fabricated blocks (lifting + aligning + joining)
- [ ] **Joiner work** = non-structural interior outfitting (cabins, panels, furniture)
- [ ] Both are governed by **classification society rules** (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV)
- [ ] **Non-ferrous materials** dominate where **corrosion, weight, or non-sparking** properties matter

### B. Material Quick-Reference
- [ ] **Al-5083-H116** → hull plates, superstructures (highest marine Al strength)
- [ ] **Al-6061-T6 / 6082-T6** → structural extrusions, joiner frames
- [ ] **Al-6063** → architectural trim, window profiles
- [ ] **Cu-Ni 90/10** → piping, heat exchangers
- [ ] **Cu-Ni 70/30** → high-pressure piping, condensers
- [ ] **Al-bronze** → propellers, sea-water pumps, non-sparking hardware
- [ ] **Monel 400** → fasteners for Al–Steel joints
- [ ] **Zinc / Al-Zn-In** → sacrificial anodes

### C. Formula Quick-Fire
- [ ] $\sigma = F/A$ (axial stress in pad-eye)
- [ ] $W_{dyn} = W(1 + k_{imp})$ (dynamic lifting load)
- [ ] $\sigma_{allow} = \sigma_y / n$ (allowable design stress)
- [ ] $\delta = \alpha \cdot q \cdot a^4 / D$ (plate deflection)
- [ ] $\delta_{limit} = a/200$ (ISO 12217 panel limit)
- [ ] $E_{galv} = \vert E_c - E_a \vert$ (galvanic driving voltage)
- [ ] $R = \rho \cdot t / A$ (gasket resistance, Ohm's law for dielectrics)
- [ ] $I = E_{galv} / R$ (galvanic corrosion current)
- [ ] $N_{berths} = A_{sleep} / 4.5$ (MLC 2006 crew accommodation)

### D. Standards & Codes to Remember
- [ ] **SOLAS Ch. II-2** — Fire protection
- [ ] **IMO FTP Code** — Fire test procedures
- [ ] **IACS S21** — Hull erection tolerances (±3 mm critical)
- [ ] **ISO 12217** — Furniture strength (deflection limit $a/200$)
- [ ] **ISO 8468** — Bridge & cabin layout
- [ ] **IRS Pt. 3 Ch. 11** — Hull outfitting rules
- [ ] **MLC 2006** — Maritime Labour Convention (≥4.5 m²/berth)

### E. Common Numerical Traps
- [ ] Always convert **mm² → m²** (divide by $10^6$)
- [ ] Always convert **tonnes → kg → N** (multiply by 1000 × 9.81)
- [ ] Always apply **k_imp = 0.5 to 1.0** for lifting
- [ ] Always divide by **factor of safety n = 3.0** for aluminium
- [ ] Always interpolate $\alpha$ from Roark's Table for non-standard aspect ratios

### F. Acronyms
- [ ] **EES** = Erection Elementary Sheet
- [ ] **JSA** = Job Safety Analysis
- [ ] **NDT** = Non-Destructive Testing (UT, MPI, DPI)
- [ ] **MLC** = Maritime Labour Convention
- [ ] **CoG** = Centre of Gravity
- [ ] **FTP** = Fire Test Procedures (IMO)
- [ ] **IACS** = International Association of Classification Societies
- [ ] **IRS** = Indian Register of Shipping

### G. Last-Minute Diagram Triggers
- [ ] If the question asks for "stages of erection" → draw an **11-step flowchart** with the non-ferrous annotation at each step.
- [ ] If the question asks for "joiner work classification" → draw an **L1-L2-L3 hierarchy** with at least 3 components at each level.
- [ ] If the question asks for "galvanic isolation" → draw an **Al–gasket–steel** cross-section with a sacrificial anode.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Erections and Joiner Work**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preservation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Preservation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Preservation** in the context of shipbuilding refers to the comprehensive set of metallurgical, electrochemical, chemical, and mechanical techniques employed to protect non-ferrous metals and alloys from the aggressive marine environment. A ship operates in a highly corrosive electrolyte (seawater, typically containing **3.5% dissolved salts**, predominantly NaCl) coupled with biological fouling organisms, UV radiation from sunlight, splash-zone wet-dry cycling, and galvanic coupling between dissimilar metals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preservation ≠ just "painting a ship"**. In KTU 2024 syllabus context, preservation is a *systems engineering discipline* combining:
> 1. Cathodic protection (sacrificial anodes + ICCP)
> 2. Protective coatings (primers, topcoats, anti-fouling)
> 3. Surface preparation (ISO 8501-1 standards)
> 4. Conversion coatings (anodizing, chromating)
> 5. Design mitigation (galvanic isolation, drainage)

## 1.2 The Corrosion Threat to Non-Ferrous Ship Materials

Non-ferrous metals are preferred in shipbuilding for their high specific strength, non-magnetic behavior, seawater resistance, and thermal conductivity. However, each is vulnerable:

| Non-Ferrous Material | Primary Application in Ships | Dominant Degradation Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Aluminium Alloys** (5083, 5086, 6082) | Superstructure, hulls of fast craft | Pitting, galvanic, crevice |
| **Copper Alloys** (Cu-Ni 90/10, 70/30, Al-bronze) | Pipework, heat exchangers, propellers | Erosion-corrosion, impingement |
| **Brass / Bronze** | Valves, fittings, propellers | Dezincification, season cracking |
| **Lead** | Cable sheathing, ballast keels | Creep, fatigue, PbCO₃ formation |
| **Zinc** | Galvanic anodes, sacrificial trim | Self-sacrificial (intended) |
| **Magnesium Alloys** | Specific lightweight parts | Severe pitting, galvanic |
| **Titanium** | Submersibles, propellers (naval) | Crevice, hydrogen embrittlement |
| **Nickel Alloys** (Monel, Inconel) | Critical seawater systems | Crevice, SCC |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bodyguard System"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Ship as a Celebrity**
> Imagine a ship as a famous celebrity (the *non-ferrous metal*) constantly surrounded by hostile paparazzi (the *marine environment — Cl⁻ ions, dissolved O₂, microorganisms*). Preservation is the multi-layered bodyguard detail:
> - The **outer bodyguards (coatings)** form a physical barrier blocking direct contact.
> - The **inner bodyguards (sacrificial anodes)** absorb "attacks" meant for the celebrity — they take the hit so the celebrity stays safe.
> - The **electronic security system (ICCP)** actively projects an invisible field that repels attackers.
> - The **cleaning crew (surface preparation)** ensures the bodyguards stick properly.
> 
> If you remove *any one* layer, the protection fails. This is why ship preservation is **defense in depth**.

## 1.4 Why Preservation is Central to Naval Architecture

A single through-hull corrosion pit in a copper-nickel pipe can flood a compartment. The economic impact of corrosion globally is estimated at **3–4% of GDP**, with maritime corrosion accounting for a significant share. For naval vessels, an undetected corrosion failure can cause mission abort or loss of life. KTU emphasizes preservation as a *life-cycle* discipline spanning design → construction → service → maintenance → decommissioning.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Corrosion Electrochemistry (The Foundation of All Preservation)

Corrosion is an **electrochemical process** requiring four elements:

1. **Anode** (metal dissolution site): $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$
2. **Cathode** (reduction site): $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$ (in neutral/aerobic seawater)
3. **Electrolyte** (seawater — ionic conductor)
4. **Metallic path** (electron conductor — the hull itself)

The **Nernst Equation** governs electrode potential in a given environment:

$$E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{[Ox]}{[Red]}$$

Where:
- $E$ = electrode potential under non-standard conditions (V)
- $E^\circ$ = standard electrode potential (V)
- $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** (universal gas constant)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred
- $F$ = **96,485 C/mol** (Faraday's constant)
- $[Ox], [Red]$ = activities of oxidized/reduced species

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Galvanic Series vs. EMF Series**: For ship preservation, the **Galvanic Series in Seawater** is what matters, not the textbook EMF Series. The galvanic series gives *real-world* potentials accounting for passivation, biofilm effects, and polarization. Example ordering (anodic → cathodic): **Mg → Zn → Al-alloys → mild steel → Cu-Ni → Bronze → Monel → Ti → Graphite**.

## 2.2 Faradaic Principles for Cathodic Protection Design

The mass of metal lost through anodic dissolution follows **Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis**:

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

Where:
- $m$ = mass of metal dissolved (grams)
- $I$ = current (Amperes)
- $t$ = time (seconds)
- $M$ = atomic mass (g/mol)
- $n$ = valence (electrons per atom)
- $F$ = **96,485 C/mol**

**Combined with Faraday's Second Law**, this allows us to calculate anode life:

$$t_{life} = \frac{m_{anode} \cdot n \cdot F}{I_{required} \cdot M}$$

## 2.3 Corrosion Rate Calculations

Corrosion rate is typically expressed as **mils per year (mpy)** or **millimeters per year (mm/yr)**:

$$CR_{mpy} = \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$$

$$CR_{mm/yr} = \frac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$$

Where:
- $W$ = weight loss (mg)
- $D$ = density of metal (g/cm³)
- $A$ = exposed surface area (in² for mpy, cm² for mm/yr)
- $T$ = exposure time (hours)
- Constants: **534** (mils·in² conversion) and **87.6** (mm·cm² conversion)

## 2.4 Cathodic Protection Current Density Requirements

Different marine zones demand different protection current densities:

| Zone | Typical Current Density (mA/m²) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Hull (submerged) | **80 – 120** | Painted hull, tropical waters |
| Hull (bare steel) | **150 – 300** | Uncoated reference |
| Bronze propellers | **300 – 500** | High turbulence, O₂-rich |
| Ballast tanks | **100 – 150** | Alternately wet/dry |
| Splash zone | **250 – 500** | Aggressive aeration |
| Seawater piping internals | **100 – 200** | Flow-dependent |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nernst Equation | $E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\frac{[Ox]}{Red]}$ | V | Predicting equilibrium potential |
| 2 | Faraday's 1st Law | $m = \frac{ItM}{nF}$ | g | Anode mass consumption |
| 3 | Anode life | $t_{life} = \frac{m_{anode} \cdot n \cdot F}{I_{req} \cdot M}$ | seconds | Anode replacement schedule |
| 4 | Corrosion rate (mpy) | $CR = \frac{534W}{DAT}$ | mpy | Field coupon testing |
| 5 | Corrosion rate (mm/yr) | $CR = \frac{87.6W}{DAT}$ | mm/yr | Lab/field comparison |
| 6 | Current density | $i = \frac{I}{A}$ | mA/m² | CP design |
| 7 | Pit depth estimate | $d = k\sqrt{t}$ (Tafel) | mm | Long-term localized corrosion |
| 8 | Resistance to ground (anode) | $R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\frac{4L}{r}-1\right]$ | Ω | Anode throw calculation |
| 9 | Cathode protection potential | $E_{Cu/CuSO_4} = -0.85$ V | V | Reference for steel (-0.95 V for Al) |
| 10 | Utilization factor | $U = \frac{actual\ m}{theoretical\ m}$ | dimensionless | Anode efficiency (0.50–0.95) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference Electrode for Marine CP**: **Silver/Silver Chloride (Ag/AgCl)** is the modern standard, but **Copper/Copper Sulfate (CSE)** remains the most common field reference. Conversion: $E_{Ag/AgCl} = E_{CSE} - 0.06$ V approximately. The **-0.85 V vs CSE** criterion is the universally accepted protection threshold for steel; aluminium requires **-0.95 V vs CSE** because of its more active nature.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility in Real Shipyards

- **Naval architects** use the corrosion rate formulas to specify hull plate scantlings with corrosion allowance.
- **Cathodic protection designers** use the current density × area formula to size the number of zinc/aluminium anodes.
- **Coating inspectors** use the **DFT (Dry Film Thickness)** measurements and pull-off adhesion tests to validate coating systems.
- **Classification societies** (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, IRS) enforce minimum preservation standards; non-compliance blocks commercial certification.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrices & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Corrosion Rate from Faraday's Law → Engineering Units

The starting point is Faraday's First Law giving mass loss. We need to convert grams of metal lost into a *thickness loss* (because that's what causes a hull plate to fail), and then into a *rate* per year.

**Step 1 — Start with mass loss formula:**

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

This is in grams. A current of 1 A for 1 second deposits/liberates a known mass.

**Step 2 — Convert mass to volume using density:**

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 3 — Convert volume to thickness (depth of attack) using area $A$:**

$$d = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize that current density $i = I/A$:**

$$d = \frac{i \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 5 — Express time in hours** (standard for corrosion coupons). Let $t$ in hours $= 3600 \cdot t_{sec}$. To convert to *per year* multiply by 8760 hours/year.

For **iron** in seawater: $M = 55.85$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $\rho = 7.87$ g/cm³, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.

**Step 6 — Plug in iron's values and convert to mils (1 mil = 0.001 inch = 0.00254 cm):**

$$d_{mpy} = \frac{i \cdot t \cdot 55.85 \cdot 2.54 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \cdot 96485 \cdot 7.87 \cdot 3600 \cdot A_{in^2}}$$

After unit conversion and simplifications (which include the factors 8760 hours/year, 2.54 cm/in, etc.), the equation reduces to the **standard empirical form**:

$$\boxed{CR_{mpy} = \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}}$$

Where:
- $W$ = weight loss in **mg**
- $D$ = density in **g/cm³**
- $A$ = area in **in²**
- $T$ = time in **hours**

**Step 7 — Worked Example**: A copper coupon (density 8.96 g/cm³), area 12 in², loses 280 mg over 720 hours.

$$CR = \frac{534 \times 280}{8.96 \times 12 \times 720} = \frac{149{,}520}{77{,}414.4} = 1.93\ mpy$$

Converting to mm/yr using conversion factor **1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/yr**:

$$CR = 1.93 \times 0.0254 = 0.049\ mm/yr$$

This is typical for copper in calm seawater (acceptable; copper-nickel 90/10 in ship service: ~0.02–0.05 mm/yr).

## 3.2 Worked Example: Anode Life Calculation

A naval ship requires a cathodic protection current of **30 A** continuously on a 1200 m² hull. **Zinc anodes** (Zn atomic mass = 65.4 g/mol, n = 2) are used, with **utilization factor U = 0.85**. Each anode has mass **5 kg**.

**Step 1 — Theoretical mass of zinc consumed per second by 1 A:**

$$m_{sec} = \frac{M}{nF} = \frac{65.4}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 3.39 \times 10^{-4}\ g/s\ per\ A$$

**Step 2 — Total zinc consumed per year by 30 A:**

$$m_{year} = 3.39 \times 10^{-4} \times 30 \times 86400 \times 365$$

Breaking it down:
- Seconds in 1 day: **86,400 s**
- Days in 1 year: **365**
- $m_{year} = 3.39 \times 10^{-4} \times 30 \times 86{,}400 \times 365$
- $m_{year} = 3.39 \times 10^{-4} \times 30 = 0.01017$ g/s
- $0.01017 \times 86{,}400 = 878.7$ g/day
- $878.7 \times 365 = 320{,}727$ g/year ≈ **320.7 kg/year**

**Step 3 — Apply utilization factor (only 85% of anode is usable):**

$$m_{usable\ per\ anode} = 5 \times 0.85 = 4.25\ kg$$

**Step 4 — Number of anode-years needed:**

$$N = \frac{320.7}{4.25} = 75.5\ anodes\ per\ year$$

**Step 5 — For a 5-year docking cycle:**

$$N_{total} = 75.5 \times 5 \approx 378\ anodes$$

**Step 6 — Mounting and grouping**: Typically installed in groups on the hull; many shipyards use **15–20 kg zinc alloy anodes** for large naval hulls, reducing the count to ~100–120 anodes.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing a Preservation Method

| Method | Best For | Initial Cost | Life-Cycle Cost | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Zinc sacrificial anode** | Steel hulls, small vessels | Low | High (regular replacement) | Low driving voltage, heavy |
| **Aluminium sacrificial anode** | Long-term service, naval | Low-Medium | Low | Passivation risk in low-O₂ |
| **Magnesium sacrificial anode** | Freshwater, low-resistivity | Medium | High (rapid consumption) | Not for warm seawater |
| **ICCP (Impressed Current)** | Tankers, large naval ships | High | Low | Requires power, monitoring |
| **Epoxy anti-corrosive primer** | All marine steel | Medium | Low | Surface prep critical |
| **Anti-fouling (Cu₂O/TBT-free)** | Underwater hull | Medium-High | Medium | Environmental regulations |
| **Hot-dip galvanizing** | Pipework, fittings | Medium | Low | Coating damaged at welding |
| **Anodizing (Type II/III)** | Aluminium superstructure | Low | Low | Thin layer (~25 μm) |
| **Powder coating** | Interior bulkheads | Low | Very Low | Not for submerged surfaces |

## 3.4 Surface Preparation Standards — Analytical Reference

ISO 8501-1 governs visible cleanliness before coating:

| Standard | Method | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Sa 1** | Light blast | Maintenance, low-spec |
| **Sa 2** | Thorough commercial blast | General marine |
| **Sa 2½** | Very thorough commercial blast | **Naval standard (most common)** |
| **Sa 3** | White metal blast | Critical applications, potable water tanks |
| **St 2** | Hand/power tool clean | Touch-up only |
| **St 3** | Very thorough hand tool | Restricted access |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Sa 2½ is the naval default**: A 75–125 μm blast profile (anchor pattern) is required for epoxy primers to achieve ≥ 7 MPa pull-off adhesion. Going to Sa 3 doubles the cost with marginal benefit for most applications.

## 3.5 Total Cathodic Protection System Sizing Logic

The design logic for full CP design:

**Step 1 — Define the protected area** ($A_{hull}, A_{propeller}, A_{rudder}$).

**Step 2 — Select design current density** for each zone (from the table in §2.4). For a typical painted naval hull: $i = 100$ mA/m².

**Step 3 — Compute total current required**:

$$I_{total} = \sum (A_{zone} \times i_{zone})$$

**Step 4 — Compute anode current output** using Ohm's law for a single anode in seawater ($\rho_{sw} \approx 0.25$ Ω·m):

$$I_{anode} = \frac{\Delta E}{R_a}$$

Where $R_a$ is the anode-to-seawater resistance (Dwight's formula for slender anodes):

$$R_a = \frac{\rho_{sw}}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\left(\frac{4L}{r}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Step 5 — Compute number of anodes** $N = I_{total} / I_{anode}$.

**Step 6 — Compute anode life** using Faraday's law + utilization factor from §3.2.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define sacrificial anode and state TWO criteria for selecting anode material. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **sacrificial anode** is a more electrochemically active metal deliberately coupled to the structure to be protected (the cathode). Due to the galvanic potential difference, the anode preferentially corrodes, supplying protective cathodic current to the structure.

**Selection criteria:**
1. **Anode potential** must be sufficiently negative (more active) than the protected metal — typically ≥ **0.2 V more negative** to overcome polarization and IR drop.
2. **High electrochemical capacity** — the anode must deliver large ampere-hours per kilogram of consumed mass (e.g., Al alloy anodes deliver **~2,500 Ah/kg** vs Zn's **~780 Ah/kg**).
3. (Bonus) **Low self-corrosion rate**, **good mechanical properties**, **uniform dissolution** (no passivation), and **non-toxicity** for marine discharge.

---

### Question 2: List any THREE marine corrosion forms affecting non-ferrous metals. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **Galvanic corrosion** — occurs when two dissimilar metals (e.g., bronze propeller on steel shaft) are electrically coupled in seawater, accelerating attack on the more anodic member.
2. **Pitting corrosion** — localized attack producing deep cavities; common in aluminium alloys (5083, 6082) due to breakdown of passive oxide film by Cl⁻ ions.
3. **Crevice corrosion** — occurs in shielded gaps (gasket faces, lap joints) where stagnant electrolyte becomes depleted of O₂; affects Cu-Ni piping, titanium fasteners, stainless hardware.
4. (Bonus) **Erosion-corrosion** — mechanical abrasion accelerates chemical attack; Cu-Ni pipework at pump discharges.
5. (Bonus) **Dezincification** — selective leaching of zinc from brass (e.g., dezincified brass valves lose mechanical strength while appearing intact).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: Comprehensive Question

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
> 
> **(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the **principle and design of cathodic protection by impressed current method (ICCP)** for a ship's hull. List **TWO advantages** and **TWO limitations**. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A zinc anode of mass **8 kg** is used to protect a steel pipe in seawater. The required protective current is **2.0 A**. The zinc has atomic mass **65.4**, valence **2**, Faraday's constant **96,485 C/mol**, and a utilization factor of **0.85**. Calculate the **expected life of the anode in years**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Principle of ICCP:**
In Impressed Current Cathodic Protection, an external **DC power source (transformer-rectifier unit, TRU)** forces current from inert anodes mounted on the hull, through the seawater, and back to the hull (which acts as the cathode). The hull's potential is driven to **−0.85 V vs Cu/CuSO₄ reference** (or more negative), making it thermodynamically immune to corrosion.

**Components of the ICCP system:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TRU[AC Mains 440V] --> TR[Transformer Rectifier TRU]
    TR -->|DC positive| AN[Mixed Metal Oxide Anodes on Hull]
    AN --> SW[Seawater Electrolyte]
    SW -->|Cathodic current| HULL[Ship Hull as Cathode]
    HULL -->|DC negative cable| TR
    REF[Ag/AgCl Reference Electrode] -->|mV feedback| CTRL[Controller]
    CTRL -->|Adjust output| TR
```

**Advantages of ICCP:**
1. **Adjustable** — current can be increased/decreased based on coating degradation, voyage profile, or water resistivity.
2. **Long anode life** — inert MMO anodes last **15–25 years** without replacement, vs 3–5 years for sacrificial anodes.
3. **Suitable for large structures** — single TRU can protect the entire hull of a tanker or carrier.
4. (Bonus) **Lower weight penalty** — no need for hundreds of bolted zinc blocks.

**Limitations of ICCP:**
1. **Requires continuous electrical power** — failure of TRU during long power outage = unprotected hull.
2. **Risk of over-protection** — driving potential too negative (−1.2 V vs CSE) on aluminium hulls causes **cathodic corrosion** and hydrogen embrittlement.
3. **Higher initial cost** and complexity vs sacrificial system.
4. (Bonus) Stray current interference with adjacent moored vessels.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given data:**
- Anode mass $m_{anode} = 8$ kg = **8000 g**
- Protective current $I = 2.0$ A
- Atomic mass of Zn $M = 65.4$ g/mol
- Valence $n = 2$
- Faraday's constant $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- Utilization factor $U = 0.85$

**Step 1 — Usable mass of anode:**

$$m_{usable} = m_{anode} \times U = 8000 \times 0.85 = 6800\ g$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical lifetime using Faraday's law:**

Rearranging Faraday's 1st law: $m = \frac{ItM}{nF}$, solve for $t$:

$$t = \frac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot M}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$t = \frac{6800 \times 2 \times 96485}{2.0 \times 65.4}$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator:**

$$6800 \times 2 = 13{,}600$$
$$13{,}600 \times 96{,}485 = 1{,}312{,}196{,}000\ C$$

**Step 5 — Compute denominator:**

$$2.0 \times 65.4 = 130.8$$

**Step 6 — Divide:**

$$t = \frac{1{,}312{,}196{,}000}{130.8} = 10{,}031{,}757\ seconds$$

**Step 7 — Convert to years** (1 year = 365 × 24 × 3600 = **31,536,000 s**):

$$t_{years} = \frac{10{,}031{,}757}{31{,}536{,}000} = 0.318\ years$$

**Final Answer:** The anode will last approximately **0.318 years (~116 days)**. This is unrealistically short — indicating that a single 8 kg zinc anode is grossly undersized for a 2.0 A load, demonstrating why ship design uses either larger anodes (15–20 kg) or many parallel anodes.

---

### Question B: Alternative Comprehensive Question

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**
> 
> **(a)** Explain the **mechanism of sacrificial (galvanic) cathodic protection**. Compare **zinc, aluminium, and magnesium** anodes with respect to **electrochemical capacity, driving voltage, and typical marine application**. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A copper-nickel (90/10) plate of area **50 cm²** loses **85 mg** in **720 hours** during a sea-immersion test. Density of Cu-Ni = **8.94 g/cm³**. Calculate the **corrosion rate in mpy and mm/yr**. Comment on its suitability for seawater piping. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Mechanism of Sacrificial Cathodic Protection:**

The protected structure (hull, pipe, tank) is electrically connected to a more active metal (the **anode**) via welding or brazing. When immersed in the electrolyte (seawater), the more active anode preferentially dissolves, releasing electrons. These electrons flow to the structure (cathode), suppressing its anodic dissolution. The structure's potential is shifted to a region where the anodic reaction $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$ is thermodynamically unfavorable.

Key condition: the structure's potential must be maintained at or more negative than **−0.85 V vs CSE** for steel, or **−0.95 V vs CSE** for aluminium.

**Comparative Table:**

| Property | Zinc (Zn) | Aluminium (Al-alloy) | Magnesium (Mg-alloy) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Open-circuit potential (V vs CSE)** | −1.10 | −1.05 to −1.15 | −1.55 |
| **Driving voltage (vs steel)** | ~0.25 V | ~0.20–0.30 V | ~0.70 V |
| **Electrochemical capacity (Ah/kg)** | **780** | **2,500** (with In/Hg activators) | **1,230** |
| **Seawater utilisation factor** | 0.85–0.95 | 0.50–0.85 (passivation issues) | < 0.50 (chunks fall off) |
| **Service life (per anode)** | 5–10 yr | 15–25 yr | 1–3 yr |
| **Typical application** | Ship hulls, small craft | Naval hulls, long-term ICCP hybrid | Freshwater tanks, buried pipelines, low-resistivity |
| **Key limitation** | Heavy for capacity | Passivates in low-O₂ water | Too active in warm seawater; high self-corrosion |

**Application selection rule:**
- **Zinc** → general marine use, predictable, inexpensive.
- **Aluminium alloy** (with activators like In, Hg, Sn) → modern naval/merchant use, long life, light.
- **Magnesium** → not recommended in seawater; only freshwater/soil.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- Area $A = 50$ cm² → convert to in²: $A = 50 / 6.4516 = 7.75$ in²
- Weight loss $W = 85$ mg
- Time $T = 720$ hours
- Density $D = 8.94$ g/cm³

**Step 1 — Corrosion rate in mpy:**

$$CR_{mpy} = \frac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$CR_{mpy} = \frac{534 \times 85}{8.94 \times 7.75 \times 720}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**

$$534 \times 85 = 45{,}390$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator:**

$$8.94 \times 7.75 = 69.285$$
$$69.285 \times 720 = 49{,}885.2$$

**Step 5 — Divide:**

$$CR_{mpy} = \frac{45{,}390}{49{,}885.2} = 0.91\ mpy$$

**Step 6 — Convert to mm/yr** (1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/yr):

$$CR_{mm/yr} = 0.91 \times 0.0254 = 0.0231\ mm/yr$$

**Final Answer:** $CR = 0.91\ mpy = 0.023\ mm/yr$.

**Comment on suitability:**
A corrosion rate of **0.023 mm/yr** is well within the design limit for Cu-Ni 90/10 in seawater service. The accepted design limit per **DNV-RP-B401** is typically **0.025–0.05 mm/yr** for Cu-Ni piping, with absolute maximum of **0.10 mm/yr**. Therefore, the Cu-Ni 90/10 plate is **suitable for seawater piping systems** such as heat exchanger inlets, bilge lines, and firefighting mains. If used in **high-velocity (>3 m/s) flow**, erosion-corrosion may accelerate attack and the system may need velocity limits or sand-free filtration.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error** in corrosion rate: most students forget to convert cm² to in² before applying the mpy formula. Marks lost: **1–2 per question**.
> 2. **Forgetting the utilization factor** in anode life problems: theoretical life assumes 100% utilization, but actual is 50–95%. Marks lost: **2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing galvanic series with EMF series** in protection potential question: ship CP uses **galvanic series in actual environment**, not lab standard potentials. Marks lost: **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Missing reference electrode specification** in ICCP answer: must explicitly mention **Ag/AgCl** or **CSE** with the value **−0.85 V**. Marks lost: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Not sketching the CP cell** in 7-mark questions — KTU examiners allocate ~1 mark for the diagram alone.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Preservation = defence in depth**: coatings + cathodic protection + design + maintenance.
- ✅ **Corrosion is electrochemical** — anode, cathode, electrolyte, metallic path all required.
- ✅ **Galvanic Series in Seawater** (not EMF Series) governs ship CP design.
- ✅ **Sacrificial anodes** protect by being more active — Zn for general, Al for long-term, Mg for freshwater.
- ✅ **ICCP** uses external TRU + MMO anodes — adjustable, long life, but needs monitoring.
- ✅ **Protection potential**: **−0.85 V vs CSE** for steel, **−0.95 V vs CSE** for aluminium.
- ✅ **Faraday's Law** $m = \frac{ItM}{nF}$ is the foundation for anode sizing.
- ✅ **Corrosion rate formula** $CR_{mpy} = \frac{534W}{DAT}$ — remember the constant **534**.
- ✅ **Surface prep standard Sa 2½** is the default for marine coatings.
- ✅ **Current density** for painted hull ≈ **100 mA/m²**; for bare propeller ≈ **300–500 mA/m²**.
- ✅ **Utilization factor** must be applied; typical Al anode = 0.85, Zn = 0.90, Mg = 0.50.
- ✅ **Forms of corrosion to remember**: galvanic, pitting, crevice, erosion-corrosion, dezincification, SCC.
- ✅ **Anti-fouling** uses **Cu₂O** (cuprous oxide) historically, but **TBT-free** coatings are now mandatory under **IMO AFS Convention** (2008).
- ✅ **Naval ship standard coating system**: zinc-rich primer + epoxy + PU topcoat + anti-fouling.
- ✅ **Classification society rules** (DNV, ABS, Lloyd's, IRS) **enforce** preservation; non-compliance = no commercial certification.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aluminium and Its Alloys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Aluminium and Its Alloys

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aluminium (Al)** is a lightweight, non-ferrous, silvery-white metallic element with atomic number **13** and an atomic weight of **26.98 g/mol**. In shipbuilding metallurgy, aluminium is rarely used in its commercially pure form (1xxx series) and is instead deployed as **aluminium alloys** — homogeneous metallic mixtures of aluminium with one or more alloying elements (Cu, Mg, Si, Mn, Zn) engineered to deliver specific combinations of **strength, ductility, corrosion resistance, weldability, and marine durability** required for marine structural service.

In the context of **PENST523 — Non Ferrous Materials in Shipbuilding**, the term "Aluminium and Its Alloys" specifically refers to the **5xxx (Al–Mg)** and **6xxx (Al–Mg–Si)** series alloys that are the **workhorse materials of modern commercial marine construction**, including patrol vessels, fast ferries, naval combatants, offshore patrol boats, LNG carrier secondary structures, and superstructures of large ocean-going vessels.

## 1.2 Key Physical & Mechanical Properties

> [!NOTE]
> The following property values are the **standard reference metrics** you must memorize for KTU board examinations. These are non-negotiable numerical facts.

| Property | Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Density ($\rho$)** | **2.70 g/cm³** | ~1/3 the density of steel (7.85 g/cm³) |
| **Young's Modulus ($E$)** | **70 GPa** | ~1/3 that of steel (210 GPa) |
| **Melting Point** | **660.3 °C** | Low compared to steel (~1500 °C) |
| **Ultimate Tensile Strength (Pure)** | **90 MPa** | Why alloying is mandatory |
| **Yield Strength (Pure)** | **35 MPa** | Why alloying is mandatory |
| **Ultimate Tensile Strength (Alloy 5083-H116)** | **305 MPa** | Marine grade benchmark |
| **Yield Strength (Alloy 5083-H116)** | **215 MPa** | Marine grade benchmark |
| **Fatigue Strength** | ~50% of UTS | Critical for wave-loaded hulls |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | Excellent (due to Al₂O₃ passive film) | Self-healing oxide layer |
| **Electrical Conductivity** | 61% IACS (pure) / 30–35% (alloys) | Important for cathodic protection design |
| **Thermal Expansion Coefficient** | **23 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** | ~2× that of steel (12 × 10⁻⁶ /°C) |

## 1.3 The "Hollow-Box Analogy" — Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the shipbuilding industry care so much about aluminium?**

Imagine you are building a boat hull. You have two options:

1. **Steel hull (a heavy, solid brick):** It is strong, but you need a thick wall. The ship becomes heavy, sits low in the water, needs more engine power, burns more fuel, and is slower.

2. **Aluminium hull (a strong but hollow cardboard tube):** Aluminium is 1/3 the weight of steel for the same volume. Because of the lower density, you can either (a) carry more cargo at the same displacement, or (b) make the structure thicker (larger section modulus) to compensate for the lower stiffness, while STILL being lighter overall.

**The Engineering Pay-off Equation** that the naval architect always evaluates:

$$\text{Weight Saved} = (\rho_{\text{steel}} - \rho_{\text{Al}}) \times \text{Volume} = (7.85 - 2.70) \times V = 5.15 \times V \text{ kg per m}^3$$

For every cubic meter of steel replaced by aluminium in a hull, you save approximately **5.15 tonnes per 1000 m³** of structural volume. On a 100 m fast ferry, this translates into **30–50% displacement reduction** compared to an equivalent steel vessel.

## 1.4 Why Aluminium Dominates Modern Shipbuilding

- **Weight Reduction** → higher payload, higher speed, lower fuel consumption.
- **Corrosion Resistance in Seawater** → the natural Al₂O₃ passive film is stable in the marine pH range of **4–8.5**.
- **Formability** → easy to roll, extrude, and form into complex ship structural profiles.
- **Non-Magnetic** → critical for minesweepers and degaussing-friendly naval ships.
- **Recyclability** → aluminium scrap retains 95% of its parent metal's properties; recycling uses only 5% of the energy required for primary production.
- **Weldability** (especially 5xxx and 6xxx) → compatible with **GTAW (TIG)** and **GMAW (MIG)** processes used in shipyards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** Pure aluminium is mechanically weak (~90 MPa UTS). Almost ALL marine applications use **alloys** where Mg, Si, Mn, Cu, or Zn are added. The classification of these alloys is the most frequently asked KTU topic in Module 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Aluminium Alloys (THE Most Important Concept)

Aluminium alloys are classified along **two independent axes** — both are board-exam favourites.

### Axis 1: By Fabrication Method

- **Wrought Alloys (W):** Worked mechanically by rolling, extrusion, forging. Used for plates, sheets, extrusions (T-bars, L-angles, I-sections). **Dominant in shipbuilding.**
- **Cast Alloys (C):** Poured into moulds. Used for complex-shaped fittings, valve bodies, winch housings.

### Axis 2: By Heat-Treatability

- **Non-Heat-Treatable (Strengthened by Cold Work only):** 1xxx, 3xxx, 4xxx, 5xxx series. Strength comes from strain hardening (H-tempers).
- **Heat-Treatable (Strengthened by Precipitation Hardening):** 2xxx (Al-Cu), 6xxx (Al-Mg-Si), 7xxx (Al-Zn-Mg-Cu), some 8xxx. Strength comes from T-tempers (T4, T5, T6, T7).

## 2.2 The Eight-Series Designation System (UNS / AA System)

The **Aluminum Association (AA)** four-digit system is the international standard. **You must memorize this for KTU exams.**

> [!NOTE]
> **The first digit (XXXX) tells you the principal alloying element. This is the single most-tested fact in Module 2.**

| Series | Principal Alloying Element | Heat-Treatable? | Marine Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1xxx** | None (≥99% pure Al) | No | Chemical tank linings, electrical busbars |
| **2xxx** | Copper (Cu) | **Yes** | Aerospace (NOT marine — prone to SCC) |
| **3xxx** | Manganese (Mn) | No | Roofing, non-structural panels |
| **4xxx** | Silicon (Si) | No | Welding filler wires (e.g., 4043) |
| **5xxx** | Magnesium (Mg) | No (cold work) | **★ KING OF MARINE — hulls, decks** |
| **6xxx** | Magnesium + Silicon (Mg₂Si) | **Yes** | **★ Superstructures, masts, extrusions** |
| **7xxx** | Zinc (Zn) | **Yes** | Aerospace, military bridges |
| **8xxx** | Other (Li, Fe, etc.) | Varies | Aerospace lithium alloys |

The **second digit** indicates alloy modification (0 = original, 1 = first modification, etc.). The **third and fourth digits** are arbitrary serial numbers (except 1xxx where they indicate purity above 99%).

## 2.3 The Four Marine-Workhorse Alloys (Memorize These Properties)

### 2.3.1 Alloy 5083 — "The Shipbuilder's Favourite"
- **Nominal Composition:** Al – 4.5% Mg – 0.7% Mn – 0.15% Cr
- **Temper:** H116 / H321 (special marine tempers that resist exfoliation)
- **UTS / YS:** 305 / 215 MPa
- **Key Trait:** Highest strength among non-heat-treatable marine alloys. Excellent seawater corrosion resistance. Excellent weldability (no HAZ strength loss).
- **Used for:** Hull plates, decks, superstructures of fast ferries and naval ships.

### 2.3.2 Alloy 5086
- **Nominal Composition:** Al – 4.0% Mg – 0.45% Mn
- **UTS / YS:** 260 / 195 MPa
- **Key Trait:** Slightly lower strength than 5083 but better formability.
- **Used for:** Smaller craft, pressure vessels.

### 2.3.3 Alloy 5454
- **Nominal Composition:** Al – 2.7% Mg – 0.8% Mn
- **UTS / YS:** 250 / 180 MPa
- **Key Trait:** Better elevated-temperature performance than 5083 (used up to 65°C). Good for marine pressure vessels and boiler shells.
- **Used for:** Marine pressure vessels, road tankers.

### 2.3.4 Alloy 6061 (Marine Superstructure Grade)
- **Nominal Composition:** Al – 1.0% Mg – 0.6% Si – 0.25% Cu
- **Temper:** T6 (solution heat-treated + artificially aged)
- **UTS / YS:** 310 / 275 MPa
- **Key Trait:** Excellent extrudability → ideal for complex superstructure profiles, masts, helicopter decks.
- **Limitation:** Lower corrosion resistance than 5xxx; needs paint/coating or to be in a position where it is not immersed.

### 2.3.5 Alloy 6082 (European Marine Standard)
- **Nominal Composition:** Al – 1.0% Mg – 0.9% Si – 0.7% Mn
- **Temper:** T6
- **UTS / YS:** 340 / 310 MPa
- **Key Trait:** Higher strength than 6061, widely used in European shipbuilding (DNV-GL certified).

## 2.4 Heat Treatment Temper Designations (Critical for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temper codes appear in the alloy designation** (e.g., 5083-H116, 6061-T6). The full temper code is a high-frequency KTU exam question.

| Code | Meaning | Applies To |
|---|---|---|
| **F** | As-fabricated (no treatment) | All |
| **O** | Annealed (soft condition) | All |
| **H1x** | Strain-hardened only | Non-heat-treatable |
| **H2x** | Strain-hardened + partially annealed | Non-heat-treatable |
| **H3x** | Strain-hardened + stabilized (low Mg diffusion) | **5xxx marine alloys** |
| **H116** | Special marine temper (resists exfoliation) | **5xxx hull plates** |
| **H321** | Stabilized + cold-worked | **5xxx hull plates** |
| **T4** | Solution heat-treated + naturally aged | 2xxx, 6xxx, 7xxx |
| **T6** | Solution heat-treated + **artificially aged** | 2xxx, 6xxx, 7xxx |
| **T7** | Solution heat-treated + over-aged (stabilized) | 7xxx (to prevent SCC) |

## 2.5 The Precipitation Hardening Mechanism (T6 Temper)

This is the most conceptually rich theory in Module 2 and the most-examined mechanism.

**The T6 sequence has THREE steps:**

1. **Solution Treatment (~530 °C for 6xxx, ~495 °C for 2xxx):** Heat the alloy to a temperature where all alloying elements (e.g., Mg and Si) dissolve completely into a single-phase α-aluminium solid solution. Quench rapidly in water → supersaturated solid solution (SSSS) is "frozen in" at room temperature. The alloy is now soft and ductile.

2. **Quenching:** Rapid cooling (water or polymer) traps the solute atoms in a metastable state.

3. **Artificial Ageing (~175 °C for 6–12 hours for 6xxx):** The supersaturated solution is heated to an intermediate temperature. Solute atoms diffuse and form **coherent nano-scale precipitates** (e.g., β″ Mg₂Si in 6xxx, θ″ Al₂Cu in 2xxx). These precipitates **pin dislocations** and dramatically increase strength.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** If you over-age (e.g., 250°C for too long), precipitates grow larger and become **incoherent** — strength DROPS. This is the over-aged condition (T7). Do not confuse it with T6.

**Why the strength increases — the dislocation-pinning mechanism:**

The critical resolved shear stress $\tau_c$ required to move a dislocation past a coherent precipitate of radius $r$ is given by:

$$\tau_c = \frac{k \cdot G \cdot b}{L - 2r}$$

where $G$ is the shear modulus, $b$ is the Burgers vector, $L$ is the inter-precipitate spacing, $k$ is a constant, and $r$ is the precipitate radius. As precipitates form and grow (during ageing), $L$ decreases and $r$ increases — both effects **increase $\tau_c$**, hence yield strength rises.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is your exam-day reference. Every formula below has been asked in previous KTU question papers.

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_y = \frac{F}{A}$ | $\sigma_y$ = yield stress, $F$ = load, $A$ = area | Basic strength check |
| 2 | $\text{Specific Strength} = \frac{\sigma_{UTS}}{\rho}$ | $\sigma_{UTS}$ = ultimate tensile strength, $\rho$ = density | Weight-critical design (hulls) |
| 3 | $\text{Specific Modulus} = \frac{E}{\rho}$ | $E$ = Young's modulus, $\rho$ = density | Stiffness-to-weight ratio |
| 4 | $\Delta W = (\rho_{\text{steel}} - \rho_{\text{Al}}) \times V$ | $\Delta W$ = weight saving, $V$ = structural volume | Hull weight savings |
| 5 | $\rho_{\text{alloy}} = \frac{1}{\sum \frac{w_i}{\rho_i}}$ | $w_i$ = mass fraction, $\rho_i$ = density of element $i$ | Rule of mixtures for alloy density |
| 6 | $\tau_c = \frac{k G b}{L - 2r}$ | $G$ = shear modulus, $b$ = Burgers vector, $L$ = precipitate spacing, $r$ = precipitate radius | Precipitation hardening theory |
| 7 | $\text{Speed Ratio} = \sqrt{\frac{W_{\text{steel}}}{W_{\text{Al}}}}$ | $W$ = displacement weight | Speed gain from light hull |
| 8 | $E_{\text{abs}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho v^2 C_d A$ | $E_{\text{abs}}$ = absorbed energy, $v$ = velocity, $C_d$ = drag coefficient | Crashworthiness / slamming |
| 9 | $R = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot t}{D}$ | $R$ = Reynolds number for flow | Hull surface flow (correlates with corrosion) |
| 10 | $E_{\text{aluminium}} = 0.05 \times E_{\text{primary}}$ | Energy for recycling vs primary production | Sustainability argument |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The Reynolds number formula uses `R` and the recycling energy uses `E` — both single letters. In the markdown table above, vertical bars were replaced with ratios, fractions, and word descriptions to prevent table-breaking. **When writing in your own answer sheets, always wrap inline absolute-value expressions inside `\vert...\vert` or `\mid...\mid` LaTeX delimiters** — never raw `|...|` — or your KTU exam answer sheets will render incorrectly.

## 2.7 Why 5xxx Alloys are the Marine "Sweet Spot"

The reason 5xxx dominates hulls is the **combination** of four properties:

1. **Mg (4–5%) provides solid-solution strengthening** — Mg atoms (~12% larger than Al) distort the FCC lattice and impede dislocation motion.
2. **No quench sensitivity** — strength is obtained by cold-rolling, not heat treatment. Welded joints retain parent-metal strength.
3. **Mg is not susceptible to stress-corrosion cracking (SCC)** the way Cu is in 2xxx alloys.
4. **The H116/H321 tempers add a thermal stabilization step** that precipitates Mg-rich β-phase (Al₃Mg₂) along grain boundaries as a fine, dispersed network — this prevents the **intergranular corrosion / exfoliation** that affects 5xxx alloys with >3% Mg in the H112 temper.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Warning:** Students often state that "5xxx alloys cannot be heat-treated" — this is **technically misleading**. They cannot be **strengthened** by heat treatment, but they **are** heat-treated for **stabilization** (H116, H321 tempers). This distinction is a 2-mark KTU question almost every year.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Weight Saving in a Typical Fast Ferry Hull

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-pattern):** A fast ferry hull requires 250 m³ of structural plate volume. Calculate the weight saving if the design switches from mild steel ($\rho_s = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$) to aluminium alloy 5083 ($\rho_{Al} = 2.66 \text{ g/cm}^3$). Express the saving in tonnes and as a percentage.

### Step 1 — Convert density to consistent SI units
Both densities are given in g/cm³. We need them in **kg/m³** (or equivalently t/m³) for a volume in m³.

$$\rho_s = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 7.85 \times 10^3 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\rho_{Al} = 2.66 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 2.66 \times 10^3 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 2660 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Logic:** 1 g/cm³ = 1000 kg/m³ because 1 g = 10⁻³ kg and 1 cm³ = 10⁻⁶ m³, so 1 g/cm³ = 10⁻³ / 10⁻⁶ = 10³ kg/m³.

### Step 2 — Compute the steel hull mass

$$m_s = \rho_s \times V = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 250 \text{ m}^3$$

$$m_s = 1{,}962{,}500 \text{ kg} = 1962.5 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Logic:** Mass = density × volume. The 250 m³ of plate, if it were steel, weighs ~1962 tonnes.

### Step 3 — Compute the aluminium hull mass

$$m_{Al} = \rho_{Al} \times V = 2660 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 250 \text{ m}^3$$

$$m_{Al} = 665{,}000 \text{ kg} = 665.0 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Logic:** Same volume, but aluminium is roughly one-third the density.

### Step 4 — Compute absolute weight saving

$$\Delta m = m_s - m_{Al} = 1962.5 - 665.0 = 1297.5 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Logic:** Direct subtraction of the two mass results.

### Step 5 — Compute percentage saving

$$\% \text{ Saving} = \frac{\Delta m}{m_s} \times 100 = \frac{1297.5}{1962.5} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Saving} = 66.12\%$$

**Logic:** Percentage saving is always relative to the **baseline (original) value**, not the new value.

### Step 6 — Engineering interpretation

A **66% weight saving** is enormous. In practice, the hull plate thickness is increased to compensate for aluminium's lower Young's modulus (70 GPa vs 210 GPa), so the **real** saving is **50–55%**. The remaining 11–16% is consumed by the thicker sections needed to keep hull stiffness ($EI$) comparable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Strength Comparison (Al 5083 vs Mild Steel)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Compare the specific strength (strength-to-weight ratio) of 5083-H116 aluminium ($\sigma_{UTS} = 305 \text{ MPa}$, $\rho = 2.66 \text{ g/cm}^3$) against mild shipbuilding steel ($\sigma_{UTS} = 400 \text{ MPa}$, $\rho = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$). Which is superior for a lightweight hull?

### Step 1 — Write the specific strength formula

$$\text{Specific Strength} = \frac{\sigma_{UTS}}{\rho}$$

**Logic:** This ratio is a proxy for how much strength you get per unit mass — exactly what a weight-critical ship structure needs.

### Step 2 — Compute specific strength for steel

$$\text{SS}_{\text{steel}} = \frac{400 \text{ MPa}}{7850 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 0.05096 \text{ MPa}\cdot\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$

Converting to kN·m/kg (1 MPa = 1000 kPa = 1000 kN/m²):

$$\text{SS}_{\text{steel}} = 50.96 \text{ kN}\cdot\text{m}/\text{kg}$$

**Logic:** 1 MPa = 1 N/mm² = 10⁶ N/m² = 10⁶ Pa. Multiplying by 1 m³/kg cancels volume, giving units of N·m/kg = Pa·m³/kg. We then scale to kN·m/kg for readability.

### Step 3 — Compute specific strength for 5083-H116

$$\text{SS}_{5083} = \frac{305 \text{ MPa}}{2660 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 0.11466 \text{ MPa}\cdot\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$

$$\text{SS}_{5083} = 114.66 \text{ kN}\cdot\text{m}/\text{kg}$$

**Logic:** Identical procedure, lower numerator but much lower denominator — the denominator drops faster, so the ratio improves.

### Step 4 — Compute the ratio of specific strengths

$$\frac{\text{SS}_{5083}}{\text{SS}_{\text{steel}}} = \frac{114.66}{50.96} = 2.25$$

**Logic:** The ratio is dimensionless, so no unit conversion is needed beyond keeping both in the same system.

### Step 5 — Engineering interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** 5083-H116 aluminium has **2.25 times the specific strength of mild steel**. This is THE fundamental reason aluminium is preferred for fast ferries, hydrofoils, and SWATH vessels, where every tonne of hull weight saved directly increases speed and payload.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Alloy Density via the Rule of Mixtures

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Alloy 5083 has a nominal composition of 4.5% Mg, 0.7% Mn, 0.15% Cr, balance Al. Compute the theoretical density using the rule of mixtures. Densities: Al = 2.70, Mg = 1.74, Mn = 7.21, Cr = 7.19 g/cm³. Compare to the standard value of 2.66 g/cm³.

### Step 1 — State the rule of mixtures for density

For a multi-component alloy with mass fractions $w_i$ and component densities $\rho_i$:

$$\frac{1}{\rho_{\text{alloy}}} = \sum_i \frac{w_i}{\rho_i}$$

$$\rho_{\text{alloy}} = \frac{1}{\sum_i \frac{w_i}{\rho_i}}$$

**Logic:** This is the **harmonic mean** of densities, weighted by mass fraction. It is used (not the arithmetic mean) because mass fractions must be normalized through the volume contributions, not directly.

### Step 2 — Tabulate the input data

| Element | Mass fraction $w_i$ | Density $\rho_i$ (g/cm³) | $w_i / \rho_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Al | 0.9465 | 2.70 | 0.35056 |
| Mg | 0.0450 | 1.74 | 0.02586 |
| Mn | 0.0070 | 7.21 | 0.00097 |
| Cr | 0.0015 | 7.19 | 0.00021 |
| **Sum** | 1.0000 | — | **0.37760** |

**Logic:** Mass fractions must sum to 1.000. Each row's $w_i / \rho_i$ is the partial specific volume contribution.

### Step 3 — Sum the partial specific volumes

$$\sum_i \frac{w_i}{\rho_i} = 0.35056 + 0.02586 + 0.00097 + 0.00021 = 0.37760 \text{ cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Logic:** Arithmetic sum — each term already accounts for its own units.

### Step 4 — Invert to obtain alloy density

$$\rho_{\text{alloy}} = \frac{1}{0.37760} = 2.6482 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Logic:** Simple inversion of the harmonic-mean formula.

### Step 5 — Compare with the standard value

$$\text{Error} = \frac{2.6482 - 2.66}{2.66} \times 100 = -0.44\%$$

**Logic:** The minus sign indicates our rule-of-mixtures value is slightly below the standard. The 0.44% deviation is **excellent agreement** — the rule of mixtures works because the alloy phases are in solid solution (no compound formation that would change packing).

### Step 6 — Engineering interpretation

The 0.44% deviation arises because:
- Real alloys contain trace Fe, Si, and other impurities not in our list.
- Mg and Al form substitutional solid solutions that may have small lattice distortions, slightly altering the actual mean atomic volume.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Why Higher Mg Content Lowers Alloy Density

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Show mathematically that adding magnesium (a lower-density metal) to aluminium reduces the alloy's density. Use Al + 5% Mg as the example.

### Step 1 — Define the rule-of-mixtures density

$$\rho_{\text{alloy}} = \frac{1}{\frac{w_{Al}}{\rho_{Al}} + \frac{w_{Mg}}{\rho_{Mg}}}$$

**Logic:** Two-component form of the harmonic-mean rule.

### Step 2 — Substitute values

For 5% Mg (mass fraction) and 95% Al:

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{0.95}{2.70} + \frac{0.05}{1.74}$$

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = 0.35185 + 0.02874 = 0.38059 \text{ cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Logic:** Straightforward division and addition. The Mg term is small (0.029) but non-negligible.

### Step 3 — Invert

$$\rho_{\text{alloy}} = \frac{1}{0.38059} = 2.628 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Logic:** Inversion yields the final density.

### Step 4 — Verify the trend

Pure Al = 2.70 g/cm³. With 5% Mg → 2.628 g/cm³. **Density decreased by 2.7%** — exactly as expected, since $\rho_{Mg} = 1.74 < \rho_{Al} = 2.70$.

**Logic:** This is why 5xxx marine alloys (4–5% Mg) are even lighter than pure aluminium — a small but useful density advantage that compounds with the strength gain from solid-solution hardening.

---

## 3.5 KTU-Style Numerical Problem with Complete Solution

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Full 14-mark style):** A ship superstructure is to be redesigned from 6061-T6 aluminium ($\sigma_y = 275$ MPa, $\rho = 2.70$ g/cm³, $E = 70$ GPa) replacing the original mild steel ($\sigma_y = 235$ MPa, $\rho = 7.85$ g/cm³, $E = 210$ GPa). The original steel plate was 10 mm thick. To keep the same flexural rigidity $EI$ per unit width, the new aluminium plate thickness $t_{Al}$ must satisfy $E_{Al} I_{Al} = E_s I_s$. (a) Calculate the required aluminium thickness. (b) Compute the weight per square metre of both plates. (c) Comment on the engineering trade-off.

### Part (a) — Required Thickness for Equal Flexural Rigidity

For a plate of unit width, the second moment of area per unit width is:

$$I = \frac{t^3}{12}$$

**Logic:** For a rectangular cross-section of width $b$ and thickness $t$, $I = bt^3/12$. Per unit width, $b = 1$ so $I = t^3/12$.

Setting $E_{Al} \cdot t_{Al}^3 = E_s \cdot t_s^3$:

$$t_{Al}^3 = \frac{E_s}{E_{Al}} \cdot t_s^3 = \frac{210}{70} \cdot (10)^3 = 3 \times 1000 = 3000 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$t_{Al} = \sqrt[3]{3000} = 14.42 \text{ mm}$$

**Logic:** We cube the thickness ratio because stiffness is proportional to $t^3$, not $t$. The aluminium plate must be **44% thicker** than the steel plate to match stiffness.

### Part (b) — Weight Comparison

**Steel plate weight per m²:**

$$w_s = \rho_s \cdot t_s = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 0.010 \text{ m} = 78.5 \text{ kg/m}^2$$

**Logic:** Weight per area = density × thickness. Convert 10 mm to 0.010 m.

**Aluminium plate weight per m²:**

$$w_{Al} = \rho_{Al} \cdot t_{Al} = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 0.01442 \text{ m} = 38.93 \text{ kg/m}^2$$

**Logic:** Same formula, with 14.42 mm = 0.01442 m.

**Weight saving per m²:**

$$\Delta w = 78.5 - 38.93 = 39.57 \text{ kg/m}^2 \quad (\approx 50.4\% \text{ saving})$$

**Logic:** Subtraction, then express as a percentage of the original (steel) weight: $39.57/78.5 \times 100 \approx 50.4\%$.

### Part (c) — Engineering Trade-off Commentary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The redesigned superstructure is **50% lighter** despite being **44% thicker**. This means more internal volume is consumed, but the ship gains dramatically in payload, speed, and fuel economy. The increased thickness also improves dent resistance, ballistic tolerance (important for naval vessels), and buckling resistance. The trade-off is acceptable whenever the cargo or speed benefit exceeds the volume penalty — which is almost always the case for high-speed craft.

## 4.2 The T6 Heat Treatment Cycle (Block-Level Process Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    START[AS CAST OR AS FABRICATED ALLOY]:::input

    S1[STEP 1 SOLUTION TREATMENT 530 C 2 HRS]:::process
    S2[STEP 2 RAPID WATER QUENCH]:::process
    S3[STEP 3 ARTIFICIAL AGEING 175 C 8 TO 12 HRS]:::process

    OUT1[SOFT DUCTILE SSSS]:::state
    OUT2[PEAK STRENGTH T6]:::state
    OUT3[OVER AGED T7 LOWER STRENGTH]:::state

    START --> S1 --> OUT1
    OUT1 --> S2 --> S3
    S3 -- EXACT TIME --> OUT2
    S3 -- TOO LONG --> OUT3

    classDef input fill:#5a4a7b,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef process fill:#7b4b3f,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef state fill:#3f6b4a,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The "as-fabricated" alloy is the input.
- Solution treatment dissolves all Mg₂Si (in 6xxx) into a single-phase α-Al matrix.
- Quenching freezes this supersaturated state — the alloy is now soft (the SSSS state).
- Controlled ageing precipitates **fine coherent β″ Mg₂Si** particles — peak strength (T6).
- Over-ageing grows the precipitates into coarse incoherent β Mg₂Si — strength drops (T7).

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the 5xxx series aluminium alloys and state their significance in shipbuilding. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

> The **5xxx series** aluminium alloys are wrought alloys in which **magnesium (Mg)** is the principal alloying element, typically in the range of **2.5% to 5%** by mass. They are **non-heat-treatable** but can be strengthened by **cold working (strain hardening)**, with the resulting strength coded using **H-tempers**. Special marine tempers such as **H116 and H321** include a stabilization heat treatment that prevents the precipitation of brittle β-phase (Al₃Mg₂) at grain boundaries, thereby eliminating susceptibility to intergranular corrosion and exfoliation.
>
> **Significance in shipbuilding:** 5xxx alloys — particularly **5083, 5086, and 5454** — are the **primary hull construction materials** for fast ferries, naval patrol vessels, hydrofoils, and SWATH ships. They offer an exceptional combination of high strength (305 MPa UTS for 5083-H116), excellent weldability without HAZ strength loss, superior corrosion resistance in seawater (due to the stable Al₂O₃ passive film), and a low density of 2.66 g/cm³ — yielding specific strength ~2.25 times that of mild steel. They are certified by all major classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, BV) for marine structural use.

---

### Question 2: What is meant by the "T6" temper in aluminium alloys? Describe the steps involved. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

> The **T6 temper** designation indicates that the alloy has been **solution heat-treated and then artificially aged** to achieve maximum strength through precipitation hardening.
>
> **The three steps are:**
>
> 1. **Solution Treatment:** The alloy is heated to a high temperature (typically ~530 °C for 6xxx alloys, ~495 °C for 2xxx alloys) for a sufficient time to dissolve all the alloying elements (e.g., Mg and Si in 6xxx) into a single-phase α-aluminium solid solution.
>
> 2. **Quenching:** The alloy is rapidly cooled (usually in water or a polymer solution) to room temperature. This "freezes" the solute atoms in a **supersaturated solid solution (SSSS)**. The alloy is now in a soft, ductile, and workable state.
>
> 3. **Artificial Ageing:** The quenched alloy is reheated to an intermediate temperature (typically ~175 °C for 6–12 hours for 6xxx alloys). During this stage, fine, coherent nano-precipitates (β″-Mg₂Si in 6xxx, θ″-Al₂Cu in 2xxx) form throughout the matrix. These precipitates **pin dislocations** and dramatically increase yield strength — this is the peak-strength T6 condition.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Focus on Classification, Properties, and Application

> **[a] Classify aluminium alloys used in shipbuilding based on the UNS/AA four-digit designation system. With suitable examples, explain the role of 5xxx and 6xxx series alloys in marine structural applications. [7 Marks]**
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction:**
The Aluminum Association (AA) four-digit system classifies wrought aluminium alloys based on their **principal alloying element**, which is indicated by the first digit. The second digit indicates modifications of the original alloy, while the third and fourth digits are arbitrary serial numbers (except 1xxx, where they denote purity above 99%).

**Classification Table (8 Series):**

| Series | Alloying Element | Heat-Treatable | Marine Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1xxx | None (≥ 99% Al) | No | Tank linings, electrical |
| 2xxx | Cu | Yes | Aerospace (not marine — SCC) |
| 3xxx | Mn | No | Roofing panels |
| 4xxx | Si | No | Welding filler (4043) |
| **5xxx** | **Mg** | **No (cold work)** | **★ HULLS, DECKS — marine king** |
| **6xxx** | **Mg + Si (Mg₂Si)** | **Yes (T6)** | **★ SUPERSTRUCTURES, MASTS** |
| 7xxx | Zn + Mg + Cu | Yes | Aerospace, military bridges |
| 8xxx | Other (Li, Fe) | Varies | Aerospace |

**Role of 5xxx Series in Marine Applications:**

The 5xxx series alloys (Al–Mg) are the **primary structural material for ship hulls and decks**. The principal alloying element, magnesium, provides **solid-solution strengthening** because the Mg atom is ~12% larger than Al, producing lattice strain that impedes dislocation motion. The most widely used marine alloy is **5083-H116**, with 4.5% Mg, 0.7% Mn, and 0.15% Cr. This alloy offers UTS = 305 MPa, YS = 215 MPa, density = 2.66 g/cm³, and excellent weldability — the welded joint retains parent-metal strength because no quench-sensitive phases form in the HAZ.

The **H116 temper** includes a stabilization treatment that precipitates Mg-rich β-phase (Al₃Mg₂) as a fine, dispersed network along grain boundaries, which prevents the intergranular corrosion and exfoliation that can affect 5xxx alloys with > 3% Mg in other tempers. 5083 is certified by DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, and Bureau Veritas for primary hull construction. Other marine 5xxx grades include **5086** (slightly lower strength, better formability) and **5454** (better elevated-temperature performance for marine pressure vessels up to 65 °C).

**Role of 6xxx Series in Marine Applications:**

The 6xxx series alloys (Al–Mg–Si) are the **primary materials for superstructures, masts, deck beams, and extruded structural profiles**. The Mg and Si combine to form the strengthening precipitate **Mg₂Si (β-phase)**. The two most important marine grades are **6061-T6** and **6082-T6**.

**6061-T6** has nominal composition Al – 1.0% Mg – 0.6% Si – 0.25% Cu. After T6 treatment it achieves UTS = 310 MPa, YS = 275 MPa. Its **key advantage is excellent extrudability** — it can be hot-extruded into complex hollow profiles (T-bars, L-angles, tubular masts, I-beams) that would be impossible with 5xxx alloys.

**6082-T6** (Al – 1.0% Mg – 0.9% Si – 0.7% Mn) is the **European standard** for marine structural extrusions, certified by DNV-GL, and offers slightly higher strength (UTS = 340 MPa, YS = 310 MPa) due to higher Si content. 6xxx alloys are used above the waterline where they are not immersed, because their corrosion resistance is slightly lower than 5xxx — they are usually **painted or coated** for marine atmospheric exposure.

> **(b) A 12 mm thick steel plate ($\rho = 7850$ kg/m³, $E = 210$ GPa) used in a deck is to be replaced by 5083-H116 aluminium plate ($\rho = 2660$ kg/m³, $E = 70$ GPa). For equal flexural rigidity, determine (i) the new aluminium thickness, and (ii) the percentage weight saving per m². [7 Marks]**
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Steel: $t_s = 12$ mm, $\rho_s = 7850$ kg/m³, $E_s = 210$ GPa
- Aluminium: $\rho_{Al} = 2660$ kg/m³, $E_{Al} = 70$ GPa

**Step 1 — Equal flexural rigidity condition**

For a plate of unit width ($b = 1$ m), the second moment of area is:

$$I = \frac{b \cdot t^3}{12} = \frac{t^3}{12}$$

**Logic:** For a rectangular section, $I = bt^3/12$. Per unit width, $b = 1$ so the equation reduces.

For equal rigidity:

$$E_{Al} \cdot I_{Al} = E_s \cdot I_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_{Al} \cdot t_{Al}^3 = E_s \cdot t_s^3$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $t_{Al}$**

$$t_{Al}^3 = \frac{E_s}{E_{Al}} \cdot t_s^3 = \frac{210}{70} \times (12)^3 = 3 \times 1728 = 5184 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$t_{Al} = \sqrt[3]{5184} = 17.30 \text{ mm}$$

**Logic:** Cube root of 5184. Cube: $17^3 = 4913$, $17.3^3 = 5177$, $17.31^3 = 5187$. So $t_{Al} \approx 17.30$ mm.

**Step 3 — Weight per square metre for steel**

$$w_s = \rho_s \cdot t_s = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 0.012 \text{ m} = 94.2 \text{ kg/m}^2$$

**Logic:** Mass per area = density × thickness. Convert 12 mm to 0.012 m.

**Step 4 — Weight per square metre for aluminium**

$$w_{Al} = \rho_{Al} \cdot t_{Al} = 2660 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 0.01730 \text{ m} = 46.02 \text{ kg/m}^2$$

**Logic:** Same formula, 17.30 mm = 0.01730 m.

**Step 5 — Percentage weight saving**

$$\% \text{ Saving} = \frac{w_s - w_{Al}}{w_s} \times 100 = \frac{94.2 - 46.02}{94.2} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Saving} = \frac{48.18}{94.2} \times 100 = 51.15\%$$

**Logic:** Percentage savings is always relative to the **original** (steel) value, not the new value.

**Conclusion:** The 5083-H116 aluminium deck is **44% thicker** (17.30 mm vs 12 mm) but **51% lighter per m²** — an excellent trade-off for high-speed vessels.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Focus on Heat Treatment, Marine Failure Modes, and Galvanic Corrosion

> **[a] Explain the precipitation hardening mechanism in 6xxx series aluminium alloys. Why is this mechanism NOT used in 5xxx marine hull alloys? [7 Marks]**
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

**Precipitation Hardening in 6xxx Alloys:**

Precipitation hardening (also called age hardening) is a three-step heat treatment that produces fine, dispersed nano-precipitates within the aluminium matrix to impede dislocation motion and increase strength.

**Step 1 — Solution Treatment (~530 °C for 6xxx):**
The alloy is heated above the **solvus temperature** of the Mg₂Si phase (the highest temperature at which Mg₂Si can co-exist with the α-Al solid solution). At ~530 °C, all the Mg and Si atoms dissolve completely into the FCC aluminium lattice, forming a **single-phase α-solid solution**. The alloy is held at this temperature for ~1–2 hours to ensure full homogenization.

**Step 2 — Quenching (water or polymer):**
The alloy is rapidly cooled to room temperature. Because diffusion is slow at low temperatures, the Mg and Si atoms are **"frozen" in solution** — a **supersaturated solid solution (SSSS)** is obtained. The lattice is strained (Mg atoms are larger than Al atoms, Si atoms are smaller) but the alloy is **soft and ductile** because the strengthening precipitates have not yet formed.

**Step 3 — Artificial Ageing (~175 °C, 8–12 hours):**
The quenched alloy is reheated to an **intermediate temperature** that is high enough to allow limited diffusion of Mg and Si atoms, but low enough to prevent over-ageing. During this step:
- Mg and Si atoms cluster into **Guinier–Preston (GP) zones** — these are coherent, plate-like clusters only a few atoms thick.
- GP zones transform into the metastable **β″-phase (Mg₂Si)** — fully coherent, needle-shaped precipitates. This corresponds to **peak strength (T6 condition)**.
- The β″ needles **pin dislocations**, requiring the critical resolved shear stress $\tau_c$ to rise per the equation:

$$\tau_c = \frac{k \cdot G \cdot b}{L - 2r}$$

where $r$ is the precipitate radius and $L$ is the inter-precipitate spacing. As $r$ increases and $L$ decreases, $\tau_c$ rises → strength increases.

- If ageing continues too long, β″ transforms to coarse, **incoherent β-phase (Mg₂Si)** → strength drops. This is the **over-aged T7 condition**.

**Why 5xxx Marine Hull Alloys Do NOT Use Precipitation Hardening:**

5xxx alloys (Al–Mg) **cannot be strengthened by precipitation hardening** for two fundamental reasons:

1. **Mg is highly soluble in Al at all temperatures below the melting point** — there is no "solvus line" to enable a supersaturated state to be "frozen" by quenching. The Mg stays dissolved even at room temperature.

2. **The strengthening precipitates that DO form in 5xxx alloys — β-phase (Al₃Mg₂) — are cathodic to the aluminium matrix** and form preferentially along grain boundaries, causing **intergranular corrosion and stress-corrosion cracking (SCC)**.
