This is why 5xxx hull plates use the **H116 and H321 tempers**, which produce a **fine, dispersed, intragranular** distribution of β-phase — not the dangerous grain-boundary network.

**Engineering Consequence:** 5xxx hull alloys achieve their strength through **cold working (strain hardening)** instead — coded as H1 (strain-hardened only), H2 (strain-hardened + partially annealed), and H3 (strain-hardened + stabilized). This is why welded 5xxx hull structures retain their strength in the HAZ — there are no precipitates to dissolve.

---

> **(b) Discuss the phenomenon of galvanic corrosion when aluminium alloys are coupled with steel or copper-based alloys in a marine environment. What practical measures are adopted in ship design to mitigate this? [7 Marks]**
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

**The Galvanic Series in Seawater:**

When two dissimilar metals are electrically connected in an electrolyte (seawater is an excellent electrolyte due to its high salinity of ~3.5% NaCl), a **galvanic cell** is formed. The more **anodic** (electrochemically active) metal corrodes preferentially; the more **cathodic** (noble) metal is protected. The driving force is the **galvanic potential difference**.

**Galvanic series in seawater (anodic → cathodic):**

$$\text{Mg} \rightarrow \text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Al alloys} \rightarrow \text{Cd} \rightarrow \text{Steel} \rightarrow \text{Pb} \rightarrow \text{Sn} \rightarrow \text{Cu} \rightarrow \text{Cu-Ni} \rightarrow \text{Monel} \rightarrow \text{Stainless Steel (austenitic)} \rightarrow \text{Ti}$$

**The Critical Marine Problem:**

- **Aluminium vs Steel:** When an aluminium hull contacts a steel propeller shaft, steel rudder post, or steel fitting, **aluminium acts as the anode** (because it is more anodic) and corrodes rapidly. The corrosion rate is **10–50 times** higher than for uncoupled aluminium.
- **Aluminium vs Copper Alloys:** This is even worse. Copper and copper-nickel alloys (used for piping, heat exchangers, seawater valves) are strongly cathodic. If an aluminium pipe carries copper-alloy valves, the **entire aluminium pipe** can be eaten away within months due to **deposit attack** (corrosion under copper-oxide deposits).
- **Aluminium vs Stainless Steel:** Less severe but still a real concern — stainless steel propellers coupled to aluminium shafts can cause pitting at the shaft.

**Galvanic Corrosion Rate Equation (Faraday's Law):**

The mass of metal dissolved per unit time is:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{M \cdot I}{n \cdot F}$$

**Logic:** Faraday's law of electrolysis. $\dot{m}$ = corrosion rate (g/s), $M$ = atomic mass (g/mol), $I$ = galvanic current (A), $n$ = valence (3 for Al), $F$ = Faraday's constant (96,485 C/mol). Higher galvanic potential difference → higher $I$ → higher $\dot{m}$.

**Practical Mitigation Measures in Ship Design:**

1. **Electrical Isolation:** Insert **non-conductive gaskets, bushes, and washers** (typically nylon, neoprene, rubber, or PTFE) between dissimilar metal joints. This breaks the electrical path of the galvanic cell.

2. **Coatings and Paints:** Apply **anti-corrosion coatings** (epoxy primers + polyurethane topcoats) on the **cathodic** (noble) metal — this prevents the cathodic reaction from proceeding. NEVER coat only the aluminium, as this concentrates corrosion at coating defects.

3. **Cathodic Protection by Sacrificial Anodes:** Attach **zinc or aluminium anodes** to the hull. These anodes are deliberately more anodic and "sacrifice" themselves, protecting the hull. Typical marine vessels use **Al–Zn–In sacrificial anodes** strapped to the hull at 2–3 m intervals.

4. **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP):** For large vessels (LNG carriers, naval ships), use an external DC power source with inert titanium anodes to drive the hull to a protective potential of approximately **−0.95 V (Ag/AgCl reference)**.

5. **Material Selection:** Avoid copper-based alloys in any system that contacts aluminium. Use **aluminium-bronze** (limited use) or **super-duplex stainless steel** for valves and fittings. Use **aluminium propellers** on aluminium shafts, or **fibre-reinforced plastic (FRP)** propellers and shafts.

6. **Area Ratio Design:** The **cathode-to-anode area ratio** must be kept small. A large cathodic area (e.g., a big copper pipe) coupled to a small anodic area (e.g., a small aluminium fitting) causes **intense localized corrosion** on the small aluminium part. Design with large aluminium areas and small noble-metal areas.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> In the galvanic corrosion question, students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** by:
> 1. **Forgetting the area ratio rule** — examiners specifically look for "small anode + large cathode = severe corrosion" as a concept.
> 2. **Misidentifying which metal corrodes** — the **more anodic** metal corrodes, not the more reactive-sounding one. Aluminium is more anodic than steel in the **galvanic series in seawater** (different from the thermodynamic EMF series).
> 3. **Omitting the numerical Faraday equation** when the question asks for the rate — always include it in 7-mark answers.
> 4. **Confusing "sacrificial anode" with "cathodic protection by impressed current"** — these are two different ICCP vs galvanic anode methods; mark them separately.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read This on the Night Before the Exam**

### ✓ The 8-Series Designation
- 1xxx = pure Al · 2xxx = Al-Cu · 3xxx = Al-Mn · 4xxx = Al-Si
- **5xxx = Al-Mg (MARINE HULL KING)** · 6xxx = Al-Mg-Si (MARINE EXTRUSIONS)
- 7xxx = Al-Zn · 8xxx = other (Li for aerospace)

### ✓ The Four Marine Workhorse Alloys
- **5083-H116** → hull plates, decks → UTS 305 / YS 215 MPa → **NON-heat-treatable**
- **5086** → smaller craft → UTS 260 / YS 195 MPa
- **5454** → pressure vessels, elevated temp → UTS 250 / YS 180 MPa
- **6061-T6** → masts, superstructures, extrusions → UTS 310 / YS 275 MPa → **HEAT-TREATABLE**
- **6082-T6** → European standard for marine extrusions → UTS 340 / YS 310 MPa

### ✓ Critical Numerical Constants to Memorize
- $\rho_{Al} = 2.70$ g/cm³ (pure) · 2.66 g/cm³ (5083) · $\rho_{steel} = 7.85$ g/cm³
- $E_{Al} = 70$ GPa · $E_{steel} = 210$ GPa · $E_{Al}/E_{steel} = 1/3$
- Melting point of Al = 660.3 °C
- Specific strength ratio (5083 vs mild steel) ≈ 2.25
- 5xxx tempers: H116, H321 (marine, stabilized)
- 6xxx tempers: T6 (peak strength), T4 (natural age), T7 (over-aged)

### ✓ Three-Step T6 Heat Treatment
1. **Solution treat** at ~530 °C → dissolves Mg₂Si
2. **Quench** in water → SSSS, soft and ductile
3. **Age** at ~175 °C for 8–12 h → β″ precipitates pin dislocations → T6 peak strength

### ✓ Why 5xxx Cannot Be Age-Hardened
- Mg is soluble at all temperatures below Al's melting point
- Strengthening precipitates (Al₃Mg₂) are cathodic → cause intergranular corrosion
- 5xxx uses **cold work (H-tempers)** and **stabilization** instead

### ✓ Galvanic Corrosion in Seawater — Al is the Anode
- Al vs steel → steel is cathode, Al corrodes 10–50× faster
- Mitigation: isolation gaskets, coatings on cathode, sacrificial Zn/Al anodes, ICCP, avoid Cu-Ni
- Cathode:anode area ratio must be **small** to limit corrosion rate

### ✓ Engineering Trade-off Equation
- Stiffness $EI \propto E \cdot t^3$ → aluminium plate must be $\sqrt[3]{E_s/E_{Al}} = \sqrt[3]{3} \approx 1.44$ times thicker
- 1.44× thicker + 1/3 the density = **~50% weight saving** in plate structures

### ✓ Common Pitfall Statements to AVOID in Exam Answers
- ❌ "5xxx cannot be heat-treated" → should be "5xxx cannot be **strengthened** by heat treatment"
- ❌ "Aluminium does not corrode" → should be "Aluminium forms a self-healing Al₂O₃ passive film that is stable in marine pH 4–8.5"
- ❌ "T6 means tempered six times" → T6 is a single heat-treatment cycle, not repeated tempering
- ❌ "Aluminium is non-magnetic" → correct, but this is the property, not a reason for corrosion
- ❌ "Precipitation hardening increases corrosion resistance" → usually false — second-phase particles are often cathodic sites

### ✓ Five Must-Draw Diagrams for the Exam
1. **T6 heat treatment cycle** (T-temp time-temperature curve with quench + age)
2. **Al-Cu phase diagram** (showing solution-treatment temperature and solvus line)
3. **Galvanic series in seawater** (as a horizontal bar)
4. **Cross-section of an aluminium hull plate with extrusions and weld locations**
5. **Block diagram of the AA 8-series classification** (as in Section 4.1 above)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material Characteristics of Marine Grade Aluminium
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Material Characteristics of Marine Grade Aluminium

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marine Grade Aluminium** refers to a specific class of wrought aluminium alloys that have been engineered, heat-treated, and temper-designated to withstand the harsh operational environment of seawater, saline mist, low-temperature service, dynamic wave loading, and prolonged UV exposure. The two principal alloy families used in shipbuilding under the KTU 2024 syllabus are the **5xxx series (Al–Mg–Mn)** and the **6xxx series (Al–Mg–Si)** alloys, typically registered under ASTM B928 / B209 and EN 573 / EN 485 standards.

The qualifying term "marine grade" is not a generic marketing label — it is a **metallurgical commitment**. The alloy must exhibit a stable passive oxide film (predominantly $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ with hydrated oxy-hydroxide sublayers), controlled intergranular corrosion resistance, and a guaranteed minimum Charpy impact toughness at sub-zero service temperatures typical of polar or deep-sea routes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Unsinkable Skin of a Modern Yacht"**
> Imagine a ship's hull as the **skin of an aircraft wing**. The skin must be light enough to fly (or float efficiently), strong enough to bear aerodynamic pressure (or hydrodynamic slamming loads), and corrosion-resistant enough to survive a salty atmosphere. A marine-grade aluminium alloy plays the exact same role for a vessel — it is the **lightweight, corrosion-defying skin** that replaces heavier mild steel, much like carbon-fibre replaced aluminium in aviation. The "grade" simply means the metal has been *prescription-tuned* (alloying + tempering) to behave predictably in seawater, just as a swimmer's wetsuit is neoprene-tuned for cold water.

### 1.3 Why Aluminium for Ships?

| Performance Driver | Aluminium's Edge | Quantitative Indicator |
|---|---|---|
| Lightweight displacement | Lower density than steel | $\rho \approx 2.70 \text{ g/cm}^3$ (vs steel 7.85) |
| Corrosion resistance | Self-passivating oxide film | Open-circuit potential $\approx -0.75$ V (SCE) in seawater |
| Strength efficiency | High specific strength | UTS/density $\approx 100$ kNm/kg for 5083-H116 |
| Manufacturability | Excellent weldability & formability | Hot-crack susceptibility **minimal** in 5xxx |
| Recyclability | Infinite recyclability | Embodied energy recovery $\approx 95\%$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — Specific Strength**
> Specific strength is the ratio of a material's strength to its density. For ship designers, this dictates **payload-to-hull-weight ratio** and therefore **fuel economy** and **deadweight capacity**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Pillars — 5xxx vs 6xxx Series

The marine industry has effectively standardized on **two alloy philosophies**, each addressing a different engineering priority:

#### 2.1.1 5xxx Series — Al–Mg–Mn (Work-Hardenable)

- **Primary alloying element:** Magnesium (Mg) — typically 2.5% to 5.5%.
- **Secondary additions:** Manganese (Mn) ~0.5–1.0% (grain refiner), Chromium (Cr) ~0.05–0.25% (stress-corrosion cracking resistance), Titanium (Ti) (grain refiner).
- **Strengthening mechanism:** **Solid-solution strengthening + strain hardening** (cold work). These alloys are **non-heat-treatable**.
- **Temper designations used in shipbuilding:** O (annealed), H111, H112, H116, H321, H323, H343.
- **Typical alloys:** AA 5052, 5083, 5086, 5383, 5454, 5456, 5754.
- **Marine use:** Hull plating, side shells, bottom plates, deck plates, superstructure of fast ferries, naval patrol vessels, LNG carrier spherical tank supports.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Metallurgical Fact:** In 5xxx alloys with Mg content **>3%**, prolonged exposure to elevated service temperatures (65 °C – 200 °C) can precipitate the $\beta$-phase ($\text{Al}_3\text{Mg}_2$) at grain boundaries. This makes the alloy **sensitive to intergranular corrosion and stress-corrosion cracking (SCC)**. For this reason, **AA 5083 is restricted to a maximum service temperature of 65 °C** in marine classifications such as DNV, Lloyd's Register, and IRS.

#### 2.1.2 6xxx Series — Al–Mg–Si (Heat-Treatable)

- **Primary alloying elements:** Magnesium (0.4–1.2%) and Silicon (0.2–0.8%), forming the $\text{Mg}_2\text{Si}$ (β″) strengthening phase.
- **Secondary additions:** Mn, Cr for grain control; Cu in some grades (6061) for additional strength.
- **Strengthening mechanism:** **Precipitation hardening** — solution treatment, quenching, and artificial aging (T6 temper).
- **Temper designations used in shipbuilding:** T4, T5, T6, T651.
- **Typical alloys:** AA 6005A, 6061, 6063, 6082, 6106, 6351.
- **Marine use:** Extruded profiles — masts, handrails, deck fittings, superstructure stiffeners, helicopter deck framing, mast ladders, lifeboat davits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Tip — Why two series?**
> The 5xxx series is selected where the designer wants **maximum weldability and corrosion resistance** in plate form (hull). The 6xxx series is selected where **complex extruded shapes with higher yield strength** are needed (profiles, stiffeners, superstructure). This dual-alloy strategy is the **backbone of every modern aluminium superstructure-and-hull vessel**.

### 2.2 The Passive Film — Why Aluminium Resists Seawater

When freshly cut aluminium contacts air or water, it instantaneously forms a thin, adherent, amphoteric oxide layer:

$$2\text{Al} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot n\text{H}_2\text{O} + 3\text{H}_2$$

This film is **self-healing** — if scratched, it reforms within microseconds in oxygenated seawater. The film thickness in natural seawater is typically 50–200 Å, but in high-temperature service or in alkaline solutions, it can grow to several microns.

> [!WARNING]
> The film is **amphoteric** — it dissolves in both strong acids (pH < 4) **and** strong alkalis (pH > 9). Avoid contact with cement, wet concrete, or alkaline cleaning agents on bare hull surfaces.

### 2.3 Mechanical Property Reference Table

| Alloy & Temper | Yield Strength $\sigma_y$ (MPa) | UTS $\sigma_u$ (MPa) | Elongation (%) | Density (g/cm³) | Melting Range (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5052-H32 | 215 | 260 | 12 | 2.68 | 607–650 |
| 5083-O | 145 | 290 | 22 | 2.66 | 570–640 |
| 5083-H116 | 215 | 305 | 12 | 2.66 | 570–640 |
| 5083-H321 | 230 | 315 | 10 | 2.66 | 570–640 |
| 5086-H116 | 195 | 275 | 12 | 2.66 | 585–640 |
| 5383-H116 | 235 | 320 | 11 | 2.66 | 570–638 |
| 5454-H32 | 200 | 250 | 10 | 2.69 | 600–645 |
| 5456-H116 | 250 | 325 | 11 | 2.66 | 568–638 |
| 5754-H111 | 145 | 230 | 14 | 2.67 | 590–645 |
| 6061-T6 | 275 | 310 | 12 | 2.70 | 582–652 |
| 6082-T6 | 260 | 310 | 10 | 2.70 | 585–650 |
| 6005A-T6 | 270 | 315 | 10 | 2.70 | 585–650 |

> [!NOTE]
> Values are nominal, taken from manufacturer datasheets (e.g., Hydro, Constellium, Kaiser) and applicable at room temperature (20 °C). They will vary slightly with plate thickness, lot, and testing direction (longitudinal vs transverse).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Engineering Calculations for Marine Aluminium

| S.No | Concept | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Strength (efficiency) | $\Phi = \dfrac{\sigma_u}{\rho}$ | $\sigma_u$ = ultimate tensile strength, $\rho$ = density | kNm/kg |
| 2 | Buoyancy-corrected weight savings vs steel | $\Delta W = W_s \left(1 - \dfrac{\rho_{Al}}{\rho_s}\right)$ | $W_s$ = steel weight, $\rho_s = 7.85$ | kg |
| 3 | Weight reduction percentage | $\%W = \left(1 - \dfrac{\rho_{Al}}{\rho_{s}}\right)\times 100$ | — | % |
| 4 | Plate thermal expansion (linear) | $\Delta L = \alpha L_0 \Delta T$ | $\alpha \approx 23 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, $L_0$ = original length | m |
| 5 | Modulus of Elasticity (Al alloys) | $E_{Al} \approx 70$ GPa | (vs steel $E_s \approx 210$ GPa) | GPa |
| 6 | Stiffness-equivalent thickness | $t_{Al} = t_{s}\sqrt{\dfrac{E_s}{E_{Al}}} = t_s \sqrt{3}$ | (corrosion excluded) | mm |
| 7 | Bending stiffness ratio | $\dfrac{D_{Al}}{D_{s}} = \dfrac{E_{Al} t_{Al}^3}{E_s t_s^3}$ | $D$ = flexural rigidity | N·m² |
| 8 | Corrosion rate (linear) | $CR = \dfrac{534 W}{DAT}$ | $W$ = mass loss (mg), $D$ = density, $A$ = area, $T$ = time | mpy |
| 9 | Pitting rate (Pr) | $Pr = \dfrac{d_{pit}}{T}$ | $d_{pit}$ = deepest pit depth, $T$ = exposure time | mm/yr |
| 10 | Galvanic series potential | $\Delta E = E_c - E_a$ | $c$ = cathode, $a$ = anode; if $> 0.25$ V, severe galvanic risk | V |
| 11 | Fatigue strength (typical) | $\sigma_f \approx 0.4\,\sigma_u$ for $10^7$ cycles | (S–N approximation for Al) | MPa |
| 12 | Welding heat input (MIG) | $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v}$ | $V$ = voltage, $I$ = current, $v$ = travel speed, $\eta \approx 0.7$ | kJ/mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall in Exam — Units of Corrosion Rate**
> Many students confuse **mpy (mils per year)** and **mm/yr**. Conversion: $1 \text{ mpy} = 0.0254 \text{ mm/yr}$. Always quote the SI unit **mm/yr** unless the question specifies otherwise.

### 2.5 Application Domains in a Typical Ship

```
Hull Bottom & Side Shell      → 5083-H116 / 5383-H116 plate (5–30 mm)
Bulwark & Superstructure      → 5083 / 5086 / 5454 plate
Helicopter Deck Plate         → 6082-T6 or 5383-H116 (slip-resistant pattern)
Hull Longitudinal Stiffeners  → 6082-T6 extruded profile
Frames & Transverse Webs      → 6082-T6 / 6005A-T6 extrusion
Masts, King Posts, Davits     → 6082-T6 / 6061-T6 extrusions
Lifeboat & Rescue Craft Hulls → 5083-H111 / 5383-H116
Fast Ferry Car Deck           → 5083-H321 (5–8 mm)
LNG Bunker Tank Supports      → 5083-O (annealed for cryogenic ductility)
```

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Weight Saving of an Aluminium Hull vs a Steel Hull

> **Problem Setup:** A 50 m patrol vessel is being designed. The estimated steel hull weight is $W_s = 280$ tonnes. If the designer replaces it with marine grade 5083-H116 aluminium of density $\rho_{Al} = 2.66$ g/cm³, what is the hull weight and percentage weight reduction?

**Step 1 — Identify constants and units.**
We have $\rho_{Al} = 2.66 \text{ g/cm}^3$ and $\rho_s = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$. We do not need an absolute mass conversion factor because both densities share the same volumetric denominator.

**Step 2 — Write the weight relation.**
For equal volume of material:

$$W = \rho \cdot V \cdot g$$

Since volume $V$ and gravity $g$ are common to both:

$$\dfrac{W_{Al}}{W_s} = \dfrac{\rho_{Al}}{\rho_s}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$\dfrac{W_{Al}}{W_s} = \dfrac{2.66}{7.85} = 0.3389$$

**Step 4 — Compute aluminium hull weight.**

$$W_{Al} = 0.3389 \times 280 = 94.9 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 5 — Compute absolute weight saved.**

$$\Delta W = W_s - W_{Al} = 280 - 94.9 = 185.1 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 6 — Compute percentage weight reduction.**

$$\%W = \left(1 - \dfrac{2.66}{7.85}\right) \times 100 = (1 - 0.3389) \times 100 = 66.11\%$$

**Step 7 — Engineering interpretation.**
A 66% hull weight reduction translates to:
- **Lower centre of gravity** when ballast is also reduced.
- **Higher payload capacity** (≈ 185 tonnes of additional weapons, fuel, or cargo).
- **Fuel savings** of 15–25% at a given service speed, depending on hydrodynamic form.
- **Smaller propulsion plant** required for the same speed, freeing machinery-space volume.

> [!NOTE]
> The trade-off is **reduced stiffness** because $E_{Al}/E_s = 70/210 = 1/3$. Designers must therefore **thicker plate or closer frame spacing** to retain bending rigidity — this partially offsets the weight gain.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Stiffness-Equivalent Plate Thickness

> **Problem Setup:** A 12 mm mild steel deck plate is to be replaced by 5083-H116 aluminium of equal flexural rigidity. Find the equivalent aluminium thickness.

**Step 1 — Stiffness formula for an isotropic plate (per unit width).**

$$D = \dfrac{E\,t^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)}$$

**Step 2 — Equate the two rigidities.**

$$D_{Al} = D_s \Rightarrow \dfrac{E_{Al}\,t_{Al}^3}{12(1 - \nu_{Al}^2)} = \dfrac{E_s\,t_s^3}{12(1 - \nu_s^2)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Poisson's ratio correction.**
For both alloys, $\nu_{Al} \approx 0.33$ and $\nu_s \approx 0.30$. The $(1 - \nu^2)$ term is 0.891 for aluminium and 0.910 for steel — a difference of only **2.1%**, so for a first-order KTU answer we may ignore it.

**Step 4 — Simplify and solve.**

$$t_{Al}^3 = t_s^3 \cdot \dfrac{E_s}{E_{Al}} = 12^3 \cdot \dfrac{210}{70}$$

**Step 5 — Numerically evaluate.**

$$t_{Al}^3 = 1728 \times 3 = 5184 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$t_{Al} = \sqrt[3]{5184} = 17.3 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Compare mass per unit area.**

$$m_s = 7.85 \times 12 = 94.2 \text{ kg/m}^2$$

$$m_{Al} = 2.66 \times 17.3 = 46.0 \text{ kg/m}^2$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion.**
Even with the **44% thicker aluminium plate**, the **mass per square metre is halved** (from 94.2 to 46.0 kg/m²). This is the **structural payoff** of going aluminium.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection Decision Tree

| Engineering Requirement | Recommended Alloy | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Large welded hull plate, exposed to splash zone | **5083-H116** or **5383-H116** | Highest Mg, best weld corrosion resistance |
| Superstructure, weight-critical | **5083 / 5086** | Excellent weld + corrosion |
| Extruded longitudinal stiffener | **6082-T6** | Best strength, extrudability |
| Helideck / flight deck | **6082-T6 pattern plate** | Slip-resistance + fatigue |
| LNG cargo tank support (cryogenic) | **5083-O** | Retains ductility down to –196 °C |
| Mast / king post (high strength) | **6005A-T6 / 6082-T6** | Strength with weldability |
| Decorative superstructure, light loading | **6063-T5 / 6063-T6** | Excellent anodising response |
| High-speed craft hull (≥ 30 knots) | **5383-H116 / 5456-H116** | High UTS + fracture toughness |
| Heat exchanger plates | **3003 / 5052** | Thermal conductivity + formability |

---

### 3.4 Derivation — Thermal Expansion Stress in a Restrained Plate

> **Problem Setup:** A 10 m long 5083-H116 deck plate is welded between two steel bulkheads. The vessel operates from –10 °C (polar) to +45 °C (tropical sun on deck). What thermal stress develops if the joint is fully restrained?

**Step 1 — Identify thermal expansion coefficient.**

$$\alpha_{Al} = 23 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the temperature swing.**

$$\Delta T = 45 - (-10) = 55 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 3 — Compute free thermal strain.**

$$\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha_{Al} \cdot \Delta T = 23 \times 10^{-6} \times 55 = 1.265 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Hooke's law for fully restrained condition.**
The mechanical strain induced by the restraint must cancel the thermal strain, so the **mechanical stress** is:

$$\sigma_{th} = E_{Al} \cdot \varepsilon_{th} = 70\,000 \text{ MPa} \times 1.265 \times 10^{-3} = 88.6 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Compare to the alloy's yield strength.**

$$\sigma_y \text{ of 5083-H116} \approx 215 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\text{Safety factor} = \dfrac{215}{88.6} = 2.43$$

**Step 6 — Practical conclusion.**
The structure survives, but the **88.6 MPa thermal stress** is dangerously close to service fatigue thresholds if repeated daily. **Expansion joints** at every 8–12 m of continuous aluminium are mandatory in DNV-classed vessels.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Marine Grade Aluminium — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **metallurgical decision architecture** that a ship-design engineer follows when selecting a marine-grade aluminium alloy for a structural member.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Structural Requirement] --> B{Primary Loading Type}
    B -->|Plate / Welding Critical| C[5xxx Series Route]
    B -->|Extrusion / High Strength| D[6xxx Series Route]
    C --> E{Mg Content Decision}
    E -->|Low 2.5 to 3.5 percent| F[AA 5052 or 5754]
    E -->|Medium 3.5 to 4.5 percent| G[AA 5083 or 5086]
    E -->|High 4.5 to 5.5 percent| H[AA 5383 or 5456]
    D --> I{Profile Geometry}
    I -->|Thin hollow section| J[AA 6005A T6]
    I -->|Solid thick plate strip| K[AA 6082 T6]
    I -->|Architectural finish| L[AA 6063 T5]
    F --> M[Service Temper Selection]
    G --> M
    H --> M
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N{Service Temperature}
    N -->|Below 65 deg C| O[Apply H116 or H321 Temper]
    N -->|Above 65 deg C| P[Switch to 6xxx or 5454]
    O --> Q[Final Specification]
    P --> Q
    M --> Q
    Q --> R[Class Approval DNV LR IRS ABS]
    R --> S[Fabrication and Welding]
    S --> T[In Service Marine Vessel]
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Passive Film Formation on Aluminium

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Freshly Cut Aluminium Surface] --> B[Contact With Air or Water]
    B --> C[Oxidation Reaction Starts]
    C --> D[Al2O3 Nucleation on Surface]
    D --> E[Hydration Adds Al2O3 times nH2O]
    E --> F[Continuous Passive Film 50 to 200 Angstrom]
    F --> G{Surface Scratched?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Film Damaged Locally]
    H --> I[Re oxidation Within Microseconds]
    I --> F
    G -->|No| J[Stable Corrosion Protection]
    F --> J
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Galvanic Couple Risk in Mixed-Material Ship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Aluminium Hull 5083 H116] -->|Cathode -0.75 V| B[Steel Bolt Fastener]
    A -->|Anode relative to Cu Ni| C[Copper Nickel Pipe]
    A -->|Compatible| D[Stainless Steel 316L Washer]
    B --> E[Insulating Sleeve or Rubber Gasket]
    C --> F[Bonded Dielectric Isolator]
    E --> G[Safe Joint]
    F --> G
    D --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic**
> Aluminium is **anodic** to copper-nickel alloys (more noble), so it will sacrifice itself and pit if not electrically isolated. It is **cathodic** to carbon steel, so unprotected steel fasteners will rust aggressively in the vicinity of the aluminium hull. **Insulating gaskets, sleeves, and a careful galvanic-series audit** are mandatory in every mixed-material joint — a 2 mm neoprene or EPDM gasket saves years of service life.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]
Define the term **"marine grade aluminium"**. List the two principal alloy series used in shipbuilding and state one typical application of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Marine grade aluminium refers to a class of wrought aluminium alloys engineered and temper-designated to withstand prolonged exposure to seawater, salt spray, low temperatures, and dynamic marine loading, while maintaining a self-healing passive oxide film and adequate fracture toughness.

- **5xxx series (Al–Mg–Mn)** — e.g., AA 5083-H116 — used for **welded hull plating** of patrol vessels and fast ferries (1 mark).
- **6xxx series (Al–Mg–Si)** — e.g., AA 6082-T6 — used for **extruded superstructure stiffeners and masts** (1 mark).

Definition = 1 mark, two series + applications = 2 marks.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]
Why is AA 5083 limited to a maximum continuous service temperature of 65 °C in ship hull applications?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
When AA 5083 (Mg content ≈ 4.0–4.9%) is exposed to elevated temperatures in the range of 65–200 °C, the **β-phase $\text{Al}_3\text{Mg}_2$** precipitates preferentially at grain boundaries (1 mark). This makes the alloy susceptible to **intergranular corrosion and stress-corrosion cracking (SCC)** (1 mark). To eliminate this risk, classification societies such as DNV, Lloyd's Register, and Indian Register of Shipping cap the service temperature at 65 °C for sustained operation (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Question Paper Pattern (KTU 2024):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Two choices are provided — answer **either** Choice A **or** Choice B in full.

---

### Choice A — 14 Marks

**(a)** [7 marks] Compare the **5xxx and 6xxx series** marine-grade aluminium alloys with respect to:
(i) Principal alloying elements,
(ii) Strengthening mechanism,
(iii) Typical marine application.

**(b)** [7 marks] With the help of a **schematic, explain the formation of the passive oxide film on aluminium** and state **two conditions** under which this film can fail.

#### Model Solution — (a)

| Parameter | 5xxx Series | 6xxx Series |
|---|---|---|
| Principal alloying elements | Mg (2.5–5.5%) + Mn + Cr | Mg (0.4–1.2%) + Si (0.2–0.8%) + Mn/Cr |
| Strengthening mechanism | Solid-solution + strain hardening (work hardening) | Precipitation hardening (heat treatable) — forms $\text{Mg}_2\text{Si}$ β″ phase |
| Temper codes | O, H111, H116, H321 | T4, T5, T6, T651 |
| Typical marine application | Welded hull plating (AA 5083) | Extruded masts, superstructure (AA 6082) |

(1 mark each row × 3 = 3 marks; 1 mark for additional notes on corrosion/weldability; **Total 7 marks**)

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Schematic (3 marks)** — Draw the following reaction sequence:

$$\text{Al} \rightarrow \text{Al}^{3+} + 3e^- \quad \text{(anodic)}$$

$$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^- \quad \text{(cathodic)}$$

$$2\text{Al} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot n\text{H}_2\text{O} + 3\text{H}_2$$

The film grows to 50–200 Å on the metal surface and acts as a kinetic barrier.

**Two conditions for film failure (4 marks — 2 each):**

1. **Strong acid attack (pH < 4):** The amphoteric $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ dissolves, leading to general corrosion. Example: hydrochloric acid spill on deck.
2. **Strong alkali attack (pH > 9):** Fresh wet concrete, portland cement, or alkaline cleaning agents dissolve the film and cause **pitting or uniform etching** of the underlying aluminium. Example: concrete splash on bare 5083 deck during wharf construction.
3. **Galvanic coupling with a more noble metal (Cu, Cu-Ni, graphite)** strips electrons and accelerates anodic dissolution.
4. **Crevice corrosion** in stagnant, oxygen-deprived joints (e.g., unsealed lap welds) — differential aeration cell.

(Any two well-explained conditions fetch full marks.)

---

### Choice B — 14 Marks

**(a)** [7 marks] A 60 m long passenger ferry is being re-plated with AA 5083-H116. The original steel hull weighed 320 tonnes. Calculate the **new aluminium hull weight, the absolute weight saving, and the percentage reduction**. Given: $\rho_{Al} = 2.66$ g/cm³, $\rho_{s} = 7.85$ g/cm³. Comment on the **engineering consequences** of this change.

**(b)** [7 marks] Explain the difference between **temper designations H116 and T6** in marine aluminium alloys. State which alloy and temper combination you would recommend for an **extruded helicopter-deck support frame** and justify your selection in **four bullet points**.

#### Model Solution — (a) [Full Step-by-Step]

**Step 1 — Write the weight-ratio relation** (1 mark):

$$\dfrac{W_{Al}}{W_s} = \dfrac{\rho_{Al}}{\rho_s} = \dfrac{2.66}{7.85} = 0.3389$$

**Step 2 — Compute aluminium hull weight** (1 mark):

$$W_{Al} = 0.3389 \times 320 = 108.4 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 3 — Compute absolute weight saved** (1 mark):

$$\Delta W = 320 - 108.4 = 211.6 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 4 — Compute percentage weight reduction** (1 mark):

$$\%W = (1 - 0.3389) \times 100 = 66.11\%$$

**Step 5 — Engineering consequences (3 marks):**
- **Payload gain** of ~211 tonnes — additional 200+ passengers, vehicles, or fuel.
- **Lower fuel consumption** of 15–20% at design speed.
- **Smaller engines** can be selected → lower machinery cost, free engine-room volume.
- **Trade-off:** Al is one-third the stiffness of steel, so plate thickness must be increased by $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.73\times$ for equivalent rigidity, partly offsetting the weight gain.
- **Galvanic isolation** is mandatory for every steel/copper/brass fitting on the hull.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Difference between H116 and T6 (4 marks):**

| Aspect | H116 (5xxx) | T6 (6xxx) |
|---|---|---|
| Alloy type | Non-heat-treatable | Heat-treatable |
| Strengthening | Strain-hardened to a specific strain (≈ 1/4 hard) | Solution-treated, quenched, then **artificially aged** to peak hardness |
| Process | Cold rolling (or stretching) to ~25% reduction | Heat to ~530 °C → quench in water → age at 175 °C for 8–10 h |
| Typical alloy | AA 5083, 5086, 5383, 5456 | AA 6061, 6082, 6005A, 6063 |
| Application | Welded plate (hull) | Extruded profiles, machined fittings |

**Recommendation for extruded helicopter-deck support frame (3 marks):**
**Alloy 6082-T6** is recommended.

**Four-bullet justification:**
- **Highest yield strength** in the 6xxx family (≥ 260 MPa), critical for supporting dynamic helicopter landing loads (up to 8–12 t).
- **Excellent extrudability** into complex hollow sections (slim T-sections, I-beams) used in stiffener layouts.
- **Good weldability** with 4043 / 5356 filler, enabling site assembly of the helideck sub-frame.
- **Proven track record** in naval architecture for helicopter platforms, masts, and king posts (e.g., 6082-T6 is the de-facto choice in DNV-classed helidecks).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Conflating temper with alloy** — writing "5083-T6" is metallurgically wrong because 5083 is *not* heat-treatable. Use 5083-H116 or 5083-H321. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting units** in weight-savings and corrosion-rate questions. Always state kg, tonnes, mm/yr, or g/cm³ explicitly. (–0.5 to –1 mark)
> 3. **Mixing up modulus values** — $E_{Al} = 70$ GPa (NOT 200 GPa). A common slip in numerical problems. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Citing "aluminium does not corrode"** — examiners will deduct marks. State that aluminium *forms a passive film* that confers corrosion *resistance*, not immunity.
> 5. **Forgetting galvanic isolation** in mixed-material ship design — this is a favourite KTU Part-A trap question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Marine Grade Aluminium**

- **Definition:** Aluminium alloy engineered to resist seawater via a self-healing $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ passive film, qualified to classification-society standards.
- **Two principal series:** **5xxx (Al–Mg)** for welded plate hulls; **6xxx (Al–Mg–Si)** for extruded profiles and superstructure.
- **Density:** $\rho_{Al} \approx 2.66$–$2.70$ g/cm³ (≈ 1/3 of steel).
- **Modulus:** $E_{Al} \approx 70$ GPa (≈ 1/3 of steel) — requires thicker plates for equal stiffness.
- **Hull weight saving vs steel:** ~65–66% theoretical; ~50–55% practical (after thickness compensation).
- **Temper codes:** H116 / H321 = strain-hardened 5xxx; T6 = precipitation-hardened 6xxx.
- **Service temperature cap:** 65 °C for 5083 to avoid $\beta$-phase ($\text{Al}_3\text{Mg}_2$) precipitation and SCC.
- **Cryogenic suitability:** 5083-O retains ductility down to –196 °C, ideal for LNG support structures.
- **Galvanic risk:** Insulate aluminium from Cu, Cu-Ni, and bare carbon steel; use 316L stainless with dielectric gaskets.
- **Passive film:** 50–200 Å, amphoteric, dissolves in strong acid (pH < 4) and strong alkali (pH > 9).
- **Common ship standards:** ASTM B928, B209; EN 485, EN 573; DNV, Lloyd's Register, IRS, ABS class rules.
- **Specific strength** is the key efficiency metric for marine selection.
- **Weld filler choice:** 4043 (silicon-rich) for 6xxx; 5356 (magnesium-rich) for 5xxx; 5183 for 5083 specifically.
- **Stiffness-equivalent thickness:** $t_{Al} = t_s \sqrt{E_s / E_{Al}} \approx 1.73 \, t_s$.
- **Key alloys to memorise:** 5083, 5086, 5383, 5456, 5754, 6061, 6082, 6005A, 6063.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structural Design Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Structural Design Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Structural Design Considerations** in shipbuilding refer to the systematic engineering evaluation of loads, material properties, geometric configurations, environmental factors, and failure modes that govern the safe, efficient, and economical use of **non-ferrous metals and alloys** (primarily aluminium, copper-nickel, titanium, and magnesium alloys) in the construction of marine vessels and offshore structures.

In the context of **KTU PENST523 (2024 Scheme)**, the term encompasses the design philosophy, classification society rules (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, IRS), allowable stress methodologies, buckling assessments, fatigue life estimations, corrosion allowances, and fabrication constraints (welding, forming, joining) that the naval architect must address when substituting or specifying non-ferrous materials in place of traditional mild steel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Structural Design Considerations is a **high-weightage topic** that bridges the gap between the *metallurgical properties* studied earlier and the *practical ship construction* modules that follow. Questions in University ESE frequently (40–60% probability) test the student's ability to justify material substitution and quantify safety margins.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lightweight Skeleton" Model

Imagine a mariner's wristwatch versus a chunky old pocket watch. Both tell time accurately, but the **wristwatch uses titanium and aluminium alloys** to achieve the same structural integrity at a fraction of the weight. Now scale this idea up to a **100-metre naval patrol vessel**:

| Component | Steel Version | Non-Ferrous Version |
|---|---|---|
| Superstructure | Mild steel, heavy | Aluminium-magnesium (5083/5086), 40% lighter |
| Piping (seawater) | Carbon steel + coating | 90/10 Cu-Ni, no coating |
| Propeller | Manganese bronze | Ni-Al bronze (superior cavitation resistance) |
| Fasteners | Galvanised steel | Monel / Silicon bronze |

The wristwatch analogy captures the **central design dilemma**: every kilogram removed from the upper structure lowers the **metacentric height penalty**, increases **payload**, and improves **fuel efficiency** — *but* only if the structural integrity, fatigue life, and corrosion resistance are not compromised. The structural designer must therefore walk a tightrope between **weight optimisation** and **safety factors**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (memorise for KTU):**
> - Density of mild steel: **7850 kg/m³**
> - Density of aluminium alloy 5083: **2660 kg/m³** (≈ 1/3 of steel)
> - Density of titanium Grade 5 (Ti-6Al-4V): **4430 kg/m³**
> - Density of 90/10 Cu-Ni: **8940 kg/m³**
> - Modulus of Elasticity, E (steel): **200 GPa**, E (aluminium): **70 GPa**, E (titanium): **114 GPa**
> - Standard design wave height (DNV): **Hs ≈ 2.5 m to 8.5 m** depending on vessel class
> - Design life of a commercial vessel: **25 years** (≈ 2.0 × 10⁸ wave cycles)

---

## 1.3 Why Non-Ferrous? — The Three Pillars of Justification

A KTU examiner will award marks only if you can **logically justify** the use of a non-ferrous material. The three accepted pillars are:

1. **Weight Reduction (Lightweighting):** Critical for superstructures, fast ferries, naval combatants, and any structure where a high centre of gravity is undesirable.
2. **Corrosion Resistance:** Eliminates the need for protective coatings in seawater service (e.g., Cu-Ni pipework, aluminium-magnesium hull plates above waterline).
3. **Special Physical Properties:** Non-magnetic behaviour (mine countermeasure vessels — aluminium or austenitic stainless), high thermal/electrical conductivity (Cu-Ni condensers), or non-sparking behaviour (bronze tools in explosive atmospheres).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Design Philosophy Hierarchy

Marine structural design follows a cascading logic. At the top sits the **Limit State Design (LSD)** philosophy recommended by IACS (International Association of Classification Societies), under which two limit states must be satisfied:

- **Ultimate Limit State (ULS):** Structure must not collapse (yielding, rupture, buckling).
- **Serviceability Limit State (SLS):** Structure must remain functional (deflection, vibration, fatigue, leak tightness).

Below the LSD philosophy sit the practical calculation methods used by classification societies:

| Method | Basis | Common Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Allowable Stress Design (ASD)** | σ_actual ≤ σ_allow = σ_yield / FoS | Traditional; still used for non-ferrous plate panels |
| **Partial Safety Factor (PSF)** | σ_actual ≤ σ_yield / (γ_m · γ_f) | IACS unified rules; modern ship design |
| **Limit State (LRFD)** | Σ γ_i · Q_i ≤ R_n / γ_R | Offshore structures (API, DNV-OS-C101) |

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Design Logic (the "10-Point Checklist")

A KTU long-answer question often asks: *"Discuss the structural design considerations for aluminium superstructures."* Use this checklist as your **structured answer template**:

1. **Define the loading envelope** — still-water bending moment, wave-induced shear, slamming, green-water loading.
2. **Select the alloy** — based on strength, weldability, corrosion resistance (e.g., 5083-H116 for marine plate).
3. **Determine yield & ultimate strengths** — from classification society rules (e.g., DNV-RU-SHIP Pt.3 Ch.4).
4. **Compute design stresses** — σ = M·y/I, τ = V·Q/(I·b).
5. **Apply partial safety factors** — γ_m (material), γ_f (load).
6. **Check buckling** — Euler (stiffeners) and plate buckling (panels).
7. **Check fatigue** — S-N curve approach (e.g., DNV-RP-C203).
8. **Add corrosion allowance** — typically **0.5 mm to 1.5 mm** for aluminium, **1.0 mm to 3.0 mm** for Cu-Ni in seawater.
9. **Verify deflection & vibration** — SLS criteria.
10. **Check fabrication** — weld procedure specification (WPS), post-weld heat treatment (PWHT), galvanic isolation.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this checklist works for KTU:**
> Examiners reward **structured answers**. A bulleted logical sequence like the above demonstrates Outcome-Based Education (OBE) competencies CO₂ (Design/Development of Solutions) and CO₃ (Modern Tool Usage).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations are tested almost every semester. Memorise the symbols and units.**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | σ_allow = σ_y / FoS | Allowable stress (ASD) | MPa |
| 2 | σ_design = γ_f · σ_actual ≤ σ_y / γ_m | Limit state (PSF) | MPa |
| 3 | σ = M·c \mid I | Bending stress (Euler–Bernoulli) | MPa |
| 4 | τ = V·Q \mid (I·b) | Transverse shear stress | MPa |
| 5 | σ_cr = π²E \mid (L/r)² | Euler column buckling | MPa |
| 6 | σ_cr,plate = k · π²E \mid [12(1−ν²)] · (t/b)² | Plate buckling (simply supported) | MPa |
| 7 | Δ_allow = L \mid 250 to L \mid 300 | Deflection limit (SLS) | mm |
| 8 | S-N: N · S^m = C | Fatigue life (Basquin) | cycles |
| 9 | σ_eq,VM = √(σ² + 3τ²) | Von-Mises equivalent stress | MPa |
| 10 | K_t = σ_max \mid σ_nom | Stress concentration factor | — |
| 11 | SF_buckling = σ_cr \mid (γ_m · σ_actual) | Buckling utilisation factor | — |
| 12 | W_saved = (ρ_steel − ρ_alu) \mid ρ_steel × 100% | Weight saving percentage | % |

> **Note on notation:** The vertical bar symbol has been replaced with `\mid` to render cleanly in LaTeX. In your answer script, use a horizontal fraction bar (—) above the denominator.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These design equations are not academic exercises — they are the **backbone of every classification society rulebook** (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, BV, KR, IRS). A naval architect at Cochin Shipyard or Mazagon Dock calculates, for every longitudinal stiffener, whether:

$$\sigma_{\text{bending}} + \sigma_{\text{axial}} + \sigma_{\text{wave-induced}} \leq \frac{\sigma_{0.2}}{\gamma_m \cdot \gamma_f}$$

A failure to satisfy this inequality means the **vessel will not be classed**, and the insurer will refuse underwriting. The structural design considerations are therefore not optional — they are **legally and commercially binding**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Euler Buckling Load for a Marine Stiffener

**Scenario.** A 5083-O aluminium stiffener of length L, with rectangular cross-section b × t, is built-in at one end and simply supported at the other (effective length factor K = 0.7). The classification society requires the proof of buckling resistance.

**Step 1 — Assumptions.**
The derivation assumes:
- The column is **initially perfectly straight** (idealised geometry).
- The material is **homogeneous and isotropic** (acceptable for rolled aluminium plate).
- The load is **purely axial concentric** (no eccentricity).
- **Small deflection theory** applies (deflection < 0.1 L).

**Step 2 — Differential equation of the deflected column.**

Consider a column AB of length L, hinged at both ends, loaded axially with compressive force P. Let the deflection at distance x from the bottom be y(x). The bending moment at section x is:

$$M(x) = -P \cdot y(x)$$

Using the Euler–Bernoulli beam equation:

$$EI \cdot \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x) = -P \cdot y(x)$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + \frac{P}{EI} \cdot y(x) = 0$$

Let $\omega^2 = P / (EI)$. The general solution is:

$$y(x) = A \cos(\omega x) + B \sin(\omega x)$$

**Step 3 — Apply boundary conditions.**

For a pinned-pinned column:
- At x = 0: y = 0  →  A = 0
- At x = L: y = 0  →  B · sin(ωL) = 0

For a non-trivial solution (B ≠ 0):

$$\sin(\omega L) = 0 \implies \omega L = n\pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$

**Step 4 — Critical (lowest) buckling load.**

The smallest value occurs at n = 1, giving:

$$\omega = \frac{\pi}{L} \implies \frac{P_{cr}}{EI} = \frac{\pi^2}{L^2}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}}$$

Dividing by the cross-sectional area A to obtain critical stress:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{(L/r)^2}$$

where the **radius of gyration** $r = \sqrt{I / A}$.

**Step 5 — Incorporate effective length factor K.**

For non-pinned end conditions, replace L with the effective length $L_e = K \cdot L$:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{(K L / r)^2}$$

**Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example for KTU Exam Practice.**

> **Question.** A 5083-H116 aluminium plate stiffener has:
> - L = 2.0 m, K = 0.7 (one end fixed, one end pinned)
> - Cross-section: b = 80 mm, t = 12 mm
> - E = 70 GPa, σ_y = 215 MPa, FoS = 1.67
> - Axial compressive load: P_actual = 50 kN
>
> *Check if the stiffener satisfies the buckling criterion.*

**Solution.**

Step A — Cross-sectional properties:
$$A = b \cdot t = 80 \times 12 = 960 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$I = \frac{b t^3}{12} = \frac{80 \times 12^3}{12} = 11520 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$r = \sqrt{I/A} = \sqrt{11520 / 960} = \sqrt{12} = 3.464 \text{ mm}$$

Step B — Effective slenderness ratio:
$$L_e = K L = 0.7 \times 2000 = 1400 \text{ mm}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r} = \frac{1400}{3.464} = 404.2$$

Step C — Euler critical stress:
$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 70000}{404.2^2} = \frac{690827}{163378} \approx 4.23 \text{ MPa}$$

Step D — Allowable buckling stress:
$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_{cr}}{FoS} = \frac{4.23}{1.67} \approx 2.53 \text{ MPa}$$

Step E — Actual stress:
$$\sigma_{actual} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{50000}{960} \approx 52.08 \text{ MPa}$$

Step F — Conclusion:
$$\sigma_{actual} = 52.08 \text{ MPa} \; \gg \; \sigma_{allow} = 2.53 \text{ MPa}$$

> **Result:** The stiffener **fails the buckling check** by a factor of ~20. The designer must either increase t, add intermediate stiffeners, or change the section to a bulb-flat or T-profile. ✔ *This is a classic KTU-style problem; expect a 7-mark version in Part B.*

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Von-Mises Equivalent Stress (for combined loading)

For a plate element subjected to in-plane stresses σ_x, σ_y, and shear τ_xy, the **distortion-energy (Von-Mises)** criterion states that yielding begins when:

$$\sigma_{eq,VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 - \sigma_x \sigma_y + 3 \tau_{xy}^2} = \sigma_y$$

**Proof sketch (energy method):**

The total strain energy per unit volume in an elastic body is:
$$U = \frac{1}{2E}\left(\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2\right) - \frac{\nu}{E}\left(\sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y\sigma_z + \sigma_z\sigma_x\right) + \frac{1}{2G}\left(\tau_{xy}^2 + \tau_{yz}^2 + \tau_{zx}^2\right)$$

For a **thin plate**, σ_z ≈ 0, τ_xz ≈ τ_yz ≈ 0, leaving:
$$U = \frac{1}{2E}\left(\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 - 2\nu\sigma_x\sigma_y\right) + \frac{1}{2G}\tau_{xy}^2$$

The **volumetric (dilatational)** part is subtracted to obtain the **distortion energy** U_d, which by Hencky's hypothesis governs yielding. After algebraic manipulation (substituting G = E/[2(1+ν)] and simplifying), one obtains the boxed equation above.

> **Practical KTU use:** When a stiffened aluminium panel is simultaneously loaded by hull-girder bending (σ_x) and hydrostatic pressure (σ_y), the Von-Mises check is mandatory under DNV Pt.3 Ch.4 Sec.6.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Non-Ferrous Alloys vs Design Suitability

| Design Criterion | Al 5083-H116 | Cu-Ni 90/10 | Ti Grade 5 | Bronze (Ni-Al) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Yield strength (MPa)** | 215 | 105 | 880 | 345 |
| **Density (kg/m³)** | 2660 | 8940 | 4430 | 8500 |
| **Specific strength (σ_y/ρ)** | 80.8 kN·m/kg | 11.7 kN·m/kg | 198.6 kN·m/kg | 40.6 kN·m/kg |
| **Seawater corrosion** | Excellent | Outstanding | Outstanding | Excellent |
| **Weldability** | Excellent (GTAW/GMAW) | Good (TIG) | Difficult (atmosphere) | Poor (cast only) |
| **Cost index (steel=1)** | ~3.0 | ~6.5 | ~25 | ~5 |
| **Typical shipboard use** | Superstructure, hull (small craft) | Pipework, condensers | Submarine pressure hulls, fasteners | Propellers, pump casings |
| **Galvanic risk with steel** | High — needs isolation | Moderate | Low (nobler) | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Design Process Flow for Non-Ferrous Ship Structures

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **sequential decision logic** a naval architect follows when designing a non-ferrous structural member.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: Design Brief] --> A2[Define Service Environment]
    A2 --> A3{Seawater Immersion?}
    A3 -- Yes --> A4[Select Cu-Ni or Al-Mg]
    A3 -- No --> A5[Lightweighting Required?]
    A5 -- Yes --> A6[Select Al 5083 or 6082]
    A5 -- No --> A7[Select Ti or Bronze]
    A4 --> A8[Determine Loads ASD or PSF]
    A6 --> A8
    A7 --> A8
    A8 --> A9[Compute Bending and Shear Stresses]
    A9 --> A10[Check Buckling Euler and Plate]
    A10 --> A11[Check Fatigue S-N Curve]
    A11 --> A12[Apply Corrosion Allowance]
    A12 --> A13[Verify Deflection SLS]
    A13 --> A14{All Checks Passed?}
    A14 -- No --> A15[Iterate Section or Alloy]
    A15 --> A8
    A14 -- Yes --> A16[Issue Structural Drawing]
    A16 --> A17[End]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Material Selection Decision Tree for Shipboard Applications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Component Identification] --> B2{Primary Function?}
    B2 -- Pressure Hull --> B3[High Strength + Toughness]
    B3 --> B4[Titanium Grade 5 or Al 7075]
    B2 -- Piping --> B5[Seawater + Thermal]
    B5 --> B6[90/10 or 70/30 Cu-Ni]
    B2 -- Propeller --> B7[Cavitation + Corrosion]
    B7 --> B8[Ni-Al Bronze Cast]
    B2 -- Superstructure --> B9[Lightweight + Weldable]
    B9 --> B10[Al 5083-H116]
    B2 -- Fastener --> B11[Galvanic Compatibility]
    B11 --> B12[Monel or Silicon Bronze]
    B4 --> B13[Final Specification]
    B6 --> B13
    B8 --> B13
    B10 --> B13
    B12 --> B13
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Failure Modes Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Structural Limit States] --> C2[Ultimate Limit State ULS]
    C1 --> C3[Serviceability Limit State SLS]
    C2 --> C4[Material Yielding]
    C2 --> C5[Column Buckling]
    C2 --> C6[Plate Buckling]
    C2 --> C7[Rupture]
    C3 --> C8[Excessive Deflection]
    C3 --> C9[Fatigue Cracking]
    C3 --> C10[Vibration Resonance]
    C3 --> C11[Leak Tightness Loss]
    C4 --> C12[Safety Action: Increase Section]
    C5 --> C12
    C6 --> C13[Safety Action: Add Stiffener]
    C7 --> C14[Safety Action: Change Alloy]
    C8 --> C15[Safety Action: Limit Service Load]
    C9 --> C16[Safety Action: Improve WPS]
    C10 --> C15
    C11 --> C17[Safety Action: Seal Weld]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** Each top-level branch (ULS / SLS) is anchored to a specific clause in IACS S21 (Hull Girder Strength) and S23 (Fatigue). Mentioning these references in a 14-mark answer signals **CO₅ — Ethics & Professional Practice** awareness, which carries bonus impression marks with KTU examiners.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any three structural advantages and two limitations of using aluminium alloys in ship superstructures.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Advantages:** (1) Low density (~2660 kg/m³, reducing upper-deck weight and improving stability), (2) High corrosion resistance in marine atmosphere, (3) Good weldability using MIG/TIG processes for Al-Mg alloys.
- **Limitations:** (1) Low modulus of elasticity (70 GPa, leading to higher deflections and buckling risk), (2) Susceptibility to galvanic corrosion when in contact with steel or copper alloys.
*(Award full 3 marks if at least 3 advantages + 2 limitations are correctly stated with brief justification.)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define "corrosion allowance" and state typical values for mild steel, aluminium 5083, and 90/10 Cu-Ni piping.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition:** Corrosion allowance is the extra thickness of material added to the calculated design thickness to compensate for expected material loss due to corrosion over the vessel's design life.
- **Typical values:** Mild steel (uncoated) = **2.0 mm to 3.0 mm**; Aluminium 5083 = **0.5 mm to 1.0 mm**; Cu-Ni 90/10 = **1.0 mm to 1.5 mm**.
*(Allocate 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three numeric values with units.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students answer EITHER "Question A" OR "Question B" in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** *Discuss the structural design considerations that govern the selection of non-ferrous materials for ship superstructures. Highlight the differences in design philosophy when compared with mild steel.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| S.No. | Consideration | Mild Steel | Non-Ferrous (Al 5083) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Yield strength | 235 MPa | 215 MPa (slightly lower) |
| 2 | Density | 7850 kg/m³ | 2660 kg/m³ (1/3 weight) |
| 3 | Modulus E | 200 GPa | 70 GPa (3× lower — buckling-critical) |
| 4 | Corrosion allowance | 2–3 mm | 0.5–1.0 mm |
| 5 | Welding process | SMAW, FCAW | MIG/TIG essential; preheat not needed |
| 6 | Galvanic isolation | Not required | Mandatory (use rubber gaskets, nylon washers) |
| 7 | Buckling design | Secondary concern | **Primary** concern (low E) |
| 8 | Cost | Low | 2–3× higher |

**Key design philosophy differences:**
1. **Buckling dominance:** Aluminium's low E (70 GPa) means Euler buckling often governs long stiffeners; designers must use **closer stiffener spacing** or **deeper sections**.
2. **Deflection control:** SLS deflection limit (L/250) is harder to meet for aluminium beams.
3. **Fatigue:** Aluminium has **no distinct fatigue limit**; the S-N curve keeps falling. S-N design must be performed for all cyclic loads.
4. **Galvanic compatibility:** Aluminium is **anodic** to most metals; it sacrifices itself to protect steel/copper. This must be electrically isolated.
5. **Weld strength efficiency:** Welded aluminium has only **60–80%** of parent metal strength (vs 100% for steel); joint design becomes critical.
6. **Fire rating:** Aluminium loses strength above **200 °C**; fire-protection coatings are mandatory for certain compartments.
7. **Cost-benefit justification:** Weight saving of ~40% on superstructure translates to a **2–4% increase in payload or speed** — economically justified for ferries and naval vessels.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** *A simply-supported aluminium 5083-H116 plate panel of dimensions 1200 mm × 800 mm × 8 mm is subjected to a uniaxial compressive stress of 60 MPa. Calculate the critical buckling stress and the utilisation factor. Take E = 70 GPa, ν = 0.33, and assume simply supported edges with k = 4.0.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the loading type.**
This is a **plate buckling** problem (not column buckling), governed by:

$$\sigma_{cr,\text{plate}} = \frac{k \pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \cdot \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Identify the smaller dimension (governing).**
The plate buckles about the shorter side (b = 800 mm) as the panel is loaded along the 1200 mm direction. Take b = 800 mm, t = 8 mm.

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$12(1-\nu^2) = 12(1 - 0.33^2) = 12(1 - 0.1089) = 12 \times 0.8911 = 10.6932$$

$$\left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{8}{800}\right)^2 = (0.01)^2 = 0.0001$$

$$\frac{k \pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} = \frac{4.0 \times \pi^2 \times 70000}{10.6932} = \frac{4 \times 9.8696 \times 70000}{10.6932}$$

$$= \frac{2763488}{10.6932} \approx 258440 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Final critical buckling stress.**

$$\sigma_{cr} = 258440 \times 0.0001 = 25.84 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with applied stress.**

Applied: σ_actual = 60 MPa  >>  σ_cr = 25.84 MPa

**Step 6 — Utilisation factor.**

$$UF = \frac{\sigma_{\text{actual}}}{\sigma_{cr}} = \frac{60}{25.84} = 2.32$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion and recommendation.**
Since UF = 2.32 ≫ 1.0, the panel **fails** the buckling check. Recommended remedies: (i) increase t to at least 12 mm, or (ii) add intermediate stiffeners at 400 mm spacing, or (iii) select a higher-strength alloy (e.g., 6082-T6 with σ_y = 260 MPa). *(Award full 7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for computation, 1 for UF, 2 for conclusion + remedy.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 marks]** *Explain the partial safety factor (PSF) method of structural design as recommended by IACS for ship structures. Compare it with the conventional Allowable Stress Design (ASD) method.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**ASD Method (1 mark):**
$$\sigma_{\text{actual}} \leq \sigma_{\text{allow}} = \frac{\sigma_y}{FoS}$$

A single, global Factor of Safety (typically **1.5 to 2.5**) is applied to the material strength. Simple but **lacks granularity** — cannot distinguish between different load types or material uncertainties.

**PSF / LRFD Method (3 marks):**
The design inequality becomes:
$$\sigma_{\text{actual}} \cdot \gamma_f \leq \frac{\sigma_y}{\gamma_m}$$

where:
- $\gamma_f$ = **load factor** (typically 1.2 for still-water, 1.4 for wave, 1.0 for buoyancy)
- $\gamma_m$ = **material factor** (typically 1.05–1.15 for steel, 1.15–1.25 for aluminium, 1.20 for titanium)
- $\gamma_f$ may also include a **partial load combination factor** ψ for combined loads.

**Advantages of PSF (2 marks):**
1. **Probabilistic basis** — each γ is calibrated to a target reliability index β (typically 3.5 for ship hulls).
2. **Different safety levels** for different failure modes (yielding, fatigue, buckling) and different load types.
3. **Economy of design** — the structure is sized to a uniform reliability, not uniformly over-conservative.
4. **Modernity** — adopted in IACS S21, S23, DNV-RU-SHIP, and Lloyd's SSC.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Feature | ASD | PSF / LRFD |
|---|---|---|
| Number of safety factors | 1 | 2 or more |
| Probabilistic basis | Empirical | Code-calibrated |
| Load differentiation | No | Yes |
| Adopted by | Older class rules | IACS, DNV (modern) |

---

**(b) [7 marks]** *A 90/10 copper-nickel pipe (outer diameter 200 mm, wall thickness 6 mm) carries seawater at 4 m/s in a shipboard cooling system. Determine the hoop stress and check if the design satisfies the allowable stress criterion. Take σ_y = 105 MPa, FoS = 2.0.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the governing stress.**
For a thin-walled pressure pipe, the **hoop (circumferential) stress** is dominant:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot D}{2 t}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the internal pressure from velocity (using Bernoulli).**
Assuming the line is pressurised to overcome hydrostatic + frictional head, take a typical design pressure p = **1.0 MPa** (10 bar) for shipboard seawater mains. *(If velocity-based, p = ½ρv²; here v = 4 m/s gives dynamic head ≈ 10 kPa, which is negligible. Use p ≈ 0.8 MPa based on system pump head.)*

Let p = 0.8 MPa = 0.8 N/mm², D = 200 mm, t = 6 mm.

**Step 3 — Compute hoop stress.**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{0.8 \times 200}{2 \times 6} = \frac{160}{12} = 13.33 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Compute allowable stress.**

$$\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \frac{\sigma_y}{FoS} = \frac{105}{2.0} = 52.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Check.**

$$\sigma_h = 13.33 \text{ MPa} \ll \sigma_{\text{allow}} = 52.5 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\text{Utilisation Factor } = \frac{13.33}{52.5} = 0.254 \; (25.4\%)$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion.**
The pipe is **well within safe limits** (UF = 0.25 ≪ 1.0). The wall thickness could even be reduced to 3 mm (t = pD/(2σ_allow) = 0.8×200/(2×52.5) = 1.52 mm minimum), but 6 mm is retained to accommodate **corrosion allowance + erosion allowance** (seawater carries suspended sand). *(Full 7 marks.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in the Euler formula** — KTU examiners *deliberately* allocate 1 mark for "correct unit consistency." Students often write σ_cr = π²E/(L/r)² but forget to convert E from GPa to MPa.
> 2. **Confusing column buckling with plate buckling** — Column uses L/r (length/radius of gyration). Plate uses t/b (thickness/width) with a different formula and a *k* factor. Many students interchange these, losing **2 to 3 marks** outright.
> 3. **Omitting the corrosion allowance** — The design thickness t_design = t_calc + t_corrosion. Forgetting t_corrosion results in an under-thick design and is a serious professional error (loss of 2 marks).
> 4. **Ignoring galvanic isolation** — In any question combining aluminium with steel, students must explicitly mention **insulating gaskets/washers** to avoid full-mark deduction.
> 5. **Using ASD in a limit-state paper** — If the question says "as per IACS unified rules," use the **PSF/LRFD** form; using the older ASD form loses 1–2 marks for outdated methodology.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** — Euler buckling assumes "initially perfect column" and "small deflection." Failure to state these assumptions in 14-mark answers costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist (print and pin to your wall):**

- [x] **Definition:** Structural Design Considerations = load analysis + material selection + safety verification for non-ferrous ship structures.
- [x] **Three Justifications for Non-Ferrous Use:** weight saving, corrosion resistance, special properties (non-magnetic, thermal, non-sparking).
- [x] **Density Hierarchy (lightest → heaviest):** Magnesium (~1740) < Aluminium 5083 (2660) < Titanium (4430) < Steel (7850) < Bronze (8500) < 90/10 Cu-Ni (8940) kg/m³.
- [x] **Modulus Hierarchy (stiffest → least stiff):** Steel (200) > Ti (114) > Bronze (~100) > Cu-Ni (130) > Aluminium (70) GPa.
- [x] **Specific Strength Winner:** **Titanium Grade 5** (~199 kN·m/kg) — strongest non-ferrous per unit weight.
- [x] **Corrosion Allowance Defaults:** Steel 2–3 mm; Al 0.5–1.0 mm; Cu-Ni 1.0–1.5 mm.
- [x] **Two Buckling Modes:** Euler (column) uses π²E/(L/r)²; Plate uses k·π²E/[12(1−ν²)]·(t/b)².
- [x] **Design Philosophies:** ASD (single FoS) vs PSF/LRFD (separate γ_f and γ_m).
- [x] **Fatigue:** Aluminium and titanium have **no endurance limit** — design S-N curve required.
- [x] **SLS Deflection Limit:** L/250 to L/300 for ship plating.
- [x] **Von-Mises criterion** governs yielding under combined stresses.
- [x] **Galvanic Series:** Aluminium is anodic (sacrificial) to steel and copper — always isolate.
- [x] **Weld Strength Efficiency:** Al welded joint ≈ 60–80% of parent metal; design accordingly.
- [x] **Fire Concern:** Al loses ~50% strength above 200 °C — fire-protection mandatory in certain compartments.
- [x] **IACS Reference:** S21 (Hull Girder), S23 (Fatigue), S11 (Longitudinal Strength) for non-ferrous substitution.
- [x] **DNV Reference:** DNV-RU-SHIP Pt.3 Ch.4 (Hull Structural Design — Aluminium).
- [x] **Lloyd's Reference:** Lloyd's Register SSC Rules for Special Service Craft (aluminium vessels).

> **Final KTU Tip:** In every 14-mark answer, open with the **definition**, present the **formula** in a box, show the **substitution**, state the **result with units**, and close with a **design recommendation** or **engineering justification**. This is the 5-step structure that consistently scores 13–14/14 in KTU ESE papers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welding and Fabrication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Welding and Fabrication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Welding in Shipbuilding — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Welding** is a **fusion-based (or solid-state) joining process** in which two or more non-ferrous metal pieces are permanently bonded through the application of heat, pressure, or both, with or without a filler material, to produce a metallurgically continuous joint whose mechanical, chemical, and corrosion properties are restored across the interface.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context (PENST523), welding is treated as the *primary fabrication route* for marine non-ferrous structures because the **hull, superstructure, piping, propellers, heat exchangers, and cryogenic tanks** of modern ships are largely built from **aluminium alloys (Al-Mg, Al-Mg-Si, Al-Zn), copper-nickel (Cu-Ni 90/10, 70/30), titanium (Grade 2, Grade 5 / Ti-6Al-4V), nickel-aluminium bronze (NAB), and austenitic stainless steels (SS 304L, 316L)**. The Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) classification **IS 812** and the **American Bureau of Shipping (ABS), DNV, Lloyd's Register, and IACS UR W28** rules govern the qualification of welding procedures for these alloys.

**Fabrication**, in contrast, is the *broader umbrella term* that includes:
1. **Cutting** (plasma, laser, water-jet, shearing)
2. **Forming** (rolling, bending, press-braking, spinning)
3. **Machining** (milling, drilling, turning of non-ferrous castings/forgings)
4. **Assembly** (fit-up, tack welding, jigging)
5. **Welding & joining** (arc, resistance, solid-state, brazing, soldering)
6. **Inspection & testing** (NDT, mechanical, metallurgical)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (the "Lego + Glue" idea):**
> Imagine building a giant ship hull out of two different coloured Lego sets — one set made of **aluminium-bronze blocks** and the other of **marine-grade aluminium blocks**. The blocks do not naturally snap together with the same mechanical integrity as identical blocks. **Welding is the "metallurgical glue"** that *melts and merges* the contact faces so that the joint becomes as strong (ideally, stronger) than the parent blocks themselves. The **weld metal** is the new material that solidifies in the joint, the **heat-affected zone (HAZ)** is the region of the original block that got warm enough to microstructurally change, and the **fusion zone (FZ)** is where melting actually occurred. In shipbuilding, because ship hulls are huge curved plates (often 6–25 m long), **welding replaces riveting** as the dominant joining method — it saves **15–25 % weight**, eliminates leak paths, and distributes stress uniformly.

## 1.2 Classification of Welding Processes Used for Non-Ferrous Marine Alloys

| Family | Process | Acronym | Typical Use in Shipbuilding |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Arc — Non-Consumable** | Gas Tungsten Arc Welding | **GTAW (TIG)** | Root pass & critical joints in Al / Ti / Cu-Ni |
| **Arc — Consumable** | Gas Metal Arc Welding | **GMAW (MIG)** | High-deposition fill passes, thick plate Al hulls |
| **Arc — Pulsed** | Pulsed GMAW | **P-GMAW** | Thin aluminium superstructure, out-of-position welds |
| **Arc — Plasma** | Plasma Arc Welding | **PAW** | Precision joints in titanium piping & cryogenic tanks |
| **Arc — Stud** | Stud Arc Welding | **SW** | Fixing studs on aluminium deck plates |
| **Resistance** | Resistance Spot / Seam | **RSW / RSEW** | Sheet-metal fabrication, mast sections |
| **Solid-State** | Friction Stir Welding | **FSW** | Long aluminium hull panels, decks, bulkheads |
| **Solid-State** | Friction Stud Welding | **FRSW** | Repair welds in aluminium superstructure |
| **High-Energy Beam** | Electron Beam | **EBW** | Pressure-vessel diaphragms, special steels |
| **High-Energy Beam** | Laser Beam | **LBW** | Cladding, sealing, precision aerospace-maritime parts |
| **Hybrid** | Laser-MAG Hybrid | **LB-MAG** | Modern high-speed aluminium shipbuilding lines |
| **Oxy-Fuel (limited)** | Oxy-Acetylene | **OAW** | Only for copper & bronze small parts / repair |
| **Underwater** | Hyperbaric Wet/Dry | **UHW / UHD** | Emergency hull repair, propeller-shaft brazing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why so many processes?** Because non-ferrous marine alloys have *very different* thermal conductivities, oxide chemistries, and solidification behaviours. A process that works perfectly for aluminium (high thermal conductivity, refractory oxide) is **completely unsuitable** for titanium (reactive at >400 °C, needs inert shroud). KTU examiners **love** asking "which process would you choose for X alloy and why?" — always answer with **(a) process name, (b) shielding gas, (c) filler, (d) heat input range**.

## 1.3 Key Material-Specific Challenges When Welding Non-Ferrous Marine Alloys

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table must be memorised; it is the **#1 KTU Part-A (3 mark) favourite**.

| Alloy System | Critical Welding Issue | Engineering Counter-Measure |
|---|---|---|
| **Aluminium & its alloys (5xxx, 6xxx)** | (i) Refractory Al₂O₃ oxide film (mp ≈ 2072 °C) (ii) High thermal conductivity (≈ 160–230 W/m·K) (iii) Hot cracking in 6xxx | AC-GTAW with **He/Ar** ; high travel speed; **4xxx-series filler (Al-Si)** or **5xxx (Al-Mg)** |
| **Copper & Cu-Ni (90/10, 70/30)** | (i) Very high thermal conductivity (≈ 100–400 W/m·K) (ii) Hot shortness above 400 °C | Pre-heat to **150–600 °C**; high heat input; **Cu-Sn (ERCuSn-A)** filler; use backing gas |
| **Titanium (Gr 2, Gr 5)** | (i) Embrittlement by O, N, H above 400 °C (ii) Severe loss of corrosion resistance | Trailing shield + backing shield of **pure Ar/He**; ERTi-2 filler for Gr 2; cleanliness < 50 ppm O₂ |
| **Nickel-Aluminium Bronze (NAB)** | (i) Complex 4-phase microstructure (α, β, κᵢ, κᵢᵢᵢ) (ii) Hot cracking susceptibility | Pre-heat 150–250 °C; ER CuAl-A filler; interpass < 150 °C; PWHT 600–650 °C optional |
| **Austenitic SS 304L/316L** | (i) Sensitisation & intergranular corrosion (ii) Weld decay | Use **L-grade** (low C ≤ 0.03 %); ER308L / ER316L filler; avoid prolonged HAZ exposure |
| **Magnesium alloys (AZ31, AZ91)** | (i) Flammability (ii) High chemical reactivity | Special fluxes; TIG with **He-rich** shielding; inert-atmosphere chamber welding |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Arc Welding Physics

Every arc-welding process — whether it is **GTAW, GMAW, PAW, or Stick (SMAW)** — obeys the same four physical pillars. **Master these and you can answer 80 % of welding questions.**

### Pillar 1 — The Welding Arc
The arc is a **sustained electrical discharge** between the **electrode (cathode/anode)** and the **workpiece**. For a stable arc we need:
- **Voltage (V)**: 10–35 V for GTAW/GMAW
- **Current (I)**: 50–500 A depending on plate thickness
- **Arc length / gap (l)**: 1.5–4 mm for TIG; 6–13 mm stick-out for MIG
- **Ionised gas column temperature**: 6 000 – 20 000 K (core plasma)
- **Heat distribution**: in **GTAW**, ~70 % heat at workpiece, ~30 % at electrode (when electrode is negative — *DCEN / straight polarity*). In **GMAW**, ~70 % heat at workpiece when electrode is **positive (DCEP / reverse polarity)**.

### Pillar 2 — Heat Input (the single most important KTU parameter)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat input** is the energy delivered per unit length of weld. It is the master variable controlling the **HAZ width, grain size, distortion, and residual stress**. Too low → lack of fusion; too high → grain coarsening, hot cracking, burn-through.

### Pillar 3 — Shielding Gas Function
Shielding gas does **three jobs**:
1. **Atmospheric exclusion** — prevents O₂, N₂, H₂O vapour from reacting with molten pool.
2. **Arc plasma modifier** — He increases arc temperature & voltage; Ar gives smooth stable arc; CO₂/O₂/CO₂ mixes increase penetration in MIG.
3. **Heat-transfer control** — He/Ar blends widen the heat distribution; useful for Al, Cu, Ti (high thermal conductivity).

### Pillar 4 — Weld Metal Solidification & HAZ
As the arc moves on, the molten pool **solidifies epitaxially** from the fusion boundary inward, forming a **columnar dendritic grain structure**. The HAZ experiences sub-solidus thermal cycles that cause:
- **Grain growth** (aluminium, titanium)
- **Sensitisation** (austenitic SS)
- **Age-hardening dissolution & re-precipitation** (6xxx Al, 17-4 PH SS)
- **Phase transformation** (Ti α → β → α')

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **board-exam verifiable**. KTU examiners routinely ask for derivations or numerical substitutions of any of these.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heat Input (arc processes)** | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | **kJ/mm** | Energy per mm of weld; $\eta$ = process efficiency |
| 2 | Process efficiency $\eta$ | GTAW = 0.6 ; GMAW = 0.8 ; SAW = 1.0 ; SMAW = 0.85 | dimensionless | Fraction of arc energy that enters workpiece |
| 3 | **Heat input from travel speed** | $S = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{H \cdot 1000}$ | **mm/min** | Required travel speed for desired H |
| 4 | **Deposition rate (GMAW/SAW)** | $D_{rate} = \dfrac{I \cdot k}{S}$ (with k = empirical constant) | **kg/h** | kg of weld metal deposited per hour |
| 5 | **Power density** | $P_d = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{A_{arc}}$ | **W/mm²** | Determines penetration mode (conduction vs keyhole) |
| 6 | Cooling rate (3D heat flow, thick plate) | $t_{8/5} = \dfrac{q'}{2\pi \lambda c_p \rho} \left[\dfrac{1}{(T_c - T_0)^2} - \dfrac{1}{(T_p - T_0)^2}\right]^{-1}$ | seconds | Critical for HAZ microstructure |
| 7 | Weld Dilution | $\%D = \dfrac{A_{BM}}{A_{BM} + A_{FW}} \times 100$ | **%** | % of base metal in weld pool |
| 8 | Theoretical deposit from wire | $W = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot v_f \cdot \rho \cdot t$ | **kg** | kg of filler consumed in time *t* |
| 9 | Filler wire feed rate (MIG) | $v_f = \dfrac{I \cdot k_1}{d^2}$ | **m/min** | Required wire feed for a given current |
| 10 | **Weld shrinkage (transverse)** | $\Delta w = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{c \cdot \rho \cdot S \cdot 1000} \cdot \alpha$ | mm | Width loss from solidification |
| 11 | **Heat-affected zone width (empirical)** | $w_{HAZ} = \dfrac{k \cdot H^{n}}{v^{m}}$ | mm | Depends on process-specific constants |
| 12 | **Arc force (plasma jet)** | $F_{arc} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I^2}{8\pi^2 r^2}$ | **N** | Force pushing molten pool downward |

> Where $V$ = arc voltage (V), $I$ = current (A), $S$ = travel speed (mm/min), $\eta$ = process efficiency, $\lambda$ = thermal conductivity, $c_p$ = specific heat, $\rho$ = density, $T_c$ = cooling start temp, $T_p$ = peak temp, $T_0$ = initial plate temp, $d$ = wire diameter, $v_f$ = wire feed rate, $\alpha$ = thermal expansion coefficient, $q'$ = heat input per unit length (J/mm), $A_{arc}$ = arc spot area, $A_{BM}$ = base-melted area, $A_{FW}$ = filler-melted area.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Marine & Shipbuilding Engineering

- **Heat input limits** for aluminium ship hulls (per **IACS UR W28 / DNV**): maximum H = **1.0 kJ/mm** for 5xxx series, **0.8 kJ/mm** for 6xxx to avoid HAZ softening & hot cracking.
- **Pre-heat tables** for Cu-Ni 90/10 piping: 150 °C for 3 mm, 300 °C for 6 mm, 600 °C for > 10 mm (per **ASME B31.3** & **DEF STAN 02-781 Part 3**).
- **Filler selection logic** (KTU favourite): *match strength first → match corrosion second → match colour third* — but in marine Cu-Ni service, **over-matching filler (Cu-Ni 70/30 to weld 90/10 plate)** is sometimes used to control dilution cracking.
- **Welding procedure qualification**: every shipyard must qualify its WPS per **ISO 15614-1** (steel) / **ISO 15614-2** (Al) / **ASME Section IX**. A single WPS is specific to (a) base metal PQR group, (b) thickness range, (c) joint type, (d) process, (e) shielding gas, (f) heat input window.
- **FSW in modern shipbuilding** — the Royal Navy **Type 26 Global Combat Ship** uses FSW aluminium deckhouses (saves 20 % weight vs riveted), the **US Navy Littoral Combat Ship** uses FSW for its 7000-series aluminium hull panels.

---

## 2.4 Shielding Gas Selection Logic (Board-Review Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this for the 14-mark question.** A KTU 14-mark question will usually test your ability to justify a shielding-gas choice for a given non-ferrous alloy.

| Alloy | Primary Gas | Secondary / Backing | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Aluminium alloys (5xxx, 6xxx)** | **100 % Ar** (GTAW) or **Ar + 30–75 % He** (GMAW) | **Argon** backing on root | Ar stable arc, He adds heat for high-cond. metal |
| **Titanium** | **100 % Ar** (GTAW), with trailing shield + backing shield | Same | Prevent O / N pickup; brightness test passes if straw/silver coloured |
| **Copper / Cu-Ni** | **100 % Ar** (thin); **Ar + 25–50 % He** (thick) | Argon backing on root | He compensates for extreme thermal conductivity |
| **Nickel-Aluminium Bronze** | **100 % Ar** | Argon backing | Avoids Sn / Zn loss; minimises porosity |
