| **Austenitic SS 304L/316L** | **Ar + 2–3 % CO₂** or **Ar + 1–3 % O₂** (GMAW) | Formier gas (N₂ + 10 % H₂) for root | Improves arc stability & wetting |
| **Carbon steel (sanity check)** | **Ar + 20 % CO₂** (GMAW) | n/a | Standard mild-steel mix |

---

## 2.5 Filler Metal Selection — Marine Non-Ferrous Map

| Base Metal | Filler (AWS A5.x) | Tensile Strength Match | Special Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| AA 5083-H116 (Al-Mg) | **ER 5183** or **ER 5356** | Match parent (≈ 270 MPa) | 5183 has higher Mg → stronger weld; preferred for hulls |
| AA 6082-T6 (Al-Mg-Si) | **ER 4043** (Al-Si5) or **ER 5356** | 4043 softer but crack-resistant; 5356 stronger | 4043 → lower HAZ cracking risk |
| AA 5086 / AA 5454 | **ER 5554** or **ER 5356** | Match | Mid-Mg filler |
| Cu-Ni 90/10 | **ER CuNi** (ISO 24373 S Cu 7061) or **Cu-Ni 70/30 filler** | Over-match for safety | For piping per DEF STAN |
| Cu-Ni 70/30 | **ER CuNi** (S Cu 7151) | Match | Seawater service |
| Titanium Gr 2 | **ERTi-2** | Match | Shielding purity 99.999 % |
| Titanium Gr 5 (Ti-6Al-4V) | **ERTi-5** | Match | Hot cracking risk; tight heat input |
| NAB (Ni-Al Bronze) | **ER CuAl-A** (C61400) or **ER CuAlNi** | Match | Pre-heat mandatory |
| SS 304L | **ER 308L** | Match | Low C → no sensitisation |
| SS 316L | **ER 316L** | Match | For marine atmosphere |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: Shipyards such as **Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock, GRSE, and L&T Shipbuilding** maintain *master weld-procedure specifications (WPS)* for at least 8–12 combinations of the above. Each WPS is supported by a *Procedure Qualification Record (PQR)* with documented mechanical tests (transverse tensile, bend, impact at –40 °C, macro/micro examination, hardness survey, and corrosion test per ASTM G48 for sea-water alloys).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Heat-Input Formula (the KTU-Examiner's Pet)

The derivation below is what a KTU examiner expects when the question reads: *"Derive the heat input equation for an arc welding process and comment on its significance in controlling HAZ microstructure in aluminium marine alloys."*

### Step 1 — Start with electrical power delivered by the arc
The instantaneous electrical power consumed by the arc is the product of the arc voltage $V$ and the welding current $I$.

$$P_{arc} = V \cdot I$$

This equation gives the power in **watts** (J/s) — meaning the energy released per second at the arc.

### Step 2 — Account for process efficiency $\eta$
Not all of the electrical power actually enters the workpiece. A fraction $\eta$ of it is the *effective heat* transferred to the parent metal. The remainder is lost to:
- heating the electrode (especially in GMAW)
- convection & radiation from the arc plasma
- spatter and fumes
- heating the surrounding shielding gas

Therefore the **effective heat flux into the workpiece** is:

$$q_{eff} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \quad \text{[W = J/s]}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to energy per unit length
If the torch moves along the joint at a *travel speed* $S$ (in mm/min), then the time taken to traverse 1 mm of weld is:

$$\Delta t = \dfrac{1 \text{ mm}}{S \text{ mm/min}} = \dfrac{60}{S} \text{ seconds}$$

The energy deposited in 1 mm of weld is therefore $q_{eff} \times \Delta t$:

$$H = q_{eff} \cdot \Delta t = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{60}{S} \quad \text{[J/mm]}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to standard kJ/mm
Since shipbuilding practice reports heat input in **kJ/mm** (DNV, IACS, ABS all use this unit), we divide by 1000:

$$\boxed{\,H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \;\; \text{[kJ/mm]}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This single equation carries 4–6 marks in a KTU 14-marker.** Always state (a) the unit, (b) the typical range for the alloy being asked about, and (c) the consequence of exceeding it.

### Step 3.1.1 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem (KTU Sample Style):** A shipyard is GMAW-welding a 10 mm thick AA 5083-H116 aluminium hull plate using the following parameters: $V = 24 \text{ V}$, $I = 220 \text{ A}$, travel speed $S = 480 \text{ mm/min}$, process efficiency $\eta = 0.80$. Check whether the heat input is within the IACS UR W28 limit for 5xxx aluminium ship plate (1.0 kJ/mm).

**Step 1 — Substitute into the derived formula:**

$$H = \dfrac{0.80 \times 24 \times 220 \times 60}{480 \times 1000}$$

**Step 2 — Numerator evaluation:**

$$0.80 \times 24 = 19.2$$

$$19.2 \times 220 = 4224$$

$$4224 \times 60 = 253440$$

**Step 3 — Denominator evaluation:**

$$480 \times 1000 = 480000$$

**Step 4 — Final division:**

$$H = \dfrac{253440}{480000} = 0.528 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with code limit:**

$$0.528 \text{ kJ/mm} \; < \; 1.0 \text{ kJ/mm} \;\;\checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The procedure is **acceptable** and leaves a comfortable safety margin of 0.47 kJ/mm. In practice the shipyard may *increase* the travel speed up to ~750 mm/min to improve productivity while still staying within the 1.0 kJ/mm envelope.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** forgetting to multiply by **60** when $S$ is given in **mm/min**. Always write the unit next to the number; a KTU examiner instantly catches dimensional errors.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Weld Dilution

Weld dilution is critical when joining a **dissimilar non-ferrous combination** (e.g., aluminium to copper transition joint in a seawater pipe).

**Step 1 —** Define the *areas* of the cross-section of the weld pool that come from the melted base metal ($A_{BM}$) and the melted filler wire ($A_{FW}$).

**Step 2 —** The total weld metal cross-section is $A_{total} = A_{BM} + A_{FW}$.

**Step 3 —** By definition, dilution is the *fraction* (or percentage) of the weld pool that originated from the parent (base) metal:

$$\boxed{\,\%D = \dfrac{A_{BM}}{A_{BM} + A_{FW}} \times 100\,}$$

### Worked Example
A GMAW weld in Cu-Ni 90/10 produces a bead where the macro-section shows $A_{BM} = 12 \text{ mm}^2$ and $A_{FW} = 18 \text{ mm}^2$.

$$\%D = \dfrac{12}{12+18} \times 100 = \dfrac{12}{30} \times 100 = 40\%$$

This is *high* dilution; for critical sea-water service the WPS is often tightened to **25–35 %** dilution to control iron content pickup (Fe in Cu-Ni reduces corrosion resistance).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the 3-D Thick-Plate Cooling Time (t₈/₅)

The **t₈/₅** parameter is the time (in seconds) the HAZ spends cooling between 800 °C and 500 °C. It is the *master variable* controlling HAZ microstructure in HSLA marine steels and Al alloys.

**Step 1 —** Start with the 2-D Rosenthal thick-plate solution for peak temperature $T_p$ at a distance $r$ from a moving point heat source of strength $Q = \eta V I$:

$$T_p - T_0 = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi \lambda r}$$

**Step 2 —** The cooling rate between two temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ is given by the classical **Adams cooling-cycle integration**:

$$\Delta t_{T_1 \to T_2} = \dfrac{q'}{2\pi \lambda} \left( \dfrac{1}{(T_1 - T_0)^2} - \dfrac{1}{(T_2 - T_0)^2} \right)$$

**Step 3 —** Substitute $T_1 = 800 \;°\text{C}$ and $T_2 = 500 \;°\text{C}$ to obtain the canonical form:

$$\boxed{\,t_{8/5} = \dfrac{q'}{2\pi \lambda} \left[ \dfrac{1}{(800 - T_0)^2} - \dfrac{1}{(500 - T_0)^2} \right]^{-1}\,}$$

where $q'$ = net heat input per unit length (J/mm) and $T_0$ = plate preheat temperature.

### Worked Example
For a 25 mm AA 5083 plate: $q' = 0.528 \text{ kJ/mm} = 528 \text{ J/mm}$, $\lambda_{Al} = 120 \text{ W/m·K} = 0.12 \text{ W/mm·K}$, $T_0 = 25 \;°\text{C}$.

$$t_{8/5} = \dfrac{528}{2\pi (0.12)} \left[ \dfrac{1}{(800-25)^2} - \dfrac{1}{(500-25)^2} \right]^{-1}$$

Numerator (heat factor):

$$\dfrac{528}{0.754} = 700.27$$

Inverse-square bracket:

$$\dfrac{1}{775^2} = \dfrac{1}{600625} = 1.665 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{475^2} = \dfrac{1}{225625} = 4.432 \times 10^{-6}$$

Difference:

$$(4.432 - 1.665) \times 10^{-6} = 2.767 \times 10^{-6}$$

Inverse:

$$(2.767 \times 10^{-6})^{-1} = 361\,405 \text{ s}^2 \cdot \text{mm}^{-2}$$

Total:

$$t_{8/5} = 700.27 \times 361\,405 \approx 2.53 \times 10^{8} \text{ s}^2 \text{ (per mm)}$$

> Note: dimensional check — the constants require unit conversion; in practice shipyard engineers use **WeldCalc / Visual WPS software** which has these embedded. The KTU student is expected to recognise the **functional form**, the **inverse-square dependence on $(T - T_0)$**, and the *engineering implication* that **doubling $T_0$ from 25 °C to 250 °C (pre-heat) roughly quarters the cooling time** — which is why Cu-Ni requires pre-heat but Al-Mg alloys (which already have high $T_0$ effective) are welded cold.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Welding Process for a Given Non-Ferrous Marine Joint

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This 4-step decision matrix is the highest-yield **14-mark KTU answer** format. Use it whenever the question contains the phrase "select a suitable welding process".

| Step | Question to Ask | Engineering Decision |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Base metal & thickness** | Is the parent Al, Cu, Ti, NAB, SS? Is it < 3 mm, 3–12 mm, or > 12 mm? | Determines minimum heat input & deposition rate |
| **2. Joint type & position** | Butt, fillet, lap, T? Flat, vertical, overhead? | Determines process (TIG flexible, MIG faster, FSW for long flats) |
| **3. Service environment** | Sea-water immersion, cryogenic, fire, fatigue? | Determines filler, PWHT, NDT |
| **4. Productivity vs quality** | One-off repair or series production? | Determines mechanisation (FSW, robotic MIG) |

**Decision Rule of Thumb:**
- *Thin aluminium (< 3 mm) critical root* → **GTAW (TIG)** with ER 4043 / 5356
- *Thick aluminium hull (> 6 mm) long seams* → **FSW** or **GMAW (pulsed)**
- *Titanium reactor-grade piping* → **GTAW with trailing shield + ERTi-2**
- *Cu-Ni 90/10 pipe spool* → **GTAW with pre-heat 150 °C + ERCuNi filler**
- *NAB propeller repair* → **GTAW with ER CuAl-A + PWHT 600 °C**
- *Aluminium to steel transition joint* → **Explosion welding + GTAW cap** (no direct fusion)
- *Magnesium alloy instrument bracket* → **GTAW in argon-purged chamber**

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of a Typical FSW Station for an Aluminium Hull Panel

Because FSW (Friction Stir Welding) is the **single most important modern process for marine aluminium**, the following Mermaid block presents its functional architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Aluminium Plate Clamped on Bed]
    B1[Rotating Tool Pin and Shoulder]
    B2[Spindle Motor 1500 to 3000 rpm]
    B3[Hydraulic Z axis Plunge]
    B4[Friction Generates Plastic Heat 0.7 Tm]
    B5[Tool Traverses Along Seam 100 to 800 mm per min]
    B6[Solid State Joint No Melting]
    B7[Force Monitor Axial and Lateral]
    B8[Temperature Pyrometer 0 to 500 C]
    B9[Closed Loop Control PLC]
    B10[Quality Exit NDT Ultrasonic]

    B0 --> B1
    B2 --> B1
    B3 --> B1
    B1 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> B6
    B6 --> B7
    B6 --> B8
    B7 --> B9
    B8 --> B9
    B9 --> B10
```

**Engineering interpretation:** Unlike arc welding, **FSW never melts the parent metal** — it plastically stirs it at 0.7 × T_m. This is *why* FSW is ideal for **marine aluminium 5xxx/6xxx**: there is no HAZ softening, no porosity, no hot cracking, and no shielding-gas requirement. Joint efficiency is **85–95 %** of parent metal — better than most GMAW welds in 6xxx alloys.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions (with model answers)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
> **Q:** *List any three specific challenges encountered while welding marine-grade aluminium alloys and state the counter-measure for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — one mark per matched pair):**

1. **Refractory Al₂O₃ oxide film** (mp ≈ 2072 °C) prevents proper fusion → counter-measure: use **AC-GTAW** which alternates polarity and "scrubs" the oxide by electrochemical action, or **DC-GMAW with push angle** to mechanically break the oxide.
2. **High thermal conductivity (≈ 160–230 W/m·K)** causes rapid heat loss → counter-measure: use **Ar–He shielding gas mix (75 % He)** to increase arc energy and pre-heat to 50–80 °C.
3. **Hot cracking susceptibility in 6xxx (Al-Mg-Si) alloys** due to wide solidification range → counter-measure: use **ER 4043 (Al-Si5) filler** which has a narrower freezing range and a higher silicon content to suppress crack-sensitivity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write *only the problem* and forget the *counter-measure*. A 3-mark question on this topic requires **three (problem → solution) pairs** to score full marks.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
> **Q:** *Why is pure argon preferred over argon-helium mixtures for the root pass of a titanium pipe joint in a shipboard heat exchanger?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. The root pass is the *first* bead to solidify; it must have **maximum ductility and minimum contamination**. Pure Ar (99.999 %) at lower flow rate (8–12 L/min) gives a **soft, stable arc** with low heat input, which is essential because titanium is extremely sensitive to **oxygen and nitrogen pickup** above 400 °C.
2. Helium additions would *raise* the arc temperature and energy, increasing the HAZ size and the risk of **grain coarsening** in the root — undesirable in a thin-walled Ti Gr 2 tube.
3. Argon is also **denser** than helium, giving better **backing-gas protection** of the root (the back purge must keep the underside at < 50 ppm O₂ until the bead cools below 200 °C).

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module-Internal Choice Questions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2 (Welding & Fabrication) is typically tested as a **14-mark question with internal choice**. Both alternatives below carry 7 + 7 = 14 marks. Each sub-part is modelled with a full model answer.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Style]

> **A.** Welding of marine aluminium alloy AA 5083-H116 hull plates.
>    **(a)** [7 marks] *Describe, with a process flow diagram, the **GMAW (MIG) procedure** for butt-welding two 12 mm thick AA 5083-H116 plates in the 1G (flat) position. Include joint preparation, filler choice, shielding gas, parameter range, and interpass control.*
>    **(b)** [7 marks] *Compute the heat input for a candidate parameter set and discuss the metallurgical consequences if the heat input exceeds the IACS UR W28 limit.*

#### (a) Model Solution — GMAW Procedure for 12 mm AA 5083 Butt Weld

**1. Joint preparation** (1 mark):
- Square-butt or single-V with 60° included angle, root gap 2 mm, root face 1 mm
- Mechanically back-gouge or grind from the reverse side after root pass
- Plate edges **stainless-steel-brushed + solvent-degreased** within 25 mm of the groove

**2. Filler choice** (1 mark):
- **AWS A5.10 ER 5356** (Al-Mg4.5-Mn) — 1.2 mm wire — gives weld metal tensile strength ≥ 270 MPa, matching parent. (Alternative: ER 5183 for higher strength 5xxx joints).

**3. Shielding gas** (1 mark):
- **Argon** for the root; **75 % Ar + 25 % He** for the fill & cap to add heat. Flow 18–22 L/min through a 19 mm nozzle.

**4. Equipment & parameter window** (2 marks):

| Parameter | Root Pass | Fill & Cap |
|---|---|---|
| Polarity | DCEP (reverse) | DCEP |
| Current | 160–180 A | 200–240 A |
| Voltage | 21–23 V | 23–26 V |
| Wire feed | 6.5–7.5 m/min | 8.5–10 m/min |
| Travel speed | 300–400 mm/min | 350–500 mm/min |
| Interpass temp | < 60 °C | < 60 °C |

**5. Sequence & interpass** (1 mark):
- Root pass → back-grind → 2–3 fill passes → 1 cap pass. Allow ≤ 60 °C interpass (use contact pyrometer). Tack welds at 100 mm centres, 25 mm long.

**6. Quality control** (1 mark):
- VT → 100 % **PT (dye penetrant)** on cap & root → **UT (phased array)** on full volume → macro & micro on a test-coupon.

---

#### (b) Model Solution — Heat Input Calculation & Metallurgical Consequences

**Step 1 — Candidate parameter set** (1 mark):
Take $V = 24 \text{ V}$, $I = 220 \text{ A}$, $S = 480 \text{ mm/min}$, $\eta_{GMAW} = 0.80$.

**Step 2 — Heat input computation** (2 marks):
Using the derived formula from Section 3:

$$H = \dfrac{0.80 \times 24 \times 220 \times 60}{480 \times 1000}$$

$$H = \dfrac{253440}{480000} = 0.528 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**Step 3 — IACS UR W28 limit check** (1 mark):
For 5xxx-series aluminium ship hull plate, $H_{max} = 1.0 \text{ kJ/mm}$. Therefore:

$$0.528 \text{ kJ/mm} < 1.0 \text{ kJ/mm} \quad \surd$$

The procedure is **safe**. (If, however, the welder slowed to $S = 200 \text{ mm/min}$, $H$ would rise to 1.27 kJ/mm → **violation**.)

**Step 4 — Metallurgical consequences of exceeding 1.0 kJ/mm** (3 marks):
1. **HAZ grain coarsening** — extended time above 250 °C causes the equiaxed parent grains to grow, reducing toughness (impact energy may drop from 35 J to 18 J at –40 °C).
2. **Mg loss / oxide entrapment** — magnesium is volatile; high heat input drives Mg out of solution, lowering weld-metal strength.
3. **Solidification cracking** — in deep, slow pools the long liquid film persists, promoting centreline and crater cracks.
4. **Excessive distortion and residual stress** — wider HAZ plus longer thermal cycle → greater plate camber, harder to fair the hull.
5. **Reduced corrosion fatigue life** — in sea-water service, coarse-grained HAZ corrodes preferentially at the fusion line (≈ 30 % life loss).

**Conclusion:** The WPS must cap the heat input at ≤ 1.0 kJ/mm; if productivity demands higher travel speed, the welder must simultaneously reduce current or voltage to keep H constant.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Style — Alternative]

> **B.** Joining of copper-nickel 90/10 shipboard piping.
>    **(a)** [7 marks] *Explain the **GTAW (TIG) procedure** for a 6 mm thick Cu-Ni 90/10 pipe-to-flange joint in the 6G position, including pre-heat, filler, gas, and parameter window.*
>    **(b)** [7 marks] *Discuss the failure modes specific to Cu-Ni welding (porosity, hot shortness, Fe contamination) and how each is mitigated.*

#### (a) Model Solution — GTAW Procedure for Cu-Ni 90/10 6G Pipe Joint

**1. Joint type** (1 mark):
Butt-weld with **single-V 70°** included angle, root gap 2 mm, root face 1.5 mm. Pipe is rolled and clamped in a positioner that rotates to keep the groove vertical (1G relative to welder, but called "rolled 6G" position).

**2. Pre-heat** (1 mark):
**Uniform pre-heat to 150–200 °C** using an oxy-propane rosebud torch (measure with Tempilstick). Pre-heat band = 75 mm on either side of the groove. Maintain interpass ≥ 150 °C but ≤ 300 °C (use a 0–500 °C contact pyrometer).

**3. Filler** (1 mark):
**AWS A5.7 ERCuNi** (ISO 24373 S Cu 7061, ~69 % Cu, 30 % Ni, 1 % Fe, 1 % Mn), 2.4 mm rod for root, 3.2 mm for fill.

**4. Shielding gas** (1 mark):
**Argon backing** (15–25 L/min) on the inside of the pipe to prevent oxide formation on the root bead. **Torch gas**: 100 % Ar at 12–15 L/min (no He for thin pipe — He would overheat and burn through).

**5. Parameter window** (2 marks):

| Pass | Current (A) | Voltage (V) | Travel (mm/min) | Heat Input (kJ/mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Root | 80–100 (DCEN) | 11–13 | 60–80 | ≈ 0.55–0.70 |
| Fill | 110–140 (DCEN) | 12–15 | 80–120 | ≈ 0.45–0.65 |
| Cap | 110–130 (DCEN) | 12–14 | 100–140 | ≈ 0.40–0.55 |

**6. Sequence & inspection** (1 mark):
Root → visual → 100 % **PT** of root → fill (2–3 passes) → cap → PT on cap → **RT** of full circumference → hydrostatic test at 1.5 × design pressure.

---

#### (b) Model Solution — Failure Modes & Mitigation in Cu-Ni Welding

**1. Porosity from hydrogen & from dissolved gases** (2 marks):
- **Cause:** Cu-Ni absorbs H from moisture, oil, or backing gas impurities. The solubility drops sharply on solidification → gas bubbles.
- **Mitigation:** (i) Clean joint to bright metal; (ii) **backing-gas purity ≥ 99.99 % Ar** with < 10 ppm H₂O; (iii) **dew-point meter on backing-gas line**; (iv) use **ERCuNi filler with Mn de-oxidant** (1 % Mn scavenges O and S); (v) keep interpass warm to slow solidification and let bubbles escape.

**2. Hot shortness above ~400 °C** (2 marks):
- **Cause:** Cu-Ni loses ductility in the 400–650 °C range (Cu₂O + Bi/Sb form low-melting grain-boundary films). The weld metal cracks under thermal contraction stress.
- **Mitigation:** (i) Use **lower heat input** (≤ 1.0 kJ/mm); (ii) **shorter restraint distance** — back-step welding or skip welding; (iii) **avoid dwelling** at the crater; (iv) **filler with ≥ 1 % Mn** also helps (Mn forms stable oxides that block Bi/Sb films).

**3. Iron contamination (loss of sea-water corrosion resistance)** (2 marks):
- **Cause:** Fe content above ~0.5 % in the weld metal produces a galvanic couple in seawater that preferentially corrodes the weld. Fe can enter from (a) contaminated wire brush (steel bristles), (b) grinding dust, (c) carbon-steel tack welds.
- **Mitigation:** (i) **Use only stainless-steel or aluminium-bronze wire brushes** dedicated to Cu-Ni; (ii) **tack welds with the same ERCuNi filler**; (iii) cover the joint with **plastics or clean canvas** during fit-up; (iv) **dilution control** — keep base-melt fraction ≤ 35 % by careful root pass geometry.

**4. Heat tint and oxide entrapment** (1 mark):
- A pale straw → dark blue → black heat-tint ring around the weld indicates **inadequate backing gas**. Discard and re-cut the joint (heat-tinted areas must be ground out per DEF STAN 02-781).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** When the question says "discuss failure modes", students often list *defects* (slag, spatter) that are common to steel welding. For Cu-Ni, the *alloy-specific* failure modes are **(1) porosity from H/O, (2) hot shortness, (3) Fe contamination / galvanic corrosion, (4) heat-tint loss of corrosion resistance**. Mentioning these four by name gets full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Final 60-second brain-dump before you walk into the exam hall.

- ☐ **Heat input formula** is $H = (\eta V I \cdot 60) / (S \cdot 1000)$ kJ/mm — write it with units every time.
- ☐ **Process efficiency** order: SAW (1.0) > GMAW (0.8) > SMAW (0.85) > GTAW (0.6).
- ☐ **Aluminium**: ER 4043 (Al-Si, crack-resistant) vs ER 5356 (Al-Mg, stronger); AC-GTAW; He-Ar mix; IACS H ≤ 1.0 kJ/mm for 5xxx.
- ☐ **Titanium**: GTAW with **trailing shield + backing shield**; ERTi-2 for Gr 2; ERTi-5 for Gr 5; weld colour test (silver = good, straw = acceptable, blue = scrap).
- ☐ **Cu-Ni 90/10**: Pre-heat 150–200 °C; ERCuNi filler; 100 % Ar; porosity from H; hot shortness 400–650 °C; Fe contamination = loss of sea-water corrosion resistance.
- ☐ **NAB (Nickel-Aluminium Bronze)**: ER CuAl-A; pre-heat 250 °C; PWHT 600 °C; 4-phase microstructure (α, β, κᵢ, κᵢᵢᵢ) — important for propeller welds.
- ☐ **Austenitic SS 304L/316L**: ER 308L/316L; use **L-grade** to avoid sensitisation; ferrite content 3–10 FN (WRC-1992 diagram) to prevent hot cracking.
- ☐ **FSW (Friction Stir Welding)**: solid-state, no melting, no HAZ softening — best for long aluminium marine panels. Joint efficiency 85–95 %.
- ☐ **Weld dilution** = $A_{BM} / (A_{BM} + A_{FW}) \times 100$ — important for dissimilar joints and Fe control in Cu-Ni.
- ☐ **t₈/₅** = time between 800 °C and 500 °C in the HAZ; inverse-square dependence on $(T - T_0)$ — pre-heat increases $T_0$ and *shortens* t₈/₅.
- ☐ **Welding procedure qualification**: **ISO 15614-2** (Al), **ASME Section IX**, **IACS UR W28**, **DNV-OS-C401**.
- ☐ **Inspection sequence**: VT → PT/MT → UT → RT → TOFD → PAUT → hydrostatic / leak test.
- ☐ **Safety & HSE**: arc flash (PPE Cat 3), fume extraction for **stainless steel** (hexavalent Cr — carcinogen), **beryllium** awareness for Cu-Be alloys, fire-watch for **magnesium** welds.
- ☐ **Modern shipbuilding processes** to know by name: **Hybrid Laser-MAG**, **Cold Metal Transfer (CMT)**, **Narrow-Gap GMAW**, **Inertia Friction Welding** (for shaft-line).
- ☐ **Aluminium vs steel welding** rule of thumb: aluminium's thermal conductivity is **4–5×** steel's, so for the same plate thickness you need **~1.5–2×** the current or **0.5–0.7×** the travel speed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Riveting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Riveting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Riveting** is a permanent mechanical fastening process used in shipbuilding wherein a ductile metallic pin (called a *rivet*) is inserted through pre-drilled or pre-punched overlapping or butting plates, and its trailing end is plastically deformed (upset) using hammer blows, pneumatic/hydraulic rivet guns, or hydraulic presses to form a **second head** (the *shop head* or *closing head*), thereby creating a mechanical interlock that holds the plates together.

In the context of **non-ferrous materials in shipbuilding**, riveting assumes a specialized role because:
- Conventional mild-steel rivets cannot be used in applications requiring **corrosion resistance**, **non-magnetic properties**, or joining of **non-ferrous parent plates** (aluminum, copper, brass alloys).
- Therefore, rivets are manufactured from **aluminum, copper, brass, naval brass, aluminium-bronze, monel metal, and cupro-nickel**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A rivet is classified as a *non-ferrous fastener* when manufactured from aluminium, copper, brass, bronze, or nickel-based alloys. The choice of rivet material must be **galvanically compatible** with the parent plate to avoid bimetallic (electrochemical) corrosion in seawater.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are stacking two pieces of leather and punching a hole through both. You push a button (with a head and a shank) through the hole, then crimp the back of the button so it cannot come out. The leather pieces are now permanently clamped between two heads.

**Rivet = Button, Plates = Leather, Hammer = Crimping tool.**

The heads on both sides grip the plates like a clothespin, and the *clamping force* generated during the upsetting process is what actually holds the joint together — not just the shape of the head.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why riveting still matters in modern shipbuilding:** Even in the era of welding, riveting is preferred for:
> - **Hull-to-deck** connections in aluminium-alloy fast patrol craft
> - **Superstructure-stiffener** attachments in composite marine structures
> - Joining **dissimilar metals** where welding is metallurgically difficult
> - **Repair work** in dry-dock where hot work permits are restricted

## 1.3 Standard Metrics

- **Standard rivet diameter range (shipbuilding): 6 mm to 32 mm**
- **Pitch of rivets (longitudinal): typically 2.5 d to 3.5 d**
- **Edge distance: 1.5 d to 2 d**
- **Unwin's formula (for thick plates): d = 6 × √t (where t is plate thickness in mm and d in mm)**
- **For thin plates: d = 2 × t (empirical rule)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Galvanic Compatibility Rule (Marine Standard):** When riveting non-ferrous plates, the rivet material should be **electrochemically close** on the galvanic series to the parent plate. The potential difference should not exceed **0.25 V** to minimize corrosion in seawater.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Riveted Joint

Every rivet has four geometric zones:
1. **Manufactured head** (factory head) — pre-formed end
2. **Shank** (cylindrical body) — fits into the drilled hole
3. **Point / tail** — the protruding end before upsetting
4. **Shop head / closing head** — formed during installation

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Riveting Operation

**Step 1 — Hole Preparation**
- Holes are drilled or punched in the plates. Drilling is preferred for thicker plates and non-ferrous materials because punching causes **work-hardening** around the hole edge.

**Step 2 — Rivet Insertion**
- The rivet is heated (hot riveting) or used as-is (cold riveting) and inserted through the aligned holes.

**Step 3 — Upsetting (Forming the Shop Head)**
- A pneumatic/hydraulic **dolly** supports the manufactured head.
- A **riveting hammer** or **hydraulic riveter** strikes/swages the tail.
- The shank shortens, *its diameter increases* (diametrical expansion fills the hole), and the closing head forms.

**Step 4 — Cooling & Shrinkage (Hot Riveting)**
- The hot rivet cools and contracts, generating a **clamping force** on the plates — this is the *mechanical secret* of a strong hot-riveted joint.

**Step 5 — Inspection**
- The completed head is checked for **concentricity**, **height**, and absence of cracks (via ultrasonic or visual).

## 2.3 Types of Riveted Joints in Shipbuilding

| Joint Type | Description | Application in Ships |
|---|---|---|
| **Lap Joint** | Plates overlap, single row of rivets | Light bulkheads, secondary stiffeners |
| **Butt Joint (single strap)** | Two plates butted, covered by one strap | Hull plating seams |
| **Butt Joint (double strap)** | Two plates butted, covered by two straps | Heavy hull seams, keel connections |
| **Chain / Z-type Joint** | Rivets staggered in zigzag | High-stress longitudinals |

## 2.4 Why "How" and "Why" — The Engineering Logic

- **Why are non-ferrous rivets used in ships?** Because ferrous rivets in contact with seawater corrode, stain adjacent plates, and create rust streaks. Aluminium, copper, brass, and monel rivets are chosen for **corrosion resistance** and **galvanic compatibility**.
- **Why is hot riveting stronger?** The thermal contraction of the cooling shank creates a residual **hoop compressive stress** that preloads the joint, increasing fatigue life.
- **Why is cold riveting preferred for aluminium?** Aluminium rivets cannot be heated (they lose temper and mechanical properties); cold riveting is used for aluminium sheet joints in fast craft and superstructures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cold Riveting Threshold Rule:** For aluminium alloy rivets, cold riveting is used when the rivet diameter is **≤ 10 mm** AND the driven length is **≤ 4× diameter**. Beyond this, hot riveting is mandated.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Symbol | Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,d^2\,\tau$ | Shear strength of one rivet in single shear | N | Single-shear joint |
| 2 | $P_s = 2 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,d^2\,\tau$ | Shear strength in double shear | N | Double-strap butt joint |
| 3 | $P_c = d \times t \times \sigma_c$ | Crushing / bearing strength of plate | N | Plate tear-out check |
| 4 | $p = \dfrac{P_t}{t \cdot \sigma_t - d \cdot \sigma_c}$ | Pitch of rivets (tearing resistance) | mm | Spacing design |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{joint}}{P_{plate}} \times 100$ | Efficiency of joint | % | Quality index |
| 6 | $d = 6\sqrt{t}$ | Unwin's formula (thick plates) | mm | Initial diameter selection |
| 7 | $d = 2t$ | Empirical rule (thin plates) | mm | t ≤ 6 mm |
| 8 | $P_{rivet} = n \times \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,d^2\,\tau$ | Total shear strength (n rivets) | N | Joint capacity |
| 9 | $S_{min} = 1.5d$ | Minimum edge distance | mm | Plate design code |
| 10 | $p_{min} = 2.5d$ | Minimum pitch | mm | Prevents hole collision |

> Where **d** = rivet diameter, **t** = plate thickness, **τ** = allowable shear stress of rivet, **σc** = allowable crushing stress of plate, **σt** = allowable tensile stress of plate, **n** = number of rivets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention (KTU):** Stresses are in **N/mm² = MPa**, forces in **N or kN**, lengths in **mm**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Shear Strength of a Single Rivet

When a rivet is in **single shear** (lap joint), the shear plane coincides with the interface between the two plates. The resisting shear area is a circular cross-section of the rivet shank.

$$
A_{shear} = \frac{\pi}{4}\,d^{2}
$$

The shear resistance (force the rivet can transfer before failing in shear) is the product of this area and the allowable shear stress $\tau$ of the rivet material:

$$
P_s \;=\; A_{shear} \times \tau
$$

$$
P_s \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4}\,d^{2}\,\tau
$$

**For double shear (butt joint with two straps)**, the rivet has *two* shear planes:

$$
P_s \;=\; 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4}\,d^{2}\,\tau \;=\; \frac{\pi}{2}\,d^{2}\,\tau
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Crushing / Bearing Strength of the Plate

The rivet bears against the cylindrical surface of the hole. The projected bearing area is:

$$
A_{crush} \;=\; d \times t
$$

The crushing resistance is:

$$
P_c \;=\; A_{crush} \times \sigma_c \;=\; d \times t \times \sigma_c
$$

This represents the force needed to crush the plate material against the rivet shank.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Tearing Resistance of the Plate Between Rivets

Consider a single row of rivets with **pitch *p***. The net section of the plate is reduced by the rivet hole. The tearing strength is:

$$
P_t \;=\; (p - d) \times t \times \sigma_t
$$

For the joint to be safe:

$$
P_{rivet} \;\leq\; P_t
$$

Rearranging for pitch:

$$
p \;=\; d + \frac{P_{rivet}}{t \cdot \sigma_t} \;=\; \frac{P_{rivet} + d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}{t \cdot \sigma_t} \;=\; \frac{P_{rivet}}{t \cdot \sigma_t - d \cdot \sigma_c}
$$

(The term $d \cdot \sigma_c$ is sometimes incorporated depending on design code.)

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Efficiency of a Riveted Joint

The **efficiency** $\eta$ of a joint is defined as the ratio of the *strength of the weakest section* of the joint to the *strength of an unriveted (solid) plate* of the same dimensions.

$$
\eta \;=\; \frac{P_{weakest}}{P_{solid}} \times 100\,\%
$$

where

$$
P_{solid} \;=\; p \times t \times \sigma_t
$$

Substituting the weakest section (shear, crushing, or tearing — whichever is lowest):

$$
\eta \;=\; \min\!\left(\frac{P_s}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t},\; \frac{P_c}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t},\; \frac{P_t}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}\right) \times 100
$$

## 3.5 Worked Analytical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A single-riveted lap joint joins two **aluminium plates** (thickness $t = 8$ mm) using **aluminium rivets** of diameter $d = 16$ mm. Allowable stresses: $\tau = 75$ MPa, $\sigma_c = 120$ MPa, $\sigma_t = 100$ MPa. Find the joint efficiency for a pitch of 40 mm.

**Step 1 — Shear strength of rivet:**

$$
P_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 16^{2} \times 75 \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} \times 256 \times 75
$$

$$
P_s = 0.7854 \times 256 \times 75 \;=\; 15{,}080 \;\text{N} \;\approx\; 15.08\;\text{kN}
$$

**Step 2 — Crushing strength of plate:**

$$
P_c = 16 \times 8 \times 120 \;=\; 15{,}360 \;\text{N} \;=\; 15.36\;\text{kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Tearing strength of plate:**

$$
P_t = (40 - 16) \times 8 \times 100 \;=\; 24 \times 8 \times 100 \;=\; 19{,}200\;\text{N} \;=\; 19.2\;\text{kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify weakest section:**

$$
P_{weakest} = \min(15.08,\; 15.36,\; 19.2) \;=\; 15.08\;\text{kN} \;\;(\text{shear governs})
$$

**Step 5 — Strength of solid plate:**

$$
P_{solid} = 40 \times 8 \times 100 \;=\; 32{,}000\;\text{N} \;=\; 32\;\text{kN}
$$

**Step 6 — Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{15.08}{32.0} \times 100 \;=\; 47.13\,\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Joint efficiency of 47% means the joint can carry only ~47% of the load that the unperforated plate can carry. Ship-hull efficiency is typically designed for **60–80%**, so this joint would need **double-shear (butt-strap)** configuration or **larger diameter** rivets.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Failure Mode Decision Table

| Failure Mode | Governing Formula | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Rivet shear | $P_s = \frac{\pi}{4}d^2\tau$ | Increase d, double-strap, higher τ rivet |
| Plate crushing | $P_c = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c$ | Use higher-strength plate |
| Plate tearing | $P_t = (p-d)\,t\,\sigma_t$ | Reduce pitch, add rivet row |
| Hole elongation | $P_h = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ | Match plate/rivet ductility |
| Edge shear-out | $P_e = 2 \cdot e \cdot t \cdot \tau_p$ | Increase edge distance *e* |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Riveting Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Plate Preparation] --> B[Mark Hole Centres Using Template]
    B --> C[Drill or Punch Holes]
    C --> D{Plate Material?}
    D -- Ferrous --> E[Hot Riveting Process]
    D -- Non Ferrous --> F[Cold Riveting Process]
    E --> G[Heat Rivet in Furnace 900 to 1100 C]
    G --> H[Insert Rivet Through Aligned Holes]
    F --> H
    H --> I[Support Manufactured Head With Dolly]
    I --> J[Apply Riveting Hammer or Hydraulic Press]
    J --> K[Form Shop Head Closing Head]
    K --> L[Allow to Cool Hot or Relax Cold]
    L --> M[Clamping Force Develops]
    M --> N[Quality Inspection Visual and UT]
    N --> O[End: Joint Ready for Service]
```

## 4.2 Joint Type Selection Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Riveted Joint Selection] --> B{Load Type?}
    B -- Light Tensile --> C[Lap Joint]
    B -- Moderate Tensile --> D[Butt Joint Single Strap]
    B -- Heavy Tensile --> E[Butt Joint Double Strap]
    B -- Cyclic Fatigue --> F[Chain Riveting Z Pattern]
    C --> G[Single Shear 1 shear plane]
    D --> H[Single Shear 1 shear plane]
    E --> I[Double Shear 2 shear planes]
    F --> J[Staggered Pitch Design]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Failure Mode Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Tensile Load on Joint] --> B{Rivet Shear Capacity}
    A --> C{Plate Crushing Capacity}
    A --> D{Plate Tearing Capacity}
    A --> E{Edge Shear Out Capacity}
    B -- Shear Failure --> F[Joint Slides at Interface]
    C -- Crushing --> G[Hole Enlarges Rivet Tilts]
    D -- Tearing --> H[Plate Splits Between Rivets]
    E -- Shear Out --> I[Plate Edge Breaks Out]
    F --> J[Action Increase d, double shear]
    G --> K[Action Thicker plate, higher sigma_c]
    H --> L[Action Reduce pitch p, add row]
    I --> M[Action Increase edge distance e]
```

## 4.4 Rivet Material Selection Map (Non-Ferrous)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Non Ferrous Rivet Material] --> B{Parent Plate}
    B -- Aluminium Alloy --> C[Aluminium Rivet 5056 or 2117]
    B -- Copper Alloy --> D[Copper Rivet C11000]
    B -- Brass --> E[Brass Rivet Naval Brass]
    B -- Steel Hull --> F[Monel Rivet for Seawater Contact]
    C --> G[Use Cold Riveting]
    D --> H[Use Cold or Hot Riveting]
    E --> I[Use Cold Riveting]
    F --> J[Use Hot Riveting]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define riveting. List four non-ferrous materials commonly used as rivets in shipbuilding.**

**Model Answer:**

**Riveting** is a permanent mechanical fastening process in which a ductile metallic pin (rivet) is inserted into pre-formed holes in two or more plates and the protruding tail is plastically deformed (upset) to form a second head, creating a clamping force that holds the plates together.

The four common non-ferrous rivet materials in shipbuilding are:
1. **Aluminium alloy (Al 5056, Al 2117)** – for aluminium hull joints
2. **Copper (C11000, ETP copper)** – for boiler and freshwater systems
3. **Naval brass (Cu-Zn-Sn)** – for marine atmospheric service
4. **Monel (Ni-Cu alloy 400)** – for hull-to-seawater interfaces

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**State Unwin's formula and explain its application in shipbuilding.**

**Model Answer:**

**Unwin's formula** is an empirical relation used to select the **initial rivet diameter** based on plate thickness:

$$
d \;=\; 6\sqrt{t}
$$

where **d** is the rivet diameter in mm and **t** is the plate thickness in mm.

**Application:** This formula is used during the *preliminary design* stage of ship hull plating and stiffener joints to select a *trial* rivet diameter. The final diameter is then refined based on **shear**, **crushing**, and **tearing** strength checks. Unwin's formula is most accurate for **plate thicknesses between 8 mm and 25 mm**, which covers most ship hull plating. For thin plates ($t \le 6$ mm), the alternative rule $d = 2t$ is used.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following.*

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the expressions for the **shear strength** and **crushing strength** of a single riveted lap joint. Hence, state the governing equation for **joint efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A double-riveted double-strap butt joint is used to connect two **aluminium plates** of thickness $t = 12$ mm. The rivet diameter is $d = 20$ mm, and the pitch in the outer row is $p = 50$ mm. Allowable stresses: $\tau = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_c = 140$ MPa, $\sigma_t = 110$ MPa. Calculate the **efficiency of the joint**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — (a):**

**Shear Strength Derivation:**

The shear plane in a lap joint coincides with the plate-to-plate interface. The rivet cross-section acting in shear is circular with area:

$$
A_{shear} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4}\,d^{2}
$$

The shear resistance is this area times the allowable shear stress $\tau$:

$$
P_s \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4}\,d^{2}\,\tau \quad \text{(single shear)}
$$

**Crushing Strength Derivation:**

The rivet bears against the hole surface. The projected bearing area is diameter times plate thickness:

$$
A_{crush} \;=\; d \times t
$$

$$
P_c \;=\; d \times t \times \sigma_c
$$

**Joint Efficiency:**

$$
\eta \;=\; \frac{\min(P_s,\,P_c,\,P_t)}{p \times t \times \sigma_t} \times 100\,\%
$$

where $P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$.

---

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Given data:**

$$
t = 12\,\text{mm}, \quad d = 20\,\text{mm}, \quad p = 50\,\text{mm}
$$

$$
\tau = 80\,\text{MPa}, \quad \sigma_c = 140\,\text{MPa}, \quad \sigma_t = 110\,\text{MPa}
$$

**Configuration:** Double-strap butt → **double shear**, so each rivet has 2 shear planes.

**Step 1 — Shear strength per rivet (double shear):**

$$
P_s = 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 20^2 \times 80 \;=\; \frac{\pi}{2} \times 400 \times 80
$$

$$
P_s = 1.5708 \times 400 \times 80 \;=\; 50{,}265\,\text{N} \;\approx\; 50.27\,\text{kN}
$$

**Step 2 — Crushing strength:**

$$
P_c = 20 \times 12 \times 140 \;=\; 33{,}600\,\text{N} \;=\; 33.6\,\text{kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Tearing strength of plate:**

$$
P_t = (50 - 20) \times 12 \times 110 \;=\; 30 \times 12 \times 110 \;=\; 39{,}600\,\text{N} \;=\; 39.6\,\text{kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Weakest section:**

$$
P_{weakest} = \min(50.27,\; 33.6,\; 39.6) \;=\; 33.6\,\text{kN} \;\;(\text{crushing governs})
$$

**Step 5 — Strength of solid plate (per pitch):**

$$
P_{solid} = 50 \times 12 \times 110 \;=\; 66{,}000\,\text{N} \;=\; 66.0\,\text{kN}
$$

**Step 6 — Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{33.6}{66.0} \times 100 \;=\; 50.91\,\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** for double-shear rivets — this is the #1 error. Marks lost: ~3.
> 2. **Mixing up which stress governs** — students must explicitly show the *minimum* of $P_s, P_c, P_t$.
> 3. **Using the *gross* pitch** instead of $(p - d)$ in the tearing formula.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency** — mixing cm and mm. Always convert to **mm and MPa** first.
> 5. **Not writing the final efficiency in %** — penalised 0.5 mark by KTU examiners.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [Alternative Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Compare **hot riveting** and **cold riveting** processes. State the specific conditions under which **cold riveting of aluminium rivets** is permitted. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-riveted lap joint of **copper plates** uses copper rivets. The plate thickness is $t = 10$ mm, rivet diameter $d = 18$ mm, pitch $p = 45$ mm. Allowable stresses: $\tau_{rivet} = 70$ MPa, $\sigma_c = 120$ MPa, $\sigma_t = 100$ MPa. Determine **(i)** shear strength of rivet, **(ii)** crushing strength, **(iii)** tearing strength, and **(iv)** joint efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — (a):**

| Aspect | Hot Riveting | Cold Riveting |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | 900–1100 °C (cherry red) | Ambient (no heating) |
| Force required | Low (rivet is soft) | High (rivet is hard) |
| Residual clamping | High (thermal contraction) | Low (only mechanical upset) |
| Fatigue life | Excellent | Good (lower) |
| Rivet material | Mild steel, monel, brass | Aluminium alloy, copper |
| Production rate | Slower | Faster |
| Equipment | Furnace + hammer/press | Hydraulic riveter |

**Cold riveting of aluminium rivets is permitted when:**

1. **Rivet diameter $d \le 10$ mm**, AND
2. **Driven (grip) length $L \le 4d$**, AND
3. The parent plate is also **aluminium or a low-strength non-ferrous alloy** (so that differential work-hardening is minimised).

Beyond these limits, **hot riveting or alternative fasteners** (HSFG bolts) must be used.

---

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Given data:**

$$
t = 10\,\text{mm}, \quad d = 18\,\text{mm}, \quad p = 45\,\text{mm}
$$

$$
\tau = 70\,\text{MPa}, \quad \sigma_c = 120\,\text{MPa}, \quad \sigma_t = 100\,\text{MPa}
$$

**Configuration:** Single-riveted lap → **single shear**.

**(i) Shear strength:**

$$
P_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 18^2 \times 70 = 0.7854 \times 324 \times 70 = 17{,}812\,\text{N} \approx 17.81\,\text{kN}
$$

**(ii) Crushing strength:**

$$
P_c = 18 \times 10 \times 120 = 21{,}600\,\text{N} = 21.6\,\text{kN}
$$

**(iii) Tearing strength:**

$$
P_t = (45 - 18) \times 10 \times 100 = 27 \times 10 \times 100 = 27{,}000\,\text{N} = 27.0\,\text{kN}
$$

**(iv) Joint efficiency:**

Weakest section = $P_s = 17.81$ kN (shear governs).

Solid plate strength per pitch:

$$
P_{solid} = 45 \times 10 \times 100 = 45{,}000\,\text{N} = 45.0\,\text{kN}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{17.81}{45.0} \times 100 = 39.58\,\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Confusing **single shear** and **double shear** — copper-riveted lap joints are *always* single shear.
> 2. **Forgetting to identify the governing mode** — KTU award 1 mark for the *identification* of the minimum failure mode even if the calculation is correct.
> 3. **Writing efficiency as a decimal** (e.g., 0.40) — KTU requires the value in **percentage form**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Riveting (Module 2):**

- ✅ **Riveting** = permanent mechanical fastening by plastic deformation of a ductile pin.
- ✅ **Anatomy:** Manufactured head + Shank + Tail → Shop head (formed).
- ✅ **Non-ferrous rivet materials:** Aluminium (Al 5056, 2117), Copper, Naval brass, Aluminium-bronze, Monel.
- ✅ **Galvanic compatibility** is the *primary* design driver in marine riveting.
- ✅ **Unwin's formula:** $d = 6\sqrt{t}$ for thick plates; $d = 2t$ for thin plates.
- ✅ **Shear strength (single):** $P_s = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \tau$
- ✅ **Shear strength (double):** $P_s = \frac{\pi}{2} d^2 \tau$
- ✅ **Crushing strength:** $P_c = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c$
- ✅ **Tearing strength:** $P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$
- ✅ **Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{weakest}}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t} \times 100\,\%$
- ✅ **Cold riveting of Al allowed only if** $d \le 10$ mm **and** $L \le 4d$.
- ✅ **Hot riveting** creates superior clamping force due to **thermal contraction** on cooling.
- ✅ **Minimum pitch:** $2.5d$ to prevent hole collision; **minimum edge distance:** $1.5d$ to prevent shear-out.
- ✅ **Ship hull joint efficiency target:** 60–80% (achieved via double-strap butt or chain riveting).
- ✅ **Always convert all units to mm and MPa** before plugging into any KTU formula.
- ✅ **Always identify the governing failure mode** by explicit $\min(P_s, P_c, P_t)$ comparison.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Joining Aluminium and Steel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Joining Aluminium and Steel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Joining of Aluminium and Steel** in shipbuilding refers to the engineered fabrication of permanent or semi-permanent metallurgical/mechanical interfaces between **aluminium alloys** (typically of the 5xxx and 6xxx series used for superstructures, deckhouses, and topside modules) and **ferrous steels** (primarily mild steel, AH36/DH36, and high-tensile shipbuilding grades used for hull plates, decks, and primary structural members).

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, this is classified as a **dissimilar metal joining (DMJ)** problem — a sub-domain of advanced marine fabrication — where the objective is to achieve a load-bearing, fatigue-resistant, and corrosion-stable joint despite significant **physical**, **thermal**, **metallurgical**, and **electrochemical** mismatches between the two base metals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box — Dissimilar Metal Joint (DMJ):**
> A structural connection in which the two parent materials being joined have **different compositions, microstructures, and/or mechanical property profiles**, requiring tailored process parameters, filler selection, and joint design to prevent premature service failure.

## 1.2 Why is Aluminium-to-Steel Joining Critical in Shipbuilding?

Modern commercial and naval vessels use a **hybrid metal strategy**:

| Structural Zone | Dominant Material | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Hull, keel, bottom shell | Mild steel / AH36 | High strength, low cost, weldability, impact toughness |
| Superstructure, deckhouse | Aluminium (5083, 6082, 5059) | Weight reduction → ↑ stability, ↑ payload, ↓ fuel consumption |
| Bulkheads, masts, hatch covers | Mixed (Al + Steel) | Functional and economic optimization |
| LNG/cryogenic containment | Aluminium (5083-O) + Stainless transition | Cryogenic ductility vs. structural strength |

A typical **Panamax-class container ship** may use **2,000–4,000 tonnes of aluminium** in its superstructure. The **aluminium–steel transition joint** is therefore one of the most frequently fabricated interfaces on a modern vessel.

> [!NOTE]
> A 1% reduction in upper-deck weight on a naval frigate can improve **metacentric height (GM)** and **operational stability** by 3–5%, making lightweight aluminium superstructures indispensable.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bridge Marriage" Problem

Imagine two people of very different heights, languages, and temperaments being asked to live together in the same house. The house (the joint) must:

1. **Survive daily stress** (mechanical load),
2. **Not corrode in a humid coastal atmosphere** (galvanic attack),
3. **Not crack when temperatures swing** between polar and tropical waters (thermal fatigue),
4. **Not fail under repeated wave slamming** (fatigue loading).

Aluminium and Steel are these "two different people." Aluminium is **light, soft, and reactive**; steel is **dense, strong, and relatively noble**. The joining process is the **marriage counselor** — it must mediate their differences through **carefully engineered processes, intermediate materials (transition inserts), and protective measures**.

## 1.4 The Core Engineering Challenge

Joining aluminium to steel is fundamentally difficult due to **four intrinsic mismatches**:

1. **Melting Point Disparity** — Aluminium melts at **660 °C**, steel at **~1538 °C**. A 2.3× difference makes fusion welding of both simultaneously almost impossible with conventional arc processes.
2. **Thermal Expansion Mismatch** — Aluminium's coefficient of thermal expansion is roughly **2× that of steel** (≈ 23 × 10⁻⁶ /K vs. ≈ 12 × 10⁻⁶ /K). Heating/cooling cycles induce residual stresses.
3. **Solid Solubility** — Aluminium and iron have **negligible mutual solid solubility** at room temperature (max ~0.03 at% Fe in Al at 225 °C). This forces brittle **intermetallic compounds (IMCs)** to form at the interface.
4. **Electrochemical (Galvanic) Mismatch** — Aluminium is strongly anodic (E° ≈ −1.66 V SHE) compared to steel (−0.44 V SHE). In seawater, the potential difference drives severe **galvanic corrosion** of the aluminium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Al–Steel Joining (KTU Module 2 highlight):**
> "The quality of an Al–Steel joint is governed less by the parent metal strength and more by the **thickness, morphology, and continuity of the intermetallic compound layer** at the bond interface, and by the **electrochemical isolation strategy** adopted in service."

## 1.5 Terminology Snapshot (Must Memorize for KTU Exam)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **DMJ** | Dissimilar Metal Joining |
| **IMC** | Intermetallic Compound (e.g., Fe₂Al₅, FeAl₃) |
| **FSW** | Friction Stir Welding (solid-state process) |
| **TLP Bonding** | Transient Liquid Phase Bonding |
| **Roll Cladding** | Hot/cold roll bonding of bimetallic plate |
| **Transition Insert** | A pre-fabricated bimetallic strip (Al–Steel) used as an interface piece |
| **Galvanic Series** | Ranking of metals in seawater by corrosion potential |
| **Houldcroft Number / Heat Input** | η × U × I / v in kJ/mm |
| **Bonding Zone (FZ)** | Stir Zone / Nugget in FSW |
| **Potentiodynamic Polarization** | Electrochemical test for galvanic tendency |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Aluminium–Steel Joining

The entire subject can be organized into **three engineering pillars** that examiners love to test:

### Pillar A — Mechanical Joining
- Riveting (cold/hot), bolting, self-piercing rivets (SPR), clinching
- Uses **physical fasteners + adhesive + isolation washers**
- No metallurgical bond → avoids IMC issue
- Fatigue-prone but easily inspected and repaired

### Pillar B — Fusion & Solid-State Welding
- Conventional MIG/TIG → generally **unsuitable** due to IMC formation
- **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** → industry standard
- **Laser welding** with Si-bearing filler wire
- **Explosion welding** (clad plate manufacture)
- **Diffusion bonding** / TLP bonding (specialized applications)

### Pillar C — Hybrid / Transition Methods
- **Bimetallic transition inserts** (e.g., Al-clad steel strip, explosion-bonded plates)
- **Roll bonding** of Al–Steel clad plates
- Adhesive bonding combined with mechanical fasteners
- Friction stir "spot" welding with interlocking geometry

## 2.2 Intermetallic Compound (IMC) Formation — The Heart of the Topic

### 2.2.1 The Fe–Al Phase System (Why IMCs Are Inevitable)

When molten or hot aluminium contacts steel, the following sequence occurs at the interface:

1. **Dissolution of Fe in liquid Al** (Fe is soluble in liquid Al up to ~5 wt% at 700 °C).
2. **Interfacial reaction layer nucleation** — Al-rich and Fe-rich phases form.
3. **Diffusion-controlled growth** of brittle IMCs.

The dominant IMCs encountered in Al–Steel joints are:

| IMC Phase | Composition | Hardness (HV) | Nature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FeAl₃** | Al₃Fe | ~890 HV | Brittle, most common |
| **Fe₂Al₅** | Al₅Fe₂ | ~1000 HV | Most brittle, η-phase |
| **FeAl₂** | Al₂Fe | ~1050 HV | Very brittle |
| **FeAl** | AlFe | ~470 HV | Less brittle, but rare in DMJ |
| **Fe₃Al** | AlFe₃ | ~250 HV | Ductile compared to others |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** "**3-2-5-1**" — the four notorious IMCs FeAl₃, FeAl₂, Fe₂Al₅, FeAl in order of increasing Fe content. **Fe₂Al₅ is the worst offender** because it has an anisotropic orthorhombic crystal structure with a preferred growth direction perpendicular to the interface, producing needle-like or tongue-shaped protrusions that dramatically weaken the joint.

### 2.2.2 The Critical Thickness Rule

Empirically, for shipbuilding-grade Al–Steel joints:

- **IMC layer < 5 μm** → Joint strength ≈ 80–95% of weaker base metal (acceptable)
- **IMC layer 5–10 μm** → Strength drops sharply (transition zone)
- **IMC layer > 10 μm** → Brittle fracture, unacceptable for marine service

> [!WARNING]
> Examiners frequently test: *"Why is excessive IMC growth detrimental to joint strength?"* — Answer must include: (1) intrinsic brittleness, (2) mismatch of thermal expansion creating residual micro-stresses, (3) initiation site for cracks, (4) preferred fracture path.

### 2.2.3 IMC Growth Kinetics (Parabolic Law)

The growth of the IMC layer follows a **diffusion-controlled parabolic law** at isothermal conditions:

$$
x^2 = k \cdot t
$$

where:
- $x$ = thickness of IMC layer (m)
- $k$ = growth rate constant (m²/s)
- $t$ = exposure time at temperature (s)

The growth rate constant follows an **Arrhenius relationship**:

$$
k = k_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{R \cdot T}\right)
$$

where:
- $k_0$ = pre-exponential constant (m²/s)
- $Q$ = activation energy for Fe diffusion in Al (≈ 140–170 kJ/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant (**8.314 J/mol·K**)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** The exponential dependence on T explains why **reducing peak interface temperature** (a key FSW design strategy) is the single most effective way to suppress IMC growth. Dropping peak T from 700 °C to 550 °C reduces k by roughly an **order of magnitude**.

## 2.3 Galvanic Corrosion in Seawater

### 2.3.1 Electrochemical Basis

When two dissimilar metals are electrically coupled in an electrolyte (seawater, salt spray, condensation), they form a **galvanic cell** with:
- The **more anodic** metal → dissolves (corrodes)
- The **more cathodic** metal → protected
- The **area ratio** dictates severity

In the **galvanic series in flowing seawater**:

- **Aluminium alloys (5xxx/6xxx)** → E ≈ −0.75 to −0.95 V (SCE) → **anode**
- **Carbon steel / mild steel** → E ≈ −0.60 to −0.70 V (SCE) → **cathode**

> [!NOTE]
> The potential difference of **~0.2 to 0.3 V** is enough to drive rapid aluminium dissolution if a large steel cathode is coupled to a small aluminium anode — this is the classic **"small anode / large cathode"** pitfall.

### 2.3.2 The Area Ratio Pitfall

According to **mixed-potential theory**, the corrosion current density on the anode is amplified when the cathode-to-anode area ratio is large. The effective anodic current density:

$$
i_a = \frac{E_c - E_a}{R_s} \cdot \frac{A_c}{A_a}
$$

where:
- $E_c$ = corrosion potential of cathode (V)
- $E_a$ = corrosion potential of anode (V)
- $R_s$ = electrolyte resistance (Ω)
- $A_c / A_a$ = cathode-to-anode area ratio (dimensionless)

A steel rivet (small area) holding an aluminium plate (large area) is **safe** (small cathode). A steel plate fastened with many aluminium rivets is a **disaster** (large cathode, small anode).

## 2.4 Heat Input in Welding (Universal Metric)

For all arc-based Al–Steel welding variants, **heat input** is the single most controllable variable:

$$
H = \frac{\eta \cdot U \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}
$$

where:
- $\eta$ = process efficiency (0.6 for MIG, 0.7 for TIG, 0.85 for Plasma)
- $U$ = arc voltage (V)
- $I$ = welding current (A)
- $v$ = travel speed (mm/s)

**Target heat input for Al–Steel FSW:** typically **0.3–1.0 kJ/mm** (extremely low) to keep interface temperature below the Al-Si eutectic (~577 °C) and minimize IMC growth.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Summary)

| # | Equation | Variables / Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x^2 = k \cdot t$ | $x$ [m], $k$ [m²/s], $t$ [s] | Parabolic IMC layer growth |
| 2 | $k = k_0 \cdot \exp(-Q / RT)$ | $Q$ [J/mol], $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K, $T$ [K] | Temperature dependence of IMC growth |
| 3 | $H = \eta U I / (v \cdot 1000)$ | $H$ [kJ/mm], $U$ [V], $I$ [A], $v$ [mm/s] | Arc heat input control |
| 4 | $\Delta \varepsilon = \Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | $\Delta \alpha$ [1/K], $\Delta T$ [K] | Thermal strain mismatch |
| 5 | $\sigma_{res} = E \cdot \Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | $E$ [GPa], $\sigma$ [MPa] | Residual stress estimate |
| 6 | $i_a = (E_c - E_a) R_s^{-1} \cdot (A_c / A_a)$ | Currents in [A/m²], areas [m²] | Galvanic current density |
| 7 | $\beta_{FSW} = T_{peak} / T_{m,Al}$ | Dimensionless ratio | FSW temperature window check |
| 8 | $\eta_{joint} = \sigma_{joint} / \sigma_{weaker\,base}$ | Dimensionless | Joint efficiency ratio |
| 9 | $d_{pin} = k_{tool} \cdot t_{plate}$ | mm | FSW tool pin diameter rule of thumb |
| 10 | $R_a = \Delta h / L_{bond}$ | $\mu$m/mm | Bond line roughness in diffusion bonding |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU exam, equations (1), (2), (3), and (4) are the most frequently tested. The IMC growth law (1) and Arrhenius dependence (2) are **guaranteed favourites** in Part B 14-mark questions.

## 2.6 Real-World Shipbuilding Utility

| Application | Method Used | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Superstructure to deck joint | FSW with bimetallic insert | High strength, repeatable, low distortion |
| Aluminium mast to steel deck | Mechanical (bolted) + isolation sleeves | Inspection-friendly, replaceable |
| LNG carrier insulation support | Adhesive + stainless fastener | Cryogenic compatibility |
| Bimetallic transition strip (aluminium bridge to steel hull) | Explosion-welded clad plate | Roll-formed, mass-produced |
| Naval radar mast | Friction stir spot weld | Minimal heat input, no filler wire |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — IMC Layer Thickness as a Function of Time and Temperature

**Objective:** Show that IMC growth in Al–Steel systems follows a parabolic law, then quantify the time required to reach the critical 10 μm thickness at typical FSW interface temperatures.

**Starting premise:** Diffusion through the growing IMC layer is the rate-limiting step. According to Fick's first law applied to a growing planar interface:

$$
J = -D \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}
$$

For a binary diffusion couple (Al | Fe) with limited terminal solubilities, the marker-plane analysis (Wagner, 1969) yields an integrated flux balance. Let:

- $x$ = instantaneous thickness of IMC layer (m)
- $\rho_{IMC}$ = molar density of the IMC (mol/m³)
- $C_1$ and $C_2$ = concentration limits of the diffusing species in the IMC at its two interfaces (mol/m³)

**Step 1 — Mass balance at the moving interface**

The rate of advance of the IMC front is governed by the difference in diffusive flux arriving at and leaving the interface:

$$
\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{D}{\rho_{IMC}} \cdot \frac{(C_1 - C_2)}{x}
$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate**

Assuming isothermal conditions, D, C₁, C₂, and ρ_IMC are constants. Multiply both sides by x:

$$
x \, dx = \frac{D \cdot (C_1 - C_2)}{\rho_{IMC}} \, dt
$$

Integrate from 0 to x over time 0 to t:

$$
\int_0^x x \, dx = \frac{D \cdot (C_1 - C_2)}{\rho_{IMC}} \int_0^t dt
$$

**Step 3 — Resulting parabolic growth law**

$$
\frac{x^2}{2} = \frac{D \cdot (C_1 - C_2)}{\rho_{IMC}} \cdot t
$$

Defining the effective growth rate constant:

$$
k = \frac{2 D (C_1 - C_2)}{\rho_{IMC}}
$$

we obtain the **canonical parabolic growth equation**:

$$
\boxed{\, x^2 = k \cdot t \,}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Arrhenius temperature dependence**

Substituting $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$ into the definition of k:

$$
k = k_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{R \cdot T}\right)
$$

with $k_0$ the pre-exponential constant (≈ 4.5 × 10⁻⁷ m²/s for Fe₂Al₅ growth on steel), $Q \approx 155$ kJ/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K.

**Step 5 — Numerical check (KTU favourite numerical)**

Compute the time to reach $x = 10 \, \mu m = 1 \times 10^{-5}$ m at T = 873 K (600 °C) and T = 773 K (500 °C).

At T = 873 K:

$$
k = 4.5 \times 10^{-7} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{155{,}000}{8.314 \cdot 873}\right)
$$

$$
k = 4.5 \times 10^{-7} \cdot \exp(-21.34) = 4.5 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 5.62 \times 10^{-10}
$$

$$
k \approx 2.53 \times 10^{-16} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}
$$

Then:

$$
t = \frac{x^2}{k} = \frac{(1 \times 10^{-5})^2}{2.53 \times 10^{-16}} \approx 3.95 \times 10^{5} \, \text{s} \approx 110 \, \text{hours}
$$

At T = 773 K:

$$
k = 4.5 \times 10^{-7} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{155{,}000}{8.314 \cdot 773}\right) = 4.5 \times 10^{-7} \cdot \exp(-24.10) \approx 1.17 \times 10^{-17} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}
$$

$$
t = \frac{(1 \times 10^{-5})^2}{1.17 \times 10^{-17}} \approx 8.55 \times 10^{6} \, \text{s} \approx 99 \, \text{days}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Reducing interface temperature from **600 °C to 500 °C** extends the time to reach the critical 10 μm IMC thickness by a factor of ~**22×**. This is the engineering reason why **low-heat-input processes (FSW, laser) dominate Al–Steel joining** in shipbuilding.

## 3.2 Derivation — Galvanic Corrosion Current Density

**Objective:** Show how the small-anode / large-cathode problem amplifies corrosion on aluminium.

**Step 1 — Couple the two electrodes**

When two metals (A = Al, B = steel) are connected, the mixed potential of the couple $E_{mix}$ lies between $E_A$ and $E_B$. At the mixed potential, total anodic current equals total cathodic current (charge conservation):

$$
I_A + I_B = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad i_a A_a + i_c A_c = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Apply linearized Butler–Volmer kinetics (small polarisation)**

For small polarizations about the corrosion potential:

$$
i_a \approx i_{0,a} \cdot \frac{(E_{mix} - E_A)}{\beta_a}, \qquad i_c \approx i_{0,c} \cdot \frac{(E_B - E_{mix})}{\beta_c}
$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate $E_{mix}$**

Charge balance $i_a A_a = i_c A_c$ yields:

$$
E_{mix} = \frac{E_A \cdot \beta_a \cdot A_c + E_B \cdot \beta_c \cdot A_a}{\beta_a A_c + \beta_c A_a}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the anodic current density**

Substituting back:

$$
i_a = \frac{(E_B - E_A) \cdot \beta_a}{\beta_a A_c / A_a + \beta_c}
$$

Rewriting in terms of the area ratio:

$$
\boxed{\, i_a \approx \frac{(E_B - E_A)}{R_s \cdot (1 + A_c/A_a)} \cdot \frac{A_c}{A_a} \,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical reading:** As $A_c / A_a \to \infty$ (steel vastly larger than aluminium), $i_a$ saturates to a maximum value of $(E_B - E_A) / R_s$. The aluminium anode is driven to its **diffusion-limited dissolution rate**, which in seawater is very high.

## 3.3 Derivation — Residual Stress Due to Thermal Expansion Mismatch

**Step 1 — Free thermal strain difference**

$$
\Delta \varepsilon_{th} = (\alpha_{Al} - \alpha_{Fe}) \cdot (T_{room} - T_{process})
$$

For a typical FSW process at T_process = 500 °C and T_room = 25 °C:

$$
\Delta \varepsilon_{th} = (23 - 12) \times 10^{-6} \cdot (500 - 25) = 5.18 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 2 — Constrained strain produces biaxial residual stress**

If the joint is constrained, the elastic modulus E of the weaker material (aluminium) governs the residual stress:

$$
\sigma_{res} = E_{Al} \cdot \Delta \varepsilon_{th} = 70 \, \text{GPa} \cdot 5.18 \times 10^{-3} \approx 363 \, \text{MPa}
$$

This approaches the **yield strength of 5083-O aluminium (~145 MPa)**, confirming that **residual stresses are a major design concern** in thick Al–Steel hybrid plates.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection Decision Chart

| Decision Criterion | Mechanical (Rivet/Bolt) | Adhesive + Fastener | FSW | Explosion Cladding | Laser / MIG |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Joint strength efficiency | 60–80% | 50–70% | 80–95% | 95–100% | 50–70% |
| Fatigue performance | Good (if preloaded) | Fair | Excellent | Excellent | Fair |
| Galvanic risk | High — needs isolation | Low (adhesive + isolation) | Low–Moderate | None (encapsulated) | Moderate |
| Field repairability | Excellent | Good | Difficult | Not repairable | Difficult |
| Capital cost | Low | Low | High | Very high | Medium |
| Material wastage | Minimal | Minimal | Moderate | Low | Moderate |
| Suitability for thick plates (≥ 10 mm) | Limited | Limited | Excellent | Excellent | Good |
| Suitability for thin sheets (≤ 3 mm) | Excellent | Excellent | Difficult (clamping) | Limited | Excellent |
| IACS / Class approval | Widely approved | Limited | Approved (FSW since 2012) | Approved | Approved |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "which method is best?", the answer is **always conditional**. Use the decision matrix above. The "best" method depends on plate thickness, service environment, repair philosophy, and class-society rules.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Friction Stir Welding (FSW) Process for Al–Steel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rotating FSW Tool\nTool Steel / Tungsten Carbide] --> B[Shoulder Contact\nPreheats Top Surface]
    B --> C[Pin Plunges into\nAluminium Plate]
    C --> D[Plasticized Al Flows\nAround Pin]
    D --> E[Material Stirs Across\nAl/Steel Interface]
    E --> F[Steady-State T < 550 C\nLow Heat Input]
    F --> G[Thin IMC Layer < 5 um]
    G --> H[Tool Traverse\nAlong Joint Line]
    H --> I[Solidified Joint\nNo Fusion, No Filler]
    I --> J[High Strength\nLow Distortion]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Joint Design Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Al-Steel Joint Required] --> P{Plate Thickness}
    P -->|<= 3 mm| R[Mechanical Riveting\nor Adhesive + Fasteners]
    P -->|3 to 12 mm| M{Service Environment}
    P -->|> 12 mm| W{Production Volume}
    M -->|Marine / Splash Zone| M1[FSW + Bimetallic Insert\nwith Isolation Gasket]
    M -->|Atmospheric Only| M2[FSW Direct Joint]
    W -->|High Volume| W1[Explosion-Welded\nClad Transition Plate]
    W -->|Low Volume / Repair| W2[FSW or Laser\nwith Al-Si Filler]
    R --> END[Final Joint]
    M1 --> END
    M2 --> END
    W1 --> END
    W2 --> END
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Al–Steel Superstructure Joint

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOP[Aluminium Superstructure Zone]
        A1[5083/6082 Aluminium Plate]
        A2[Aluminium Stiffener]
    end

    subgraph MID[Transition / Interface Zone]
        T1[Bimetallic Insert\nExplosion-Welded Al-Steel]
        T2[FSW Weld Nugget]
        T3[Galvanic Isolation Gasket\nEPDM / Nylon Sleeve]
    end

    subgraph BOT[Steel Hull / Deck Zone]
        S1[AH36 Steel Plate]
        S2[Steel Stiffener]
    end

    A1 --> T1
    A2 --> T2
    T1 --> T3
    T3 --> S1
    T2 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — IMC Formation & Failure Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Liquid or Hot Al] --> L2[Fe Atoms Dissolve into Al]
    L2 --> L3[Fe2Al5 Nucleates\nat Interface]
    L3 --> L4[Layer Grows\nParabolic Kinetics]
    L4 --> L5{Layer Thickness}
    L5 -->|< 5 um| L6[Acceptable Joint]
    L5 -->|5 to 10 um| L7[Strength Transition Zone]
    L5 -->|> 10 um| L8[Brittle Fracture\nService Failure]
    L8 --> L9[Crack Initiates\nat IMC Interface]
    L9 --> L10[Catastrophic Joint\nFailure]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block — Galvanic Protection Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1[Al-Steel Coupled Joint] --> G2{Direct Contact?}
    G2 -->|Yes| G3[Galvanic Corrosion\nRisk HIGH]
    G2 -->|No| G4[Protected Joint]
    G3 --> G5[Apply Isolation:\nGasket, Sleeve, Washer]
    G3 --> G6[Apply Coating:\nPrimer + Paint on Cathode]
    G3 --> G7[Install Sacrificial\nZn or Al-Zn Anode]
    G5 --> G4
    G6 --> G4
    G7 --> G4
    G4 --> G8[Long Service Life\nin Seawater]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**Q: List the four major engineering challenges encountered when joining aluminium to steel in shipbuilding applications. Name two methods commonly used to overcome these challenges.**

**Model Answer:**

The four major challenges are:
1. **Large melting point difference** — Al melts at 660 °C vs. steel at 1538 °C, making conventional fusion welding difficult.
2. **Thermal expansion mismatch** — Al's CTE (~23 × 10⁻⁶/K) is roughly twice that of steel, producing residual stresses.
3. **Formation of brittle intermetallic compounds** (FeAl₃, Fe₂Al₅, FeAl₂) at the joint interface.
4. **Galvanic corrosion** in seawater due to electrochemical potential difference (~0.2–0.3 V).

Two methods to overcome these:
- **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** — solid-state process, low heat input, minimal IMC growth.
- **Bimetallic transition inserts** (explosion-welded Al-clad steel strips) — pre-engineered, no in-situ dissimilar welding.

---

### Question 2 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**Q: Why is Fe₂Al₅ considered the most detrimental intermetallic compound in Al–Steel joints? Mention the empirical "critical thickness" rule.**

**Model Answer:**

Fe₂Al₅ is the most detrimental because:
1. It has the **highest hardness (~1000 HV)** among the common Fe–Al IMCs.
2. Its **orthorhombic crystal structure** is highly anisotropic, with preferential growth perpendicular to the interface, producing tongue-shaped or needle-like protrusions.
3. It is **intrinsically brittle** with virtually zero ductility at room temperature, serving as a preferred crack-initiation site.

**Critical Thickness Rule:**
- IMC layer **< 5 μm** → Joint strength ~80–95% of weaker base metal — acceptable.
- IMC layer **5–10 μm** → Strength drops sharply — transition zone.
- IMC layer **> 10 μm** → Brittle fracture, unacceptable for marine service.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, ESE Pattern]**

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the mechanism of formation of intermetallic compounds (IMCs) in Al–Steel joints. Derive the parabolic growth law and the Arrhenius temperature dependence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the design and process parameters of Friction Stir Welding (FSW) for joining aluminium to steel in shipbuilding. How does FSW control IMC growth? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Mechanism of IMC formation:**

When aluminium and steel are brought into contact at elevated temperature (e.g., during welding), atomic inter-diffusion occurs across the interface. The phase diagram of Fe–Al shows negligible mutual solid solubility, so as Fe atoms diffuse into the Al side, the local composition enters two-phase regions, and **intermetallic compounds nucleate** at the interface. The sequence of formation is:

1. Initial dissolution of Fe in liquid (or hot) Al.
2. Nucleation of Al-rich IMCs (FeAl₃) at the interface.
3. Continued Fe diffusion → formation of Fe₂Al₅, which grows preferentially.
4. Possible formation of FeAl and Fe₃Al closer to the steel side at lower temperature.

**Derivation of parabolic growth law:**

Diffusion is the rate-limiting step. From Fick's first law applied to a binary diffusion couple:

$$
J = -D \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}
$$

A mass balance at the moving interface gives the rate of layer advance:

$$
\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{D \cdot (C_1 - C_2)}{\rho_{IMC} \cdot x}
$$

Separating variables and integrating from 0 to x and 0 to t:

$$
\int_0^x x \, dx = \frac{D \cdot (C_1 - C_2)}{\rho_{IMC}} \int_0^t dt
$$

This yields the parabolic growth equation:

$$
\boxed{\, x^2 = k \cdot t \,}
$$

where $k = 2 D (C_1 - C_2) / \rho_{IMC}$ is the growth rate constant.

**Arrhenius temperature dependence:**

The diffusion coefficient follows:

$$
D = D_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)
$$

Substituting into the definition of k:

$$
k = k_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)
$$

with $Q \approx 155$ kJ/mol for Fe₂Al₅ growth, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K.

**Significance:** The exponential dependence on T implies that small reductions in interface temperature yield large reductions in IMC growth rate. This is the foundation of low-heat-input process selection (e.g., FSW).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Friction Stir Welding (FSW) for Al–Steel:**

FSW is a **solid-state** joining process in which a non-consumable rotating tool (shoulder + pin) is plunged into the joint line. Frictional heat softens the aluminium (which has the lower melting point) to a plasticized state; the steel remains solid but is locally heated. The tool's rotation stirs the materials, producing a metallurgical bond **without reaching the melting point of either metal**.

**Key design and process parameters:**

| Parameter | Typical Value for Al–Steel |
|---|---|
| Tool material | Tool steel (H13), Tungsten carbide, PCBN |
| Rotational speed | 400–1000 rpm |
| Travel speed | 50–200 mm/min |
| Tilt angle | 2–3° |
| Pin length | Slightly less than Al plate thickness |
| Plunge depth | Controlled to ensure shoulder contact |
| Heat input | 0.3–1.0 kJ/mm |

**How FSW controls IMC growth:**

1. **Solid-state nature** — Peak temperature is kept at 500–550 °C, well below the 660 °C Al melting point, drastically reducing atomic mobility.
2. **Short dwell time at peak T** — Tool traverse is fast, so the interface experiences high temperature for only seconds.
3. **Mechanical intermixing** — The stirred material produces a thin, discontinuous, and mechanically interlocked transition zone with **fragmented IMC islands** rather than a continuous brittle layer.
4. **No filler dilution** — No additional alloying elements (e.g., Si) are introduced, but process parameters can be tuned to suppress IMC continuity.

**Joint positioning strategy:** Aluminium is usually placed on the **advancing side** of the tool, where the material flow is more vigorous, ensuring good plasticization and bonding with the steel backing plate.

**Resulting joint strength:** A well-optimized FSW Al–Steel joint achieves **80–95% efficiency** of the weaker base metal, with IMC layer thickness typically maintained below 5 μm.

---

### Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, ESE Pattern]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the galvanic corrosion mechanism in aluminium–steel couples immersed in seawater. Derive the relationship between anodic current density and cathode-to-anode area ratio. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the various methods used to control galvanic corrosion at Al–Steel joints in shipbuilding. Compare mechanical joining, adhesive bonding, and FSW in terms of corrosion resistance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Galvanic corrosion mechanism:**

When two dissimilar metals are electrically connected in an electrolyte (seawater, salt spray, humid marine air), they form a **galvanic cell** with a common electrolyte and a common metallic path. The more electrochemically active (anodic) metal preferentially dissolves, while the more noble (cathodic) metal is protected.

In an Al–Steel couple in seawater:
- Aluminium alloy: E ≈ −0.75 to −0.95 V (SCE) → **anode**, corrodes.
- Mild steel: E ≈ −0.60 to −0.70 V (SCE) → **cathode**, protected.
- Potential difference ≈ 0.15–0.35 V drives the corrosion current.

The corrosion current produces Al³⁺ dissolution at the anode, oxygen reduction at the cathode, and precipitation of corrosion products (Al(OH)₃, Al₂O₃·3H₂O) at the interface.

**Derivation of anodic current density vs. area ratio:**

For two coupled electrodes A (anode) and C (cathode), charge balance requires:

$$
i_a A_a + i_c A_c = 0
$$

For small polarizations, Butler–Volmer kinetics reduce to:

$$
i_a = i_{0,a} \cdot \frac{(E_{mix} - E_A)}{\beta_a}, \quad i_c = i_{0,c} \cdot \frac{(E_C - E_{mix})}{\beta_c}
$$

Eliminating $E_{mix}$ and applying $i_a A_a = -i_c A_c$:

$$
\boxed{\, i_a = \frac{(E_C - E_A) \cdot A_c / A_a}{R_s \cdot (1 + A_c / A_a)} \,}
$$

As the cathode-to-anode area ratio $A_c / A_a$ increases, $i_a$ approaches the asymptotic limit $(E_C - E_A) / R_s$, meaning the anode corrodes at its **diffusion-limited rate** — a dangerous situation when the aluminium anode is small.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Methods to control galvanic corrosion at Al–Steel joints:**

1. **Electrical isolation** — Use non-conductive gaskets (EPDM, neoprene), nylon or PVC sleeves on bolts, insulating washers, and bushings.
