2. **Coatings** — Apply marine-grade epoxy primer and anti-fouling paint on both the aluminium and steel surfaces, with extra thickness on the cathodic (steel) member.
3. **Cathodic protection** — Install sacrificial anodes of **zinc** or **Al–Zn–In** alloy near the joint to preferentially corrode.
4. **Bimetallic transition inserts** — Use explosion-welded Al-clad steel plates to physically separate the two metals.
5. **Material selection** — Use aluminium alloys with high corrosion resistance (e.g., 5083 in marine atmosphere, 5059 for naval applications).
6. **Joint design** — Avoid crevices; use sealed, drained designs to prevent electrolyte accumulation.

**Comparison of three joining methods:**

| Criterion | Mechanical (Rivet/Bolt) | Adhesive Bonding | FSW |
|---|---|---|---|
| Galvanic risk | **High** (direct metal contact) — mitigated with isolation washers | **Low** (adhesive layer electrically isolates) | **Moderate** (metallurgical bond means no isolation, but small cathode area in FSW nugget) |
| Inspection | Easy, visual | Difficult (non-destructive testing required) | Difficult (radiography, ultrasonic) |
| Repair at sea | Excellent (replace fasteners) | Difficult | Very difficult |
| Long-term corrosion performance | Good (with maintenance) | Good (if adhesive stays sealed) | Excellent (homogeneous bond) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B:**
> A common mistake is to **omit the "small anode / large cathode" effect** in galvanic corrosion answers. Examiners expect you to discuss the **area ratio**, derive the relation, and explain why steel rivets on aluminium plates are safer than aluminium rivets on steel plates. Missing this loses 2–3 marks out of 7. Also, when discussing FSW and corrosion, students often **incorrectly state that FSW eliminates galvanic risk** — it does not; it merely reduces the cathode area exposed to electrolyte.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🚢 Shipbuilding-Specific Highlights
- **Aluminium superstructures reduce top weight** → ↑ GM, ↑ stability, ↑ payload.
- **Al–Steel transition joints** are the most frequently welded dissimilar interface on a modern vessel.
- **FSW is the gold standard** for marine Al–Steel joining since IACS approval in 2012.
- **Bimetallic transition strips** are pre-fabricated by explosion welding and roll-bonded into hull-superstructure interfaces.

### 🔬 Metallurgical Essentials
- Four notorious IMCs: **FeAl₃, FeAl₂, Fe₂Al₅, FeAl** (memorize "**3-2-5-1**").
- **Fe₂Al₅** is the most brittle and most detrimental.
- **IMC critical thickness: < 5 μm acceptable, > 10 μm failure.**
- Parabolic growth: $x^2 = k t$, with $k = k_0 \exp(-Q / RT)$.
- Activation energy Q ≈ 140–170 kJ/mol for Fe diffusion in Al.

### ⚙️ Process Selection
- **Mechanical (rivet/bolt)** → simple, repairable, galvanic risk.
- **Adhesive + fastener** → corrosion-resistant, structural.
- **FSW** → strongest, lowest IMC growth, expensive tooling.
- **Explosion welding** → for transition plate manufacture, not in-situ.
- **Laser / MIG** → generally not recommended for primary Al–Steel joints.

### 🌊 Corrosion & Electrochemistry
- Aluminium is the **anode** (−0.75 to −0.95 V SCE), steel is the **cathode** (−0.60 to −0.70 V SCE).
- **Small anode / large cathode** → catastrophic corrosion.
- Use **isolation gaskets, sleeves, coatings, sacrificial anodes**.
- **EPDM, nylon, PVC** are standard isolation materials.

### 📐 High-Yield Equations
- $x^2 = k t$ (IMC growth)
- $k = k_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$ (Arrhenius)
- $H = \eta U I / (v \cdot 1000)$ (Heat input, kJ/mm)
- $\sigma_{res} = E \Delta \alpha \Delta T$ (Thermal stress)
- $i_a = (E_C - E_A)(A_c / A_a) / [R_s (1 + A_c / A_a)]$ (Galvanic current)

### 🎯 KTU 2024 Exam Strategy
- For 3-mark questions: focus on **definitions + 2 methods + 1 numerical example**.
- For 14-mark questions: expect a derivation in part (a) and a process discussion in part (b).
- **Always draw a labelled sketch** of joint geometry or FSW tool in long answers.
- **Always mention both mechanical and corrosion aspects** — examiners reward holistic answers.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Mnemonic for KTU Viva & Exam:**
> **"M-I-G-C"** — **M**elting mismatch, **I**MC control, **G**alvanic isolation, **C**oating/protection. These are the four pillars every Al–Steel joint answer must address.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Residual Stresses and Distortion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Residual Stresses and Distortion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Residual Stresses?

**Residual Stresses (RS)** are the internal stresses that remain locked within a material after the original cause of the stress (external force, heat gradient, or plastic deformation) has been removed. In shipbuilding, these stresses persist in welded plates, rolled sections, and cast components even when the structure is not subjected to any service load. They are self-equilibrating — tensile stresses in one region are balanced by compressive stresses in another.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Residual stresses are time-independent, locked-in elastic stresses that exist in a free body in the absence of any applied external loading. They originate from non-uniform plastic deformation, phase transformations, or thermal gradients induced during manufacturing (welding, forming, machining, heat treatment).*

### 1.2 What is Distortion?

**Distortion** is the irreversible change in shape or dimensions of a fabricated component caused by the non-uniform distribution of residual stresses and plastic strains. In welded ship structures, distortion manifests as warping, bending, twisting, and dimensional inaccuracy — directly affecting hull line accuracy, plate alignment, and structural fit-up.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Sun-Baked Wooden Plank" Model

Imagine a thick wooden plank left out in the sun. The top surface heats up, expands, and dries faster than the shaded underside. As the sun sets and the plank cools unevenly, the top fibers contract more than the bottom ones. The plank warps into a gentle curve — the *convex side facing the sun*. The wood now contains "locked-in" stresses even though no external force acts on it. If you cut the plank, it springs open because the stresses release.

**A welded non-ferrous ship plate behaves identically.** The weld pool heats a small zone to near melting (~650 °C for aluminum), the surrounding metal remains cold. On cooling, the heated zone wants to shrink, but is restrained by the cooler base metal → internal residual stresses + macroscopic shape change (distortion).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for Shipbuilding:** Non-ferrous materials (especially **aluminum alloys** used in superstructures and **copper-nickel alloys** for piping) have **thermal expansion coefficients nearly twice that of mild steel** → they are **far more susceptible** to welding distortion. This is one of the most tested KTU concepts.

## 1.4 Significance in Shipbuilding

| Application Area | Non-Ferrous Material | Distortion Concern |
|------------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Superstructure | Al-Mg alloys (5083, 5086) | Panel buckling, hull line deviation |
| Piping systems | Cu-Ni (90/10, 70/30) | Pipe misalignment, flange leakage |
| Propellers | Ni-Al bronze, Manganese bronze | Blade pitch deviation |
| Fasteners & rivets | Monel, Aluminium bronze | Stress-corrosion cracking |
| Cryogenic tanks | Aluminium 5083-O | Warping affecting insulation gap |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Reference:** Det Norske Veritas (DNV), Bureau Veritas (BV), and **IACS (International Association of Classification Societies)** welding standards set acceptable distortion tolerances for ship hull panels — typically **L/1000** (where L is plate length) for butt welds.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Residual Stresses (by Length Scale)

KTU examiners frequently ask to classify RS. The **three-tier system** is mandatory:

1. **Macro / Type I Residual Stresses** — Vary over dimensions comparable to the component size (millimeters to meters). They cause distortion. They are the only type that can be measured by X-ray diffraction over macroscopic areas.
2. **Micro / Type II Residual Stresses** — Vary over grain dimensions (micrometers). They exist between grains and are caused by anisotropic plastic flow.
3. **Sub-micro / Type III Residual Stresses** — Atomic-level stresses within the lattice, around dislocations, vacancies, and solute atoms.

> [!NOTE]
> In shipbuilding practice, "residual stresses" usually refers to **Type I macro stresses**, since they directly cause measurable distortion and affect structural integrity.

## 2.2 Sources of Residual Stresses in Non-Ferrous Ship Structures

### A. Welding-Induced Residual Stresses (Dominant Source)
- **Thermal origin:** Steep temperature gradients during weld pool solidification
- **Mechanical origin:** Restraint from surrounding cool base metal
- **Metallurgical origin:** Precipitation hardening alloys (e.g., Al-Cu 2014, Al-Zn 7075) suffer phase-transformation stresses

### B. Forming-Induced Residual Stresses
- Cold rolling of aluminium plates (used for ship side shells)
- Cold bending of pipe sections for piping
- Hydroforming of complex panels

### C. Heat Treatment Residual Stresses
- Quenching of thick aluminium forgings (propeller hubs)
- Age-hardening of 6061-T6

### D. Mechanical Processing
- Shot peening (intentionally introduces compressive RS to improve fatigue life)
- Machining (cuts through previously balanced RS fields)

## 2.3 The Three-Stage Genesis of Welding Residual Stress

The KTU board loves a clear process-based answer. Use this **three-stage framework** in 14-mark answers:

**Stage 1 — Heating (Thermal Expansion Phase)**
- Heat source creates molten weld pool
- Surrounding HAZ (Heat Affected Zone) tries to expand thermally
- Cooler base metal restrains this expansion → **compressive stresses develop**
- Material in HAZ may yield (yield strength drops sharply at elevated temperature)

**Stage 2 — Peak Temperature / Solidification**
- Weld pool solidifies, metal is essentially stress-free at high T
- Peak temperature reached; yield strength at minimum

**Stage 3 — Cooling (Thermal Contraction Phase)**
- Weld metal and HAZ contract on cooling
- If unrestrained, contraction would be: $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T$
- Surrounding cool base metal **restrains** the contraction
- Because contraction strain cannot be accommodated, **tensile residual stresses** are locked in
- These tensile stresses are typically of magnitude **equal to the yield strength at room temperature** in the longitudinal direction (near the weld line)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why non-ferrous alloys distort more than steel?**
> - **Aluminium 5083:** $\alpha \approx 23 \times 10^{-6}/°C$
> - **Mild Steel:** $\alpha \approx 12 \times 10^{-6}/°C$
> - Aluminium contracts **~2× more** for the same temperature drop → **~2× greater shrinkage strain** → **~2× greater distortion tendency**

## 2.4 Types of Welding Distortion (The "Five Villains")

This is a KTU 2024 high-yield classification — memorize it.

1. **Longitudinal Shrinkage (Transverse Distortion)** — Contraction along weld axis pulls the plate edges → causes the plate to **bow in the direction perpendicular to the weld** (transverse bowing)
2. **Transverse Shrinkage (Longitudinal Distortion)** — Contraction perpendicular to weld axis → plate **shortens** along the weld line
3. **Angular Distortion (Rotation)** — Uneven heat distribution through plate thickness → one face contracts more → plate **rotates about the weld axis** (typical in V-groove butt welds)
4. **Buckling Distortion (Wave-like)** — Compressive longitudinal residual stresses cause thin plates to **buckle out-of-plane** (a wavy, undulating pattern). **Most common in aluminium superstructure panels.**
5. **Twisting Distortion** — Combination of longitudinal + transverse + angular stresses in an unbalanced joint → plate **twists helicoidally**

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in Part A, Part B numericals, and viva voce.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Free thermal strain | $\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | dimensionless | Strain that *would* occur if unrestrained |
| 2 | Free thermal expansion | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T$ | m, mm | Length change of a free bar |
| 3 | Welding Heat Input (per unit length) | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | Energy deposited per mm of weld |
| 4 | Restraint-induced residual stress (elastic) | $\sigma_{rs} = E \cdot \varepsilon_{th} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | MPa (N/mm²) | Stress if full restraint, elastic response |
| 5 | Plastic strain component (if yielded) | $\varepsilon_{pl} = \varepsilon_{th} - \dfrac{\sigma_{ys}}{E}$ | dimensionless | Permanent strain locked in |
| 6 | Angular distortion (Butt joint, thick plate) | $\theta \approx \dfrac{\alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot h}{2L}$ | radians | Rotation angle of the plate |
| 7 | Buckling stress (thin plate, longitudinal) | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k \cdot \pi^{2} \cdot E}{12 \cdot (1-\nu^{2})} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^{2}$ | MPa | Critical compressive stress for out-of-plane buckling |
| 8 | Neutral axis position (T-joint) | $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum A_i y_i}{\sum A_i}$ | mm | Used for shrinkage force calculation |
| 9 | Effective shrinkage force (per unit length) | $F_s = \sigma_{ys} \cdot t \cdot (1 - R)$ | N/mm | Force pulling weld region inward; R = restraint factor |
| 10 | Distortion angular strain | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot Q}{c \cdot \rho \cdot h}$ | rad | Relationship with heat input Q |

> Where:
> - $\alpha$ = coefficient of linear thermal expansion (per °C)
> - $\Delta T$ = temperature change (°C or K)
> - $E$ = Young's modulus (GPa)
> - $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio (≈ 0.33 for Al, 0.34 for Cu-Ni, 0.30 for steel)
> - $\sigma_{ys}$ = yield strength at room temperature (MPa)
> - $V$ = arc voltage (V), $I$ = welding current (A), $S$ = travel speed (mm/s)
> - $\eta$ = arc efficiency (0.6–0.85 for GMAW, 0.4–0.6 for GTAW)
> - $t$ = plate thickness, $b$ = plate width, $h$ = heat input parameter
> - $k$ = buckling coefficient (≈ 4.0 for simply supported long plate, 6.97 for clamped)

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hull block assembly:** Predicting shrinkage of aluminium panels before final fit-up avoids costly re-cutting
- **Propeller manufacture:** Predicting blade twist in Ni-Al bronze castings sets machining allowance
- **Piping systems:** Estimating spring-back in Cu-Ni bends prevents flange mis-match at shipboard joints
- **Fatigue life assessment:** Residual tensile stresses superimposed on cyclic service loads drastically reduce fatigue endurance (using the **Goodman/Soderberg** criterion, the mean stress shifts upward by $\sigma_{rs}$)
- **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC):** Residual tensile stress is one of the **three pillars of SCC** (susceptible alloy + corrosive environment + tensile stress) — especially critical for aluminium-bronze propellers in seawater

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Origin of Longitudinal Residual Stress in a Restrained Bar

### Problem Setup
A non-ferrous bar (length $L_0$, modulus $E$, CTE $\alpha$) is uniformly heated by $\Delta T$ while being **fully restrained** at both ends. After cooling back to room temperature, find the **locked-in residual stress**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Free thermal strain (unrestrained case):**
If the bar were free to expand, the strain would be:

$$\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — Restraint condition (real case):**
The bar is clamped, so total strain must be zero:

$$\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_{th} + \varepsilon_{mech} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the mechanical (elastic) strain:**

$$\varepsilon_{mech} = -\alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 4 — Apply Hooke's Law to get mechanical stress:**

$$\sigma_{mech} = E \cdot \varepsilon_{mech} = -E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

The negative sign indicates **compressive** stress while the bar is hot and restrained.

**Step 5 — Cooling and stress reversal:**
On cooling, $\Delta T$ reverses. If the bar yields during the hot phase (because $E \alpha \Delta T > \sigma_{ys}$), plastic strain $\varepsilon_{pl}$ is locked in. The remaining elastic strain upon cooling is:

$$\varepsilon_{el} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T - \varepsilon_{pl}$$

**Step 6 — Final residual stress at room temperature:**

$$\sigma_{rs} = E \cdot (\alpha \cdot \Delta T - \varepsilon_{pl})$$

If the bar yields fully during the hot phase, the residual stress equals the room-temperature yield strength (tensile):

$$\sigma_{rs} = +\sigma_{ys}$$

### Numerical Worked Example (Aluminium 5083-H116)

Given:
- $\alpha_{Al} = 23 \times 10^{-6} / °C$
- $E_{Al} = 70$ GPa $= 70\,000$ MPa
- $\Delta T = 580$ °C (from peak ~625 °C down to 25 °C)
- $\sigma_{ys} = 215$ MPa (5083-H116)

**Check whether yielding occurs during heating:**

$$E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T = 70\,000 \times 23 \times 10^{-6} \times 580 = 933.8 \text{ MPa}$$

Since 933.8 MPa $\gg$ 215 MPa (yield strength at room temperature, and even more so at high T where it is much lower), **the material yields in compression** during heating. Therefore, plastic strain develops.

**Locked-in tensile residual stress after cooling:**

$$\sigma_{rs} \approx +\sigma_{ys} = +215 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** The weld zone in a ship panel carries residual tensile stresses of the **same order as the yield strength of the parent metal**. This is why the IACS and DNV rules require **post-weld stress-relief treatments** (e.g., hammer peening, low-frequency vibratory stress relief) for critical non-ferrous marine components.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Welding Heat Input and Its Link to Distortion

### Step 1 — Definition of Heat Input
The arc energy per unit length of weld is:

$$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \text{(kJ/mm)}$$

### Step 2 — Net Heat Available for Distortion
Only a fraction of $H$ raises the plate temperature above the "distortion-causing" threshold. An empirical fit for non-ferrous welding:

$$Q_{net} = 0.8 \cdot H \cdot A_{weld}$$

where $A_{weld}$ is the cross-section of the fusion zone (mm²).

### Step 3 — Transverse Angular Distortion Estimate
For a single-V butt weld in a plate of thickness $t$:

$$\theta = \frac{0.5 \cdot \alpha \cdot Q_{net}}{c \cdot \rho \cdot t^{2}}$$

**Step-by-step substitution for a typical aluminium 5083 panel:**

Given:
- $V = 24$ V, $I = 220$ A, $S = 5$ mm/s
- $\eta = 0.75$ (GMAW), $c = 900$ J/(kg·K), $\rho = 2660$ kg/m³, $t = 6$ mm
- $A_{weld} = 18$ mm² (single-V 60° included angle, 6 mm root + 2 mm land)
- $\alpha = 23 \times 10^{-6} / °C$

**Step 4 — Compute H:**

$$H = \frac{0.75 \times 24 \times 220}{5 \times 1000} = \frac{3960}{5000} = 0.792 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Q\_net:**

$$Q_{net} = 0.8 \times 0.792 \times 18 = 11.4 \text{ J/mm} = 11\,400 \text{ J/m}$$

**Step 6 — Compute angular distortion:**

$$\theta = \frac{0.5 \times 23 \times 10^{-6} \times 11\,400}{900 \times 2660 \times (0.006)^{2}}$$

$$\theta = \frac{0.1311}{86.23} = 1.52 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to degrees:**

$$\theta_{deg} = 1.52 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 0.087°$$

Over a 1-metre panel, the edge lifts by:

$$\delta = L \cdot \tan\theta \approx L \cdot \theta = 1000 \times 1.52 \times 10^{-3} = 1.52 \text{ mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This 1.52 mm distortion across 1 m exceeds the IACS tolerance of L/1000 = 1.0 mm** → corrective action is required (backstep welding, clamping, or reduced heat input).

## 3.3 Derivation III — Critical Buckling Stress for a Welded Aluminium Plate

### Setup
A 6 mm × 1000 mm × 2000 mm aluminium 5083 panel has longitudinal compressive residual stress $\sigma_c = 80$ MPa after welding. Will it buckle?

### Step 1 — Plate geometry parameters
- $b = 1000$ mm (width, restrained along longitudinal edges by stiffeners)
- $t = 6$ mm
- $L = 2000$ mm (length, simply supported at ends)
- Aspect ratio $L/b = 2.0$ → plate behaviour controlled by width

### Step 2 — Buckling coefficient
For a long plate ($L/b \geq 2$) clamped on loaded edges:

$$k \approx 6.97$$

### Step 3 — Material constants
- $E = 70\,000$ MPa
- $\nu = 0.33$

### Step 4 — Apply the buckling formula:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{k \cdot \pi^{2} \cdot E}{12 \cdot (1 - \nu^{2})} \cdot \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^{2}$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{6.97 \times (3.1416)^{2} \times 70\,000}{12 \times (1 - 0.33^{2})} \times \left(\frac{6}{1000}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate numerator:**

$$6.97 \times 9.8696 \times 70\,000 = 4\,815\,800$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate denominator (first part):**

$$12 \times (1 - 0.1089) = 12 \times 0.8911 = 10.6932$$

**Step 7 — Plate slenderness term:**

$$\left(\frac{6}{1000}\right)^{2} = 3.6 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 8 — Combine:**

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{4\,815\,800}{10.6932} \times 3.6 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = 450\,336 \times 3.6 \times 10^{-5} = 16.21 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 9 — Compare with applied compressive residual stress:**

$$\sigma_c = 80 \text{ MPa} \;\; \text{vs} \;\; \sigma_{cr} = 16.21 \text{ MPa}$$

Since $\sigma_c \gg \sigma_{cr}$, **the panel WILL buckle** → out-of-plane wave distortion is expected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design remedy:** Increase plate thickness to 8 mm or add intermediate stiffeners. Re-checking with $t = 8$ mm yields $\sigma_{cr} = 28.8$ MPa (still insufficient → need stiffeners at 500 mm spacing, which raises $k$ to ~10 and reduces effective $b$ to 500 mm → $\sigma_{cr}$ becomes ~96 MPa, safe).

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Mitigation Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as the structured answer backbone for "Discuss methods to control distortion in aluminium ship welds" type questions.**

| Mitigation Method | Physics Used | Effectiveness for Al | Cost Impact |
|-------------------|--------------|----------------------|-------------|
| **Reduced heat input** | Lowers $H$, smaller HAZ | High — primary method | Low (process control) |
| **Backstep / Skip welding** | Disperses shrinkage strain | High for thin panels | Low (slower welding) |
| **Symmetrical weld sequence** | Balances shrinkage forces | Very high — *primary shipyard technique* | Free |
| **Clamping / Fixturing** | External restraint | Very high | High (fixture cost) |
| **Preheating (≤ 80 °C for Al)** | Reduces $\Delta T$ gradient | Moderate (Al is sensitive) | Moderate |
| **Vibratory Stress Relief (VSR)** | Micro-plastic relaxation | Moderate, controversial | Low |
| **Hammer peening** | Induces surface compressive RS | High — used in marine practice | Low |
| **Weld sequence planning** | Distributes total shrinkage | Very high | Free (engineering) |
| **Cryogenic cooling (CO₂)** | Steepens local cooling | Used rarely | Moderate |
| **Use of low-shrinkage filler** (e.g., 4043 vs 5356) | Lower $\sigma_{ys}$ of weld metal | Moderate | Marginal |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Genesis of Welding Residual Stress in Non-Ferrous Ship Plates

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid diagram** showing the causal chain from welding process input to final structural consequence. All node IDs are alphanumeric and contain no reserved keywords.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Process Input: Welding Heat] --> B1[Temperature Field Formation]
    B1 --> C1[Weld Pool at Melting Point]
    C1 --> D1[Steep Thermal Gradient across HAZ]
    D1 --> E1[Non-uniform Thermal Expansion]
    E1 --> F1[Restraint by Surrounding Cool Base Metal]
    F1 --> G1[Plastic Yielding in HAZ]
    G1 --> H1[Locking-in of Plastic Strain on Cooling]
    H1 --> I1[Equilibrium of Tensile and Compressive Zones]
    I1 --> J1[Type I Macro Residual Stress Field]
    J1 --> K1[Macroscopic Distortion: Bowing and Angular Rotation]
    J1 --> L1[Fatigue Life Reduction]
    J1 --> M1[Stress Corrosion Cracking Risk]
    K1 --> N1[Hull Line Deviation and Re-work]
    L1 --> O1[In-service Crack Initiation]
    M1 --> P1[Marine Environment Failure]

    Q1[Material Property: High Alpha] --> E1
    Q1 --> K1
    R1[Material Property: Low E] --> F1
    R1 --> G1
    S1[Process Parameter: High Heat Input] --> B1
    S1 --> D1
    S1 --> H1
    T1[Geometric Factor: Thin Plate] --> K1
    T1 --> L1
    U1[Mitigation Block: Symmetric Sequence] --> N1
    V1[Mitigation Block: Reduced Heat Input] --> D1
    V1 --> H1
    W1[Mitigation Block: External Fixturing] --> F1
    W1 --> K1
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Distortion Type Classification Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Welding Residual Stress and Distortion] --> B1[Longitudinal Direction]
    A1 --> C1[Transverse Direction]
    A1 --> D1[Through-thickness Direction]
    A1 --> E1[In-plane vs Out-of-plane]

    B1 --> B2[Transverse Shrinkage causes Plate Shortening]
    B1 --> B3[Longitudinal Tensile Stress along Weld]

    C1 --> C2[Longitudinal Shrinkage causes Edge Bowing]
    C1 --> C3[Transverse Compressive Stress away from Weld]

    D1 --> D2[Angular Distortion from Thickness Gradient]
    D1 --> D3[Lamellar Tearing Risk]

    E1 --> E2[Buckling Distortion for Thin Plates]

```

## 4.3 Schematic Process Flow — Shipyard Distortion Control Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Design Stage: Plate Layout and Joint Selection] --> B1[Process Planning: WPS and PQR]
    B1 --> C1[Pre-fabrication: Edge Preparation and Fit-up]
    C1 --> D1[Fixture Selection: Strong-back vs Light Clamp]
    D1 --> E1[Welding Stage 1: Tack Welds Symmetric]
    E1 --> F1[Welding Stage 2: Backstep Sequence]
    F1 --> G1[Welding Stage 3: Final Cap Pass]
    G1 --> H1[In-process Measurement: Laser Scanner]
    H1 --> I1{Distortion Within Tolerance?}
    I1 -->|Yes| J1[Release for Next Block]
    I1 -->|No| K1[Correction: Flame Heating or Mechanical Straightening]
    K1 --> H1
    J1 --> L1[Post-weld Stress Relief: Peening or VSR]
    L1 --> M1[NDT Inspection]
    M1 --> N1[Block Ready for Erection]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B1 --> B2[Examples: Cu-Zn Brasses, Al-Cu 2014, Al-Zn 7075]
    C1 --> C2[Chloride Ions, Dissolved Oxygen, Marine Biofilm]

    D2 --> D4[Service Stress from Hull Loads]
    D2 --> D5[Fitting Stress from Cold Bending]

    D1 --> D2[Sources of Tensile Stress]
    B2 --> E1[Initiation of Intergranular Cracks]
    C2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define residual stress. List any two sources of residual stress in welded non-ferrous ship structures.**

**Model Answer:**

Residual stress is the internal stress that remains in a material after the original cause (external load, thermal gradient, or plastic deformation) has been removed. It is self-equilibrating within the body.

Two sources in welded non-ferrous ship structures:
1. **Welding thermal gradient** — differential heating and cooling across the weld zone locks in tensile stresses near the weld and compressive stresses in the surrounding base metal.
2. **Cold forming of plates and pipes** — plastic deformation during rolling or bending leaves surface residual stresses that oppose the original deformation direction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "residual stress comes from load" — this is wrong. RS exists *without* external load. State this distinction explicitly in the opening line.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
**Why are aluminium alloys more prone to welding distortion than mild steel?**

**Model Answer:**

Aluminium alloys have a coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha_{Al} \approx 23 \times 10^{-6} / °C$, which is **nearly twice** that of mild steel ($\alpha_{steel} \approx 12 \times 10^{-6} / °C$). For the same temperature drop $\Delta T$, the unrestrained thermal strain $\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ is therefore approximately **double** in aluminium.

Since welding residual stress and distortion are directly proportional to $\alpha \cdot \Delta T$, aluminium welds contract more aggressively on cooling and produce larger shrinkage forces and more pronounced distortion (especially buckling in thin superstructure panels). Additionally, aluminium's lower modulus ($E_{Al} = 70$ GPa vs $E_{steel} = 200$ GPa) provides less resistance to bending, amplifying the visible distortion.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — With Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Q7]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **five major types of welding distortion** encountered during fabrication of aluminium ship superstructures. Mention the dominant physical cause for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6 mm thick aluminium 5083 plate (1000 mm × 2000 mm) is welded along its centre line by GMAW with the following parameters: $V = 24$ V, $I = 220$ A, $S = 5$ mm/s, arc efficiency $\eta = 0.75$. The thermal expansion coefficient is $23 \times 10^{-6} /°C$. Calculate the **heat input per unit length** and the **free thermal strain** for $\Delta T = 580 °C$. Comment on whether the resulting shrinkage will exceed the IACS tolerance of L/1000. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Five Types of Distortion:**

| # | Type | Sketch (verbal description) | Dominant Physical Cause |
|---|------|------------------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Transverse Shrinkage** | Plate shortens along weld axis (length reduces) | Contraction perpendicular to weld restrained by base metal |
| 2 | **Longitudinal Shrinkage (Bowing)** | Plate edges bow inward, away from weld | Contraction along weld axis pulls edges toward weld line |
| 3 | **Angular Distortion** | One face of plate tilts relative to other at the weld | Uneven temperature distribution across thickness in V-groove welds |
| 4 | **Buckling Distortion** | Wavy, undulating out-of-plane pattern in thin plate | Compressive longitudinal RS exceeds critical buckling stress |
| 5 | **Twisting Distortion** | Plate ends rotate in opposite directions (helicoidal) | Combined unbalanced shrinkage in unsymmetric T or L joints |

> **Note:** Each distortion is worth 1.4 marks. The sketch + cause together fetch 1.4 × 5 = 7 marks. Use a simple top-view and side-view drawing.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Heat input per unit length:**

$$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000}$$

$$H = \frac{0.75 \times 24 \times 220}{5 \times 1000} = \frac{3960}{5000} = 0.792 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**Step 2 — Free thermal strain:**

$$\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T = 23 \times 10^{-6} \times 580 = 0.01334 = 1.334\%$$

**Step 3 — Free thermal expansion (unrestrained):**

$$\Delta L = \varepsilon_{th} \cdot L_0 = 0.01334 \times 2000 = 26.68 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Effective shrinkage considering partial restraint:**
The restraint factor for a 1000 mm wide plate is approximately $R = 0.45$ (empirical for ship panels):

$$\Delta L_{eff} = \Delta L \cdot (1 - R) = 26.68 \times 0.55 = 14.67 \text{ mm}$$

Wait — this is the unrestrained case. For the actual distortion observed, the **angular distortion** at each weld pass is the relevant quantity. From the previous derivation (Section 3.2):

$$\delta_{edge} = L \cdot \theta = 1000 \times 1.52 \times 10^{-3} = 1.52 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — IACS tolerance check:**

$$\text{Tolerance} = \frac{L}{1000} = \frac{1000}{1000} = 1.0 \text{ mm}$$

Since $1.52 \text{ mm} > 1.0 \text{ mm}$, **the distortion EXCEEDS the IACS tolerance**.

**Step 6 — Recommended corrective action:**
- Adopt **backstep welding sequence** to reduce effective heat input per zone
- Apply **symmetric tack welding and clamping fixture**
- Consider **hammer peening** of final cap pass to introduce surface compressive stresses
- Alternative: Reduce $I$ to 180 A → $H$ becomes 0.648 kJ/mm → $\theta$ reduces by ~18% → distortion drops below 1.0 mm

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert $S$ to mm/s when using the kJ/mm formula — denominator must be $S \times 1000$, not $S$ alone.
> 2. Using $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ (steel value) for aluminium — guarantees zero marks.
> 3. Not stating units (kJ/mm) — examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 4. Skipping the "IACS tolerance check" interpretation — you must conclude with whether the result is acceptable.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Module 2, Q8]**

**(a)** With the aid of a stress-distortion diagram, discuss the **three-stage genesis** of welding residual stress. Explain why the locked-in residual stress magnitude equals the **yield strength at room temperature** in the longitudinal direction near the weld. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4 mm × 800 mm × 1500 mm aluminium 5083 panel is butt-welded. The longitudinal compressive residual stress adjacent to the weld is 65 MPa. Given $E = 70$ GPa and $\nu = 0.33$, determine whether the panel will undergo **out-of-plane buckling distortion**. Use the plate buckling formula with $k = 4.0$ (simply supported edges). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Three-Stage Genesis:**

*Stage 1 — Heating Phase:*
When the welding arc moves along the joint, the heat source raises the local metal temperature to near the melting point (~650 °C for Al 5083). The weld pool and surrounding HAZ attempt to expand thermally. Surrounding cold base metal restrains this expansion, generating **compressive stresses** in the HAZ. Because the yield strength drops sharply at high temperature, the material yields in compression.

*Stage 2 — Peak Temperature / Solidification:*
The weld pool solidifies. Stresses in the molten pool are essentially zero (fluid cannot sustain shear). As solidification completes, the metal begins to cool.

*Stage 3 — Cooling Phase:*
The HAZ tries to contract thermally. The surrounding base metal, which is now acting as a rigid anchor, prevents this contraction. The previously compressed-and-yielded material is now pulled into tension. Because plastic strain $\varepsilon_{pl}$ is locked in, the final elastic strain upon reaching room temperature is:

$$\varepsilon_{el} = \varepsilon_{th} - \varepsilon_{pl}$$

The corresponding stress is:

$$\sigma_{rs} = E \cdot \varepsilon_{el}$$

If yielding was complete during the hot phase, $\varepsilon_{el}$ adjusts such that $\sigma_{rs} = \sigma_{ys}^{RT}$ (room-temperature yield strength). Therefore, the longitudinal residual stress near the weld line is tensile and **bounded by the room-temperature yield strength** of the base metal.

**Part (b) — Buckling Check:**

**Step 1 — Plate parameters:**
- $b = 800$ mm
- $t = 4$ mm
- $L = 1500$ mm → $L/b = 1.875$ → plate behaviour dominated by $b$ (use $b$ as controlling dimension)
- $E = 70\,000$ MPa, $\nu = 0.33$, $k = 4.0$

**Step 2 — Apply plate buckling formula:**

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{k \cdot \pi^{2} \cdot E}{12 \cdot (1 - \nu^{2})} \cdot \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the geometry term:**

$$\left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{4}{800}\right)^{2} = (0.005)^{2} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate denominator:**

$$1 - \nu^{2} = 1 - 0.1089 = 0.8911$$

$$12 \times 0.8911 = 10.6932$$

**Step 5 — Compute the leading factor:**

$$\frac{k \cdot \pi^{2} \cdot E}{12 (1 - \nu^{2})} = \frac{4.0 \times 9.8696 \times 70\,000}{10.6932}$$

$$= \frac{2\,763\,488}{10.6932} = 258\,426 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by geometry term:**

$$\sigma_{cr} = 258\,426 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5} = 6.46 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 7 — Compare with applied compressive residual stress:**

$$\sigma_{c,RS} = 65 \text{ MPa} \;\; \text{vs} \;\; \sigma_{cr} = 6.46 \text{ MPa}$$

Since $65 \text{ MPa} \gg 6.46 \text{ MPa}$, **the panel WILL buckle out-of-plane**.

**Step 8 — Design recommendation:**
To prevent buckling, $\sigma_{cr}$ must exceed 65 MPa. Required plate thickness:

$$t_{req} = b \cdot \sqrt{\frac{12 \cdot (1 - \nu^{2}) \cdot \sigma_{applied}}{k \cdot \pi^{2} \cdot E}}$$

$$t_{req} = 800 \times \sqrt{\frac{10.6932 \times 65}{2\,763\,488}} = 800 \times \sqrt{2.516 \times 10^{-4}} = 800 \times 0.01586 = 12.7 \text{ mm}$$

A more practical solution: introduce **intermediate stiffeners** at 400 mm spacing to reduce effective $b$ to 400 mm → $\sigma_{cr}$ rises by factor of 4 → $\sigma_{cr} = 25.8$ MPa (still insufficient) → use 200 mm spacing → $\sigma_{cr} = 103.4$ MPa ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. Choosing the wrong $b$ — use the **smaller** plate dimension (width), not the length.
> 2. Forgetting the $(1 - \nu^{2})$ correction — without it, $\sigma_{cr}$ is overestimated by ~12%.
> 3. Failing to **conclude with design recommendation** — the problem asks whether buckling will occur AND what to do. Skipping the remediation loses 2 marks.
> 4. Mixing up units (mm vs m in $b$ and $t$) — the ratio $t/b$ is dimensionless, but consistency matters when computing other quantities.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist — read this 30 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- **Residual Stress Definition:** Locked-in stress with no external load; self-equilibrating; classified as Type I (macro), II (micro), III (sub-micro). **Shipbuilding cares about Type I only.**

- **Three-Stage Genesis:** Heating (compressive yield) → Solidification (stress-free) → Cooling (tensile lock-in). The final tensile stress = **room-temperature yield strength** near the weld.

- **Free Thermal Strain Formula:** $\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ — the master equation. Memorize it.

- **Welding Heat Input:** $H = \eta V I / (S \times 1000)$ kJ/mm — required for every numerical problem in Part B.

- **Five Villains of Distortion:** Longitudinal bowing, Transverse shrinkage, Angular rotation, Buckling, Twisting. **Buckling is the silent killer in thin aluminium panels** — always check $\sigma_{cr}$ for any plate thinner than 8 mm.

- **Plate Buckling Formula:** $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k \pi^{2} E}{12(1 - \nu^{2})} (t/b)^{2}$ — memorize the entire expression including the $(1-\nu^{2})$ term. $k$ depends on edge conditions: 4.0 (simply supported), 6.97 (clamped), higher for stiffened panels.

- **Why Non-Ferrous Distorts More:** $\alpha_{Al} \approx 2 \times \alpha_{steel}$ AND $E_{Al} \approx 1/3 \times E_{steel}$. Both effects compound to make aluminium ~2× more distortion-prone.

- **Material Constants (memorize these three):**
  - Aluminium 5083: $\alpha = 23 \times 10^{-6} /°C$, $E = 70$ GPa, $\nu = 0.33$, $\sigma_{ys} = 215$ MPa
  - Cu-Ni 90/10: $\alpha = 17 \times 10^{-6} /°C$, $E =130$ GPa
  - Mild steel: $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$, $E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.30$

- **SCC Triangle in Marine Service:** Susceptible alloy + Chloride environment + Tensile RS → Cracking. Aluminium bronzes and Cu-Zn brasses are especially vulnerable.

- **Mitigation Hierarchy (use in 14-mark answers):** Symmetric weld sequence → Reduced heat input → Backstep technique → Fixturing → Peening → Stress-relief heat treatment.

- **IACS Tolerance Rule of Thumb:** Plate edge distortion $\leq L/1000$ (longitudinal) and $\leq b/1000$ (transverse). Always quote this when answering design-type questions.

- **Numerical Pitfalls:**
  1. Unit conversion for $S$ in heat input formula
  2. Choosing correct $b$ (smaller dimension) in buckling formula
  3. Including $(1-\nu^2)$ correction
  4. Stating final answer with units
  5. Adding a concluding engineering interpretation

- **Examiner's Pet Topics (high probability of being asked):**
  1. Comparison of distortion tendency — aluminium vs steel
  2. Five types of distortion with diagrams
  3. Heat input numerical for a given GMAW/GTAW process
  4. Buckling check for a thin welded panel
  5. Mitigation methods for superstructure welding

> **Final Tip:** Always end a 14-mark answer with one **practical shipyard recommendation** (e.g., "In the context of an LNG carrier's aluminium dome support, the use of symmetric backstep welding with $I \leq 180$ A is recommended"). This signals engineering maturity and routinely fetches 1–2 bonus marks from KTU examiners.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fatigue and Fracture Design and Analysis Procedures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Fatigue and Fracture Design and Analysis Procedures ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Fatigue** is the progressive, localized structural damage caused by cyclic loading at stress levels significantly below the static yield strength of the material, leading to crack initiation, sub-critical crack propagation, and ultimately sudden catastrophic fracture. In the context of **non-ferrous materials in shipbuilding**, fatigue analysis addresses the response of aluminum alloys (5083-H116, 6082-T6, marine-grade Al-Mg-Si), copper-nickel alloys (Cu-Ni 90/10, 70/30), titanium alloys (Ti-6Al-4V), and fiber-reinforced composites to wave-induced, propeller-excited, and thermal cyclic loads. **Fracture Mechanics Design** is the quantitative engineering discipline that predicts the load-carrying capacity of structures containing pre-existing flaws (cracks, weld defects, corrosion pits) using the **Stress Intensity Factor (K)**, **J-Integral**, and **Energy Release Rate (G)**. For non-ferrous marine structures, fracture design is governed by **detailed classification society rules** (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, BV) that mandate defect acceptance criteria and inspection intervals. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlight:** The syllabus integrates classical (S-N) fatigue with modern fracture mechanics (Paris Law) because non-ferrous marine alloys — particularly **aluminum ship hulls and superstructures** — often fail by fatigue at welded joints, propeller roots, and bilge struts where stress concentrations are unavoidable. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition **The Paper Clip Analogy:** Take a standard steel paperclip and bend it back and forth repeatedly. Each individual bend applies a stress far below the paperclip's yield strength — you could never break it with a single bend. Yet, after 15–20 cycles, it snaps cleanly with no warning. The **fatigue limit** has been exceeded. **The Marine Translation:** A ship's hull plating experiences millions of wave-slamming cycles during its 25–30 year service life. Each wave imparts a small stress range (typically 30–80 MPa in aluminum hulls). Individually, these stresses are harmless. Cumulatively, they nucleate a microscopic crack at a weld toe. The crack grows invisibly for years. Suddenly — perhaps during a North Atlantic storm — the hull plate fractures. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Fatigue is **cumulative damage** — it is a *history-dependent* phenomenon. The order of loading, the mean stress, and the environment (corrosion, temperature) all influence life. This is why ship classification societies mandate **spectral fatigue analysis** rather than simple peak-stress checks. ### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics in Non-Ferrous Marine Fatigue - **S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve):** Stress amplitude (S) vs. cycles to failure (N). For aluminum alloys, no true endurance limit exists — fatigue strength is quoted at **N = 2×10⁶** or **5×10⁶** cycles. - **Fatigue Life Categories:** *Low-cycle fatigue* (LCF, N < 10⁴) — strain-controlled; *High-cycle fatigue* (HCF, N > 10⁴) — stress-controlled. - **Stress Range, Δσ = σ_max − σ_min** (MPa). The KTU convention uses the **two-parameter** R-ratio = σ_min / σ_max. - **Fracture Toughness, K_IC** (MPa√m) — critical stress intensity factor in plane strain. For marine Al-5083: K_IC ≈ 30–35 MPa√m. For Ti-6Al-4V: K_IC ≈ 55–75 MPa√m. --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Fatigue Failure — The Three-Stage Mechanism Fatigue is universally decomposed into three sequential stages. Each stage has a distinct physical mechanism and a corresponding analytical model: **Stage I — Crack Initiation (Nucleation):** - Microplastic slip bands form at persistent slip planes within surface grains. - Intrusions and extrusions develop on the free surface (slip-band decohesion). - In welded marine structures, initiation almost always occurs at the **weld toe** due to geometric stress concentration (typically K_t = 2.5–4.0) and microstructural softening in the HAZ (Heat-Affected Zone). - For non-ferrous alloys, initiation occupies **5–15%** of total life. For ultra-high-strength steels it is < 1%. **Stage II — Stable Crack Propagation:** - Crack advances incrementally per cycle, with the increment `da/dN` governed by the **Stress Intensity Factor range, ΔK**. - The fracture surface shows characteristic **striations** — each striation = one load cycle. - This stage is dominant (80–95% of life) and is the basis for damage-tolerant design. **Stage III — Unstable Fracture (Final Failure):** - When K_max approaches the material's fracture toughness **K_C** (or K_IC in plane strain), the crack accelerates catastrophically. - For ship hull plates, this is the final breach event. The transition from Stage II to III must be prevented by inspection scheduling. ### 2.2 The Three Design Philosophies (KTU Board-Exam Favourite) | Philosophy | Core Premise | Governing Equation | Marine Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Infinite-Life (Safe-Life)** | Keep stress below endurance limit | σ_a < σ_e | Non-critical superstructure panels | | **Finite-Life (S-N)** | Calculate damage using Miner's rule | D = Σ (n_i / N_i) | Hull plating, longitudinals | | **Damage-Tolerant (Fracture Mechanics)** | Assume cracks exist; schedule inspection | da/dN = C(ΔK)^m | Primary hull, propellers, rudder stocks | ### 2.3 The Mean Stress Problem — Goodman's Correction Because naval structures experience both wave-induced and residual-stress (from welding) mean stresses, the **fully-reversed fatigue strength (σ'_f)** must be corrected. The most common correction taught in KTU 2024 is the **Goodman relation**: $$\sigma_{ar} = \frac{\sigma'_f \cdot \sigma_u}{\sigma_u - \sigma_m}$$ For aluminum alloys (where S-N curves show no knee), the **Walker equation** is preferred: $$\sigma_{ar} = \sigma'_f \left( \frac{\sigma_u - \sigma_m}{\sigma_u} \right)^{1-\gamma}$$ where `γ` is the Walker exponent (typically 0.4–0.7 for marine Al-alloys). ### 2.4 Fracture Mechanics Foundation — The Stress Intensity Factor For a through-thickness crack of length `2a` in an infinite plate under remote uniform tension `σ`, the **Stress Intensity Factor (Mode I — opening mode)** is: $$K_I = \sigma \sqrt{\pi a} \cdot Y$$ The dimensionless factor `Y` accounts for geometry (crack shape, plate width, eccentricity, free-surface effects). For a central through-crack in a finite-width plate, **Tada's solution** gives: $$Y = \sqrt{\sec\left(\frac{\pi a}{W}\right)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Failure Criterion:** Brittle fracture occurs when K_I = K_IC. The critical crack size is: > $$a_c = \frac{1}{\pi Y^2} \left( \frac{K_{IC}}{\sigma_{applied}} \right)^2$$ > This is the foundation of **fitness-for-service (FFS)** assessments under DNV-RP-F112 and BS 7910. ### 2.5 The Paris-Erdogan Crack Growth Law (Stage II Master Equation) In 1963, P.C. Paris demonstrated that crack growth rate follows a power-law relationship with the cyclic stress intensity range: $$\frac{da}{dN} = C \cdot (\Delta K)^m$$ where: - `da/dN` = crack extension per cycle (m/cycle) - `ΔK = K_max − K_min = Y·Δσ·√(πa)` (MPa√m) - `C` and `m` are material-environment constants For typical marine applications: - **Al-alloy 5083 in seawater:** C ≈ 2.0×10⁻¹², m ≈ 3.0 (m, MPa√m units) - **Al-alloy 6082-T6 in air:** C ≈ 1.5×10⁻¹², m ≈ 3.4 - **Ti-6Al-4V (helicopter rotor hubs analog):** C ≈ 3.0×10⁻¹², m ≈ 3.5 The **integration** of Paris' law yields the cycles to grow a crack from initial size `a_i` to critical size `a_c`: $$N_f = \int_{a_i}^{a_c} \frac{da}{C \cdot (Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi a})^m}$$ For a constant-amplitude, constant-Y loading, this integrates analytically (see Section 3). ### 2.6 Cumulative Damage — Miner's Rule When the stress history is irregular (as in wave loading), it is discretized into `k` stress blocks: $$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i} = \frac{n_1}{N_1} + \frac{n_2}{N_2} + \cdots$$ - **Failure criterion:** D ≥ 1.0 (some codes use 0.5–0.8 for safety). - For non-ferrous marine structures, **DNV-RP-C203** uses a design fatigue factor (DFF) of 1.0–3.0 multiplied on the right-hand side. ### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Application | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\Delta\sigma = \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}$ | Stress range definition | MPa | | 2 | $R = \sigma_{min} / \sigma_{max}$ | Stress ratio | dimensionless | | 3 | $\sigma_{ar} = \sigma'_f \sigma_u / (\sigma_u - \sigma_m)$ | Goodman correction | MPa | | 4 | $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$ | Basquin S-N law | MPa | | 5 | $\varepsilon_a = (\sigma'_f/E)(2N_f)^b + \varepsilon'_f(2N_f)^c$ | Coffin-Manson (LCF) | dimensionless | | 6 | $K_I = \sigma \sqrt{\pi a} \cdot Y$ | Stress intensity factor | MPa√m | | 7 | $a_c = (K_{IC} / (\sigma Y \sqrt{\pi}))^2$ | Critical crack size | m | | 8 | $da/dN = C(\Delta K)^m$ | Paris law | m/cycle | | 9 | $N_f = (a_c^{1-m/2} - a_i^{1-m/2}) / (C(1-m/2)(Y\Delta\sigma\sqrt{\pi})^m)$ | Integrated life (m≠2) | cycles | | 10 | $D = \sum n_i / N_i$ | Miner cumulative damage | dimensionless | | 11 | $G = K_I^2 / E'$ | Energy release rate | N/m | | 12 | $K_{th} = \Delta K_0 (1-R)^{\gamma}$ | Walker threshold | MPa√m | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** These equations are codified in **DNV-RP-C203** (Fatigue), **DNV-RP-F112** (Fracture), **BS 7910** (UK), and **API 579-1/ASME FFS-1** — all of which are examiner-referenced standards for shipbuilding structures. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Integration of Paris' Law for Constant-Amplitude Loading **Starting Equation (Paris-Erdogan):** $$\frac{da}{dN} = C \cdot (Y \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi a})^m$$ **Step 1: Rearrange to separate variables** Rearranging by moving all `a`-dependent terms to the left and `dN` to the right: $$\frac{da}{[Y \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi a}]^m} = C \cdot dN$$ **Step 2: Extract constants** Note that `Y`, `Δσ`, and `π` are constants for a given load case. The denominator becomes: $$[Y \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi}]^m \cdot a^{m/2}$$ Therefore: $$\frac{da}{a^{m/2}} = C \cdot [Y \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi}]^m \cdot dN$$ **Step 3: Integrate both sides from initial to critical crack size** Integrating from `a = a_i` to `a = a_c` on the left, and from `N = 0` to `N = N_f` on the right: $$\int_{a_i}^{a_c} a^{-m/2} \, da = C \cdot [Y \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi}]^m \cdot \int_0^{N_f} dN$$ **Step 4: Evaluate the left integral** The antiderivative of `a^(-m/2)` with respect to `a` is `a^(1-m/2) / (1 - m/2)`, provided `m ≠ 2`: $$\left[ \frac{a^{1-m/2}}{1 - m/2} \right]_{a_i}^{a_c} = C \cdot [Y \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi}]^m \cdot N_f$$ **Step 5: Substitute the limits of integration** $$\frac{a_c^{1-m/2} - a_i^{1-m/2}}{1 - m/2} = C \cdot [Y \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi}]^m \cdot N_f$$ **Step 6: Solve for N_f** $$\boxed{N_f = \frac{a_c^{1-m/2} - a_i^{1-m/2}}{C \cdot (1 - m/2) \cdot (Y \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi})^m}}$$ This is the **master closed-form solution** for cycles-to-failure under constant amplitude. For `m = 2` (rare in marine non-ferrous), a logarithmic form applies. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Board Pattern) **Problem:** A marine **Al-5083-H116** hull plate (W = 200 mm, K_IC = 32 MPa√m) contains a central through-thickness crack of initial length 2a_i = 4 mm. The applied stress range is Δσ = 80 MPa with R = 0. Mean stress σ_m = 40 MPa. Material constants: C = 2.0×10⁻¹², m = 3.0, σ_u = 305 MPa, σ'_f = 130 MPa. Use Y = 1.0 for short cracks. Calculate: (a) The critical crack length 2a_c. (b) The total fatigue life N_f (in cycles) using Paris' law. #### Part (a): Critical Crack Size **Step A1 — Apply the critical-crack formula:** $$a_c = \frac{1}{\pi} \left( \frac{K_{IC}}{Y \cdot \sigma_{max}} \right)^2$$ **Step A2 — Determine σ_max from the given data:** For R = 0, σ_min = 0, so σ_max = Δσ = 80 MPa. (Mean stress σ_m = (σ_max + σ_min)/2 = 40 MPa, which is consistent.) **Step A3 — Substitute numerical values:** $$a_c = \frac{1}{\pi} \left( \frac{32}{1.0 \times 80} \right)^2$$ $$a_c = \frac{1}{\pi} \times (0.4)^2 = \frac{0.16}{\pi} = 0.05093 \text{ m} \approx 50.93 \text{ mm}$$ **Step A4 — Express the full crack length:** $$2a_c = 101.86 \text{ mm} \approx 102 \text{ mm}$$ This is **51% of plate width** — a clear indicator that fracture toughness, not fatigue, will dominate the failure mode. #### Part (b): Fatigue Life via Paris' Law Integration **Step B1 — State the formula (m = 3.0, so 1 − m/2 = 1 − 1.5 = −0.5):** $$N_f = \frac{a_c^{-0.5} - a_i^{-0.5}}{C \cdot (-0.5) \cdot (Y \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi})^m}$$ **Step B2 — Compute the geometric terms:** $$a_i = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}, \quad a_c = 0.05093 \text{ m}$$ $$a_i^{-0.5} = (2.0 \times 10^{-3})^{-0.5} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2.0 \times 10^{-3}}} = \frac{1}{0.04472} = 22.361$$ $$a_c^{-0.5} = (0.05093)^{-0.5} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.05093}} = \frac{1}{0.22568} = 4.4307$$ **Step B3 — Compute the denominator term:** $$Y \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi} = 1.0 \times 80 \times 1.7725 = 141.80 \text{ MPa·m}^{1/2}$$ Raised to power m = 3: $$(141.80)^3 = 141.80^2 \times 141.80 = 20,107.24 \times 141.80 = 2,851,205 \approx 2.851 \times 10^6$$ **Step B4 — Assemble the full denominator:** $$C \cdot (-0.5) \cdot (Y \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi})^3 = (2.0 \times 10^{-12}) \times (-0.5) \times (2.851 \times 10^6)$$ $$= -1.0 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.851 \times 10^6 = -2.851 \times 10^{-6}$$ **Step B5 — Compute the numerator:** $$a_c^{-0.5} - a_i^{-0.5} = 4.4307 - 22.361 = -17.930$$ **Step B6 — Final division:** $$N_f = \frac{-17.930}{-2.851 \times 10^{-6}} = 6.289 \times 10^6 \text{ cycles}$$ **Result:** The predicted fatigue life is approximately **6.29 × 10⁶ cycles**. This corresponds to roughly **8–10 years of service** in typical North Sea wave conditions for a fast patrol vessel. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Method | Problem Signature | Recommended Method | Governing Equation | Justification | |---|---|---|---| | Ductile Al-alloy, N > 10⁴ cycles, smooth surface | **S-N + Miner** | $D = \sum n_i/N_i$ | Crack initiation dominates; cheap, conservative | | Welded joint, N unknown, defects possible | **S-N (hot-spot) + DFF** | DNV-RP-C203 FAT class | Welds have inherent crack-like flaws | | Hull plate, periodic inspection required | **Damage-tolerant (Paris)** | $da/dN = C(\Delta K)^m$ | Allows inspection-interval optimization | | Thick propellers, stress gradients | **Linear Elastic F/M** | $K_I = \sigma\sqrt{\pi a} Y$ | Plane-strain condition satisfied | | Thin plate, gross plasticity | **Elastic-Plastic (J, CTOD)** | $J = J_{IC}$ | LEFM invalid; use BS 7910 Annex E | --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Fatigue Life Cycle Block Architecture The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional decision architecture** of a fatigue and fracture design procedure as applied to a non-ferrous marine structural component.
