The flow is the same as the workflow codified in **DNV-RP-C203** and **ABS Guide for Fatigue Assessment of Ship Structures**. --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Set B]** *Define fatigue failure in the context of non-ferrous marine structures. Why is it particularly critical for aluminum ship hulls compared to mild steel hulls?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Fatigue failure is the progressive structural damage of a material subjected to fluctuating loads at stress levels below the static yield strength, culminating in sudden fracture. In non-ferrous marine structures, fatigue is critical for aluminum hulls (e.g., Al-5083, Al-6082) for two reasons: 1. **Aluminum lacks a true endurance limit** — unlike mild steel, fatigue strength continues to decrease with cycles, so a 25-year service life must be checked against an ever-declining allowable stress. 2. **Lower modulus of elasticity (E ≈ 70 GPa vs 210 GPa for steel)** — for the same load, elastic strains are higher, accelerating slip-band decohesion at weld toes. (1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the comparative reasoning.) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State Paris' law for fatigue crack propagation. Identify the physical meaning of each symbol and specify the units in the SI system.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** $$\frac{da}{dN} = C \cdot (\Delta K)^m$$ - `da/dN` = crack growth rate per cycle (m/cycle) - `ΔK` = cyclic stress intensity factor range, K_max − K_min (MPa√m) - `C` = crack-growth coefficient (m/cycle per (MPa√m)^m) - `m` = crack-growth exponent (dimensionless; typically 2.5–4.0 for non-ferrous alloys) (1.5 marks for the equation, 1.5 marks for symbol-and-unit identification.) --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — Fracture Mechanics Dominant **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** (a) **[7 marks]** A transverse fillet welded joint in a marine **Al-6082-T6** superstructure panel is subjected to a stress range Δσ = 90 MPa (R = 0). The plate thickness is 12 mm, plate width is 150 mm. Non-destructive testing has revealed a surface crack of depth `a = 1.5 mm` and length `2c = 6 mm` at the weld toe. The fracture toughness of Al-6082-T6 in marine atmosphere is K_IC = 28 MPa√m. Material constants: C = 1.5×10⁻¹², m = 3.4. Using the embedded elliptical crack solution Y = 1.12, calculate: (i) The critical crack depth a_c. (ii) The cycles remaining to failure using the Paris law. (b) **[7 marks]** Explain the **damage-tolerant design philosophy** as applied to primary ship hull structures. Compare it with the **safe-life** and **finite-life (S-N)** philosophies. For each, give one specific marine application where it is the preferred choice. --- ### **Model Solution — Question A** #### Part (a)(i) — Critical Crack Depth [3.5 marks] **Step 1:** Apply the critical-crack formula with σ_max = Δσ = 90 MPa (since R = 0, σ_min = 0): $$a_c = \frac{1}{\pi} \left( \frac{K_{IC}}{Y \cdot \sigma_{max}} \right)^2$$ **Step 2:** Substitute: $$a_c = \frac{1}{\pi} \left( \frac{28}{1.12 \times 90} \right)^2 = \frac{1}{\pi} \left( \frac{28}{100.8} \right)^2$$ $$a_c = \frac{1}{\pi} \times (0.2778)^2 = \frac{0.07717}{\pi} = 0.02457 \text{ m} \approx 24.6 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 3:** Convert: **a_c ≈ 24.6 mm.** Since a_c > plate thickness (12 mm), the plate is in **plane stress** — a valid LEFM assumption with no thickness correction. #### Part (a)(ii) — Cycles to Failure [3.5 marks] **Step 1:** Identify the Paris-law integration form for m = 3.4 (so 1 − m/2 = 1 − 1.7 = −0.7): $$N_f = \frac{a_c^{-0.7} - a_i^{-0.7}}{C \cdot (-0.7) \cdot (Y \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi})^{3.4}}$$ **Step 2:** Compute geometric terms: - a_i = 1.5×10⁻³ m, a_c = 2.457×10⁻² m - a_i^(-0.7) = (1.5×10⁻³)^(-0.7) = 217.6 - a_c^(-0.7) = (2.457×10⁻²)^(-0.7) = 18.32 Numerator: 18.32 − 217.6 = **−199.3** **Step 3:** Compute denominator term: - Y·Δσ·√π = 1.12 × 90 × 1.7725 = 178.68 MPa·m^(1/2) - (178.68)^3.4 = 178.68³ × 178.68^0.4 = 5.703×10⁶ × 8.07 = **4.60×10⁷** - C × (−0.7) × 4.60×10⁷ = (1.5×10⁻¹²) × (−0.7) × (4.60×10⁷) = **−4.83×10⁻⁵** **Step 4:** Final division: $$N_f = \frac{-199.3}{-4.83 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.13 \times 10^6 \text{ cycles}$$ **Result:** Approximately **4.13 million cycles** remaining to failure. #### Part (b) — Three Design Philosophies [7 marks] | Philosophy | Principle | Marine Application | |---|---|---| | **Safe-Life** | Design so the structure never fails within its service life; no inspection mandated | Secondary superstructure, accommodation ladders | | **Finite-Life (S-N)** | Calculate cumulative damage using S-N curves and Miner's rule; safety factor on life | Hull plate, longitudinal stiffeners | | **Damage-Tolerant** | Assume pre-existing cracks; schedule NDT so crack is detected before reaching a_c | Primary hull girders, propeller shafts, rudder stocks | **Justification for damage tolerance:** For primary hull structures, classification societies (DNV, Lloyd) mandate that any 2a ≥ 5 mm initial flaw must be detectable by the in-service inspection scheme before it grows to a_c. This couples the *fracture mechanics analysis* with the *inspection capability* (probability of detection curves) to define inspection intervals. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often forget that for a 12 mm Al-6082 plate, plane-strain conditions require thickness `B ≥ 2.5(K_IC/σ_y)²`. If this is satisfied, use K_IC; if not, the problem is plane stress and a higher apparent K_C must be used. Always check the validity of LEFM first. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** Sign error in Paris-law integration. The exponent `(1 − m/2)` is **negative** when m > 2 (true for all non-ferrous alloys). The numerator `(a_c^(1−m/2) − a_i^(1−m/2))` is also negative — the negatives cancel. Skipping the sign bookkeeping costs 1 full mark. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — S-N and Cumulative Damage Dominant **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Restyled]** (a) **[7 marks]** A marine **Al-5083-H116** hull plate is subjected to the following variable-amplitude stress spectrum during a typical 24-hour North Atlantic passage: | Block | σ_max (MPa) | σ_min (MPa) | n_i (cycles) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 75 | 25 | 80,000 | | 2 | 95 | 30 | 25,000 | | 3 | 115 | 35 | 5,000 | The S-N curve for Al-5083 in marine atmosphere is given by: $$\sigma_a = 230 \cdot (N_f)^{-0.10}$$ where σ_a is the stress amplitude (MPa). Using **Miner's rule**, calculate the cumulative damage D. State whether the structure survives a 30-year design life (assume 5,000 passages = 1.5×10⁵ blocks of this type). (b) **[7 marks]** Discuss the limitations of Miner's rule for non-ferrous marine alloys. Why might the actual damage sum at failure deviate from D = 1.0? Suggest two engineering modifications that designers use to compensate. --- ### **Model Solution — Question B** #### Part (a) — Cumulative Damage via Miner's Rule [7 marks] **Step 1:** Calculate stress amplitude σ_a for each block: $$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$$ - Block 1: σ_a1 = (75 − 25)/2 = **25 MPa** - Block 2: σ_a2 = (95 − 30)/2 = **32.5 MPa** - Block 3: σ_a3 = (115 − 35)/2 = **40 MPa** **Step 2:** Calculate cycles to failure N_i for each block from the S-N equation: $$N_i = \left( \frac{230}{\sigma_{a,i}} \right)^{1/0.10} = \left( \frac{230}{\sigma_{a,i}} \right)^{10}$$ - Block 1: N_1 = (230/25)^10 = (9.2)^10 = 2.7 × 10⁹ cycles - Block 2: N_2 = (230/32.5)^10 = (7.077)^10 = 3.27 × 10⁸ cycles - Block 3: N_3 = (230/40)^10 = (5.75)^10 = 4.36 × 10⁷ cycles **Step 3:** Calculate n_i / N_i for each block: - n_1/N_1 = 80,000 / (2.7×10⁹) = 2.96×10⁻⁵ - n_2/N_2 = 25,000 / (3.27×10⁸) = 7.65×10⁻⁵ - n_3/N_3 = 5,000 / (4.36×10⁷) = 1.147×10⁻⁴ **Step 4:** Sum to get D for one 24-hour block: $$D_{block} = 2.96 \times 10^{-5} + 7.65 \times 10^{-5} + 1.147 \times 10^{-4} = 2.21 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Step 5:** Multiply by 1.5×10⁵ blocks (30 years): $$D_{30yr} = 1.5 \times 10^5 \times 2.21 \times 10^{-4} = 33.15$$ **Conclusion:** D >> 1.0. The structure will **fail well before** the 30-year design life. The designer must either: - Increase plate thickness (reduces σ_a) - Add longitudinal stiffeners (reduces stress range) - Specify a higher-strength alloy - Apply a Design Fatigue Factor (DFF) to reduce the allowable D to < 1.0 #### Part (b) — Limitations of Miner's Rule [7 marks] 1. **Load-sequence independence:** Miner's rule ignores the order of loading. Yet experiments on Al-alloys show that low-to-high loading sequences (LTH) yield D ≈ 1.0–1.5, while high-to-low sequences (HTL) yield D ≈ 0.5–0.7. Block 3 in the example above is a high-amplitude outlier. 2. **No account of mean-stress relaxation:** In Al-alloys, peak-loaded cycles cause local plastic ratcheting that *reduces* the local mean stress — a beneficial effect not captured by Miner's linear sum. 3. **Interaction effects:** Miner's rule treats each block as if it were applied to virgin material, but in reality, Block 1 may pre-damage the microstructure, accelerating Block 3 damage. 4. **Corrosion-fatigue interaction:** In marine environments, simultaneous cyclic loading and seawater exposure causes synergistic acceleration. Miner's rule as a stand-alone tool is non-conservative; DNV-RP-C203 mandates a **corrosion factor** to scale the S-N curve downward. **Engineering modifications:** - **Design Fatigue Factor (DFF):** DNV mandates DFF = 2–3 on the right-hand side, giving an effective failure criterion of D × DFF ≥ 1. - **Spectral fatigue analysis:** Replace the discrete block spectrum with the *continuous wave spectrum* (JONSWAP, Pierson-Moskowitz) and integrate over the sea-state scatter diagram for a more accurate long-term damage estimate. - **Sequence-aware modifications:** Use the **Manson-Hirschberg** method or **Corten-Dolan** modification to account for load-order effects. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #3:** When applying Miner's rule, students often mix *cycles per year* with *cycles per design life*. Always convert to a **common reference period** before summing. Forgetting to multiply by the number of voyages / years is a guaranteed 2-mark loss. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #4:** The S-N equation σ_a = A·(N_f)^B is given in many textbook variants with **B negative or positive** depending on the sign convention. KTU 2024 uses the form above (B = −0.10). If you mistakenly write N_f = A·(σ_a)^B, you will get the wrong answer by orders of magnitude. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 — Module 2)** - **Fatigue is history-dependent; static strength is not.** Always think in terms of cycles, not single loads. - **Al-alloys have no endurance limit** — quote fatigue strength at N = 2×10⁶ or 5×10⁶ cycles, never at "infinite life" without justification. - **Three-stage failure model:** Initiation → Stable propagation (Paris) → Unstable fracture (K_IC). Damage-tolerant design only addresses Stages 2–3. - **Paris' law** is the *master equation* of damage-tolerant design: `da/dN = C(ΔK)^m`. Memorize the integrated form for `m ≠ 2`. - **Critical crack size** is the designer's control parameter: a_c ∝ (K_IC / σ)². Halving applied stress quadruples the safe crack length. - **Welded joints are the #1 fatigue hot-spot in non-ferrous ship structures** — 80% of marine fatigue failures initiate at weld toes. Always use **DNV FAT class** curves. - **Goodman correction** converts variable-mean-stress loading to fully-reversed equivalent stress. KTU board problems almost always include a mean-stress check. - **Miner's rule** says D = Σn_i/N_i. Failure when D ≥ 1.0, but apply a **Design Fatigue Factor (DFF)** of 2–3 for non-ferrous marine structures. - **Plane strain vs plane stress** decides whether to use K_IC (thick) or K_C (thin). Rule of thumb: B ≥ 2.5(K_IC/σ_y)² for valid LEFM. - **Corrosion-fatigue synergy** in seawater: C increases, m may shift, and the threshold ΔK_th drops. Never quote an air-data S-N curve for in-service marine assessment. - **Inspection interval calculation** = (cycles to grow from NDE detection limit a_d to a_c) / (cycles per year). This is the bridge between fracture mechanics and ship operational planning. - **Units discipline:** a in m, σ in MPa, K in MPa√m, da/dN in m/cycle. Mixing cm and mm is the most common KTU answer-script error. - **Preferred marine non-ferrous alloys and their K_IC:** - Al-5083-H116: 30–35 MPa√m - Al-6082-T6: 25–30 MPa√m - Cu-Ni 90/10: 35–45 MPa√m - Ti-6Al-4V: 55–75 MPa√m - Marine-grade GFRP (transverse): 5–15 MPa√m - **Classification society rules (must cite in answers):** DNV-RP-C203 (fatigue), DNV-RP-F112 (fracture), Lloyd's Register SSC Rules, ABS Guide for Ship Structural Fatigue.

topics.title: Fire Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fire Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

**Fire Protection** in shipbuilding refers to the integrated set of engineering measures — both **structural (passive)** and **operational (active)** — designed to prevent fire ignition, limit fire spread, ensure the structural integrity of the vessel under fire exposure, and provide safe evacuation and fire-fighting capability for crew and passengers. In the context of non-ferrous materials, it specifically concerns the selection, application, and performance of **aluminium, titanium, copper, magnesium alloys, ceramic composites, and mineral-based non-combustible materials** that resist, contain, or suppress fire aboard marine vessels.

The regulatory baseline is set by:
- **SOLAS Chapter II-2** — "Construction — Fire protection, fire detection, and fire extinction"
- **FTP Code** (International Code for Application of Fire Test Procedures)
- **FSS Code** (International Code for Fire Safety Systems)
- **MARPOL Annex VI** (for machinery spaces with non-ferrous components)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For naval architecture students, the focus is on how *non-ferrous metals* (Al, Ti, Cu, Mg) and their composites behave under fire loading — their **limiting temperatures, fire-resistance ratings, and suitability as substitutes for steel** in superstructures and outfitting.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Floating Building" Concept

Imagine a ship as a **40-storey apartment building floating on water with no escape route except lifeboats**. Now imagine each cabin as a sealed box, and the corridors as the "lungs" of the building. A fire in one cabin is exactly like a fire in one flat — except:
1. The building **tilts and rolls** in a storm, so firewater, smoke, and foam behave unpredictably.
2. The structure is made of **thin metal plates**, not concrete, so heat passes through quickly.
3. The escape route is **down a corridor, up a ladder, and into a swinging lifeboat** — slow and dangerous.

**Fire protection in a ship is therefore like a "nested fire-containment strategy":**
- The **outer hull** must hold the vessel together for at least 30–120 minutes (depending on vessel type).
- The **bulkheads** (walls between compartments) must stop fire from jumping rooms.
- The **non-ferrous lining materials** must not melt, drip, or release toxic smoke.
- The **active systems** (sprinklers, CO₂, water mist) must react within seconds.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Fire Triangle" on a Ship:** Aboard a vessel, all three elements of the fire triangle — *Fuel (oil, paint, LNG, plastics), Oxygen (ventilation), and Heat (engines, galley, welding)* — are abundant. Fire protection aims to **break at least one side of the triangle** as early as possible.

## 1.3 Why Non-Ferrous Materials Dominate Marine Fire Protection

| Property Required | Why Steel Falls Short | Non-Ferrous Advantage |
|---|---|---|
| **Low weight** | Steel density = **7850 kg/m³** | Aluminium = **2700 kg/m³** (≈ 65% weight saving) |
| **Corrosion resistance** | Requires constant repainting | Al, Ti, Cu-Ni alloys form protective oxide layers |
| **Non-magnetic** | Steel distorts compasses | Al & Ti are non-magnetic |
| **Formability for complex panels** | Hard to roll into curved panels | Al alloys (5xxx, 6xxx) are easily roll-formed |
| **Smoke toxicity** | Steel doesn't burn but coatings may | Inherent non-combustibility of Al, Ti |

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Fire Protection on Ships

### 2.1.1 Passive (Structural) Fire Protection
**Definition:** Materials that are *permanently built into* the ship's structure to resist fire without any moving parts or human action.

**Sub-categories (per SOLAS):**
- **A-Class Divisions** — Steel or equivalent non-combustible bulkheads/decks with **60/30/15 minute** ratings. Must prevent passage of flame + smoke for the rated time.
- **B-Class Divisions** — Non-combustible panels with **30/15/0 minute** ratings. Restrict flame spread but allow some heat passage.
- **C-Class Divisions** — Non-combustible materials with **0-minute** rating (merely non-combustible).
- **"H" Class (Helideck)** — Special rating for helicopter landing decks (no flame spread for 2 minutes under fuel-spill fire).

### 2.1.2 Active Fire Protection
**Definition:** Systems that **detect and suppress** a fire once ignited. Include:
- **Water-based:** Sprinklers, water mist, deluge systems
- **Gas-based:** CO₂, FM-200, Novec 1230
- **Foam-based:** AFFF (Aqueous Film-Forming Foam), protein foam
- **Powder-based:** Dry chemical (monoammonium phosphate)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** When asked the difference between *active* and *passive* fire protection, a high-scoring answer mentions that **passive protection works even when all power is lost**, while active systems depend on pumps, valves, and electricity.

## 2.2 Non-Ferrous Materials Used in Marine Fire Protection

### 2.2.1 Aluminium Alloys
- **5083, 5086, 5454, 5754** (Al-Mg series) — Used for superstructures and bulkheads. Melting point ≈ **660 °C**.
- **6061, 6082** (Al-Mg-Si series) — Used for structural stiffeners. Heat-treated to T6 condition.
- **Critical Limitation:** Aluminium loses **~50% of its yield strength at 200 °C** and is structurally useless above **350 °C**.

### 2.2.2 Titanium Alloys
- **Ti-6Al-4V** (Grade 5) — Used in naval and submarine pressure hulls.
- Melting point ≈ **1668 °C** — exceptional high-temperature performance.
- **Limitation:** Cost (≈ 10–20× that of steel) and difficulty in fabrication.

### 2.2.3 Copper & Copper-Nickel Alloys
- **Cu-Ni 90/10 and 70/30** — Used for fire-fighting water piping (seawater-resistant).
- **Phosphor bronze** — Used in fire-resistant valve seats.

### 2.2.4 Mineral & Ceramic Non-Metallic Materials
- **Mineral wool (Rockwool/Slag wool)** — Density 30–200 kg/m³, melting point > **1000 °C**.
- **Calcium silicate boards** — Used as fire-rated linings.
- **Vermiculite boards** — Lightweight, non-combustible, used for cable transit penetrations.
- **Intumescent coatings** — Expand 10–100× when heated, forming an insulating char.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Variables | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Standard Cellulosic Fire Curve (ISO 834) | $T(t) = T_0 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$ | $T$ = furnace temp, $T_0$ = ambient (20 °C), $t$ = time | °C, min | Testing A/B class bulkheads |
| Hydrocarbon Fire Curve (HCM) | $T(t) = 1080 \left(1 - 0.325 e^{-0.167 t} - 0.675 e^{-2.5 t}\right) + T_0$ | $t$ = time | °C, min | Testing offshore/petrochemical structures |
| Marine Hydrocarbon Curve (SOLAS) | $T(t) = T_0 + 1100 \left(1 - 0.325 e^{-0.167 t} - 0.675 e^{-2.5 t}\right)$ | $t$ = time | °C, min | Testing helidecks, oil tankers |
| Critical Steel Temperature | $\theta_{cr} \approx 550 \text{ to } 600$ °C | — | °C | Limit state design under fire |
| Critical Aluminium Temperature | $\theta_{cr,Al} \approx 200 \text{ to } 250$ °C | — | °C | Al alloy bulkheads/structures |
| Heat Transfer through Wall (Conduction) | $q = \dfrac{k \cdot \Delta T}{L}$ | $k$ = thermal conductivity, $L$ = thickness | W/m² | Insulation thickness design |
| Insulating Material Efficiency | $R = \dfrac{L}{k}$ | $R$ = thermal resistance | m²·K/W | Selecting non-ferrous claddings |
| Radiation Heat Flux (Stefan-Boltzmann) | $q_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$ | $\varepsilon$ = emissivity, $\sigma$ = $5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ | W/m² | Fire exposure assessment |
| Fire Resistance Time Approximation | $t_f = \dfrac{L \cdot \rho \cdot c}{k} \cdot f(\theta_{cr})$ | $\rho$ = density, $c$ = specific heat | min | Quick estimation of panel rating |
| Coverage of Intumescent Coating | $\text{DFT} = \dfrac{WFT \times \% \text{ solids}}{100}$ | DFT = dry film thickness, WFT = wet | µm | Required coating thickness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Alert for KTU Exams:** For the cellulosic fire curve, the **log₁₀** base is mandatory. Forgetting the "log" and writing linear temperature is the most common error.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Passenger Ships (SOLAS Reg. II-2/9):** All bulkheads separating cabin corridors from cabins must be **"B-15" class** minimum — meaning they hold back flame for 15 minutes. This is achieved using **aluminium-faced mineral wool panels** (typical mass 12–18 kg/m²).
2. **LNG Carriers (IGC Code):** The cargo containment system uses **stainless steel + aluminium alloy secondary barrier** with **cryogenic + fire protection** integrated.
3. **Naval Warships (STANAG 4564 / MIL-STD-1629):** Aluminium superstructures are protected with **intumescent epoxy coatings** to achieve 60-minute fire rating without adding steel plating.
4. **Offshore Platforms (NORSOK N-001, N-004):** Use **HCM hydrocarbon fire curve** for design — temperatures reach **1100 °C in 5 minutes**, requiring ceramic-fibre or calcium-silicate insulation on critical structural members.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Time-Temperature Relationship under Standard Cellulosic Fire

**Starting Point:** Empirical formula established by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO 834)** for the standard cellulosic fire exposure.

$$T(t) - T_0 = 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$$

**Step 1: Define variables and initial conditions**

- $T(t)$ = gas temperature in the furnace at time $t$ (in °C)
- $T_0$ = initial (ambient) temperature, taken as **20 °C** per ISO 834
- $t$ = time elapsed since fire ignition (in minutes)
- 345 = empirical constant derived from cellulosic fuel (wood, paper) combustion

**Step 2: Validate initial condition at $t = 0$**

$$T(0) - T_0 = 345 \log_{10}(8 \cdot 0 + 1) = 345 \log_{10}(1) = 345 \times 0 = 0$$

Therefore, $T(0) = T_0 = 20$ °C ✓ (matches expected ambient start)

**Step 3: Validate behaviour at $t = 30$ min (typical A-30 rating test)**

$$T(30) - 20 = 345 \log_{10}(8 \times 30 + 1) = 345 \log_{10}(241)$$

$$\log_{10}(241) \approx 2.382$$

$$T(30) = 20 + 345 \times 2.382 = 20 + 821.8 = 841.8 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 4: Validate behaviour at $t = 60$ min (A-60 rating test)**

$$T(60) - 20 = 345 \log_{10}(8 \times 60 + 1) = 345 \log_{10}(481)$$

$$\log_{10}(481) \approx 2.682$$

$$T(60) = 20 + 345 \times 2.682 = 20 + 925.3 = 945.3 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 5: Behaviour at $t = 120$ min (A-120 rating test)**

$$T(120) - 20 = 345 \log_{10}(961)$$

$$\log_{10}(961) \approx 2.983$$

$$T(120) = 20 + 345 \times 2.983 = 20 + 1029.1 = 1049.1 \text{ °C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The cellulosic fire curve grows **logarithmically**, so temperature rise slows down with time — it never exceeds ~1100 °C asymptotically. This contrasts with the **hydrocarbon curve**, which spikes rapidly to 1100 °C within 5 minutes.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical Temperature Check for Aluminium Bulkhead

**Problem Context:** A marine bulkhead is made of **AA 5083-H116 aluminium alloy** (yield strength = 215 MPa at room temperature). Determine the temperature at which the bulkhead loses its load-bearing capacity.

**Step 1: Recall the European code (EN 1999-1-2) reduction factor for AA 5083**

For aluminium alloy 5083 (non-heat-treatable, 5xxx series), the **strength reduction factor** as a function of temperature is:

$$k_{0,\theta} = \frac{f_{0.2,\theta}}{f_{0.2,20}}$$

where $f_{0.2,\theta}$ is the 0.2% proof strength at temperature $\theta$, and $f_{0.2,20}$ is the room-temperature value.

**Step 2: Use the EN 1999-1-2 tabulated values for 5083**

| Temperature $\theta$ (°C) | $k_{0,\theta}$ |
|---|---|
| 20 | 1.000 |
| 100 | 0.940 |
| 150 | 0.870 |
| 200 | 0.700 |
| 250 | 0.500 |
| 300 | 0.310 |
| 350 | 0.150 |
| 400 | 0.000 |

**Step 3: Define failure criterion**

Structural failure occurs when $f_{0.2,\theta}$ drops below the **stress induced by applied loads** plus safety margin. For a typical bulkhead, assume working stress = **70% of yield** = $0.70 \times 215 = 150.5$ MPa.

The required reduction factor: $k_{0,\theta,req} = \dfrac{150.5}{215} = 0.700$

**Step 4: Interpolate the critical temperature**

From the table, $k_{0,\theta} = 0.700$ corresponds to **θ_cr = 200 °C** exactly for 5083 alloy.

**Step 5: Compare with cellulosic fire curve**

For the bulkhead to last 30 minutes in a cellulosic fire, the *unexposed* side of the bulkhead must stay below 200 °C. With 6 mm aluminium plate:

$$\Delta T = \dfrac{q \cdot L}{k_{Al}} = \dfrac{q \times 0.006}{167}$$

where $k_{Al,5083} \approx 167 \text{ W/(m·K)}$ and $q$ = incident heat flux from fire side.

For the unexposed side to stay at 200 °C maximum, an **insulation layer** of mineral wool must be added on the fire side.

**Step 6: Calculate required insulation thickness**

Required thermal resistance of insulation: $R_{ins} = \dfrac{T_{fire} - T_{cr}}{q}$

Assuming $q = 25 \text{ kW/m}^2$ and $T_{fire} = 950 \text{ °C}$:

$$R_{ins} = \dfrac{950 - 200}{25000} = 0.030 \text{ m}^2 \cdot \text{K/W}$$

Mineral wool thermal conductivity: $k_{MW} \approx 0.04 \text{ W/(m·K)}$

Required thickness: $L_{MW} = R_{ins} \times k_{MW} = 0.030 \times 0.04 = 0.0012 \text{ m} = 1.2 \text{ mm}$

> [!NOTE]
> **In practice**, the insulation is much thicker (30–50 mm) because the heat flux $q$ can spike to 50+ kW/m² during a fully developed fire, and the bulkhead is not uniformly heated.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Heat Release Rate and Time to Flashover

**Starting Point:** In an enclosed ship cabin, fire growth is governed by the **$t^2$ fire growth model** (used by NFPA and SFPE):

$$Q(t) = \alpha t^2$$

where:
- $Q(t)$ = heat release rate (HRR) in kW
- $\alpha$ = fire growth coefficient (slow: 0.00293, medium: 0.01172, fast: 0.0469, ultra-fast: 0.1876 kW/s²)
- $t$ = time (s)

**Step 1: Determine HRR at flashover**

Flashover in a typical ship cabin occurs at **Q_fo ≈ 1000 kW** (standard assumption for a 4 m × 4 m × 2.5 m cabin).

**Step 2: Solve for time to flashover — slow fire**

$$t_{fo} = \sqrt{\dfrac{Q_{fo}}{\alpha}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1000}{0.00293}} = \sqrt{341,297} \approx 584 \text{ s} \approx 9.7 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3: Solve for time to flashover — fast fire (e.g., spilled fuel, plastic furnishings)**

$$t_{fo} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1000}{0.0469}} = \sqrt{21,322} \approx 146 \text{ s} \approx 2.4 \text{ min}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** On a ship, the **fast fire scenario is realistic** because of synthetic furnishings, lubricating oils, and LNG cargo. This is why SOLAS requires detection and alarm systems that react in **less than 60 seconds** — the entire fire development phase lasts only 2–3 minutes before flashover.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Non-Ferrous Materials vs. Fire Performance

| Material | Melting Point (°C) | Critical Structural Temp (°C) | Density (kg/m³) | Relative Cost | Fire Protection Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AA 5083-H116 (Al-Mg) | 660 | 200 | 2700 | Low | Superstructure bulkheads |
| AA 6082-T6 (Al-Mg-Si) | 660 | 220 | 2700 | Low | Stiffeners, frames |
| Ti-6Al-4V | 1668 | 540 | 4430 | Very High | Submarine pressure hulls |
| Cu-Ni 90/10 | 1150 | 350 | 8940 | Medium | Fire-water piping |
| Phosphor Bronze | 1050 | 380 | 8800 | High | Valve seats, fire-resistant fittings |
| Mineral Wool (insulation) | >1000 | N/A (non-structural) | 30–200 | Low | A/B class panel infill |
| Calcium Silicate (insulation) | >1000 | N/A | 600–900 | Medium | Cable transit, hot pipe insulation |
| Intumescent Epoxy Coating | Decomposes 250–300 | N/A (coating) | 1300 (cured) | High | Applied to Al structures for FRR |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Shipboard Fire Protection Architecture (SOLAS-Compliant)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[HULL STRUCTURE\nSteel / Al Hull Plate] --> B[STRUCTURAL FIRE PROTECTION]
    A --> C[NON-FERROUS FITTINGS\nAl Mg Alloy 5xxx]
    B --> D[A-Class Divisions\n60 / 30 / 15 min]
    B --> E[B-Class Divisions\n30 / 15 / 0 min]
    B --> F[C-Class Non-Combustible\n0 min]
    D --> G[Aluminium-Faced Panels\n5083 / 6082]
    D --> H[Mineral Wool Infill\nDensity 100 kg per m3]
    D --> I[Intumescent Coating\nEpoxy Base]
    J[ACTIVE FIRE PROTECTION] --> K[Detection Layer\nSmoke + Heat + Flame]
    J --> L[Suppression Layer\nWater Mist]
    J --> M[Suppression Layer\nCO2 System]
    J --> N[Suppression Layer\nAFFF Foam]
    K --> O[Alarm and Evacuation\nGeneral Alarm]
    L --> P[Pumps and Piping\nCu-Ni 90/10]
    M --> P
    N --> P
    G --> Q[FIRE RATED COMPARTMENT\n60 to 120 minutes integrity]
    H --> Q
    I --> Q
    P --> Q
    O --> R[CREW and PASSENGER SAFETY\nSOLAS Reg II-2 Compliant]
    Q --> R
```

## 4.2 Flowchart: Selection Logic for Non-Ferrous Fire Protection Material

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[START: Identify Application] --> S2{Structural or\nNon-Structural?}
    S2 -- Structural --> S3{Required Fire\nRating?}
    S2 -- Non-Structural --> S4[Select Mineral Wool\nor Calcium Silicate]
    S3 -- A-60 or higher --> S5[Use Al Alloy 5083\nwith Intumescent\nCoating DFT 1 to 2 mm]
    S3 -- A-30 or A-15 --> S6[Use Al Alloy 5083\nwith Mineral Wool\nLining 30 to 50 mm]
    S3 -- B-Class --> S7[Use Al-Faced\nCalcium Silicate\nPanel]
    S5 --> S8[Validate Critical\nTemperature\ntheta less than 200 C]
    S6 --> S8
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9{Exposed to\nHydrocarbon Fire?}
    S9 -- Yes --> S10[Apply HCM Curve\n1100 C in 5 min\nUse Ceramic Fibre]
    S9 -- No --> S11[Apply ISO 834\nCellulosic Curve\n950 C in 60 min]
    S10 --> S12[FINAL: Material\nSelected and Validated]
    S11 --> S12
    S4 --> S12
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Heat Transfer Path in an A-60 Aluminium Bulkhead

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[FIRE SIDE\nTemperature up to 950 C] --> H1[CONVECTION\nh fire 25 W per m2 K]
    H1 --> L1[CONDUCTION\nthrough Al Plate 6 mm\nk Al 167 W per m K]
    L1 --> L2[CONDUCTION\nthrough Mineral Wool 30 mm\nk MW 0.04 W per m K]
    L2 --> R1[RADIATION and CONVECTION\nto Cabin Interior]
    R1 --> C1[UNEXPOSED SIDE\nMust remain below\ntheta cr for Al 200 C]
    C1 --> SAF[SAFETY MARGIN\nTime to reach 200 C\nmust exceed 60 min]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reading of the Diagram:** The mineral wool layer carries the **bulk of the temperature drop** because its thermal conductivity is **4000× lower** than aluminium. This is why even a thin layer of insulation is critical for non-ferrous structures.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** Define "A-Class" and "B-Class" divisions as per SOLAS Chapter II-2. Give two examples of non-ferrous materials used in their construction.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**A-Class Division (1.5 marks):** A bulkhead or deck that is:
- Constructed of **steel or equivalent non-combustible material**
- Has **insulation such that the average temperature of the unexposed face does not rise by more than 140 °C** above the original temperature, with no single point exceeding 180 °C rise
- Has **standard fire ratings of A-60, A-30, A-15, or A-0** (numerals indicate minutes of fire resistance under ISO 834 curve)
- Prevents passage of flame and smoke for the rated time

**B-Class Division (1 mark):** A bulkhead or deck that is:
- Constructed of **non-combustible materials only**
- Has fire resistance rating of **B-15, B-30, or B-0** minutes
- Restricts flame spread but allows some heat transfer

**Non-ferrous materials used (0.5 marks):** Aluminium alloy (5083/6082) facings + mineral wool insulation core.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** Why is aluminium alloy used extensively in ship superstructures despite its low melting point? List two fire-protection measures applied to aluminium structures.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Reasons for use (1.5 marks):**
1. **Light weight:** density 2700 kg/m³ vs 7850 kg/m³ for steel → reduces topweight, improves stability and fuel efficiency.
2. **Corrosion resistance:** Al forms a passive Al₂O₃ layer, requiring less maintenance.
3. **Non-magnetic:** important for naval vessels with sensitive electronics.
4. **Easy fabrication:** can be roll-formed, extruded, and welded efficiently.

**Fire-protection measures (1.5 marks):**
1. **Intumescent epoxy coatings** that expand to form an insulating char, slowing heat transfer to the substrate.
2. **Mineral wool or calcium silicate insulation** bonded to the fire-exposed face.
3. **Encapsulation with non-combustible ceramic-fibre blankets** for high-risk areas like engine rooms.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the ISO 834 standard cellulosic fire curve. Derive the furnace temperature at **t = 90 minutes** and discuss its significance for designing a **B-15 class aluminium bulkhead** on a passenger ship. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the role of **non-ferrous materials** (aluminium, titanium, copper-nickel, and mineral wool) in shipboard fire protection. Compare their suitability for use in (i) superstructure bulkheads, (ii) fire-fighting water mains, and (iii) cable transit penetrations. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q(a) (7 marks)

**Step 1: State the formula (1 mark)**

$$T(t) - T_0 = 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$$

where $T_0 = 20$ °C, $t$ in minutes.

**Step 2: Substitute $t = 90$ min (2 marks)**

$$T(90) - 20 = 345 \log_{10}(8 \times 90 + 1) = 345 \log_{10}(721)$$

Compute $\log_{10}(721)$:

$$\log_{10}(721) = \log_{10}(7.21 \times 10^2) = 2 + \log_{10}(7.21) = 2 + 0.8579 = 2.8579$$

Therefore:

$$T(90) = 20 + 345 \times 2.8579 = 20 + 986.0 = 1006.0 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 3: Significance for B-15 bulkhead (2 marks)**

- A **B-15** bulkhead must prevent **flame passage for 15 minutes** under the ISO 834 fire curve.
- At $t = 15$ min: $T(15) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(121) = 20 + 345 \times 2.083 = 20 + 718.6 = 738.6$ °C.
- This is the **fire-side temperature** the bulkhead must withstand.
- For an aluminium-faced bulkhead (critical temp 200 °C), the unexposed side must stay below the ignition temperature of adjacent materials (typically 200 °C for timber, 350 °C for steel) for 15 minutes.

**Step 4: Material specification (2 marks)**

A typical B-15 aluminium bulkhead specification:
- 3 mm AA 5083-H116 face sheet (fire side)
- 30 mm mineral wool insulation (density 100 kg/m³)
- 1.5 mm AA 5083-H116 face sheet (cabin side)
- Total mass ≈ 14–18 kg/m²

This achieves a fire resistance rating of **>15 minutes** with a safety margin.

---

#### Model Solution for Q(b) (7 marks)

**Part (i) — Superstructure Bulkheads (2.5 marks):**

**Aluminium alloys (5083, 6082)** are the primary choice because:
- High strength-to-weight ratio
- Easily fabricated into corrugated or sandwich panels
- Combined with mineral wool core to form **A/B class divisions**

**Titanium** is rarely used here due to high cost, except in **naval submarine pressure hulls** where fire + pressure + corrosion resistance is required simultaneously.

**Suitability ranking:** **Aluminium alloy** > Titanium (cost-prohibitive for bulkheads).

**Part (ii) — Fire-Fighting Water Mains (2.5 marks):**

**Copper-nickel 90/10 (Cu-Ni 90/10)** is the **industry standard** because:
- Excellent resistance to seawater corrosion (essential for marine pipework)
- Good strength and ductility at ambient and elevated temperatures
- Melting point ≈ 1150 °C — far above typical fire temperatures
- Biofouling resistance (no need for anti-fouling chemicals)

**Titanium** is used in critical high-pressure systems (e.g., submarine fire mains) where weight is secondary to absolute reliability. **Aluminium is NOT used** for fire mains because it reacts with seawater to form hydrogen gas.

**Suitability ranking:** **Cu-Ni 90/10** > Titanium > Aluminium (avoid).

**Part (iii) — Cable Transit Penetrations (2 marks):**

**Mineral wool (Rockwool)** with **intumescent coating** is the industry standard. The cable transit module consists of:
- Mineral wool blocks compressed into the opening
- Intumescent material that swells under heat to seal gaps

**Calcium silicate boards** are an alternative for high-temperature zones. **Aluminium and titanium are not directly used** as penetration seals because they conduct heat too efficiently.

**Suitability ranking:** **Mineral wool + intumescent** > Calcium silicate > Aluminium (not recommended).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Compare the **cellulosic (ISO 834)** and **hydrocarbon (HCM)** fire curves. State the governing equations and tabulate the furnace temperatures at $t = 5, 15, 30, 60$ minutes. Discuss the implications for fire protection of non-ferrous structures on (i) a passenger ship and (ii) an oil tanker. (7 marks)

**(b)** A marine bulkhead is constructed of **AA 5083-H116 aluminium alloy plate (thickness 5 mm)** backed with a layer of **mineral wool insulation (k = 0.04 W/m·K)**. The fire side reaches **900 °C** and the cabin side must remain below **180 °C**. Calculate the **minimum insulation thickness** required, assuming steady-state one-dimensional heat conduction and a heat flux of $q = 20$ kW/m². State the practical implications. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q(a) (7 marks)

**Step 1: State equations (1.5 marks)**

- **Cellulosic (ISO 834):** $T(t) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$
- **Hydrocarbon (HCM):** $T(t) = 1080 \left[1 - 0.325 e^{-0.167 t} - 0.675 e^{-2.5 t}\right] + 20$

**Step 2: Tabulate values (2.5 marks)**

| $t$ (min) | $T_{\text{cellulosic}}$ (°C) | $T_{\text{hydrocarbon}}$ (°C) |
|---|---|---|
| 5 | $20 + 345 \log_{10}(41) = 20 + 345 \times 1.613 = 576.5$ | $1080(1 - 0.325 e^{-0.835} - 0.675 e^{-12.5}) + 20 \approx 1024$ |
| 15 | $20 + 345 \log_{10}(121) = 738.6$ | $1080(1 - 0.325 e^{-2.505} - 0.675 e^{-37.5}) + 20 \approx 1090$ |
| 30 | $20 + 345 \log_{10}(241) = 841.8$ | $1080(1 - 0.325 e^{-5.01} - 0.675 e^{-75}) + 20 \approx 1098$ |
| 60 | $20 + 345 \log_{10}(481) = 945.3$ | $1080 \times 1 + 20 = 1100$ (asymptote) |

**Step 3: Key observations (1.5 marks)**

- **HCM curve** rises much faster — reaches ~1100 °C in 5 minutes.
- **Cellulosic** rises logarithmically — only ~950 °C after 60 minutes.
- HCM represents **petrochemical/pool fires**; cellulosic represents **wood/paper/cotton fires**.

**Step 4: Implications (1.5 marks)**

(i) **Passenger Ship:** Use **ISO 834** — fires are typically cellulosic (furniture, linens, wood). A-class bulkheads are designed to ISO 834 ratings.

(ii) **Oil Tanker / FPSO:** Use **HCM** — fires involve oil pools, LNG, or jet fuel. Helidecks and process modules on tankers are designed to the **marine hydrocarbon curve** (similar to HCM).

---

#### Model Solution for Q(b) (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Al plate thickness: $L_{Al} = 5$ mm $= 0.005$ m, $k_{Al} = 167$ W/(m·K)
- Mineral wool: $k_{MW} = 0.04$ W/(m·K)
- $T_{\text{fire}} = 900$ °C, $T_{\text{cabin}} \leq 180$ °C
- $q = 20$ kW/m² $= 20{,}000$ W/m²

**Step 1: Energy balance (1 mark)**

Heat flux must pass through Al plate and then through mineral wool:

$$q = \frac{T_{\text{fire}} - T_{\text{cabin}}}{R_{Al} + R_{MW}}$$

**Step 2: Calculate $R_{Al}$ (1 mark)**

$$R_{Al} = \frac{L_{Al}}{k_{Al}} = \frac{0.005}{167} = 2.99 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

**Step 3: Calculate required $R_{total}$ and $R_{MW}$ (2 marks)**

$$R_{total} = \frac{T_{\text{fire}} - T_{\text{cabin}}}{q} = \frac{900 - 180}{20{,}000} = \frac{720}{20{,}000} = 0.036 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{MW} = R_{total} - R_{Al} = 0.036 - 2.99 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0.036 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

(Al resistance is negligible — ~0.08% of total.)

**Step 4: Calculate minimum thickness of mineral wool (2 marks)**

$$L_{MW} = R_{MW} \times k_{MW} = 0.036 \times 0.04 = 0.00144 \text{ m} = 1.44 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Practical implications (1 mark)**

- The calculated **1.44 mm** is the **theoretical minimum** under steady-state assumptions.
- In practice, designers use **30–50 mm** of mineral wool because:
  - Real fires are transient, not steady-state
  - Heat flux $q$ can spike to 50+ kW/m²
  - Safety factors of 2–3 are standard
  - Mechanical handling, vibration, and acoustic insulation require additional thickness
- The aluminium plate's resistance to heat flow is **negligible** compared to mineral wool — confirming that insulation is the dominant fire-protection material.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Fire Protection Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the +20 °C baseline** in the cellulosic fire equation (loses 1 mark).
> 2. **Confusing the HCM and ISO 834 curves** — students often quote 1080 °C for a 30-min cellulosic fire, which is wrong (cellulosic is ~842 °C at 30 min).
> 3. **Using "aluminium melts at 660 °C" as the critical temperature** — this is wrong. Structural failure occurs at **200–250 °C**, well below the melting point.
> 4. **Skipping units** in numerical answers (mm vs m). Always show unit conversions explicitly.
> 5. **Not justifying material selection** — the examiner wants *why* a material is chosen, not just *what* is chosen. Tie every choice to a property (corrosion, weight, melting point, cost).
> 6. **Confusing A-class and B-class ratings** — A-class prevents smoke + flame; B-class only restricts flame spread.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Fire Protection (Non-Ferrous Materials in Shipbuilding):**
> - **Fire Triangle:** Fuel + Oxygen + Heat — break any side to control fire.
> - **SOLAS Chapter II-2** is the master regulation for ship fire protection.
> - **Passive vs Active:** Passive (built-in, no power) vs Active (sprinklers, gas systems, alarms).
> - **A-Class** (60/30/15/0 min, prevents smoke + flame) | **B-Class** (30/15/0 min, restricts flame only) | **C-Class** (non-combustible, 0 min).
> - **ISO 834 Cellulosic Curve:** $T = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$ — wood/paper fires.
> - **HCM Hydrocarbon Curve:** Reaches **1100 °C in 5 min** — oil/LNG/jet fuel fires.
> - **Aluminium critical temperature:** **200–250 °C** (NOT 660 °C melting point).
> - **Steel critical temperature:** 550–600 °C.
> - **Titanium** is used in submarines (Ti-6Al-4V, melting point 1668 °C) but cost-prohibitive for superstructures.
> - **Cu-Ni 90/10** is the standard for fire-fighting seawater piping.
> - **Mineral wool (density 30–200 kg/m³)** is the primary non-combustible insulation.
> - **Intumescent coatings** expand 10–100× when heated, providing an insulating char.
> - **Fire test standard for ship panels:** ISO 834 with integrity + insulation criteria (140 °C average / 180 °C max single-point rise on unexposed face).
> - **Flashover in a ship cabin** can occur in 2–3 minutes under fast fire growth — detection systems must respond in < 60 seconds.
> - **For 14-mark Part B questions**, always include: (i) formula statement, (ii) numerical evaluation, (iii) material recommendation with property justification, and (iv) practical engineering implications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vibration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vibration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Vibration** in the context of *Non-Ferrous Materials in Shipbuilding* is defined as the periodic oscillatory motion of a marine structural component or system about an equilibrium position, where the restoring forces arise from the **elastic properties** and **damping characteristics** of the constituent non-ferrous alloys (Aluminium, Copper, Titanium, Magnesium, Nickel-based superalloys, and marine composites).

In ship structural engineering, vibration analysis is critical because every ship structure possesses inherent dynamic properties — mass, stiffness, and damping — that govern its response to periodic excitations from:
- Main propulsion shaft and propeller excitation
- Main/auxiliary diesel engine imbalance forces
- Wave-impact loading on hull plating
- Cavitation-induced pressure fluctuations
- Slamming forces on bow and stern regions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Non-ferrous alloys are specifically selected in shipbuilding for their **superior damping capacity** (often 10–100× that of mild steel), **high specific stiffness (E/ρ)**, and **corrosion resistance in marine environments**, making them ideal for vibration-sensitive applications such as propeller blades, superstructures, sonar domes, and mast assemblies.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Tuning Fork on a Ship

Imagine striking a tuning fork made of **aluminium-bronze** versus one made of **mild steel**, both of identical dimensions, and pressing them against a ship's hull plating:

- The **steel fork** rings for a long time, transmitting sharp tonal vibrations into the hull — this represents **low damping capacity** and prolonged resonance risk.
- The **bronze fork** produces a dull, short-lived "thud" — it dissipates vibration energy rapidly through internal microstructural mechanisms (dislocation damping, thermoelastic effects, and grain-boundary sliding).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world maritime analogy:** A ship's propeller blade, when excited by cavitation, behaves like the tuning fork. Manganese-aluminium-bronze (MAB) and Ni-Al-bronze (NAB) propeller blades are chosen specifically because their inherent damping suppresses resonance and reduces acoustic signature (a critical concern in naval vessels). This is the engineering reason *why vibration* forms a mandatory study topic in non-ferrous materials selection.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
|--------|----------|----------------------|
| **g** | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** |
| **ρ (steel)** | Density of mild steel (reference) | **7850 kg/m³** |
| **ρ (Al)** | Density of aluminium alloys | **2700–2800 kg/m³** |
| **ρ (Ti)** | Density of titanium alloys | **4500–4650 kg/m³** |
| **E (steel)** | Young's modulus of mild steel | **200–210 GPa** |
| **E (Al-alloy)** | Young's modulus of aluminium | **68–72 GPa** |
| **E (Ti-alloy)** | Young's modulus of titanium | **100–115 GPa** |
| **G (steel)** | Shear modulus of mild steel | **77–80 GPa** |
| **ν** | Poisson's ratio (metals) | **0.30–0.34** |
| **η (loss factor)** | Material damping loss factor | **0.0001 (steel) – 0.05 (Mg-alloy)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that while aluminium has only **1/3 the stiffness** of steel, it has **1/3 the density** — so its **specific stiffness (E/ρ)** is nearly identical. This is why aluminium alloys are widely used in **vibration-critical superstructure** applications without sacrificing natural frequency.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Vibration Relevant to Shipbuilding

Marine structural vibration is broadly classified into three categories. Understanding this classification is the foundation of *why* different non-ferrous alloys are selected for different ship components.

### 2.1.1 Free (Natural) Vibration
Occurs when a system is displaced from equilibrium and allowed to oscillate **without any external forcing** — governed solely by mass, stiffness, and damping.

**Shipbuilding examples:**
- Hull girder "springing" after a wave impulse
- Propeller blade "ringing" after cavitation bubble collapse
- Shaft whirling after a sudden torque change

### 2.1.2 Forced Vibration
Sustained oscillation under a **continuous time-varying external force**.

**Shipbuilding examples:**
- Engine unbalance excitation at running speed
- Propeller blade-frequency excitation: $f_{exc} = n \times N$ where $n$ = blade count, $N$ = shaft RPM/60
- Wave-induced hull vibration (sum frequency of encounter)

### 2.1.3 Damped Vibration
Real systems always dissipate energy through **material damping**, **structural damping at joints**, and **fluid (hydrodynamic) damping**. Vibration amplitude decays exponentially with time.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Conceptual Hook:** Always remember the **three governing parameters** of any marine vibration problem:
> 1. **Inertia (mass, m)** — resists acceleration
> 2. **Stiffness (k)** — provides restoring force
> 3. **Damping (c)** — dissipates energy
> Non-ferrous materials influence *all three* (density affects mass, E-modulus affects stiffness, alloy microstructure affects damping).

## 2.2 The Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) Model

The fundamental mathematical model for vibration analysis. A ship's superstructure panel, a sonar dome, or a short propeller blade segment can often be idealized as an SDOF system.

**Equation of motion for damped forced vibration:**

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

Where:
- $m$ = equivalent mass (kg)
- $c$ = viscous damping coefficient (N·s/m)
- $k$ = stiffness (N/m)
- $F_0$ = forcing amplitude (N)
- $\omega$ = forcing frequency (rad/s)
- $x, \dot{x}, \ddot{x}$ = displacement, velocity, acceleration

### Step-by-Step Logic of the Equation
1. **Inertia term** $m\ddot{x}$ — Newton's second law; mass resists change in motion.
2. **Damping term** $c\dot{x}$ — Energy loss proportional to velocity (viscous model).
3. **Stiffness term** $kx$ — Hooke's law; elastic restoring force.
4. **Forcing term** $F_0\sin(\omega t)$ — External periodic excitation (e.g., engine unbalance).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Application in Shipbuilding |
|---|----------|---------|------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Natural circular frequency (undamped) | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s | Hull plate, sonar dome |
| 2 | Natural frequency (Hz) | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ | Hz | Engine mount, bulkhead panel |
| 3 | Damped natural frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | rad/s | Real ship components |
| 4 | Critical damping coefficient | $c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n$ | N·s/m | Threshold for non-oscillation |
| 5 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{c_c} = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | dimensionless | Vibration classification |
| 6 | Logarithmic decrement | $\delta = \dfrac{1}{n}\ln\left(\dfrac{x_0}{x_n}\right)$ | dimensionless | Damping measurement |
| 7 | Damping ratio from $\delta$ | $\zeta = \dfrac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}}$ | dimensionless | Experimental |
| 8 | Quality factor | $Q = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta}$ | dimensionless | Sharpness of resonance |
| 9 | Magnification factor (steady-state) | $MF = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | dimensionless | Resonance peak |
| 10 | Transmissibility (force) | $TR = \dfrac{\sqrt{1+(2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | dimensionless | Isolation effectiveness |
| 11 | Cantilever beam nat. freq. (1st mode) | $\omega_n = (1.875)^2 \sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{mL^4}}$ | rad/s | Propeller blade, mast |
| 12 | Shaft torsional vibration | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{GJ}{I_p}}$ | Hz | Propulsion shaft |
| 13 | Specific stiffness | $E/\rho$ | N·m/kg | Material selection |
| 14 | Specific damping capacity | $\psi = \Delta W / W$ | dimensionless | Material ranking |
| 15 | Loss factor | $\eta = \psi / 2\pi = 2\zeta$ | dimensionless | Viscoelastic modelling |

Where $r = \omega/\omega_n$ is the **frequency ratio**, $E$ = Young's modulus, $I$ = area moment of inertia, $L$ = beam length, $G$ = shear modulus, $J$ = polar moment of inertia, $I_p$ = mass moment of inertia of shaft.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Formula #11 (cantilever beam) is **extremely high-yield** because propeller blades in non-ferrous materials courses are almost always modelled as tapered/non-uniform cantilevers. The 1.875² value arises from the first root of $\cos(\beta L)\cosh(\beta L) = -1$.

## 2.4 Material Damping — The Heart of Non-Ferrous Selection

**Material damping** is the intrinsic ability of a material to convert mechanical vibration energy into heat through internal microstructural mechanisms. This is the *single most important property* that distinguishes non-ferrous alloys from steel in marine vibration applications.

### Damping Mechanisms in Non-Ferrous Alloys
1. **Dislocation damping** — Movement of dislocations under cyclic stress (dominant in Mg, pure Al, annealed Cu).
2. **Thermoelastic damping** — Heat flow across grains due to compressive/tensile cycles.
3. **Magnetomechanical damping** — Significant only in ferromagnetic steels (negligible in non-ferrous).
4. **Grain-boundary sliding** — Prominent at elevated temperatures in polycrystals.
5. **Interface damping** — In fibre/matrix interfaces of marine composites (GRP, CFRP).

### Specific Damping Capacity (SDC) Ranking for Marine Alloys

| Material | Specific Damping Capacity (SDC) | Order vs. Steel |
|----------|--------------------------------|-----------------|
| Mild Steel | 0.4 – 1.0 % | 1× (reference) |
| Aluminium 5052 | 3 – 5 % | ~5× |
| Aluminium 6061-T6 | 1 – 2 % | ~2× |
| Manganese Bronze | 5 – 8 % | ~8× |
| Manganese-Al-Bronze (MAB) | 8 – 12 % | ~10× |
| Ni-Al-Bronze (NAB) | 4 – 7 % | ~6× |
| Pure Magnesium | 30 – 60 % | ~50× |
| Mg-alloy (AZ31) | 15 – 30 % | ~25× |
| Titanium Ti-6Al-4V | 0.5 – 1 % | ~1× |
| Grey Cast Iron | 6 – 12 % | ~10× (NOT non-ferrous) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** This table is the *direct link* between material selection and vibration. **MAB propeller blades** are chosen not just for corrosion resistance but because their **8–12% SDC** keeps resonant vibration amplitudes within classification-society limits (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) without requiring additional damping treatments.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Natural Frequency of an Undamped SDOF System

**Starting Point:** Newton's second law on a mass attached to a spring.

**Step 1** — Free body diagram gives restoring force:
$$F_{restoring} = -kx$$

The negative sign indicates the force acts *opposite* to the displacement direction.

**Step 2** — Apply Newton's second law ($F = ma$):
$$m\ddot{x} = -kx$$

**Step 3** — Rearrange to standard homogeneous form:
$$m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$$

**Step 4** — Assume a harmonic solution of the form:
$$x(t) = A\cos(\omega t) + B\sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 5** — Compute the second derivative (acceleration):
$$\ddot{x}(t) = -\omega^2 A\cos(\omega t) - \omega^2 B\sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 6** — Substitute both into the equation of motion:
$$m\left[-\omega^2 A\cos(\omega t) - \omega^2 B\sin(\omega t)\right] + k\left[A\cos(\omega t) + B\sin(\omega t)\right] = 0$$

**Step 7** — Group $\cos(\omega t)$ and $\sin(\omega t)$ terms:
$$(k - m\omega^2)A\cos(\omega t) + (k - m\omega^2)B\sin(\omega t) = 0$$

**Step 8** — For this identity to hold for *all* $t$, the coefficient must vanish:
$$k - m\omega^2 = 0$$

**Step 9** — Solve for the natural circular frequency:

$$\boxed{\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} \quad \text{rad/s}}$$

**Step 10** — Convert to Hz by dividing by $2\pi$:

$$f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} \quad \text{Hz}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Damped Natural Frequency and Damping Ratio

**Starting Point:** Damped free-vibration equation:
$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

**Step 1** — Divide throughout by $m$:
$$\ddot{x} + \dfrac{c}{m}\dot{x} + \dfrac{k}{m}x = 0$$

**Step 2** — Define the critical damping coefficient. At critical damping, the system returns to equilibrium *without oscillation*, which requires the discriminant of the characteristic equation to be zero. Setting $c^2 - 4mk = 0$:
$$c_c = 2\sqrt{mk} = 2m\omega_n$$

**Step 3** — Define the **damping ratio** as the ratio of actual to critical damping:
$$\zeta = \dfrac{c}{c_c} = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{mk}} = \dfrac{c}{2m\omega_n}$$

**Step 4** — Substitute into the equation:
$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n\dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

**Step 5** — Assume exponential solution $x(t) = e^{st}$:
$$s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

**Step 6** — Solve the characteristic quadratic using the formula:
$$s = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

**Step 7** — For underdamped systems ($0 < \zeta < 1$), the term under the root is *negative*. Define the **damped natural frequency**:
$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$$

**Step 8** — The general underdamped solution is:

$$x(t) = X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\cos(\omega_d t - \phi)$$

This shows vibration *decays exponentially* with time constant $\tau = 1/(\zeta\omega_n)$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Logarithmic Decrement (Used in Material Damping Tests)

**Step 1** — Consider the peak amplitudes of an underdamped decaying sinusoid at times $t_0, t_1, t_2, \ldots$ separated by one vibration period $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$:
$$x_n = X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n (t_0 + nT_d)}$$

**Step 2** — Form the ratio of successive peaks:
$$\dfrac{x_0}{x_n} = \dfrac{X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_0}}{X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n(t_0+nT_d)}} = e^{\zeta\omega_n nT_d}$$

**Step 3** — Take the natural logarithm:
$$\ln\left(\dfrac{x_0}{x_n}\right) = \zeta\omega_n n T_d$$

**Step 4** — Define the **logarithmic decrement** $\delta$ as the natural log of successive peak ratio ($n=1$):
$$\delta = \zeta\omega_n T_d = \zeta\omega_n \cdot \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_d}$$

**Step 5** — Substitute $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$:
$$\delta = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

**Step 6** — For small $\zeta$ (typical in metals, $\zeta < 0.1$), the approximation $\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} \approx 1$ yields the **highly useful** form:

$$\boxed{\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta \quad \text{or} \quad \zeta \approx \dfrac{\delta}{2\pi}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A sonar dome panel made of aluminium alloy 5052-H32 has equivalent mass $m = 4.5 \text{ kg}$, stiffness $k = 18{,}000 \text{ N/m}$, and damping ratio $\zeta = 0.04$. Determine:
> (a) Natural frequency $f_n$  
> (b) Damped natural frequency $f_d$  
> (c) Logarithmic decrement $\delta$  
> (d) Number of cycles for amplitude to decay to 10% of initial

**Solution:**

**Part (a):**
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{18{,}000}{4.5}} = \sqrt{4000} = 63.25 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$f_n = \dfrac{63.25}{2\pi} = 10.07 \text{ Hz}$$

**Part (b):**
$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 63.25\sqrt{1-(0.04)^2} = 63.25 \times 0.9992 = 63.20 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$f_d = \dfrac{63.20}{2\pi} = 10.06 \text{ Hz}$$

**Part (c):**
$$\delta = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = \dfrac{2\pi(0.04)}{0.9992} = 0.2515$$

**Part (d):** We need $x_n/x_0 = 0.10$:
$$e^{n\delta} = 10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n\delta = \ln(10) = 2.3026$$
$$n = \dfrac{2.3026}{0.2515} = 9.16 \text{ cycles} \approx 10 \text{ cycles}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The sonar dome's vibration decays to 10% of initial amplitude in approximately **10 cycles** (~1 second), which is acceptable for acoustic-sensor applications where rapid settling of structural noise is critical.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Marine Vibration Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Marine Component Identified] --> B{Component Type}
    B -->|Propeller Blade| C[Tapered Cantilever Model]
    B -->|Hull Plate Panel| D[Simply Supported Plate]
    B -->|Shaft System| E[Torsional SDOF/MDOF]
    B -->|Superstructure| F[Frame Model with E and rho]

    C --> G[Material Selection Database]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G

    G --> G1[Aluminium Alloys]
    G --> G2[Copper Alloys - Bronze]
    G --> G3[Titanium Alloys]
    G --> G4[Magnesium Alloys]
    G --> G5[Nickel Alloys]

    G1 --> H[Compute E, rho, zeta]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    G5 --> H

    H --> I[Calculate Natural Frequency omega_n]
    I --> J{Damping Analysis}

    L --> R
    R --> S[Final Material Recommendation]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define vibration. Classify the types of vibration relevant to shipbuilding applications with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Vibration is the periodic oscillatory motion of a body or system about its equilibrium position, characterized by mass, stiffness, and damping properties.

**Classification with shipbuilding examples:**

1. **Free (Natural) Vibration** — Occurs when a system, once displaced, oscillates without external forcing. *Example:* A propeller blade "ringing" after being struck by a cavitation bubble; hull girder oscillation after wave impact.

2. **Forced Vibration** — Sustained oscillation under a continuous external force. *Example:* Hull vibration excited at the blade frequency $f_b = n \times N/60$, where $n$ is the number of blades and $N$ is the propeller RPM.

3. **Damped Vibration** — Real systems dissipate vibrational energy. *Example:* A Ni-Al-Bronze propeller blade exhibits higher material damping than a steel equivalent, reducing resonant vibration amplitudes.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the term "Specific Damping Capacity" (SDC) and rank the following marine non-ferrous alloys in order of decreasing SDC: Mild Steel, Manganese-Aluminium-Bronze, Aluminium 5052, Magnesium AZ31.**

**Model Answer:**

**Specific Damping Capacity (SDC):** It is defined as the ratio of vibrational energy dissipated per cycle ($\Delta W$) to the total vibrational energy stored in the system ($W$):

$$\psi = \dfrac{\Delta W}{W}$$

It is a dimensionless quantity (often expressed as a percentage) and is the most direct measure of a material's intrinsic ability to suppress vibration.

**Ranking (decreasing SDC):**
1. **Magnesium AZ31** — 15 to 30 % (highest)
2. **Manganese-Aluminium-Bronze (MAB)** — 8 to 12 %
3. **Aluminium 5052** — 3 to 5 %
4. **Mild Steel** — 0.4 to 1.0 % (lowest)

**Engineering significance:** Magnesium alloys and MAB are preferred where vibration suppression is critical, even though their absolute strength may be lower than steel.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) Derive the equation of motion for a damped single-degree-of-freedom system subjected to harmonic forcing. From this, obtain the expression for the magnification factor at resonance. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the system:** Consider a mass $m$ connected to a spring of stiffness $k$ and a viscous damper of coefficient $c$, with a harmonic force $F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$ applied.

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's second law:**
$$m\ddot{x}(t) = \sum F = -kx(t) - c\dot{x}(t) + F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to standard form:**
$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 4 — Assume steady-state solution:**
$$x(t) = X\sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and equate coefficients:** After differentiation and algebraic elimination, the steady-state amplitude is:

$$X = \dfrac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k-m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Introduce frequency ratio $r = \omega/\omega_n$ and damping ratio $\zeta$:** Using $k = m\omega_n^2$ and $c = 2\zeta m\omega_n$:

$$X = \dfrac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

**Step 7 — Define the Magnification Factor (MF):**

$$\boxed{MF = \dfrac{X}{X_{static}} = \dfrac{X}{F_0/k} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$

**Step 8 — At resonance** ($\omega = \omega_n$, i.e. $r = 1$):
$$MF_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta}$$

**Engineering insight:** This shows that the peak vibration amplitude at resonance is governed *inversely* by the damping ratio. Adding **MAB (high $\zeta$ ~0.03–0.05)** instead of steel ($\zeta$ ~0.001) reduces the resonant peak by a factor of **30 to 50×**.

**(b) A propeller blade made of Ni-Al-Bronze (NAB) is idealized as a cantilever beam of length $L = 0.6$ m, rectangular cross-section $0.08$ m × $0.025$ m, density $\rho = 7600$ kg/m³, Young's modulus $E = 105$ GPa, and damping ratio $\zeta = 0.03$. Calculate: (i) the first natural frequency, (ii) the logarithmic decrement, and (iii) the magnification factor at a forcing frequency of 35 Hz. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute cross-sectional area and second moment of area:**
$$A = 0.08 \times 0.025 = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$
$$I = \dfrac{bh^3}{12} = \dfrac{0.08 \times (0.025)^3}{12} = 1.042 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 2 — Compute mass per unit length:**
$$m' = \rho A = 7600 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} = 15.2 \text{ kg/m}$$

**Step 3 — Apply cantilever first-mode formula:**
$$\omega_n = (1.875)^2 \sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{m'L^4}}$$

$$= 3.516 \sqrt{\dfrac{(105 \times 10^9)(1.042 \times 10^{-7})}{(15.2)(0.6)^4}}$$

$$= 3.516 \sqrt{\dfrac{10{,}941}{1.971}} = 3.516\sqrt{5551.5} = 3.516 \times 74.51 = 261.97 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Hz:**
$$f_n = \dfrac{261.97}{2\pi} = 41.69 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Compute logarithmic decrement:**
$$\delta = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = \dfrac{2\pi(0.03)}{\sqrt{1-0.0009}} \approx \dfrac{0.1885}{0.99955} = 0.1886$$

**Step 6 — Compute frequency ratio at 35 Hz:**
$$r = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \dfrac{35}{41.69} = 0.8395$$

**Step 7 — Compute magnification factor:**
$$MF = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-0.7048)^2 + (2 \times 0.03 \times 0.8395)^2}}$$

$$= \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(0.2952)^2 + (0.05037)^2}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{0.08714 + 0.002537}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{0.08968}} = \dfrac{1}{0.2995} = 3.34$$

**Engineering interpretation:** At 35 Hz, the blade vibrates with an amplitude **3.34 times the static deflection** — well within safe limits (resonance peak at 41.69 Hz would give $MF = 1/0.06 \approx 16.7$, which is critical). The blade should be operated below 41.69 Hz or have additional damping treatment.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) With a neat sketch, explain the Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) system and derive the natural frequency. State TWO examples where SDOF idealization is used in ship vibration analysis. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**SDOF System Description:** A single mass $m$ connected to a fixed support through a linear spring (stiffness $k$) and a viscous damper (coefficient $c$), with a forcing function $F(t)$ applied to the mass.

**Free Body Diagram and Free Vibration Equation:**

The mass, when displaced by $x$ from equilibrium, experiences:
- Restoring force from spring: $F_s = -kx$
- Damping force: $F_d = -c\dot{x}$

By Newton's second law:
$$m\ddot{x} = -kx - c\dot{x}$$

Rearranging:
$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

For **undamped free vibration** ($c = 0$):
$$m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$$

**Solution by assuming $x = X\cos(\omega t)$:**
$$\ddot{x} = -\omega^2 X\cos(\omega t)$$

Substituting:
$$-m\omega^2 X\cos(\omega t) + kX\cos(\omega t) = 0$$

$$(k - m\omega^2)X\cos(\omega t) = 0$$

For non-trivial solution:
$$\omega_n^2 = \dfrac{k}{m}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} \quad \text{rad/s}, \quad f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} \text{ Hz}}$$

**Two shipbuilding examples of SDOF idealization:**

1. **Engine Mount System:** A marine diesel engine on rubber/metal isolators is modeled as a rigid mass on a spring-damper. The isolator's stiffness and damping are selected so that $f_n$ is well below the engine firing frequency.

2. **Sonar Dome Panel:** A flat or dome-shaped panel subjected to hydrodynamic pressure fluctuations is approximated as a mass-spring-damper lumped system for preliminary design of its natural frequency.

**(b) Discuss the role of non-ferrous materials in vibration suppression in shipbuilding. Compare the vibration characteristics of Aluminium 5083, Manganese-Aluminium-Bronze, and Titanium Ti-6Al-4V for marine propeller blade applications using a suitable table. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of Non-Ferrous Materials in Vibration Suppression:**

Non-ferrous alloys are critical in marine vibration control for three primary reasons:

1. **High Specific Damping Capacity (SDC):** MAB has SDC ~8–12%, ~10× that of mild steel, allowing it to absorb vibration energy internally without requiring additional damping layers.

2. **High Specific Stiffness ($E/\rho$):** Aluminium and titanium provide stiffness comparable to steel at one-third to half the weight, which directly raises the natural frequency of structures (since $f_n \propto \sqrt{k/m} = \sqrt{(E \cdot I/\rho \cdot A \cdot L^4)}$).

3. **Corrosion Resistance in Seawater:** Unlike steel, non-ferrous alloys form protective oxide layers (Al₂O₃, TiO₂, Cu₂(OH)₂CO₃ patina) that maintain their elastic and damping properties over decades of marine service.

**Comparison Table for Propeller Blade Applications:**

| Property | Aluminium 5083-H116 | Manganese-Al-Bronze (MAB) | Titanium Ti-6Al-4V |
|----------|--------------------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| Density $\rho$ (kg/m³) | 2660 | 7600 | 4430 |
| Young's Modulus $E$ (GPa) | 70 | 105 | 114 |
| Specific Stiffness $E/\rho$ (GN·m/kg) | 26.3 | 13.8 | 25.7 |
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 275 – 350 | 650 – 750 | 895 – 1050 |
| Damping Ratio $\zeta$ | 0.005 – 0.008 | 0.03 – 0.05 | 0.001 – 0.002 |
| Specific Damping Capacity (%) | 3 – 5 | 8 – 12 | 0.5 – 1.0 |
| Corrosion Resistance | Excellent | Excellent (marine) | Outstanding |
| Relative Natural Frequency | High | Moderate | High |
| Relative Vibration Amplitude at Resonance | Low | **Lowest** | Highest |
| Typical Use in Ships | Fast craft hulls, superstructure | **Large propellers, valves, pumps** | Submarine pressure hulls, fasteners |

**Selection Conclusion:**

- **Aluminium 5083** — Best for lightweight fast-ferry and naval craft hulls where speed and reduced inertia dominate.
- **MAB** — Preferred for **large-diameter propeller blades** (especially for tankers, naval frigates) where high damping and resistance to cavitation-erosion are paramount.
- **Ti-6Al-4V** — Used in **submarines and high-performance propellers** where strength-to-weight ratio is critical, and added damping is achieved through constrained-layer damping pads.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 1.875² = 3.516** for cantilever natural frequency — using 1.875 instead of 3.516 loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing $\omega_n$ (rad/s) and $f_n$ (Hz)** — failing to divide by $2\pi$ loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing damping ratio $\zeta$ with logarithmic decrement $\delta$** — they differ by a factor of $2\pi$ for small $\zeta$.
> 4. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct up to 1 mark.
> 5. **Assuming $E$ for aluminium = $E$ for steel** — this is a serious conceptual error. Aluminium's $E \approx 70$ GPa vs. steel's 200 GPa is fundamental.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Vibration** = periodic oscillatory motion governed by **mass ($m$)**, **stiffness ($k$)**, and **damping ($c$)**.
- [ ] **Three types:** Free, Forced, Damped. Most marine applications involve **damped forced** vibration.
- [ ] **SDOF natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ in rad/s; divide by $2\pi$ for Hz.
- [ ] **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = c / (2\sqrt{km})$. Underdamped: $0 < \zeta < 1$.
- [ ] **Damped natural frequency:** $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$.
- [ ] **Logarithmic decrement:** $\delta = (1/n)\ln(x_0/x_n) \approx 2\pi\zeta$ for small $\zeta$.
- [ ] **Magnification factor at resonance:** $MF_{res} = 1/(2\zeta)$ — lower damping = sharper peak.
- [ ] **Cantilever beam** (propeller blade model): $\omega_n = (1.875)^2\sqrt{EI/(mL^4)} = 3.516\sqrt{EI/(mL^4)}$.
- [ ] **Specific stiffness $E/\rho$** is the key material-selection index for vibration-critical lightweight structures.
- [ ] **Specific Damping Capacity (SDC) ranking (decreasing):** Mg-AZ31 > MAB > Grey Cast Iron > NAB > Al-5052 > Mild Steel > Ti-6Al-4V.
- [ ] **Marine applications of MAB/NAB:** Propeller blades, pump impellers, valve bodies, sonar domes.
- [ ] **Marine applications of Aluminium 5083/5052:** Superstructures, fast-ferry hulls, mast assemblies, helicopter decks.
- [ ] **Marine applications of Ti-6Al-4V:** Submarine pressure hulls, fasteners, propeller shafts in high-performance vessels.
- [ ] **Resonance avoidance:** ensure excitation frequency (e.g., blade frequency $n \cdot N/60$) is **at least ±20% away** from any structural natural frequency.
- [ ] **Class society rules:** DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, Bureau Veritas all set limits on hull vibration velocity (typically 5–15 mm/s RMS) for crew comfort and structural fatigue life.
- [ ] **Damping treatments** if material SDC is insufficient: constrained-layer damping (CLD), tuned mass dampers (TMD), viscoelastic layers, sandwich composites.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line takeaway for KTU exams:**
> *"In shipbuilding, non-ferrous alloys are chosen not only for corrosion resistance and weight saving but specifically for their **specific stiffness ($E/\rho$)** and **damping capacity (SDC)** to control vibration and avoid resonance in service."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maintenance and Repair
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Maintenance and Repair

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maintenance and Repair (M&R) of Non-Ferrous Materials in Shipbuilding** is defined as the integrated set of engineering practices, inspection protocols, surface engineering techniques, and metallurgical interventions employed to preserve, restore, and extend the operational service life of marine structural components, propulsion systems, and auxiliary outfits fabricated from non-ferrous alloys (principally aluminium, copper, titanium, and their composites) against degradation mechanisms such as **galvanic corrosion**, **erosion-corrosion**, **cavitation**, **biofouling**, and **fatigue cracking** in marine environments.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with the **NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education** framework, this topic bridges the Course Outcomes **CO2** (Identify the properties of non-ferrous materials) and **CO3** (Apply maintenance strategies to marine non-ferrous components).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Human Body Check-up for a Ship"**
> Think of a ship as a giant floating human body. The hull is the skin, the propeller is the heart, and the piping is the bloodstream. Non-ferrous materials are the **specialized tissues** — light bones (aluminium superstructure), flexible joints (copper-nickel pipes), and durable teeth (titanium propellers). Just as a human body needs regular check-ups, medicines (coatings), and sometimes surgery (welding repair) to survive in a hostile environment (saltwater + sun + waves), ships need **scheduled maintenance (preventive)**, **emergency repair (corrective)**, and **condition-based servicing (predictive)** to remain seaworthy and class-certified.

### 1.3 Classification of Maintenance Philosophies

The maritime industry recognizes **three principal maintenance philosophies** standardized under **ISO 13373** and **IACS (International Association of Classification Societies)** UR Z17/Z23:

| Philosophy | Definition | Application in Non-Ferrous Components |
|---|---|---|
| **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** | Scheduled, time-based interventions to prevent failure. | Re-coating aluminium superstructure every **5 years**; anode replacement every **2–4 years**. |
| **Corrective Maintenance (CM)** | Reactive repair after a fault or damage is detected. | Welding repair of cracked Muntz metal heat exchangers; dent removal on aluminium decks. |
| **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** | Condition-monitoring driven, performed just before failure. | Vibration analysis of bronze propellers; thermography of copper busbars. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Industry Metric:** The **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** for properly maintained aluminium superstructures is **15–25 years**, whereas untreated components fail within **5–7 years** due to pitting and exfoliation corrosion in splash zones.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Degradation Mechanisms in Marine Non-Ferrous Materials

Non-ferrous materials degrade through **five primary mechanisms** in shipboard service. Each mechanism requires a distinct maintenance protocol.

#### 2.1.1 Galvanic (Bimetallic) Corrosion
- **Why it occurs:** Non-ferrous alloys (e.g., aluminium alloys with potential $E_{Al} = -1.05$ V vs SCE) coupled with more noble metals (e.g., copper alloys $E_{Cu} = +0.34$ V vs SCE) in seawater (conductivity $\approx 50$ mS/cm) form a galvanic cell.
- **How to control:**
  1. **Isolation** — Use neoprene gaskets, PVC sleeves, or dielectric unions.
  2. **Area ratio control** — Avoid small anode / large cathode (worst-case ratio is **1:100**).
  3. **Sacrificial anodes** — Zinc ($E_{Zn} = -0.76$ V) or aluminium anodes welded to hull.
  4. **Coatings** — Epoxy-based, $200–500\,\mu m$ DFT (Dry Film Thickness).

#### 2.1.2 Erosion-Corrosion & Cavitation
- **Why it occurs:** High-velocity seawater flow (typically $V > 3$ m/s in copper pipes) disrupts the protective oxide film.
- **How to control:**
  1. Limit flow velocity in **Cu-Ni (90/10) pipes** to $\leq 3.5$ m/s.
  2. Use **aluminium-bronze (AB2)** for pump impellers and valves.
  3. Apply **cathodic protection (CP)** with reference electrode monitoring.

#### 2.1.3 Exfoliation & Pitting (Aluminium Alloys)
- **Why it occurs:** Marine-grade aluminium alloys (5083, 5086, 5456) suffer exfoliation along grain boundaries when exposed to saltwater spray.
- **How to control:**
  1. Specify **H116 / H117 tempers** (resistant tempers).
  2. Apply chromate conversion coatings (Alodine 1200) followed by epoxy primer.
  3. Conduct annual **ultrasonic thickness (UT) gauging** with $\pm 0.1$ mm precision.

#### 2.1.4 Biofouling & Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion (MIC)
- **Why it occurs:** Barnacles, tubeworms, and sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB) colonize hull surfaces, creating differential aeration cells.
- **How to control:**
  1. **Anti-fouling (AF) coatings** with cuprous oxide ($Cu_2O$) or tin-free biocides.
  2. **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)** at $-0.85$ V vs Ag/AgCl reference.
  3. Periodic **hull cleaning** every **6–12 months** (or as per **AF coating supplier's leach rate**).
