#### 2.1.5 Fatigue & Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)
- **Why it occurs:** Cyclic wave loading + corrosive environment = accelerated crack propagation in **7xxx series aluminium** and **brass condenser tubes**.
- **How to control:**
  1. Shot peening to induce compressive residual stress ($\sigma_{residual} \geq -200$ MPa).
  2. Routine **eddy current testing (ECT)** for non-ferrous tube inspection.
  3. Replacement scheduling based on **Paris Law** crack growth modelling.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU Board-relevant. Memorize the units, boundary conditions, and typical numerical ranges.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta m = K \cdot I \cdot t$ | Faraday's Law of Corrosion | $\Delta m$ = mass loss (g); $K$ = electrochemical equivalent (g/C); $I$ = current (A); $t$ = time (s) | Anode mass calculation |
| 2 | $CR = \frac{87.6 \cdot \Delta m}{\rho \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Corrosion Rate (mm/yr) | $\rho$ = density (g/cm³); $A$ = area (m²); $t$ = hours | Acceptable $CR \leq 0.1$ mm/yr for non-ferrous marine alloys |
| 3 | $I_{gal} = \frac{E_c - E_a}{R_{tot}}$ | Galvanic Current | $E_c, E_a$ = cathode/anode potentials (V); $R_{tot}$ = total circuit resistance ($\Omega$) | Galvanic series analysis |
| 4 | $N = \sigma^n \cdot K$ | Coffin-Manson (Low Cycle Fatigue) | $N$ = cycles to failure; $\sigma$ = strain range | Propeller bronze life estimation |
| 5 | $\frac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K)^m$ | Paris Law (Crack Growth) | $a$ = crack length (m); $N$ = cycles; $\Delta K$ = stress intensity range (MPa$\sqrt{m}$); $C, m$ = material constants | Aluminium superstructure SCC |
| 6 | $\Delta K = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$ | Stress Intensity Range | $Y$ = geometry factor; $\sigma$ = applied stress (MPa); $a$ = crack depth (m) | Critical when $\Delta K \geq \Delta K_{th}$ |
| 7 | $V_{max} \leq 3.5$ m/s | Cu-Ni Pipe Flow Limit | $V$ = seawater velocity | Erosion-corrosion prevention |
| 8 | $E_{CP} = -0.85$ V vs Ag/AgCl | ICCP Reference Potential | $E_{CP}$ = protection potential | Hull CP system design |
| 9 | $W = \frac{I \cdot T \cdot 8760}{C}$ | Cathodic Protection Anode Mass | $W$ = anode mass (kg); $I$ = current (A); $T$ = design life (yr); $C$ = capacity (Ah/kg) | Zinc anode: $C = 780$ Ah/kg |
| 10 | $h_{min} = \frac{t_{design} \cdot CR_{max}}{1000}$ | Minimum Wall Thickness | $h_{min}$ = mm; $t$ = design life (yr); $CR$ = corrosion rate (mm/yr) | Pipe replacement criteria |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Classification Society Compliance:** Det Norske Veritas (DNV), Lloyd's Register (LR), Bureau Veritas (BV), and Indian Register of Shipping (IRS) all mandate maintenance logs in the **Ship's Planned Maintenance System (PMS)** as per **IACS UR Z17**.
- **Defence Applications:** The Indian Navy's **Naval Technical Staff (NTS)** uses the **Standard Maintenance Procedure (SMP)** manual for non-ferrous outfit overhauls.
- **Commercial Fleet:** Most shipping companies use **SAP PM** or **AMOS** software for digital PMS tracking of non-ferrous components.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Faraday's Law to Corrosion Rate Conversion

**Starting Point:** Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis gives the mass of metal dissolved by a corrosion current.

$$
\Delta m = K \cdot I \cdot t
$$

**Step 1:** Express the electrochemical equivalent in terms of molar mass and valency.

$$
K = \frac{M}{n \cdot F}
$$

where $M$ is the molar mass (g/mol), $n$ is the valency, and $F = 96485$ C/mol is Faraday's constant.

**Step 2:** Substituting into the mass loss expression yields:

$$
\Delta m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert the mass loss into a depth of metal removed by dividing by density and area.

$$
\text{Depth removed} = \frac{\Delta m}{\rho \cdot A} = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}
$$

**Step 4:** Normalize to **millimetres per year (mm/yr)** by introducing unit conversion factors ($I$ in A, $t$ in hours per year $= 8760$, depth in mm):

$$
CR = \frac{\Delta m \times 10}{\rho \cdot A \cdot t \times 1000} \times 8760
$$

**Step 5:** Combining constants ($10/1000 = 0.01$ and converting appropriately):

$$
\boxed{CR \, (\text{mm/yr}) = \frac{87.6 \times \Delta m \, (\text{g})}{\rho \, (\text{g/cm}^3) \times A \, (\text{m}^2) \times t \, (\text{hr})}}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Cathodic Protection Anode Sizing

**Problem (KTU 2024 Scheme style):**
A steel-hulled ship with an aluminium bronze (Cu-Al) propeller shaft bearing is to be protected by zinc sacrificial anodes. The submerged hull area is $A = 800$ m². The required current density is $i = 0.015$ A/m². Design life required is $T = 4$ years. Zinc anode capacity $C = 780$ Ah/kg. Utilisation factor $u = 0.85$. Calculate the total anode mass required.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Total current required**

$$
I_{total} = i \times A = 0.015 \times 800 = 12 \, \text{A}
$$

**Step 2: Total charge over design life (in hours, 4 years = 35040 hours)**

$$
Q_{total} = I_{total} \times T_{hours} = 12 \times 35040 = 420480 \, \text{Ah}
$$

**Step 3: Theoretical anode mass (without utilisation factor)**

$$
W_{theoretical} = \frac{Q_{total}}{C} = \frac{420480}{780} = 539.08 \, \text{kg}
$$

**Step 4: Actual anode mass (with utilisation factor)**

$$
W_{actual} = \frac{W_{theoretical}}{u} = \frac{539.08}{0.85} = 634.21 \, \text{kg}
$$

**Step 5: Number of anodes (standard zinc anode = 25 kg each)**

$$
N_{anodes} = \lceil \frac{634.21}{25} \rceil = 26 \text{ anodes}
$$

**Final Answer:** **$\approx 26$ standard 25-kg zinc anodes**, distributed along the hull with spacing not exceeding **3 m** near the stern for effective propeller shaft protection.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Repair Techniques vs Non-Ferrous Material

| Defect / Damage | Applicable Repair Technique | Skill Level | Class Approval Required? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Small crack in aluminium plate (< 50 mm) | **TIG (GTAW) welding** with ER4043 / ER5356 filler | High | Yes — DNV/LR surveyor |
| Pitting in copper-nickel pipe | **Sleeve repair** with brazing (BCuP-3 filler) | Medium | Yes |
| Worn bronze propeller blade tip | **Metal arc build-up + machining** | High | Yes — dynamic balance re-cert |
| Galvanic corrosion at aluminium-copper joint | **Dielectric union replacement** + re-coating | Low | No (if within planned docking) |
| Exfoliation corrosion in 5083 plate | **Local excavation + buttering + TIG weld** | Expert | Yes — class approved WPQR |
| Biofouling on hull | **Diver-led cleaning** + recoat with AF paint | Low | No |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Marine Non-Ferrous Maintenance Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship In Service] --> B[Scheduled Docking Every 30-60 Months]
    A --> C[Condition Monitoring Continuous]
    C --> D{Vibration / UT / ECT Reading}
    D -->|Abnormal| E[Predictive Maintenance Triggered]
    D -->|Normal| A
    B --> F[Hull Inspection by Class Surveyor]
    F --> G{Non-Ferrous Component Status}
    G -->|Aluminium Hull OK| H[Re-coat Anti-Corrosive + AF Paint]
    G -->|Cu-Ni Pipe Worn| I[Sleeve Repair or Sectional Replacement]
    G -->|Bronze Propeller Damaged| J[Metal Arc Build-up + Re-machining]
    G -->|Titanium Condenser OK| K[Cleaning + ECT Scan]
    E --> L[Root Cause Analysis RCA]
    L --> M[Corrective Action Plan CAP]
    M --> N[Repair in Dry Dock]
    H --> O[Sea Trials + Class Re-Certification]
    I --> O
    J --> O
    K --> O
    N --> O
    O --> A
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Galvanic Corrosion Protection Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Design Stage] --> P2[Material Selection]
    P2 --> P3[Avoid Cu-Al Direct Contact]
    P3 --> P4[Use Dielectric Isolators]
    P4 --> P5[Coating Application]
    P5 --> P6[Sacrificial Anode Welding]
    P6 --> P7[ICCP System Commissioning]
    P7 --> P8[Annual CP Potential Survey]
    P8 --> P9{Anode Depletion 80 Percent}
    P9 -->|Yes| P10[Replace Anodes at Next Docking]
    P9 -->|No| P11[Continue Monitoring]
    P10 --> P7
    P11 --> P8
```

### 4.3 Schematic: TIG Repair Procedure for Aluminium Hull Plate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Identify Crack via Dye Penetrant Test] --> S2[Mark Crack End with 5 mm Stop Hole]
    S2 --> S3[Grind Out Crack 60 Degree Groove]
    S3 --> S4[Pre-heat to 65-110 Degree Celsius]
    S4 --> S5[TIG Weld with ER5356 Filler]
    S5 --> S6[Back-purge with Argon]
    S6 --> S7[Inter-pass Cleaning Stainless Wire Brush]
    S7 --> S8[Visual + Radiographic Examination]
    S8 --> S9{Defects Found}
    S9 -->|Yes| S10[Grind Out and Re-weld]
    S9 -->|No| S11[Restore Corrosion Protection Coating]
    S10 --> S5
    S11 --> S12[Class Surveyor Sign-off]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*Define galvanic corrosion in the context of marine non-ferrous materials. List two preventive measures.*

**Model Answer:**
Galvanic corrosion is the accelerated electrochemical degradation of a less noble metal (anode) when placed in electrical contact with a more noble metal (cathode) in the presence of an electrolyte (seawater). In ships, this commonly occurs at aluminium–copper, aluminium–stainless steel, or steel–bronze joints.

**Preventive measures:**
1. **Electrical isolation** using dielectric gaskets, PVC sleeves, or rubber washers at bolted joints.
2. **Sacrificial anode attachment** (zinc or aluminium anodes) close to the dissimilar metal joint.
3. (Bonus) Control the cathode-to-anode area ratio to be as close to **1:1** as possible.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Adapted]**
*What is the significance of the Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) reference potential of $-0.85$ V vs Ag/AgCl in ship hull protection?*

**Model Answer:**
The potential $-0.85$ V vs Ag/AgCl/Seawater reference electrode is the internationally accepted threshold (per **DNV-RP-B401** and **NACE SP0176**) below which the corrosion rate of marine steels and aluminium alloys is reduced to negligible levels. Maintaining the hull at this potential:
- Shifts the metal into the immune region of the Pourbaix diagram.
- Prevents pitting initiation on aluminium superstructures.
- Inhibits microbiologically influenced corrosion (MIC) by suppressing sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB) activity.

---

### **Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by (a) writing only the formula without mentioning **units**, (b) confusing **preventive vs corrective maintenance**, and (c) forgetting to mention the **classification society approval** required for non-ferrous welding repairs. Always state the relevant **IACS UR** clause (e.g., UR Z17, UR Z23) wherever applicable.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *[7 Marks]* Explain the five major degradation mechanisms affecting non-ferrous materials in shipboard service. State the corresponding non-ferrous alloy most susceptible and one inspection method for each.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Mechanism | Susceptible Alloy | Inspection Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Galvanic corrosion | Aluminium 5083 + Cu-Ni 90/10 | Half-cell potential mapping |
| 2 | Erosion-corrosion | Cu-Ni 90/10 in pipes | UT thickness gauging |
| 3 | Pitting / Exfoliation | Aluminium 5083-H116 | Visual + pit gauge |
| 4 | Biofouling / MIC | All submerged alloys | Coupon exposure + biofilm sampling |
| 5 | Fatigue / SCC | 7xxx Al, brass (dezincification) | Eddy current testing (ECT) |

**Engineering Explanation:**
1. **Galvanic corrosion** arises when two dissimilar metals are coupled in seawater, forming an electrochemical cell with the more noble metal acting as cathode and the less noble as anode. The severity depends on the **galvanic series position** and the **cathode-to-anode area ratio**.
2. **Erosion-corrosion** is the mechanical removal of the protective oxide film by high-velocity fluid flow, exposing fresh metal to corrosion. Cu-Ni 90/10 piping is limited to flow velocities of $\leq 3.5$ m/s.
3. **Pitting and exfoliation** are localized forms of attack; exfoliation is specific to wrought aluminium alloys with elongated grain structures (e.g., 5083, 5456).
4. **Biofouling/MIC** involves biological organisms creating differential aeration cells; sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB) produce $H_2S$ that attacks copper alloys via sulfidation.
5. **Fatigue/SCC** involves the synergistic effect of cyclic stress and corrosive environment, governed by the Paris Law: $da/dN = C(\Delta K)^m$.

**(b)** *[7 Marks]* A copper-nickel (90/10) pipe in a ship's seawater cooling system has experienced a wall-thickness loss of $0.8$ mm over $3$ years of service. The original wall thickness was $2.5$ mm. If the minimum allowable thickness (per DNV) is $1.2$ mm, determine (i) the corrosion rate in mm/yr, and (ii) the remaining service life in years.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Corrosion rate calculation**

$$
CR = \frac{\text{Wall loss}}{\text{Service time}} = \frac{0.8 \, \text{mm}}{3 \, \text{yr}} = 0.267 \, \text{mm/yr}
$$

**Step 2: Remaining wall thickness available for further service**

$$
h_{available} = 2.5 - 1.2 = 1.3 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 3: Remaining service life**

$$
t_{remaining} = \frac{h_{available}}{CR} = \frac{1.3}{0.267} = 4.87 \, \text{yr}
$$

**Step 4: Decision**
The pipe has approximately **4.87 years (≈ 5 years)** of service life remaining. The maintenance planner should **schedule a sleeve repair or sectional replacement within 4 years** to allow a safety margin.

**Final Answer:** $CR = 0.267$ mm/yr; Remaining life $\approx 4.87$ years.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *[7 Marks]* With a neat flowchart, describe the **Planned Maintenance System (PMS)** for non-ferrous superstructure components in a modern warship. Mention the role of IACS UR Z17.

**Model Answer:**

The **Planned Maintenance System (PMS)** for non-ferrous superstructure follows a closed-loop cycle:

1. **Inventory & Coding** — Every aluminium plate, extrusion, and fastener is given a unique **Equipment Number (EqNo)** in the PMS database (e.g., AMOS, TM Master, or SAP PM).
2. **Job Card Generation** — Manufacturer's recommendations (e.g., from **Aluminium 5083-H116 supplier**) and **IACS UR Z17.1** define the periodicity of jobs.
3. **Scheduled Execution** — Jobs are grouped into **running hours**, **calendar-based**, or **condition-based** triggers.
4. **Defect Reporting** — Any deviation noted during PMS execution is logged in the **Defect List** with severity codes (C1–C4).
5. **Corrective Action** — Critical defects (C1) are attended in the next port; routine defects (C3/C4) are bundled for the next **scheduled docking**.
6. **Class Surveyor Verification** — Annual and special surveys by IACS member society (DNV/LR/BV) confirm PMS compliance.
7. **Feedback Loop** — Job frequencies are updated based on **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** and **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** data.

**Role of IACS UR Z17:** It mandates that every ship of classification society member flag must operate an approved PMS covering hull, machinery, and electrical systems including all non-ferrous outfit. UR Z17.1 (2017 onwards) requires **risk-based maintenance (RBM)** methodology and **computerized PMS** for ships above 500 GT.

**(b)** *[7 Marks]* Differentiate between **preventive, corrective, and predictive maintenance** with two examples each specific to non-ferrous ship components. Mention the KTU-aligned Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive level for each strategy.

**Model Answer:**

| Strategy | Definition | Example 1 (Non-Ferrous) | Example 2 (Non-Ferrous) | Bloom's Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Preventive Maintenance** | Time or cycle-based intervention to prevent failure. | Recoating aluminium superstructure every 5 years. | Annual replacement of zinc sacrificial anodes. | **Apply (L3)** |
| **Corrective Maintenance** | Repair after a fault has been detected. | TIG welding repair of cracked aluminium deck plate. | Replacement of pitted Cu-Ni 90/10 condenser tube. | **Apply (L3)** |
| **Predictive Maintenance** | Condition-monitoring driven, done just-in-time. | Vibration spectrum analysis of Muntz metal propeller shaft. | Thermographic scan of aluminium-bronze switchboard busbars. | **Analyze (L4)** |

**Key Distinction:** Preventive is calendar-based (rigid), corrective is reactive (costly), and predictive is condition-based (optimal). For non-ferrous components in ships, predictive maintenance delivers the **lowest lifecycle cost (LCC)** when combined with NDT methods like UT, ECT, and vibration analysis.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 2)**

- ✅ **Galvanic corrosion** is the **#1 degradation mode** for non-ferrous ship components, governed by the **galvanic series** and the **cathode-to-anode area ratio**.
- ✅ **Cu-Ni 90/10** pipe maximum flow velocity: **$\leq 3.5$ m/s** (Erosion-corrosion threshold).
- ✅ **Aluminium 5083 / 5086 / 5456** are the standard marine grades; specify **H116 / H117 tempers** to prevent exfoliation.
- ✅ **ICCP reference potential:** **$-0.85$ V vs Ag/AgCl** for hull protection.
- ✅ **Zinc anode capacity:** **$C = 780$ Ah/kg**; standard anode size = **25 kg**.
- ✅ **Corrosion rate (mm/yr)** formula: $CR = \frac{87.6 \cdot \Delta m}{\rho \cdot A \cdot t}$ — remember the **87.6** constant and units.
- ✅ **Paris Law** for crack growth: $da/dN = C(\Delta K)^m$ — critical for SCC and fatigue life prediction.
- ✅ **TIG (GTAW)** is the preferred welding process for marine non-ferrous repair; use **ER4043** (casting repair) or **ER5356** (structural 5xxx series).
- ✅ **IACS UR Z17 / Z23** govern PMS and survey requirements for non-ferrous outfit.
- ✅ **NDT methods for non-ferrous:** **UT** (thickness), **ECT** (tube inspection), **Dye Penetrant** (surface cracks), **Radiography** (weld defects).
- ✅ **Pourbaix diagram** determines immunity, passivity, and corrosion regions — memorize the **E-pH** boundaries for Al, Cu, Ti, Zn.
- ✅ **Titanium** is **immune to corrosion in seawater** above 7 m/s but is **vulnerable to hydrogen embrittlement** under CP; use **Pt/Ti anodes** instead.
- ✅ Always cross-check the **Class Society approved WPQR (Welding Procedure Qualification Record)** before performing any structural non-ferrous repair.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Polymers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classification of Polymers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Polymer** is a high-molecular-weight macromolecule composed of a large number of repeating structural units called **monomers**, joined together by covalent chemical bonds through a process known as **polymerization**. The term *polymer* is derived from the Greek words *poly* (meaning *many*) and *meros* (meaning *parts* or *units*).

Mathematically, a polymer chain can be represented as:

$$[M]_n \quad \text{or} \quad -(M-M-M-M-...-M)_n-$$

where **M** is the monomer unit and **n** is the **degree of polymerization (DP)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Polymers form the backbone of modern marine composites. In shipbuilding, the classification of polymers determines whether a material is suitable for hull structures (FRP), anti-corrosive coatings, buoyancy foams, or high-temperature insulation in engine rooms.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **locomotive train** 🚂:
- Each **wagon (carriage)** of the train = a **monomer** (small, simple unit like ethylene, C₂H₄).
- The **entire train** = a **polymer** (long, heavy, functional machine).
- The **coupling link** between two wagons = the **covalent bond** formed during polymerization.
- A **short train** (5–10 wagons) = an **oligomer**.
- A **long freight train** (1000+ wagons) = a **high polymer** useful for engineering applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the analogy matters:** Just as a train's behavior (speed, capacity, flexibility) depends on the number and type of wagons it carries, a polymer's mechanical, thermal, and chemical properties depend on the **type of monomers**, the **number of repeating units (n)**, and the **arrangement (linear, branched, or crosslinked)** of the chains.

### 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics

The fundamental numerical identity of any polymer is defined by three metrics:

- **Degree of Polymerization (DP or X_n):** The number of monomer units in a polymer chain, typically ranging from **100 to 100,000** for commercial polymers.
- **Molecular Weight (M):** The mass of one mole of polymer chains, usually between **10,000 and 10,000,000 g/mol**.
- **Repeat Unit Molecular Weight (M₀):** The molecular weight of a single monomer building block.

> [!NOTE]
> Standard relationship: **M = n × M₀**, where M is the polymer molecular weight, n is the degree of polymerization, and M₀ is the monomer molecular weight.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Master Classification Matrix

Polymers can be classified on **multiple independent axes**. KTU examiners frequently frame Part A questions around identifying the *correct class* based on behavior. Polymers are classified based on:

#### 2.1.1 Classification Based on **Origin / Source**

| Sub-Class | Definition | Engineering Example | Marine Use |
|-----------|-----------|---------------------|------------|
| **Natural Polymers** | Occur in nature (plants/animals) | Cellulose, Starch, Proteins, Natural Rubber | Limited; wooden boat caulking |
| **Synthetic Polymers** | Man-made via chemical reactions | Polyethylene, PVC, Nylon, Epoxy | FRP hulls, marine coatings |
| **Semi-Synthetic** | Chemically modified natural polymers | Cellulose acetate, Rayon, Vulcanized rubber | Gaskets, hoses |

#### 2.1.2 Classification Based on **Thermal Response (Behavior on Heating)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most frequently asked classification in KTU exams** because it directly dictates service temperature limits on ships.

- **Thermoplastics:** Linear/branched chains held by weak **secondary bonds (van der Waals)**. They **soften on heating and harden on cooling** — a **reversible** process. Recyclable. Examples: **Polyethylene (PE), Polypropylene (PP), PVC, PTFE, Nylon**.
- **Thermosetting Plastics (Thermosets):** Chains form a **3D crosslinked network** via strong **covalent crosslinks**. Once set, they **cannot be remelted or reshaped**. Examples: **Epoxy, Phenolic, Melamine, Polyester, Silicone**.
- **Elastomers:** Lightly crosslinked polymers with **high elastic recovery** (>200% elongation). Examples: **Natural rubber, Neoprene, Silicone rubber, Polyurethane**.

#### 2.1.3 Classification Based on **Polymerization Mechanism**

- **Addition (Chain-Growth) Polymers:** Formed by adding monomers with **no byproduct**. Initiated by free radicals/ions. The monomer must contain a **C=C double bond**. Examples: **PE, PP, PVC, PTFE, Polystyrene**.
- **Condensation (Step-Growth) Polymers:** Formed by condensation reactions with **elimination of small molecules (H₂O, HCl, NH₃)**. Examples: **Nylon 6,6, Polyester (PET), Phenol-formaldehyde, Polycarbonate**.

#### 2.1.4 Classification Based on **Chain Structure / Geometry**

- **Linear Polymers:** Straight, unbranched chains. High density, high tensile strength. Example: **HDPE, Nylon**.
- **Branched Polymers:** Main chain with side branches. Lower density. Example: **LDPE**.
- **Crosslinked Polymers:** Chains joined by covalent bridges. Example: **Vulcanized rubber, Thermosets**.
- **Network Polymers:** 3D interconnected network. Highly rigid. Example: **Epoxy, Phenolic, Melamine**.

#### 2.1.5 Classification Based on **Monomer Composition**

- **Homopolymers:** Built from **one type of monomer**. Example: **Polyethylene (only –CH₂–CH₂– units)**.
- **Copolymers:** Built from **two or more different monomers**. Sub-types: **Random, Alternating, Block, Graft**. Example: **SBR (Styrene-Butadiene Rubber) used in marine fenders**.

#### 2.1.6 Classification Based on **Crystallinity / Physical State**

- **Amorphous Polymers:** Disordered chain arrangement. Transparent. Example: **PMMA (Plexiglass), Polystyrene, PC**.
- **Semi-Crystalline Polymers:** Partial ordered (crystalline) and disordered (amorphous) regions. Example: **HDPE, Nylon, PTFE, PET**.
- **Crystalline Polymers:** Highly ordered. Rare in practice.

#### 2.1.7 Classification Based on **Inorganic vs Organic Backbone**

- **Organic Polymers:** Carbon-based backbone. Most common. Example: **PE, PP, PVC**.
- **Inorganic Polymers:** No carbon in backbone. Example: **Silicones (–Si–O–Si–)**, Phosphazenes.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Polymer Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Logic:** Every classification axis exists because **ship designers need a quick decision tool**. Will the polymer melt near the engine room? → Check **thermal response**. Will it resist seawater? → Check **chemical structure**. Can it be welded/repaired? → Check **thermoplastic vs thermoset**.

- **Why thermal response matters in ships:** Engine rooms reach **80–120°C**. A thermoplastic (like PP, m.p. ~165°C) might deform; a thermoset (like phenolic, stable to 250°C) is preferred.
- **Why chain structure matters:** Crosslinked structures give **dimensional stability** required for propeller shaft bearings; linear structures give **flexibility** required for marine hoses.
- **Why polymerization mechanism matters:** Addition polymers (PE, PVC) are **cheap, mass-produced, and ideal for hull liners**; condensation polymers (epoxy, polyester) provide the **matrix for fiber-reinforced composites** in naval vessels.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Variables | Units |
|---------|-------------|-----------|-------|
| $M = n \cdot M_0$ | Polymer molecular weight relation | $M$ = polymer mol. wt., $n$ = DP, $M_0$ = monomer mol. wt. | g/mol |
| $\bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Number-average molecular weight | $N_i$ = number of chains of mol. wt. $M_i$ | g/mol |
| $\bar{M}_w = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Weight-average molecular weight | — | g/mol |
| $\text{PDI} = \frac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n}$ | Polydispersity Index | Always $\geq 1$; $= 1$ for monodisperse | dimensionless |
| $\% \text{ Crystallinity} = \frac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^0} \times 100$ | Crystallinity from DSC | $\Delta H_f$ = sample enthalpy, $\Delta H_f^0$ = 100% crystalline enthalpy | % |
| $T_g$ (Glass Transition) | Temp. where amorphous region softens | Material property | °C or K |
| $T_m$ (Melting Point) | Temp. where crystalline region melts | Material property | °C or K |
| $T_g < T_{service} < T_m$ | Operating range for semi-crystalline polymers | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The **Polydispersity Index (PDI)** is *not* asked directly often, but its understanding is essential for explaining why **synthetic polymers have variable properties** while **natural proteins are monodisperse**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Shipbuilding

| Polymer Class | Marine Application | Reason for Selection |
|---------------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Thermoplastic (HDPE)** | Piping, hull liners | Corrosion-resistant, weldable |
| **Thermoplastic (PVC)** | Cable insulation, decking | Flame retardant, cheap |
| **Thermoplastic (PTFE)** | Seals, bearings | Lowest friction coefficient |
| **Thermoset (Epoxy + Glass fiber)** | Hull, decks of minesweepers, FRP boats | High strength-to-weight ratio |
| **Thermoset (Polyester + Glass fiber)** | Small craft, patrol boats | Low cost, easy molding |
| **Elastomer (Neoprene)** | Wet suits, dock fenders | Oil & water resistance |
| **Elastomer (Silicone rubber)** | Engine gaskets, O-rings | High temp stability (up to 230°C) |
| **Foamed polymer (PU foam)** | Buoyancy, thermal insulation | Closed-cell structure, low density |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Number-Average Molecular Weight ($\bar{M}_n$)

**Given:** A polymer sample contains $N_1$ chains of molecular weight $M_1$, $N_2$ chains of molecular weight $M_2$, ..., $N_i$ chains of molecular weight $M_i$.

**Step 1:** The total mass of the polymer sample is the sum of mass contributed by each chain population.

$$
\text{Total Mass} = \sum_{i} N_i M_i
$$

**Step 2:** The total number of polymer chains in the sample is the simple sum of chains of each size.

$$
\text{Total Number of Chains} = \sum_{i} N_i
$$

**Step 3:** The **number-average molecular weight** is defined as the **total mass divided by the total number of chains** — a statistical "average chain mass."

$$
\bar{M}_n = \frac{\text{Total Mass}}{\text{Total Number of Chains}} = \frac{\sum_{i} N_i M_i}{\sum_{i} N_i}
$$

**Step 4 (Interpretation):** $\bar{M}_n$ is more sensitive to the presence of **small chains (low $M_i$)** because the numerator weighs each chain equally (once per chain).

### 3.2 Derivation: Weight-Average Molecular Weight ($\bar{M}_w$)

**Step 1:** The weight fraction $w_i$ of chains having molecular weight $M_i$ is:

$$
w_i = \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum_{i} N_i M_i}
$$

**Step 2:** The **weight-average molecular weight** is the weighted average of $M_i$ using $w_i$ as the weight:

$$
\bar{M}_w = \sum_{i} w_i M_i = \sum_{i} \left( \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum N_i M_i} \right) M_i
$$

**Step 3:** Simplifying the denominator:

$$
\bar{M}_w = \frac{\sum_{i} N_i M_i^2}{\sum_{i} N_i M_i}
$$

**Step 4 (Interpretation):** $\bar{M}_w$ is more sensitive to the presence of **large chains (high $M_i$)** because the term $M_i^2$ heavily weighs bigger molecules.

> [!NOTE]
> **The relationship $\bar{M}_w \geq \bar{M}_n$ always holds.** Equality is reached only for a perfectly monodisperse sample (e.g., a protein).

### 3.3 Derivation: Polydispersity Index (PDI)

**Step 1:** The Polydispersity Index quantifies the **breadth of the molecular weight distribution**.

$$
\text{PDI} = \frac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n}
$$

**Step 2:** For a **monodisperse polymer** (single chain length, e.g., insulin):
- $\bar{M}_w = \bar{M}_n$
- Therefore, $\text{PDI} = 1$

**Step 3:** For a **polydisperse polymer** (e.g., commercial polyethylene):
- $\bar{M}_w > \bar{M}_n$
- Therefore, $\text{PDI} > 1$ (typically **2 to 20** for synthetic polymers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marine Application Insight:** A **narrow PDI (close to 1)** gives **predictable mechanical behavior** — crucial for safety-critical FRP hulls. A wide PDI means inconsistent strength and is avoided in primary load-bearing marine structures.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Solved Numerical Examples

#### **Example 1: Calculating Molecular Weight from DP**

A sample of **Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC)** has a degree of polymerization of 1000. The repeat unit of PVC is –CH₂–CHCl–. Calculate the molecular weight of the polymer.

**Step 1:** Identify the atomic composition of the monomer unit.

- Carbon (C): 2 atoms × 12 = 24
- Hydrogen (H): 3 atoms × 1 = 3
- Chlorine (Cl): 1 atom × 35.5 = 35.5

**Step 2:** Calculate the repeat unit molecular weight $M_0$:

$$
M_0 = (2 \times 12) + (3 \times 1) + (1 \times 35.5) = 24 + 3 + 35.5 = 62.5 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the relation $M = n \cdot M_0$:

$$
M = 1000 \times 62.5 = 62{,}500 \text{ g/mol} = 62.5 \text{ kg/mol}
$$

**Result:** The PVC sample has a molecular weight of **62,500 g/mol**, classifying it firmly in the **high polymer** range.

#### **Example 2: Calculating $\bar{M}_n$ from a Mixture**

A polymer sample consists of:
- 50 chains of $M_1 = 10{,}000$ g/mol
- 30 chains of $M_2 = 50{,}000$ g/mol
- 20 chains of $M_3 = 100{,}000$ g/mol

Calculate the number-average molecular weight.

**Step 1:** Compute the numerator $\sum N_i M_i$:

$$
\sum N_i M_i = (50 \times 10{,}000) + (30 \times 50{,}000) + (20 \times 100{,}000)
$$

$$
= 500{,}000 + 1{,}500{,}000 + 2{,}000{,}000 = 4{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the denominator $\sum N_i$:

$$
\sum N_i = 50 + 30 + 20 = 100
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\bar{M}_n$:

$$
\bar{M}_n = \frac{4{,}000{,}000}{100} = 40{,}000 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**Result:** The number-average molecular weight is **40,000 g/mol**.

#### **Example 3: Calculating PDI**

For the same sample above, calculate $\bar{M}_w$ and PDI.

**Step 1:** Compute the numerator $\sum N_i M_i^2$:

$$
\sum N_i M_i^2 = (50 \times 10{,}000^2) + (30 \times 50{,}000^2) + (20 \times 100{,}000^2)
$$

$$
= (50 \times 10^8) + (30 \times 25 \times 10^8) + (20 \times 100 \times 10^8)
$$

$$
= 50 \times 10^8 + 750 \times 10^8 + 2000 \times 10^8 = 2800 \times 10^8
$$

**Step 2:** Divide by $\sum N_i M_i$ from Example 2:

$$
\bar{M}_w = \frac{2800 \times 10^8}{4 \times 10^6} = 70{,}000 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the PDI:

$$
\text{PDI} = \frac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n} = \frac{70{,}000}{40{,}000} = 1.75
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** PDI of 1.75 indicates a **moderately polydisperse** polymer — typical for commercial-grade synthetic polymers used in non-critical marine applications like cable insulation or buoyancy foams.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Polymers

The following Mermaid diagram presents the **complete hierarchical classification** of polymers as covered in the KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 3. It maps the decision logic used by marine materials engineers to select the right polymer class for each shipboard application.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[POLYMERS]:::root
    A --> B[Based on Origin]
    A --> C[Based on Thermal Response]
    A --> D[Based on Polymerization]
    A --> E[Based on Chain Structure]
    A --> F[Based on Monomer Type]
    A --> G[Based on Crystallinity]
    A --> H[Based on Backbone]

    B --> B1[Natural Polymers]
    B --> B2[Synthetic Polymers]
    B --> B3[Semi-Synthetic Polymers]

    C --> C1[Thermoplastics]
    C --> C2[Thermosetting Plastics]
    C --> C3[Elastomers]

    C1 --> C1a[Examples PE PP PVC PTFE Nylon]
    C2 --> C2a[Examples Epoxy Phenolic Polyester]
    C3 --> C3a[Examples Natural Rubber Neoprene Silicone]

    D --> D1[Addition Chain Growth]
    D --> D2[Condensation Step Growth]

    D1 --> D1a[No Byproduct Needs C=C Bond]
    D2 --> D2a[Eliminates H2O HCl NH3]

    E --> E1[Linear Polymers]
    E --> E2[Branched Polymers]
    E --> E3[Crosslinked Polymers]
    E --> E4[Network Polymers]

    F --> F1[Homopolymers]
    F --> F2[Copolymers]
    F2 --> F2a[Random Alternating Block Graft]

    G --> G1[Amorphous]
    G --> G2[Semi Crystalline]
    G --> G3[Highly Crystalline]

    H --> H1[Organic C Backbone]
    H --> H2[Inorganic Si P Backbone]

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef sub fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Polymer Selection Decision Logic for Marine Applications

The following Mermaid **block-level functional architecture flow** illustrates the decision process a marine engineer follows to classify and select polymers for a specific shipboard requirement (e.g., selecting a material for a propeller-shaft bearing).

```mermaid
graph TD
    R1[Marine Application Requirement]:::input
    Q1{Service Temperature gt 100C}:::decision
    R2[Choose Thermoset Epoxy or Phenolic]:::output
    R3[Go to Next Filter]:::process

    Q2{Need Elastic Recovery gt 100}:::decision
    R4[Choose Elastomer Neoprene or Silicone]:::output
    R5[Go to Next Filter]:::process

    Q3{Need to Remelt and Recycle}:::decision
    R6[Choose Thermoplastic HDPE PP Nylon]:::output
    R7[Go to Final Filter]:::process

    Q4{Load Bearing Structure}:::decision
    R8[Use Composite FRP Epoxy plus Glass Fiber]:::output
    R9[Use Thermoplastic Liner]:::output

    R1 --> Q1
    Q1 -- Yes --> R2
    Q1 -- No --> R3
    R3 --> Q2
    Q2 -- Yes --> R4
    Q2 -- No --> R5
    R5 --> Q3
    Q3 -- Yes --> R6
    Q3 -- No --> R7
    R7 --> Q4
    Q4 -- Yes --> R8
    Q4 -- No --> R9

    classDef input fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Comparative Schematic: Thermoplastic vs Thermoset vs Elastomer

The following Mermaid block diagram compares the **chain-level structural arrangement** of the three thermal-response classes — a common KTU Part A question topic.

```mermaid
graph LR
    TP[Thermoplastic]:::tp
    TS[Thermoset]:::ts
    EL[Elastomer]:::el

    TP --> TP1[Linear or Branched Chains]
    TP1 --> TP2[Weak Van der Waals Bonds]
    TP2 --> TP3[Softens on Heating Reversible]

    TS --> TS1[Dense 3D Network]
    TS1 --> TS2[Strong Covalent Crosslinks]
    TS2 --> TS3[Cannot Remelt Irreversible]

    EL --> EL1[Lightly Crosslinked Chains]
    EL1 --> EL2[Coiled Structure]
    EL2 --> EL3[High Elastic Recovery]

    classDef tp fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000
    classDef ts fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef el fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** Whenever a question describes a polymer that "softens on heating and reshapes on cooling," the answer is unambiguously a **thermoplastic**. When the question states "sets permanently on heating and cannot be remelted," the answer is a **thermoset**. "Stretches and returns to original shape" → **elastomer**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Classification Based on Thermal Response**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]`

**Q:** Classify the following polymers based on their response to heating and give one marine use for each: (i) Polyethylene, (ii) Epoxy resin, (iii) Neoprene rubber.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per correct classification + example):**

- **(i) Polyethylene** → **Thermoplastic.** Softens on heating, hardens on cooling, recyclable. *Marine use:* Pipeline and hull liner for small vessels.
- **(ii) Epoxy resin** → **Thermosetting plastic.** Forms permanent 3D crosslinked network on curing; cannot be remelted. *Marine use:* Matrix material for FRP hulls of minesweepers and patrol boats.
- **(iii) Neoprene rubber** → **Elastomer.** Lightly crosslinked; exhibits high elastic recovery (>200% elongation). *Marine use:* Wet suit fabric, dock fender covers, propeller-shaft seals.

---

#### **Question 2: Distinguish Between Addition and Condensation Polymerization**
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Frequently Asked]`

**Q:** Differentiate between addition and condensation polymerization with two examples each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Addition Polymerization | Condensation Polymerization |
|---------|-------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Byproduct** | No byproduct released | Small molecules (H₂O, HCl, NH₃) eliminated |
| **Monomer requirement** | Must contain unsaturated C=C bond | Must contain ≥2 functional groups (–OH, –COOH, –NH₂) |
| **Mechanism** | Chain-growth (free radical/ionic) | Step-growth |
| **Examples** | Polyethylene (PE), Polyvinyl chloride (PVC), Polystyrene (PS) | Nylon 6,6, Polyester (PET), Phenol-formaldehyde (Bakelite) |
| **Molecular weight buildup** | Rapid; high MW achieved early | Slow; high MW only at very high conversion (>99%) |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split as **7 marks + 7 marks** in sub-parts (a) and (b). Students answer **one full question** (with both sub-parts) out of the internal choice provided.

---

#### **Question A: Comprehensive Classification and Marine Applications**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**Q (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the classification of polymers based on **(i) thermal response**, **(ii) polymerization mechanism**, and **(iii) chain structure**. Give two examples for each sub-class.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Classification Based on Thermal Response:**

- **Thermoplastics:** Linear/branched chains held by weak secondary bonds. **Soften on heating and harden on cooling** — fully reversible. Can be remolded, recycled, and welded.
  - *Examples:* Polyethylene (PE), Polypropylene (PP), Polyvinyl chloride (PVC), Nylon, Polystyrene.
- **Thermosetting Plastics:** Chains form a **permanent 3D crosslinked network** via covalent bonds during curing. Once set, they **decompose on heating rather than melt**.
  - *Examples:* Epoxy resin, Phenol-formaldehyde (Bakelite), Melamine, Polyester resin, Urea-formaldehyde.
- **Elastomers:** Lightly crosslinked polymers with **coiled chain structure** and **high elastic recovery** (200–1000% elongation).
  - *Examples:* Natural rubber, Neoprene (polychloroprene), Silicone rubber, Polyurethane.

**(ii) Classification Based on Polymerization Mechanism:**

- **Addition (Chain-Growth) Polymerization:** Monomers with C=C double bonds add sequentially without loss of byproduct. Initiated by free radicals, cations, or anions.
  - *Examples:* Polyethylene from ethylene (C₂H₄), Polystyrene from styrene (C₆H₅CH=CH₂).
- **Condensation (Step-Growth) Polymerization:** Monomers with two or more functional groups react stepwise, **eliminating small molecules** such as water, HCl, or ammonia.
  - *Examples:* Nylon 6,6 from hexamethylenediamine + adipic acid (releases H₂O); PET from ethylene glycol + terephthalic acid (releases H₂O).

**(iii) Classification Based on Chain Structure:**

- **Linear Polymers:** Straight, unbranched chains, closely packed, high crystallinity, high tensile strength.
  - *Examples:* HDPE, Nylon 6,6, PTFE.
- **Branched Polymers:** Main chain with short or long side branches, lower density, lower crystallinity.
  - *Examples:* LDPE, LLDPE.
- **Crosslinked Polymers:** Chains connected by covalent bridges at intervals. Rigid, infusible.
  - *Examples:* Vulcanized rubber, Cured epoxy.
- **Network Polymers:** Highly crosslinked 3D network, very rigid and brittle.
  - *Examples:* Phenol-formaldehyde, Melamine-formaldehyde, Epoxy.

**Q (b) [7 marks]:** Identify the polymer class suitable for the following marine applications and justify your choice:
1. Hull of a 25-meter patrol boat
2. Buoyancy foam for life jackets
3. Cable insulation in the engine room

**Model Answer:**

1. **Hull of a 25-meter patrol boat → Glass-fiber reinforced Epoxy (Thermoset + Fiber).** *Justification:* Epoxy provides a strong, rigid, crosslinked matrix that resists hull flexure, while glass fibers provide tensile strength. This composite has a high strength-to-weight ratio, corrosion resistance, and zero magnetic signature (important for naval patrol craft).

2. **Buoyancy foam for life jackets → Closed-cell Polyurethane (PU) foam (Thermoset).** *Justification:* PU foam has a very low density (~30–50 kg/m³) and contains trapped gas bubbles providing buoyancy. The closed-cell structure prevents water absorption, and the crosslinked thermoset structure is durable and resistant to marine fouling.

3. **Cable insulation in the engine room → Crosslinked Polyethylene (XLPE) or PVC.** *Justification:* Engine rooms reach 80–100°C. XLPE is a thermoset-grade polymer with high thermal stability (up to 125°C continuous), excellent dielectric strength, and resistance to oils and seawater — making it ideal for marine-grade cable sheathing.

---

#### **Question B: Alternative Choice Question**
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Modified]`

**Q (a) [7 marks]:** Define the following with one example each:
1. Homopolymer
2. Copolymer
3. Elastomer
4. Thermoplastic
5. Thermoset
6. Addition polymer
7. Condensation polymer

**Model Answer:**

1. **Homopolymer:** A polymer formed from **only one type of monomer** unit. *Example:* Polyethylene (–CH₂–CH₂–)ₙ from ethylene monomer only.
2. **Copolymer:** A polymer formed from **two or more different monomer** units. *Example:* SBR (Styrene-Butadiene Rubber) used in marine tire fenders.
3. **Elastomer:** A lightly crosslinked polymer that can be **stretched to several times its length and rapidly returns to its original shape** on release of stress. *Example:* Neoprene (polychloroprene) used in wet suits.
4. **Thermoplastic:** A polymer that **softens on heating and hardens on cooling**, a reversible physical change. *Example:* Polypropylene used in marine piping.
5. **Thermoset:** A polymer that, once cured, forms a **permanent crosslinked network** and **cannot be remelted or reshaped**. *Example:* Phenol-formaldehyde (Bakelite) used in marine electrical switchboards.
6. **Addition polymer:** Formed by **addition of monomer units containing C=C double bonds with no elimination of byproducts**. *Example:* PVC from vinyl chloride.
7. **Condensation polymer:** Formed by **stepwise reactions between bifunctional monomers with elimination of small molecules** (H₂O, HCl, NH₃). *Example:* Nylon 6,6 eliminating water.

**Q (b) [7 marks]:** A polymer sample has the following molecular weight distribution:

| Fraction | Number of chains $N_i$ | Molecular weight $M_i$ (g/mol) |
|----------|------------------------|----------------------------------|
| 1 | 100 | 10,000 |
| 2 | 50 | 50,000 |
| 3 | 20 | 100,000 |

Calculate **(i)** the number-average molecular weight $\bar{M}_n$, **(ii)** the weight-average molecular weight $\bar{M}_w$, and **(iii)** the Polydispersity Index (PDI). Comment on the nature of the polymer distribution.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Calculation of $\bar{M}_n$:**

$$
\sum N_i M_i = (100 \times 10{,}000) + (50 \times 50{,}000) + (20 \times 100{,}000)
$$

$$
= 1{,}000{,}000 + 2{,}500{,}000 + 2{,}000{,}000 = 5{,}500{,}000
$$

$$
\sum N_i = 100 + 50 + 20 = 170
$$

$$
\bar{M}_n = \frac{5{,}500{,}000}{170} = 32{,}352.94 \text{ g/mol} \approx 32{,}353 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**(ii) Calculation of $\bar{M}_w$:**

$$
\sum N_i M_i^2 = (100 \times 10{,}000^2) + (50 \times 50{,}000^2) + (20 \times 100{,}000^2)
$$

$$
= (100 \times 10^8) + (50 \times 25 \times 10^8) + (20 \times 100 \times 10^8)
$$

$$
= 100 \times 10^8 + 1250 \times 10^8 + 2000 \times 10^8 = 3350 \times 10^8
$$

$$
\bar{M}_w = \frac{3350 \times 10^8}{5.5 \times 10^6} = \frac{3.35 \times 10^{11}}{5.5 \times 10^6} = 60{,}909.09 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**(iii) Polydispersity Index (PDI):**

$$
\text{PDI} = \frac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n} = \frac{60{,}909.09}{32{,}352.94} = 1.883
$$

**Comment:** Since PDI = 1.883 > 1, the polymer is **polydisperse** (broad molecular weight distribution). This is typical of **commercial-grade synthetic polymers** produced via free-radical polymerization. For marine applications requiring **consistent mechanical strength** (e.g., FRP hulls), manufacturers prefer polymers with **narrow PDI** to ensure uniform performance.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the Exam Hall:**
>
> 1. **Confusing thermoplastic and thermoset:** Many students write "thermoplastic can be melted and remolded" but forget to mention that **thermosetting plastics decompose rather than melt** on heating. This single missing phrase can cost 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting byproducts in condensation polymerization:** Always explicitly state that **small molecules (H₂O, HCl, NH₃) are eliminated**. Examiners look for this keyword.
> 3. **Mixing up addition vs condensation examples:** Polyethylene is **addition**; Nylon is **condensation**. PVC is **addition**; PET is **condensation**. This is the most-tested factual recall.
> 4. **Wrong formula for $\bar{M}_w$:** Students often write $\bar{M}_w = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ (which is $\bar{M}_n$). The correct formula has $M_i^2$ in the numerator. **Memorize the squared term.**
> 5. **Not justifying polymer selection:** In application-based questions, **naming the polymer alone is insufficient** (3 marks). You must also **justify** with at least 2 properties (e.g., "epoxy is selected because it is a thermoset with high crosslink density and corrosion resistance").
> 6. **Marine context missing:** The course is *Non-Ferrous Materials in Shipbuilding*. Always relate polymer answers to **marine applications** (hulls, coatings, buoyancy, insulation) wherever the question allows.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Classification of Polymers**

- ✅ A **polymer** is a large molecule made of repeating **monomer** units joined by covalent bonds. Symbol: $[M]_n$, where $n$ = degree of polymerization.
- ✅ The **master relation** is $M = n \cdot M_0$ (polymer molecular weight = DP × monomer molecular weight).
- ✅ **Three thermal response classes:** Thermoplastic (reversible, recyclable), Thermoset (irreversible, crosslinked), Elastomer (elastic recovery).
- ✅ **Two polymerization mechanisms:** Addition (chain-growth, no byproduct, needs C=C) vs Condensation (step-growth, eliminates H₂O/HCl/NH₃).
- ✅ **Four chain structures:** Linear, Branched, Crosslinked, Network.
- ✅ **Monomer composition:** Homopolymer (one monomer) vs Copolymer (≥2 monomers — random, alternating, block, graft).
- ✅ **Crystallinity classes:** Amorphous (transparent, e.g., PMMA), Semi-crystalline (e.g., HDPE, Nylon), Crystalline (rare).
- ✅ **Number-average molecular weight:** $\bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$.
- ✅ **Weight-average molecular weight:** $\bar{M}_w = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$.
- ✅ **Polydispersity Index:** $\text{PDI} = \bar{M}_w / \bar{M}_n \geq 1$. PDI = 1 means monodisperse (e.g., proteins); PDI > 1 means polydisperse (typical synthetic polymers).
- ✅ **Marine use quick-map:** FRP hulls → Epoxy/Polyester (thermosets) + glass fibers; Piping → HDPE/PVC (thermoplastics); Engine seals → Silicone/Neoprene (elastomers); Buoyancy → PU foam (thermoset foam); Coatings → Epoxy/Epoxy-amide systems.
- ✅ **Glass transition ($T_g$) and melting ($T_m$):** Operating temperature of a marine polymer must lie **between $T_g$ (lower bound for use) and $T_m$ or decomposition point (upper bound)**.
- ✅ **Inorganic polymers** (silicones) have a **Si–O backbone** and offer exceptional thermal stability for high-temperature marine engine seals.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Polymerization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Types of Polymerization # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Polymerization? (Foundational Concept) **Polymerization** is the chemical process by which small, low-molecular-weight monomer units (typically containing reactive functional groups or unsaturated bonds) are covalently linked together to form long-chain, high-molecular-weight macromolecules known as **polymers**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** > Polymerization is a chemical reaction in which monomer molecules combine sequentially through covalent bond formation to yield a polymer whose structural, mechanical, and chemical properties are a function of the monomer type, molecular weight, and the specific reaction mechanism (chain-growth or step-growth). Mathematically, polymerization can be represented as: $$ n \cdot M \longrightarrow (M)_n $$ where: - $M$ = monomer (e.g., ethylene, styrene, vinyl chloride) - $n$ = degree of polymerization (number of repeating units) - $(M)_n$ = polymer chain - Molecular weight: $M_w = n \times M_0$ where $M_0$ is the monomer molecular weight In the **shipbuilding context**, polymers dominate as: - Hull coatings and antifouling paints - Glass-Reinforced Plastic (GRP) hulls for small craft and superstructures - Marine-grade epoxy and polyester resins - Polyurethane deck and insulation foams - Elastomeric seals, gaskets, and rubber bearings ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Beads on a String" Model > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Analogy (Intuition):** > Imagine a box of **identical LEGO bricks** (monomers). Polymerization is the process of **snapping these bricks together** in long, repeating sequences to build a giant, unbreakable chain (the polymer). > - If you use **only red bricks** → homopolymer (e.g., polyethylene). > - If you **alternate red and blue bricks** → copolymer (e.g., SBR rubber used in marine fenders). > - Some bricks snap together **only when heated or with glue** (step-growth) — like building a brick wall, one brick at a time. > - Other bricks have a **self-propagating "zipped-up" fastener** (chain-growth) — like unzipping a jacket from the bottom and re-zipping the chain link-by-link, growing fast and long. ## 1.3 The Two Master Categories of Polymerization Polymerization is broadly classified into **two fundamental types**: | # | Type | Other Names | Initiation Trigger | Marine Use Case | |---|------|-------------|--------------------|----------------| | 1 | **Chain-Growth Polymerization** | Addition Polymerization | Initiator (radical, ion) | PVC pipes, HDPE fenders | | 2 | **Step-Growth Polymerization** | Condensation Polymerization | Heat / Catalyst | Nylon ropes, epoxy hulls | A third advanced category — **Coordination Polymerization** (Ziegler-Natta) — is also covered, which is critical for producing marine-grade HDPE and UHMWPE. # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Chain-Growth (Addition) Polymerization — Mechanism Deep Dive Chain-growth polymerization proceeds via a **reactive center** (radical, cation, or anion) that propagates by adding one monomer unit at a time. It is **exclusively applicable to monomers containing C=C double bonds** (vinyl monomers). ### 2.1.1 The Four Mandatory Steps > [!IMPORTANT] > **All chain-growth polymerizations — whether free radical, cationic, or anionic — must proceed through these four sequential kinetic steps.** **Step 1: Initiation** The initiator $I$ decomposes (thermally, photochemically, or via redox reaction) to form an active species $R^*$ (a free radical, cation, or anion). $$ I \xrightarrow{\text{heat/hv}} 2R^{\bullet} $$ $$ R^{\bullet} + M \longrightarrow R-M^{\bullet} $$ where $M$ is the monomer (e.g., $CH_2=CHCl$ for vinyl chloride). **Step 2: Propagation** The active chain end attacks the next monomer's double bond, transferring the active center. This repeats thousands of times rapidly. $$ R-M^{\bullet} + M \longrightarrow R-M-M^{\bullet} $$ $$ R-(M)_n^{\bullet} + M \longrightarrow R-(M)_{n+1}^{\bullet} $$ **Step 3: Termination** Two active chain ends combine, transfer, or disproportionate, **killing the chain's reactivity**. (a) Combination (head-to-head): $$ R-(M)_n^{\bullet} + {}^{\bullet}(M)_m-R \longrightarrow R-(M)_n-(M)_m-R $$ (b) Disproportionation: $$ R-(M)_n^{\bullet} + {}^{\bullet}(M)_m-R \longrightarrow R-(M)_n-H + CH_2=CH-(M)_{m-1}-R $$ **Step 4: Chain Transfer** A growing chain transfers its active center to another molecule (solvent, monomer, dead polymer), terminating the original chain but starting a new one. This controls molecular weight. $$ R-(M)_n^{\bullet} + XH \longrightarrow R-(M)_n-H + X^{\bullet} $$ ### 2.1.2 The Three Sub-Types of Chain-Growth Polymerization | Sub-Type | Active Center | Initiator | Monomer Suitability | Example Polymer | Marine Relevance | |----------|---------------|-----------|---------------------|------------------|------------------| | **Free Radical** | $R^{\bullet}$ (unpaired electron) | Benzoyl peroxide, AIBN, redox | Electron-rich & electron-poor vinyls | PVC, PS, PMMA, SBR | Ship superstructure panels, windows | | **Cationic** | $R^{+}$ (carbocation) | $BF_3$, $AlCl_3$, $H_2SO_4$ | Electron-donating monomers (isobutylene) | Polyisobutylene, butyl rubber | Underwater cable insulation | | **Anionic** | $R^{-}$ (carbanion) | $n$-BuLi, Na/Naphthalene | Electron-withdrawing monomers (styrene, butadiene) | SBS rubber, Living polymers | Marine vibration dampers | ## 2.2 Step-Growth (Condensation) Polymerization — Mechanism Deep Dive In step-growth polymerization, **any two molecular species (monomer, oligomer, or polymer) can react at any time**, regardless of chain length. It is a **slow, statistical process** that depends on the reaction between **functional groups** (e.g., $-COOH$ + $-OH \rightarrow -COO-$ + $H_2O$). ### 2.2.1 Mechanism A bifunctional monomer $A-B$ (e.g., amino acid $H_2N-R-COOH$) reacts with another: $$ A-B + A-B \longrightarrow A-BA + \text{byproduct} $$ The resulting dimer can react with another monomer, dimer, or higher oligomer: $$ A-B + A-BA \longrightarrow A-BABA + \text{byproduct} $$ A trimer can react with a monomer, and so on. The molecular weight **rises slowly** and only achieves high $M_w$ at very high conversion (>99%). ### 2.2.2 The Two Sub-Types of Step-Growth > [!NOTE] > **Polycondensation** → byproduct is eliminated (water, HCl, methanol). Example: Nylon 6,6 from adipic acid + hexamethylenediamine. > **Polyaddition** → no byproduct; functional groups simply rearrange. Example: Polyurethane from diisocyanate + diol. ## 2.3 Coordination Polymerization (Ziegler-Natta) — Critical for Marine Polymers Developed by **Karl Ziegler and Giulio Natta (Nobel Prize, 1963)**, this uses a **transition metal catalyst** ($TiCl_4$ + $Al(C_2H_5)_3$) that coordinates to the monomer at the metal center, controlling stereoregularity. - Produces **isotactic polypropylene (iPP)** — used in marine ropes & geotextiles. - Produces **HDPE / UHMWPE** — used in ship fenders, dock bumpers, and bearing liners. - **Stereospecificity** = all substituents on same side → crystalline → high strength. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Application | |---|---------------------|------------|-------------| | 1 | Degree of Polymerization | $DP = \dfrac{\bar{M}_n}{M_0}$ | Molecular weight from viscosity | | 2 | Number-Average MW | $\bar{M}_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | End-group analysis | | 3 | Weight-Average MW | $\bar{M}_w = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Light scattering | | 4 | Polydispersity Index | $PDI = \dfrac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n}$ | PDI=1 → monodisperse | | 5 | Carothers Equation (Step-Growth) | $DP_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | $p$ = extent of reaction | | 6 | Carothers w/ stoichiometric imbalance | $DP_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ | $r = N_A / N_B \leq 1$ | | 7 | Rate of Radical Polymerization | $R_p = k_p \left(\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}\right)^{0.5} [M]$ | Reactor design | | 8 | Kinetic Chain Length | $\nu = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i} = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2(f k_d k_t [I])^{0.5}}$ | DP control | | 9 | Half-life of Initiator | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{k_d}$ | Initiator dosing | | 10 | Fox-Flory Equation | $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \dfrac{K}{\bar{M}_n}$ | Glass transition vs. $M_n$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering:** > - **Carothers' equation** is used to design marine epoxy resins: $r=1$ ensures maximum crosslink density → superior water resistance. > - **Fox-Flory equation** predicts $T_g$ of marine coatings — critical for sub-zero Arctic service vs. tropical hull service. > - **Kinetic chain length** controls molecular weight of UHMWPE dock fenders for impact absorption. # 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Carothers' Equation for Step-Growth Polymerization The Carothers equation is the **single most-tested step-growth formula in KTU exams**. Below is the complete, line-by-line derivation. ### 3.1.1 Setup and Definitions Let us consider a system with $N_0$ initial functional groups (e.g., total $-COOH$ groups at $t=0$). As polymerization proceeds, functional groups are consumed. Define: - $N$ = number of unreacted functional groups at time $t$ - $p$ = extent of reaction (fraction of functional groups that have reacted) - $N_0 - N$ = number of functional groups that have reacted - Each bond formation consumes **2 functional groups** ### 3.1.2 Derivation of $DP_n = 1/(1-p)$ **Step 1:** By definition of $p$: $$ p = \dfrac{N_0 - N}{N_0} $$ **Step 2:** Rearranging to solve for $N$: $$ N = N_0(1 - p) $$ **Step 3:** Total number of molecules in the system at time $t$ = number of unreacted functional groups / 2 (since each molecule has at least one functional group pair, and the average is 2 for a linear system): $$ \text{Number of molecules} = \dfrac{N}{2} = \dfrac{N_0(1-p)}{2} $$ **Step 4:** Initially, at $t=0$ (before reaction), the number of molecules is $N_0/2$. **Step 5:** The number-average degree of polymerization $DP_n$ is defined as the ratio of initial monomer units to current number of molecules: $$ DP_n = \dfrac{N_0/2}{N/2} = \dfrac{N_0}{N} $$ **Step 6:** Substituting $N = N_0(1-p)$: $$ DP_n = \dfrac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} = \dfrac{1}{1-p} $$ $$\boxed{DP_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}}$$ This is the **Carothers equation** for stoichiometrically balanced systems. ### 3.1.3 Modification for Non-Equimolar (Imbalanced) Reactants If reactants are not in exact stoichiometric proportions, let $r$ be the ratio of the two functional groups: $$ r = \dfrac{N_B}{N_A} \quad \text{where} \quad N_A \geq N_B,\ 0 < r \leq 1 $$ Total initial functional groups: $N_0 = N_A + N_B = N_A(1 + r)$ Number of unreacted $A$ groups at extent $p$: $N_A(1-p)$ Number of unreacted $B$ groups at extent $p$: $N_B - rpN_A = N_A(r - rp) = N_Ar(1-p)$ Total unreacted functional groups: $N = N_A(1-p) + N_Ar(1-p) = N_A(1+r)(1-p)$ Total molecules: $N/2 = N_A(1+r)(1-p)/2$ Initial molecules: $N_0/2 = N_A(1+r)/2$ $$ DP_n = \dfrac{N_A(1+r)/2}{N_A(1+r)(1-p)/2} = \dfrac{1}{1-p} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1} $$ But $p$ is defined based on the **limiting** reagent $B$ that reacts completely only when all $B$ groups are consumed. The maximum possible $p$ is $r$ (when all $B$ groups have reacted). For a balanced system, $p$ can reach 1. The correct Carothers equation for imbalanced systems is: $$\boxed{DP_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}}$$ ### 3.1.4 Numerical Worked Example (Marine Epoxy) > A shipyard is formulating an epoxy resin. They mix epoxy resin ($-CH-CH_2$ groups) with amine hardener ($-NH_2$ groups) in the ratio $r = 0.95$. If the reaction reaches $p = 0.95$, find $DP_n$. **Solution:** $$ DP_n = \dfrac{1+0.95}{1+0.95-2(0.95)(0.95)} $$ $$ DP_n = \dfrac{1.95}{1.95 - 1.805} = \dfrac{1.95}{0.145} $$ $$ DP_n \approx 13.45 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Implication:** With $r=0.95$, the maximum achievable $DP_n$ is severely limited. To get high crosslink density (essential for marine hull coatings), $r$ must be **very close to 1.0**. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Rate of Radical Polymerization ### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Rate of Initiation The rate at which initiator decomposes to radicals is: $$ R_d = -\dfrac{d[I]}{dt} = 2f k_d [I] $$ where $f$ = initiator efficiency, $k_d$ = decomposition rate constant. Each radical that successfully initiates a chain consumes one monomer, so the rate of initiation (active chain starts per second) is: $$ R_i = 2f k_d [I] $$ ### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Rate of Termination For two radicals combining or disproportionating: $$ R_t = 2k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2 $$ where $[M^{\bullet}]$ is the radical concentration. ### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Steady-State Assumption We assume radical concentration quickly reaches a steady state ($d[M^{\bullet}]/dt = 0$): $$ R_i = R_t $$ $$ 2f k_d [I] = 2k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2 $$ Solving for $[M^{\bullet}]$: $$ [M^{\bullet}] = \left( \dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t} \right)^{1/2} $$ ### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Rate of Polymerization The rate of monomer consumption (and thus polymer formation) is: $$ R_p = -\dfrac{d[M]}{dt} = k_p [M^{\bullet}][M] $$ Substituting $[M^{\bullet}]$ from above: $$ R_p = k_p \left( \dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t} \right)^{1/2} [M] $$ $$\boxed{R_p = k_p [M] \left( \dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t} \right)^{1/2}}$$ This is the **standard radical polymerization rate equation**, highly tested in KTU problems. ## 3.3 Comparison Matrix: Chain-Growth vs. Step-Growth | Parameter | Chain-Growth | Step-Growth | |-----------|--------------|-------------| | Monomer type | C=C unsaturated | Bifunctional (A-B or A-A + B-B) | | Active species | Radical / ion (at chain end) | Any two functional groups | | Growth pattern | One unit at a time, fast | Any two species, slow | | Byproduct | None | Often yes (water, HCl) | | Chain lifetime | Short (seconds) | Long (hours) | | Molecular weight vs. time | M.W. rises early & plateaus | M.W. rises only at high conversion | | Termination step | Mandatory | Not required (just stoichiometry) | | Inhibitor effect | Strong (e.g., TEMPO) | Weak | | Common initiator | Peroxides, AIBN | Heat, acid/base catalyst | | Example | Polyethylene, PVC, PMMA | Nylon 6,6, PET, Phenolic | | Marine use | Coatings, ropes, fenders | Epoxy hulls, PU insulation | # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Polymerization ```mermaid flowchart TD A[POLYMERIZATION] --> B[CHAIN GROWTH
Addition] A --> C[STEP GROWTH
Condensation] A --> D[COORDINATION
Ziegler-Natta] B --> B1[Free Radical
Initiator: Peroxide] B --> B2[Cationic
Initiator: BF3, AlCl3] B --> B3[Anionic
Initiator: n-BuLi] C --> C1[Polycondensation
Byproduct: H2O, HCl] C --> C2[Polyaddition
No byproduct: PU] D --> D1[Isotactic PP
Marine ropes] D --> D2[HDPE / UHMWPE
Dock fenders] D --> D3[Syndiotactic PS
Specialty coatings] B1 --> E1[PVC, PS, PMMA, SBR] B2 --> E2[Polyisobutylene, Butyl rubber] B3 --> E3[SBS, Living anionic polymers] C1 --> F1[Nylon 6,6, PET, Phenolic, Epoxy] C2 --> F2[Polyurethane foams] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Free Radical Polymerization Mechanism (Detailed) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[STEP 1: INITIATION] --> A1[Initiator I
decomposes] A1 --> A2[2R radicals] A2 --> A3[R plus M
yields RM radical] A3 --> B[STEP 2: PROPAGATION] B --> B1[RM plus M
yields RMM] B1 --> B2[repeat thousands of times] B2 --> B3[R Mn plus M
yields R Mn plus 1] B3 --> C[STEP 3: TERMINATION] C --> C1[Combination
R Mn plus R Mm
yields R polymer R] C --> C2[Disproportionation
yields dead polymer
plus unsaturated end] B3 --> D[STEP 4: CHAIN TRANSFER] D --> D1[To monomer: controls MW] D --> D2[To solvent: reduces DP] D --> D3[To polymer: branching] ``` ## 4.3 Architecture Flow: Polymerization Reactor Decision Tree (Marine Production) ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Select Polymer for Marine Application] --> Q1{Required molecular weight?} Q1 -->|Low to Medium MW| Q2{Service temperature?} Q1 -->|Very High MW UHMWPE| R1[ZIEGLER NATTA
Coordination catalysis
Slurry or Gas phase reactor] Q2 -->|Below 80 deg C| R2[STEP GROWTH
Batch reactor
CSTR with vacuum
for byproduct removal] Q2 -->|Above 80 deg C| Q3{Geometry?} Q3 -->|Linear chains| R3[FREE RADICAL
Bulk or Suspension
Initiator: BPO or AIBN] Q3 -->|Branched chains| R4[FREE RADICAL
High pressure autoclave
LDPE process] Q3 -->|Stereoregular| R5[ZIEGLER NATTA
Solution polymerization
Stereospecific catalyst] R2 --> APP1[Example: Marine Epoxy Resin] R3 --> APP2[Example: PMMA Porthole Windows] R4 --> APP3[Example: LDPE Cable Sheathing] R5 --> APP4[Example: iPP Marine Rope] R1 --> APP5[Example: UHMWPE Dock Fender] ``` ## 4.4 Schematic: Monomer → Polymer Conversion (Visual Concept) ```mermaid flowchart LR M1[Monomer
CH2=CHCl
Vinyl Chloride] -->|Initiation| I1[Active site R] I1 -->|Propagation| P1[Oligomer n=5] P1 -->|Propagation| P2[Polymer n=10000] P2 -->|Termination| F1[PVC Chain
MW 625000 g/mol] P2 -.Chain Transfer.-> CT1[Shorter PVC Chain
MW 50000 g/mol] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note for Students:** These flowcharts represent the **functional architecture** of polymerization processes. In your KTU exam, drawing simplified flowcharts (Initiation → Propagation → Termination) earns full credit for 14-mark mechanism questions. # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** ### Q1. Differentiate between addition and condensation polymerization. Give one marine engineering example for each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Addition (Chain-Growth) | Condensation (Step-Growth) | |---------|-------------------------|----------------------------| | Mechanism | Active center propagation | Functional group reaction | | Monomer | Vinyl, C=C unsaturation | Bifunctional A-B or A-A+B-B | | Byproduct | None | Yes (H₂O, HCl, CH₃OH) | | Initiation | Initiator (radical/ion) | Heat, catalyst | | Rate | Fast | Slow | | Marine example | PVC piping on board ships | Nylon 6,6 mooring ropes; Epoxy hull coatings | *(Each correct difference = 1 mark, with example = 1 mark. Full marks require table format.)* --- > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Exam]** ### Q2. What is the role of an initiator in free radical polymerization? Name two common initiators used in marine polymer production. **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **initiator** is a thermally or photochemically labile compound that decomposes to generate free radicals, which then attack the C=C double bond of the monomer, starting the chain. Initiators control: - Rate of polymerization ($R_p \propto [I]^{0.5}$) - Number of polymer chains formed - Molecular weight (more initiator → lower MW) **Two common initiators used in marine polymer production:** 1. **Benzoyl Peroxide (BPO)** — used in bulk polymerization of polyester resins for FRP boat hulls. 2. **Azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN)** — used in suspension polymerization of PMMA for ship porthole windows. *(Definition = 1 mark, role in R_p = 1 mark, two initiators = 1 mark.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Exam, Module 3 Choice]** --- ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1 **(a)** Describe the **mechanism of free radical polymerization** in detail, covering initiation, propagation, and termination steps with suitable chemical equations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **rate expression for radical polymerization** using the steady-state approximation, and discuss how the rate depends on monomer and initiator concentrations. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Initiation (2 marks):** The initiator (e.g., benzoyl peroxide, BPO) decomposes homolytically on heating: $$ (C_6H_5COO)_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta, k_d} 2\, C_6H_5COO^{\bullet} $$ The primary radical $R^{\bullet}$ attacks the vinyl monomer, e.g., styrene ($CH_2=CHC_6H_5$): $$ C_6H_5COO^{\bullet} + CH_2=CHC_6H_5 \longrightarrow C_6H_5COO-CH_2-{}^{\bullet}CH(C_6H_5) $$ The new radical is the active chain end. **Propagation (2 marks):** The chain radical successively adds monomer units: $$ R-CH_2-{}^{\bullet}CH(C_6H_5) + CH_2=CHC_6H_5 \longrightarrow R-(CH_2-CHC_6H_5)_2^{\bullet} $$ Repeated $n-1$ times: $$ R-M_n^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_p} R-M_{n+1}^{\bullet} $$ **Termination (2 marks):** (i) **Combination (coupling):** $$ R-M_n^{\bullet} + {}^{\bullet}M_m-R' \longrightarrow R-M_{n+m}-R' $$ (ii) **Disproportionation:** $$ R-M_n^{\bullet} + {}^{\bullet}M_m-R' \longrightarrow R-M_n + CH_2=CH-M_{m-1}-R' $$ **Chain transfer (1 mark — extra credit):** A growing radical abstracts an atom from solvent/monomer/polymer, ending the original chain but starting a new one. This controls molecular weight distribution. --- #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Setting up the rate equations (3 marks):** Rate of initiation: $$ R_i = 2f k_d [I] $$ Rate of propagation: $$ R_p = k_p [M^{\bullet}][M] $$ Rate of termination (combination + disproportionation): $$ R_t = 2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2 $$ **Applying steady-state approximation (2 marks):** Assuming radical concentration reaches steady state very quickly: $$ \dfrac{d[M^{\bullet}]}{dt} = R_i - R_t = 0 $$ $$ 2f k_d [I] = 2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2 $$ Solving: $$ [M^{\bullet}] = \left( \dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t} \right)^{1/2} $$ **Final rate expression (1 mark):** $$ \boxed{R_p = k_p \left( \dfrac{f k_d}{k_t} \right)^{1/2} [I]^{1/2} [M] } $$ **Discussion of concentration dependence (1 mark):** - $R_p \propto [M]$ — first order in monomer. - $R_p \propto [I]^{0.5}$ — half order in initiator. - Doubling $[I]$ increases $R_p$ by only $\sqrt{2}$ but **reduces kinetic chain length**, giving lower molecular weight — critical for marine applications where impact strength depends on MW. --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 **(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch, explain the step-growth (condensation) polymerization** mechanism. Derive the Carothers equation relating the degree of polymerization $DP_n$ to the extent of reaction $p$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A shipyard is producing **nylon 6,6** for marine ropes by reacting adipic acid ($HOOC-(CH_2)_4-COOH$) with hexamethylenediamine ($H_2N-(CH_2)_6-NH_2$). Calculate the **degree of polymerization** if the reaction is carried out with $r = 0.98$ and the extent of reaction $p = 0.97$. Comment on the role of stoichiometry. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Step-Growth Mechanism (3 marks):** Consider two bifunctional monomers, $A-A$ (e.g., adipic acid, $HOOC-R-COOH$) and $B-B$ (e.g., hexamethylenediamine, $H_2N-R'-NH_2$). Each functional group can react with any complementary group. - **Reaction:** $$ HOOC-R-COOH + H_2N-R'-NH_2 \longrightarrow HOOC-R-CO-NH-R'-NH_2 + H_2O $$ - **Monomer + Monomer → Dimer** (1 mark) - **Monomer + Dimer → Trimer** (1 mark) - **Dimer + Dimer → Tetramer** — any two species can react at any time (1 mark) **Carothers Equation Derivation (3 marks):** Let $N_0$ = total initial functional groups. At extent of reaction $p$: $$ p = \dfrac{N_0 - N}{N_0} \implies N = N_0(1-p) $$ Number of molecules at time $t$: $N/2$ Initial number of molecules: $N_0/2$ $$ DP_n = \dfrac{N_0/2}{N/2} = \dfrac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} = \dfrac{1}{1-p} $$ **For imbalanced stoichiometry (1 mark):** $$ DP_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp} $$ **Sketch (essential):** Show monomers with $-COOH$ and $-NH_2$ groups, formation of amide bond $-CO-NH-$, elimination of $H_2O$, growing chain with repeat unit. --- #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $r = 0.98$, $p = 0.97$ **Carothers equation for imbalanced system (3 marks):** $$ DP_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp} $$ Substitute values: $$ DP_n = \dfrac{1+0.98}{1+0.98 - 2(0.98)(0.97)} $$ **Compute numerator (1 mark):** $$ 1+0.98 = 1.98 $$ **Compute denominator (2 marks):** $$ 2(0.98)(0.97) = 1.9012 $$ $$ 1+0.98-1.9012 = 1.98-1.9012 = 0.0788 $$ **Final value (1 mark):** $$ DP_n = \dfrac{1.98}{0.0788} \approx 25.1 $$ **Comment on stoichiometry:** - With $r = 0.98$ (a 2% imbalance), the maximum achievable $DP_n$ is severely limited. - For high-molecular-weight nylon 6,6 ($DP_n > 200$), $r$ must be **> 0.99** and $p > 0.995$. - Marine ropes require high $M_w$ for tensile strength and abrasion resistance; hence **strict stoichiometric control** is critical in production. - The Carothers equation shows that small deviations from $r=1$ cause large drops in $DP_n$, which is why industrial nylon 6,6 plants use **nylon salt** (1:1 crystalline adduct) to enforce exact stoichiometry. --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Forgetting the factor 2 in $R_t$:** Many students write $R_t = k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$ instead of $2k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$ because two radicals are consumed. **Cost: -1 mark.** > > 2. **Confusing $r$ and $p$:** $r$ is the **stoichiometric ratio** (mole ratio of functional groups), while $p$ is the **extent of reaction** (fraction of groups that have reacted). They are NOT the same. **Cost: -1 to -2 marks.** > > 3. **Writing $DP_n = 1/(1-p)$ for imbalanced systems:** This formula is only valid when $r=1$. For $r<1$, the correct form is $(1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$. **Cost: -2 marks.** > > 4. **Skipping the sketch in step-growth questions:** Even a rough hand-drawn diagram showing monomer + monomer → dimer + $H_2O$ earns **at least 1 mark** in KTU valuation. > > 5. **Forgetting the marine context:** Always mention at least one marine application (rope, fender, coating, hull). It signals application-level understanding and earns the "application" component of COs. > > 6. **Mixing up Ziegler-Natta with free radical:** Ziegler-Natta uses **coordination catalysts** (TiCl₄ + AlR₃), not peroxides. **Cost: -1 mark.** --- ## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Types of Polymerization** ### ✅ Key Definitions (Memorize) - **Polymerization** = Monomer → Polymer via covalent bond formation - **Chain-growth (Addition)** = Active center propagation; monomers are vinyl - **Step-growth (Condensation)** = Functional group coupling; often releases byproduct - **Coordination (Ziegler-Natta)** = Transition metal catalyst; stereospecific ### ✅ The 4 Steps of Chain-Growth 1. **Initiation** — $I \to 2R^{\bullet}$, $R^{\bullet}+M \to RM^{\bullet}$ 2. **Propagation** — $RM_n^{\bullet}+M \to RM_{n+1}^{\bullet}$ (rate constant $k_p$) 3. **Termination** — Combination or Disproportionation (rate constant $k_t$) 4. **Chain Transfer** — Limits MW ### ✅ The 3 Sub-Types of Chain-Growth - **Free Radical** (BPO, AIBN) → PVC, PS, PMMA, LDPE - **Cationic** ($BF_3$, $AlCl_3$) → Polyisobutylene, Butyl rubber - **Anionic** ($n$-BuLi) → SBS, Living polymers ### ✅ Must-Know Formulas - $DP_n = 1/(1-p)$ — balanced step-growth - $DP_n = (1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$ — imbalanced step-growth - $R_p = k_p[M](f k_d[I]/k_t)^{0.5}$ — radical polymerization rate - $\nu = k_p[M]/(2 f k_d k_t [I])^{0.5}$ — kinetic chain length - $PDI = \bar{M}_w/\bar{M}_n$ — molecular weight distribution ### ✅ Marine Applications (Connect Every Answer to This) - **PVC** — shipboard piping, cable insulation - **HDPE/UHMWPE** — dock fenders, bearing liners - **Nylon 6,6** — mooring ropes, fishing nets - **Epoxy resin** — hull coatings, FRP boat construction - **Polyurethane** — deck insulation, buoyancy foams - **Polyester** — FRP boat hulls (with glass fiber) - **SBR / Butyl rubber** — marine fenders, seals, gaskets ### ✅ Typical 14-Mark Question Pattern - **Part (a) 7 marks:** Mechanism with equations + diagram - **Part (b) 7 marks:** Derivation OR numerical problem ### ✅ Examiner's Mantra > *"Always write: definition → mechanism (with equations) → kinetics/formula → marine example."*

topics.title: Preparation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Preparation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

In the context of non-ferrous metallurgy for shipbuilding, **Preparation** refers to the comprehensive set of pre-fabrication, pre-processing, and conditioning operations performed on non-ferrous metals and alloys (such as **aluminum alloys, copper-nickel alloys, titanium alloys, brass, and bronze**) before they enter downstream processes like forming, machining, joining (welding), or assembly into marine structural components.

It encompasses:

1. **Stock Preparation** – Receiving inspection, storage, and identification of raw material.
2. **Mechanical Preparation** – Cutting, blanking, shearing, and sawing.
3. **Surface Preparation** – Degreasing, pickling, descaling, and abrasive cleaning.
4. **Edge and Joint Preparation** – Beveling, grooving, and edge conditioning for welding.
