5. **Thermal/Heat Preparation** – Pre-heating, solution treatment, and stress-relief conditioning.
6. **Microstructural Preparation** – Annealing, homogenization, and temper conditioning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:**
> *Preparation in non-ferrous shipbuilding materials is the systematic conditioning of the parent metal — physically, chemically, thermally, and microstructurally — to ensure defect-free downstream fabrication, optimum mechanical performance, and compliance with marine classification society standards (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV, ABS).*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **preparation like preparing ingredients before cooking a complex dish**.

Imagine you are a chef preparing a gourmet meal. You wouldn't throw unpeeled potatoes, unwashed vegetables, and un-marinated meat straight into the pot. Instead, you:
- **Wash** the vegetables (surface cleaning) 🥕
- **Peel and cut** them uniformly (mechanical preparation) 🔪
- **Marinate** the meat for hours (thermal/heat treatment) 🍖
- **Organize** all ingredients in order of use (stock preparation) 📋

Similarly, in shipbuilding, a **5xxx-series aluminum plate** cannot be welded straight from the warehouse. It must be:
- Inspected for defects,
- Cleaned of oil/oxides,
- Edge-beveled to the correct groove angle,
- Pre-heated to the correct interpass temperature.

**Skipping preparation = dish with sand in it, or a weldship with cracks in it.** 🚢

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Value / Range | Unit | Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density of Aluminum Alloys (5083-H116) | **2.66 × 10³** | kg/m³ | Hull plate weight estimation |
| Density of Cu-Ni (90/10) | **8.94 × 10³** | kg/m³ | Piping systems |
| Melting Point of Pure Aluminum | **660.3** | °C | Pre-heat design |
| Melting Point of Titanium (Grade 2) | **1668** | °C | GTAW preparation |
| Standard Pre-heat (Al-Mg alloys) | **60 – 120** | °C | AWS D1.2 / ISO 9606 |
| Standard Pickling Acid (Al) | **10–15 % NaOH** | wt% | Surface prep |
| Surface Roughness (post-prep) | **Ra ≤ 6.3** | μm | Painting/coating |
| Edge Bevel Angle (V-groove) | **60 ± 5** | degrees | Welding prep |

> [!NOTE]
> All preparation parameters for marine non-ferrous materials are tightly controlled by **classification society rules** (e.g., **DNVGL-OS-C401**, **AWS D1.2 for Aluminum**, **ASME SFA-5.10**). Deviation leads to **weld defects, HAZ cracking, and pitting corrosion** in service.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Preparation (Structured Logic)

### Pillar 1 — Stock Preparation 📦

**Why?** Non-ferrous alloys are highly susceptible to **storage damage, contamination, and microstructural drift**.

**How?**
1. **Receiving Inspection:** Verify Mill Test Certificate (MTC), batch number, temper condition, dimensions.
2. **Storage Classification:** Separate by alloy series (e.g., 5xxx ≠ 6xxx ≠ 7xxx) to avoid cross-contamination.
3. **Environmental Control:** Store in dry, covered area; **RH < 60 %**; no contact with ferrous materials (prevent galvanic staining).
4. **Identification Preservation:** Bar-coding, color-coding, and traceability tags per **ISO 9001 / IRS rules**.

---

### Pillar 2 — Mechanical Preparation ✂️

**Why?** Components must be shaped to near-net geometry before joining.

**How?**
- **Shearing:** For plates up to 12 mm (Al) and 6 mm (Cu-Ni). Use sharp tools to avoid work-hardening.
- **Sawing:** Band saws for thicker sections; use coolant to prevent thermal distortion.
- **Plasma / Laser Cutting:** For titanium and aluminum hull plates (low heat input preferred).
- **Waterjet Cutting:** Cold process — ideal for thin titanium sheets (avoids HAZ).

---

### Pillar 3 — Surface Preparation 🧼

**Why?** Oxides, oils, and contaminants cause **porosity, slag inclusion, and loss of corrosion resistance**.

**How (for Aluminum Alloys):**
1. **Degreasing** — Solvent wipe (acetone / MEK) or alkaline soak.
2. **Deoxidizing / Pickling** — NaOH (10–15 %) + HNO₃ (30–50 %) dip.
3. **Mechanical Brushing** — Stainless steel wire brush (NOT carbon steel — causes contamination).
4. **Final Wipe** — Clean lint-free cloth with solvent within 4 hours of welding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aluminum's oxide layer (Al₂O₃) melts at 2050 °C — far above pure Al (660 °C).** If not removed, oxide fragments enter the weld pool, causing **tungsten inclusions and lack of fusion.**

---

### Pillar 4 — Edge and Joint Preparation 🔧

**Why?** The weld groove geometry directly controls penetration and dilution.

**How?** Common edge preparations for shipbuilding butt joints:

| Joint Type | Sketch Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Square Butt** | No bevel, gap 0–2 mm | Thin sheets < 3 mm |
| **Single-V Groove** | 60° included angle, 1–2 mm root face | Plate 3–12 mm |
| **Double-V Groove** | 60° both sides | Plate > 12 mm |
| **Single-U Groove** | 8 mm radius root | Thick Cu-Ni pipe |
| **J-Groove** | One side beveled | Tube-to-tube-sheet joints |

---

### Pillar 5 — Thermal Preparation 🔥

**Why?** Reduces thermal gradient, minimizes distortion, and prevents HAZ cracking.

**How?**
- **Pre-heating** aluminum to 60–120 °C removes surface moisture.
- **Interpass temperature control** for thick Al-Mg plates: max **150 °C** (excess → grain coarsening).
- **Solution Treatment + Aging** for 6xxx and 7xxx series (e.g., 6082-T6: solution at 530 °C, water quench, age at 175 °C for 8 hrs).

---

### Pillar 6 — Microstructural Preparation 🔬

**Why?** Final temper condition determines in-service performance.

**How?**
- **O (Annealed)** — Maximum ductility, used for forming.
- **H (Strain Hardened)** — H116, H321 for marine hull plates.
- **T (Thermally Treated)** — T6 (peak aged) for superstructures.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Symbols | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $$ T_{pre} = T_{ambient} + \Delta T_{surface} $$ | $T_{pre}$ = preheat temp, $T_{ambient}$ = ambient | Minimum pre-heat calculation | °C |
| $$ t_{prep} = \frac{\rho \cdot V}{m} $$ | $\rho$ = density, $V$ = volume, $m$ = mass | Mass estimation from stock | kg |
| $$ A_{groove} = t \cdot \left( t \cdot \tan(\theta/2) - R_f \right) $$ | $t$ = thickness, $\theta$ = groove angle, $R_f$ = root face | Cross-section of V-groove for filler calc | mm² |
| $$ Q_{pickle} = C \cdot V_{acid} $$ | $C$ = concentration, $V_{acid}$ = acid volume | Pickling solution strength | L |
| $$ Ra_{target} \le 6.3 \, \mu m $$ | $Ra$ = arithmetic roughness | Coating-ready surface | μm |
| $$ \Delta T_{max} = T_{melt} - T_{ambient} $$ | $T_{melt}$ = melting point | Maximum allowable thermal gradient | °C |
| $$ \%Dilution = \frac{A_{parent}}{A_{parent} + A_{filler}} \times 100 $$ | $A_{parent}$, $A_{filler}$ = cross-sectional areas | Weld chemistry control | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** The vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ is rendered as `\vert` in LaTeX to prevent markdown table corruption.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LNG Carrier Construction:** Preparation of **5083-O cryogenic aluminum** tanks requires rigorous surface and edge preparation to prevent brittle fracture at −163 °C.
- **Naval Frigate Superstructures:** **Al-Mg-Si (6082-T6)** requires pre-aging prep to retain ballistic impact resistance.
- **Submarine Hulls (Titanium):** **Grade 5 (Ti-6Al-4V)** demands oxygen-free surface prep and inert gas shielding preparation to prevent alpha-case formation.
- **Marine Heat Exchangers:** **Cu-Ni 90/10** tube sheets need passivation prep (chromate or benzotriazole treatment) to survive seawater corrosion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Pre-Heat Temperature for Non-Ferrous Plate

The pre-heat temperature is governed by the **thermal balance** to prevent moisture-induced porosity. Let us derive the minimum pre-heat for an aluminum plate.

### Step 1 — Energy Required to Evaporate Surface Moisture
The latent heat needed to remove a thin moisture film of thickness $h$ is:

$$
Q_{evap} = \rho_{water} \cdot h \cdot A \cdot L_v
$$

where:
- $\rho_{water}$ = density of water = **1000 kg/m³**
- $h$ = moisture film thickness ≈ **0.1 mm = 1 × 10⁻⁴ m**
- $A$ = plate surface area (m²)
- $L_v$ = latent heat of vaporization of water = **2.26 × 10⁶ J/kg**

### Step 2 — Energy Available from Pre-Heating
Energy supplied by heating the plate from $T_{amb}$ to $T_{pre}$ is:

$$
Q_{supply} = \rho_{Al} \cdot V \cdot c_{p,Al} \cdot (T_{pre} - T_{amb})
$$

where:
- $\rho_{Al}$ = density of aluminum alloy ≈ **2700 kg/m³**
- $V$ = plate volume = $A \cdot t$ ($t$ = plate thickness)
- $c_{p,Al}$ = specific heat = **900 J/(kg·K)**
- $T_{pre}$ = pre-heat temperature
- $T_{amb}$ = ambient temperature (assume 30 °C)

### Step 3 — Equilibrium Condition
Setting $Q_{supply} \ge Q_{evap}$:

$$
\rho_{Al} \cdot (A \cdot t) \cdot c_{p,Al} \cdot (T_{pre} - T_{amb}) \ge \rho_{water} \cdot h \cdot A \cdot L_v
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $T_{pre}$
The area $A$ cancels out:

$$
(T_{pre} - T_{amb}) \ge \frac{\rho_{water} \cdot h \cdot L_v}{\rho_{Al} \cdot t \cdot c_{p,Al}}
$$

Substituting numerical values (for $t = 10$ mm = 0.01 m):

$$
T_{pre} - T_{amb} \ge \frac{(1000)(1 \times 10^{-4})(2.26 \times 10^{6})}{(2700)(0.01)(900)}
$$

$$
T_{pre} - T_{amb} \ge \frac{226{,}000}{24{,}300}
$$

$$
T_{pre} - T_{amb} \ge 9.30 \, °C
$$

### Step 5 — Safety Factor Application
A safety factor of **3× to 5×** is applied (to account for plate mass acting as heat sink during welding):

$$
T_{pre} = T_{amb} + (3 \text{ to } 5) \times 9.30 \, °C
$$

$$
T_{pre} = 30 \, °C + 60 \, °C \approx \boxed{90 \, °C \, (min)}
$$

This matches the **AWS D1.2 specification** of 60–120 °C pre-heat for 5xxx-series aluminum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** Notice that thicker plates require **higher** pre-heat because the heat-sink effect scales with mass. However, **interpass temperature must not exceed 150 °C** or grain coarsening occurs in the HAZ.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Pickling Solution Strength for Al₂O₃ Removal

The reaction of aluminum oxide with NaOH is:

$$
Al_2O_3 + 2\,NaOH \rightarrow 2\,NaAlO_2 + H_2O
$$

### Stoichiometric Calculation
- Molar mass of $Al_2O_3$ = **101.96 g/mol**
- Molar mass of $NaOH$ = **40.00 g/mol**
- Stoichiometric ratio: 2 mol NaOH per 1 mol $Al_2O_3$

$$
\frac{m_{NaOH}}{m_{Al_2O_3}} = \frac{2 \times 40.00}{101.96} = \frac{80.00}{101.96} = 0.7847
$$

So, for every 1 kg of $Al_2O_3$ to be removed, **0.7847 kg of NaOH** is required stoichiometrically.

### Excess Factor (Industrial Practice)
Industrial pickling uses **20–50 % excess** NaOH to account for:
- Incomplete contact
- Side reactions with base metal:

$$
Al + NaOH + H_2O \rightarrow NaAlO_2 + \tfrac{3}{2}\,H_2 \uparrow
$$

### Final Concentration Target
A **10–15 % w/w NaOH solution** is standard:

$$
\%C = \frac{m_{NaOH}}{m_{NaOH} + m_{water}} \times 100
$$

For 10 kg of 15 % solution:

$$
m_{NaOH} = 0.15 \times 10 = 1.5 \, kg
$$

$$
m_{water} = 8.5 \, kg \equiv 8.5 \, L
$$

After caustic soak (5–15 min at 50–70 °C), the part is **desmutted** in 30–50 % $HNO_3$ to neutralize residual smut and restore shiny surface.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Preparation Route Selection

| Base Material | Primary Concern | Recommended Prep Route | Critical Risk if Skipped |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Al 5083-H116 (Hull)** | Mg-content, oxide | NaOH pickle + SS brush + acetone wipe | Weld porosity, HAZ cracking |
| **Al 6082-T6 (Superstructure)** | T-temper loss | Pre-aging awareness; limit HAZ | Strength loss |
| **Cu-Ni 90/10 (Pipe)** | Sulfide contamination | Passivation + benzotriazole dip | Pitting corrosion |
| **Ti Grade 2 (Tube)** | Oxygen embrittlement | SS brush + acetone + inert shroud | Alpha-case, embrittlement |
| **Brass (Valves)** | Dezincification | Anneal at 400 °C; pickle in HNO₃ | Grain boundary attack |
| **Bronze (Propeller)** | Lead segregation | Homogenize at 700 °C | Cavitation erosion |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Preparation Workflow in Shipbuilding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Receipt] --> B[Mill Certificate Verification]
    B --> C[Storage Allocation]
    C --> D[Stock Issuance]
    D --> E[Mechanical Cutting]
    E --> F[Edge Beveling]
    F --> G[Surface Degreasing]
    G --> H[Pickling / Deoxidation]
    H --> I[Drying Pre-heat]
    I --> J[Edge Final Inspection]
    J --> K{Conformance Check}
    K -- Pass --> L[Ready for Fabrication]
    K -- Fail --> M[Rework Loop]
    M --> G
    L --> N[Forming / Welding Station]
```

---

## 4.2 Joint Preparation Geometry (Schematic Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Thin Sheet < 3mm] --> B[Square Butt Joint]
    C[Plate 3-12mm] --> D[Single-V Groove 60°]
    E[Plate > 12mm] --> F[Double-V Groove 30° each side]
    G[Cu-Ni Pipe] --> H[Single-U Groove]
    I[Tube-Tubesheet] --> J[J Groove]
```

---

## 4.3 Process Parameter Map for Surface Preparation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Aluminum Alloy Surface] --> B{Al Surface Condition}
    B -- Light Oxide --> C[SS Wire Brush + Solvent Wipe]
    B -- Heavy Oxide / Heat Tint --> D[NaOH 10-15% Soak 50-70°C]
    B -- Heavy Contamination --> E[Alkaline Degrease + Pickle + Desmut]
    C --> F[Inspect Ra 6.3 micron max]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Within 4 hrs: Welding / Bonding]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `step1`). No reserved Mermaid keywords used. Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric without markdown formatting.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define "Preparation" in the context of non-ferrous materials used in shipbuilding. List any four types of preparation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Preparation refers to the systematic conditioning of non-ferrous metals and alloys (aluminum, copper-nickel, titanium, brass, bronze) prior to fabrication, forming, or joining operations in shipbuilding.

**Four types of preparation (1/2 mark each, 2 marks for definition):**
1. **Stock Preparation** – inspection, storage, traceability.
2. **Mechanical Preparation** – cutting, shearing, sawing.
3. **Surface Preparation** – degreasing, pickling, deoxidation.
4. **Edge/Joint Preparation** – beveling, grooving.
5. **Thermal Preparation** – pre-heating, interpass control.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Why is the removal of aluminum oxide (Al₂O₃) layer critical before welding? State its melting point and the melting point of pure aluminum.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Al₂O₃ oxide layer is **refractory and tenacious**. It must be removed because:

- **Melting point of Al₂O₃** = **2050 °C**
- **Melting point of pure Al** = **660.3 °C**
- The ratio = 2050 / 660.3 ≈ **3.1 times higher**

If not removed, the oxide remains solid while the underlying aluminum melts during welding, causing:
- **Entrapment of oxide fragments** in the weld pool → inclusions.
- **Lack of fusion** between filler and parent metal.
- **Tungsten contamination** in GTAW.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**A shipyard is fabricating an aluminum superstructure using 6082-T6 plates of 10 mm thickness. Describe in detail the complete preparation procedure before welding, including stock, mechanical, surface, edge, and thermal preparation. Justify each step with reference to classification society standards.**

#### (a) Stock and Mechanical Preparation (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Stock Preparation (2 marks):**
- Verify **Mill Test Certificate (MTC)** for batch number, chemical composition, temper condition (T6), and mechanical properties.
- Store in dry covered area; relative humidity < 60 %; temperature 15–30 °C.
- Avoid contact with ferrous materials (use wooden or plastic spacers) to prevent galvanic staining and corrosion.
- Maintain traceability per **ISO 9001** and **IRS / DNV rules**.

**2. Mechanical Preparation (2 marks):**
- Cut plates using **shearing** (for ≤ 12 mm Al) or **plasma cutting** with low heat input.
- Sawing with coolant for thicker sections to avoid work-hardening.
- All cuts should be **square, distortion-free**, and burrs removed by deburring.

**3. Edge Preparation (3 marks):**
- For 10 mm plate, prepare **Single-V groove** with 60° included angle, root face 1–2 mm, root gap 2–3 mm as per **AWS D1.2**.
- Bevel by **milling or grinding** (use aluminum-oxide or silicon-carbide abrasive; not ferrous).
- Verify bevel angle with **weld gap gauge**.

---

#### (b) Surface and Thermal Preparation (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Surface Preparation (4 marks):**
- **Degrease** with acetone or MEK solvent wipe.
- **Deoxidize** in NaOH (10–15 %) at 50–70 °C for 5–10 min.
- **Desmut** in HNO₃ (30–50 %) for 2–5 min to remove black smut.
- **Final brush** with stainless steel wire brush (not carbon).
- **Final solvent wipe** within **4 hours** of welding to prevent re-oxidation.
- Surface finish: **Ra ≤ 6.3 μm** before welding (per **AWS D3.7M**).

**2. Thermal Preparation (3 marks):**
- **Pre-heat** to **80–100 °C** using resistance heating pad or oxy-fuel torch with rosebud tip (check with temperature-indicating crayon).
- Maintain **interpass temperature ≤ 150 °C** to avoid grain coarsening.
- **Post-weld treatment:** Since 6082-T6 loses temper in HAZ, perform **artificial aging at 175 °C for 8 hours** to restore T6 properties (or use 6082-T4 if extensive welding is needed).

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 – Alternative Choice]`

**Discuss the preparation procedure for Cu-Ni 90/10 alloy tubes used in marine heat exchanger systems. Explain surface, edge, and storage preparation with reference to corrosion resistance.**

#### (a) Material Background and Stock Preparation (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Material Characteristics (2 marks):**
- Cu-Ni 90/10 contains 90 % Cu, 10 % Ni, with small additions of Fe (1–2 %) and Mn (0.5–1 %).
- Excellent seawater corrosion resistance due to formation of protective **cuprous oxide (Cu₂O)** film.
- Used in **condenser tubes, heat exchanger tubes, and seawater piping** per **ASTM B111 / B466**.

**2. Stock Preparation (2 marks):**
- Store tubes in **bundles with end-caps** to prevent ingress of dirt/moisture.
- Keep in **dry, well-ventilated indoor storage**; avoid ammonia fumes (cause SCC).
- Stack on wooden supports; avoid direct ground contact.
- Verify MTC; segregate by temper (annealed vs. drawn).

**3. Mechanical Preparation (3 marks):**
- Cut tubes using **fine-tooth hacksaw or rotary cut-off wheel** to avoid burring.
- Deburr **internal and external** edges with deburring tool — internal burrs cause **turbulence and erosion-corrosion**.
- Maintain **roundness and ovality tolerance** per **ASTM B111**.

---

#### (b) Surface, Edge, and Passivation Preparation (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Surface Preparation (3 marks):**
- **Degrease** in alkaline cleaner (e.g., 5 % Na₃PO₄ at 60 °C) to remove drawing oils.
- **Pickle** in 5–10 % H₂SO₄ for 1–3 min to remove mill scale and light oxides.
- Rinse thoroughly in **deionized water** to prevent chloride residue.
- Final rinse with **low-chloride (< 50 ppm) water** to prevent pitting initiation.

**2. Edge Preparation for Tube Joints (2 marks):**
- For tube-to-tubesheet joints, prepare **J-groove or square butt** depending on thickness.
- For butt welding of tubes, use **single-V 60° groove** with 1.5 mm root face.
- Bevel by **lathe turning or rotary grinding** to maintain concentricity.

**3. Passivation (2 marks):**
- Immerse in **0.5–2 % benzotriazole (BTA) solution** at 50 °C for 30 min, OR
- **Chromate conversion coating** (where regulations permit) for 30–60 sec.
- This forms a **protective Cu-BTA complex film** that resists chloride attack.
- Final dry in clean air; protect with **plastic end-caps** until installation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. ✗ **Failing to mention interpass temperature limits** (max 150 °C for Al) — **loses 2 marks**.
> 2. ✗ **Using "carbon steel wire brush"** for aluminum — this is a textbook error and costs 1 mark.
> 3. ✗ **Ignoring the 4-hour window** between final surface prep and welding — re-oxidation occurs.
> 4. ✗ **Not specifying the NaOH + HNO₃ two-step process** for aluminum — incomplete answer.
> 5. ✗ **Confusing T6 temper loss with HAZ cracking** — these are different mechanisms.
> 6. ✗ **Forgetting the Fe and Mn additions** in Cu-Ni 90/10 — examiners specifically test this.
> 7. ✗ **Missing end-cap protection** for Cu-Ni tubes in storage — loses 1 mark.
> 8. ✗ **Omitting classification society references** (AWS D1.2, DNV, IRS) — mandatory for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Preparation of Non-Ferrous Materials for Shipbuilding:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Pre-fabrication conditioning of non-ferrous alloys for marine use.
- ✅ **Six Pillars:** Stock → Mechanical → Surface → Edge → Thermal → Microstructural.
- ✅ **Al₂O₃ Melting Point = 2050 °C** vs Pure Al = 660.3 °C (≈ 3.1× higher).
- ✅ **Pickling Cycle for Al:** NaOH (10–15 %) → water rinse → HNO₃ (30–50 %) desmut → water rinse → dry → weld within 4 hrs.
- ✅ **Pre-heat Range for Al:** **60–120 °C** (AWS D1.2); Interpass **≤ 150 °C**.
- ✅ **Use STAINLESS STEEL brush only** — never carbon steel (contamination).
- ✅ **Cu-Ni 90/10:** Fe (1–2 %) + Mn (0.5–1 %) additions are critical for corrosion resistance.
- ✅ **Cu-Ni storage:** End-capped, dry, ammonia-free environment.
- ✅ **Cu-Ni passivation:** Benzotriazole (BTA) 0.5–2 % or chromate conversion coating.
- ✅ **Ti Grade 2 prep:** SS brush + acetone + inert gas shroud to prevent alpha-case.
- ✅ **6082-T6 temper loss:** Restored by post-weld aging at 175 °C × 8 hrs OR select 6082-T4 for heavily welded zones.
- ✅ **Surface roughness target:** **Ra ≤ 6.3 μm** before coating/welding.
- ✅ **Classification society standards:** AWS D1.2 (Al), ASTM B111 (Cu-Ni tubes), DNVGL-OS-C401, IRS Pt 3.
- ✅ **Storage humidity:** **< 60 % RH** to prevent hydration staining and galvanic corrosion.
- ✅ **Single-V Groove:** **60° ± 5°** included angle; root face 1–2 mm.
- ✅ **Common Weld Defects due to poor prep:** Porosity, slag inclusion, lack of fusion, HAZ cracking, pitting corrosion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Properties and Uses of Some Important Polymers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — PROPERTIES AND USES OF SOME IMPORTANT POLYMERS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Polymers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **POLYMER (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **polymer** is a high-molecular-weight organic compound (natural or synthetic) composed of a large number of small repeating structural units called **monomers**, joined together by covalent **primary bonds** in a long-chain macromolecular structure. The term originates from the Greek *poly* (many) + *meros* (parts).

In **shipbuilding engineering**, polymers are classified as essential **non-ferrous, non-metallic engineering materials** used wherever the limitations of steel (corrosion, weight, magnetic signature, electrical conductivity, noise radiation, biofouling) become unacceptable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Degree of Polymerization (DP):** The number of monomer units in a polymer chain. Most engineering polymers have DP ranging from **1,000 to 100,000**.

---

### 1.2 The Three Master Classes of Polymers (Syllabus Highlight)

For KTU 2024 board purposes, every shipboard polymer is classified into exactly one of these three families based on **thermal response and network topology**:

| Master Class | Network Topology | Thermal Response | Re-processable? | Shipbuilding Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Thermoplastics** | Linear / lightly branched chains, held by weak **secondary (van der Waals)** bonds | Soften on heating, harden on cooling | YES | HDPE pipes, PVC cable insulation, Nylon ropes, PTFE bearings |
| **Thermosetting Plastics (Thermosets)** | 3-D cross-linked network, held by strong **covalent crosslinks** | Irreversibly harden on heating; char on overheating | NO | Epoxy hull coatings, Phenolic laminates, GRP/FRP ship hulls, Polyester boat hulls |
| **Elastomers (Rubbers)** | Lightly cross-linked coiled chains, very high chain flexibility | Large elastic stretch; do not melt, may char | NO (but can be re-vulcanized) | Neoprene fender covers, EPDM hatch seals, Silicone O-rings, Butyl rubber cable jackets |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Exam:** "**T-P-E** — **T**hermoplastics **P**our, **E**lastomers **E**longate, **T**hermosets **T**rap (permanent)." Used by Naval Architecture students worldwide.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Spaghetti Bowl Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **INTUITION: Think of a polymer like a bowl of cooked spaghetti.**

- **Thermoplastic** = a bowl of plain boiled spaghetti. Each strand is independent. Slide them around (heat) and they rearrange freely → melts and reshapes.
- **Elastomer** = a bowl of spaghetti where every strand is **lightly tied at random points** (sulfur crosslinks in vulcanized rubber). You can pull one strand → it stretches and recoils because the network is loose.
- **Thermoset** = a bowl of spaghetti where every strand is **welded to every other strand at many points** (epoxy crosslinks). Pull one → the whole bowl moves as a rigid body. Heat only destroys it.

This single analogy predicts density, mechanical behavior, recyclability, and ship-repair welding feasibility of every polymer in this module.

---

### 1.4 Why Polymers Are Critical in Shipbuilding (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> A modern naval vessel or merchant ship contains **200–500 tonnes of polymers** distributed across **hull, superstructure, machinery, outfit, and electronics**. Steel alone cannot satisfy modern design demands for:
> - **Corrosion resistance** in saltwater (saves ₹crores in dry-dock repainting).
> - **Weight reduction** (1 tonne polymer ≈ replaces 3–5 tonnes steel, increasing payload).
> - **Magnetic & radar signature** control (submarine anechoic tiles, composite masts on US Navy DDG-1000).
> - **Acoustic damping** (rubber isolation mounts reduce engine noise by 20–30 dB).
> - **Fire safety** (intumescent coatings, low-smoke halogen-free cables).
> - **Crew comfort & habitability** (PU foam mattresses, vinyl flooring).

The **Kerala Shipyard (Cochin Shipyard Ltd.)** and **Hindustan Shipyard Visakhapatnam** routinely use **GRP (Glass Reinforced Polyester)** for fast patrol boats, lifeboats, and Naval mine-counter-measure vessels.

---

### 1.5 The 14 Polymers Covered in This KTU Module

For the 2024 scheme, focus on these fourteen polymers — they are repeatedly tested:

1. Polyethylene (PE) — LDPE, HDPE, UHMWPE
2. Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC)
3. Polypropylene (PP)
4. Polystyrene (PS) & Expanded PS (EPS)
5. Acrylonitrile-Butadiene-Styrene (ABS)
6. Polyamides (PA / Nylon 6, Nylon 6,6)

8. Polymethyl Methacrylate (PMMA / Acrylic)

10. Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE / Teflon)
11. Polyurethane (PU / PUR)
12. Epoxy Resin (EP)
13. Polyester Resin (Unsaturated Polyester — UP)
14. Phenol-Formaldehyde Resin (PF / Bakelite)

Plus four **shipbuilding-critical elastomers**: Natural Rubber (NR), Polychloroprene (Neoprene / CR), Ethylene-Propylene-Diene-Monomer (EPDM), and Silicone Rubber (SI/VMQ).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Polymerization Chemistry — How the Chains Are Built

### 2.1.1 Addition (Chain-Growth) Polymerization

Most shipboard thermoplastics (PE, PP, PVC, PTFE, PMMA) form by this mechanism.

- **Initiation:** Initiator (e.g., benzoyl peroxide) splits into two free radicals (R•). Each radical attacks the C=C double bond of a monomer.
- **Propagation:** The radical active site propagates by successive addition of monomers, growing the chain at enormous speed (chain grows in **<1 second**).
- **Termination:** Two growing radicals combine (coupling) or one abstracts a hydrogen (disproportionation) → dead polymer chain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Net Reaction for Polyethylene:**
> $n \, \text{CH}_2=\text{CH}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{radical initiator}} -(\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2)_n-$
> where **n (DP) ≈ 10,000–100,000** for HDPE shipboard pipe-grade.

### 2.1.2 Condensation (Step-Growth) Polymerization

Used for **Nylon, PET, Phenolic, Polyester, Epoxy, Polyurethane**.

- Monomers with two reactive end-groups (e.g., -OH and -COOH) combine with **elimination of a small molecule (H₂O, HCl, methanol)**.
- Reaction is **slow**, requires **heat + catalyst**, and high conversions (>99%) are needed to obtain high molecular weight (Carothers' equation, see §3).
- Every bond formed = one small molecule eliminated ⇒ **no by-product in epoxy/PU addition cure (these are technically step-growth but with no leaving group).**

### 2.1.3 Cross-Linking (Curing / Vulcanization)

- Forms the **3-D network** of thermosets (epoxy, polyester + styrene) and elastomers (sulfur vulcanization of natural rubber).
- Crosslink **density** controls rigidity, glass transition, solvent resistance, and creep.
- For shipbuilding GRP layup, **2–3 wt% MEKP catalyst + cobalt accelerator** cures unsaturated polyester at room temperature in 30 min to 24 h depending on temperature.

---

## 2.2 Polymer Microstructure — The Hidden Variable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All engineering properties of a polymer (Tg, Tm, modulus, strength, chemical resistance) are governed by **four microstructural variables**:
> 1. **Monomer chemistry** (which atoms in the repeat unit)
> 2. **Molecular weight (M_n, M_w) and MWD (polydispersity)**
> 3. **Tacticity / Isomerism** (isotactic, syndiotactic, atactic)
> 4. **Crystallinity (% C)** and morphology (spherulites, lamellae)

### 2.2.1 Tacticity in Polypropylene — A KTU Favourite

- **Isotactic PP** (i-PP): All -CH₃ groups on the same side of the chain → **highly crystalline (Tm = 165 °C)**, strong, used for ship **rope, net, woven geotextile**.
- **Atactic PP** (a-PP): -CH₃ groups random → **amorphous, soft, sticky**, used only as adhesive additive.
- **Syndiotactic PP** (s-PP): -CH₃ alternating → semi-crystalline, less common.

### 2.2.2 Crystallinity

- **Semi-crystalline polymers** (PE, PP, PA, PTFE, PET): contain both ordered crystalline regions and amorphous regions. Higher crystallinity ⇒ higher density, strength, chemical resistance, but lower impact toughness and transparency.
- **Amorphous polymers** (PS, PMMA, PC, ABS, PVC plasticized): transparent, isotropic, soften gradually over Tg, no sharp Tm.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Heuristic:** A transparent shipboard polymer (clear window, lens) ⇒ **amorphous**. A white/opaque polymer (HDPE pipe, PTFE rod) ⇒ **semi-crystalline**.

---

## 2.3 Critical Thermal Transitions — Tg and Tm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg):** The temperature at which the amorphous regions of a polymer transition from rigid glassy to flexible rubbery state. Below Tg ⇒ hard & brittle; above Tg ⇒ soft & flexible.
> - **Melting Temperature (Tm):** The temperature at which crystalline regions melt. Exists **only for semi-crystalline polymers**.

### 2.3.1 The Fox Equation (For Copolymer / Blend Tg)

For a polymer blend or random copolymer, the **glass transition temperature is predicted** by:

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{w_i}{T_{g,i}}$$

where $w_i$ is the **weight fraction** of component $i$ and $T_{g,i}$ is its glass transition temperature **in Kelvin**.

### 2.3.2 The Beaman-Boyer Rule (Empirical)

For a semi-crystalline polymer, the approximate relation between Tg and Tm (both in Kelvin) is:

$$\frac{T_g}{T_m} \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 0.67$$

This is why PTFE (Tm = 327 °C) has Tg ≈ −97 °C, and Nylon 6,6 (Tm = 265 °C) has Tg ≈ 50 °C.

---

| 1 | Degree of Polymerization | $DP = \dfrac{\overline{M_n}}{M_0}$ | $\overline{M_n}$ = number-average mol. wt; $M_0$ = monomer mol. wt | dimensionless | Length of polymer chain |
| 2 | Number-Average Molecular Weight | $\overline{M_n} = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | $N_i$ = number of chains of mol. wt $M_i$ | g/mol or kg/kmol | End-group analysis |
| 3 | Weight-Average Molecular Weight | $\overline{M_w} = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | same as above | g/mol | Light scattering, viscosity |
| 4 | Polydispersity Index | $PDI = \dfrac{\overline{M_w}}{\overline{M_n}}$ | — | dimensionless (always $\geq 1$) | Quality control of polymer batch |

| 11 | Rule of Mixtures (Composite Modulus, Voigt — iso-strain) | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | $V_f, V_m$ = fibre, matrix volume fractions | Pa | FRP hull laminate stiffness |

| 15 | Water Absorption (24 h immersion) | $\% WA = \dfrac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{dry}} \times 100$ | $W$ = mass | % | Nylon, ABS, Epoxy grade selection |

| 17 | Vicat Softening Point | $VSP$ (needle penetration 1 mm, ASTM D1525) | — | °C | PVC pipe grade selection |
| 18 | Melt Flow Index | $MFI = \dfrac{\text{mass extruded in 10 min}}{\text{}}$ (g/10 min) | — | g/10 min | Processability indicator for PE, PP |

> [!TIP]
> **Carothers' Equation is the most-tested formula** for the polymerization sub-topic. If only one formula is to be memorized, make it $DP_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$.

---

## 2.5 Mechanical Behaviour of Polymers — The Five-Region Stress-Strain Curve

A typical ductile polymer tensile curve has five regions:

- **Region I — Elastic (Hookean):** $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$, recoverable.
- **Region II — Yielding:** Stress reaches **yield strength $\sigma_y$**, onset of plastic flow.
- **Region III — Strain Softening (Necking):** Local cross-section reduces.
- **Region IV — Cold Drawing / Neck Propagation:** Polymer chains align along tensile axis ⇒ **strain-induced strengthening** (seen in HDPE, Nylon rope).
- **Region V — Strain Hardening & Fracture:** At **ultimate tensile strength (UTS)**, neck stabilizes, then breaks at **elongation at break $\varepsilon_b$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Shipbuilding relevance:** A nylon mooring rope must show Region IV cold drawing to absorb shock loads of a berthing ship without snapping. A brittle polymer like PS in Region I fractures immediately and is unsuitable for dynamic load applications.

---

## 2.6 Environmental Degradation — The "Achilles' Heel" of Shipboard Polymers

> [!WARNING]
> Shipboard polymers are attacked by **UV (sunlight), saltwater, temperature cycling, fuel oil, micro-organisms, and ozone**. The following degradative mechanisms must be specified when choosing a polymer:

1. **Hydrolysis:** Ester or amide bonds break in presence of water → degradation of PET, Nylon, unsaturated polyester (ester hydrolysis is why GRP boat hulls blister with age — **osmosis blistering**).
2. **UV Degradation:** Breaks C-C and C-H bonds, generates free radicals → chain scission (PS yellows, PP becomes brittle). Mitigated by **2–3 wt% carbon black** or HALS stabilizers.
3. **Thermal Oxidation:** Free-radical chain reaction with O₂ → embrittlement. Mitigated by **antioxidants (Irganox, hindered phenols)**.
4. **Environmental Stress Cracking (ESC):** Combined effect of tensile stress + surfactant (e.g., detergent, seawater + soap) → brittle fracture of PE. **Important for HDPE pipes carrying grey water.**
5. **Creep:** Long-term deformation under constant load at T > Tg. **Critical for thermoplastic pipes carrying hot fluids.**

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Carothers' Equation (Step-Growth Polymerization)

This is the most important derivation in Module 3 and has appeared in KTU past papers.

### 3.1.1 Statement of the Problem

A condensation polymerization of a difunctional monomer A–B (or a stoichiometric mixture of A–A and B–B) proceeds with the elimination of a small molecule. Let:

- $N_0$ = initial number of monomer molecules (functional groups of type A or B in stoichiometric balance).
- $N$ = number of molecules (chains) remaining at time $t$.
- $p$ = fraction of functional groups that have reacted at time $t$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Counting argument:** Initially each monomer is a single molecule. As the reaction proceeds, two molecules become one, three become one, etc. **The number of bonds formed equals the number of molecules eliminated.**

### 3.1.2 Derivation (Full Step-by-Step)

Let us count the number of bonds formed.

- Each reacted functional group has formed **one bond**. The total number of functional groups present initially = $N_0$ (taking only A groups in stoichiometric balance).
- The number of A groups that have reacted by time $t$ = $p \cdot N_0$.
- Each bond consumes **two functional groups** (one A, one B), so the total number of bonds formed is:

$$\text{Bonds formed} = p \cdot N_0 \quad (\text{since every bond = one A reacted})$$

- Each bond formed **reduces the molecule count by exactly one** (two molecules become one chain).

Therefore, the number of molecules remaining is:

$$N = N_0 - (\text{new bonds formed}) = N_0 - p \cdot N_0 = N_0 (1 - p)$$

By definition, the **number-average degree of polymerization** is the **total number of monomer units divided by the number of chains**:

$$\overline{DP_n} = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)}$$

$$\boxed{\overline{DP_n} = \frac{1}{1-p}}$$

### 3.1.3 Numerical Verification (Worked Example)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example:** A nylon 6,6 polymerization. Find $\overline{DP_n}$ when $p = 0.99$ (99% conversion).

$$\overline{DP_n} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.99} = \frac{1}{0.01} = 100$$

So at 99% conversion, the average chain has 100 monomer units. To reach $\overline{DP_n} = 200$ (a strong fibre-grade nylon), we need:

$$200 = \frac{1}{1-p} \implies 1-p = \frac{1}{200} = 0.005 \implies p = 0.995 \text{ (99.5 %)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** The DP is **extremely sensitive** to the last 1% of conversion. This is why **exact stoichiometry** and **efficient removal of the water by-product** (Le Chatelier's principle) are critical in industrial nylon and PET reactors.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Number-Average Molecular Weight

### 3.2.1 Setup

Suppose a polymer sample contains $N_1$ chains of molecular weight $M_1$, $N_2$ chains of mol. wt $M_2$, ..., $N_i$ chains of mol. wt $M_i$.

- Total mass of sample = $\sum N_i M_i$ (grams, if $M_i$ in g/mol and $N_i$ in mol of chains).
- Total number of molecules = $\sum N_i$.

### 3.2.2 Derivation

By definition of **number average**:

$$\overline{M_n} = \frac{\sum (\text{mass contributed by each species})}{\sum (\text{number of molecules of each species})}$$

$$\overline{M_n} = \frac{N_1 M_1 + N_2 M_2 + \cdots + N_i M_i}{N_1 + N_2 + \cdots + N_i}$$

$$\boxed{\overline{M_n} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}}$$

### 3.2.3 Derivation of $\overline{M_w}$

The **weight-average molecular weight** weights each chain by its mass, so it biases toward high-molecular-weight chains. The fraction of total mass contributed by species $i$ is:

$$w_i = \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum N_i M_i}$$

The weighted average of $M_i$ using weights $w_i$ is:

$$\overline{M_w} = \sum w_i M_i = \sum \left(\frac{N_i M_i}{\sum N_j M_j}\right) M_i$$

$$\boxed{\overline{M_w} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Always $\overline{M_w} \geq \overline{M_n}$**, with equality **only for monodisperse** polymer (PDI = 1, which is impossible in real synthesis). Commercial polymerizations have PDI between 1.5 and 50.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem — Polydispersity Index

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Q:** A polymer sample consists of 30 % by number of chains with $M = 10{,}000$ g/mol and 70 % by number of chains with $M = 50{,}000$ g/mol. Calculate $\overline{M_n}$, $\overline{M_w}$ and PDI.

**Solution:**

Take a basis of 100 chains total. Then $N_1 = 30$ at $M_1 = 10{,}000$ and $N_2 = 70$ at $M_2 = 50{,}000$.

**Step 1 — Number-average molecular weight:**

$$\overline{M_n} = \frac{N_1 M_1 + N_2 M_2}{N_1 + N_2} = \frac{30 \times 10{,}000 + 70 \times 50{,}000}{30 + 70}$$

$$\overline{M_n} = \frac{300{,}000 + 3{,}500{,}000}{100} = \frac{3{,}800{,}000}{100} = 38{,}000 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Weight-average molecular weight:**

$$\overline{M_w} = \frac{N_1 M_1^2 + N_2 M_2^2}{N_1 M_1 + N_2 M_2} = \frac{30 \times (10{,}000)^2 + 70 \times (50{,}000)^2}{3{,}800{,}000}$$

$$\overline{M_w} = \frac{30 \times 10^8 + 70 \times 25 \times 10^8}{3{,}800{,}000} = \frac{(3 + 175) \times 10^9}{3{,}800{,}000} = \frac{178 \times 10^9}{3.8 \times 10^6}$$

$$\overline{M_w} = 46{,}842 \text{ g/mol (approximately)}$$

**Step 3 — Polydispersity Index:**

$$PDI = \frac{\overline{M_w}}{\overline{M_n}} = \frac{46{,}842}{38{,}000} \approx 1.23$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** PDI = 1.23 is typical of a **well-controlled industrial polymer** (e.g., anionic polymerization of polystyrene gives PDI ≈ 1.05; free-radical polymerization of commercial PVC gives PDI ≈ 2.0). The narrowness indicates fairly uniform chain lengths.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem — Rule of Mixtures for a GRP Hull

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Q:** A GRP ship hull laminate is made of E-glass fibre (volume fraction $V_f = 0.45$) and unsaturated polyester matrix ($V_m = 0.55$). Given $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\rho_f = 2.54$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³, find the laminate longitudinal modulus and density.

**Step 1 — Volume fractions satisfy** $V_f + V_m = 0.45 + 0.55 = 1.00$ ✓

**Step 2 — Longitudinal (iso-strain) Modulus (Voigt):**

$$E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = (72)(0.45) + (3.5)(0.55)$$

$$E_c = 32.4 + 1.925 = 34.325 \text{ GPa} \approx 34.3 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 3 — Density:**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = (2.54)(0.45) + (1.20)(0.55)$$

$$\rho_c = 1.143 + 0.660 = 1.803 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight for Ship Design:** GRP at 1.80 g/cm³ is **3.3× lighter than mild steel (7.85 g/cm³)** while still providing 34 GPa stiffness. A 100 m GRP patrol boat hull can weigh 50–60 % less than the equivalent steel hull, dramatically increasing fuel efficiency and payload.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Problem — Fox Equation for a Polymer Blend

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Q:** A shipboard damping pad is made by blending 60 wt% PU (Tg₁ = −40 °C) and 40 wt% epoxy (Tg₂ = 100 °C). Find the Tg of the blend in °C.

**Step 1 — Convert Tg to Kelvin:**

$$T_{g,1} = -40 + 273 = 233 \text{ K}$$
$$T_{g,2} = 100 + 273 = 373 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Fox equation:**

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \frac{w_2}{T_{g,2}} = \frac{0.60}{233} + \frac{0.40}{373}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = 0.002575 + 0.001072 = 0.003647 \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $T_g$ in Kelvin, then convert to °C:**

$$T_g = \frac{1}{0.003647} = 274.2 \text{ K}$$

$$T_g = 274.2 - 273 = 1.2 \text{ °C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The blend Tg is dominated by the higher Tg component (epoxy). To get a damping pad that works at room temperature, the Tg of the blend must be **near 25 °C**, which requires either a softer epoxy or higher PU content.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are designed to mirror the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern for Module 3, with the standard mark distribution: **Part A = 3 marks (short answer)**, **Part B = 14 marks (full descriptive, internal choice)**.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
**Differentiate between thermoplastic, thermosetting plastic, and elastomer with one shipbuilding example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Thermoplastic | Thermoset | Elastomer |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bond type | Weak secondary bonds between chains | Strong covalent crosslinks form 3-D network | Light crosslinks, coiled chains |
| Thermal response | Softens on heating, re-processable | Irreversibly hardens; chars on heating | Stretches elastically; does not melt |
| Shipbuilding example | HDPE ballast pipe | Epoxy hull coating | Neoprene fender cover |

*(Full marks require 3 points of difference + 1 example each. Award 3 marks for correct table.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**State the Carothers equation. What is the degree of polymerization at 98 % conversion?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Carothers' equation for step-growth polymerization is:

$$\overline{DP_n} = \frac{1}{1 - p}$$

At $p = 0.98$:

$$\overline{DP_n} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.98} = \frac{1}{0.02} = 50$$

**Hence the degree of polymerization at 98 % conversion is 50.** (1 mark for equation, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for correct numerical answer.)

---

## PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the mechanism of addition (chain-growth) polymerization with neat sketches of the three stages. Why is high pressure required for the industrial production of low-density polyethylene (LDPE)?

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare the properties and shipbuilding uses of HDPE, PP, and UHMWPE.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

**Mechanism of Addition Polymerization (7 marks):**

Addition polymerization proceeds via three distinct stages, each governed by free-radical chemistry.

**Stage 1 — Initiation (2 marks)**

- An **initiator** (e.g., benzoyl peroxide, azobisisobutyronitrile AIBN) decomposes under heat or UV to give **two free radicals**.

$$\text{(R-O-O-R)} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \, R-O^{\bullet}$$

- Each radical attacks the C=C double bond of a monomer, forming a new radical — the **active centre**.

$$R^{\bullet} + CH_2=CH_2 \rightarrow R-CH_2-CH_2^{\bullet}$$

**Stage 2 — Propagation (2 marks)**

- The active centre attacks the next monomer in rapid succession, with the radical site migrating to the chain end. A single chain can add **10,000 monomers in <1 second**.

$$R-(CH_2-CH_2)_n^{\bullet} + CH_2=CH_2 \rightarrow R-(CH_2-CH_2)_{n+1}^{\bullet}$$

- The chain grows only at the **reactive end**; the rest of the chain is inert.

**Stage 3 — Termination (2 marks)**

- **Coupling:** Two growing radical chains combine head-to-head, forming one dead chain.

$$R-\sim^{\bullet} + ^{\bullet}\sim-R \rightarrow R-\sim-\sim-R$$

- **Disproportionation:** One chain abstracts a hydrogen atom from another, giving a saturated chain and an unsaturated chain (no change in number of chains but molecular weight changes).

**Why High Pressure for LDPE (1 mark):**

LDPE is produced at pressures of **1000–3000 atm** and temperatures of **200–300 °C** because the free-radical polymerization at high pressure favours **chain transfer to polymer**, which creates **long-chain branches**. These branches prevent close packing, lowering density (0.91–0.93 g/cm³) and crystallinity (≈ 50 %), and giving LDPE its characteristic **flexibility and transparency** in thin films.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

**Comparison of HDPE, PP, and UHMWPE (7 marks):**

| Property | HDPE | PP (Isotactic) | UHMWPE |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density (g/cm³) | 0.94–0.97 | 0.90–0.91 | 0.93–0.94 |
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 25–40 | 30–40 | 40 (yield) |
| Melting Point (°C) | 135 | 165 | 135 (with high melt viscosity) |
| Degree of Polymerization | ~ 10⁵ | ~ 3 × 10⁴ | ~ 10⁶ |
| Crystallinity | 70–80 % | 50–60 % | 40–50 % |
| Moisture Absorption | Nil | Nil | Nil |
| Chemical Resistance | Excellent to acids, alkalis, salt | Excellent | Excellent |
| Key Distinguishing Trait | High strength + easy processing | Lowest density, high Tm | Highest abrasion resistance (15× steel) |
| **Shipboard Use** | Ballast pipes, fuel tanks, liner | Mooring rope, geotextile, filters | Bearing liners, dock fenders, chute linings |

**Distinguishing property (1 mark each):** HDPE is the work-horse marine pipe polymer because of its combination of stiffness, weldability, and zero moisture absorption. PP has the **lowest density of any commodity plastic** (floats on water) and the **highest Tm**, making it ideal for ropes that pass over sheaves in tropical harbours. UHMWPE has **M_w = 3–6 million g/mol** (hence "ultra-high"), giving it the **highest abrasion resistance** of any polymer — it is used where steel wears out (chute linings, dock-fender bearing pads).

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With chemical structure and mechanism, explain the formation of **Nylon 6,6** by interfacial polycondensation. List four key properties that make Nylon 6,6 suitable for ship mooring ropes.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the term **glass transition temperature (Tg)**. How is Tg determined experimentally? How does plasticizer addition affect Tg, and why is this important for PVC shipboard cable insulation?

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

**Formation of Nylon 6,6 (7 marks):**

- **Monomers:** Hexamethylenediamine (H₂N-(CH₂)₆-NH₂) and adipic acid (HOOC-(CH₂)₄-COOH). Both are difunctional.
- **Reaction type:** Step-growth (condensation) polycondensation with elimination of water.

**Mechanism (3 marks):**

The amine -NH₂ of hexamethylenediamine attacks the carboxyl carbon of adipic acid (acid-base + nucleophilic acyl substitution), forming an **amide (-CO-NH-)** linkage and releasing a water molecule.

$$H_2N-(CH_2)_6-NH_2 + HOOC-(CH_2)_4-COOH \rightarrow H_2N-(CH_2)_6-NH-CO-(CH_2)_4-COOH + H_2O$$

The new chain has an amine at one end and a carboxyl at the other, so it can react further with another monomer or another chain. As $p \to 0.995$, $\overline{DP_n} \to 200$ (fibre-grade).

**Interfacial Polycondensation (2 marks):**

At the **boundary** between an aqueous phase (containing the diamine) and an **organic phase (e.g., hexane or CCl₄, containing the diacid chloride adipoyl chloride instead of adipic acid)** the reaction occurs at the interface, instantly producing a thin film of nylon that can be continuously withdrawn. This technique is the **Nylon rope trick** experiment commonly shown in undergraduate labs.

**Why Nylon 6,6 is Ideal for Mooring Ropes (2 marks):**

1. **High tensile strength** (75–90 MPa) — handles ship berthing shock loads.
2. **High elongation at break** (20–300 %) — absorbs energy, prevents snap-back fatalities.
3. **Excellent abrasion resistance** — survives chafe against bollards and pier edges.
4. **Resistance to saltwater, oil, and biological attack** — long service life in marine environment.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

**Glass Transition Temperature Tg (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Tg is the temperature at which the amorphous regions of a polymer transition from a rigid, glassy state to a soft, rubbery state due to the onset of long-range segmental motion of the polymer chains. Below Tg, only vibrational motion occurs; above Tg, 10–20 monomer units ("segments") gain rotational and translational freedom.

**Experimental Determination (3 marks):**

Tg is determined by any of the following techniques, all of which show an abrupt change in property at Tg:

1. **DSC (Differential Scanning Calorimetry):** A step-change in heat capacity $C_p$ at Tg. Heating rate typically 10 °C/min.
2. **DMA (Dynamic Mechanical Analysis):** A peak in the loss modulus $E''$ and tan δ at Tg. Most sensitive method.
3. **TMA / Dilatometry:** A change in slope of thermal expansion coefficient.
4. **Refractive index vs. temperature:** n drops sharply at Tg.

**Effect of Plasticizer (2 marks):**

A plasticizer is a small, low-volatility molecule (e.g., dioctyl phthalate DOP for PVC) that intersperses between polymer chains, **increasing free volume** and lubricating chain motion. This **lowers Tg** by ~5–10 °C per 10 phr (parts per hundred resin) of plasticizer.

**Importance for PVC Cable Insulation (1 mark):**

Pure (rigid) PVC has Tg ≈ 80 °C and is hard and brittle at room temperature — unsuitable for bending around cable runs. By adding 30–50 phr of DOP plasticizer, Tg drops to **−20 to 0 °C**, making the PVC flexible enough to bend around tight corners on a ship while retaining its **flame-retardant chlorine content**. Without plasticizer, shipboard PVC cables would crack during installation and vibration.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Module 3 polymer questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Tg with Tm.** Tg exists for **all polymers** (above which amorphous regions soften). Tm exists **only for semi-crystalline polymers** (above which crystals melt). The examiner **deducts 1 full mark** for using these interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the SI unit of stress is Pa (N/m²), not MPa**, when writing equations. Always state units.
> 3. **In polymerization derivations, students often write** $\overline{DP_n} = N/N_0$ (the **reciprocal** of the correct expression). Memorize: $\overline{DP_n} = \dfrac{N_0}{N} = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$.
> 4. **No sketch = no full marks** for mechanisms (initiation/propagation/termination in Part B). Even a hand-drawn box-and-arrow diagram of a chain with active end and incoming monomer earns 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Omitting shipbuilding application.** Module 3 is part of a **shipbuilding** course. Generic polymer answers (e.g., "PVC is used for pipes") lose 1 mark; **ship-specific** answers (e.g., "uPVC for shipboard deck scupper drainage") earn the full mark.
> 6. **Forgetting the by-product** in condensation polymerization. The examiner specifically looks for "**+ H₂O**" or "**+ HCl**" on the product side. Missing it costs 0.5 mark.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final 5-minute revision before entering the exam hall — read these bullets aloud:**

- **Three master classes of polymers:** Thermoplastic (re-meltable), Thermoset (crosslinked, char on heating), Elastomer (rubbery, lightly crosslinked).
- **Fourteen polymers in Module 3:** PE (LDPE/HDPE/UHMWPE), PVC, PP, PS/EPS, ABS, Nylon 6 & 6,6, PET, PMMA, PC, PTFE, PU, Epoxy, Polyester, Phenolic — plus 4 elastomers (NR, CR, EPDM, Silicone).
- **Carothers' equation:** $\overline{DP_n} = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$. At $p=0.99$, DP = 100; at $p=0.995$, DP = 200.
- **Polydispersity Index (PDI):** $\overline{M_w}/\overline{M_n} \geq 1$. Commercial polymer PDI = 1.5–50. Monodisperse (PDI = 1) is a theoretical ideal.
- **Fox Equation for blend Tg:** $1/T_g = \sum w_i/T_{g,i}$ — **Tg must be in Kelvin** in this equation.
- **Tg/Tm ratio (Beaman-Boyer):** ≈ 0.5–0.67 (both in Kelvin).
- **Rule of Mixtures for FRP:** $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ (iso-strain, longitudinal); $1/E_c = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ (iso-stress, transverse).
- **Highest Tm thermoplastic:** PTFE (327 °C). **Lowest Tg:** PE/PTFE (~ −100 °C).
- **Lowest coefficient of friction of any solid:** PTFE. **Highest abrasion resistance of any polymer:** UHMWPE.
- **Self-extinguishing (flame-resistant) polymer:** PVC (contains 57 % chlorine by mass).
- **Light transmission > 90 %:** PMMA (92 %). **Impact strength 250× PS:** Polycarbonate.
- **Common shipboard composite:** Glass Reinforced Polyester (GRP) — E-glass fibre + unsaturated polyester + MEKP catalyst. Used in lifeboats, patrol boats, mine counter-measure vessels.
- **Creep = enemy of thermoplastics above Tg.** Below Tg, polymer is glassy and dimensionally stable.
- **Hydrolysis danger for GRP hulls:** ester groups in polyester + water → **osmosis blistering**. Mitigate with vinyl ester or epoxy resin and surface gelcoat.
- **PTFE cannot be solvent-welded or adhesive-bonded** — must be mechanically fastened or sintered. This is why it is used as a **gasket only** in shipbuilding, not as a structural joint.
- **Nylon is hygroscopic** (absorbs 1–9 % water). Wet nylon swells and loses ~ 30 % of its stiffness. Account for this in mooring-rope load calculations.
- **Epoxy vs. Polyester matrix:** Epoxy is stronger, lower shrinkage, more chemical-resistant, more expensive. Polyester is cheaper, easier to lay up, used for 80 % of GRP boat hulls.
- **Naval classification of shipboard polymers:** must satisfy **NAVSEA / DNV / Lloyd's Register** flammability, smoke, and toxicity (FST) standards — e.g., **Lloyd's Register FTP Code Part 2** for surface flammability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LAST-MINUTE MNEMONIC:** "**The Hungry Sailor Prefers Crispy Pancakes**" → **T**hermoplastic, **H**eat-softening, **S**econdary bonds; **P**olymer chains; **C**an **P**rocess repeatedly. This is sufficient to lock the thermoplastic vs. thermoset distinction in long-term memory.

---

**END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — POLYMERS IN SHIPBUILDING**
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme • Outcome-Based Education • NEP 2020 Aligned • Bloom's Levels: Remember → Understand → Apply → Analyze*

topics.title: Fabrication of Plastics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fabrication of Plastics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fabrication of Plastics** refers to the comprehensive set of manufacturing processes by which raw polymeric materials (resins, thermoplastics, thermosets, elastomers, and fiber-reinforced composites) are shaped, molded, extruded, cast, or otherwise converted into finished or semi-finished engineering components. In the context of **shipbuilding (PENST523)**, plastic fabrication primarily deals with **Fiber-Reinforced Plastics (FRP)** / **Glass-Reinforced Plastics (GRP)** used for hulls, superstructures, decks, bulkheads, masts, radomes, pipes, and interior outfitting — components where the superior **strength-to-weight ratio, corrosion resistance in marine environments, and design flexibility** of plastics offer decisive advantages over traditional ferrous metals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> *Fabrication of plastics encompasses molding (compression, injection, transfer, blow), forming (thermoforming, calendering), casting, laminating, extrusion, and composite lay-up techniques used to convert polymer matrices and reinforcements into marine-grade structural products.*

## 1.2 Classification of Plastic Fabrication Processes

The fabrication universe can be systematically classified by the **state of the polymer** during processing and the **mechanism of shape-giving**:

### A. By Polymer State at Processing
| State | Process Family | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Solid-state forming** | Machining, bending, stamping | Sheet forming |
| **Melt-state forming** | Injection, extrusion, blow, thermoforming | All thermoplastic processes |
| **Liquid-state forming** | Casting, RIM, hand lay-up, spray-up | Thermoset/Composite processes |
| **Soft-rubbery state** | Compression, transfer molding | Dough molding compound (DMC) |

### B. By Polymer Type
- **Thermoplastic Fabrication:** Reversible; melt-solidify cycle can be repeated. (Injection, Extrusion, Blow)
- **Thermoset Fabrication:** Irreversible; crosslinking cures the resin permanently. (Compression molding, Lay-up, RTM)
- **Elastomer Fabrication:** Vulcanization-based; used for seals and marine gaskets.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Play-Doh & Jelly" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy for a B.Tech Student's First Read:**
> - **Thermoplastic fabrication** is like playing with **Play-Doh or candle wax** — you heat it until soft, push it into a shape, and it *hardens back* on cooling. Melt it again, and it works just as well! (e.g., a plastic water bucket, a toy).
> - **Thermoset fabrication** is like baking a **cake or boiling an egg** — once the egg is hard-boiled, you can never make it runny again. The chemical change is permanent. (e.g., a fiberglass boat hull).
> - **Composite fabrication** is like making **concrete with steel rebar** — the plastic resin is the "concrete paste" and the glass/carbon fibers are the "rebar" that gives the strength.
> - **Extrusion** is like squeezing **toothpaste from a tube** through a shaped nozzle — continuous profile of any cross-section.
> - **Blow molding** is like blowing up a **balloon inside a bottle-shaped mold** — air pressure pushes the soft plastic against the mold walls.

## 1.4 Significance in Marine / Shipbuilding

Modern naval architecture and commercial shipbuilding rely heavily on plastics for:
1. **Corrosion-free superstructures** (no rust, no painting, lower maintenance).
2. **Radar-transparent enclosures** (radomes made of glass-fiber composites).
3. **Lightweight masts and exhaust stacks** (FRP reduces topweight → improved stability).
4. **Marine piping** (GRP pipes for seawater, firefighting, ballast).
5. **Interior panels, doors, furniture** (fire-retardant FRP/phenolic laminates).
6. **Propeller blades & hydrodynamic fairings** (advanced composites).
7. **Buoyancy foams** (syntactic foams, expanded PVC, PUR foams in life-rafts and lifeboats).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics (Industry Standards):**
> - **Density of GRP:** 1.5 – 2.0 g/cm³ (vs. steel 7.85 g/cm³) — *weight saving up to 75%*.
> - **Tensile strength of GRP:** 200 – 1000 MPa (comparable to mild steel).
> - **Glass transition temperature (Tₘ for thermoplastics / Tg for thermosets):** Critical ceiling beyond which the polymer loses its mechanical integrity.
> - **Service temperature in marine environments:** typically -40°C to +80°C for standard polyester GRP; up to 250°C for phenolic/BMI composites.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Process Map

The fabrication of plastics branches into **four super-families** that a KTU student must internalize:

### SUPER-FAMILY 1: MOLDING PROCESSES (Closed Die)
1. **Compression Molding** — thermosets & DMC/SMC/BMC
2. **Transfer Molding** — thermosets, intricate inserts
3. **Injection Molding** — thermoplastics, high-volume
4. **Reaction Injection Molding (RIM)** — PUR, foams
5. **Blow Molding** — hollow parts (tanks, buoys)
6. **Rotational Molding** — large hollow bodies
7. **Thermoforming** — vacuum forming of sheets

### SUPER-FAMILY 2: FORMING PROCESSES (Continuous/Semi-continuous)
1. **Extrusion** — pipes, sheets, profiles
2. **Calendering** — thin films/sheets
3. **Pultrusion** — continuous FRP profiles (marine masts!)
4. **Filament Winding** — pressure vessels, pipes, masts

### SUPER-FAMILY 3: CASTING & FOAMING
1. **Liquid resin casting** — acrylic sheets (Plexiglass/PMMA portholes)
2. **Foam casting** — syntactic foam, PU foam (buoyancy)
3. **Centrifugal casting** — GRP pipes

### SUPER-FAMILY 4: COMPOSITE LAMINATION (Open Mold)
1. **Hand Lay-up** — manual, marine hulls
2. **Spray-up** — chopped fiber + resin spray
3. **Resin Transfer Molding (RTM)** — closed mold, low void
4. **Vacuum Infusion / VARTM** — marine hulls (high quality)
5. **Autoclave Molding** — aerospace/advanced composites

---

## 2.2 Process 1 — Compression Molding (The Big Squeeze)

### The "Why"
You need to make a strong, heat-resistant, large-area part from a thermoset (e.g., a phenolic electrical switchboard panel, a fiberglass cabin door, a melamine dinnerware tray). The polymer is a powder/putty/pre-preg charge.

### The "How" (Step-by-Step)
1. A **pre-measured charge** of thermoset molding compound (e.g., BMC, SMC, phenolic powder) is placed in the **lower half** of a heated matched-metal mold.
2. The mold is **closed** by a hydraulic press — the charge is **squeezed** under high pressure (10–100 MPa).
3. Under the combined action of **heat (150–200°C)** and **pressure**, the polymer flows to fill the cavity and **chemical crosslinking (curing)** begins.
4. After a **cure time** of 1–10 minutes, the part is **ejected** as a rigid, net-shape component.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Pressure?**
> Pressure (1) collapses any trapped air/voids, (2) forces resin to flow into intricate features, and (3) accelerates the cure kinetics by intimate contact with the hot mold wall.

### Governing Equation — Clamping Force
$$F_{clamp} = P_{mold} \times A_{projected}$$

Where:
- $F_{clamp}$ = required clamp tonnage (Newtons or Tons)
- $P_{mold}$ = molding pressure inside the cavity (Pa or N/mm²)
- $A_{projected}$ = projected area of the part on the parting plane (m² or mm²)

### Cure Kinetics (First-Order Approximation)
$$\alpha(t) = 1 - e^{-k t}$$

Where:
- $\alpha(t)$ = fractional conversion (degree of cure) at time $t$
- $k$ = temperature-dependent rate constant, $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$
- $E_a$ = activation energy of the curing reaction
- $A$ = pre-exponential (Arrhenius) factor
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute mold temperature (K)

---

## 2.3 Process 2 — Injection Molding (The Heart of Plastics Industry)

### The "Why"
Mass production of intricate thermoplastic parts (nylon gears, ABS housings, polypropylene ropes-clips). High volume, tight tolerances, repeatability.

### The "How" (Cyclic Sequence)
1. **Feeding:** Granular thermoplastic is fed from a hopper into a heated barrel.
2. **Melting & Plasticating:** A rotating **Archimedean screw** (or plunger) shears and melts the polymer, conveying it forward.
3. **Injection:** The screw acts as a **ram**, injecting the melt at high pressure (50–200 MPa) through a **sprue**, **runner**, and **gate** into a closed, water-cooled mold.
4. **Packing & Cooling:** Additional melt is packed in to compensate for shrinkage; the part cools below its **glass transition Tg** (for amorphous) or **crystallization temperature** (for semi-crystalline).
5. **Ejection:** The mold opens, ejector pins push the solidified part out.
6. **Cycle Repeats.**

### Governing Equations

**Screw Melt Conveying Rate (Output):**
$$Q = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} - \frac{\pi D H^3 \sin^2\phi \cdot \Delta P}{12 \eta L}$$

Where:
- $D$ = screw diameter, $H$ = channel depth, $N$ = screw RPM, $\phi$ = helix angle
- $\Delta P$ = pressure rise along the screw, $\eta$ = polymer melt viscosity, $L$ = screw length

**Shrinkage Allowance (Mold Cavity Compensation):**
$$D_{cavity} = D_{part} (1 + S)$$

Where $S$ = volumetric shrinkage (0.005–0.025 for thermoplastics, i.e., 0.5%–2.5%).

**Cycle Time:**
$$t_{cycle} = t_{inj} + t_{pack} + t_{cool} + t_{eject}$$

**Cooling Time (Simplified, for thickest wall $x$):**
$$t_{cool} \approx \frac{x^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\!\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{T_{melt} - T_{mold}}{T_{ej} - T_{mold}}\right)$$

Where $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity of the polymer, $T_{ej}$ = ejection temperature.

---

## 2.4 Process 3 — Extrusion (Continuous Toothpaste)

### The "Why"
Continuous profiles of constant cross-section: GRP pipes, PVC window frames, polyethylene sheets, thermoplastic fishing-net filaments, cable insulation.

### The "How"
1. Polymer granules enter a **hopper** → **heated barrel** with rotating **screw**.
2. Screw has **three zones**:
   - **Feed zone (solids conveying)** — frictional drag pushes pellets forward.
   - **Compression/Melting zone (transition)** — melting via conduction + shearing.
   - **Metering zone (melt pumping)** — pressurizes melt for steady flow.
3. Melt is forced through a **die** (shaped orifice).
4. The extrudate is **calibrated, cooled** (air/water), **pulled** by haul-off, and **cut**.

### Governing Equation — Flow Through Die (Newtonian Approximation)
$$Q = \frac{\pi D^4 \Delta P}{128 \eta L} \quad \text{(for a circular pipe die)}$$

For a **slit die** (sheet extrusion):
$$Q = \frac{W H^3 \Delta P}{12 \eta L}$$

Where $W$ = die width, $H$ = die gap, $L$ = die land length.

### Power Requirement of Extruder
$$P_{motor} = \frac{Q \Delta P}{\eta_{mech}}$$

### **Pultrusion — Marine Relevance**
Continuous pultrusion pulls reinforcing fibers (rovings, mats) through a **resin bath** and then a **heated die**, where curing happens. Output: continuous GRP rods, tubes, I-beams — extensively used for **masts, handrails, cable trays, and gratings** on ships.

---

## 2.5 Process 4 — Blow Molding (Balloons in a Bottle)

### The "Why"
Hollow thermoplastic parts: water tanks, marine buoys, jerry cans, floats, hollow fenders.

### The "How**
1. A **parison** (a molten plastic tube) is extruded or injection-molded.
2. The parison is clamped between two halves of a **mold**.
3. **Compressed air** is blown into the parison, inflating it against the cold mold walls.
4. The part cools, is **ejected**, and **flash** is trimmed.

### Governing Equation — Parison Inflation
$$P_{blow} = \frac{2 \sigma_{hoop} \cdot t}{R}$$

Where:
- $P_{blow}$ = required inflation pressure
- $\sigma_{hoop}$ = hoop stress in the inflated parison
- $t$ = wall thickness
- $R$ = parison radius

---

## 2.6 Process 5 — Thermoforming (Vacuum Forming)

### The "Why"
Large-area thin-walled parts from thermoplastic sheets: ABS shower stalls, PE boat deck hatches, PMMA aircraft windows, FRP shower trays.

### The "How"
1. A thermoplastic **sheet is clamped** in a frame and **heated** (IR heaters) to its **softening temperature** (above Tg).
2. The heated sheet is **draped over a mold**.
3. **Vacuum** is applied from below (vacuum forming) OR **air pressure** from above (pressure forming) pushes the sheet against the mold.
4. The sheet **cools**, is **trimmed**.

### Governing Equation — Sag Depth (Heating)
The sag depth is governed by sheet stiffness vs. gravitational loading, often simplified as:
$$y_{sag}(t) = \frac{\rho g L^4}{E h^2} \cdot f(t, T)$$

For design purposes, a simpler rule of thumb is used: vacuum-forming pressure (~0.1 MPa atmospheric) is generally adequate when the polymer is heated **~20–40°C above its Tg**.

---

## 2.7 Process 6 — Casting of Plastics

### The "Why"
Large, low-stress, optically clear parts: PMMA (acrylic) portholes, clear sight glasses, polyurethane encapsulations.

### The "How**
1. Liquid monomer + initiator (e.g., methyl methacrylate + AIBN, or epoxy + amine hardener) is poured into a mold.
2. **Curing** proceeds in-situ (room temperature or oven-assisted).
3. Part is demolded.

**Critical for marine use:** Cast acrylic has excellent **transparency** (92% light transmission) and **UV resistance** — used for submarine viewports and bridge windows.

---

## 2.8 Process 7 — Composite Fabrication (The Heart of Marine Plastic Fabrication)

This is the **most KTU-relevant section** for Module 3 of PENST523. Ships, boats, and submarines use FRP for hulls; this demands mastery of these techniques.

### A. Hand Lay-up (Open Mold, Manual)
1. **Gel coat** (pigmented resin) is sprayed/painted on the mold surface for a smooth, weather-resistant outer skin.
2. **Reinforcement** (woven roving, chopped strand mat) is placed manually.
3. **Resin** (polyester or vinylester + catalyst MEKP) is brushed or rolled.
4. Layers are built up; **rolling** consolidates and removes air.
5. Cure (exothermic); demold; trim.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why hand lay-up dominates shipbuilding?**
> - Low tooling cost (wood/fiberglass master + plaster mold).
> - Suits very large parts (30 m+ hulls).
> - Tolerates design changes.
> - 70% of the world's FRP boats <30 m are made by hand lay-up.

### B. Spray-up
- Chopped fibers (10–40 mm) + resin are **simultaneously sprayed** from a dual-nozzle gun onto the mold.
- Faster than hand lay-up; lower fiber volume fraction (≈20–30%).

### C. Resin Transfer Molding (RTM) / VARTM
1. Dry reinforcement is placed in a **closed mold**.
2. Resin is **injected** under low pressure (RTM) or drawn by **vacuum** (VARTM, Vacuum-Assisted Resin Transfer Molding).
3. Cure; demold.

**Darcy's Law for Resin Flow in a Porous Medium:**
$$v = -\frac{K}{\mu} \nabla P$$

$$Q = \frac{K \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$

Where:
- $v$ = superficial velocity, $Q$ = volumetric flow rate
- $K$ = permeability of the fiber preform (m²)
- $\mu$ = resin viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\nabla P$ = pressure gradient
- $A$ = flow cross-section, $L$ = flow length

**Filling Time:**
$$t_{fill} = \frac{\mu L^2}{2 K \Delta P}$$

### D. Filament Winding
- Continuous fiber rovings are passed through a **resin bath** and wound on a rotating mandrel under controlled tension and winding angles.
- Produces **pipework, pressure vessels, missile motor casings, and marine propeller shrouds**.
- **Helical winding angle α** affects burst pressure and stiffness.
- **Maximum burst pressure** for a filament-wound pipe (thin-walled, netting analysis):
$$P_{burst} = \frac{2 N \cdot t_f \cdot \sigma_f}{\pi R^2 \cdot \cos^2\alpha}$$

Where $N$ = number of rovings, $t_f$ = fiber thickness, $\sigma_f$ = fiber tensile strength, $R$ = pipe radius, $\alpha$ = winding angle.

### E. Pultrusion (revisited for completeness)
Continuous FRP profile — pull rovings through resin bath → heated die → cured profile → cut.

---

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet (Rapid-Revision Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Clamping force in compression/injection molding | $F_{clamp} = P_{mold} \cdot A_{projected}$ | $F$ in N, $P$ in Pa, $A$ in m² |
| 2 | Cure kinetics (first-order) | $\alpha(t) = 1 - e^{-k t}$, with $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ | $\alpha$ dimensionless, $t$ in s, $E_a$ in J/mol, $R$ = **8.314 J/mol·K** |
| 3 | Screw output (simplified) | $Q = \tfrac{\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} - \tfrac{\pi D H^3 \sin^2\phi \cdot \Delta P}{12 \eta L}$ | $D, H, L$ in m, $N$ in rev/s, $\eta$ in Pa·s, $Q$ in m³/s |
| 4 | Mold cavity size (shrinkage) | $D_{cavity} = D_{part}(1+S)$ | $S$ = shrinkage (typ. 0.005–0.025) |
| 5 | Pipe-die flow (Newtonian) | $Q = \tfrac{\pi D^4 \Delta P}{128 \eta L}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille, $Q$ in m³/s |
| 6 | Slit-die flow | $Q = \tfrac{W H^3 \Delta P}{12 \eta L}$ | Sheet extrusion, $W, H, L$ in m |
| 7 | Blow-molding pressure | $P_{blow} = \tfrac{2 \sigma_{hoop} t}{R}$ | Membrane equilibrium |
| 8 | Darcy's law in RTM | $Q = \tfrac{K A \Delta P}{\mu L}$ | $K$ in m², $\mu$ in Pa·s |
| 9 | RTM fill time | $t_{fill} = \tfrac{\mu L^2}{2 K \Delta P}$ | $t$ in s |
| 10 | Filament winding burst pressure | $P_{burst} = \tfrac{2 N t_f \sigma_f}{\pi R^2 \cos^2\alpha}$ | Thin-wall netting |
| 11 | Glass transition rule of thumb | $T_g \approx \tfrac{2}{3} T_m$ (in Kelvin) | For polymers (Beaman rule) |
| 12 | Composite rule of mixtures (longitudinal) | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | $V_f + V_m = 1$ |
| 13 | Composite rule of mixtures (transverse) | $\tfrac{1}{E_c} = \tfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \tfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Inverse rule |
| 14 | Density of composite | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Weight-critical marine designs |

## 2.10 Real-World Marine Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Where each process shows up in a real ship:**
> 1. **Hull of a 25 m patrol boat** — Hand lay-up / vacuum infusion of glass/polyester.
> 2. **Radar mast of a frigate** — Filament-wound carbon/epoxy (stealth, non-magnetic).
> 3. **Pipework for seawater cooling** — Centrifugally cast GRP or pultruded GRP.
> 4. **Porthole windows** — Cast PMMA (plexiglass) — 60 mm thick, hydrostatically tested.
> 5. **Helicopter deck overlay** — Phenolic/glass laminate (fire-retardant, FST-compliant).
> 6. **Life-raft buoyancy** — Rotationally molded LDPE shell + PU foam core.
> 7. **Ship fenders** — Extruded EPDM rubber; large rubber fenders may be compression-molded.
> 8. **Cable trays, gratings, ladders** — Pultruded FRP profiles.
> 9. **Radar dome (radome)** — RTM or autoclave-cured glass/epoxy.
> 10. **Internal doors & cabin linings** — SMC compression molded, fire-retardant phenolic.
