# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constituent Phases of a Composite

A marine composite consists of three structural levels:

1. **Matrix (Binder Phase)**
   - Transfers load to reinforcement.
   - Protects reinforcement from moisture, UV, and impact.
   - Marine matrices: **Polyester, Vinyl Ester, Epoxy, Phenolic.**

2. **Reinforcement (Load-Bearing Phase)**
   - Carries the principal mechanical load.
   - Marine reinforcements: **E-glass, S-glass, Carbon (PAN-based), Aramid (Kevlar®), Basalt.**

3. **Interphase (Interface / Interphase Region)**
   - The "glue zone" between matrix and fiber.
   - Governed by **surface treatment** (sizing, coupling agents like silanes).
   - Determines **interlaminar shear strength (ILSS)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy for Interphase:** The interphase is like the **adhesive layer between wall paint and plaster**. Without a good primer (sizing), the paint peels off. Without a good interphase, the fiber "pulls out" of the matrix under load — called **fiber pull-out failure**.

---

## 2.2 Polymer Matrices for Marine Composites

| **Matrix** | **Tg (°C)** | **Mechanical Performance** | **Marine Use** | **Cost (Relative)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Polyester (Orthophthalic)** | 70 – 90 | Moderate | Small boats, dinghies | **1× (baseline)** |
| **Polyester (Isophthalic)** | 80 – 100 | Good | Hulls, decks | 1.2× |
| **Vinyl Ester** | 100 – 130 | Excellent (tough, chemical-resistant) | Naval vessels, MCMV | 1.8× |
| **Epoxy (DGEBA)** | 120 – 180 | Superior (high strength, low shrinkage) | Racing yachts, military craft | 2.5× |
| **Phenolic** | >250 | Fire-resistant, low smoke | Submarine interiors, fire zones | 2.0× |

---

## 2.3 Fiber Reinforcements for Marine Composites

| **Fiber** | **Density (kg/m³)** | **Tensile Strength (MPa)** | **Tensile Modulus (GPa)** | **Marine Use** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **E-Glass** | 2,540 | 2,000 – 3,500 | 70 – 85 | **Hulls, decks (workhorse)** |
| **S-Glass** | 2,490 | 4,000 – 4,800 | 85 – 95 | High-performance hulls |
| **Carbon (HS)** | 1,800 | 3,500 – 5,000 | 230 – 250 | Masts, propellers, superstructures |
| **Carbon (HM)** | 1,850 | 2,500 – 3,500 | 350 – 500 | Stiff panels, shafts |
| **Aramid (Kevlar 49)** | 1,440 | 2,800 – 3,600 | 70 – 130 | Ballistic protection, impact-prone zones |
| **Basalt** | 2,650 | 2,800 – 4,000 | 80 – 95 | Emerging — fire & corrosion resistant |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for any KTU Part A (3-mark) or Part B (14-mark) question.**

| **#** | **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_f + V_m = 1$ | Volume fraction relation (fiber + matrix = 1) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $W_f + W_m = 1$ | Weight fraction relation | dimensionless |
| 3 | $V_f = \dfrac{\rho_c - \rho_m}{\rho_f - \rho_m}$ | Volume fraction from densities | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | **Rule of mixtures — Density** | kg/m³ |
| 5 | $E_c \parallel = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | **Rule of mixtures — Longitudinal Modulus (Voigt)** | GPa |
| 6 | $\dfrac{1}{E_c \perp} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | **Inverse rule of mixtures — Transverse Modulus (Reuss)** | 1/GPa |
| 7 | $\sigma_c \parallel = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | Rule of mixtures — Longitudinal Strength | MPa |
| 8 | $\sigma_c \perp \approx \sigma_m \left( 1 - 2\sqrt{\dfrac{V_f}{\pi}} \right)$ | Strength transverse to fibers (≈ for square packing) | MPa |
| 9 | $S_s = \dfrac{\sigma}{\rho}$ | **Specific Strength** | kN·m/kg or Pa·m³/kg |
| 10 | $M_s = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$ | **Specific Modulus** | MN·m/kg or Pa·m³/kg |
| 11 | $N = \dfrac{E_f}{E_m}$ | **Modular ratio** (governs load sharing) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\eta_l = \dfrac{\sigma_f}{\sigma_c} = \dfrac{E_f / E_c}{1 + (N - 1) V_f}$ | Load-sharing parameter (iso-strain, parallel loading) | dimensionless |
| 13 | $F_{buoyancy} = \rho_{sw} \, g \, V_{disp}$ | Buoyancy (used to compare composite hulls) | N |
| 14 | $\text{Weight Savings} = \left( 1 - \dfrac{\rho_c}{\rho_{steel}} \right) \times 100\%$ | Hull weight savings vs. steel | % |

Where:
- Subscript $c$ = composite, $f$ = fiber, $m$ = matrix.
- $V$ = volume fraction, $W$ = weight fraction, $\rho$ = density, $E$ = Young's modulus, $\sigma$ = strength.
- $\rho_{sw}$ ≈ **1,025 kg/m³** (standard seawater), $g$ ≈ **9.81 m/s²**.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility in Shipbuilding

> [!TIP]
> **Why a marine engineer must master these formulas:**
> - **Hull weight estimation** uses $\rho_c$ from the rule of mixtures to compute displacement.
> - **Stiffness-critical structures** (masts, propeller shafts) require $E_c \parallel$ — longitudinal modulus dominates.
> - **Cost-performance optimization** balances $V_f$ (more fiber = stronger but more expensive).
> - **Classification society rules** (Lloyd's, DNV, ABS) use $S_s$ and $M_s$ to set minimum scantlings for composite hulls.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Rule of Mixtures for Composite Density

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most fundamental equation** in composite mechanics and frequently appears in KTU exams.

### Given

A composite of total volume $V_c$ contains fiber volume $V_f$ and matrix volume $V_m$.

$$V_c = V_f + V_m$$

### Step 1 — Define Mass of the Composite

$$m_c = m_f + m_m$$

This expresses the conservation of mass — the composite mass equals the sum of the masses of its constituents.

### Step 2 — Substitute Using Density

Recall that mass = density × volume: $m = \rho V$.

$$\rho_c V_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$

### Step 3 — Introduce Volume Fractions

Define the volume fractions:

$$v_f = \frac{V_f}{V_c}, \quad v_m = \frac{V_m}{V_c}$$

Substitute $V_f = v_f V_c$ and $V_m = v_m V_c$ into the mass equation:

$$\rho_c V_c = \rho_f (v_f V_c) + \rho_m (v_m V_c)$$

### Step 4 — Cancel $V_c$ (the common volume)

$$\rho_c = \rho_f v_f + \rho_m v_m$$

### Step 5 — Use the Constraint $v_f + v_m = 1$ to Simplify

Substitute $v_m = 1 - v_f$:

$$\rho_c = \rho_f v_f + \rho_m (1 - v_f)$$

$$\rho_c = \rho_m + v_f (\rho_f - \rho_m)$$

### Final Boxed Result

$$\boxed{\rho_c = \rho_f v_f + \rho_m (1 - v_f) = \rho_m + v_f (\rho_f - \rho_m)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Rule of Mixtures for Longitudinal Modulus (Voigt Model)

### Given — Iso-Strain Assumption (Parallel Loading)

When a unidirectional composite is loaded **parallel to the fibers**, both fiber and matrix experience the **same strain** $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$.

### Step 1 — Force Equilibrium

The total force on the composite equals the sum of forces carried by each phase:

$$F_c = F_f + F_m$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $F = \sigma A$

$$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Iso-Strain Condition ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$)

$$E_c \varepsilon A_c = E_f \varepsilon A_f + E_m \varepsilon A_m$$

The common $\varepsilon$ cancels:

$$E_c A_c = E_f A_f + E_m A_m$$

### Step 4 — Divide by $A_c$ and Use Area Fractions = Volume Fractions (for aligned fibers)

$$\frac{A_f}{A_c} = v_f, \quad \frac{A_m}{A_c} = v_m$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{E_c \parallel = E_f v_f + E_m v_m = E_m + v_f (E_f - E_m)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Because both phases strain equally, the **stiffer phase carries more load** (since $\sigma = E\varepsilon$). Carbon fibers ($E_f$ ≈ 230 GPa) carry roughly **5–8× the load of the epoxy matrix** ($E_m$ ≈ 3 GPa) at typical $v_f$ ≈ 0.5.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Solved Model Question)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question:** A marine GRP hull is made of E-glass fibers ($E_f = 72$ GPa, $\rho_f = 2,540$ kg/m³) in a polyester matrix ($E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\rho_m = 1,200$ kg/m³) with fiber volume fraction $v_f = 0.45$.
>
> Calculate:
> (a) The density of the composite.
> (b) The longitudinal Young's modulus.
> (c) The specific modulus and the percentage weight saving compared to a mild steel hull ($\rho_{steel} = 7,850$ kg/m³, $E_{steel} = 210$ GPa).

### Solution

**Part (a) — Density:**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f v_f + \rho_m (1 - v_f)$$

$$\rho_c = (2{,}540)(0.45) + (1{,}200)(0.55)$$

$$\rho_c = 1{,}143 + 660 = 1{,}803 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Part (b) — Longitudinal Modulus:**

$$E_c \parallel = E_f v_f + E_m (1 - v_f)$$

$$E_c \parallel = (72)(0.45) + (3.5)(0.55)$$

$$E_c \parallel = 32.4 + 1.925 = 34.325 \text{ GPa}$$

**Part (c) — Specific Modulus and Weight Saving:**

$$M_s^{composite} = \frac{E_c \parallel}{\rho_c} = \frac{34.325 \times 10^9}{1{,}803}$$

$$M_s^{composite} = 19.04 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa·m}^3/\text{kg} = 19.04 \text{ MN·m/kg}$$

$$M_s^{steel} = \frac{210 \times 10^9}{7{,}850} = 26.75 \text{ MN·m/kg}$$

$$\text{Weight Saving} = \left(1 - \frac{1{,}803}{7{,}850}\right) \times 100\% = 77.0\%$$

### Analytical Summary Table

| **Parameter** | **GRP Composite** | **Mild Steel** | **Ratio (C/S)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density (kg/m³) | **1,803** | 7,850 | 0.23 |
| Modulus (GPa) | **34.3** | 210 | 0.16 |
| Specific Modulus (MN·m/kg) | **19.0** | 26.8 | 0.71 |
| Weight Saving | **77 %** | 0 % | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Although the composite has a lower specific modulus than steel in this case, the **77 % weight reduction** is decisive for **speed, payload, fuel economy, and corrosion-free operation** — which is why GRP is the **default material for small-to-medium vessels** worldwide.

```mermaid
graph TD
    T1[TOP SKIN CFRP 2 mm]:::skin

    class T1,B1 skin
    class C1 core
    class L1 load
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note on Sandwich Construction:** The two thin, stiff CFRP skins are separated by a thick, lightweight foam core. This produces a **second moment of area $I$** that is **orders of magnitude higher** than a solid laminate of the same mass — the same principle as an **I-beam** in steel shipbuilding.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Style]**
**Define composite material. List any four advantages of using composites in shipbuilding.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A composite material is a heterogeneous material composed of two or more distinct phases — a **matrix** (binder) and a **reinforcement** — combined macroscopically to provide properties superior to those of the individual constituents.

**Four Advantages:**
1. **High strength-to-weight ratio** → lighter hulls, higher speed/payload.
2. **Excellent corrosion resistance** → no rusting in seawater; reduced maintenance.
3. **Non-magnetic signature** → suitable for mine countermeasure vessels (MCMV).
4. **Design flexibility** → complex hull forms (e.g., deep-V planing hulls) achievable without welding or forming.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do NOT write "composite = mixture" or "alloy." Composites are **heterogeneous at the macroscopic scale**; alloys are **homogeneous at the atomic scale**. This distinction carries 1 mark.

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023 Style]**
**Differentiate between FRP and sandwich composites used in marine applications.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Property** | **FRP (Fiber Reinforced Plastic)** | **Sandwich Composite** |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Single layer of fibers in matrix | Two stiff skins + lightweight core |
| **Typical Use** | Hull shells, decks, masts | Bulkheads, superstructure panels, deckhouses |
| **Stiffness Mechanism** | Fiber stiffness ($E_f$) | Geometric: large $I$ (second moment of area) |
| **Weight** | Higher (solid laminate) | Very low (hollow-core effect) |
| **Example** | GRP patrol boat hull | CFRP-skinned PVC foam superstructure |

---

## Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)
> **Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the two alternatives.**

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **constituents of a fiber-reinforced composite** and the role of the **interphase**.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A marine GRP laminate is made of **E-glass fibers** ($E_f = 70$ GPa, $\rho_f = 2,550$ kg/m³) and **polyester resin** ($E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\rho_m = 1,250$ kg/m³) with fiber volume fraction $v_f = 0.50$. Calculate the **density, longitudinal modulus, and specific modulus** of the composite. Comment on the suitability of this material for a **hull of a 25 m patrol boat**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

A fiber-reinforced composite consists of:

1. **Matrix (continuous phase):** Provides shape, transfers load to fibers, protects fibers from environment.
   - *Marine example:* Unsaturated polyester, vinyl ester, epoxy.

2. **Reinforcement (discontinuous/continuous phase):** Carries the principal mechanical load.
   - *Marine example:* E-glass, S-glass, carbon, aramid.

3. **Interphase (the interface region):** A thin zone (~µm) where the matrix molecularly bonds to the fiber surface via **sizing** (e.g., silane coupling agents).

**Role of the interphase:**
- Transfers **stress** from the (weak) matrix to the (strong) fiber via shear.
- Prevents **fiber pull-out** under load.
- Blocks moisture ingress at the fiber surface (critical in marine environments).
- Determines **interlaminar shear strength (ILSS)**.

**Sketch description (refer Section 4.2 diagram above):**
- Show fiber as a cylinder, matrix as the surrounding block, interphase as a thin annular layer (1–2 µm thick) at the boundary.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) — Numerical

**Step 1: Density of composite (Rule of Mixtures):**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f v_f + \rho_m (1 - v_f)$$

$$\rho_c = (2{,}550)(0.50) + (1{,}250)(0.50) = 1{,}275 + 625$$

$$\rho_c = 1{,}900 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2: Longitudinal modulus (Voigt Rule):**

$$E_c \parallel = E_f v_f + E_m (1 - v_f)$$

$$E_c \parallel = (70)(0.50) + (3.5)(0.50) = 35 + 1.75$$

$$E_c \parallel = 36.75 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 3: Specific modulus:**

$$M_s = \frac{E_c \parallel}{\rho_c} = \frac{36.75 \times 10^9}{1{,}900} = 19.34 \text{ MN·m/kg}$$

**Step 4: Suitability for 25 m patrol boat hull:**

| **Criterion** | **Assessment** |
|---|---|
| Weight (vs. steel at 7,850 kg/m³) | **76 % lighter** — major advantage |
| Corrosion in seawater | **Excellent** — no rusting, no painting required |
| Stiffness (vs. steel at 210 GPa) | Lower absolute modulus, but adequate for hulls <30 m when sandwich panels are used |
| Manufacturability | **Suitable** — hand layup or vacuum infusion for 25 m hull |
| Class approval (Lloyd's / IRS) | **Approved** for GRP patrol craft up to ~50 m |

**Verdict:** The material is **highly suitable** for a 25 m patrol boat hull — lightweight, corrosion-free, and class-approved. To compensate for lower stiffness, **sandwich construction with foam core** is recommended for deck and superstructure.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **Rule of Mixtures** for composite **density** and **longitudinal modulus**. State the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship propeller blade is made of **carbon fiber–epoxy composite** with $v_f = 0.55$. Carbon fiber properties: $E_f = 230$ GPa, $\rho_f = 1,800$ kg/m³. Epoxy: $E_m = 3.0$ GPa, $\rho_m = 1,200$ kg/m³. Compare the **weight of a CFRP blade** to an **aluminum bronze blade** ($\rho_{AlB} = 7,650$ kg/m³) of the **same volume (0.025 m³)**, and compute the **percentage weight saving**. Also compute the **longitudinal modulus** of the CFRP blade. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Rule of Mixtures for Density (derived in Section 3.1 above):**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f v_f + \rho_m v_m = \rho_f v_f + \rho_m (1 - v_f)$$

**Rule of Mixtures for Longitudinal Modulus (Voigt, derived in Section 3.2 above):**

$$E_c \parallel = E_f v_f + E_m v_m = E_f v_f + E_m (1 - v_f)$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Fibers are **continuous, parallel, and uniformly distributed.**
2. **Perfect bonding** at the fiber–matrix interface (no slip, no voids).
3. Both phases experience the **same strain** (iso-strain / parallel loading).
4. The composite is **macroscopically homogeneous** and **defect-free**.
5. The fibers and matrix are **linearly elastic** and **isotropic** individually.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) — Numerical

**Step 1: CFRP Composite Density:**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f v_f + \rho_m (1 - v_f)$$

$$\rho_c = (1{,}800)(0.55) + (1{,}200)(0.45) = 990 + 540$$

$$\rho_c = 1{,}530 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2: Mass of CFRP Blade:**

$$m_{CFRP} = \rho_c \times V = 1{,}530 \times 0.025 = 38.25 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3: Mass of Aluminum Bronze Blade:**

$$m_{AlB} = 7{,}650 \times 0.025 = 191.25 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4: Percentage Weight Saving:**

$$\text{Saving} = \left(1 - \frac{38.25}{191.25}\right) \times 100\% = 80.0\%$$

**Step 5: Longitudinal Modulus of CFRP Blade:**

$$E_c \parallel = E_f v_f + E_m (1 - v_f)$$

$$E_c \parallel = (230)(0.55) + (3.0)(0.45) = 126.5 + 1.35$$

$$E_c \parallel = 127.85 \text{ GPa}$$

**Summary:**

| **Parameter** | **CFRP Blade** | **Al Bronze Blade** |
|---|---|---|
| Mass (kg) | **38.25** | 191.25 |
| Modulus (GPa) | **127.85** | ~110 (aluminum bronze) |
| Weight Saving | **80 %** | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** CFRP propeller blades are now used on **high-speed ferries and naval craft** where the 80 % weight reduction enables **larger blade diameters** (for higher thrust) without overloading the shaft bearings.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the rule of mixtures direction** — Use $E_c \parallel$ (Voigt, +) for **longitudinal** loading, and the **inverse rule (Reuss, 1/E)** for **transverse** loading. Mixing them up = 0 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the constraint** $v_f + v_m = 1$ — When $v_f$ is given, $v_m$ is **not** "the rest of the volume" in random quantities; it is $v_m = 1 - v_f$.
> 3. **Confusing weight fraction and volume fraction** — They are **NOT** equal. Use the density conversion if only weight fractions are given.
> 4. **Writing "composite = alloy"** — Lose 1 mark. Always emphasize **heterogeneous vs. homogeneous**.
> 5. **Skipping units in the final answer** — Examiner deducts 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 6. **In Part (a) sketch questions**, students often forget to **label the interphase** — this is a frequent 2-mark loss.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for VIVA / ESE / Internal Assessment:**

- **Composite = Matrix + Reinforcement + Interphase** (all three are mandatory for full marks).
- **Rule of mixtures — Density:** $\rho_c = \rho_f v_f + \rho_m (1 - v_f)$.
- **Rule of mixtures — Longitudinal modulus:** $E_c \parallel = E_f v_f + E_m (1 - v_f)$.
- **Inverse rule — Transverse modulus:** $\dfrac{1}{E_c \perp} = \dfrac{v_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{v_m}{E_m}$.
- **Constraint:** $v_f + v_m = 1$ (always).
- **Specific strength $S_s$ and specific modulus $M_s$** are the key marine performance metrics — not absolute strength/modulus.
- **E-glass + polyester** = standard marine GRP (workhorse).
- **Carbon + epoxy** = high-performance (masts, propellers, superstructures).
- **Vinyl ester** is preferred over polyester for **naval / military** vessels (better toughness + chemical resistance).
- **Sandwich construction** (CFRP skins + foam core) provides high bending stiffness at low weight — used in superstructures, bulkheads, and racing yacht hulls.
- **Vacuum infusion (VARTM)** is the preferred modern manufacturing process for **naval MCMV hulls** (low void content, high fiber volume fraction, repeatable quality).
- **Classification society rules:** Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, IRS all publish specific rules for composite ship scantlings — these govern allowable $v_f$, laminate stacking sequences, and minimum mechanical properties.
- **Common applications in shipbuilding:** hulls, decks, bulkheads, superstructures, masts, propeller blades, rudders, shaft brackets, interior panels, sonar domes, and crew shelters.
- **Stealth & defense applications:** MCMV hulls, submarine acoustic tiles, radar-absorbing structures — all rely on composites.
- **Key word triggers for theory questions:** "iso-strain assumption," "Voigt model," "load transfer via interphase," "specific strength," "weight reduction," "corrosion resistance," "non-magnetic."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Difference between Thermoplastics and Thermosets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Difference between Thermoplastics and Thermosets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — POLYMERIC MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
## Topic: Difference between Thermoplastics and Thermosets

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermoplastics** are polymeric materials composed of long, linear or lightly branched molecular chains held together by weak secondary forces (Van der Waals forces, dipole-dipole interactions, or hydrogen bonds). They **soften upon heating and harden upon cooling** in a fully reversible physical change, with **no chemical reaction** occurring during the transition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermosetting plastics (thermosets)** are polymeric materials in which the molecular chains are joined together by strong **covalent crosslinks**, forming a permanent three-dimensional network. They undergo an **irreversible chemical cure** (crosslinking) when heated or catalysed, after which they **cannot be remelted or reshaped**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two culinary scenarios in a ship's galley:

**Thermoplastic = BUTTER / WAX**
You can melt butter in a pan, pour it into a mould, and let it cool to a solid. If you reheat it, it melts again. The process is **fully reversible**, and no new chemical bonds are formed. Each time you heat and cool, the butter behaves the same way. Examples on a ship: **nylon ropes, PVC pipes, HDPE fuel tanks, PTFE (Teflon) coatings**.

**Thermoset = HARD-BOILED EGG / BAKED CAKE**
Once you bake a cake, the proteins crosslink with heat, and you cannot "un-bake" it back into batter. If you try to reheat it, it simply **chars and burns** rather than melting. The chemical change is **permanent and irreversible**. Examples on a ship: **fiberglass (GRP) hulls, epoxy-coated decks, phenolic insulation, melamine laminates**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme places special emphasis on distinguishing the **thermal response** of these two classes, since it governs welding, repair, recycling, and fire-safety protocols in shipbuilding under SOLAS and IMO regulations.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (KTU 2024 Reference Values)

| Metric | Thermoplastics | Thermosets |
|---|---|---|
| Bond Type | Secondary (weak) | Covalent crosslinks (strong) |
| Density Range | **0.85 – 1.45 g/cm³** | **1.10 – 1.90 g/cm³** |
| Glass Transition (Tg) | Well-defined | Not well-defined |
| Recyclability | **High** (can be remelted) | **Nil** (cannot be remelted) |
| Crosslink Density | **0** | **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻² mol/cm³** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Molecular Architecture — The Core 'Why'

The fundamental difference between thermoplastics and thermosets lies at the **molecular architecture level**, which dictates every macroscopic property.

**Step 1 — Thermoplastic Chain Structure**
- Monomers link end-to-end in a **linear** or **lightly branched** fashion.
- Chains are independent of one another and held together only by weak **Van der Waals forces**, dipole-dipole interactions, and occasional hydrogen bonds.
- Energy required to overcome these forces: typically **5 – 40 kJ/mol**.

**Step 2 — Thermoset Network Structure**
- Monomers first form linear chains (pre-polymer stage).
- During curing, **crosslinking agents** (e.g., hardeners, peroxides, amines) create covalent bridges between chains.
- Energy of a covalent C–C bond: approximately **350 kJ/mol** — roughly **10–70× stronger** than secondary bonds in thermoplastics.

**Step 3 — Effect of Heat**
- On heating a thermoplastic, the secondary bonds break, chains slide past one another, and the material flows. On cooling, bonds reform — a **physical change**.
- On heating a thermoset, the existing crosslinks remain intact. If the temperature is high enough, the polymer **degrades chemically** (charring) rather than melting.

**Step 4 — Mechanical Consequence**
- Thermoplastics exhibit **viscoelastic** behaviour — they creep under sustained load and can undergo plastic deformation.
- Thermosets are **elastomeric-to-rigid**, with high dimensional stability, low creep, and excellent fatigue resistance.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property / Concept | Thermoplastics | Thermosets | Shipbuilding Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Molecular weight (M̄w) | **10⁵ – 10⁶ g/mol** (distribution) | **Effectively infinite** (network) | Affects melt viscosity |
| Crosslink density (νₑ) | **0** | **10⁻⁴ – 10⁻² mol/cm³** | Determines rigidity |
| Bond dissociation energy | **5–40 kJ/mol** (secondary) | **~350 kJ/mol** (covalent) | Predicts thermal resistance |
| Glass transition (Tg) | **Distinct** (e.g., PVC ≈ 80°C, Nylon 6 ≈ 50°C) | **Obscured** by degradation | Limits service temperature |
| Melting point (Tm) | **Defined** (e.g., HDPE ≈ 130°C, PTFE ≈ 327°C) | **None** (degrades before melting) | Determines weldability |
| Young's modulus (E) | **1 – 4 GPa** | **2 – 10 GPa** | Hull stiffness |
| Tensile strength (σᵤ) | **20 – 100 MPa** | **30 – 200 MPa** | Hull strength |
| Elongation at break | **5 – 600 %** | **0 – 5 %** | Ductility vs. brittleness |
| Recyclability | **Yes** (re-melt, re-process) | **No** (only grinding/filler) | IMO sustainability rules |
| Repair in-service | **Welding, hot-plate, extrusion** | **Bonding, patching with resin** | Dry-dock procedures |
| Common ship examples | HDPE, PVC, PTFE, Nylon, PP | GRP (polyester), Epoxy, Phenolic, Melamine | Piping, hulls, coatings, insulation |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Shipbuilding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Application Question:**
> Ship designers must choose the correct polymer class for each application:
> - **Thermoplastic HDPE** → ballast pipework, fuel tanks, fenders (must be weldable, repairable, recyclable).
> - **Thermoset GRP (Glass-Reinforced Polyester)** → small-craft hulls, superstructures, deckhouses (must be stiff, dimensionally stable, fire-resistant).
> - **Thermoset Epoxy** → hull coatings, composite laminates for naval vessels (must adhere strongly, resist seawater, cure at low temperatures).
> - **Thermoplastic PTFE** → bearing liners, valve seats (must have low friction, chemical inertness, wide thermal range).

### 2.4 The 'Why' Behind Reversibility — A Thermodynamic View

The reversibility of thermoplastic behaviour is governed by the **Gibbs free energy** of the polymer:

$$
\Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S
$$

- On heating a thermoplastic, the **entropy term TΔS** dominates because the chains gain conformational freedom. ΔG becomes negative, the process is spontaneous, and melting occurs.
- In a thermoset, the crosslinked network **cannot gain conformational freedom** even at high T — entropy is restricted. Thus melting is thermodynamically forbidden; only **bond scission (degradation)** occurs above the decomposition temperature.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Detailed Property Derivation & Comparison

Below is a complete analytical breakdown of how the molecular architecture of each class leads to its observed engineering properties. Every transition is explicitly stated.

#### 3.1.1 Crosslink Density Derivation

Crosslink density (νₑ) for a thermoset is defined as the moles of elastically effective network chains per unit volume of polymer:

$$
\nu_e = \frac{\rho}{M_c}
$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of the cured polymer (g/cm³)
- $M_c$ = average molecular weight between two successive crosslinks (g/mol)

**Step 1:** Take a typical cured unsaturated polyester (used in GRP ship hulls) with $\rho = 1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3$ and $M_c = 850 \text{ g/mol}$.

**Step 2:** Substitute the values into the formula:

$$
\nu_e = \frac{1.20}{850} = 1.412 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/cm}^3
$$

**Step 3:** Compare to a thermoplastic (e.g., HDPE) where crosslinks are absent:

$$
\nu_e = \frac{1.20}{\infty} = 0 \text{ mol/cm}^3
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation: The polyester has **1.412 × 10⁻³ mol/cm³ of elastically effective crosslinks**, while HDPE has **none**. This single number quantifies why thermosets are dimensionally stable and thermoplastics are reprocessable.

---

#### 3.1.2 Modulus–Crosslink Density Relationship

The rubber-elasticity theory (kinetic theory of elasticity) gives the shear modulus of a lightly crosslinked polymer as:

$$
G = \nu_e \cdot R \cdot T
$$

**Step 1:** Use $\nu_e = 1.412 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/cm}^3$, convert to SI: $1.412 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^6 = 1412 \text{ mol/m}^3$.

**Step 2:** Universal gas constant $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$, room temperature $T = 298 \text{ K}$.

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$
G = 1412 \times 8.314 \times 298
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply stepwise:

$$
G = 1412 \times 8.314 = 11739.4 \text{ J/(m}^3\text{·K)}
$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by T:

$$
G = 11739.4 \times 298 = 3.498 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 3.498 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 6:** Young's modulus $E \approx 3G$ for an incompressible network:

$$
E \approx 3 \times 3.498 = 10.49 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This matches order-of-magnitude experimental values for lightly crosslinked polyesters. Higher crosslink density (e.g., in heavily crosslinked epoxies, $M_c \approx 200$ g/mol) gives $E$ values in the **2 – 10 GPa** range — typical of structural thermosets used in shipbuilding.

---

#### 3.1.3 Thermal Decomposition Threshold

For both polymer classes, the maximum service temperature is bounded by the **bond dissociation energy** of the weakest bond in the backbone.

For a C–C covalent backbone bond:

$$
E_{bond} \approx 350 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

For a secondary Van der Waals interaction in thermoplastics:

$$
E_{vdW} \approx 5 - 40 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

**Step 1:** Compute the temperature at which thermal energy $k_B T$ equals the bond energy for a secondary bond:

$$
T_{softening} = \frac{E_{vdW}}{k_B} = \frac{20 \times 10^3}{1.381 \times 10^{-23}} \approx 1.45 \times 10^6 \text{ K / (Avogadro)} \approx 350 \text{ K} = 77°\text{C}
$$

(Here we used a per-molecule basis divided by Avogadro's number $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$.)

**Step 2:** Compute the same for covalent C–C bond:

$$
T_{decomposition} = \frac{350 \times 10^3 \times N_A}{N_A} = \frac{3.5 \times 10^5}{1.381 \times 10^{-23}} = 2.53 \times 10^{28} \text{ (unbounded; we use empirical values)}
$$

Empirically, polymer decomposition occurs at **300 – 500 °C** due to chain scission kinetics.

**Step 3:** Conclusion: A thermoplastic softens at **~80–150 °C**, while a thermoset survives intact until **~250–400 °C**, then degrades rather than melting.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Behavioural Comparison Matrix

| Behavioural Aspect | Thermoplastic | Thermoset | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Response to first heating** | Softens → flows | Softens (if uncured) → cures → hardens | Cure is a chemical reaction |
| **Response to second heating** | Softens again (reversible) | Remains hard (irreversible) | Covalent crosslinks persist |
| **Response to cooling** | Hardens (reversible) | Remains hard | No new bonds form on cooling |
| **Weldability** | Easy (hot-plate, extrusion, ultrasonic) | Difficult (only adhesive bonding) | Thermoplastics re-melt locally |
| **Recycling** | Mechanical reprocessing, chemical depolymerisation | Grinding into filler only | Network cannot be un-crosslinked economically |
| **Creep resistance** | Low (viscoelastic) | High (network locks chains) | Crosslinks prevent chain slippage |
| **Chemical resistance** | Moderate (solvent-sensitive) | High (network blocks diffusion) | Crosslinks reduce free volume |
| **Fire behaviour** | Melts and drips (often hazardous) | Chars and forms protective layer | Network resists melting |
| **Processing** | Injection moulding, extrusion, blow moulding | Compression moulding, resin transfer moulding, hand lay-up | Different flow characteristics |
| **Shelf life of raw material** | Long (granules are stable) | Limited (resin + hardener pot-life) | Pre-mixed thermosets cure spontaneously |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Molecular Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Thermoplastic Monomer] --> A2[Linear Chain Growth]
    A2 --> A3[Linear/Branched Chains]
    A3 --> A4[Weak Secondary Bonds]
    A4 --> A5{Heat Applied?}
    A5 -->|Yes| A6[Chains Slide, Material Flows]
    A5 -->|No| A7[Material Solidifies on Cooling]
    A6 --> A8[Reversible Process]
    A7 --> A8

    B1[Thermoset Monomer + Crosslinker] --> B2[Pre-polymer Formation]
    B2 --> B3[Linear Pre-polymer Chains]
    B3 --> B4[Cure Reaction Initiated]
    B4 --> B5[Covalent Crosslink Formation]
    B5 --> B6[3D Network Structure]
    B6 --> B7{Heat Applied After Cure?}
    B7 -->|Yes| B8[Network Intact, No Flow, Possible Degradation]
    B7 -->|No| B9[Material Remains Rigid]
    B8 --> B10[Irreversible Process]
    B9 --> B10
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Shipbuilding Application Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Polymer Required for Shipboard Component] --> Q1{Need Remeltable and Repairable?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| TP[Choose Thermoplastic]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Need High Dimensional Stability and Stiffness?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| TS[Choose Thermoset]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Need High Temperature Resistance Above 200 deg C?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| TS2[Choose Thermoset: Phenolic or Silicone]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Need Chemical Resistance to Fuels and Oils?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| TP2[Choose Thermoplastic: HDPE or PTFE]
    Q4 -->|No| Default[Consult IMO and SOLAS Standards]

    TP --> EX1[Examples: HDPE pipes, PVC ducting, PTFE bearings]
    TS --> EX2[Examples: GRP hull, epoxy coating, phenolic insulation]
    TS2 --> EX3[Examples: Phenolic bulkhead laminates]
    TP2 --> EX4[Examples: HDPE fuel tanks, PTFE valve seats]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Thermal Response Cycle

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph THERMO
    T1[Solid Thermoplastic] -->|Heat Above Tg| T2[Molten Thermoplastic]
    T2 -->|Cool Below Tg| T1
    T1 -->|Re-heat| T2
    end

    subgraph THERMOSET
    S1[Liquid Resin + Hardener] -->|Cure Once| S2[Crosslinked Solid Network]
    S2 -->|Re-heat Above Decomposition| S3[Char and Degrade]
    S3 -->|Cannot Return| S2
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** The thermoset has a *one-way* arrow from resin to solid, reflecting the **irreversibility** of crosslinking — a key KTU 2024 exam discriminator.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]

**Differentiate, in molecular terms, between a thermoplastic and a thermosetting polymer. Give one shipbuilding example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **thermoplastic** consists of long linear or lightly branched chains held together by weak secondary forces such as Van der Waals interactions. On heating, these secondary bonds break and the chains can slide past each other, allowing the material to soften and flow. The process is reversible, so the polymer can be remoulded repeatedly. **Shipbuilding example:** HDPE pipes used in ballast and bilge systems.

A **thermoset** is formed when linear pre-polymer chains become linked by strong covalent bonds (crosslinks) during a curing reaction, producing a rigid three-dimensional network. Once cured, the material cannot be remelted; further heating causes degradation rather than flow. **Shipbuilding example:** GRP (glass-reinforced polyester) used in small-craft hulls and superstructures.

*(1 mark for thermoplastic definition + example, 1 mark for thermoset definition + example, 1 mark for clear molecular-level distinction.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]

**Why cannot thermosetting plastics be reprocessed by remelting? Explain with reference to bond types.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Thermosets cannot be reprocessed by remelting because their chains are joined by **covalent crosslinks** with bond energies of approximately **350 kJ/mol**, which is many times greater than the energy of the secondary Van der Waals forces (5–40 kJ/mol) that hold thermoplastic chains together. On heating, the energy supplied is sufficient to break the secondary bonds in thermoplastics (causing flow), but insufficient to break the covalent crosslinks in thermosets without causing **irreversible chemical degradation (charring)**. Hence, the material cannot return to a flowable state. *(1 mark each for: identifying the bond type, comparing bond energies, and stating the consequence of heating.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Adaptation]

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the **molecular structure** of thermoplastics and thermosets. How does this structure influence their **thermal behaviour** and **recyclability** in shipboard applications? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **mechanical properties**, **chemical resistance**, and **fire behaviour** of thermoplastics and thermosets, citing at least two shipbuilding-relevant examples of each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Molecular Structure and Thermal Behaviour (7 marks):**

In thermoplastics, monomer units join end-to-end to form **linear or lightly branched chains** (e.g., HDPE, PVC). The chains are independent and held together only by weak secondary forces — Van der Waals interactions, dipole-dipole forces, and hydrogen bonds. On heating above the **glass transition temperature (Tg)** or the **melting temperature (Tm)**, these secondary bonds break, the chains become mobile, and the material flows. On cooling, the bonds re-form and the material solidifies. This cycle can be repeated indefinitely, which is why thermoplastics are **fully recyclable** — making them ideal for shipboard piping, fenders, and fuel tanks that may need to be replaced or re-welded in dry dock.

In thermosets (e.g., unsaturated polyester, epoxy, phenolic), monomer units first form linear pre-polymer chains. A curing reaction, activated by heat or a hardener, introduces **covalent crosslinks** between chains, producing a permanent **3D network**. The energy of a covalent C–C bond is about **350 kJ/mol**, far above the energy of secondary bonds. On heating a cured thermoset, the crosslinks remain intact, so the material does not flow. If the temperature is raised further, the polymer **degrades** rather than melting. Because the network is permanent, thermosets **cannot be remelted or reprocessed** — they can only be ground into filler. Shipboard examples: GRP hulls (polyester) and protective coatings (epoxy).

*(Marks distribution: 2 for structure, 2 for thermal behaviour, 2 for recyclability, 1 for shipboard context.)*

---

**(b) Mechanical, Chemical, and Fire Comparison (7 marks):**

| Property | Thermoplastics | Thermosets | Ship Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tensile strength** | Moderate: 20–100 MPa | High: 30–200 MPa | HDPE pipe (TP) vs. GRP hull (TS) |
| **Young's modulus** | 1–4 GPa | 2–10 GPa | Nylon rope (TP) vs. epoxy composite (TS) |
| **Elongation at break** | High (5–600%): ductile | Low (0–5%): brittle | PP mooring rope (TP) vs. phenolic bulkhead (TS) |
| **Creep resistance** | Poor (viscoelastic flow) | Excellent (network locks chains) | Critical for sustained-load applications |
| **Chemical resistance** | Moderate; solvent-sensitive | High; network blocks diffusion | HDPE fuel tank (TP) vs. epoxy-coated bilge (TS) |
| **Fire behaviour** | Melts and drips, propagates flame | Chars, forms protective carbon layer | PVC (TP, drips) vs. phenolic (TS, low flame spread) |

For ship applications:
- **Thermoplastics** like HDPE are used for ballast and bilge piping because they can be thermally welded in situ and easily replaced.
- **Thermosets** like GRP (glass-reinforced polyester) are used for hulls and superstructures of small craft and naval vessels because they offer high stiffness, low creep, and excellent seawater durability.

*(Marks distribution: 2 for mechanical, 2 for chemical, 2 for fire, 1 for examples.)*

---

### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Adaptation]

**(a)** Explain the **cure process** of a thermosetting resin such as unsaturated polyester. Discuss how the resulting crosslink density affects the **service temperature** and **mechanical strength** of GRP used in ship hulls. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of using thermoplastics (e.g., HDPE, PTFE) in marine pipework and valve components, with specific reference to **weldability, chemical resistance, and recyclability** under IMO sustainability rules. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Cure Process and Crosslink Density (7 marks):**

The cure of unsaturated polyester resin (commonly used in GRP ship hulls) proceeds in three stages:

1. **Initiation** — A peroxide catalyst (e.g., MEKP — methyl ethyl ketone peroxide) decomposes on heating or with a promoter (cobalt naphthenate) to generate free radicals.
2. **Propagation** — The free radicals attack the C=C double bonds in the polyester backbone and in the styrene co-monomer, opening them and forming new covalent bonds.
3. **Crosslinking** — Successive chain extensions and bridging reactions create a **three-dimensional covalent network** that hardens the resin into a rigid solid.

The **crosslink density** $\nu_e$ is given by $\nu_e = \rho / M_c$ where $M_c$ is the molecular weight between crosslinks. Typical cured polyester with $M_c \approx 800 \text{–} 1000 \text{ g/mol}$ gives $\nu_e \approx 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/cm}^3$. This high crosslink density:

- **Raises the service temperature** by preventing chain mobility; the material can typically operate up to **120–150 °C** before softening.
- **Increases mechanical strength and stiffness** because the network distributes applied loads across many covalent bonds; tensile strengths of 50–200 MPa are achievable with glass-fibre reinforcement.
- **Reduces creep and solvent absorption**, both critical for hulls that experience continuous wave loading and prolonged seawater contact.

*(Marks distribution: 2 for cure mechanism, 2 for crosslink density, 2 for service temperature, 1 for mechanical strength.)*

---

**(b) Thermoplastics in Marine Pipework and Valves (7 marks):**

**Advantages:**
- **Weldability** — HDPE pipes can be joined by butt-fusion, socket-fusion, or electrofusion welding, creating leak-proof joints critical for ballast and bilge systems. PTFE-lined valves can be assembled using mechanical compression fittings.
- **Chemical resistance** — HDPE resists seawater, fuels, oils, and many chemicals, making it suitable for fuel and ballast lines. PTFE is one of the most chemically inert polymers known, ideal for aggressive chemical cargoes.
- **Recyclability** — At end-of-life, HDPE and PTFE components can be melted and re-extruded, supporting the **IMO Hong Kong Convention** requirements for sustainable ship recycling. This contrasts with thermoset GRP, which can only be shredded for landfill or downcycled as filler.
- **Lightweight and corrosion-proof** — HDPE floats in water (density ≈ 0.95 g/cm³) and does not rust, reducing maintenance and extending service life.

**Limitations:**
- **Lower service temperature** — HDPE softens above ~80 °C; PTFE is better (up to 260 °C) but more expensive.
- **Creep under sustained load** — Thermoplastics can deform over time under constant pressure, requiring derating of pipe schedules.
- **UV and flame sensitivity** — Unprotected HDPE degrades in sunlight; standard grades are combustible and may need flame-retardant additives for SOLAS compliance.
- **Permeability** — Some thermoplastics allow slow diffusion of fuel or gas molecules, limiting use in certain cargo systems.

*(Marks distribution: 2 each for weldability, chemical resistance, recyclability, 1 for limitations.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Never write "thermoplastics are weak and thermosets are strong."** This is a *gross oversimplification*. Always ground the answer in **bond type and network structure**.
> 2. **Do not confuse Tg (glass transition) with Tm (melting).** Thermoplastics have both; thermosets have neither in the conventional sense — they degrade before melting.
> 3. **Avoid claiming thermosets cannot be recycled at all.** They can be ground and reused as filler, but cannot be *remelted and reprocessed* like thermoplastics.
> 4. **Always link to shipbuilding** — KTU examiners reward context-specific examples (HDPE pipes, GRP hulls, epoxy coatings). A purely textbook answer loses marks.
> 5. **Do not mix up "thermoplastic elastomers" with thermosets.** TPEs are technically thermoplastics but behave elastically — clarify in the answer if relevant.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme**

- **Thermoplastic** = linear/branched chains, **weak secondary bonds**, **reversible** melt–solid cycle, **recyclable**.
- **Thermoset** = covalent **crosslinked 3D network**, **irreversible** cure, **cannot be remelted**, only ground for filler.
- **Bond energy contrast:** Secondary forces **5–40 kJ/mol** vs. Covalent C–C **~350 kJ/mol**.
- **Crosslink density formula:** $\nu_e = \rho / M_c$ (zero for thermoplastics, $10^{-4} \text{–} 10^{-2} \text{ mol/cm}^3$ for thermosets).
- **Modulus–crosslink relationship:** $G = \nu_e R T$ (kinetic theory of rubber elasticity).
- **Tg exists** in thermoplastics (e.g., PVC ≈ 80 °C, Nylon 6 ≈ 50 °C); **obsolete/degraded** in thermosets.
- **Tm exists** in thermoplastics (e.g., HDPE ≈ 130 °C, PTFE ≈ 327 °C); **absent** in thermosets.
- **Shipbuilding thermoplastics:** HDPE (pipes, tanks), PVC (ducting, cable trays), PTFE (bearings, seals), Nylon (ropes, gears), PP (ventilation).
- **Shipbuilding thermosets:** GRP/polyester (hulls, superstructures), Epoxy (coatings, composites), Phenolic (fire-resistant laminates), Melamine (laminated surfaces), Silicone (high-temperature seals).
- **Recycling rule:** Thermoplastics → **remelt & re-extrude**; Thermosets → **grind & use as filler only** (no remelt possible).
- **Fire rule:** Thermoplastics → **melt and drip** (often hazardous); Thermosets → **char and shield** (preferred in fire-critical applications).
- **Exam mantra:** Always explain the difference at the **molecular-bond level** before stating the macroscopic property — KTU examiners award maximum marks for this layered approach.
- **Use proper ship examples** in every answer to demonstrate KTU 2024 industry-context awareness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Resins
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Resins

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resin (KTU Board Standard Definition):**
> A **resin** is a viscous, semi-solid, or solid organic polymer (synthetic or naturally derived) that, when cured (hardened) through chemical reaction with a hardener, catalyst, or exposure to heat/UV, transforms into a rigid, cross-linked thermoset matrix. In shipbuilding, resins serve as the **matrix phase** of **fibre-reinforced polymer (FRP) composites**, binding fibrous reinforcements (glass, carbon, aramid) into a load-bearing structural component.

In the context of **PENST523 (Non-Ferrous Materials in Shipbuilding)**, resins are the single most important *non-metallic matrix* material enabling the construction of GRP (Glass Reinforced Plastic) hulls, superstructures, decks, masts, radomes, ducting, and internal outfit components. They are the **"glue-and-load-distributor"** that converts brittle fibres into a tough, monolithic engineering structure.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Rebar-in-Concrete Concept":**
> Think of building a reinforced concrete slab. The **concrete** alone is strong in compression but brittle. The **steel rebar** is strong in tension but thin and flimsy. Neither alone is useful. Together, the concrete grips the rebar and the rebar holds the concrete together when bent.
>
> - The **resin** in a ship hull plays the role of **concrete** (the continuous matrix).
> - The **fibreglass rovings/mats** play the role of **steel rebar** (the discontinuous reinforcement).
> - The **ship hull** is the finished structural slab.
>
> Without the resin, the fibres are useless loose threads. Without the fibres, the resin is a brittle plastic puck. Cure them together, and you get a hull plate that can withstand ocean waves, salt spray, and fatigue for **20–30 years**.

## 1.3 Why Resins Matter in Shipbuilding — The Engineering Justification

Modern commercial and naval shipbuilding pivoted to FRP composites in the mid-20th century because:

- **Corrosion Immunity**: Saltwater destroys steel via galvanic and electrolytic corrosion. A properly cured FRP laminate in isophthalic or vinyl ester resin is **electrolytically inert**.
- **Weight Reduction**: GRP density is **≈ 1.5–1.9 g/cm³** versus **7.85 g/cm³** for mild steel — a **~75 % weight saving** for equivalent stiffness designs.
- **Formability**: Resins flow into moulds at low viscosity, allowing single-piece moulding of complex hull geometries.
- **Damage Tolerance**: Unlike steel, FRP exhibits gradual, visible damage (crazing, delamination) before catastrophic failure.
- **Low Magnetic & Radar Signatures**: Critical for mine-countermeasure vessels (MCMVs) and stealth platforms.

## 1.4 Classification Roadmap (KTU Module-3 Scope)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies marine resins into **two super-families**:
> 1. **Thermosetting Resins** (irreversibly cross-linked — dominant in shipbuilding)
> 2. **Thermoplastic Resins** (re-meltable — limited marine use, mainly in fasteners, pipes, liners)

We will focus on **Thermosetting Resins** as they constitute **> 95 %** of all marine FRP production.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Curing Chemistries

All marine thermoset resins cure by one of two mechanisms:

### 2.1.1 Addition (Free-Radical) Polymerization
- Initiated by a **peroxide catalyst** (e.g., MEKP — Methyl Ethyl Ketone Peroxide) plus an **accelerator** (cobalt naphthenate).
- Used by: **Polyester**, **Vinyl Ester**, and **Acrylic** resins.
- No by-products. Low shrinkage. Fast room-temperature cure.

### 2.1.2 Condensation (Step-Growth) Polymerization
- Produces a small molecule by-product (water, alcohol, HCl).
- Used by: **Epoxy** (with amine hardener), **Phenolic** (with acid or heat).
- Requires careful stoichiometry; some systems need elevated temperature (post-cure).

## 2.2 Marine Resin-by-Resin Technical Breakdown

### A. Unsaturated Polyester Resins (UPR) — The Workhorse

The **most consumed** marine resin globally. Made by reacting a **diol** (e.g., propylene glycol) with **saturated diacids** (e.g., isophthalic acid) and **α-β unsaturated diacids** (e.g., maleic anhydride), then dissolved in **styrene monomer** (≈ 30–45 % by mass).

The **double bonds (C=C)** in the polyester backbone, plus those in the styrene, cross-link via free-radical mechanism to form a 3-D network.

**Two marine sub-grades:**

| Sub-grade | Key Acid | Marine Use | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Orthophthalic | Phthalic anhydride | Non-structural parts, internal mouldings | Lowest |
| Isophthalic | Isophthalic acid | Hulls, decks, superstructures | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **Isophthalic polyester** is the KTU-recommended general-purpose marine resin — superior **hydrolytic stability** and **toughness** versus orthophthalic.

### B. Vinyl Ester Resins (VE) — The Premium Choice

A hybrid: an **epoxy "backbone"** (bisphenol-A diglycidyl ether) terminated with **acrylate (–CH=CH–COO–)** groups, then diluted in styrene. The epoxy portion provides strength, the vinyl terminations provide fast, controllable cure.

- Excellent **corrosion resistance** to acids, alkalis, bleach.
- Superior **fatigue performance** and **toughness** (elongation at break ≈ 4–6 % vs. UPR's 2 %).
- Used in **chemical tankers, offshore structures, Navy mine-hunter hulls**.

### C. Epoxy Resins (EP) — The High-Performance Standard

Formed from **epichlorohydrin + bisphenol-A (DGEBA)**. Cures with **amines** (cold cure) or **anhydrides** (hot cure). **Zero styrene content** → no VOC emissions during layup.

- Highest mechanical strength of all marine resins.
- Excellent adhesion to fibres, metals, and core materials.
- Used in **carbon-fibre racing yachts, helicopter rotor blades, advanced naval composites**.
- **Drawback:** More expensive (~3–4× polyester), requires precise mix ratios, and most need **elevated-temperature post-cure** for full property development.

### D. Phenolic Resins (PF) — The Fire Specialist

Made by condensation of **phenol + formaldehyde**. Cures to a highly aromatic, densely cross-linked network.

- **Outstanding fire, smoke, and toxicity (FST) performance** — produces low smoke and minimal toxic gas on burning.
- Used in **naval interior bulkheads, gratings, escape tunnels** (SOLAS / IMO compliant).
- **Drawback:** Brittle, releases water during cure (void risk), requires careful processing.

### E. Polyurethane (PU) Resins — The Flexible Specialist

Formed by reaction of **polyol + isocyanate**. Used in marine applications mainly as:

- **Flexible coatings** (anti-corrosion hull paint binder).
- **Foam core material** (PVC, SAN, PU foam — sandwich panel cores).
- **Potting/encapsulation** compounds for marine electronics.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Resin Property Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains the standard equations a KTU examiner expects in any Part-B numerical/comparative answer.**

| # | Property | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Degree of Cure (α)** | $\alpha = \frac{\Delta H_{residual}}{\Delta H_{total}} \times 100$ | % | DSC measurement |
| 2 | **Density of FRP Laminate (Rule of Mixtures)** | $\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m$ | g/cm³ | $V_f$ = fibre volume fraction |
| 3 | **Resin-to-Fibre Mass Ratio (Hand Lay-up)** | $M_r = \frac{W_{resin}}{W_{fibre}} = \frac{1 - W_f}{W_f}$ | — | Typical 1 : 1 to 1.5 : 1 |
| 4 | **Cure Shrinkage (Volumetric)** | $S_v = \frac{V_{liquid} - V_{cured}}{V_{liquid}} \times 100$ | % | UPR ≈ 5–8 %, EP ≈ 1–2 % |
| 5 | **Glass Transition Temperature (DSC inflection)** | $T_g = T_g^{\infty} - \frac{K}{M_n}$ | °C or K | Flory–Fox relation |
| 6 | **Exotherm Peak Temperature (Lambert Equation)** | $T_{max} = T_0 + \Delta T_{ad} \left(1 - e^{-k t}\right)$ | °C | Used to control thick-laminate cure |
| 7 | **Exotherm Time to Peak** | $t_{peak} \propto \frac{1}{[Cat]^n}$ | minutes | $n \approx 0.5$ for MEKP/Co |
| 8 | **Tensile Modulus of FRP (Voigt Rule)** | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | GPa | Upper bound |
| 9 | **Tensile Modulus of FRP (Reuss Rule)** | $\frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$ | GPa | Lower bound |
| 10 | **Resin Content (Ignition Loss / Burn-off)** | $R_c = \frac{W_{burnt} - W_{fibre\,ash}}{W_{initial}} \times 100$ | % | ASTM D2584 |

## 2.4 The KTU Mnemonic — "**P-IVE-P**" for Marine Resins

A simple way to recall the 5 key resin families for the exam:

| Letter | Resin | One-Word Property |
|---|---|---|
| **P** | **P**olyester | **P**opular (cheapest) |
| **I** | — (connecting) | — |
| **V** | **V**inyl Ester | **V**ersatile (corrosion) |
| **E** | **E**poxy | **E**xcellent (strength) |
| **P** | **P**henolic | **P**rotective (fire) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Royal Navy Hunt & Sandown-class MCMVs**: Vinyl ester / glass-fibre hulls for low magnetic signature.
- **America's Cup AC75 foiling yachts**: Epoxy / carbon-fibre prepreg for extreme stiffness-to-weight.
- **Cruise ship balconies and pool surrounds**: Phenolic GRP for IMO Level-2 fire compliance.
- **Fishing trawlers, FRP lifeboats**: Isophthalic polyester / chopped-strand mat (CSM) — the global default.
- **Navy submarine command-and-control masts (periscope sheaths)**: Epoxy / carbon-fibre filament-wound tubes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Rule of Mixtures for FRP Density (Lamellar Composite)

The density of a fibre-reinforced composite, assuming perfect interfacial bonding and no voids, is a linear combination of the densities of its constituent phases.

**Given:**
- $\rho_f$ = density of fibre (e.g., E-glass = **2.55 g/cm³**)
- $\rho_m$ = density of matrix resin (e.g., cured polyester = **1.20 g/cm³**)
- $V_f$ = volume fraction of fibre (dimensionless, 0 to 1)
- $V_m$ = volume fraction of matrix (dimensionless, 0 to 1)
- $V_v$ = volume fraction of voids (dimensionless, 0 to 1)

**Constraint (closure equation):**
$$V_f + V_m + V_v = 1$$

**Step 1 — Define the mass of a representative volume element (RVE).**

The mass of the RVE equals the sum of the masses of each phase:

$$m_{RVE} = m_f + m_m + m_v$$

**Step 2 — Convert masses to densities × volumes.**

Since $m = \rho \times V$, and all phases share the same RVE volume $V$:

$$m_{RVE} = \rho_f \cdot V_f V + \rho_m \cdot V_m V + \rho_v \cdot V_v V$$

**Step 3 — Divide both sides by total RVE volume to obtain composite density.**

$$\rho_c = \frac{m_{RVE}}{V} = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m + \rho_v V_v$$

**Step 4 — Assume voids are negligible** ($V_v \approx 0$, valid for properly consolidated laminates) and apply the closure $V_m = 1 - V_f$:

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (a typical marine hand lay-up with $V_f = 0.35$).**

$$\rho_c = (2.55)(0.35) + (1.20)(0.65)$$

$$\rho_c = 0.8925 + 0.7800$$

$$\rho_c = 1.6725 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** This ~1.67 g/cm³ value matches the measured density of a typical GRP ship hull plate, confirming the rule of mixtures. Adding a 30 mm balsa core sandwich, for instance, drops the *effective* laminate density to **≈ 0.55 g/cm³** — a key lightweight-design strategy in superstructures.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stoichiometry of an Epoxy-Amine Cure (Cross-link Density)

**Background.** A DGEBA epoxy (DGEBA = diglycidyl ether of bisphenol-A) carries **2 epoxy groups** per molecule. A primary amine (e.g., TETA — triethylenetetramine) carries **4 active hydrogens** per molecule.

**Goal:** Find the **amine-to-epoxy mass ratio** that gives a fully cross-linked network.

**Step 1 — Molar masses (KTU board-grade values):**
- $M_{DGEBA} = 340 \text{ g/mol}$ (epoxy equivalent weight, EEW = 170 g/eq, so 2 eq/mol)
- $M_{TETA} = 146 \text{ g/mol}$ (amine hydrogen equivalent weight, AHEW = 36.5 g/eq, so 4 eq/mol)

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric mass ratio (epoxy : amine by mass).**

The number of equivalents of epoxy must equal the number of equivalents of amine hydrogen:

$$\frac{m_{epoxy}}{EEW} = \frac{m_{amine}}{AHEW}$$

Rearranging for the **mass ratio of amine to epoxy**:

$$\frac{m_{amine}}{m_{epoxy}} = \frac{AHEW}{EEW} = \frac{36.5}{170}$$

$$\frac{m_{amine}}{m_{epoxy}} \approx 0.215$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**

For every **100 g** of DGEBA epoxy, exactly **21.5 g** of TETA amine hardener is required for a perfect 1:1 network. Under-stoichiometry leaves unreacted epoxide groups (brittle, T$_g$ drops). Over-stoichiometry leaves unreacted amine (plasticises the network, also drops T$_g$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine rule of thumb:** Naval spec laminates are typically cured with epoxy:amine ratios of **100 : 22 to 100 : 25** to compensate for amine loss to atmospheric CO₂ and surface moisture during a shipyard lay-up.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Marine Resin Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix is the **single most important "cheat grid"** for any KTU Part-B comparative question on resins.

| Selection Criterion | Orthophthalic Polyester | Isophthalic Polyester | Vinyl Ester | Epoxy (DGEBA) | Phenolic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tensile Strength (MPa)** | 50–60 | 60–75 | 75–85 | 80–95 | 30–45 |
| **Tensile Modulus (GPa)** | 3.0–3.5 | 3.2–3.8 | 3.3–3.7 | 2.8–3.5 | 2.5–3.0 |
| **Elongation at Break (%)** | 1.5–2.0 | 2.0–2.5 | 4.0–6.0 | 3.0–6.0 | 0.5–1.0 |
| **HDT (°C, 1.82 MPa)** | 65–75 | 80–95 | 95–110 | 110–140 | 130–180 |
| **Cure Shrinkage (%)** | 7–8 | 6–7 | 5–6 | 1–2 | 0.5–1.5 |
| **Styrene Content** | 35–45 % | 35–45 % | 40–50 % | 0 % | 0 % |
| **Water Absorption (24 h, %)** | 0.3–0.5 | 0.2–0.3 | 0.15–0.25 | 0.1–0.2 | 0.3–0.6 |
| **Fire / Smoke (LOI %)** | 18–20 | 19–22 | 19–23 | 19–24 | **35–45** |
| **Marine Cost Index (vs. ortho = 1.0)** | **1.0** | 1.3–1.5 | 2.0–2.5 | 3.0–4.0 | 1.5–2.0 |
| **Primary Marine Use** | Temporary moulds, non-structural | Hulls, decks, superstructures | Corrosion liners, MCMV hulls | Racing yachts, hi-end naval | Fire-rated bulkheads, gratings |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Resin Content by Burn-off

> A GRP specimen is cut from a ship hull. Initial mass: **12.450 g**. After burn-off in a muffle furnace at 550 °C per **ASTM D2584**, the residual glass-fibre mass is **7.720 g**. Calculate: (a) fibre mass fraction $W_f$, (b) matrix mass fraction $W_m$, (c) the void content given a theoretical density of 1.80 g/cm³ and a measured density of 1.72 g/cm³.

**Part (a):**

$$W_f = \frac{m_{fibre}}{m_{initial}} = \frac{7.720}{12.450} = 0.6201$$

$$W_f = 62.01 \text{ \%}$$

**Part (b):**

$$W_m = 1 - W_f = 1 - 0.6201 = 0.3799$$

$$W_m = 37.99 \text{ \%}$$

**Part (c) — Void content from density rule of mixtures:**

The theoretical (void-free) density is given. Convert the mass fractions to volume fractions using $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³ (E-glass) and $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³ (cured polyester).

$$V_f = \frac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m} = \frac{0.6201/2.55}{0.6201/2.55 + 0.3799/1.20}$$

$$V_f = \frac{0.2432}{0.2432 + 0.3166} = \frac{0.2432}{0.5598} = 0.4345$$

$$V_m^{ideal} = 1 - V_f = 0.5655$$

The theoretical void-free density is **1.80 g/cm³**. The measured is **1.72 g/cm³**. The actual void fraction is:

$$V_v = 1 - \frac{\rho_{measured}}{\rho_{theoretical}} = 1 - \frac{1.72}{1.80} = 0.0444$$

$$V_v \approx 4.44 \text{ \%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** A void content above **5 %** is considered **rejectable** for primary hull structural laminates (per ISO 12215 and DNV rules). Hand lay-up typically yields 3–6 % voids; vacuum infusion can drop this to < 1 %.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree — Marine Resins (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Marine Resin Systems]
    rootB[Thermosetting Resins]
    rootC[Thermoplastic Resins]
    rootA --> rootB
    rootA --> rootC
    rootB --> nodeA1[Polyester Resin UPR]
    rootB --> nodeA2[Vinyl Ester Resin VE]
    rootB --> nodeA3[Epoxy Resin EP]
    rootB --> nodeA4[Phenolic Resin PF]
    rootB --> nodeA5[Polyurethane Resin PU]
    rootC --> nodeB1[Nylon PA]
    rootC --> nodeB2[Polypropylene PP]
    rootC --> nodeB3[PEEK High Performance]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA1a[Orthophthalic Acid Based]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA1b[Isophthalic Acid Based]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA1c[DCPD Modified]
    nodeA1a --> nodeA1a1[Non Structural Parts]
    nodeA1a1 --> appA1[Temporary Moulds and Boat Interiors]
    nodeA1b --> nodeA1b1[Hulls Decks Superstructures]
    nodeA1b1 --> appB1[General Marine GRP Default Choice]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA2a[Standard Vinyl Ester]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA2b[Toughened Vinyl Ester]
    nodeA2a --> appC1[Chemical Tankers Offshore]
    nodeA2b --> appC2[Navy MCMV Hulls]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA3a[DGEBA Bisphenol A Based]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA3b[Tetrafunctional TGDDM]
    nodeA3a --> appD1[Yacht and Racing Hulls]
    nodeA3b --> appD2[Carbon Fibre Naval Composites]
    nodeA4 --> nodeA4a[Resol Type]
    nodeA4 --> nodeA4b[Novolac Type]
    nodeA4a --> appE1[Solas Fire Rated Panels]
    nodeA4b --> appE2[Aerospace and Missile Parts]
    nodeA5 --> nodeA5a[Flexible Coating Grade]
    nodeA5 --> nodeA5b[Rigid Foam Core Grade]
    nodeA5a --> appF1[Hull Anti Corrosion Paint Binder]
    nodeA5b --> appF2[Sandwich Panel Buoyancy Cores]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — FRP Composite System Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    in1[FIBRE REINFORCEMENT\nE-GLASS, CARBON, ARAMID]
    in2[MATRIX RESIN\nPOLYESTER, VINYL ESTER, EPOXY]
    in3[ADDITIVES\nPIGMENTS, UV STABILISERS, FIRE RETARDANTS]
    in4[CORE MATERIAL\nFOAM BALSA HONEYCOMB]
    procA[CATALYST SYSTEM\nMEKP plus COBALT]
    procB[HARDENER SYSTEM\nAMINE or ANHYDRIDE]
    out1[FINAL MARINE COMPOSITE\nHULL DECK BULKHEAD]
    out2[PROPERTY PROFILE\nTENSILE FATIGUE FIRE CORROSION]
    in1 --> out1
    in2 --> out1
    in3 --> out1
    in4 --> out1
    procA --> out1
    procB --> out1
    out1 --> out2
```

## 4.3 Process Flow — Hand Lay-up / Vacuum Infusion of a Hull Laminate (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    s1[STEP 1: Mould Preparation and Gelcoat Application]
    s2[STEP 2: First Chopped Strand Mat Layer CSM 450 g per sqm]
    s3[STEP 3: Wet-out Resin Catalysed with MEKP at 1 to 2 percent]
    s4[STEP 4: Consolidate with Aluminium Roller to remove Air]
    s5[STEP 5: Add Woven Roving 800 g per sqm alternating]
    s6[STEP 6: Apply Vacuum Bag and Infuse Resin if VARTM Process]
    s7[STEP 7: Allow Cure at Ambient 25 Degree C for 24 hours]
    s8[STEP 8: Demould and Post Cure at 60 Degree C for 4 hours]
    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6 --> s7 --> s8
    s8 --> fprod[FINAL LAMINATE READY FOR TRIM AND FITTING]
```

## 4.4 Curing Reaction Sequence — Polyester + Styrene Cross-linking

```mermaid
graph TD
    r1[INITIATION\nMEKP decomposes to free radicals RO star]
    r2[PROPAGATION\nRadical attacks C=C on polyester chain]
    r3[Radical attacks C=C on styrene monomer]
    r4[CHAIN GROWTH\nLong chain radical forms with pendant double bonds]
    r5[TERMINATION\nTwo radicals combine chain coupling]
    r6[NET RESULT\n3D crosslinked network C-C-C-C bonds]
    r1 --> r2 --> r3 --> r4 --> r5 --> r6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** All Mermaid node IDs in this section are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA1a`, `s1`, `r1`) and contain no reserved keywords. All node labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting, ensuring clean Mermaid rendering in any compatible viewer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

### Q1. Differentiate between thermoplastic and thermosetting resins with two marine-relevant examples for each.
**(3 Marks) — KTU 2024 Pattern**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Thermoplastic | Thermosetting |
|---|---|---|
| **Molecular structure** | Linear or branched chains, no crosslinks | 3-D cross-linked network |
| **On heating** | Softens and melts — re-processable | Decomposes — **irreversibly set** |
| **Solvent resistance** | Poor to moderate | Excellent |
| **Marine examples** | Nylon (PA-6 mooring lines), Polypropylene (PP pipe fittings) | Isophthalic polyester (hulls), Epoxy (carbon-fibre yachts) |

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark definition + 1 mark mechanism + 1 mark marine examples. Any two correct marine examples accepted.)*

---

### Q2. Why is isophthalic polyester preferred over orthophthalic polyester for marine hull laminates?
**(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
1. The isophthalic acid backbone gives a more **symmetric, tightly packed molecular chain**, yielding **higher tensile strength** and **better fatigue life** (1 mark).
2. Isophthalic resin exhibits **superior hydrolytic stability** — it resists water attack on the ester linkage far better, which is critical in a **continually submerged hull** (1 mark).
3. It also provides better **chemical resistance** to fuel, oil, and bilge-water contaminants, and lower **blistering tendency** in long-term water immersion (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q A.(a)** Classify the major types of thermosetting resins used in shipbuilding. Discuss the chemistry, key properties, and specific marine applications of **polyester, vinyl ester, and epoxy resins**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Polyester Resins (UPR)** (≈ 2 marks)
- Formed by condensation of a diol + saturated diacid + α,β-unsaturated diacid, dissolved in **styrene monomer**.
- Cure mechanism: **free-radical addition** using **MEKP catalyst + cobalt accelerator** at room temperature.
- Marine variants: **orthophthalic** (cheap, non-structural) and **isophthalic** (hulls, decks, superstructures).
- Pros: Lowest cost, easy hand lay-up. Cons: Styrene emissions, moderate strength, 5–8 % cure shrinkage.

**2. Vinyl Ester Resins (VE)** (≈ 2.5 marks)
- Hybrid structure: **epoxy bisphenol-A backbone** terminated with **acrylate (–CH=CH–COO–)** groups, dissolved in styrene.
- Cures by free-radical mechanism like polyester, but the epoxy core gives **higher toughness and elongation (4–6 %)**.
- Outstanding **chemical and hydrolytic resistance** — used in **chemical tankers, offshore oil-platform gratings, naval mine-countermeasure vessel (MCMV) hulls** for low magnetic signature.
- Cost: 2–2.5 × orthophthalic polyester.

**3. Epoxy Resins (EP)** (≈ 2.5 marks)
- Made from **bisphenol-A + epichlorohydrin** → DGEBA. Cured with **amines (cold)** or **anhydrides (hot)**.
- **Zero styrene** → no VOC emissions, no fire hazard from flammable monomer.
- Highest mechanical strength, best fibre wet-out, best adhesion. Used in **America's Cup racing yachts, helicopter rotor blades, advanced naval submarines' command masts**.
- Drawback: Higher cost, requires precise mix ratio, often needs elevated-temperature **post-cure (60–80 °C)** to develop full T$_g$.

**Comparative Summary Table (1 mark):**

| Property | Polyester | Vinyl Ester | Epoxy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tensile Strength | Medium | High | Highest |
| Elongation | Low (2 %) | Medium (5 %) | High (5–6 %) |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Styrene | Yes | Yes | No |

---

**Q A.(b)** Explain the **rule of mixtures** for FRP density. A marine GRP laminate has fibre volume fraction $V_f = 0.40$, fibre density $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, and matrix density $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³. Calculate (i) the theoretical density, and (ii) the void content if the measured density is 1.65 g/cm³. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Theory (3 marks):**
The rule of mixtures states that the density of a composite equals the volume-fraction-weighted sum of its constituents' densities, assuming no voids:

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m + \rho_v V_v$$

For a void-free laminate, $V_m = 1 - V_f$, giving:

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)$$

**Part (i) — Theoretical density (2 marks):**

$$\rho_c = (2.55)(0.40) + (1.20)(0.60)$$

$$\rho_c = 1.020 + 0.720 = 1.740 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Part (ii) — Void content (2 marks):**

The actual void fraction is the relative difference between the theoretical and measured densities:

$$V_v = 1 - \frac{\rho_{measured}}{\rho_{theoretical}} = 1 - \frac{1.65}{1.740}$$

$$V_v = 1 - 0.9483 = 0.0517$$

$$V_v \approx 5.17 \text{ \%}$$

**Conclusion:** A void content of **5.17 %** is at the borderline acceptability for primary hull structure (DNV limit 5 %); the laminate would need improved compaction or vacuum-assisted resin transfer moulding (VARTM).

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**Q B.(a)** Compare **phenolic and polyurethane resins** in terms of (i) chemical structure, (ii) cure mechanism, (iii) fire/smoke behaviour, and (iv) typical shipboard applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Chemical Structure (≈ 1.5 marks)**
- **Phenolic:** Made by **condensation of phenol + formaldehyde** → highly aromatic, densely cross-linked network with stable C–C and C–O–C bonds between benzene rings.
- **Polyurethane:** Formed by step-growth reaction of **polyol (–OH) + isocyanate (–N=C=O)** → produces urethane linkage (–NH–CO–O–). Used as **coatings, elastomers, or rigid foams** in marine contexts.

**(ii) Cure Mechanism (≈ 1.5 marks)**
- **Phenolic:** Condensation cure — releases **water** as by-product. Requires **acid catalyst or heat** (resol type) or **addition of hexamine** (novolac type). Cannot be done by simple room-temperature catalyst systems.
- **Polyurethane:** **Two-component mixing** of polyol + isocyanate at room temperature → fast exothermic reaction. Foaming achieved by addition of water (reacts with isocyanate → CO₂ gas → foam).

**(iii) Fire / Smoke Behaviour (≈ 2 marks)**
- **Phenolic: SUPERIOR** — **LOI 35–45 %**, low flame spread, low smoke density, low toxic-gas emission. Meets **SOLAS / IMO Level 2 fire** requirements.
- **Polyurethane: POOR** — burns readily, releases **HCN (from nitrogen in isocyanate) and CO** — major smoke and toxicity hazard. Marine use is therefore restricted to **non-exposed structural foam cores**, never as a primary load-bearing surface.

**(iv) Marine Applications (≈ 2 marks)**
- **Phenolic:** Naval **interior bulkheads, escape-tunnel linings, cable trays, gratings** — wherever IMO FTP-Code fire ratings are mandated.
- **Polyurethane:** **Hull anti-corrosion paint binder**, **flexible marine sealants**, and as **closed-cell PU foam core** in sandwich panels for superstructure and bulkhead insulation.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Phenolic | Polyurethane |
|---|---|---|
| Cure by-product | Water | None (or CO₂ if foamed) |
| Fire performance | Excellent (LOI ≈ 40 %) | Poor (LOI ≈ 18 %) |
| Toxicity on burning | Low | High (HCN) |
| Primary marine role | Fire-rated panels | Coatings and foam cores |

---

**Q B.(b)** A **DGEBA epoxy resin** (epoxy equivalent weight = 185 g/eq) is to be cured with a **polyamine hardener** (amine-hydrogen equivalent weight = 45 g/eq). Calculate (i) the **stoichiometric mass ratio** of hardener to epoxy, and (ii) the mass of hardener required for a **2.5 kg** epoxy batch. Explain the consequences of using a **25 % under-cure** (insufficient hardener) on the final T$_g$ and mechanical strength. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Part (i) — Stoichiometric mass ratio (2 marks):**

For a perfect 1:1 functional group reaction:

$$\frac{m_{hardener}}{m_{epoxy}} = \frac{AHEW}{EEW} = \frac{45}{185} = 0.2432$$

**Stoichiometric mass ratio ≈ 24.3 g hardener per 100 g epoxy.**

**Part (ii) — Hardener mass for 2.5 kg epoxy (1 mark):**

$$m_{hardener} = 0.2432 \times 2500 \text{ g} = 608.1 \text{ g}$$

**≈ 608 g of amine hardener for 2.5 kg DGEBA.**

**Part (iii) — Consequences of 25 % under-cure (4 marks):**

If only **75 %** of the stoichiometric hardener is added, **25 % of the epoxy groups remain unreacted**.

| Consequence | Mechanism | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Lower cross-link density** | Fewer epoxy-amine links formed | Network has lower 3-D connectivity |
| **Reduced T$_g$** | T$_g$ is directly proportional to cross-link density (Flory-Fox) | T$_g$ may drop by **20–40 °C** |
| **Lower tensile strength and modulus** | Weaker network bears load less efficiently | Strength drop of **15–30 %** |
| **Poor chemical resistance** | Unreacted epoxide is hygroscopic | Higher water absorption, lower hydrolytic stability |
| **Reduced HDT (heat deflection temp)** | Network softens earlier under load | Service-temperature limit drops |
| **Tackiness / surface bleeding** | Liquid unreacted epoxide migrates to surface | Cosmetic and bonding defects in laminate stacks |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the rule-of-mixtures calculation — you will lose 1 mark if the density is given without **g/cm³** in the final answer.
> 2. **Confusing "fibre mass fraction"** ($W_f$) with **"fibre volume fraction"** ($V_f$). They are **NOT equal** because fibre and resin have different densities. Most students lose 2 marks here.
> 3. **Citing "epoxy is stronger than polyester"** without quantifying: a KTU board answer must give **numbers** (e.g., 85 MPa vs. 60 MPa tensile strength).
> 4. **Mixing up orthophthalic and isophthalic** — only **isophthalic** is recommended for hulls. State the acid type clearly.
> 5. **Omitting the styrene emission issue** in polyester answers — a key KTU environmental/safety point.
> 6. **Skipping the cross-link density vs. T$_g$** relationship in stoichiometry questions — examiners expect a direct Flory-Fox reference.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Bullet List:**

- [ ] **Resin = matrix** in FRP; **fibre = reinforcement**; together they form the marine composite.
- [ ] **5 key marine thermosets**: Polyester (UPR), Vinyl Ester (VE), Epoxy (EP), Phenolic (PF), Polyurethane (PU) — memorise via **"P-IVE-P"** mnemonic.
- [ ] **Polyester** = cheapest, most common; uses **MEKP + Co** catalyst system; **isophthalic grade** is the marine default.
- [ ] **Vinyl ester** = epoxy backbone + acrylate end-groups; **best corrosion + fatigue** combination; used in **MCMV hulls**.
- [ ] **Epoxy** = highest strength, **no styrene**, needs **amine hardener** at **AHEW/EEW = 0.21–0.25** mass ratio; used in racing yachts and high-end naval composites.
- [ ] **Phenolic** = **best fire / smoke / toxicity (FST)** properties; used in **SOLAS-rated bulkheads**; releases water on cure.
- [ ] **Polyurethane** = polyol + isocyanate; used mainly as **coatings and foam cores**, **not** as a primary structural resin due to fire toxicity.
- [ ] **Rule of Mixtures**: $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ — use this for theoretical density and void-content calculations.
- [ ] **Voigt (upper) and Reuss (lower) bounds** for modulus: $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ (Voigt) and $\frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$ (Reuss).
- [ ] **Cure shrinkage**: Polyester ≈ 7 %, Vinyl Ester ≈ 5 %, Epoxy ≈ 1–2 %, Phenolic ≈ 1 %.
- [ ] **Acceptable void content** for primary hull laminates: **< 5 %** (DNV/ISO 12215).
- [ ] **Flory-Fox** relationship: $T_g$ increases with cross-link density and number-average molecular weight $M_n$.
- [ ] **Hand lay-up** = 3–6 % voids; **VARTM / vacuum infusion** = < 1 % voids.
- [ ] **Burn-off test** (ASTM D2584) is the standard KTU-board method to measure resin content.
- [ ] **Catalyst (MEKP) percentage** for marine hand lay-up: typically **1–2 % by mass** of resin; excess causes violent exotherm and shrinkage.
- [ ] **T$_g$ hierarchy** (lowest → highest): Orthophthalic < Isophthalic < Vinyl Ester < Epoxy < Phenolic.
- [ ] **Always state the acid type** (ortho- or isophthalic) when answering any polyester-based question in a KTU exam — examiners deduct marks for vagueness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Glass and Carbon Fibres
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Glass Fibres — Formal Definition
