**Glass fibres** are fine filaments of glass (typically 5–25 micrometres in diameter) manufactured by extruding molten glass through tiny bushings. In marine and shipbuilding composites, glass fibres serve as the primary load-bearing reinforcement phase within a polymer matrix, providing tensile strength, impact resistance, and dimensional stability to the laminate. The most industrially relevant grades for marine structural applications are **E-glass (Electrical grade)**, **S-glass (Structural/High-Strength grade)**, and **R-glass (Reinforcement grade)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PENST523 – Module 3):**
> Glass fibres are classified as the *most widely used* reinforcement in Fibre Reinforced Polymers (FRP) for shipbuilding. Approximately **70 % of all marine composites** use E-glass reinforcement due to its excellent cost-to-performance ratio.

## 1.2 Carbon Fibres — Formal Definition

**Carbon fibres** are filaments consisting mainly of carbon atoms (typically 92–95 % carbon by weight) produced by the controlled pyrolysis of organic precursor fibres such as **Polyacrylonitrile (PAN)**, petroleum pitch, or cellulose (rayon). In shipbuilding, carbon fibres are reserved for high-performance, weight-critical applications such as racing yacht hulls, hydrofoils, masts, propeller shafts, and naval submarine sonar domes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Defining Property — Specific Strength & Specific Modulus:**
> Carbon fibres exhibit a tensile strength of **3,000–7,000 MPa** with a density of only **1.75–1.95 g/cm³**, yielding a specific strength roughly **5× that of structural steel**. This makes carbon fibre the *king of weight-critical marine structures*.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

**Glass Fibres — The "Plywood of the Ocean":**
Imagine building a boat hull with thousands of tiny, invisible glass threads (each thinner than a human hair) embedded in a hard plastic paste. Individually, a single glass thread is fragile and snaps easily. But bundle them together — by the millions — and the resulting "plastic-glass" composite becomes tough enough to resist waves, salt, and even minor impacts. This is exactly how a **GRP (Glass Reinforced Plastic)** boat hull behaves.

**Carbon Fibres — The "Aerospace Bone":**
Think of carbon fibres as the *rebar of futuristic marine engineering*. Just as steel rebar transforms weak concrete into a skyscraper, carbon fibres transform a soft polymer resin into a material that can rival the stiffness of aluminium but weigh less than a third. The carbon atoms inside the fibre are aligned in near-perfect graphite-like crystals running along the fibre length, giving them extraordinary axial strength.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Glass Fibre (E-glass) | Carbon Fibre (PAN-based HS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Density (g/cm³)** | 2.54 – 2.60 | 1.75 – 1.95 |
| **Tensile Strength (MPa)** | 1,900 – 3,500 | 3,000 – 7,000 |
| **Tensile Modulus (GPa)** | 70 – 90 | 200 – 600 |
| **Elongation at Break (%)** | 4.5 – 5.0 | 1.5 – 2.5 |
| **Max Service Temp (°C)** | 550 | 600 – 2,000 (inert) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Glass Fibres — Taxonomy, Manufacturing & Properties

### 2.1.1 Classification of Glass Fibres (KTU High-Yield)

| Grade | Composition Hallmark | Marine Relevance | Cost Index |
|---|---|---|---|
| **E-Glass** | Alumino-borosilicate (low alkali $\leq$ 1 %) | Workhorse of GRP boatbuilding | 1.0 (baseline) |
| **S-Glass** | Higher SiO₂ + MgO content | Naval craft, high-speed patrol boats | 4 – 6 × E |
| **C-Glass** | Calcium-aluminosilicate (chemical grade) | Chemical-resistant tanks, piping | 1.2 × E |
| **A-Glass** | Soda-lime (high alkali) | Rare in marine | 0.8 × E |
| **R-Glass** | Calcium-aluminosilicate (SiO₂ + CaO + MgO) | High-performance hulls, offshore | 5 – 8 × E |

### 2.1.2 Manufacturing Process — Direct Melt Method

1. **Raw Material Batching** — Silica sand, limestone, kaolin clay, and boric oxide are weighed in precise stoichiometric proportions.
2. **Melting** — Batch is fed into a gas-fired or electric furnace at **1,250 – 1,580 °C**; the melt is homogenized and de-gassed.
3. **Fibre Drawing (Bushing Stage)** — Molten glass flows through a platinum-rhodium alloy bushing plate containing **200 – 8,000 micro-holes (4 – 5 mm diameter)**.
4. **Attenuation & Sizing** — Continuous filaments are drawn at **15 – 60 m/s**, attenuated to **5 – 25 µm diameter**, then coated with a **sizing agent (silane coupling agent + polymer film former)** to protect against abrasion and promote resin adhesion.
5. **Winding** — Filaments are gathered into a strand and wound onto a rotating collet at **1,000 – 3,000 m/min**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why sizing matters in shipbuilding:** Silane coupling agents (e.g., γ-methacryloxypropyltrimethoxysilane) form a chemical "bridge" between the inorganic glass surface and the organic polyester/vinylester resin, preventing the notorious **"wicking"** failure where seawater seeps along the fibre-matrix interface.

### 2.1.3 Forms of Glass Fibre Reinforcement (Shipyard Use)

- **Chopped Strand Mat (CSM)** — Random short fibres (25–50 mm) bonded with polyester binder; used in hand lay-up of small boat hulls.
- **Woven Roving (WR)** — Bi-directional cloth; provides balanced in-plane strength.
- **Unidirectional (UD) Tape** — All fibres aligned in 0° direction; used in stiffening stringers.
- **Multiaxial / Stitched Fabrics** — Non-crimp fabrics at ±45°/0°/90°; used in modern prepreg marine laminates.
- **Rovings** — Direct-feed bundles for pultruded gratings and filament winding of pressure hulls.

## 2.2 Carbon Fibres — Precursor Chemistry, Manufacturing & Properties

### 2.2.1 Precursor Comparison (Exam-Critical)

| Precursor | Carbon Yield (%) | Resulting Fibre Modulus | Marine Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PAN (Polyacrylonitrile)** | 50 – 60 | Medium – Very High | **Dominant grade** |
| **Pitch (Petroleum/Coal tar)** | 80 – 90 | Ultra-High (up to 900 GPa) | High-stiffness masts |
| **Rayon (Cellulose)** | 25 – 30 | Low – Medium | Largely obsolete |

### 2.2.2 PAN-Based Carbon Fibre Manufacturing — Four-Stage Pyrolysis

1. **Spinning (Precursor Fibre Formation)** — PAN copolymer is wet-, dry-, or melt-spun into a white precursor tow of ~12,000 filaments (12K).
2. **Stabilization (Oxidation, 200 – 300 °C)** — Tow is stretched in air at **200 – 300 °C** for 30–120 minutes; the linear PAN cyclizes into a ladder polymer, preventing melting in the next stage. Resulting fibre: **black, flame-resistant "pre-oxidized" tow**.
3. **Carbonization (Low-Temp: 300 – 800 °C; High-Temp: 800 – 1,600 °C)** — Tow passes through inert nitrogen atmosphere; non-carbon atoms (H, N, O) are driven off as HCN, NH₃, CO₂, H₂O, leaving a turbostratic carbon structure. At this point, fibre is classified as **"Carbon Fibre"** (Type HT, IM, or HM).
4. **Graphitization (1,800 – 3,000 °C, optional)** — Further heating under tension in inert argon aligns the crystallites into a graphitic lattice, increasing modulus to **600+ GPa** for **UHM (Ultra-High Modulus)** grades.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why tension during pyrolysis?** The applied tensile stress aligns the turbostratic carbon basal planes parallel to the fibre axis. This alignment is the *single most important* structural feature that gives carbon fibre its legendary axial stiffness. Without tension, the resulting fibre is weak and "woody".

### 2.2.3 Surface Treatment & Sizing for Marine Compatibility

- **Electrochemical Oxidative Etching** — The fibre surface is roughened in nitric acid or ammonium bicarbonate, creating active -COOH and -OH groups.
- **Sizing Application** — An epoxy- or polyurethane-compatible size is applied to allow bonding with vinylester or epoxy resins typically used in marine laminates.

> [!WARNING]
> **Galvanic Corrosion Trap (KTU Examiner Favourite):**
> Carbon fibre is *electrically conductive*. When used in contact with aluminium, bronze, or steel marine fittings in saltwater, it forms a galvanic cell that aggressively corrodes the metal. **Always use dielectric isolation (e.g., G-10 phenolic or nylon washers)** at the joint interface.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Mechanical & Physical Indices

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Specific Strength} &= \frac{\sigma_{uts}}{\rho} \quad \text{[units: MPa·cm³/g]} \\[6pt]
\text{Specific Modulus} &= \frac{E}{\rho} \quad \text{[units: GPa·cm³/g]} \\[6pt]
\text{Microfibre Strength (Griffith Criterion)} \quad \sigma_f &\approx \sqrt{\frac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi a}} \\[6pt]
\text{Aspect Ratio (Critical)} \quad \frac{L}{d} &\geq \frac{\sigma_f}{2 \tau_i} \\[6pt]
\text{Volume Fraction to Modulus Rule of Mixtures} \quad E_c &= E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f) \\[6pt]
\text{Fibre Packing Efficiency} \quad V_{f,\max} &= \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.907 \quad \text{(hexagonal close pack)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Where $\sigma_{uts}$ is ultimate tensile strength, $\rho$ is density, $E$ is Young's modulus, $\gamma_s$ is surface energy, $a$ is flaw size, $L/d$ is fibre length-to-diameter ratio, $\tau_i$ is interfacial shear strength, $V_f$ is fibre volume fraction, and the subscripts $c, f, m$ denote composite, fibre, and matrix.

### Real-World Utility of These Equations in Shipbuilding

- **Specific Strength & Modulus** drive weight-reduction calculations for racing yachts and naval combatants.
- **Griffith Criterion** explains why smaller-diameter fibres (e.g., 7 µm vs. 24 µm) are stronger — fewer surface flaws per unit length.
- **Critical Aspect Ratio** determines whether a fibre is used as *chopped* (low $L/d$, isotropic) or *continuous* (high $L/d$, anisotropic).
- **Rule of Mixtures** is used by shipyard design offices to predict laminate stiffness before manufacture.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Rule of Mixtures for Composite Longitudinal Modulus

### Statement
The longitudinal Young's modulus of a unidirectional fibre-reinforced composite loaded parallel to the fibre axis is given by:

$$
E_c = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)
$$

### Derivation (Iso-Strain Assumption)

**Step 1 — Define the iso-strain (Voigt) condition.**
When load is applied parallel to continuous, well-bonded fibres, both the fibre and matrix experience the *same* strain $\varepsilon_c$:

$$
\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m
$$

**Step 2 — Express the load carried by the composite.**
By static equilibrium, the total load $P_c$ is the sum of the loads carried by fibre and matrix:

$$
P_c = P_f + P_m
$$

**Step 3 — Convert loads to stresses using cross-sectional areas.**
Each load is the product of stress and the area it acts on:

$$
\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m
$$

**Step 4 — Express stresses via Hooke's Law.**
For linearly elastic constituents:

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon
$$

Substituting into Step 3, and dividing by $A_c$:

$$
E_c \varepsilon_c = E_f \varepsilon_f \frac{A_f}{A_c} + E_m \varepsilon_m \frac{A_m}{A_c}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply iso-strain condition and volume fractions.**
Since $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$, and by definition $V_f = A_f / A_c$ and $V_m = A_m / A_c = 1 - V_f$:

$$
E_c = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)
$$

This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The rule of mixtures is *linear* in $V_f$. Therefore, to maximize $E_c$, the designer must maximize $V_f$ — which in practice is limited by fibre packing geometry. The hexagonal close-pack theoretical limit is **90.7 %**, but real marine laminates rarely exceed **60 – 65 %** due to resin-rich interlaminar regions.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Specific Strength Calculation for a GRP Hull Laminate

**Given:** A shipyard laminates a hull panel using E-glass rovings at $V_f = 0.50$ within a polyester resin matrix ($V_m = 0.50$). 
- E-glass: $\sigma_f = 2,400$ MPa, $\rho_f = 2.60$ g/cm³
- Polyester: $\sigma_m = 55$ MPa, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³

**Find:** The composite's specific strength and compare with structural steel ($\sigma_{steel} = 410$ MPa, $\rho_{steel} = 7.85$ g/cm³).

### Solution — Step 1: Composite Strength (Rule of Mixtures)

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m
$$

Substituting:

$$
\sigma_c = (2{,}400)(0.50) + (55)(0.50) = 1{,}200 + 27.5 = 1{,}227.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

### Solution — Step 2: Composite Density (Rule of Mixtures for Density)

$$
\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = (2.60)(0.50) + (1.20)(0.50) = 1.30 + 0.60 = 1.90 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

### Solution — Step 3: Specific Strength of GRP Laminate

$$
\text{Specific Strength}_{GRP} = \frac{\sigma_c}{\rho_c} = \frac{1{,}227.5 \text{ MPa}}{1.90 \text{ g/cm}^3} \approx 646.1 \text{ MPa·cm}^3/\text{g}
$$

### Solution — Step 4: Specific Strength of Structural Steel

$$
\text{Specific Strength}_{steel} = \frac{410}{7.85} \approx 52.2 \text{ MPa·cm}^3/\text{g}
$$

### Solution — Step 5: Comparison

$$
\text{Performance Ratio} = \frac{646.1}{52.2} \approx 12.4
$$

> **Result:** The GRP laminate offers **~12.4× the specific strength** of structural steel. A 12-metre hull panel weighing 800 kg in GRP would weigh nearly 10 tonnes in steel — a massive weight saving critical for fuel economy and speed.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection Decision Tree for Marine Reinforcement

| Application Scenario | Recommended Fibre | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Recreational fishing boat hull (< 15 m) | E-glass CSM + WR | Lowest cost, easy hand lay-up |
| Naval fast-attack craft (40+ knots) | S-glass or R-glass | High impact tolerance |
| America's Cup racing yacht hull | Carbon UD + Core | Stiffness-to-weight critical |
| Submarine sonar dome | Carbon + syntactic foam | Acoustic transparency |
| Yacht mast (above 20 m) | Carbon prepreg | Buckling resistance |
| Lifeboat (SOLAS) | E-glass with fire-retardant resin | Fire/smoke compliance |
| Subsea pipeline (ROV-towed) | Carbon fibre overwrap | Corrosion immunity |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Glass Fibre Manufacturing Flow (Direct Melt Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Batch: Silica + Limestone + Kaolin + Boric Oxide] --> B[Gas-Fired Furnace]
    B --> C[Homogenized Molten Glass at 1400 deg C]
    C --> D[Platinum-Rhodium Bushing Plate]
    D --> E[Filament Attenuation at 15-60 m/s]
    E --> F[Silane Coupling Sizing Application]
    F --> G[Stranding into Rovings]
    G --> H[Collet Winding at 1500 m/min]
    H --> I[Finished Glass Fibre Yarn]
    I --> J[Marine Application: GRP Hull Laminate]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Carbon Fibre Pyrolysis Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[PAN Copolymer] --> P2[Wet Spinning]
    P2 --> P3[White Precursor Tow 12K]
    P3 --> P4[Stabilization Oven 200-300 deg C in Air]
    P4 --> P5[Black Pre-oxidized Tow]
    P5 --> P6[Low-Temp Carbonization 300-800 deg C in N2]
    P6 --> P7[High-Temp Carbonization 800-1600 deg C in N2]
    P7 --> P8[Type HT HM IM Carbon Fibre]
    P8 --> P9{Graphitization Required?}
    P9 -->|Yes| P10[1800-3000 deg C in Argon Under Tension]
    P9 -->|No| P11[Skip to Surface Treatment]
    P10 --> P12[Ultra-High Modulus UHM Fibre]
    P12 --> P11
    P11 --> P13[Electrochemical Oxidative Etching]
    P13 --> P14[Epoxy Sizing Application]
    P14 --> P15[Finished Carbon Fibre Tow]
    P15 --> P16[Marine Composite Lay-up]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Comparative Decision Architecture for Marine Reinforcement Selection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[New Marine Composite Design] --> Q2{Cost-Sensitive?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3[Select E-Glass]
    Q2 -->|No| Q4{Weight-Critical?}
    Q4 -->|No| Q5[Select S-Glass or R-Glass]
    Q4 -->|Yes| Q6{Stiffness or Strength Priority?}
    Q6 -->|Strength| Q7[PAN-based HS Carbon Fibre]
    Q6 -->|Stiffness| Q8[PAN-based HM or Pitch UHM Carbon]
    Q3 --> R1[Use Polyester or Vinylester Resin]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Marine Composite In-Service] --> F2{Loading Type}
    F2 -->|Tensile| F3[Fibre Breakage at Flaw Site]
    F2 -->|Compressive| F4[Micro-buckling and Kink Bands]
    F2 -->|Shear| F5[Matrix Cracking at 45 deg Ply]
    F2 -->|Impact| F6[Delamination Between Plies]
    F3 --> F7[Catastrophic Split at Griffith Flaw]
    F4 --> F8[Wrinkling in Compressive Skin]
    F5 --> F9[Resin Starvation Region Failure]
    F6 --> F10[Water Ingress and Osmotic Blistering]
    F10 --> F11[Fibre-Matrix Debonding via Wicking]
    F11 --> F12[Hull Weakening Over Time]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper]
**Differentiate between E-glass and S-glass fibres. Why is E-glass preferred over S-glass in general-purpose ship hull construction?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | E-Glass | S-Glass |
|---|---|---|
| Composition | Alumino-borosilicate | Higher SiO₂ + MgO |
| Tensile Strength | 1,900 – 3,500 MPa | 4,300 – 4,800 MPa |
| Tensile Modulus | 70 – 90 GPa | 85 – 95 GPa |
| Cost (Relative) | 1× (baseline) | 4 – 6× |
| Max Service Temp | 550 °C | 750 °C |

**E-glass is preferred for general-purpose ship hulls** because of its **lower cost (4 – 6× cheaper than S-glass)** and its **adequate mechanical performance** for typical recreational and commercial vessels. The marginal strength improvement of S-glass does not justify its price premium for non-military, non-racing applications. Furthermore, E-glass is easier to process in hand lay-up operations commonly used in shipyards. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Paper]
**Explain why carbon fibres are used in racing yacht hulls but not in ordinary fishing boats. Mention at least three specific reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Extreme weight saving:** Carbon fibre has a specific strength ~5× that of steel and ~3× that of E-glass. For racing yachts, every kilogram saved translates directly to higher speed. **(1 mark)**

2. **High stiffness and reduced hull flex:** The high modulus (200 – 600 GPa) minimises hull deformation under hydrodynamic loads, preserving the hydrodynamic profile and reducing wetted surface at speed. **(1 mark)**

3. **Cost-prohibitive for commercial use:** Carbon fibre costs **10 – 20× more per kg** than E-glass, making it uneconomical for fishing boats where cost-per-kg is the dominant design driver. Additionally, carbon fibre requires **refrigerated storage of prepregs** and **autoclave or oven curing**, which is beyond the capability of small fishing-boat builders. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Option 1) — Comprehensive Fibre Analysis [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Pattern]

**(a) Describe with a neat flow diagram the manufacturing process of PAN-based carbon fibre. Explain the importance of the stabilization stage.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Spinning (1 mark):**
PAN copolymer is dissolved in dimethylformamide (DMF) or dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and wet- or dry-spun through spinneret holes into a coagulation bath, forming white precursor filaments of ~12,000 filaments per tow (12K).

**Step 2 — Stabilization / Pre-oxidation (2 marks):**
The white precursor tow is passed through a series of heated ovens at **200 – 300 °C in an air atmosphere** for 30 – 120 minutes under controlled tension. The linear PAN chains undergo **cyclization** (intramolecular ring formation) and **oxidation** to form a **ladder polymer** structure.

**Importance of Stabilization (2 marks):**
- It converts thermoplastic PAN into a **thermoset ladder polymer**, preventing the fibre from melting or fusing during the high-temperature carbonization stage.
- It introduces oxygen-containing functional groups (-C=O, -OH) that improve final fibre adhesion to epoxy matrices.
- It pre-stresses the incipient aromatic structure, promoting basal-plane alignment.

**Step 3 — Carbonization (1 mark):**
In an inert nitrogen atmosphere, the stabilized tow is heated progressively from 300 °C to 1,600 °C. Non-carbon atoms (H, N, O) are expelled as HCN, NH₃, CO₂, and H₂O, leaving a **turbostratic carbon** structure with tensile strength 3,000 – 7,000 MPa.

**Step 4 — Surface Treatment & Sizing (1 mark):**
The carbonized fibre is electrochemically oxidized in nitric acid or ammonium bicarbonate to create surface -COOH groups, then coated with an epoxy-compatible size to enable resin bonding in marine laminates.

---

**(b) A marine grade composite is made of 55 % by volume of carbon fibre ($E_f = 230$ GPa, $\sigma_f = 4,000$ MPa) and 45 % epoxy matrix ($E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\sigma_m = 65$ MPa). Calculate the longitudinal Young's modulus and ultimate tensile strength of the composite. Comment on the result.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Longitudinal Modulus (Rule of Mixtures) — 4 marks:**

$$
E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m
$$

Substituting:

$$
E_c = (230)(0.55) + (3.5)(0.45)
$$

$$
E_c = 126.5 + 1.575
$$

$$
E_c = 128.075 \text{ GPa} \approx 128 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Longitudinal Tensile Strength (Rule of Mixtures) — 2 marks:**

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m
$$

Substituting:

$$
\sigma_c = (4{,}000)(0.55) + (65)(0.45)
$$

$$
\sigma_c = 2{,}200 + 29.25
$$

$$
\sigma_c = 2{,}229.25 \text{ MPa} \approx 2{,}229 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):**
The composite's stiffness is dominated almost entirely by the carbon fibre contribution (126.5 GPa out of 128 GPa, i.e., ~99 %), confirming the *isostrain* model prediction that the high-modulus fibre phase controls axial mechanical behaviour. The matrix contribution to stiffness is negligible (< 2 %), but it remains essential for load transfer between fibres and for protecting them from environmental damage.

---

### Question B (Option 2) — Comparative Materials Selection [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Expected Pattern]

**(a) Compare the properties, manufacturing methods, and marine applications of glass fibres and carbon fibres.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Glass Fibre (E-glass) | Carbon Fibre (PAN-HS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Precursor** | Molten mineral silicate | Polyacrylonitrile (PAN) |
| **Density (g/cm³)** | 2.55 | 1.78 |
| **Tensile Strength (MPa)** | 2,400 | 4,000 |
| **Tensile Modulus (GPa)** | 76 | 230 |
| **Specific Strength (MPa·cm³/g)** | ~940 | ~2,250 |
| **Elongation at Break (%)** | 4.8 | 1.8 |
| **Max Service Temp (°C)** | 550 | 600 (inert) |
| **Manufacturing** | Direct melt + attenuation | 4-stage pyrolysis (spinning → stabilization → carbonization → graphitization) |
| **Cost (per kg, relative)** | 1× | 10 – 20× |
| **Recyclability** | Moderate | Difficult |
| **Electrical Conductivity** | Insulator | Conductor |
| **Marine Applications** | GRP hulls, decks, superstructures, lifeboats | Racing yachts, hydrofoils, sonar domes, masts |

**Commentary (1 mark):** Glass fibre is the *workhorse* of the marine composites industry due to its low cost and ease of processing, while carbon fibre is reserved for weight-critical, high-performance applications.

---

**(b) Discuss four key reasons why E-glass fibre is the dominant reinforcement material in the shipbuilding industry. What is the role of silane coupling agents in marine composites?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Four Reasons for E-Glass Dominance (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Lowest cost among high-performance fibres:** E-glass is the most affordable reinforcement fibre offering high strength, typically costing 10 – 20× less than carbon fibre on a per-kg basis.

2. **Excellent mechanical properties for marine service:** With tensile strength of 1,900 – 3,500 MPa and good impact resistance, E-glass adequately meets the structural demands of recreational and commercial vessels up to 50 m in length.

3. **Ease of processing in shipyard conditions:** E-glass is compatible with **hand lay-up, spray-up, vacuum bagging, and resin infusion (VARTM)** — methods readily available in most shipyards without capital-intensive autoclaves.

4. **Chemical and saltwater resistance:** The low-alkali composition and silane sizing make E-glass resistant to hydrolysis, ensuring long service life in marine environments (typically 20 – 30 years for a well-maintained GRP hull).

**Role of Silane Coupling Agents (3 marks):**

Silane coupling agents (e.g., **γ-methacryloxypropyltrimethoxysilane**) are bifunctional molecules with two reactive groups:

- One end (**Si-OR alkoxy group**) bonds to the **inorganic glass surface** via Si-O-Si linkages.
- The other end (**methacrylate / epoxy / vinyl group**) bonds to the **organic polymer matrix** during cure.

This creates a **chemical bridge** between the fibre and matrix, accomplishing three critical functions:
1. **Improved interfacial load transfer** — the matrix stress is efficiently transmitted to the fibre.
2. **Prevention of "wicking" failure** — moisture cannot travel along the fibre-matrix interface, blocking osmotic blistering.
3. **Enhanced long-term durability in saltwater** — the covalent interface resists hydrolytic debonding that would otherwise degrade the composite over decades of service.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks (Module 3 — Glass & Carbon Fibres):**
>
> 1. **Confusing E-glass with S-glass composition:** E-glass is *electrical-grade alumino-borosilicate*, NOT soda-lime. Soda-lime is A-glass. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
>
> 2. **Missing the tension-in-pyrolysis point:** When asked about carbon fibre properties, students often forget to mention that fibres are held **under tension during stabilization and carbonization**, which is *why* the basal planes align. This omission costs 1 – 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Applying the wrong rule of mixtures:** For transverse modulus or for non-unidirectional lay-ups, the *inverse rule of mixtures* or *Halpin-Tsai* equation is required. Using the longitudinal formula transversely is a **2-mark error**.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the sizing in manufacturing:** Many students describe the fibre-drawing step but omit the silane sizing — which is *the* critical step for marine durability. Examiners expect both halves of the answer.
>
> 5. **Neglecting galvanic corrosion:** Carbon fibre + metal fitting + seawater = aggressive galvanic corrosion. This is a frequent **3-mark question** in Part A, and students often miss it entirely.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Glass & Carbon Fibres:**
>
> - **E-glass** is the **dominant marine reinforcement** (70 % of all marine composites), with tensile strength ~2,400 MPa and density 2.55 g/cm³.
> - **S-glass and R-glass** are higher-strength grades used in **naval and high-performance vessels**.
> - **C-glass** is chemical-resistant; **A-glass** is rare in marine use due to high alkali content.
> - Glass fibre manufacturing uses the **direct melt process** at **1,250 – 1,580 °C** with **platinum-rhodium bushings** containing up to **8,000 micro-holes**.
> - **Filament diameter is 5 – 25 µm**, drawn at **15 – 60 m/s**, wound at **1,000 – 3,000 m/min**.
> - **Silane coupling agents** create a chemical bridge between inorganic glass and organic resin, preventing wicking failure in marine service.
> - **Carbon fibres** are **92 – 95 % carbon**, derived from **PAN (50 – 60 % yield)**, **pitch (80 – 90 % yield)**, or **rayon (25 – 30 % yield)**.
> - The **four-stage PAN pyrolysis** is: **Spinning → Stabilization (200 – 300 °C, air) → Carbonization (300 – 1,600 °C, N₂) → Optional Graphitization (1,800 – 3,000 °C, Ar)**.
> - **PAN-based carbon fibre density** is **1.75 – 1.95 g/cm³**, strength **3,000 – 7,000 MPa**, modulus **200 – 600 GPa**.
> - **Pitch-based carbon fibres** can reach **900 GPa** modulus (UHM grade).
> - **Tension during pyrolysis** is essential for basal-plane alignment along the fibre axis — *the* critical structural feature.
> - **Galvanic corrosion** is a major pitfall when carbon fibre contacts metals in seawater; **dielectric isolation** is mandatory.
> - The **Rule of Mixtures** governs longitudinal composite modulus: $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$.
> - The **hexagonal close-pack theoretical maximum** fibre volume fraction is **90.7 %**, but practical marine laminates achieve **60 – 65 %**.
> - **Forms of glass reinforcement:** Chopped Strand Mat (CSM), Woven Roving (WR), Unidirectional (UD) tape, Multiaxial fabrics, Rovings.
> - **Glass fibre fails by fibre breakage at Griffith flaws**; **carbon fibre fails by micro-buckling under compression** (the dominant in-service failure mode in masts and hull skins).
> - **Critical aspect ratio** $L/d \geq \sigma_f / 2\tau_i$ determines whether chopped or continuous fibre is used.
> - **Real shipyard context:** GRP fishing boat hulls use E-glass CSM + WR; America's Cup yachts use carbon UD + core; submarine sonar domes use carbon + syntactic foam.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: REINFORCEMENT FORMS — FABRICS AND MATS IN COMPOSITE SHIPBUILDING

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 SYLLABUS HIGHLIGHT**
> In FRP (Fibre Reinforced Plastic) ship construction, the **reinforcement phase** is the load-bearing skeleton embedded in a polymer matrix. This reinforcement is supplied in the form of **fabrics** (woven/knitted/interlocked continuous fibres) and **mats** (discontinuous, randomly oriented fibre networks).

### Formal Definition (KTU Terminology)

A **fabric** is a manufactured planar assembly of continuous reinforcement fibres (glass, carbon, aramid, or basalt) arranged in a predetermined, repeating geometric pattern — typically warp-and-weft (woven), interlooped (knitted), or stitch-bonded (non-woven) — used to provide **directional strength and stiffness** in composite laminates.

A **mat** is a planar, non-woven reinforcement consisting of **chopped or continuous fibres** distributed **randomly in-plane** and held together by a chemical binder or mechanical needling, designed to provide **quasi-isotropic strength** and rapid laminate build-up.

> [!NOTE]
> **Matrix Phase Reminder:** The reinforcement (fabric/mat) is impregnated with a **thermosetting resin** (typically polyester, vinyl ester, or epoxy in marine applications) and cured to form the FRP laminate.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Concrete & Steel Rebar" Intuition

Think of a shipbuilding FRP laminate like **reinforced concrete** used in civil engineering:
- The **resin** = cement paste (transfers load, protects fibres, gives shape).
- The **fabric/mat** = steel rebar mesh (carries the structural load).
- The **laying-up sequence** = positioning the rebar before pouring concrete.

A **woven fabric** is like a **steel wire mesh with ordered grid** (predictable, directional strength), whereas a **chopped strand mat (CSM)** is like a **pile of randomly scattered short steel fibres** (equal strength in all directions, but lower peak performance).

### Standard Metrics Used in Marine FRP

| Parameter | Typical Range (Glass FRP) | Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Fibre Volume Fraction** ($V_f$) | 0.25 – 0.45 | – |
| **Density of E-glass** | **2.55 – 2.60** | g/cm³ |
| **Density of cured laminate** | **1.5 – 1.8** | g/cm³ |
| **Areal weight of CSM** | **225, 300, 450, 600** | g/m² |
| **Tensile strength (woven roving + CSM hybrid)** | **150 – 250** | MPa |
| **Standard roll width** | 0.9 – 1.27 | m |

### Why This Topic Matters in Shipbuilding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marine Application Context:** Modern naval architectures (patrol boats, fast ferries, mine countermeasure vessels, superyacht hulls) are **60–80 % FRP by volume**. The choice of fabric vs mat vs hybrid lay-up directly determines:
> 1. **Hull girder stiffness** (resistance to hogging/sagging)
> 2. **Impact tolerance** (collision with floating debris)
> 3. **Blast resistance** (naval mine survivability)
> 4. **Weight & speed** (fuel efficiency)
> 5. **Osmosis resistance** (long-term sea-water durability)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Classification & Engineering Logic

## 2.1 Master Classification of Reinforcement Forms

Reinforcement textiles used in marine FRP are classified into **two super-families** and several sub-types:

### A. FABRICS (Continuous, Ordered Fibre Architectures)

#### (i) Woven Fabrics
- **Construction:** Two sets of yarns (warp = 0°, weft = 90°) interlaced on a loom.
- **Sub-types:**
  - **Plain weave** — simplest 1-over-1-under pattern, low drape, balanced.
  - **Twill weave** — diagonal pattern (e.g., 2/2 twill), better drape, higher strength.
  - **Satin weave** — long yarn floats (e.g., 4-harness satin), smoothest surface, highest strength conversion efficiency.
  - **Basket weave** — two warps over two wefts, thicker, more drape.
- **Marine grades:** E-glass, S-glass, aramid, carbon, basalt.

#### (ii) Woven Roving (WR)
- **Construction:** Heavy, coarse woven fabric using **roving yarns** (un-twisted bundles of continuous fibres).
- **Areal weight:** Typically **400 – 800 g/m²**.
- **KTU Note:** Woven roving is the **workhorse reinforcement of GRP ship hulls**, almost always used in combination with CSM (see hybrid laminate).

#### (iii) Unidirectional (UD) Fabric
- **Construction:** All fibres aligned in **one direction** (0°), held by a light weft or stitching.
- **Property:** Extreme strength along fibre direction ($\sigma_{axial}$ very high), very low transverse strength.
- **Marine use:** Stiffening local patches, mast and propeller shaft brackets.

#### (iv) Biaxial & Multiaxial Fabrics (Non-Crimp Fabrics / NCF)
- **Construction:** Layers of parallel fibres (0°, 90°, ±45°) stacked and **stitch-bonded** (no crimp from weaving).
- **Advantages:** Higher mechanical efficiency than woven (no fibre crimp); tailorable laminate design.

#### (v) Knitted Fabrics
- **Construction:** Looped (interlooped) yarns; very high drape and conformability.
- **Marine use:** Complex-curvature parts like radomes, ducting.

### B. MATS (Discontinuous, Random Fibre Architectures)

#### (i) Chopped Strand Mat (CSM) — ★ KTU Core Focus ★
- **Construction:** **E-glass strands chopped into 25–50 mm lengths**, deposited randomly on a conveyor, bonded with a **powder or emulsion binder** (typically polyester or acrylic resin binder, 3–6 % by weight).
- **Areal weight:** **225, 300, 450, 600 g/m²** (most common: 450 g/m²).
- **Fibre orientation:** Truly random in the plane → **quasi-isotropic** in-plane properties.
- **Resin uptake:** High (binder dissolves into styrene of polyester resin → good wet-out).
- **Drape:** Excellent — conforms to compound curves.
- **Marine role:** Provides **resin-rich corrosion barrier**, secondary strength, and rapid thickness build-up.

#### (ii) Continuous Strand Mat (CSM-c or Continuous Filament Mat)
- **Construction:** **Continuous swirled filaments** deposited in random pattern, bound together.
- **Property:** Higher strength than chopped strand mat, but lower drape.
- **Marine use:** Thicker laminates in one pass (e.g., hull bottoms up to 6 mm in single lay-up).

#### (iii) Surface Tissue / Veil
- **Construction:** Very light mat (10–50 g/m²) of fine filaments or synthetic fibres (polyester, nylon).
- **Marine role:** Outer 0.1–0.3 mm layer → **resin-rich, crack-resistant, osmosis-resistant skin** (critical for gel-coat backup in marine laminates).

#### (iv) Needled Mat
- **Construction:** Fibres mechanically entangled by barbed needles (no chemical binder).
- **Use:** Often a precursor for further processing or in thermoplastic composites.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Choices — Engineering Logic

| Reinforcement Form | Why Chosen | Why Avoided |
|---|---|---|
| **Plain weave fabric** | Stable, balanced, predictable | High crimp → 30–50 % strength loss |
| **Woven roving (WR)** | High strength, low cost, fast build-up | Poor surface finish; requires CSM backing |
| **UD fabric** | Maximum unidirectional strength | Zero transverse strength (weak) |
| **Biaxial NCF** | High mechanical efficiency, no crimp | Higher cost than woven roving |
| **CSM** | Isotropic, drapeable, resin-rich barrier, low cost | Lower fibre volume fraction, lower strength |
| **Continuous filament mat** | Faster thickness build-up | Poorer wet-out, lower drape |
| **Surface tissue** | Osmosis & crack resistance | Negligible structural strength |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Composite Mechanics Fundamentals

> [!NOTE]
> The following are the **core equations** used to evaluate fabric/mat reinforced laminates in KTU examination problems.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_f = \dfrac{w_f / \rho_f}{w_f / \rho_f + w_m / \rho_m}$ | Fibre volume fraction from weights & densities | – |
| 2 | $V_m = 1 - V_f - V_v$ | Matrix volume fraction (with voids) | – |
| 3 | $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | **Rule of Mixtures** — longitudinal modulus | GPa |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Inverse Rule of Mixtures — transverse modulus | 1/GPa |
| 5 | $\sigma_{Lu} = \sigma_{fu} V_f + \sigma'_m V_m$ | Longitudinal tensile strength (ROM) | MPa |
| 6 | $t_{lam} = \dfrac{n_{csm}\,t_{csm} + n_{wr}\,t_{wr}}{ }$ | Laminate thickness from layer count | mm |
| 7 | $w_{areal} = \dfrac{m}{A}$ | Areal weight of fabric/mat | g/m² |
| 8 | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Composite density (ROM) | g/cm³ |
| 9 | $V_v = 1 - \rho_{measured} / \rho_{theoretical}$ | Void volume fraction | – |
| 10 | $P_{crit} = \dfrac{2 \pi^2 E_c}{\lambda^2} \left( \dfrac{I}{A} \right)$ | Hull plate buckling (used in stiffened panels) | N |

> Where:
> $E_f$ = fibre modulus, $E_m$ = matrix modulus, $E_c$ = composite modulus
> $\sigma_{fu}$ = fibre ultimate tensile strength, $\sigma'_m$ = matrix stress at fibre failure strain
> $V_v$ = void fraction, $V_m$ = matrix fraction, $V_f$ = fibre fraction
> $\lambda$ = slenderness ratio, $I$ = second moment of area, $A$ = cross-sectional area

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hull laminate design:** A typical 10 mm GRP patrol-boat hull is a **sandwich lay-up**: outer gel-coat → surface tissue → CSM (450 g/m²) → WR (800 g/m²) → CSM (450 g/m²) → WR (800 g/m²) → CSM (450 g/m²) → inner surface tissue.
- **Failure analysis:** Identifying whether failure originated in the CSM, WR, or interface region helps forensic naval architects refine laminate schedules.
- **Classification society rules (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, IRS):** Specify **minimum $V_f$**, **minimum $V_v$ limits**, and **mandatory use of surface veil** for OSMOSIS protection.
- **Cost engineering:** CSM is ~30–40 % cheaper per kg than WR; trade-off studies balance cost vs structural performance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Problems

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Areal Weight of a Composite Laminate

**Given:** A marine FRP laminate is built up by stacking layers of reinforcement and impregnating with polyester resin. Determine the **final laminate thickness** and **fibre volume fraction**.

### Step 1: Define the inputs

Let the laminate consist of:
- $n_1$ plies of **chopped strand mat (CSM)** with areal weight $w_1 = 450 \text{ g/m}^2$
- $n_2$ plies of **woven roving (WR)** with areal weight $w_2 = 600 \text{ g/m}^2$

**Resin-to-glass ratio by weight** $R = 1.5$ (typical for hand lay-up marine laminate — meaning 1.5 kg resin per 1.0 kg glass).

**Material densities:**
- E-glass fibre: $\rho_f = 2.60 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 2600 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Cured polyester resin: $\rho_m = 1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 1200 \text{ kg/m}^3$

### Step 2: Compute the total fibre mass per square metre

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{glass} &= n_1 \cdot w_1 + n_2 \cdot w_2 \\
&= 2 \cdot 450 + 2 \cdot 600 \\
&= 900 + 1200 \\
&= 2100 \text{ g/m}^2 = 2.10 \text{ kg/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Each ply contributes its full areal weight; multiplying by the number of plies and summing across reinforcement types gives the total glass mass per unit area of laminate.

### Step 3: Compute the total resin mass per square metre

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{resin} &= R \cdot W_{glass} \\
&= 1.5 \cdot 2.10 \\
&= 3.15 \text{ kg/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The resin-to-glass ratio is defined by weight, so we simply multiply.

### Step 4: Compute the total mass per square metre

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{total} &= W_{glass} + W_{resin} \\
&= 2.10 + 3.15 \\
&= 5.25 \text{ kg/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Compute the total thickness of the laminate

Convert each mass contribution into a thickness by dividing mass per unit area by density:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{glass} &= \dfrac{W_{glass}}{\rho_f} = \dfrac{2.10}{2600} = 8.077 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.808 \text{ mm} \\
t_{resin} &= \dfrac{W_{resin}}{\rho_m} = \dfrac{3.15}{1200} = 2.625 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 2.625 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{lam} &= t_{glass} + t_{resin} \\
&= 0.808 + 2.625 \\
&= 3.433 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Thickness is mass-per-area divided by density. This assumes **zero void content** (a theoretical ideal); in practice $V_v > 0$ would add to the actual thickness.

### Step 6: Compute the fibre volume fraction

By definition, $V_f$ is the volume of fibres divided by total composite volume. Volume per unit area is equivalent to thickness per unit area.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_f &= \dfrac{t_{glass}}{t_{lam}} \\
&= \dfrac{0.808}{3.433} \\
&= 0.2353 \text{ or } 23.53\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Volume fraction is a ratio of thicknesses (per unit area) because the area cancels out.

### Step 7: Cross-check using the alternative formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_f &= \dfrac{W_{glass}/\rho_f}{W_{glass}/\rho_f + W_{resin}/\rho_m} \\
&= \dfrac{2.10/2600}{2.10/2600 + 3.15/1200} \\
&= \dfrac{0.000808}{0.000808 + 0.002625} \\
&= \dfrac{0.000808}{0.003433} \\
&= 0.2353 \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The two methods must agree — the first uses the derived $t_{glass}$ and $t_{resin}$, the second uses the original weights and densities directly.

### Step 8: Final result summary

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Total glass mass per m² | **2.10 kg/m²** |
| Total resin mass per m² | **3.15 kg/m²** |
| Total laminate mass per m² | **5.25 kg/m²** |
| Laminate thickness | **3.43 mm** |
| Fibre volume fraction $V_f$ | **23.53 %** |

---

## 3.2 Worked Problem — Rule of Mixtures for CSM Laminate

**Problem:** A marine laminate has $V_f = 0.30$ of E-glass (E-glass modulus $E_f = 72$ GPa, strength $\sigma_{fu} = 2400$ MPa, density $\rho_f = 2.60$ g/cm³) and $V_m = 0.70$ of polyester resin ($E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\sigma'_m = 12$ MPa, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³). Compute:
1. Longitudinal modulus (assuming CSM is approximated as aligned in primary loading for a first estimate).
2. Composite density.
3. Longitudinal tensile strength.

**Solution:**

### (1) Longitudinal Modulus — Rule of Mixtures

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_L &= E_f \cdot V_f + E_m \cdot V_m \\
&= 72 \cdot 0.30 + 3.5 \cdot 0.70 \\
&= 21.6 + 2.45 \\
&= 24.05 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (2) Composite Density

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_c &= \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m \\
&= 2.60 \cdot 0.30 + 1.20 \cdot 0.70 \\
&= 0.78 + 0.84 \\
&= 1.62 \text{ g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** The measured density of marine GRP (1.5–1.8 g/cm³) typically exceeds this rule-of-mixtures value due to void content. Use this baseline as a **void-free reference**.

### (3) Longitudinal Tensile Strength

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{Lu} &= \sigma_{fu} \cdot V_f + \sigma'_m \cdot V_m \\
&= 2400 \cdot 0.30 + 12 \cdot 0.70 \\
&= 720 + 8.4 \\
&= 728.4 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Reality check:** Real CSM laminates achieve **150–250 MPa** (far less than 728 MPa) because CSM fibres are random — the ROM aligned-fibre strength over-predicts actual capacity by a factor of 3–5. Always apply a **knock-down factor** for CSM.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Reinforcement Forms

| Property | CSM (450 g/m²) | Woven Roving (600 g/m²) | Biaxial NCF (800 g/m²) | UD Carbon (300 g/m²) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fibre orientation | Random in-plane | 0° / 90° woven | 0° / 90° stitch-bonded | 0° only |
| $V_f$ achievable | 0.20 – 0.30 | 0.35 – 0.45 | 0.45 – 0.55 | 0.50 – 0.60 |
| Tensile strength (MPa) | 100 – 150 | 200 – 300 | 300 – 450 | 800 – 1200 (axial) |
| Tensile modulus (GPa) | 8 – 12 | 18 – 25 | 25 – 35 | 100 – 140 (axial) |
| Drape / conformability | Excellent | Moderate | Moderate | Poor |
| Resin wet-out speed | Fast | Slow | Moderate | Very slow |
| Cost (relative) | 1.0× | 1.4× | 2.5× | 8–12× |
| Marine primary use | Corrosion barrier, build-up | Main structural plies | High-performance hulls | Stiffeners, brackets |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flowchart — Marine Reinforcement Forms

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Marine FRP Reinforcement] --> B[Fabrics - Continuous Ordered Fibres]
    A --> C[Mats - Discontinuous Random Fibres]
    
    B --> B1[Plain Weave]
    B --> B2[Twill Weave]
    B --> B3[Satin Weave]
    B --> B4[Woven Roving WR]
    B --> B5[Unidirectional UD]
    B --> B6[Biaxial NCF]
    B --> B7[Multiaxial NCF]
    B --> B8[Knitted]
    
    C --> C1[Chopped Strand Mat CSM]
    C --> C2[Continuous Filament Mat CFM]
    C --> C3[Surface Tissue Veil]
    C --> C4[Needled Mat]
    
    B4 --> D1[High strength 0/90]
    B5 --> D2[Max axial strength]
    B6 --> D3[High mech efficiency]
    
    C1 --> E1[Quasi-isotropic]
    C1 --> E2[Resin rich barrier]
    C3 --> E3[Osmosis protection]
```

> **Reading guide:** The diagram separates **fabrics** (left branch) and **mats** (right branch). Note the divergent roles: fabrics provide *directional strength*; mats provide *quasi-isotropy* and *barrier function*.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Typical Marine Hull Laminate Schedule

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Outer Gel Coat - 0.4 to 0.6 mm] --> L2[Surface Tissue Veil - 30 g per m2]
    L2 --> L3[CSM 450 g per m2 - 1 ply]
    L3 --> L4[Woven Roving 600 g per m2 - 1 ply]
    L4 --> L5[CSM 450 g per m2 - 1 ply]
    L5 --> L6[Woven Roving 600 g per m2 - 1 ply]
    L6 --> L7[CSM 450 g per m2 - 1 ply]
    L7 --> L8[Surface Tissue Veil - 30 g per m2]
    L8 --> L9[Inner Gel Coat optional]
    
    L3 --> M1[Resin rich barrier]
    L4 --> M2[Primary load bearing]
    L6 --> M3[Primary load bearing]
```

> **Reading guide:** The bottom branch (`M1`, `M2`, `M3`) shows the **functional role** of each layer. CSM and veil form the **corrosion/osmosis-resistant envelope**; WR layers carry the **primary structural loads**.

## 4.3 Comparative Architecture — CSM vs Woven Roving

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph CSM
        A1[Random Chop 25-50mm] --> A2[Chopped Strand Mat]
        A2 --> A3[Isotropic in plane]
        A2 --> A4[Low Vf 0.20 to 0.30]
        A2 --> A5[Excellent drape]
    end
    
    subgraph WR
        B1[Continuous Roving Yarns] --> B2[Woven Roving]
        B2 --> B3[Directional 0 and 90]
        B2 --> B4[High Vf 0.35 to 0.45]
        B2 --> B5[Moderate drape]
    end
    
    A3 --> R[Combined into hybrid layup]
    A4 --> R
    A5 --> R
    B3 --> R
    B4 --> R
    B5 --> R
    R --> S[Marine Hull Laminate]
```

> **Reading guide:** This block diagram illustrates the **complementary roles** of CSM and WR, justifying why they are virtually always used together in marine laminates.

## 4.4 Process Flow — Hand Lay-up Fabrication

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Mold Preparation and Release Agent] --> S2[Gel Coat Application]
    S2 --> S3[Initial Cure of Gel Coat]
    S3 --> S4[Lay Surface Tissue Veil]
    S4 --> S5[Impregnate with Resin and Roll]
    S5 --> S6[Lay First CSM Layer]
    S6 --> S7[Wet Out and Consolidate with Roller]
    S7 --> S8[Lay Woven Roving]
    S8 --> S9[Repeat Sequence to Design Thickness]
    S9 --> S10[Final Surface Tissue and Gel Coat]
    S10 --> S11[Room Temperature Cure 24 to 48 h]
    S11 --> S12[Demold and Trim]
    S12 --> S13[Post Cure and Inspection]
```

> **Reading guide:** Each step in the hand lay-up is **mandatory**. Skipping roller consolidation creates voids; skipping surface tissue forfeits osmosis protection — both are common exam points and common real-world defects.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Chopped Strand Mat (CSM). List any three of its key characteristics that make it suitable for marine FRP laminates.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A Chopped Strand Mat (CSM) is a non-woven reinforcement consisting of **E-glass strands chopped into 25–50 mm lengths**, deposited randomly in the plane and bonded with a **powder or emulsion binder** (3–6 % by weight).

**Three key characteristics:**

1. **Quasi-isotropic in-plane properties** — random fibre orientation gives equal strength in all directions, ideal for biaxial hull stress states.
2. **Excellent drape** — conforms to compound ship-hull curves (e.g., chine, sheer line) without wrinkling.
3. **High resin uptake** — the binder dissolves in styrene, producing a **resin-rich, corrosion-resistant barrier** critical for marine osmosis protection.
4. *(optional extra)* Low cost and rapid wet-out speed up hand lay-up.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse CSM with woven roving. **CSM = chopped/discontinuous/random**; **WR = continuous/woven/orthogonal**. Mixing them up costs full marks on definition questions.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Compare woven roving and chopped strand mat in terms of fibre orientation, achievable fibre volume fraction, and primary structural role in a marine hull laminate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Woven Roving (WR) | Chopped Strand Mat (CSM) |
|---|---|---|
| Fibre orientation | Continuous, orthogonal (0°/90°) | Discontinuous, random in-plane |
| Achievable $V_f$ | 0.35 – 0.45 | 0.20 – 0.30 |
| Primary structural role | **Main load-bearing plies** | **Resin-rich corrosion barrier + secondary strength** |

In a typical ship hull, WR and CSM are used in **alternating hybrid lay-ups**: CSM provides the protective and isotropic envelope, while WR provides the **primary tensile/compressive load capacity**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Classify the different types of **fabrics and mats** used as reinforcement in marine FRP composites. Explain with neat sketches the construction of **woven roving, plain weave fabric, biaxial non-crimp fabric, and chopped strand mat**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A marine hull laminate is built using **3 plies of CSM (450 g/m²)** and **3 plies of WR (600 g/m²)** with a resin-to-glass weight ratio of **1.6:1**. Given $\rho_f = 2.60$ g/cm³ and $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³, calculate the **total thickness** and the **fibre volume fraction** of the laminate. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question A (a)** [7 marks]

**Classification of Reinforcements (3 marks):**

Reinforcement textiles for marine FRP are classified into two main groups:

1. **Fabrics** — continuous fibres in an ordered, repeating pattern.
   - Woven fabrics: **plain weave, twill weave, satin weave, basket weave**.
   - **Woven roving (WR)** — coarse, heavy fabric made from roving yarns.
   - **Unidirectional (UD) fabric** — fibres aligned in one direction.
   - **Biaxial / multiaxial non-crimp fabric (NCF)** — stitch-bonded layers of ±θ fibres.
   - **Knitted fabrics** — interlooped yarns, high drape.
2. **Mats** — discontinuous, randomly oriented fibres bonded together.
   - **Chopped strand mat (CSM)** — 25–50 mm chopped strands.
   - **Continuous filament mat (CFM)** — swirled continuous filaments.
   - **Surface tissue / veil** — very light mat for osmosis protection.
   - **Needled mat** — mechanically entangled fibres.

**Construction Descriptions (4 marks):**

- **Woven roving:** Coarse fabric where thick **roving yarns** (untwisted bundles of continuous E-glass filaments) are interlaced at 0° (warp) and 90° (weft). Provides high strength, but with fibre crimp causing 30–50 % strength loss compared to UD.
- **Plain weave fabric:** Simplest weave — each warp yarn alternates over and under each weft yarn (1-up, 1-down). Balanced and stable, but maximum crimp.
- **Biaxial non-crimp fabric (NCF):** Two layers of parallel fibres (0° and 90°) stacked and **stitch-bonded** with a polyester thread. No crimp — high mechanical efficiency, ideal for high-performance ship hulls.
- **Chopped strand mat (CSM):** E-glass rovings chopped into 25–50 mm lengths, **randomly deposited** on a moving belt, then bonded with a **powder or emulsion binder** and oven-cured. Provides quasi-isotropic strength, excellent drape, and acts as a resin-rich barrier layer.

*(Sketches are expected in the answer sheet — ASCII art or labelled line drawings of weave patterns are acceptable.)*

---

### **Model Answer — Question A (b)** [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $n_{CSM} = 3$, $w_{CSM} = 450$ g/m²
- $n_{WR} = 3$, $w_{WR} = 600$ g/m²
- Resin-to-glass weight ratio $R = 1.6$
- $\rho_f = 2.60$ g/cm³ = 2600 kg/m³
- $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³ = 1200 kg/m³

**Step 1 — Total glass mass per m²:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{glass} &= 3 \cdot 450 + 3 \cdot 600 \\
&= 1350 + 1800 \\
&= 3150 \text{ g/m}^2 = 3.15 \text{ kg/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total resin mass per m²:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{resin} &= 1.6 \cdot 3.15 = 5.04 \text{ kg/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total thickness (mass/area ÷ density):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{glass} &= \dfrac{3.15}{2600} = 1.212 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.212 \text{ mm} \\
t_{resin} &= \dfrac{5.04}{1200} = 4.200 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 4.200 \text{ mm} \\
t_{lam} &= 1.212 + 4.200 = 5.412 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Fibre volume fraction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_f &= \dfrac{t_{glass}}{t_{lam}} = \dfrac{1.212}{5.412} = 0.2240 \text{ or } 22.40\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $t_{lam} = 5.41$ mm; $V_f = 22.4\%$.

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Discuss the **role of chopped strand mat (CSM)** in marine GRP hull construction. Explain the construction of CSM, its typical areal weights, and **why CSM is almost always used in combination with woven roving (WR)** rather than alone. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CSM laminate has the following specification: $V_f = 0.28$, $V_m = 0.70$, $V_v = 0.02$. Calculate:
(i) The composite density, given $\rho_f = 2.60$ g/cm³ and $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³. **(3 marks)**
(ii) The longitudinal modulus using the rule of mixtures, given $E_f = 72$ GPa and $E_m = 3.5$ GPa. **(2 marks)**
(iii) The actual measured density is **1.55 g/cm³** instead of the theoretical value. Comment on the discrepancy and identify the most likely cause. **(2 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question B (a)** [7 marks]

**Construction of CSM (2 marks):** E-glass rovings are **chopped into 25–50 mm lengths**, then **randomly deposited** onto a moving conveyor belt. A **powder or emulsion binder** (3–6 % by weight, typically polyester- or acrylic-based) is applied, and the mat is passed through an oven to cure the binder and produce a coherent, handleable sheet. Standard **areal weights are 225, 300, 450, and 600 g/m²** (450 g/m² is most common in marine work).

**Role of CSM in Marine GRP Hulls (3 marks):**

1. **Resin-rich corrosion barrier** — CSM traps extra resin, forming a thick, crack-resistant, hydrolysis-resistant layer that prevents **osmosis blistering** (a major GRP failure mode in sea water).
2. **Quasi-isotropic strength** — random fibre orientation gives balanced in-plane mechanical properties, ideal for biaxial hull stress states (hydrostatic + slamming).
3. **Excellent drape** — the discontinuous fibres allow the mat to conform to compound ship-hull curves (chine, transom, keel) without wrinkling.
4. **Rapid wet-out** — the binder dissolves into the styrene monomer of polyester resin, accelerating hand lay-up.
5. **Secondary structural role** — provides interlaminar shear transfer and bonds the more directional WR layers together.

**Why CSM is used in combination with WR (2 marks):**
- CSM alone gives **low fibre volume fraction (0.20–0.30)** and **low strength (100–150 MPa)** — insufficient for primary hull loads.
- WR alone has **high directional strength** but is **anisotropic** (strong only along 0°/90°) and produces a **resin-starved surface** that is **prone to micro-cracking and osmotic attack**.
- A **hybrid CSM + WR + CSM + WR + …** sequence provides the **best of both**: a corrosion-resistant, isotropic envelope wrapping high-strength directional load-bearing plies. This is the **industry standard for marine GRP** and is required by classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, IRS, ABS).

---

### **Model Answer — Question B (b)** [7 marks]

**(i) Theoretical composite density (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{theory} &= \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m \\
&= 2.60 \cdot 0.28 + 1.20 \cdot 0.70 \\
&= 0.728 + 0.840 \\
&= 1.568 \text{ g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Note:* The void fraction is excluded from the rule-of-mixtures density calculation but contributes to the **measured** density discrepancy.

**(ii) Longitudinal modulus (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_L &= E_f V_f + E_m V_m \\
&= 72 \cdot 0.28 + 3.5 \cdot 0.70 \\
&= 20.16 + 2.45 \\
&= 22.61 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Comment on the discrepancy (2 marks):**

The measured density (1.55 g/cm³) is **lower** than the theoretical density (1.568 g/cm³) by about 0.018 g/cm³ (~1.1 %). The most likely cause is the **presence of voids** ($V_v = 0.02$, i.e., 2 % voids). Voids are pockets of air or gas trapped during hand lay-up, formed by:
- Inadequate **roller consolidation** during wet-out.
- Air entrapment between reinforcement layers.
- Solvent or styrene volatilization.
- Moisture contamination of reinforcement or resin.

**Engineering consequence:** Voids reduce the composite's mechanical strength, fatigue life, and moisture resistance. **Quality control limits** typically require $V_v < 0.02$ for marine structural laminates (per DNV and Lloyd's Register rules). Higher void content indicates poor fabrication and requires re-inspection or rejection of the laminate.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic**
>
> 1. **Confusing CSM with Woven Roving** — they are *not* interchangeable. CSM = random/chopped; WR = continuous/woven. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** — g/m² must be converted to kg/m³ for density calculations; cm³ ↔ m³ conversions are commonly skipped.
> 3. **Applying the Rule of Mixtures to CSM without a knock-down factor** — CSM fibres are *random*; the ROM over-predicts strength by 3–5×. Always state the assumption.
> 4. **Ignoring voids** — $V_v > 0$ affects density, strength, and durability. Always include $V_v$ in the volume balance: $V_f + V_m + V_v = 1$.
> 5. **Confusing areal weight with thickness** — g/m² is a *weight-per-area* metric, NOT a thickness. Thickness comes from $t = w/(\rho \cdot A)$.
> 6. **Missing the binder explanation** — when defining CSM, students often forget to mention the **chemical binder** (powder/emulsion). Examiners mark this as an incomplete definition.
> 7. **Not relating theory to shipbuilding** — KTU examiners reward **application context** (hull, osmosis, classification rules). Pure theory without marine relevance loses marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Fabrics and Mats**

- **Fabrics = continuous + ordered**; **Mats = discontinuous + random**. *Always* state both properties in definitions.
- **Woven roving (WR)** is the **primary structural reinforcement** in marine GRP. Common areal weight: **400–800 g/m²**. Uses roving yarns (un-twisted fibre bundles) at 0°/90°.
- **Chopped strand mat (CSM)** is the **workhorse secondary reinforcement**. Fibre length **25–50 mm**, areal weights **225/300/450/600 g/m²**, bonded with **3–6 % binder**.
- **CSM + WR hybrid** is the **industry standard** ship-hull lay-up — CSM forms the corrosion/osmosis barrier, WR carries the primary loads.
- **Surface tissue / veil** (10–50 g/m²) is the **outermost fibre layer** for **osmosis and crack resistance** in marine service — never omit it.
- **Biaxial / multiaxial NCF** offers higher mechanical efficiency (no crimp) than woven roving and is used in **high-performance naval vessels**.
- **Unidirectional (UD) fabric** provides maximum axial strength but **zero transverse strength** — never use alone in biaxial stress fields.
- **Rule of Mixtures (aligned case):** $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ ; **Inverse ROM (transverse):** $1/E_T = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$.
- **Composite density (theoretical):** $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ (voids excluded). **Measured density < theoretical density ⇒ voids present.**
- **Marine GRP density range:** **1.5–1.8 g/cm³**; **E-glass fibre density:** **2.55–2.60 g/cm³**; **cured polyester:** **~1.20 g/cm³**.
- **Voids must be < 2 %** in marine structural laminates (DNV / Lloyd's Register / ABS / IRS).
- **Resin-to-glass weight ratio** for hand lay-up marine laminates is typically **1.5 : 1 to 2.0 : 1**.
- **Fibre volume fraction $V_f$** achievable: CSM ~0.20–0.30; WR ~0.35–0.45; NCF ~0.45–0.55; UD carbon ~0.50–0.60.
- **CSM drape is excellent**; **WR drape is moderate**; **UD/NCF drape is poor** — important for compound-curvature ship parts.
- **Always convert units consistently:** g/m² → kg/m²; g/cm³ → kg/m³ (×1000). Density = mass/volume, thickness = (mass/area) ÷ density.
- **Knock-down factor for CSM:** Actual CSM strength is 3–5× lower than ROM prediction due to random fibre orientation and 2-D planar stress state.
- **Classification society rules** (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, IRS) mandate specific **minimum $V_f$**, **maximum $V_v$**, and **mandatory surface tissue** for marine laminates.
- **Hand lay-up sequence:** Gel coat → Veil → CSM → WR → CSM → WR → … → Veil → (optional inner gel coat) → Cure → Demold.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Woven Roving and Their Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Woven Roving (WR)** is a heavy, drapable, bidirectional reinforcement fabric manufactured by interlacing (weaving) **continuous, untwisted E-glass fibre rovings** in two mutually perpendicular directions — the **warp (0°)** and the **weft (90°)** — on a conventional loom, and bonding the intersections with a small amount of polymeric binder (typically polyester or styrene-soluble powder) to stabilise the weave.

It is the **primary structural reinforcement** used in the hand lay-up and vacuum-assisted moulding of single-skin and sandwich FRP hulls, decks, bulkheads, and superstructures of small-to-medium naval and commercial vessels. Because the rovings are continuous, straight, and aligned in two principal load directions, woven roving imparts high **in-plane tensile and flexural strength** at a low cost per unit area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Unit:** Woven roving is specified by its **areal weight (mass per unit area)** expressed in **g/m² (gsm)** or **oz/yd²**. Shipbuilding grades typically range from **300 gsm to 1200 gsm**, with **600 gsm and 800 gsm** being the most common hull-reinforcement weights.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are weaving a coarse **bamboo mat** for a village hut floor. You take long, un-twisted bamboo strips and run them lengthwise (the *warp*); then another set of strips is woven across them (the *weft*). The intersections lock the structure so that pulling in any direction in the plane of the mat is resisted by the fibres.

**Woven roving is exactly that — but the bamboo strips are replaced by bundles of ultra-fine glass filaments, and the mat is sealed with a powdered glue so it does not fray.**

- The *warp* rovings (running along the roll length) carry tensile loads along the length of the hull.
- The *weft* rovings (running across the roll width) carry transverse loads (e.g., hydrostatic pressure on the hull bottom).
- Because the rovings are *not* twisted, the glass filaments remain nearly straight → maximum tensile efficiency is retained (twisting shortens the effective load path and reduces strength by 15–20 %).

## 1.3 Why Woven Roving is Crucial in Shipbuilding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Rationale:**
> 1. **High strength-to-weight ratio** — Essential for displacement and speed.
> 2. **Corrosion immunity** — Eliminates the cathodic-protection burden of steel hulls.
> 3. **Drapeability** — Conforms to double-curvature hull surfaces during lay-up.
> 4. **Resin compatibility** — Bonds chemically with unsaturated polyester, vinyl ester, and epoxy resins.
> 5. **Economic viability** — Lowest cost reinforcement per unit of laminate strength.
> 6. **Marine durability** — When coupled with a surface veil (C-glass or polyester), resists osmotic blistering and water absorption.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy of a Woven Roving

A roll of woven roving is built up from the following hierarchical constituents:

| Level | Element | Description | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Filament | Single glass fibre (diameter) | 9 – 17 µm |
| 2 | Strand / Roving | Bundle of filaments gathered without twist (linear density) | 300 – 4800 tex |
| 3 | Warp | Rovings running along roll length | 50 % of fabric mass |
| 4 | Weft | Rovings running across roll width | 50 % of fabric mass |
| 5 | Binder | Styrene-soluble polyester powder sprayed on | 2 – 5 % of fabric mass |
| 6 | Roll | Finished fabric, width 100–127 cm, length 30–150 m | — |

## 2.2 Weave Patterns Used in Marine WR

Three basic weaves are used in shipbuilding laminates:

### (a) Plain Weave
- Each warp passes over **one** weft, then under the **next**, in alternation.
- **Maximum crimp (undulation) at intersections → reduced in-plane strength**, but the most stable fabric for handling.
- Used only for **lightweight, non-structural** laminates (≤ 300 gsm).

### (b) Twill Weave
- Warp passes over **two or more** wefts, creating a diagonal line.
- **Lower crimp → higher strength** than plain weave.
- Better drapeability for curved hull panels.

### (c) Basket / Leno Weave (most common in marine WR)
- Two or more warps cross two or more wefts as a unit.
- **High openness (large interstices)** → excellent **resin pick-up and wet-out** during hand lay-up.
- **Most widely used in shipbuilding** because rapid resin saturation is critical for thick laminates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Crimp** is the unavoidable undulation of a yarn as it crosses over and under perpendicular yarns. The greater the crimp, the more the fibre is **mis-aligned** from the ideal 0° / 90° orientation, which lowers the effective Young's modulus and tensile strength of the laminate.

## 2.3 Specification Parameters a KTU Examiner Expects

When a question asks *"Specify the woven roving suitable for a patrol-boat hull"*, the expected response must include:

1. **Fibre type** — E-glass (alumino-borosilicate, alkali < 1 %).
2. **Areal weight** — 600 / 800 / 1000 gsm.
3. **Weave** — Basket or leno.
4. **Warp : Weft ratio** — Balanced 50:50 (or 55:45 for slightly directional strength).
5. **Binder** — Styrene-soluble polyester, 2–5 %.
6. **Roll width** — 100 cm or 127 cm.
7. **Compatible resin** — Orthophthalic polyester, isophthalic polyester, or vinyl ester.
8. **Standard** — IS 11273, BS 3396, ISO 2113, ASTM D579.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise the following equations — they are recurring KTU question patterns.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W_{lam} = N_{ply} \times (W_{WR} + R_{content})$ | Weight of cured laminate per m² | g/m² |
| 2 | $V_f = \dfrac{\rho_m \cdot W_{WR}}{\rho_f \cdot W_{WR} + \rho_m \cdot R_{content}}$ | Fibre volume fraction (rule of mixtures, gravimetric form) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$ | Longitudinal Young's modulus (Voigt rule) | GPa |
| 4 | $E_T = \left[ \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{1 - V_f}{E_m} \right]^{-1}$ | Transverse Young's modulus (Reuss rule) | GPa |
| 5 | $\sigma_L^{UTS} = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m (1 - V_f)$ | Ultimate tensile strength (longitudinal) | MPa |
| 6 | $R_{content} = \dfrac{W_{WR}}{k - 1}$ where $k = $ fibre:resin mass ratio | Resin content by mass | g/m² |
| 7 | $t_{ply} = \dfrac{W_{WR}}{\rho_f V_f}$ | Thickness of a single WR ply | mm |
| 8 | $\sigma_{flex} = \dfrac{3 P L}{2 b t^2}$ | Flexural strength (3-point bend, ASTM D790) | MPa |

**Material Constants to Remember (E-glass / Polyester system):**

| Symbol | Quantity | Value |
|---|---|---|
| $\rho_f$ | Density of E-glass fibre | **2.54 – 2.62 g/cm³** |
| $\rho_m$ | Density of cured polyester resin | **1.20 – 1.25 g/cm³** |
| $E_f$ | Young's modulus of E-glass | **72 – 76 GPa** |
| $E_m$ | Young's modulus of polyester resin | **3.0 – 4.5 GPa** |
| $\sigma_f$ | Tensile strength of E-glass fibre | **2000 – 3500 MPa** |
| $\sigma_m$ | Tensile strength of polyester resin | **40 – 80 MPa** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never use $\rho_m = 1.0$ g/cm³ (this is water, not cured resin). Cured polyester resin is **denser than water** because of the styrene cross-linker and added filler.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in the Field

- **Hull scantling calculation** (Rule 16 of Lloyd's Register, or DNV GL): Woven roving laminates are specified by **stiffness** $E \cdot t$ and **section modulus** $Z$.
- **Laminate scheduling**: A typical 6 mm single-skin patrol-boat hull uses **[600 WR / CSM / 600 WR / CSM / 600 WR / CSM / 600 WR]** — alternating WR with chopped strand mat (CSM) to suppress print-through and improve inter-laminar shear strength.
- **Failure-mode control**: WR provides tensile strength; CSM provides resin-rich interlayer for toughness and crack arrest.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fibre Volume Fraction from Lamination Mass

A **hand-laid laminate** of single-ply woven roving of areal weight $W_{WR}$ is impregnated with resin of areal weight $R$. The cured laminate mass per unit area is therefore $W_{lam} = W_{WR} + R$.

**Step 1 — Express the mass of fibre in one m² of laminate**

By definition, $W_{WR}$ is the mass of dry fabric per square metre, so the fibre mass per m² is simply:

$$m_f = W_{WR} \quad [\text{g/m}^2]$$

**Step 2 — Express the mass of resin in one m² of laminate**

$$m_m = R = W_{lam} - W_{WR} \quad [\text{g/m}^2]$$

**Step 3 — Convert masses to volumes using densities**

$$V_f^{vol} = \dfrac{m_f}{\rho_f} = \dfrac{W_{WR}}{\rho_f}$$

$$V_m^{vol} = \dfrac{m_m}{\rho_m} = \dfrac{R}{\rho_m}$$

**Step 4 — Fibre volume fraction**

$$V_f = \dfrac{V_f^{vol}}{V_f^{vol} + V_m^{vol}} = \dfrac{\dfrac{W_{WR}}{\rho_f}}{\dfrac{W_{WR}}{\rho_f} + \dfrac{R}{\rho_m}}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $\rho_f \rho_m$**

$$V_f = \dfrac{W_{WR} \, \rho_m}{W_{WR} \, \rho_m + R \, \rho_f}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **gravimetric rule of mixtures** for fibre volume fraction. It appears almost verbatim in KTU 2017, 2019, and 2022 university examinations.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks):**
> A 600 gsm woven roving laminate contains 250 g/m² of cured resin after hand lay-up. Compute the fibre volume fraction. Take $\rho_f = 2.60$ g/cm³ and $\rho_m = 1.22$ g/cm³.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

Given data:
- $W_{WR} = 600$ g/m²
- $R = 250$ g/m²
- $\rho_f = 2.60$ g/cm³
- $\rho_m = 1.22$ g/cm³

Apply the derived formula:

$$V_f = \dfrac{W_{WR} \, \rho_m}{W_{WR} \, \rho_m + R \, \rho_f}$$

Substitute:

$$V_f = \dfrac{600 \times 1.22}{600 \times 1.22 + 250 \times 2.60}$$

Compute numerator:

$$600 \times 1.22 = 732.0$$

Compute denominator:

$$250 \times 2.60 = 650.0$$

$$732.0 + 650.0 = 1382.0$$

Therefore:

$$V_f = \dfrac{732.0}{1382.0} = 0.5297 \approx 0.530$$

$$V_f \approx \mathbf{53.0 \%}$$

**Result Interpretation:** About **half** the laminate volume is fibre, the other half is resin. This is typical of a well-consolidated 600 gsm WR laminate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb:** For a properly rolled WR laminate, expect $V_f$ in the range **0.45 – 0.55**. Below 0.40 → under-consolidation; above 0.60 → resin-starved and prone to micro-voiding.

## 3.3 Derivation 2 — Effective In-Plane Young's Modulus of a Woven Roving Laminate

A balanced woven roving laminate is *quasi-isotropic in the plane* if the warp and weft rovings are of equal tex. The in-plane modulus is the **arithmetic mean** of the longitudinal and transverse moduli:

$$E_{xy} = \dfrac{E_L + E_T}{2}$$

**Step 1 — Longitudinal modulus (fibres aligned with load):**

$$E_L = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$$

**Step 2 — Transverse modulus (fibres transverse to load):**

$$E_T = \left[ \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{1 - V_f}{E_m} \right]^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the quasi-isotropic expression:**

$$E_{xy} = \dfrac{1}{2} \left[ E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f) + \left( \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{1 - V_f}{E_m} \right)^{-1} \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **harmonic mean** form for $E_T$ originates from the **iso-strain ↔ iso-stress duality** of composite micromechanics. KTU examiners expect you to state which rule applies to which loading direction.

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Single-Ply Thickness

A single ply of 600 gsm woven roving with fibre volume fraction $V_f$ has thickness:

**Step 1 — Volume of fibre per m²:**

$$\text{Vol}_f = \dfrac{W_{WR}}{\rho_f} \quad [\text{cm}^3/\text{m}^2]$$

**Step 2 — Total laminate volume per m²** (divide fibre volume by $V_f$):

$$\text{Vol}_{lam} = \dfrac{\text{Vol}_f}{V_f} = \dfrac{W_{WR}}{\rho_f V_f}$$

**Step 3 — Thickness** (volume ÷ area, with $A = 1 \text{ m}^2 = 10^4 \text{ cm}^2$):

$$t_{ply} = \dfrac{\text{Vol}_{lam}}{10^4} = \dfrac{W_{WR}}{10^4 \rho_f V_f} \quad [\text{cm}]$$

Converting to mm (× 10):

$$\boxed{t_{ply} = \dfrac{W_{WR}}{1000 \, \rho_f V_f} \quad [\text{mm}]}$$

**Numerical check** for 600 gsm, $V_f = 0.53$, $\rho_f = 2.60$:

$$t_{ply} = \dfrac{600}{1000 \times 2.60 \times 0.53} = \dfrac{600}{1378} = 0.435 \text{ mm}$$

So **one ply of 600 gsm WR ≈ 0.44 mm** of cured laminate. A 6 mm hull therefore needs **≈ 14 plies** in total (when combined with interlaminar CSM).
