# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q:** Differentiate between **expendable mould** and **permanent mould** casting. Give **one example** of a non-ferrous ship component made by each.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Expendable Mould | Permanent Mould |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mould life | Single use (destroyed) | 1,000 – 100,000 cycles |
| Material | Sand + binder (silicone, resin, bentonite) | Cast iron / H13 tool steel |
| Lead time | Long | Short |
| Cost per unit | High (low volume) | Low (high volume) |
| Tolerance | ± 0.5 mm | ± 0.05 mm |
| Surface finish | Ra 6.3 – 25 µm | Ra 0.8 – 3.2 µm |
| Ship example | Marine propeller (sand) | Al-Si cylinder head (die cast) |

**Exam Tip (2 lines):**
- Write the difference in a **table** – KTU examiners award full marks if the table is clean.
- Always give a **ship-specific example** to show application context.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q:** Define **Chvorinov's Rule** and state its **significance** in riser design for non-ferrous castings.

**Model Answer:**
Chvorinov's Rule states that the **solidification time of a casting** is proportional to the square of the ratio of volume to surface area (the modulus).

$$
t_s = B \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^{2}
$$

**Significance in riser design:**
- The **riser** is sized so that its solidification time is **greater than** that of the casting.
- This ensures the riser remains **molten** long enough to feed liquid metal into the casting as it shrinks.
- Chvorinov's Rule gives the **quantitative basis** to compute riser dimensions using the criterion $M_r \geq 1.2 \cdot M_c$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE (End Semester Examination) Note:** Part B questions carry 14 marks. As per KTU 2024 Scheme norms, there is an **internal choice** between two sub-questions. Here, **Q. A** OR **Q. B** must be attempted.

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **Investment Casting (Lost-Wax) Process** with a neat block diagram. List its **five major advantages** and **three limitations** for non-ferrous ship components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **gunmetal (Cu-Sn-Zn) valve body** for a marine pipeline is to be cast in a green sand mould. The casting has volume $V = 18{,}000 \text{ cm}^3$ and surface area $A = 4200 \text{ cm}^2$. The mould constant is $B = 3.8 \text{ s/cm}^2$. Determine:
- (i) The **modulus** of the casting.
- (ii) The **solidification time** of the casting.
- (iii) The **minimum diameter** of a top cylindrical riser (aspect ratio $H_r/D_r = 1.5$). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Definition (1 mark)
Investment casting uses a **disposable wax pattern** and a **ceramic shell mould** built around it. After wax removal, molten metal is poured into the ceramic shell.

**Step 2:** Sequential steps (4 marks – 0.5 each):
1. **Wax pattern production** — Injected into a metal die (Al-alloy).
2. **Tree assembly** — Multiple wax patterns welded to a central wax sprue.
3. **Primary ceramic coating** — Tree dipped in silica-sol + zircon slurry + stuccoed with coarse grains.
4. **Secondary coatings (6–8 layers)** — Successive stuccoing builds shell thickness to 6–15 mm.
5. **Wax removal (dewaxing)** — Autoclave at 7 bar steam (150 °C) → wax drains out.
6. **High-temperature firing** — 1100 °C, 2 hr → burns residual wax, sinters ceramic.
7. **Hot pouring** — Molten bronze / gunmetal poured into the still-hot shell (>500 °C).
8. **Knockout** — Shell is vibrated or hammered off; casting is cut from the tree.

**Step 3:** Advantages (1 mark – list any 5):
- Near-net shape (no machining required for most surfaces)
- Excellent surface finish (Ra 1.6 – 3.2 µm)
- Complex geometry possible (undercuts, thin walls, internal cavities)
- Tight dimensional tolerance (± 0.1 mm on small parts)
- Wide range of non-ferrous alloys can be cast
- No parting line

**Step 4:** Limitations (1 mark – list any 3):
- Long process cycle (5–7 days)
- High cost for small parts
- Size limitation (typically < 50 kg per casting)
- Skilled labour required for wax assembly

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
$V = 18{,}000 \text{ cm}^3$, $A = 4200 \text{ cm}^2$, $B = 3.8 \text{ s/cm}^2$, $H_r/D_r = 1.5$

**(i) Modulus of casting:**

$$
M_c = \dfrac{V}{A} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{4200} = 4.2857 \; \text{cm}
$$

**(ii) Solidification time:**

$$
t_s = B \cdot M_c^{2} = 3.8 \cdot (4.2857)^{2}
$$

Compute $(4.2857)^2 = 18.367 \; \text{cm}^2$

$$
t_s = 3.8 \cdot 18.367 = 69.79 \; \text{s} \approx 70 \; \text{s}
$$

**(iii) Riser diameter:**

Riddel's criterion: $M_r = 1.2 \cdot M_c = 1.2 \cdot 4.2857 = 5.143 \; \text{cm}$

From Section 3.3.2 derivation with $\alpha = 1.5$:

$$
D_r = 2 M_r \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{2}{\alpha}\right) = 2 \cdot 5.143 \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{2}{1.5}\right) = 10.286 \cdot 2.333 = 24.0 \; \text{cm}
$$

**Final Answers (boxed):**

$$
\boxed{\, M_c = 4.29 \; \text{cm}, \quad t_s \approx 69.8 \; \text{s}, \quad D_r \approx 24.0 \; \text{cm} \,}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain **Centrifugal Casting** with a labelled sketch. Derive the expression for **centrifugal pressure** at any radius $r$ and state its significance for marine condenser liners. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Green Sand Moulding** and **Shell Moulding** under the following heads: (i) binder used, (ii) mould material, (iii) surface finish, (iv) dimensional tolerance, (v) production rate, (vi) typical ship application. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Introduction (1 mark)
Centrifugal casting is a process in which molten metal is poured into a **rotating mould** (RPM 300–1200). Centrifugal force drives the metal against the mould wall, where it solidifies radially inward. It is used for **hollow cylindrical components** (pipes, liners, bushings).

**Step 2:** Types (1 mark)
- **True centrifugal casting** (axis horizontal, no core) — pipes.
- **Semi-centrifugal casting** (axis vertical, central core allowed) — gear blanks.
- **Centrifuge casting** — multiple small castings arranged around the axis.

**Step 3:** Derivation of centrifugal pressure (4 marks)

Consider a fluid element of mass $dm = \rho \cdot A \cdot dr$ at radius $r$. The centrifugal force is:

$$
dF_c = dm \cdot \omega^{2} \cdot r = \rho \cdot A \cdot dr \cdot \omega^{2} \cdot r
$$

This force acts on the liquid column of cross-section $A$ extending from inner radius $r_0$ to outer radius $r$. The pressure difference is:

$$
P(r) - P(r_0) = \int_{r_0}^{r} \rho \cdot \omega^{2} \cdot r \, dr
$$

With $P(r_0) = 0$ (free surface):

$$
P(r) = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \omega^{2}}{2} \cdot (r^{2} - r_0^{2})
$$

**Step 4:** Significance for marine condenser liners (1 mark)
- Centrifugal pressure (1–5 bar) ensures **complete filling** of mould.
- Promotes **directional solidification** from outer wall inward → sound, dense casting.
- Eliminates need for cores → **no core shift, no core gas defects**.
- Outer surface is **fine-grained** → improved corrosion resistance in seawater.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table:**

| S.No | Parameter | Green Sand Moulding | Shell Moulding |
| :--: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) | Binder | Bentonite clay + water | Thermosetting phenolic resin (6 %) |
| (ii) | Mould material | Silica sand (SiO₂ > 95 %), moisture 4–8 % | Resin-coated silica sand, fused into a shell (6–25 mm thick) |
| (iii) | Surface finish | Ra 6.3 – 25 µm | Ra 1.6 – 3.2 µm |
| (iv) | Dimensional tolerance | ± 0.5 – 1.0 mm | ± 0.1 – 0.3 mm |
| (v) | Production rate | Low (mould destroyed each cycle) | Higher (backing flask reused, only shell replaced) |
| (vi) | Typical ship application | 9 m manganese-bronze propeller, large stern-tube | Pump impeller, small bronze valve body, marine hardware |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in "Different Types of Moulding Techniques" questions:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in Chvorinov's equation** – mixing cm and m. Always write units of $V/A$ explicitly before squaring. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing Chvorinov's exponent $n$** – writing $n = 1$ instead of $n = 2$. The exponent is **empirically 2** for sand moulds. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Forgetting to state mould constant units** – $B$ is **not dimensionless**. (–1 mark)
> 4. **In investment casting, students forget the "hot pouring" step** – pouring at room temperature causes thermal shock and ceramic shell fracture. (–1 mark)
> 5. **In centrifugal casting, students draw the mould horizontal but label it "vertical"** – true centrifugal casting is **always horizontal-axis**. (–1 mark)
> 6. **In riser design, students forget the 1.2 safety factor** in Riddel's criterion. (–1 mark)
> 7. **No neat labelled diagram** – a 14-mark question **must** have a block diagram or sketch. (–2 marks if missing)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Chvorinov's Rule** is the **most-tested derivation** — memorise $t_s = B \cdot (V/A)^2$ and the meaning of $B$.
- [x] **Riddel's criterion** for riser: **$M_r \geq 1.2 \cdot M_c$**.
- [x] **Green sand** has moisture **4–8 %**; **dry sand** has moisture **< 0.5 %**; **skin-dried** has dried surface up to 5 mm.
- [x] **Shell moulding (Croning)** uses a **heated pattern (200–250 °C)** and a **dump box** of **resin-coated sand**; shell thickness **6–25 mm**.
- [x] **Investment casting** uses **wax + ceramic shell**; dewaxing in **autoclave at 7 bar, 150 °C**; firing at **1100 °C**.
- [x] **Die casting** types: **Gravity, Pressure (hot/cold chamber), Low-Pressure** — LPDC uses **0.5–1.5 bar** furnace pressure.
- [x] **Centrifugal casting** centrifugal pressure: $P(r) = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \omega^{2}}{2} \cdot (r^{2} - r_0^{2})$.
- [x] **Continuous casting** uses a **water-cooled copper mould** and produces long, uniform cross-section billets.
- [x] For non-ferrous **ship propellers**: **Green Sand Moulding** is the only feasible method for sizes up to 9 m.
- [x] For **marine pump impellers**: **Shell Moulding** gives the smoothest hydrodynamic surface.
- [x] For **valve bodies** with intricate internal passages: **Investment Casting** is preferred.
- [x] For **Cu-Ni condenser liners**: **Centrifugal Casting** — outer surface is fine-grained, corrosion-resistant in seawater.
- [x] For **Al-Si marine cylinder heads**: **Low-Pressure Die Casting (LPDC)** gives the best combination of strength and weldability.
- [x] **Density of Cu-Ni (90/10)** ≈ **8.9 g/cm³**; **Al-bronze** ≈ **7.6 g/cm³**; **Manganese bronze** ≈ **8.5 g/cm³**.
- [x] Always quote **pouring temperature** as $T_{pour} = T_m + \Delta T_{super}$; for bronze $\Delta T_{super} = 100–150$ °C.
- [x] The **modulus $V/A$** has the **same unit as length** — always state it (cm or m) before squaring in Chvorinov.
- [x] In a KTU 14-mark question, always include a **block diagram + derivation + numerical + application to ship** — examiners reward cross-linkage to shipbuilding explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lay-up Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lay-up Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — LAY-UP TECHNIQUES IN SHIPBUILDING COMPOSITES

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lay-up Techniques** are a family of **composite manufacturing processes** in which reinforcing fibers (glass, carbon, aramid, or natural fibers) are systematically positioned, oriented, and consolidated within a liquid or pre-catalyzed **polymer matrix** (typically polyester, vinylester, or epoxy resin) to produce a monolithic, load-bearing laminate. In the KTU 2024 shipbuilding context, lay-up techniques specifically refer to the fabrication of **non-ferrous, fiber-reinforced polymer (FRP) structures** that replace or supplement traditional ferrous (steel) hulls, decks, bulkheads, and superstructure components.

The term *"lay-up"* originates from the literal placement (laying) of reinforcement plies (plies) one over the other (up), each impregnated with a thermosetting resin that subsequently cures to form a rigid, corrosion-resistant composite skin. The technique is governed by the principles of **fiber architecture**, **resin chemistry**, and **pressure/temperature consolidation kinetics**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Layered Cake & Mortar" Model

Imagine you are baking a **structural layered cake**:

- The **cake batter** is the **liquid resin** (e.g., polyester resin mixed with catalyst MEKP — Methyl Ethyl Ketone Peroxide).
- The **layers of bread or wafers** are the **fiber reinforcements** (woven roving, chopped strand mat, or unidirectional fabric).
- Each layer is **soaked** in the batter and stacked carefully, ensuring **no air pockets** (which would be analogous to **voids** — the #1 defect in composites).
- The whole assembly is then **baked (cured)** in an oven or at room temperature, transforming the wet stack into a hard, rigid, load-bearing cake.
- A **vacuum bag** placed over the cake before baking acts like a **press**, squeezing out excess batter and air, producing a denser, stronger final product.

> [!NOTE]
> In shipbuilding, this "cake" is a **boat hull**, and the oven is either ambient shipyard temperature (cold cure) or a heated mold. A poorly laid-up hull is like a cake full of air pockets — it will crack under wave impact loading.

### 1.3 Why Lay-up Techniques Matter in Non-Ferrous Shipbuilding

The shift from **ferrous (steel)** to **non-ferrous (FRP, aluminum, composites)** in shipbuilding is driven by:

- **Corrosion resistance** — FRP hulls do not rust in saltwater (rule of thumb: a well-maintained GRP hull lasts **30–50 years** vs. **15–25 years** for unprotected steel).
- **Weight reduction** — Glass fiber composites have a density of approximately **1.5–2.0 g/cm³** compared to steel's **7.85 g/cm³** (a **~75% weight saving**).
- **Fatigue performance** — composites exhibit superior fatigue resistance under cyclic wave loading.
- **Design flexibility** — complex double-curvature hull forms achievable in a single lay-up operation.
- **Reduced maintenance** — no anti-fouling paint systems required on internal structures.

### 1.4 Classification of Lay-up Techniques (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

| **Category** | **Process** | **Capital Intensity** | **Shipbuilding Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Open Mold** | Hand Lay-up | Low | Small boats, patrol craft, repair |
| **Open Mold** | Spray Lay-up | Low | Decks, bulkheads, large panels |
| **Closed Mold** | Vacuum Bagging | Medium | Hulls, radar domes |
| **Closed Mold** | Resin Transfer Molding (RTM) | Medium-High | Mass production of small craft |
| **Closed Mold** | Vacuum Infusion (VARTM/SCRIMP) | Medium | Yacht hulls, mine countermeasure vessels |
| **Filament-Based** | Filament Winding | High | Pressure hulls, masts, tanks |
| **Automated** | Automated Tape Laying (ATL) | Very High | Large composite decks, naval ships |
| **Pre-impregnated** | Prepreg Lay-up + Autoclave | Very High | High-performance naval structures |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 emphasizes **Hand Lay-up, Spray Lay-up, Vacuum Bagging, Vacuum Infusion, Resin Transfer Molding (RTM), and Filament Winding** as the **core lay-up techniques** relevant to shipbuilding. Students must be conversant with the **process steps, defects, fiber volume fractions, and quality control metrics** of each.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Process Analysis — The Six Core Lay-up Techniques

### 2.1.1 Hand Lay-up (Wet Lay-up) — The Workhorse of Shipbuilding

**The Process — Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **Mold Preparation** — A female (or male) mold is treated with **release agent (wax or PVA — Polyvinyl Alcohol)** to prevent adhesion. The mold surface dictates the **cosmetic finish** of the laminate's outer skin.
2. **Gelcoat Application** — A pigmented **gelcoat** (typically 0.4–0.8 mm thick, isophthalic polyester or vinylester) is sprayed or brushed onto the mold. This becomes the **outer aesthetic and protective layer** of the hull, providing UV resistance and waterproofing.
3. **First Reinforcement Layer** — A **surface mat (50 g/m² CSM — Chopped Strand Mat)** is laid onto the wet gelcoat. This thin, binder-rich layer masks the print-through of subsequent coarse fabrics.
4. **Resin Application** — Liquid **thermosetting resin** (polyester, vinylester, or epoxy) pre-mixed with **catalyst (1–2% MEKP by weight)** and **accelerator (cobalt octoate, 0.5–1%)** is poured and spread with a **roller**.
5. **Fiber Placement & Consolidation** — **Woven roving, unidirectional fabric, or CSM** is laid down and **debulked** (compacted) using **rollers and squeegees** to remove entrapped air and wet-out the fibers completely.
6. **Layer Build-up** — Steps 4 and 5 are repeated until the **design laminate thickness (typically 6–25 mm for ship hulls)** is achieved.
7. **Curing** — The laminate cures at **room temperature (exothermic reaction, peak temperature 60–80 °C)** or in a heated mold (40–60 °C for post-cure).
8. **Demolding & Trimming** — After cure (typically 24 hours at 25 °C for full demold strength), the part is removed and trimmed.

**Why It Works (The Chemistry Behind It):**
The MEKP catalyst decomposes into **free radicals** that initiate the **cross-linking (polymerization)** of the unsaturated polyester/vinylester chains. This **addition polymerization** is **exothermic** and **irreversible**, transforming a liquid resin into a rigid, three-dimensional thermoset network.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Hand" Lay-up?** Because **human operators** manually place each ply, control resin content, and consolidate. This makes it labor-intensive but **extremely versatile** for large, complex hull shapes.

### 2.1.2 Spray Lay-up

In this variant, a **chopper gun** simultaneously:
- Sprays **continuous rovings** that are **chopped into short fibers (10–40 mm length)** by an integrated cutter.
- Co-sprays **atomized resin + catalyst** from a separate nozzle.
- The combined chopped fiber/resin stream deposits onto the mold surface.

**Key Engineering Trade-off:** Spray lay-up is **faster** than hand lay-up but produces laminates with **lower fiber volume fraction (typically 20–30%)** and **shorter, randomly oriented fibers** — resulting in **lower mechanical properties** (especially in-plane stiffness and strength).

### 2.1.3 Vacuum Bagging (Vacuum-Assisted Lay-up)

A **flexible polymeric film (nylon, polyethylene, or fluoropolymer)** is placed over the wet lay-up and sealed to the mold with **tacky tape**. A vacuum pump evacuates the air beneath the bag, generating **atmospheric pressure (~101 kPa or 1 atm)** that consolidates the laminate.

**Key Equations Governing Bag Consolidation:**

$$P_{\text{bag}} = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{absolute}}$$

$$P_{\text{consolidation}} \approx 90 \text{ to } 100 \text{ kPa (practical)}$$

The compaction pressure dramatically:
- Reduces **void content** (from ~5% in hand lay-up to <1% in vacuum bagging).
- Increases **fiber volume fraction** (from 30–40% to 50–55%).
- Improves **fiber wet-out** and **interlaminar adhesion**.

### 2.1.4 Vacuum Infusion (VARTM / SCRIMP / RTM Light)

**VARTM = Vacuum Assisted Resin Transfer Molding.** This is the **gold standard for large marine structures**.

**Process Logic:**
1. Dry reinforcement (fabrics, cores) is placed in the mold.
2. A **flow media** (mesh or grooved core) is placed on top to distribute resin.
3. A **vacuum bag** is sealed, and **full vacuum (typically -95 kPa gauge)** is applied.
4. **Liquid resin** is drawn into the mold via the vacuum, impregnating the fibers from one end to the other.
5. After full wet-out, the resin is **cured** (room temp or heated).

**Why This Works:** The vacuum creates a **pressure differential (ΔP ≈ 95–100 kPa)** that drives resin flow through the fiber preform, following **Darcy's Law for porous media flow**.

### 2.1.5 Resin Transfer Molding (RTM)

A **closed, matched-die mold** is used. Dry fibers are placed inside, the mold is clamped shut, and resin is **injected under pressure (typically 1–7 bar)**. Heated molds (40–80 °C) accelerate cure. RTM produces parts with **excellent surface finish on both sides** and **tight dimensional tolerances**.

### 2.1.6 Filament Winding

Continuous fibers (rovings) are pulled through a **resin bath** and wound onto a **rotating mandrel** at precise, computer-controlled angles (**winding angle, typically ±10° to ±89° from the mandrel axis**). After cure, the mandrel is extracted. In shipbuilding, filament winding is used for **cylindrical pressure hulls** (submarines, ROV housings), **masts**, and **storage tanks**.

**Winding Angle Geometry:**

$$\tan(\alpha) = \frac{\pi D}{L}$$

where $\alpha$ is the winding angle, $D$ is the mandrel diameter, and $L$ is the lead (axial advance per revolution).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Master these equations — at least 2–3 will appear in any 14-mark lay-up technique question. Memorize units and typical value ranges.

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Typical Value (Shipbuilding FRP)** | **Significance** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fiber Volume Fraction** | $V_f$ | $V_f = \dfrac{w_f / \rho_f}{w_f / \rho_f + w_m / \rho_m}$ | 30–55% | Higher $V_f$ ⇒ higher strength & stiffness |
| **Void Content** | $V_v$ | $V_v = 1 - (V_f + V_m)$ | <1% (infusion), 2–5% (hand lay-up) | Voids are the #1 killer of fatigue life |
| **Resin-to-Fiber Ratio (by weight)** | $R_{wf}$ | $R_{wf} = \dfrac{w_m}{w_f}$ | 1:1 to 1:3 | Too much resin = brittle, heavy laminate |
| **Laminate Density (Rule of Mixtures)** | $\rho_c$ | $\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m$ | 1.5–1.9 g/cm³ | Used for weight & buoyancy calculations |
| **Laminate Thickness** | $t$ | $t = \dfrac{w_{\text{total}}}{\rho_c \cdot A}$ | 6–25 mm (hulls) | Determines structural stiffness |
| **Theoretical Tensile Strength (Rule of Mixtures)** | $\sigma_c$ | $\sigma_c = V_f \sigma_f + V_m \sigma_m$ (longitudinal, UD) | 200–600 MPa (GFRP) | Valid for **unidirectional** fibers only |
| **Longitudinal Young's Modulus (ROM)** | $E_c$ | $E_c = V_f E_f + V_m E_m$ | 15–40 GPa (GFRP) | Iso-strain (Voigt) model |
| **Transverse Young's Modulus (Inverse ROM)** | $E_T$ | $\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | 5–12 GPa (GFRP) | Iso-stress (Reuss) model |
| **Resin Viscosity (Cure Kinetics)** | $\eta$ | Arrhenius: $\eta = \eta_0 \exp\left(\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | 200–500 cP at 25 °C | Determines flow & wet-out |
| **Darcy's Law (Resin Flow in Infusion)** | $Q$ | $Q = -\dfrac{K \cdot A}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$ | K ≈ 10⁻¹⁰ to 10⁻¹² m² | Governs infusion filling time |
| **Fiber Areal Weight** | $W_A$ | $W_A = \dfrac{w_f}{A}$ | 200–800 g/m² (woven roving) | Used for ply-by-ply weight estimation |
| **Catalyst (Initiator) Concentration** | $C_{\text{cat}}$ | Mass fraction | 1–2% MEKP (polyester) | Controls gel time & exotherm |
| **Gel Time** | $t_{\text{gel}}$ | Empirical: $t_{\text{gel}} \propto \dfrac{1}{C_{\text{cat}}^{\,n}}$ | 15–60 minutes at 25 °C | Working time available for lay-up |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** Questions often ask for **comparison** between lay-up methods (e.g., "Compare hand lay-up and VARTM in terms of fiber volume fraction, void content, and mechanical properties"). Use the formula sheet to **quantify** your answers — examiners reward numbers over vague qualitative claims.

## 2.3 Why These Equations Matter in Real Shipbuilding

- **$V_f$ (Fiber Volume Fraction)** directly determines whether a hull meets **classification society rules** (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS) for scantlings. Naval architects specify $V_f$ as a contractual requirement.
- **$V_v$ (Void Content)** is the **single most regulated quality parameter** — most shipyards enforce $V_v < 2\%$ via ultrasonic C-scan non-destructive testing (NDT).
- **$\rho_c$ (Laminate Density)** governs **displacement, draft, and stability** calculations — critical for any vessel.
- **Darcy's Law** determines **infusion time** — a 20 m yacht hull may require 4–8 hours of infusion time, affecting production scheduling.

# 🧮 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Fiber Volume Fraction from First Principles

> **Problem Context:** A shipbuilding laminate is built from **5 plies of woven roving**, each with **areal weight $W_A = 600 \text{ g/m}^2$**, and a total **resin content of 1.5 kg/m²**. Given: $\rho_f = 2.55 \text{ g/cm}^3$ (E-glass), $\rho_m = 1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3$ (polyester resin). Compute the **fiber volume fraction** and the **void content**, assuming a cured laminate area of $A = 1 \text{ m}^2$.

### Step 1: Define Mass Components Per Unit Area

The total fiber mass per unit area:

$$w_f = N \cdot W_A = 5 \times 600 = 3000 \text{ g/m}^2 = 3.0 \text{ kg/m}^2$$

The total resin mass per unit area is given:

$$w_m = 1.5 \text{ kg/m}^2 = 1500 \text{ g/m}^2$$

### Step 2: Convert Mass Per Unit Area to Volume Per Unit Area

Volume of fibers per unit area (thickness contribution):

$$\frac{V_f}{A} = \frac{w_f}{\rho_f} = \frac{3000 \text{ g/m}^2}{2.55 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

To convert units, note that $1 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 1000 \text{ g/(m} \cdot \text{cm}^2)$, but more directly:

$$\frac{w_f}{\rho_f} = \frac{3.0 \text{ kg/m}^2}{2550 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 1.1765 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2 = 1.1765 \text{ mm (thickness)}$$

### Step 3: Compute Resin Volume Per Unit Area

$$\frac{V_m}{A} = \frac{w_m}{\rho_m} = \frac{1.5 \text{ kg/m}^2}{1200 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2 = 1.25 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 4: Compute Total Cured Laminate Thickness (Sum of Volumetric Contributions)

$$t = \frac{V_f + V_m}{A} = 1.1765 + 1.25 = 2.4265 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 5: Apply Fiber Volume Fraction Definition

By definition:

$$V_f = \frac{V_f / A}{V_{\text{total}} / A} = \frac{V_f / A}{(V_f + V_m) / A}$$

Substituting:

$$V_f = \frac{1.1765 \text{ mm}}{2.4265 \text{ mm}} = 0.4848 = 48.48\%$$

### Step 6: Compute Matrix Volume Fraction

$$V_m = 1 - V_f = 1 - 0.4848 = 0.5152 = 51.52\%$$

### Step 7: Compute Void Content

Since voids occupy the remaining volume not filled by fiber or matrix:

$$V_v = 1 - (V_f + V_m) = 1 - (0.4848 + 0.5152) = 0.0000 = 0.00\%$$

In this idealized example, $V_v = 0$ because we assumed full consolidation. In practice, $V_v \approx 2\text{–}5\%$ for hand lay-up.

### Step 8: Sanity Check via Density (Rule of Mixtures)

$$\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m = (0.4848)(2550) + (0.5152)(1200)$$

$$\rho_c = 1236.2 + 618.2 = 1854.4 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 1.854 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

This density is **physically realistic** for a glass-polyester marine laminate (typical range: **1.5–1.9 g/cm³**). ✔

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A $V_f$ of ~48% is **typical for a well-consolidated hand lay-up** with moderate roller compaction. A **vacuum-infused** version of the same laminate would achieve $V_f \approx 55\text{–}60\%$ with significantly less resin consumption.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Theoretical Laminate Strength — Longitudinal (Iso-Strain) Loading

> **Problem Context:** A unidirectional (UD) E-glass/polyester laminate used for a **ship's longitudinal stiffener** is loaded in tension along the fiber direction. Compute the **rule-of-mixtures (ROM) tensile strength**. Given: $V_f = 0.50$, $\sigma_f = 2400 \text{ MPa}$ (E-glass), $\sigma_m = 60 \text{ MPa}$ (polyester), $V_v = 0.02$ (2% voids).

### Step 1: State the Governing Assumption (Iso-Strain / Voigt Model)

In **iso-strain** loading, fiber and matrix experience the **same strain** $\varepsilon$:

$$\varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_c$$

The composite stress is the **volume-weighted sum** of fiber and matrix stresses:

$$\sigma_c = V_f \sigma_f + V_m \sigma_m$$

### Step 2: Account for Void Content (Modified ROM)

Voids carry **no load**, so the effective load-bearing volume is reduced:

$$\sigma_c = V_f \sigma_f + V_m \sigma_m = V_f \sigma_f + (1 - V_f - V_v) \sigma_m$$

### Step 3: Substitute Numerical Values

$$V_f = 0.50, \quad V_v = 0.02, \quad V_m = 1 - 0.50 - 0.02 = 0.48$$

$$\sigma_c = (0.50)(2400) + (0.48)(60)$$

$$\sigma_c = 1200 + 28.8 = 1228.8 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 4: Apply a Strength Knock-Down for Voids (Empirical Correction)

A common empirical correction for void-induced strength reduction is:

$$\sigma_c^{\text{actual}} = \sigma_c^{\text{ROM}} \cdot (1 - k \cdot V_v)$$

where $k \approx 5\text{–}10$ is an empirical constant (let $k = 7$ for hand lay-up):

$$\sigma_c^{\text{actual}} = 1228.8 \times (1 - 7 \times 0.02) = 1228.8 \times 0.86 = 1056.8 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 5: Interpret the Result

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **unidirectional** glass-polyester laminate theoretically achieves **>1 GPa tensile strength** in the fiber direction. However, in a **real ship hull**, fibers are **multi-directional** (woven, ±45° layers), and the **actual strength** drops to **200–400 MPa** because the load is not aligned with all fibers simultaneously. This is why **fiber orientation** is a critical design parameter in marine laminates.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Infusion Filling Time via Darcy's Law

> **Problem Context:** A 10 m long yacht hull deck is being infused with epoxy resin at a pressure differential of $\Delta P = 90 \text{ kPa}$. The preform permeability is $K = 5 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^2$, viscosity $\mu = 0.35 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, preform thickness $h = 10 \text{ mm}$, and flow channel width $w = 1.5 \text{ m}$. Estimate the **filling time** $t_{\text{fill}}$.

### Step 1: State Darcy's Law for 1D Flow in a Porous Preform

$$v = -\dfrac{K}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$$

where $v$ is the superficial (Darcy) velocity, $K$ is permeability, $\mu$ is viscosity, and $L$ is the flow front position.

### Step 2: Convert to Volumetric Flow Rate

$$Q = v \cdot A = v \cdot (h \cdot w)$$

### Step 3: Express Flow Front Position vs. Time

The flow front position $x(t)$ is found by integrating $v$:

$$x(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot K \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot \phi} \cdot t}$$

where $\phi \approx 0.5$ is the preform porosity.

### Step 4: Solve for Filling Time

Set $x(t_{\text{fill}}) = L_{\text{flow}} = 10 \text{ m}$:

$$t_{\text{fill}} = \dfrac{\mu \cdot \phi \cdot L^2}{2 \cdot K \cdot \Delta P}$$

### Step 5: Substitute Numerical Values

$$t_{\text{fill}} = \dfrac{(0.35)(0.5)(10)^2}{2 \times (5 \times 10^{-11}) \times (90 \times 10^3)}$$

$$t_{\text{fill}} = \dfrac{0.35 \times 0.5 \times 100}{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-11} \times 9 \times 10^4}$$

$$t_{\text{fill}} = \dfrac{17.5}{9 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.944 \times 10^6 \text{ seconds}$$

$$\boxed{t_{\text{fill}} \approx 1.94 \times 10^6 \text{ s} \approx 540 \text{ hours} \approx 22.5 \text{ days}}$$

### Step 6: Sanity Check & Practical Implication

> [!WARNING]
> **This result is unreasonably long** for a 10 m deck — clearly our permeability is too low. In practice, shipbuilders use **flow media** (permeability $K \approx 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2$) that reduces the effective flow path. Recomputing with $K_{\text{eff}} = 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2$:

$$t_{\text{fill}} \approx \dfrac{17.5}{2 \times 10^{-8} \times 9 \times 10^4} = \dfrac{17.5}{1.8 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 9722 \text{ s} \approx 2.7 \text{ hours}$$

This is **realistic** — a 10 m deck typically infuses in 2–4 hours. The **flow media** is therefore **engineered** to bypass the preform's low permeability and distribute resin rapidly.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Defect Diagnosis for Each Lay-up Technique

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked *"What are the defects of process X?"*, use this matrix as your mental template. For **full marks**, link each defect to its **root cause** and **remedy**.

| **Process** | **Primary Defects** | **Root Cause** | **Remedy** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hand Lay-up** | Voids, dry spots, resin-rich pockets, fiber mis-orientation | Inadequate rolling, operator error, high resin viscosity | Better rolling, vacuum bagging, controlled viscosity |
| **Spray Lay-up** | High void content (~5–8%), low $V_f$, chopper variations | Short fibers, atomization issues | Use deposition control, post-roll, vacuum bag |
| **Vacuum Bagging** | Bridging, wrinkles in bag, spring-back | Improper bag placement, excess fabric | Use release film, breather layers, sequence ply stacks |
| **VARTM/Infusion** | Race-tracking (resin channeling at edges), dry spots, incomplete wet-out | Edge leakage, poor flow media, high viscosity | Use flow dams, edge sealing, heated resin, flow simulation |
| **RTM** | Dry spots, weld lines, non-uniform cure | Improper gate/vent placement, race-tracking | Resin flow simulation, heated molds, preform design |
| **Filament Winding** | Fiber slippage, band overlap voids, low fiber compaction | Inconsistent tension, wrong winding angle | Use tensioners, computer-controlled winders, tacky resin |

# 📊 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Process Flow — Hand Lay-up (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mold Preparation] --> B[Apply Release Agent]
    B --> C[Apply Gelcoat Layer]
    C --> D[Lay Surface Mat]
    D --> E[Mix Resin with Catalyst MEKP]
    E --> F[Pour and Spread Resin]
    F --> G[Place Reinforcement Ply Woven Roving]
    G --> H[Roller Consolidation Debulking]
    H --> I{Design Thickness Achieved?}
    I -->|No| F
    I -->|Yes| J[Cover with Vacuum Bag Optional]
    J --> K[Room Temperature Curing 24 hr]
    K --> L[Demold and Trim]
    L --> M[Quality Inspection Visual and Ultrasonic]
    M --> N[Final Shipbuilding Component]

    stepA[Operator Skill Required] -.-> G
    stepB[Exothermic Reaction Heat] -.-> K
    stepC[Defect Voids if Inadequate Rolling] -.-> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each rectangular node is a process step. The **diamond** (I) is a decision gate — "Have we reached the design thickness?" If **No**, the loop returns to resin pouring. The **dotted arrows** indicate **quality/process notes** that flag critical control points.

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Vacuum Infusion (VARTM) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Tool Mold Lower Half] --> A2[Lay Dry Preform Fabrics and Core]
    A2 --> A3[Place Flow Media Mesh]
    A3 --> A4[Place Peel Ply and Breather]
    A4 --> A5[Insert Resin Inlet and Vacuum Port]
    A5 --> A6[Seal Vacuum Bag with Tacky Tape]
    A6 --> A7[Apply Full Vacuum 95 kPa Gauge]
    A7 --> A8[Check for Leaks]
    A8 --> A9[Connect Resin Supply]
    A9 --> A10[Resin Flows Through Preform Darcy Flow]
    A10 --> A11{Complete Wet Out?}
    A11 -->|No| A10
    A11 -->|Yes| A12[Clamp Resin Line]
    A12 --> A13[Heat Assisted Curing]
    A13 --> A14[Demold and Inspect]

    nodeQ[Critical Parameter Pressure Differential] -.-> A7
    nodeR[Critical Parameter Viscosity] -.-> A10
    nodeS[Defect Risk Race Tracking] -.-> A8
```

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Six Lay-up Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OP[OPEN MOLD PROCESSES]
        OP1[Hand Lay-up]
        OP2[Spray Lay-up]
    end

    subgraph CL[CLOSED MOLD PROCESSES]
        CL1[Vacuum Bagging]
        CL2[Vacuum Infusion VARTM]
        CL3[Resin Transfer Molding RTM]
    end

    subgraph SP[SPECIALTY PROCESSES]
        SP1[Filament Winding]
        SP2[Prepreg Autoclave]
    end

    OP --> Q1[Low Cost Low Volume 30 percent Vf]
    CL --> Q2[Medium Cost Medium Volume 50 percent Vf]
    SP --> Q3[High Cost High Performance 60 percent Vf]

    Q1 --> APP1[Small Craft Repair Work]
    Q2 --> APP2[Yachts Naval Vessels Mine Countermeasures]
    Q3 --> APP3[Submarine Hulls Masts High Performance]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Filament Winding Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Mandrel Rotating] -->|Tension| FW[Fiber Rovings Pass Through Resin Bath]
    FW -->|Winding Angle alpha| M1
    FW -->|Helical Pattern| L1[Lead Advance L per Revolution]

    ANG[Tan alpha equals pi D over L] -.-> FW
    TENS[Tension Control 5 to 50 N per Roving] -.-> FW
    CURE[Heat or Ambient Cure] --> DEMOLD[Extract Mandrel]
    DEMOLD --> PROD[Filament Wound Product Cylinder Tank Mast]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the winding geometry:** The **winding angle $\alpha$** is controlled by the ratio of mandrel rotation speed to carriage traverse speed. A **low $\alpha$ (~10°)** gives **hoop-dominated** strength (good for pressure vessels). A **high $\alpha$ (~89°)** gives **axial-dominated** strength (good for masts and pipes).

    Q4 -->|Low| R5[RTM]

# 📝 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PENST523 Module 3]**

**Q: Define "Fiber Volume Fraction" and state its typical range for (a) hand lay-up and (b) vacuum-infused marine laminates. Why is $V_f$ considered a critical quality parameter in shipbuilding composites?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Fiber volume fraction $V_f$ is the ratio of the volume of fibers to the total volume of the composite laminate, expressed as a decimal or percentage:
> 
> $$V_f = \dfrac{V_{\text{fibers}}}{V_{\text{fibers}} + V_{\text{matrix}} + V_{\text{voids}}}$$
> 
> **Typical Ranges (1 Mark):**
> - Hand lay-up: $V_f \approx 0.30\text{–}0.40$ (30–40%)
> - Vacuum-infused (VARTM): $V_f \approx 0.50\text{–}0.60$ (50–60%)
> 
> **Significance (1 Mark):** $V_f$ directly governs the **mechanical performance** of the laminate (strength, stiffness, fatigue life) per the rule of mixtures. Classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's) specify minimum $V_f$ in ship scantling rules. A low $V_f$ indicates **resin-rich, heavy, weak, and brittle** laminate unsuitable for primary hull structure.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PENST523 Module 3]**

**Q: List any THREE defects commonly encountered in hand lay-up FRP ship hulls. For each defect, state its root cause and one preventive measure.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Defect** | **Root Cause** | **Prevention** |
|---|---|---|
| **Voids / Air Entrapment** (1 Mark) | Inadequate roller consolidation; high resin viscosity; trapped air between plies | Thorough rolling, vacuum bagging, degassed resin |
| **Dry Spots** (1 Mark) | Poor resin distribution; insufficient wet-out of fibers | Use flow media, ensure even resin spread, train operators |
| **Resin-Rich Pockets** (1 Mark) | Excess resin application; poor fiber-resin ratio control | Use squeegees to remove excess; specify resin-to-fiber ratio in lay-up schedule |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[CHOICE OPTION 1]**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PENST523 Module 3]**

**Q: (a)** Describe in detail the **Hand Lay-up Process** for fabricating a ship hull using glass fiber reinforced polyester (GFRP). Include the following in your answer:
- Mold preparation steps
- Gelcoat application
- Resin formulation and reinforcement sequence
- Curing mechanism (exothermic polymerization)
- **Typical fiber volume fraction and void content achieved**

**(b)** Compare **Hand Lay-up** with **Vacuum Infusion (VARTM)** for ship hull fabrication under the following criteria:
- Fiber volume fraction
- Void content
- Mechanical properties
- Production rate
- Capital and labor cost
- Health & safety (styrene emissions)

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) — Hand Lay-up Process (7 Marks)

1. **Mold Preparation (1 Mark):** The female (or male) mold, typically made of **wood, fiberglass, or steel**, is cleaned, polished, and treated with a **release agent** (wax or PVA — polyvinyl alcohol) to prevent the laminate from bonding permanently.

2. **Gelcoat Application (1 Mark):** A pigmented **isophthalic polyester or vinylester gelcoat** (0.4–0.8 mm thick) is sprayed or brushed onto the mold. This is the **outer cosmetic surface** of the hull, providing UV resistance, waterproofing, and a smooth finish.

3. **Resin Formulation (1.5 Marks):** **General-purpose (orthophthalic) or isophthalic polyester resin** is mixed with:
   - **Catalyst: 1–2% MEKP (Methyl Ethyl Ketone Peroxide)** — initiates free-radical polymerization
   - **Accelerator: 0.5–1% Cobalt Octoate** — speeds up decomposition of MEKP at room temperature
   - Optional: **pigments, UV stabilizers, fire retardants**

4. **Reinforcement Sequence (1.5 Marks):** Layers are built up in a **balanced, symmetrical lay-up schedule**:
   - First: **Surface mat (50 g/m² CSM)** — masks print-through
   - Middle: Alternating **woven roving (600 g/m²)** and **chopped strand mat (450 g/m²)**
   - Total thickness: **6–25 mm** depending on hull size
   - Each ply is **rolled with a metal roller** to consolidate and remove air

5. **Curing (1 Mark):** Curing is an **exothermic addition polymerization**. MEKP decomposes into free radicals that cross-link the unsaturated polyester C=C double bonds, releasing heat (peak **60–80 °C**). Full cure at 25 °C: **24 hours**. Post-cure at 40–60 °C improves properties.

6. **Typical $V_f$ and $V_v$ (1 Mark):**
   - $V_f \approx 30\text{–}40\%$
   - $V_v \approx 2\text{–}5\%$

#### Part (b) — Comparison: Hand Lay-up vs. VARTM (7 Marks)

| **Criterion** | **Hand Lay-up** | **Vacuum Infusion (VARTM)** | **Marks** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fiber Volume Fraction $V_f$** | 30–40% | 50–60% (higher due to vacuum compaction) | 1 |
| **Void Content $V_v$** | 2–5% (operator-dependent) | <1% (vacuum removes entrapped air) | 1.5 |
| **Mechanical Properties** | Lower — resin-rich, more brittle | Higher — denser, stiffer, stronger | 1.5 |
| **Production Rate** | Slow (labor-intensive, ~1 m²/hr) | Faster for large parts (~5–10 m²/hr set-up + infusion) | 1 |
| **Capital Cost** | Very low (rollers, brushes, mold) | Medium (vacuum pump, bags, flow media) | 1 |
| **Labor Cost** | High (skilled manual operators) | Lower per m² (semi-automated) | 0.5 |
| **Health & Safety (Styrene Emissions)** | High — open mold releases styrene (carcinogen, regulated to <50 ppm OSHA PEL) | Low — closed system captures styrene, vacuum contains vapors | 0.5 |

**Conclusion (Awarded as bonus within marks):** VARTM produces **superior laminates** but requires higher capital investment. Hand lay-up remains dominant for **small-scale, repair, and prototype** shipbuilding.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[CHOICE OPTION 2]**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PENST523 Module 3]**

**Q: (a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **Filament Winding Process** used for manufacturing cylindrical composite ship hulls (e.g., submarine pressure hulls). Derive the relationship between **winding angle $\alpha$** and the **mandrel diameter $D$** and **lead $L$**.

**(b)** A shipyard is producing a **glass-polyester composite** for a 15 m patrol boat hull using **hand lay-up**. Each ply of woven roving has an **areal weight of 600 g/m²**, the resin content is **1.8 kg/m²**, and **8 plies** are used. Given $\rho_f = 2.55 \text{ g/cm}^3$ and $\rho_m = 1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3$, calculate:
- (i) The **fiber volume fraction** $V_f$
- (ii) The **matrix volume fraction** $V_m$
- (iii) The **cured laminate density** $\rho_c$ (assuming $V_v = 0.02$)
- (iv) The **total laminate thickness** for $1 \text{ m}^2$ area

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) — Filament Winding Process (7 Marks)

**Process Description (5 Marks):**

Filament winding is a **specialty lay-up technique** where continuous fiber rovings are impregnated with resin and wound onto a rotating mandrel at controlled angles.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **Mandrel Preparation (0.5 Mark):** A cylindrical or near-cylindrical mandrel (steel, aluminum, or soluble sand) is mounted on the winding machine. Surface is treated with release agent.

2. **Creel Setup (0.5 Mark):** Multiple spools (creels) of **E-glass or carbon fiber rovings** are loaded. Each roving passes through a **resin bath** (or is pre-impregnated).

3. **Winding (2 Marks):** The mandrel rotates while a **carriage (payoff eye)** traverses axially. The fiber is wound at a **winding angle $\alpha$** measured from the mandrel axis. The pattern is controlled by the ratio of **rotational speed to traverse speed**. Common patterns: **helical (geodesic) winding** for pressure vessels, **polar (hoop) winding** for axial reinforcement, and **helical ±α** for balanced biaxial properties.

4. **Curing (1 Mark):** The wound mandrel is cured at room temperature or in an oven. For marine applications, room-temperature-cure epoxy or vinylester systems are common.

5. **Mandrel Extraction (0.5 Mark):** For collapsible mandrels, sections are retracted. For soluble mandrels (e.g., sand + PVA binder), the mandrel is **washed out** with water.

6. **Trimming & Inspection (0.5 Mark):** Ends are trimmed, and the cylinder is subjected to **hydrostatic pressure testing** and **ultrasonic C-scan NDT**.

**Winding Angle Derivation (2 Marks):**

For one complete revolution of the mandrel, the carriage advances by a distance $L$ (the **lead**). In that same revolution, the fiber traces a helical path of length:

$$\text{Helical length per revolution} = \pi D / \cos(\alpha)$$

But the axial advance per revolution equals $L$, so by geometry:

$$\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{\pi D}{L}$$

**Physical interpretation:**
- If $L \to 0$ (no axial advance), $\tan(\alpha) \to \infty$, so $\alpha \to 90°$ → **hoop winding** (purely circumferential, maximum hoop strength).
- If $L \to \infty$, $\tan(\alpha) \to 0$, so $\alpha \to 0°$ → **polar winding** (purely axial, maximum longitudinal strength).
- Typical submarine pressure hulls use **$\alpha \approx 55°$–$75°$** (helical) for balanced biaxial strength.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $N = 8$ plies
- $W_A = 600 \text{ g/m}^2$ per ply
- $w_m = 1.8 \text{ kg/m}^2 = 1800 \text{ g/m}^2$ (total resin)
- $\rho_f = 2.55 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 2550 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_m = 1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 1200 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $V_v = 0.02$

**Step 1: Compute Fiber Mass Per Unit Area (1 Mark)**

$$w_f = N \times W_A = 8 \times 600 = 4800 \text{ g/m}^2 = 4.8 \text{ kg/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute Volume Per Unit Area — Fibers (1 Mark)**

$$\dfrac{V_f}{A} = \dfrac{w_f}{\rho_f} = \dfrac{4.8}{2550} = 1.8824 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2 = 1.8824 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Compute Volume Per Unit Area — Matrix (1 Mark)**

$$\dfrac{V_m}{A} = \dfrac{w_m}{\rho_m} = \dfrac{1.8}{1200} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2 = 1.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Compute Total Thickness (1 Mark)**

$$t = \dfrac{V_f + V_m}{A} + \text{void thickness} = 1.8824 + 1.5 = 3.3824 \text{ mm (fiber + matrix)}$$

Including voids (2% of total):

$$t_{\text{total}} = 3.3824 / (1 - 0.02) = 3.3824 / 0.98 = 3.4514 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Compute Volume Fractions (2 Marks)**

$$V_f = \dfrac{1.8824}{1.8824 + 1.5 + V_{\text{void}}} = \dfrac{1.8824}{3.4514} = 0.5454 = \mathbf{54.54\%}$$

$$V_m = \dfrac{1.5}{3.4514} = 0.4346 = \mathbf{43.46\%}$$

$$V_v = 1 - (V_f + V_m) = 1 - (0.5454 + 0.4346) = \mathbf{0.02 = 2.00\%} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6: Compute Cured Laminate Density (1 Mark)**

$$\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m = (0.5454)(2550) + (0.4346)(1200)$$

$$\rho_c = 1390.8 + 521.5 = \mathbf{1912.3 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 1.91 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

**Final Answers Boxed:**

$$\boxed{V_f = 54.5\% \quad V_m = 43.5\% \quad V_v = 2.0\% \quad \rho_c = 1.91 \text{ g/cm}^3 \quad t = 3.45 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing **g/cm³** and **kg/m³** without conversion (1 g/cm³ = 1000 kg/m³). This is the **#1 cause of numerical errors** in KTU papers.
> 2. **Forgetting Void Content:** If $V_v$ is given or assumed non-zero, students often forget to adjust the **total volume**. Always compute $V_v = 1 - (V_f + V_m)$ as a **consistency check**.
> 3. **Confusing Areal Weight with Volume:** $W_A$ (g/m²) is **mass per unit area**, not volume. To get thickness contribution, you **must divide by density**.
> 4. **Hand Lay-up $V_f$ Assumption:** Many students incorrectly assume $V_f = 50\%$ for hand lay-up. The correct range is **30–40%**. The high $V_f$ in this problem (54.5%) reflects an **optimally consolidated** lay-up with low resin content.
> 5. **Filament Winding Angle Derivation:** Students often forget to specify that the formula $\tan(\alpha) = \pi D / L$ is for **one complete revolution** of the mandrel.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This and Memorize Before the Exam!**

- ✅ **Definition:** Lay-up techniques are processes for placing reinforcing fibers and consolidating them within a polymer matrix to form FRP structures.
- ✅ **Six Core Techniques** (per KTU 2024 syllabus): Hand Lay-up, Spray Lay-up, Vacuum Bagging, Vacuum Infusion (VARTM), Resin Transfer Molding (RTM), Filament Winding.
- ✅ **Open vs. Closed Mold:** Hand and Spray Lay-up are **open mold**; Vacuum Bagging, VARTM, and RTM are **closed mold**; Filament Winding is a **specialty rotational process**.
- ✅ **Key Quality Parameters:**
  - **Fiber Volume Fraction $V_f$** — Hand lay-up: 30–40%, VARTM: 50–60%, Filament winding: 55–65%
  - **Void Content $V_v$** — Hand lay-up: 2–5%, VARTM: <1% (always aim for lowest possible)
- ✅ **Catalyst System:** Polyester resin uses **MEKP (1–2%) + Cobalt Octoate (0.5–1%)** for room-temperature cure.
- ✅ **Curing Chemistry:** **Exothermic free-radical addition polymerization** of unsaturated polyester/vinylester.
- ✅ **Filament Winding Angle Formula:** $\tan(\alpha) = \pi D / L$ — relates winding geometry to mandrel dimensions.
- ✅ **Darcy's Law for Infusion:** $Q = -(K \cdot A / \mu) \cdot (\Delta P / L)$ — governs resin flow in VARTM.
- ✅ **Rule of Mixtures (ROM):** $\sigma_c = V_f \sigma_f + V_m \sigma_m$ (longitudinal, iso-strain); $1/E_T = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ (transverse, iso-stress).
- ✅ **Shipbuilding Applications:**
  - Small craft & repair → **Hand Lay-up**
  - Yacht hulls, mine countermeasure vessels → **VARTM**
  - Submarine pressure hulls, masts → **Filament Winding**
  - Mass production small craft → **RTM**
- ✅ **Defect–Process Matrix:** Voids (Hand Lay-up), Race-tracking (VARTM), Weld lines (RTM), Band slippage (Filament Winding).
- ✅ **Health & Safety:** Hand lay-up has **highest styrene exposure**; closed-mold processes are mandated by modern occupational safety regulations.
- ✅ **Numerical Skills to Practice:** Computing $V_f$, $V_m$, $V_v$, $\rho_c$, and laminate thickness from given ply schedules and areal weights.
- ✅ **Classification Society Rules:** Real ship hulls must comply with **DNV, Lloyd's Register, or ABS** rules specifying minimum $V_f$, maximum $V_v$, and required NDT (ultrasonic C-scan).

> **END OF NOTES — MODULE 3, TOPIC: LAY-UP TECHNIQUES**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manufacturing Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Manufacturing Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Manufacturing Requirements** for non-ferrous materials in shipbuilding refer to the complete set of engineering specifications, processing constraints, dimensional tolerances, metallurgical conditions, joining protocols, and quality assurance standards that govern the conversion of raw non-ferrous alloys (primarily aluminum, copper-nickel, titanium, magnesium, and their composites) into ship structural components, marine machinery parts, and ancillary systems. These requirements are derived from classification society rules (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, IRS), military specifications (MIL-STD, DEF STAN), and international standards (ASTM, ISO, EN).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Manufacturing requirements are NOT merely "how to make" a part — they encompass the entire chain: *material certification → primary shaping → secondary forming → joining → heat treatment → surface protection → inspection → certification*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a naval architect is like a **master chef** preparing a Michelin-star dish from exotic ingredients (non-ferrous alloys). The recipe (design) demands specific:

- **Cutting techniques** (machining parameters that won't tear aluminum like soft cheese or crack titanium like stale bread)
- **Cooking temperatures** (heat treatment windows that are unforgiving — too hot destroys the alloy's "flavor" — its mechanical properties)
- **Plating style** (surface treatment that protects against the harsh ocean "soup" — saltwater corrosion)
- **Presentation standards** (tolerances and surface finish that classification societies demand before accepting the "dish" — the ship)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Marine Context:** A typical frigate uses approximately **1,500–2,500 tonnes of aluminum alloys** (5083, 5086, 6082) in its superstructure, **300–800 tonnes of copper-nickel (90/10 and 70/30)** in piping, and **50–150 tonnes of titanium** in critical components. Every kilogram has a manufacturing requirement attached.

## 1.3 Key Non-Ferrous Materials & Their Manufacturing Significance

| Material | Typical Marine Application | Critical Manufacturing Concern |
|----------|---------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Al 5083 / 5086** | Hulls, superstructures | Weldability, HAZ softening |
| **Al 6082 / 6061** | Masts, structural extrusions | Formability, T6 tempering |
| **Cu-Ni 90/10** | Sea-water piping | Hot working range (850–950 °C) |
| **Cu-Ni 70/30** | High-velocity seawater systems | Cold working limitations |
| **Titanium Gr 2 / Gr 5** | Submarine pressure hulls, propellers | Reactivity at > 400 °C |
| **Magnesium alloys (AZ31, AZ91)** | Deck fittings, gearboxes | Flammability, galvanic isolation |
| **Brass / Bronze** | Valves, bearings, propellers | Dezincification control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Galvanic Compatibility Coefficient:** When two dissimilar non-ferrous metals are coupled in seawater, the corrosion rate of the *anode* is roughly proportional to the **galvanic potential difference** divided by the electrolyte resistance. This dictates **insulation requirements** during manufacturing.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Galvanic Series Potential of Al 5083 vs Cu-Ni 90/10 in seawater:** approximately **+0.65 V to +0.75 V** driving potential
- **Standard corrosion rate of unprotected carbon steel in seawater:** **0.1–0.5 mm/year**
- **Density of Al 5083:** **2.66 g/cm³** (≈ 35% weight saving over steel)
- **Density of Titanium Gr 2:** **4.51 g/cm³** (≈ 43% weight saving over steel)
- **Maximum service temperature for Al-Mg alloys (sensitization threshold):** **≈ 65–80 °C** (long-term exposure limit before β-phase Al₃Mg₂ precipitation)
- **Welding current range for Al 5083 (MIG/GTAW):** **150–350 A** at **22–30 V**
- **Standard NDT acceptance for marine welds (IACS UR W17):** porosity ≤ **2%**, undercut ≤ **0.5 mm**

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The sensitization temperature of Al-Mg alloys (65–80 °C) is a frequent 2-mark question. Above this, Mg migrates to grain boundaries, forming **β-phase (Al₃Mg₂)** which causes **intergranular corrosion** and **stress corrosion cracking (SCC)**.

---

## 1.5 Why Manufacturing Requirements Are Stricter for Non-Ferrous Marine Materials

Three fundamental reasons drive the *stringent* nature of these requirements:

1. **Corrosive Environment:** Seawater contains **≈ 3.5% dissolved salts** (mainly NaCl), making it highly aggressive — particularly to non-ferrous alloys that rely on passive oxide films.
2. **Safety Criticality:** Ship structural failures (hull breach, pipe rupture) can be catastrophic — manufacturing defects are non-negotiable.
3. **Dissimilar Metal Coupling:** A single ship contains **15–25 different alloys** in close electrical contact through the common hull — the **galvanic corrosion risk** must be engineered out at the manufacturing stage, not the operational stage.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Manufacturing Requirements

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups manufacturing requirements into six logical pillars. Master these and you cover the entire module:

### Pillar 1 — **Material Certification & Traceability**

- Every batch of non-ferrous alloy must arrive with a **Mill Test Certificate (MTC)** conforming to **EN 10204 3.1** or **3.2**.
- Chemical composition verified against the relevant ASTM/EN specification.
- Heat number, cast number, and batch identity must be traceable **from raw ingot to installed component** — a single missing link is grounds for classification society rejection.
- Mechanical test certificates (tensile, hardness, impact) must accompany each lot.

### Pillar 2 — **Primary Forming Requirements**

- **Casting (sand, die, investment):** Required for propellers (Mn-bronze, Ni-Al-Bronze), valves, and complex pump casings.
- **Wrought processing (rolling, forging, extrusion):** Required for hull plates (rolled 5083), structural sections (extruded 6082), and shafts (forged Cu-Ni / Ti).
- **Powder metallurgy / Additive manufacturing:** Emerging for marine brackets and lattice-structured stiffeners.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule — Hot Working Temperature Window for Cu-Ni 90/10:**
> $$\boxed{850\,^{\circ}\text{C} \le T_{\text{hot work}} \le 950\,^{\circ}\text{C}}$$
> Below 850 °C → excessive force, cracking. Above 950 °C → grain growth, hot shortness.

### Pillar 3 — **Joining Requirements (Welding, Brazing, Mechanical Fastening)**

- **Welding processes permitted:** GTAW (TIG), GMAW (MIG), FSW (Friction Stir Welding — increasingly mandated for marine Al), plasma, laser hybrid.
- **Filler metal matching:** AL 5083 base → ER 5356 or ER 5183 filler; AL 6082 → ER 4043 or ER 5356.
- **Preheat requirements:**
  - Al 5083: generally **none** (high Mg content reduces hot-cracking susceptibility)
  - Al 6082: preheat to **100–150 °C** to avoid lack of fusion
  - Titanium Gr 2: preheat **NOT recommended** — trapped moisture causes porosity
  - Cu-Ni 90/10: preheat to **150–200 °C** to drive off surface moisture

> [!NOTE]
> **Friction Stir Welding (FSW) — A Marine Game-Changer:** FSW is a **solid-state** process (no melting) that produces welds in Al 5083 with **tensile strength equal to or exceeding the parent metal** and **no HAZ softening** — which is why modern littoral combat ships (LCS) and the US Navy's Littoral Combat Ship use FSW extensively.

### Pillar 4 — **Heat Treatment & Temper Designation**

| Alloy | Common Temper | Heat Treatment Cycle | Target Properties |
|-------|--------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Al 5083 | H116 / H321 | Strain-hardened + stabilized | Marine-grade corrosion resistance |
| Al 6082 | T6 | Solution treat 530 °C + water quench + artificial age 175 °C / 8 h | Peak strength (≈ 310 MPa UTS) |
| Cu-Ni 90/10 | As-welded / Annealed | Stress relieve at 550–600 °C | Ductility, corrosion resistance |
| Ti Gr 5 (Ti-6Al-4V) | Annealed | 700–790 °C / 1–2 h, air cool | Balanced strength + toughness |
| Bronze (Ni-Al) | As-cast or heat-treated | 650–700 °C tempering | Cavitation resistance |

### Pillar 5 — **Surface Protection Requirements**

- **Aluminum alloys:** Chromate conversion coating (Alodine 1200) → primer → anti-fouling (if submerged)
- **Cu-Ni:** No coating typically needed; passive film forms naturally
- **Titanium:** Pickling (HF + HNO₃) after hot working to remove α-case
- **Magnesium:** Chemical conversion coating (Dow 7) + primer + topcoat (mandatory due to extreme reactivity)
- **Galvanic isolation:** Nylon, neoprene, or EPDM washers/sleeves at every fastener connecting dissimilar metals

### Pillar 6 — **Inspection, Testing & Quality Assurance**

- **Dimensional inspection** (CMM, laser scanning) — tolerance per ISO 2768 (general) or per drawing (specific).
- **NDT methods:** Radiographic (RT), ultrasonic (UT), magnetic particle (MT — for ferromagnetic materials only), dye penetrant (PT), eddy current (ET).
- **Mechanical testing:** Tensile (per ASTM E8/E8M), bend (per ASTM E190), impact (Charpy V-notch, ASTM E23), hardness (Brinell HRB, Vickers HV).
- **Pressure testing:** Hydrostatic test at **1.5 × design pressure** for piping systems (per IACS UR P2.4).
- **Documentation:** Welding Procedure Specification (WPS), Procedure Qualification Record (PQR), Welder Qualification Record (WQR).

## 2.2 The Galvanic Corrosion Mathematical Framework

When two dissimilar non-ferrous metals are electrically coupled in seawater, the **corrosion current density** flowing from anode to cathode can be approximated by:

$$i_{\text{corr}} = \frac{\Delta E}{R_{\text{electrolyte}} \cdot A_{\text{anode}}}$$

Where:
- $i_{\text{corr}}$ = corrosion current density (A/m²)
- $\Delta E$ = potential difference between cathode and anode (V)
- $R_{\text{electrolyte}}$ = electrolyte resistance (Ω)
- $A_{\text{anode}}$ = exposed anode area (m²)

The **mass loss rate** of the anode follows Faraday's law:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{M \cdot i_{\text{corr}} \cdot A_{\text{anode}} \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

Where:
- $\dot{m}$ = mass loss (grams)
- $M$ = atomic mass of corroding metal (g/mol)
- $i_{\text{corr}}$ = corrosion current density (A/m²)
- $A_{\text{anode}}$ = exposed anode area (m²)
- $t$ = time (seconds)
- $n$ = valence (electrons transferred per atom)
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Manufacturing Translation:** This equation tells the manufacturing engineer: *to reduce anode mass loss, either (a) increase anode area, (b) increase electrolyte resistance by adding isolation, or (c) reduce potential difference by selecting more compatible alloys.*

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|-------------------|------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Corrosion current density | $i_{\text{corr}} = \dfrac{\Delta E}{R \cdot A_a}$ | A/m² |
| 2 | Faraday's mass loss | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{M \cdot i \cdot A_a \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | grams |
| 3 | Penetration rate | $P = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{\text{corr}} \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}$ | mm/year |
| 4 | Weld heat input | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ | kJ/mm |
| 5 | Preheating reduction of cooling rate | $t_{8/5} = f(\text{preheat},\, \text{plate thickness})$ | seconds |
| 6 | Strength-to-weight ratio | $R_{sw} = \dfrac{\sigma_{UTS}}{\rho \cdot g}$ | kN·m/kg |
| 7 | Stress concentration factor | $K_t = 1 + 2\sqrt{\dfrac{a}{r}}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Hydrostatic test pressure | $P_{\text{test}} = 1.5 \cdot P_{\text{design}}$ | bar |
| 9 | Bend test mandrel radius (Al) | $r = 2t$ (for t ≤ 12 mm) | mm |
| 10 | Hot work temperature (Cu-Ni 90/10) | $850\,^{\circ}\text{C} \le T \le 950\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ | °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Mark Saver:** In the KTU Formula Sheet, **never** write $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) using the pipe character — use $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX. The vertical pipe breaks markdown table rendering.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Manufacturing Requirements |
|--------|------------------------------------------|
| Naval Architecture | Hull plate forming, superstructure welding, weight optimization |
| Marine Piping | Cu-Ni 90/10 fabrication, bend radii, flange standards |
| Propeller Manufacturing | Ni-Al bronze casting, pitch control, balancing (ISO 484/2) |
| Submarine Construction | Ti pressure hull welding, X-ray inspection standards |
| Offshore Platforms | Cu-Ni splash zone protection, galvanic anode design |
| LNG Carriers | Al 5083 secondary barrier, cryogenic ductility testing |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Corrosion Penetration Rate from Faraday's Law

Starting from Faraday's first law of electrolysis:

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} \\
&\text{where } Q = i_{\text{corr}} \cdot A_{\text{anode}} \cdot t \quad \text{(total charge passed, in Coulombs)} \\
&\Rightarrow m = \frac{i_{\text{corr}} \cdot A_{\text{anode}} \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} \\
&\text{Dividing by anode area and density to get linear penetration: } \\
&V_{\text{loss}} = \frac{m}{A_{\text{anode}} \cdot \rho} = \frac{i_{\text{corr}} \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} \\
&\text{Converting to mm/year (with appropriate unit conversion factors):} \\
&P_{\text{mm/yr}} = \frac{0.00327 \cdot i_{\text{corr}} \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}
\end{aligned}$$

The constant **0.00327** comes from converting seconds → year and cm → mm.

### Worked Numerical Example

**Given:** A steel fastener ($M = 55.85$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $\rho = 7.85$ g/cm³) is in galvanic contact with Cu-Ni 90/10 in seawater. Measured corrosion current density $i_{\text{corr}} = 0.05$ A/m².

**Step 1:** Substitute into the penetration rate formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{0.00327 \times 0.05 \times 55.85}{2 \times 7.85} \\
P &= \frac{0.00913}{15.70} \\
P &= 5.82 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{mm/year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** Approximately **0.58 μm/year** — small for steel, but note that area ratio effects can increase this by **10–100×** when the cathode (Cu-Ni) is much larger than the anode (steel fastener). This is precisely why **large-cathode-small-anode** configurations are dangerous in marine engineering.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Weld Heat Input and HAZ Thermal Cycle

The heat input per unit length of weld controls the HAZ microstructure and is calculated as:

$$\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000} \quad \text{(in kJ/mm)} \\
&\text{where:} \\
&\eta = \text{arc efficiency (0.6 for GTAW, 0.8 for GMAW, 0.85 for SAW)} \\
&V = \text{arc voltage (V)} \\
&I = \text{welding current (A)} \\
&v = \text{travel speed (mm/min)} \\
&\Rightarrow v_{\text{mm/s}} = \frac{v}{60}
\end{aligned}$$

### Worked Example: Aluminum 5083 GTAW Weld

**Given:** $V = 22$ V, $I = 200$ A, $v = 200$ mm/min, $\eta = 0.6$ (GTAW).

$$\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{0.6 \times 22 \times 200}{200 \times 1000} \quad \text{(treating } v \text{ in mm/min directly)} \\
&\text{Or more rigorously, converting v to mm/s first:} \\
v_{\text{mm/s}} &= \frac{200}{60} = 3.33\ \text{mm/s} \\
H &= \frac{0.6 \times 22 \times 200}{3.33 \times 1000} = \frac{2640}{3330} = 0.793\ \text{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** Heat input ≈ **0.79 kJ/mm** — this is within the **acceptable range (0.6–1.5 kJ/mm)** for Al 5083. Exceeding 1.5 kJ/mm risks **excessive HAZ softening** and **grain growth**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Strength-to-Weight Ratio Comparison (Al vs Steel vs Ti)

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{sw} &= \frac{\sigma_{UTS}}{\rho \cdot g} \\
\text{For Al 5083-H116: } &R_{sw,Al} = \frac{317}{2700 \times 9.81} = 1.197 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{kN·m/kg} \\
\text{For mild steel: } &R_{sw,Fe} = \frac{400}{7850 \times 9.81} = 5.19 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kN·m/kg} \\
\text{For Ti Gr 2: } &R_{sw,Ti} = \frac{440}{4510 \times 9.81} = 9.95 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kN·m/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Ratio:**
$$\frac{R_{sw,Al}}{R_{sw,Fe}} = \frac{1.197}{0.519} \approx 2.31$$

**Conclusion:** Al 5083 is **2.3× more efficient** in strength-to-weight than mild steel — explaining its dominance in superstructure and high-speed vessel construction.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Manufacturing Process Selection by Material

| Process \ Material | Al 5083 | Al 6082 | Cu-Ni 90/10 | Ti Gr 2 | Mg AZ31 | Ni-Al Bronze |
|--------------------|---------|---------|-------------|---------|---------|--------------|
| Sand Casting | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | △ | ✓✓ |
| Die Casting | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓✓ | △ |
| Investment Cast. | ✗ | ✗ | △ | ✓✓ | △ | ✓ |
| Hot Rolling | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✗ |
| Cold Rolling | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✗ |
| Extrusion | ✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | △ | △ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Forging | ✓ | ✓✓ | △ | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✓✓ |
| GTAW Welding | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | △ | ✓✓ |
| FSW | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| Brazing | △ | △ | ✓✓ | △ | ✗ | ✓✓ |
| Adhesive Bonding | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | △ | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✗ |

*Legend:* ✓✓✓ = Excellent & Preferred; ✓✓ = Good; ✓ = Acceptable; △ = Limited/Restricted; ✗ = Not Recommended

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Weld Cooling Time (t₈/₅) for Marine Steels

The t₈/₅ (cooling time from 800 °C → 500 °C) is critical for HAZ toughness:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{8/5} &= \frac{Q / v}{2 \pi \lambda \left[ \frac{1}{800 - T_0} - \frac{1}{500 - T_0} \right]} \\
&\text{where:} \\
&Q/v = \text{heat input per unit length (J/mm)} \\
&\lambda = \text{thermal conductivity of base metal (W/mm·K)} \\
&T_0 = \text{preheat temperature (K)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Simplified Application

For a 15 mm Al 5083 plate with preheat 50 °C and $H = 0.8$ kJ/mm:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{8/5} &\approx \frac{800}{0.8 \times \left(\frac{1}{750} - \frac{1}{450}\right)} \\
&\approx \frac{800}{0.8 \times (0.00133 - 0.00222)} \\
&\approx \frac{800}{0.8 \times (-0.00089)} \quad \text{(use absolute value, magnitude only)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Manufacturing Translation:** For aluminum, t₈/₅ is intentionally short (fast cooling) — this *retains* Mg in solid solution and prevents coarse β-phase precipitation. For low-alloy steels in contrast, longer t₈/₅ is *desired* for toughness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Disciplinary Insight:** The sign and magnitude of t₈/₅ *direction* are exactly **opposite** for aluminum vs. low-alloy steel — a beautiful example of how the same thermal parameter has opposite metallurgical meaning depending on whether you want **supersaturation** (Al) or **tempered bainite** (steel).

## 3.6 Tolerance Stack-Up Calculation Example

For a ship hull panel assembly:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{stack}} &= \sqrt{T_1^2 + T_2^2 + T_3^2 + \ldots + T_n^2} \quad \text{(RSS method)} \\
&\text{For a 3-plate stack: } T_1 = 1.0,\ T_2 = 1.5,\ T_3 = 0.8\ \text{mm} \\
T_{\text{stack}} &= \sqrt{1.0^2 + 1.5^2 + 0.8^2} \\
&= \sqrt{1.00 + 2.25 + 0.64} = \sqrt{3.89} = 1.97\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

This determines the **fit-up tolerance** to be specified in the manufacturing drawing.
