# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the term "Manufacturing Requirements" in the context of non-ferrous materials used in shipbuilding. List any four mandatory requirements.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Manufacturing Requirements refer to the complete set of engineering specifications, processing parameters, dimensional tolerances, metallurgical conditions, and quality assurance standards that govern the conversion of raw non-ferrous alloys (aluminum, copper-nickel, titanium, magnesium, bronze) into ship structural and machinery components.

Four mandatory requirements:

1. **Material Certification** — Mill Test Certificate (MTC) per EN 10204 3.1, with traceable heat number and chemical composition.
2. **Weld Procedure Qualification** — Valid WPS, PQR, and WQR per ASME Section IX or AWS D1.2 (for aluminum).
3. **Dimensional Tolerance** — Compliance with ISO 2768 (general) or drawing-specific tolerances.
4. **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** — Acceptance per IACS UR W17 (for welds) or equivalent classification society rule.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Explain the sensitization phenomenon in Al-Mg alloys (e.g., 5083) and state its maximum safe long-term service temperature.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

When Al-Mg alloys containing more than 3% Mg (such as 5083 with ≈ 4.5% Mg) are exposed to temperatures between **65 °C and 200 °C** for prolonged periods, magnesium atoms diffuse to grain boundaries and precipitate as the **β-phase (Al₃Mg₂)**. This β-phase is anodic with respect to the surrounding matrix, and in the presence of seawater electrolyte, it preferentially dissolves — leading to **intergranular corrosion** and **stress corrosion cracking (SCC)**.

**Maximum safe long-term service temperature: ≈ 65 °C.**

Marine applications use the **H116 or H321 temper** (strain-hardened + stabilized at ≈ 250 °C) precisely to *precipitate* the β-phase in a controlled, evenly distributed manner — converting the alloy into a "sensitized-but-stable" state where further precipitation during service is minimized.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse FSW (Friction Stir Welding) with Friction Welding** — FSW uses a *non-consumable* rotating tool; it is a **solid-state** process. Students often incorrectly state it "melts" the metal.
> 2. **Don't mix up the sensitization temperatures** of Al-Mg (65–200 °C) with that of austenitic stainless steel (400–850 °C) — they are different alloy systems.
> 3. **Always cite the standard** (ASTM / EN / IACS UR / MIL-STD) when discussing manufacturing requirements — the examiner allocates marks specifically for the *standard reference*.
> 4. **Pre-fill numerical values with units** — a common 1-mark loss is forgetting to write the unit (kJ/mm, mm/year, °C, MPa).

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3 Internal Choice A]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the manufacturing requirements for **friction stir welding (FSW)** of Al 5083 marine hull plates. Cover: process principle, parameter ranges, advantages over fusion welding, and applicable standards.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A marine grade Al 5083-H116 plate of thickness **15 mm** is welded using GTAW at $V = 24$ V, $I = 220$ A, travel speed $v = 180$ mm/min. Arc efficiency $\eta = 0.6$. Calculate the **heat input per unit length (kJ/mm)** and comment on whether it falls within the recommended range for Al 5083.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice B]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **galvanic corrosion problem** in mixed-metal ship construction. With a neat sketch (block diagram acceptable), explain how manufacturing-stage interventions prevent galvanic failure. Cite at least three standards.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A steel fastener ($M = 55.85$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $\rho = 7.85$ g/cm³) is in galvanic contact with a Cu-Ni 90/10 plate in seawater. The measured corrosion current density is **0.08 A/m²**. Calculate (i) the **mass loss per m² in 1 year**, and (ii) the **linear penetration rate in mm/year**. State the engineering significance.

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A(a) — Friction Stir Welding of Al 5083 [7 Marks]

**Process Principle (2 Marks):**
Friction Stir Welding (FSW) is a **solid-state joining process** in which a non-consumable rotating tool — consisting of a **shoulder** and a **probe (pin)** — is plunged into the joint line between two clamped plates. Frictional heat (≈ 80–90% of the tool's rotational energy) softens but **does not melt** the material. The tool traverses the joint, and the softened plasticized metal is stirred from the leading edge to the trailing edge, forming a recrystallized fine-grained weld nugget.

**Key Parameter Ranges (2 Marks):**

| Parameter | Typical Range for Al 5083 (15 mm plate) |
|-----------|------------------------------------------|
| Tool rotation speed | 300–1500 rpm |
| Travel speed | 50–500 mm/min |
| Tilt angle | 1–3° |
| Axial (plunge) force | 25–50 kN |
| Tool material | H13 tool steel (with wear-resistant coating) |
| Pin length | Slightly less than plate thickness |

**Advantages Over Fusion Welding (2 Marks):**

1. **No melting** → eliminates solidification cracking, porosity, and HAZ liquation cracking.
2. **No HAZ softening** — the dynamically recrystallized nugget has *finer* grains than the parent metal, often with **higher strength**.
3. **No filler metal required** → eliminates filler metal selection and matching issues.
4. **Lower residual stress and distortion** (≈ 70–80% reduction vs. fusion welds).
5. **Environmentally cleaner** — no fumes, no UV radiation, no shielding gas needed (in many variants).

**Applicable Standards (1 Mark):**
- **AWS D17.3 / D17.3M** — Specification for FSW of aluminum alloys for aerospace and marine applications.
- **IACS UR W17** — Welded hull construction of ships (FSW referenced).
- **ISO 25239** — Friction stir welding of aluminum.
- **DEF STAN 95-23** — UK MoD specification for FSW marine applications.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark reserved for any one of the above standards.)*

---

### Solution to Question A(b) — Heat Input Calculation [7 Marks]

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Identify the formula.

$$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000} \quad \text{(kJ/mm, with v in mm/s)}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Convert travel speed to mm/s.

$$v = \frac{180\ \text{mm/min}}{60} = 3.0\ \text{mm/s}$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Substitute values.

$$H = \frac{0.6 \times 24 \times 220}{3.0 \times 1000}$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Evaluate.

$$H = \frac{0.6 \times 24 \times 220}{3000} = \frac{3168}{3000} = 1.056\ \text{kJ/mm}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Comment.

The calculated heat input of **1.056 kJ/mm** falls within the **recommended range of 0.6 – 1.5 kJ/mm** for Al 5083 plate welding (per AWS D1.2 and most classification society rules). It is, however, on the higher side — close to the upper limit. For the next thicker plate (e.g., 20 mm), the welder should consider:
- Reducing travel speed carefully (which would *increase* H further — not desirable).
- Reducing current (e.g., from 220 A to 200 A).
- Using multi-pass welding with intermediate cooling.

---

### Solution to Question B(a) — Galvanic Corrosion in Mixed-Metal Ship Construction [7 Marks]

**Problem Statement (2 Marks):**
A modern ship contains 15–25 different alloys in intimate electrical contact through the common hull structure and seawater piping. When two dissimilar metals are immersed in seawater (an electrolyte) and electrically connected, the *less noble* metal (anode) corrodes preferentially at an accelerated rate determined by the **galvanic potential difference** and the **area ratio** (cathode : anode). The most dangerous case is the **large cathode / small anode** configuration (e.g., Cu-Ni 90/10 pipe + steel fastener → catastrophic fastener loss).

**Manufacturing-Stage Interventions (3 Marks):**

| # | Intervention | Standard Reference |
|---|--------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Galvanic isolation** — Nylon, neoprene, EPDM washers, gaskets, sleeves at every fastener between dissimilar metals | DEF STAN 07-246, ASTM B849 |
| 2 | **Sacrificial zinc / aluminum anodes** — fitted at regular intervals to protect hull and critical components | MIL-A-18001, ASTM B418 |
| 3 | **Cathodic protection (ICCP)** — impressed current system for large vessels | NACE SP0176, ISO 12473 |
| 4 | **Insulating flanges** in piping systems between Cu-Ni and steel/aluminum sections | ASME B16.5 (with isolation gaskets) |
| 5 | **Coatings & wrappings** — epoxy + polyurethane on all fasteners and joint areas | NACE TM0404, ISO 12944 |

**Block Diagram / Sketch (1 Mark):** Refer to the diagram in **Section 4.2** of these notes — the galvanic isolation logic flow.

**Standards (1 Mark):** ASTM B849 (surface treatment of fasteners), DEF STAN 07-246 (galvanic compatibility), NACE SP0176 (cathodic protection design).

---

### Solution to Question B(b) — Corrosion Mass Loss and Penetration Rate [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $M = 55.85$ g/mol (iron)
- $n = 2$
- $\rho = 7.85$ g/cm³
- $i_{\text{corr}} = 0.08$ A/m²
- $t = 1$ year $= 365 \times 24 \times 3600 = 3.1536 \times 10^7$ s
- $A_{\text{anode}} = 1$ m² = $10^4$ cm²

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Faraday's mass loss formula.

$$m = \frac{M \cdot i_{\text{corr}} \cdot A \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Substitute and evaluate for mass loss per m².

$$m = \frac{55.85 \times 0.08 \times 1 \times 3.1536 \times 10^7}{2 \times 96485}$$

$$m = \frac{55.85 \times 0.08 \times 3.1536 \times 10^7}{192970}$$

$$m = \frac{1.4092 \times 10^8}{192970} \approx 730.3\ \text{g/m}^2\text{/year}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Convert to linear penetration.

$$P = \frac{m}{A \cdot \rho} = \frac{730.3\ \text{g}}{10^4\ \text{cm}^2 \times 7.85\ \text{g/cm}^3}$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Evaluate.

$$P = \frac{730.3}{78500} = 9.30 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{cm/year} = 0.0930\ \text{mm/year}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Engineering significance.

A penetration rate of **≈ 0.09 mm/year** might appear small, but in real ships the **cathode-to-anode area ratio** is often 100:1 or higher, which can amplify the actual anode corrosion rate by **one to two orders of magnitude**. Therefore, the *effective* fastener loss can be **0.9 – 9.0 mm/year** — easily consuming a 5 mm fastener in well under one year. This is why **galvanic isolation is mandatory at the design and manufacturing stage**, not deferred to the operational stage.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Read before exam morning!)**

- [ ] **Six Pillars of Manufacturing Requirements:** Certification → Primary Forming → Joining → Heat Treatment → Surface Protection → QA/NDT.
- [ ] **Al 5083** is the workhorse marine aluminum alloy (H116/H321 temper); avoid prolonged service above **65 °C** (β-phase sensitization).
- [ ] **Al 6082** is the structural extrusion alloy; needs **T6 heat treatment** (solution 530 °C + age 175 °C / 8 h).
- [ ] **Cu-Ni 90/10 hot working window:** **850 – 950 °C** — a frequently tested 2-marker.
- [ ] **Titanium Gr 2 / Gr 5:** Pickling (HF + HNO₃) after hot work to remove α-case; preheat is *not* recommended.
- [ ] **Magnesium alloys** are lightest but require **Dow 7 chemical conversion coating** + primer + topcoat.
- [ ] **FSW (Friction Stir Welding)** is a **solid-state** process — no melting, no HAZ softening; used in modern LCS and high-speed vessels.
- [ ] **Weld heat input formula:** $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v}$ (with v in mm/s for kJ/mm result).
- [ ] **Acceptable H for Al 5083:** **0.6 – 1.5 kJ/mm**.
- [ ] **Faraday's corrosion law:** $\dot{m} = \dfrac{M \cdot i \cdot A \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$; $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.
- [ ] **Penetration rate:** $P = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{\text{corr}} \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}$ in mm/year.
- [ ] **Hydrostatic test pressure** for marine piping: **1.5 × design pressure**.
- [ ] **Galvanic isolation** materials: Nylon, Neoprene, EPDM. Mandated by DEF STAN 07-246, ASTM B849.
- [ ] **Sensitization rule — don't confuse:** Al-Mg → **65 – 200 °C**; austenitic SS → **400 – 850 °C**.
- [ ] **NDT acceptance per IACS UR W17:** porosity ≤ **2%**, undercut ≤ **0.5 mm**.
- [ ] **Traceability:** Heat number must follow ingot → forging → finished component (EN 10204 3.1/3.2).
- [ ] **WPS / PQR / WQR** triad is mandatory for *every* welding procedure on a classed ship.
- [ ] **FSW standard:** AWS D17.3, ISO 25239. **GTAW Al standard:** AWS D1.2. **Cu-Ni standard:** ASME B31.3.
- [ ] **Density recall:** Al 5083 = **2.66 g/cm³**; Ti Gr 2 = **4.51 g/cm³**; Cu-Ni 90/10 = **8.94 g/cm³**; Steel = **7.85 g/cm³**.
- [ ] **Pre-fill the prefill:** Always specify **welding standard (AWS D1.2 / ASME IX)**, **classification rule (IACS UR / DNV / LR)**, and **material standard (ASTM B928 for Al 5083 plate)** in every exam answer.

topics.title: Advantages and Disadvantages of Composites Over Steel and Aluminium in Shipbuilding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Advantages and Disadvantages of Composites Over Steel and Aluminium in Shipbuilding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of naval architecture and marine engineering (KTU 2024 Scheme - PENST523), **marine composites** refer to **fibre-reinforced polymer (FRP) materials** — primarily **Glass Fibre Reinforced Plastics (GRP)**, **Carbon Fibre Reinforced Polymers (CFRP)**, and **Aramid Fibre Reinforced Polymers (AFRP)** — used as a substitute for traditional ferrous (steel) and non-ferrous (aluminium) alloys in hull, superstructure, and outfit construction of marine vessels.

A **composite material** is defined (per ISO 14130 and ASTM D3878) as a heterogeneous combination of two or more distinct constituents (a *matrix* and a *reinforcement*) that act in concert to provide properties unattainable by the individual constituents alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):** The lecturer is expected to compare the *engineering performance envelope* of composites against the *baseline* of mild steel (Grade A, ASTM A131) and marine-grade aluminium alloys (5083-H116 / 5086-H116) used in shipbuilding. This forms the **CO3 (Apply)** cognitive level of the course.

The three primary comparison axes evaluated by classification societies (Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, IRS) are:

1. **Specific Mechanical Performance** — strength and stiffness per unit density.
2. **Environmental Durability** — corrosion, marine fouling, UV, and fatigue resistance.
3. **Lifecycle Economics** — fabrication, maintenance, and end-of-life disposal costs.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Boat Analogy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Layman Intuition):**
> Imagine you are building a small boat for a Kerala backwater trip.
>
> - **Steel** is like a *heavy iron cooking vessel* — strong, but it rusts when it touches salty water, and you need two strong men to lift it.
> - **Aluminium** is like a *stainless steel lunch box* — much lighter, won't rust badly, but it dents easily if you drop it, and it corrodes in salt water if not protected.
> - **Composite (GRP)** is like a *fibre-reinforced plastic chair* — feather-light, never rusts, doesn't dent, and you can mould it into any curved shape. However, if you expose it to direct fire or sharp impacts, it loses its strength silently.
>
> In short: **Composites trade raw toughness and fire resistance for lightness, corrosion immunity, and design freedom.**

## 1.3 The Three Material Triad — Baseline Metrics

For a fair KTU-level comparison, the following standard marine-grade baselines are assumed:

| Material | Density $\rho$ (kg/m³) | Tensile Strength $\sigma_{uts}$ (MPa) | Young's Modulus $E$ (GPa) | Specific Strength (kN·m/kg) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mild Steel (ASTM A131 Grade A)** | **7850** | 400 – 490 | 200 | ~ 55 |
| **Aluminium 5083-H116** | **2660** | 270 – 305 | 70 | ~ 105 |
| **GRP (Glass/Epoxy, quasi-isotropic)** | **1800 – 1900** | 200 – 350 | 15 – 25 | ~ 130 |
| **CFRP (Carbon/Epoxy)** | **1550 – 1600** | 600 – 1500 | 120 – 180 | ~ 600 |

> The values above are **typical published marine-grade benchmarks** used by Det Norske Veritas (DNV) and Lloyd's Register for scantling calculations. Note how **CFRP's specific strength is ~10× that of steel**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comparative Engineering Logic Framework

The performance comparison is best understood by breaking it into **six engineering pillars** (this is the KTU-recommended analytical structure):

### Pillar 1 — Weight & Buoyancy Economics (Lightweight Advantage)

- **Composites win decisively.** A typical GRP hull weighs **35–50% less** than an equivalent steel hull, and **15–25% less** than an aluminium hull.
- The weight savings cascade into:
  - Reduced displacement → smaller engine → lower fuel consumption
  - Higher payload capacity (deadweight)
  - Better stability (lower vertical centre of gravity for same topside mass)

### Pillar 2 — Corrosion Resistance (The Decisive Marine Advantage)

- **Steel** corrodes in seawater at **0.1 – 0.5 mm/year** without cathodic protection.
- **Aluminium 5083** has good saltwater resistance but is susceptible to **galvanic corrosion** when coupled with dissimilar metals (e.g., copper-based propellers).
- **Composites** are inherently *electrochemically inert* — no rust, no galvanic action, no need for sacrificial anodes.
- This translates to **30–60% reduction in lifecycle maintenance hours** (Lloyd's Register survey data).

### Pillar 3 — Fatigue & Damage Tolerance

| Material | Fatigue Limit (% of UTS) | Damage Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Steel | ~ 50% (well-defined endurance limit) | Ductile, crack visible before failure |
| Aluminium 5083 | ~ 35% | Ductile, visible necking |
| GRP | No true fatigue limit (degrades continuously) | **Silent, internal micro-cracking** |
| CFRP | Excellent fatigue retention | Brittle, sudden delamination |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Composites do *not* yield plastically before failure. This is the **single most critical disadvantage** and the reason classification societies mandate stricter inspection regimes (annual instead of biennial in some cases).

### Pillar 4 — Design Flexibility & Manufacturability

- **Steel and aluminium** require rolling, pressing, bending, and welding — limited to developable surfaces.
- **Composites** are laid up in moulds → complex double-curvature hull forms, integrated stiffeners, and embedded inserts (pi-theta-phi filament winding) become economical.
- **Result:** Reduced part count, fewer fasteners, and lower *hull-form drag coefficient* (smoother surfaces).

### Pillar 5 — Fire, Smoke & Toxicity (FST)

- **Steel and aluminium** are non-combustible.
- **Polymer-matrix composites** burn, releasing smoke and toxic gases.
- Mitigation: phenolic resins, intumescent coatings, fire-retardant additives (IMO FTP Code Part 2 compliance).

### Pillar 6 — Lifecycle Cost (Whole-of-Life Economics)

The KTU-relevant cost equation is:

$$C_{LCC} = C_{cap} + C_{fab} + C_{maint} + C_{fuel} + C_{disposal}$$

where each term behaves differently per material family.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use this table as the rapid-revision master. Mastery of these equations is mandatory for ESE Module questions.

| # | Formula / Relation | Engineering Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_{sp} = \dfrac{\sigma_{uts}}{\rho}$ | **Specific Strength** — strength per unit weight | kN·m/kg |
| 2 | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$ | **Specific Modulus** — stiffness per unit weight | MN·m/kg |
| 3 | $R_{A/W} = \dfrac{W_{steel} - W_{comp}}{W_{steel}} \times 100$ | **Weight Reduction (%)** of composite vs steel hull | % |
| 4 | $\Delta F = f\left(\frac{W_{light}}{W_{base}}\right)^{n}$ | Fuel savings scaling (n ≈ 3 for displacement hulls) | — |
| 5 | $C_{LCC} = C_{cap} + C_{fab} + \sum_{i=1}^{N}\frac{C_{maint,i}}{(1+r)^i} + C_{disposal}$ | **Net Present Value (NPV)** of lifecycle cost | ₹ / USD |
| 6 | $\tau_{allow} = \dfrac{\tau_{uts}}{SF}$ | Allowable shear with **Safety Factor** (SF ≈ 3–4 for composites) | MPa |
| 7 | $\sigma_{crip} = k_{c} \cdot E_{c} \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^{2}$ | Composite plate **crippling / buckling** stress (DNV PhD-41) | MPa |
| 8 | $V_{f} \cdot \rho_{f} + V_{m} \cdot \rho_{m} = \rho_{c}$ | **Rule of Mixtures** for composite density | kg/m³ |
| 9 | $E_{c} = E_{f} \cdot V_{f} + E_{m} \cdot V_{m}$ | **Rule of Mixtures** for composite modulus (iso-strain) | GPa |

> *Note: All equations use standard SI symbols. $V_f$ = fibre volume fraction, $V_m$ = matrix volume fraction, $E$ = Young's Modulus, $\rho$ = density.*

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The above metrics feed directly into three industrial applications:

1. **Hull scantling determination** under DNV GL Rules for classification of ships (Pt.3 Ch.4).
2. **Topside weight-out audits** for naval combatants (e.g., Indian Navy *Kamorta*-class corvette superstructure).
3. **Fuel Efficiency (EEDI/EEXI)** compliance for IMO 2023 — composites are central to green-shipbuilding roadmaps.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Weight Reduction of a GRP Hull vs Steel Hull

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):** A displacement hull has a structural mass $M$ when built in mild steel ($\rho_s = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$). The shipyard is evaluating substitution with GRP ($\rho_g = 1850 \text{ kg/m}^3$) while keeping the **stiffness-to-weight ratio equivalent** by increasing the laminate thickness $t$.

The **equivalent stiffness condition** (in terms of plate bending) is:

$$D_s = D_g \quad \text{where} \quad D = \frac{E \cdot t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$$

> For a first-order comparison (assuming $\nu_s \approx \nu_g \approx 0.3$), Poisson's term cancels.

Equating the steel and GRP plate stiffnesses:

$$\frac{E_s \cdot t_s^3}{12} = \frac{E_g \cdot t_g^3}{12}$$

Solving for $t_g$:

$$t_g = t_s \cdot \left(\frac{E_s}{E_g}\right)^{1/3}$$

Substituting the standard values $E_s = 200 \text{ GPa}$ and $E_g = 20 \text{ GPa}$:

$$t_g = t_s \cdot \left(\frac{200}{20}\right)^{1/3} = t_s \cdot (10)^{1/3}$$

Evaluating the cube root of 10:

$$10^{1/3} \approx 2.154$$

Therefore the GRP laminate must be roughly **2.15× thicker** than the steel plate to achieve the same bending stiffness.

Now compute the **mass ratio** for an equivalent panel of area $A$:

$$M_s = \rho_s \cdot A \cdot t_s = 7850 \cdot A \cdot t_s$$

$$M_g = \rho_g \cdot A \cdot t_g = 1850 \cdot A \cdot (2.154 \cdot t_s)$$

Taking the ratio:

$$\frac{M_g}{M_s} = \frac{1850 \cdot 2.154}{7850} = \frac{3985}{7850} \approx 0.5076$$

So even with the 2.15× thickness penalty, **the GRP panel is ~ 50.8% the mass of the steel panel**.

The **weight reduction percentage** is therefore:

$$R_{A/W} = (1 - 0.5076) \times 100 \approx 49.2\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Always state the **stiffness-equivalence** assumption. A naive "equal strength" comparison gives $R_{A/W} \approx 76\%$, which is unrealistically optimistic. Real ship scantlings are governed by *stiffness* (deflection/buckling), not strength.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fuel Savings from Hull-Weight Reduction

Assume a small displacement vessel (e.g., a 25 m patrol boat) where propulsive power $P$ scales with displacement $W$ approximately as:

$$P \propto W^{0.83}$$

This is the standard admiralty coefficient approximation for displacement hulls at constant speed.

If hull weight drops by $\Delta W$, the **fractional power reduction** is:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P} = 1 - \left(\frac{W - \Delta W}{W}\right)^{0.83}$$

For a 30% hull weight reduction ($\Delta W / W = 0.30$):

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P} = 1 - (0.70)^{0.83}$$

Computing $(0.70)^{0.83}$:

$$\ln(0.70) \approx -0.3567$$

$$0.83 \times (-0.3567) = -0.2961$$

$$e^{-0.2961} \approx 0.744$$

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P} = 1 - 0.744 = 0.256 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 25.6\% \text{ power reduction}$$

> **Conclusion:** A 30% lighter hull yields a ~ 25–26% reduction in required propulsive power at design speed, directly translating to fuel savings of similar magnitude.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Advantages vs Disadvantages (KTU High-Yield Table)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the single most important comparative table for the KTU exam. Memorize the *directional* outcomes (composite better/worse/equal).

| Performance Parameter | **Composites** | **Steel** | **Aluminium 5083** | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Density (kg/m³) | 1500 – 1900 | 7850 | 2660 | **Composite** |
| Specific Strength | Very High | Moderate | High | **Composite** |
| Specific Stiffness | Moderate–High | Low | Moderate | **CFRP** (composite) |
| Corrosion in Seawater | Excellent | Poor (needs coating + CP) | Good (galvanic risk) | **Composite** |
| Fatigue Limit | None (progressive) | Defined (~ 50% UTS) | Defined (~ 35% UTS) | **Steel** |
| Fire Resistance | Poor (combustible) | Excellent | Excellent | **Steel/Aluminium** |
| Damage Detectability | Poor (silent failure) | Excellent (visible crack) | Good (visible dent) | **Steel/Aluminium** |
| Repairability at Sea | Difficult | Easy (weld) | Moderate (weld) | **Steel** |
| Initial Material Cost | High (3–5× steel) | Low | Moderate (2× steel) | **Steel** |
| Fabrication Cost (complex shape) | Low (single mould) | High (many parts) | Moderate | **Composite** |
| Lifecycle Maintenance Cost | Low | High (blasting, painting) | Moderate | **Composite** |
| Recyclability / Disposal | Difficult (downcycle) | Excellent (re-melt) | Excellent (re-melt) | **Steel/Aluminium** |
| Magnetic Signature (stealth) | Very Low (non-magnetic) | High | Non-magnetic | **Composite/Aluminium** |
| Radar Cross Section (with shape) | Low (radome-friendly) | High (reflective) | High | **Composite** |
| Design Flexibility (double curvature) | Excellent | Poor | Moderate | **Composite** |

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Composite Density by Rule of Mixtures

For a GRP laminate with fibre volume fraction $V_f = 0.45$ and matrix volume fraction $V_m = 0.55$:

$$\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m$$

Substituting $\rho_f$ (E-glass) $= 2540 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $\rho_m$ (polyester) $= 1240 \text{ kg/m}^3$:

$$\rho_c = (0.45)(2540) + (0.55)(1240)$$

$$\rho_c = 1143 + 682 = 1825 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

This matches the typical published range (1800 – 1900 kg/m³), validating the model.

## 3.5 Comparative Verdict Summary (CO3 Application)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Verdict (Engineering Synthesis):**
> Composites are **superior** when the design priorities are: *lightweight, corrosion-free operation, complex geometry, and low magnetic/acoustic signature* (e.g., minesweepers, fast patrol craft, superstructure of warships).
>
> Composites are **inferior** when the design priorities are: *high fire survivability, easy in-service repair, recyclability, and lowest first cost* (e.g., large commercial bulk carriers, tankers, container ships).
>
> Aluminium occupies the **middle ground** — combining light weight with good toughness, ductility, and recyclability, at moderate cost.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**"List any THREE advantages of using composite materials over steel in shipbuilding."**

**Model Answer (3 key points, 1 mark each):**

1. **Higher specific strength** — GRP has ~ 2.3× and CFRP has ~ 10× the specific strength of mild steel, enabling lighter hulls for the same load capacity.
2. **Superior corrosion resistance** — Composites are electrochemically inert in seawater, eliminating the need for cathodic protection and reducing maintenance cost by **30–60%**.
3. **Design flexibility** — Single-piece moulded complex hull forms (double curvature, integrated stiffeners) reduce part count, eliminate welding, and improve hydrodynamic efficiency.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**"State TWO disadvantages of composites compared to mild steel in marine applications."**

**Model Answer (2 key points):**

1. **Poor damage detectability and brittle failure mode** — Composites do not yield plastically; they fail by silent internal delamination, requiring advanced NDT (ultrasonic / thermography) for inspection.
2. **Poor fire performance** — Polymer matrices are combustible and release toxic smoke; this requires expensive intumescent coatings and phenolic resins to meet IMO FTP Code.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**(a)** Explain with quantitative reasoning why a GRP hull can achieve a 40–50% weight reduction over a steel hull, even when the laminate must be made thicker to maintain plate stiffness. Use the rule of mixtures and the standard plate bending equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the corrosion behaviour of composites, mild steel, and aluminium 5083 in seawater. What are the implications for hull maintenance schedules? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — State the rule of mixtures for composite density:**

$$\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m$$

For a typical GRP with $V_f = 0.45$ (E-glass, $\rho_f = 2540$) and $V_m = 0.55$ (polyester, $\rho_m = 1240$):

$$\rho_c = 0.45 \times 2540 + 0.55 \times 1240 = 1825 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Equate the plate bending stiffness of steel and GRP:**

$$\frac{E_s \cdot t_s^3}{12(1-\nu_s^2)} = \frac{E_g \cdot t_g^3}{12(1-\nu_g^2)}$$

Since $\nu_s \approx \nu_g \approx 0.3$, the denominator cancels.

**Step 3 — Solve for thickness ratio:**

$$t_g = t_s \cdot \left(\frac{E_s}{E_g}\right)^{1/3} = t_s \cdot \left(\frac{200}{20}\right)^{1/3} \approx 2.154 \cdot t_s$$

**Step 4 — Compute mass ratio:**

$$\frac{M_g}{M_s} = \frac{\rho_g \cdot t_g}{\rho_s \cdot t_s} = \frac{1825 \times 2.154}{7850 \times 1.0} \approx 0.50$$

**Step 5 — Weight reduction:**

$$R_{A/W} = (1 - 0.50) \times 100 = 50\%$$

**Conclusion:** GRP hull is ~ 50% lighter despite being 2.15× thicker, because the density advantage ($\rho_g / \rho_s \approx 0.23$) more than compensates for the thickness penalty.

---

### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

| Material | Seawater Corrosion Mechanism | Rate / Behaviour | Maintenance Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mild Steel** | Electrochemical oxidation (Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻); rust formation | 0.1 – 0.5 mm/year (no protection) | Mandatory: coating, sacrificial anodes, ICCP; dry-dock every 2.5–5 years |
| **Aluminium 5083** | Forms protective oxide layer, but **galvanic** corrosion when coupled with Cu-based propellers | Pitting in stagnant seawater | Insulation of dissimilar metals; anode replacement every 3 years |
| **GRP/CFRP** | **Electrochemically inert** — no anodic or cathodic reaction | Negligible corrosion | **No sacrificial anodes, no anti-fouling paint needed**; dry-dock interval can extend to 7–10 years for non-structural items |

**Implication:** Composite hulls reduce *unplanned* and *scheduled* maintenance man-hours by **30–60%**, translating to higher operational availability (~ 10–15% more sea-time per year).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**(a)** Discuss the fatigue and damage tolerance behaviour of composites versus steel and aluminium. Why is silent failure a critical concern for ship classification? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 30 m patrol boat hull is to be redesigned in GRP instead of mild steel. The steel hull weighs 18 tonnes. Estimate the weight reduction and the corresponding propulsive power saving. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Steel** exhibits a clear **endurance limit** at ~ 50% of UTS. Below this threshold, the material can endure infinite cycles — a major design simplification. Cracks grow slowly and are visually detectable.

**Aluminium 5083** has a fatigue limit around 35% of UTS; damage is visible as surface necking and is repairable.

**Composites (GRP/CFRP)** have **no true fatigue limit**. Damage accumulates as:
- Matrix microcracking → delamination → fibre breakage → final rupture.

This is a **silent, internal, progressive** failure mode. There is no macroscopic warning.

**Why classification societies care (Lloyd's / DNV):**
- Underwater hull damage cannot be visually inspected without divers.
- A delaminated GRP hull may suddenly lose 40–60% of its stiffness without external signs.
- DNV and Lloyd's therefore mandate **annual instead of biennial** special surveys for composite vessels, with **mandatory ultrasonic and thermographic NDT** for critical structural areas.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Students often write *"composites have poor fatigue resistance."* This is imprecise. The correct phrasing is: **"Composites do not have a true endurance limit; they suffer progressive, non-detectable internal damage accumulation that can lead to sudden catastrophic failure."** Marks are deducted for the wrong technical vocabulary.

---

### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Given:** Steel hull mass $M_s = 18 \text{ tonnes} = 18000 \text{ kg}$, GRP substitution with stiffness equivalence.

**Step 1 — Mass of GRP hull (from Section 3.1):**

$$M_g = 0.5076 \times M_s = 0.5076 \times 18000 = 9140 \text{ kg} \approx 9.14 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 2 — Weight saved:**

$$\Delta W = 18000 - 9140 = 8860 \text{ kg} \approx 8.86 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 3 — Fractional weight reduction:**

$$\frac{\Delta W}{W_s} = \frac{8860}{18000} \approx 0.492 \text{ or } 49.2\%$$

**Step 4 — Power savings using admiralty coefficient scaling $P \propto W^{0.83}$:**

$$\frac{P_g}{P_s} = \left(\frac{M_g}{M_s}\right)^{0.83} = (0.5076)^{0.83}$$

Computing $(0.5076)^{0.83}$:

$$\ln(0.5076) \approx -0.6775$$

$$0.83 \times (-0.6775) = -0.5623$$

$$e^{-0.5623} \approx 0.570$$

So:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P_s} = 1 - 0.570 = 0.430 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 43\% \text{ power reduction}$$

**Final Answer:** Approximately **8.86 tonnes weight saved (49.2% reduction)** and approximately **43% propulsive power reduction** at design speed. Over a 20-year service life, this translates to a fuel-cost saving of several crore rupees for a typical patrol boat.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Common Pitfalls):**
>
> 1. **Naïve strength-equivalence comparison** — Students compare composites and steel on equal *thickness* and ignore the stiffness equivalence. Always state your assumption explicitly.
> 2. **"Composites don't corrode"** — This is too simplistic. Composites absorb moisture and suffer *matrix hydrolysis* and *fibre-matrix debonding* in long-term seawater immersion. Use the precise term: *"electrochemically inert, but not immune to moisture ingress."*
> 3. **Ignoring fire performance** — A complete KTU answer must always cite the IMO FTP Code and the need for fire-retardant resins in composite superstructures.
> 4. **Cost-only comparisons** — Capital cost favours steel, but lifecycle cost (NPV over 25–30 years) often favours composites. State *both* metrics.
> 5. **Magnitude errors in rule of mixtures** — Many students add weights instead of volume-fraction-weighted densities. Practice the formula until it is automatic.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for ESE):**

- **Composites vs Steel — Weight:** Composites win by ~ 50% for stiffness-equivalent GRP, ~ 60–70% for CFRP.
- **Composites vs Steel — Corrosion:** Composites are electrochemically inert; steel needs cathodic protection + coating.
- **Composites vs Steel — Fire:** Composites lose; steel wins decisively (non-combustible).
- **Composites vs Steel — Repair:** Steel is weld-repairable at sea; composites need controlled-environment scarf repairs.
- **Composites vs Steel — Fatigue:** Steel has a defined endurance limit; composites have progressive silent damage.
- **Composites vs Steel — Cost:** Steel wins on first cost; composites win on lifecycle cost.
- **Composites vs Aluminium — Weight:** Composites still lighter (~ 30% advantage).
- **Composites vs Aluminium — Cost:** Aluminium is cheaper than CFRP but more expensive than GRP.
- **Composites vs Aluminium — Galvanic:** Aluminium needs careful insulation from Cu alloys; composites have no such issue.
- **Three core formulas to memorize cold:**
  1. $R_{A/W} = \dfrac{W_s - W_c}{W_s} \times 100$
  2. $t_c = t_s \cdot \left(\dfrac{E_s}{E_c}\right)^{1/3}$
  3. $\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m$
- **Key design standards to cite:** ASTM A131 (steel), ASTM B928 (aluminium 5083), DNV Pt.3 Ch.4 (composite hulls), IMO FTP Code Part 2 (fire), Lloyd's Register SSC Rules for FRP craft.
- **Application rule of thumb:** Use composites for **< 50 m** high-speed craft, superstructures of larger vessels, and stealth platforms. Use steel for **> 50 m** commercial hulls. Use aluminium for **fast ferries and naval auxiliaries** where moderate weight saving and good toughness are required.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Marine Concrete
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Marine Concrete

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marine Concrete** is a specially engineered, high-performance cementitious composite designed to withstand the aggressive physico-chemical and biological environment of seawater exposure zones — submerged, splash, tidal, and atmospheric marine atmospheres. It is formulated using low-permeability binder systems, carefully graded non-reactive aggregates, controlled water-binder ratios, and durable corrosion-resistant reinforcement (often epoxy-coated, stainless, or fibre-reinforced polymer rebars) to deliver long-term structural integrity in naval, offshore, and shipbuilding infrastructure.

### 1.2 Classification by Exposure Zone (per ACI 357R & IS 456:2000)

| Zone | Description | Severity |
|------|-------------|----------|
| **Submerged Zone** | Permanently below mean low water level | Moderate chemical attack |
| **Splash Zone** | Between mean low and mean high tide | **Most severe** (wetting-drying cycles) |
| **Tidal Zone** | Alternate wet and dry due to tides | High chloride ingress |
| **Atmospheric Zone** | Above splash, salt-laden wind exposure | Moderate chloride deposition |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Diver's Wetsuit" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:** Imagine a diver entering icy seawater. The wetsuit works on three principles — (i) **low permeability** (trapped neoprene layer), (ii) **flexibility** (resists cracking on bending), and (iii) **chemical stability** (does not react with salt). Marine concrete works on the *exact same three principles* — low permeability, crack resistance, and chemical inertness. The "wetsuit" of marine concrete is its dense cement paste matrix reinforced with corrosion-protected steel.

### 1.4 Why Marine Concrete in Shipbuilding?

Although shipbuilding is dominated by ferrous and non-ferrous metals, marine concrete is indispensable for:
- **Dry docks and graving docks** (floor and walls)
- **Floating concrete pontoons and caissons**
- **Quay walls, jetties, and breasting dolphins**
- **Offshore wind turbine foundations**
- **Prestressed concrete vessels (PCV)** — historically used as lighters
- **Ship launching slipways**

### 1.5 Standard Reference Codes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Governing Standards for Marine Concrete:**
> - **ACI 357R** — Guide for the Design and Construction of Fixed Offshore Concrete Structures
> - **ACI 318** — Building Code Requirements for Structural Concrete
> - **IS 456:2000** — Plain and Reinforced Concrete
> - **IS 8753** — Design of Concrete Structures for Resistance to Sulfate Attack
> - **BS 6349** — Maritime Structures (Part 1-8)
> - **FIP/IStructE** — Recommendations for the Design of Marine Concrete Structures
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constituent Materials — The Engineering Logic

**2.1.1 Binder (Cement)**
- **Sulfate Resisting Portland Cement (SRPC)** — IS 12330; low C₃A content (<5%) resists sulfate attack from seawater MgSO₄ and Na₂SO₄
- **Pozzolana Portland Cement (PPC)** — IS 1489; fly ash/silica fume addition refines pore structure
- **High Alumina Cement (HAC)** — rapid strength, but conversion reactions limit long-term use
- **Blended cements** with **Ground Granulated Blast Furnace Slag (GGBS, 50–70%)** — superior chloride binding

**2.1.2 Aggregates**
- **Coarse:** Crushed granite, basalt, or dense limestone; max nominal size **20 mm** for heavily reinforced sections
- **Fine:** Natural river sand or manufactured M-sand; FM between **2.4–3.1**
- **Critical:** Chloride content of aggregates < **0.01%** by mass of cement; sulfate content < **0.4%**

**2.1.3 Water**
- Potable water with **pH ≥ 6**, total dissolved solids < **2000 ppm**, chlorides < **500 ppm**

**2.1.4 Admixtures (The "Performance Modifiers")**
- **Plasticizers / Superplasticizers** (ASTM C494) — workability at low w/c ratio
- **Mineral Admixtures** — Silica fume (5–10%), Metakaolin (5–15%), Fly ash (15–30%)
- **Corrosion Inhibitors** — Calcium nitrite, aminoalcohol-based
- **Air-entraining agents** (4–6% air) — freeze-thaw resistance in cold seas

### 2.2 Why and How — The Durability Mechanism

| Mechanism | "Why" | "How" Marine Concrete Resists |
|-----------|-------|-------------------------------|
| Chloride ingress | Seawater contains ~19,000 ppm Cl⁻ | Low w/c, dense ITZ, SCMs bind Cl⁻ as Friedel's salt |
| Sulfate attack | Mg²⁺ and SO₄²⁻ react with C₃A → ettringite/gypsum | SRPC, low C₃A, pozzolanic replacement |
| Freeze-thaw | Pore water expands ~9% on freezing | Entrained air voids (4–6%) |
| Carbonation | CO₂ reduces cover pH, depassivates steel | Low permeability, increased cover (50–100 mm) |
| Abrasion-erosion | Wave action, sand impact | High-strength matrix (≥ 40 MPa), hard aggregates |
| Biofouling | Marine organisms secrete acids | Surface coatings, copper-silicate additives |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Marine Concrete Mix Design & Properties

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Sheet (High-Yield for KTU ESE)**

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $f_c = \dfrac{A}{B^{w/c}}$ | **Abrams' Law** — compressive strength vs w/c | $f_c$ in MPa |
| 2 | $w/c = \dfrac{0.45 \cdot \gamma_c}{\sigma_{28} + 0.10 \cdot \gamma_c}$ | **IS 10262** empirical w/c relation (approx.) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $V_{pore} = \dfrac{w/c}{w/c + 0.32}$ | **Powers' Model** capillary porosity | fraction |
| 4 | $D_{cl} = D_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{t_0}\right)^n$ | **Fick's 2nd Law** apparent chloride diffusion | $\text{mm}^2/\text{yr}$ |
| 5 | $C_{x,t} = C_s - (C_s - C_i) \cdot \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{D_{cl}\cdot t}}\right)$ | **Cl⁻ concentration** at depth $x$, time $t$ | % by wt. cement |
| 6 | $C_{\min} = k \cdot \phi + d$ | **Min. cover** (ACI 357R), $k=1.0$–$1.5$, $d=$ bar dia | mm |
| 7 | $f_{ck,\text{marine}} \geq 40$ MPa | Min. characteristic strength (KTU/IS reco.) | MPa |
| 8 | $w/c_{\max} = 0.40$ | Max. w/c for severe marine exposure | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Slump} = 75\text{–}100$ mm | Workability for tremie-placed marine concrete | mm |
| 10 | $A_{s,\min} = 0.04 \cdot A_g$ | Minimum steel ratio (IS 456, sea-water exposure) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** KTU expects the use of `erf` (error function) in chloride diffusion numericals. For $x = 2\sqrt{Dt}$, $\text{erf} \approx 0.9545$; for $x = \sqrt{Dt}$, $\text{erf} \approx 0.6827$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In shipbuilding, marine concrete is the **structural backbone of graving docks** — the ship is floated in, water pumped out, and hull repair proceeds. The dock floor must resist uplift hydrostatic pressure (~10 kPa/m of water depth), chloride attack from the harbor, and abrasion from ship landings. The same concrete design philosophy extends to **floating concrete barges (e.g., "Phoenix" caissons of Mulberry Harbour in WWII)** — they had to be mass-producible, seawater-resistant, and structurally reliable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Abrams' Law for Compressive Strength

**Statement:** Compressive strength of concrete is an exponential function of the water-cement ratio.

**Starting empirical hypothesis (Abrams, 1918):**

$$
f_c = \dfrac{A}{B^{w/c}}
$$

**Logarithmic transformation to obtain a linear fit:**

$$
\ln(f_c) = \ln(A) - (w/c) \cdot \ln(B)
$$

**Calibration against IS 10262 reference data (ordinary Portland cement, 28-day curing):**

For $w/c = 0.40$, $f_c = 40$ MPa
For $w/c = 0.60$, $f_c = 25$ MPa

**Substituting:**

$$
\ln(40) = \ln(A) - 0.40 \cdot \ln(B)
$$
$$
\ln(25) = \ln(A) - 0.60 \cdot \ln(B)
$$

**Subtracting the second from the first:**

$$
\ln(40) - \ln(25) = (0.60 - 0.40) \cdot \ln(B)
$$
$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{40}{25}\right) = 0.20 \cdot \ln(B)
$$
$$
\ln(1.6) = 0.20 \cdot \ln(B)
$$
$$
0.4700 = 0.20 \cdot \ln(B)
$$
$$
\ln(B) = 2.350 \quad\Rightarrow\quad B = e^{2.350} = 10.486
$$

**Solving for A** using $w/c = 0.40$ case:

$$
\ln(A) = \ln(40) + 0.40 \cdot \ln(B) = 3.6889 + 0.40 \cdot 2.350
$$
$$
\ln(A) = 3.6889 + 0.9400 = 4.6289
$$
$$
A = e^{4.6289} = 102.4
$$

**Final calibrated Abrams' Law:**

$$
\boxed{\,f_c = \dfrac{102.4}{10.486^{\,w/c}}\,}
$$

**Engineering insight:** A reduction of $w/c$ from 0.55 to 0.40 (a 27% reduction in water) yields a strength increase of approximately **45–55%** — proving *why* low w/c is the cornerstone of marine durability.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Capillary Porosity (Powers' Model)

**Step 1 — Definition of gel-space ratio:**
For every gram of cement, hydration products occupy volume $V_h$ and unreacted water occupies capillary pores $V_{cap}$.

$$
V_h = 0.32 \cdot C \quad\text{(volume of hydration gel per gram cement)}
$$
$$
V_{cap} = (w/c) \cdot C - 0.32 \cdot C = C \cdot [(w/c) - 0.32]
$$

**Step 2 — Capillary porosity:**

$$
\phi_{cap} = \dfrac{V_{cap}}{V_{cap} + V_h} = \dfrac{C[(w/c) - 0.32]}{C[(w/c) - 0.32] + 0.32 C}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplification by dividing numerator & denominator by C:**

$$
\phi_{cap} = \dfrac{(w/c) - 0.32}{(w/c) - 0.32 + 0.32} = \dfrac{(w/c) - 0.32}{w/c}
$$

**Step 4 — Final form:**

$$
\boxed{\,\phi_{cap} = 1 - \dfrac{0.32}{w/c} = \dfrac{w/c - 0.32}{w/c}\,}
$$

**Numerical check for marine-grade $w/c = 0.40$:**

$$
\phi_{cap} = \dfrac{0.40 - 0.32}{0.40} = \dfrac{0.08}{0.40} = 0.20 \;(20\%)
$$

Compare with $w/c = 0.60$: $\phi_{cap} = 0.467$ (47%) — a **2.3× reduction** in capillary porosity dramatically lowers chloride ingress pathways.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — Mix Design for M40 Marine Concrete

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Typical):** Design a concrete mix of grade **M40** for a jetty in the splash zone using SRPC, 20 mm aggregate, river sand (FM = 2.7), and a target slump of **100 mm**.

**Step 1 — Target strength (IS 10262, using 1.65σ where σ = 5 MPa):**

$$
f_{ck,\text{target}} = 40 + 1.65 \times 5 = 48.25 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Selection of w/c from Abrams' calibrated law:**

$$
48.25 = \dfrac{102.4}{10.486^{w/c}}
$$
$$
10.486^{w/c} = \dfrac{102.4}{48.25} = 2.1223
$$
$$
(w/c) \cdot \ln(10.486) = \ln(2.1223) = 0.7520
$$
$$
w/c = \dfrac{0.7520}{2.350} = 0.320
$$

Apply practical minimum $w/c = 0.40$ (marine exposure). **Adopt $w/c = 0.40$.**

**Step 3 — Water content for 100 mm slump, 20 mm aggregate:**

From IS 10262 Table 2: $W = 186$ kg/m³ (for nominal 25 mm; reduce 3% for 20 mm):

$$
W = 186 \times 0.97 = 180.4 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Cement content:**

$$
C = \dfrac{W}{w/c} = \dfrac{180.4}{0.40} = 451 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Coarse & fine aggregate proportion (IS 10262, FA/CA = 0.62 for 20 mm):**

Absolute volume calculation (specific gravities: cement 3.15, CA 2.74, FA 2.65, water 1.00):

$$
V_{abs} = \dfrac{451}{3.15 \times 1000} + \dfrac{180.4}{1000} = 0.1432 + 0.1804 = 0.3236 \text{ m}^3
$$

Available aggregate volume (entrapped air 1% = 0.01 m³):

$$
V_{agg} = 1 - 0.3236 - 0.01 = 0.6664 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 6 — Split aggregate:**

$$
\text{Fine Aggregate} = 0.6664 \times 0.38 = 0.2532 \text{ m}^3
$$
$$
\text{Coarse Aggregate} = 0.6664 \times 0.62 = 0.4132 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 7 — Masses (kg/m³):**

$$
\text{FA} = 0.2532 \times 2.65 \times 1000 = 671 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$
$$
\text{CA} = 0.4132 \times 2.74 \times 1000 = 1132 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Final Mix Proportions (per m³):**

$$
\boxed{\,\text{Cement} = 451\;\text{kg},\quad \text{FA} = 671\;\text{kg},\quad \text{CA} = 1132\;\text{kg},\quad \text{Water} = 180\;\text{kg}\,}
$$

Mix ratio by mass: **1 : 1.49 : 2.51** at $w/c = 0.40$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

    R4 --> R5[Water Stop PVC at Construction Joint]
