
### 4.4 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards (Applied)

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs above use **alphanumeric prefixes** (`A1`, `B1`, `P1`, `C1`, `M1`, `R1`, etc.) — none collide with reserved Mermaid keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`). Labels are written in plain uppercase alphanumeric text without `**` bold, italics, or embedded HTML tables, ensuring clean rendering.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*List the essential properties required of concrete used in marine structures. Why is the water-cement ratio the single most influential parameter?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Essential properties of marine concrete:
1. **High strength** (≥ 40 MPa characteristic compressive strength) to resist wave-induced stresses.
2. **Low permeability** to limit chloride and sulfate ingress.
3. **High durability** with adequate cover (≥ 75 mm in splash zone) to protect reinforcement.
4. **Good workability** (slump 75–100 mm) for placement by tremie in submerged zones.
5. **Chemical resistance** to MgSO₄ and Na₂SO₄ attack via SRPC.

Water-cement ratio is the most influential parameter because, per **Abrams' Law**, compressive strength varies exponentially with $w/c$. It also directly controls **capillary porosity** (Powers' model: $\phi_{cap} = 1 - 0.32/(w/c)$), which governs the ingress pathways for chlorides and sulfates. Reducing $w/c$ from 0.60 to 0.40 reduces porosity by ~57%, dramatically extending service life.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*Explain the role of pozzolanic and latent hydraulic admixtures in marine concrete. Give two examples of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Pozzolanic admixtures** (silica fume, fly ash, metakaolin, RHA) react with portlandite $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ released during cement hydration to form additional C-S-H gel, **refining the pore structure** and **consuming the weakest hydration product**, thereby reducing permeability and chloride diffusivity. Example: **Silica fume (8%)** and **Fly ash (25%)**.

**Latent hydraulic admixtures** (GGBS, granulated slag) hydrate in the presence of an alkaline activator (CH from cement) to form C-S-H and hydrotalcite phases. They bind chlorides as Friedel's salt, increase long-term strength, and reduce heat of hydration — beneficial for mass marine pours. Example: **GGBS (50–70%)** and **High calcium fly ash**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Module contributes 1 × 14-mark question with **internal choice** (Question A OR Question B). Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Define marine concrete. Explain in detail the various exposure zones in marine environments and the corresponding durability requirements for concrete in each zone. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the constituents of marine concrete (cement, aggregates, water, admixtures) with their quality requirements as per **IS 456:2000** and **ACI 357R**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition and Exposure Zones (7 marks):**
Marine concrete is a high-durability, low-permeability cementitious composite engineered to resist the aggressive chemical, physical, and biological actions of seawater. As per **ACI 357R**, marine exposure is classified into four zones:

- **Submerged Zone** — permanently below low water level; concrete is always saturated; oxygen supply limited; corrosion rates reduced; sulfate attack dominates.
- **Splash Zone** — most severe zone with continuous wetting-drying; highest chloride ingress, oxygen availability, and biological fouling; demands **maximum cover (75–100 mm)** and **densest mix**.
- **Tidal Zone** — alternate wet/dry cycles similar to splash; moderate severity.
- **Atmospheric Zone** — above splash, exposed to salt spray; corrosion moderate; lower cover acceptable (50 mm).

Durability requirements include: $f_{ck} \geq 40$ MPa, $w/c \leq 0.40$, minimum cement content **400 kg/m³**, and crack control via maximum bar spacing and temperature control.

**(b) Constituents & Quality (7 marks):**
As per **IS 456:2000** for severe exposure:
- **Cement:** SRPC (IS 12330), OPC with pozzolana, or supersulfated cement. Maximum C₃A = 5% in SRPC. Min. cement content = 400 kg/m³ (severe) and 350 kg/m³ (moderate).
- **Aggregates:** Clean, durable, free from chlorides (< 0.01% by mass of cement), sulfates (< 0.4%). 20 mm nominal max size.
- **Water:** Potable, pH > 6, chlorides < 500 ppm, sulfates < 400 ppm.
- **Admixtures:** Plasticizers (ASTM C494), silica fume (5–10%), air-entraining agents (4–6%).

As per **ACI 357R**: silica fume addition (≥ 7%), corrosion-inhibitor use, cover = 50 mm + bar diameter (minimum 75 mm for splash), supplementary cementing materials preferred.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the Fick's 2nd Law of chloride diffusion in marine concrete. A concrete has an apparent chloride diffusion coefficient of $D_{cl} = 1.5 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, surface chloride $C_s = 0.60\%$ (by wt. of cement), initial chloride $C_i = 0.05\%$, and reinforcement cover of **75 mm**. Estimate the chloride concentration at the rebar surface after **20 years**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe in detail the methods of protection of steel reinforcement in marine concrete (cover, coatings, inhibitors, cathodic protection, FRP rebars). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Fick's 2nd Law Application (7 marks):**

Fick's 2nd Law in semi-infinite media, for constant surface concentration $C_s$:

$$
C_{x,t} = C_s - (C_s - C_i) \cdot \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{D_{cl}\cdot t}}\right)
$$

**Given:**
- $D_{cl} = 1.5 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- $C_s = 0.60\%$, $C_i = 0.05\%$
- $x = 75\;\text{mm} = 0.075\;\text{m}$
- $t = 20\;\text{yr} = 20 \times 365.25 \times 86400 = 6.31 \times 10^{8}\;\text{s}$

**Step 1 — Compute the argument of erf:**

$$
2\sqrt{D_{cl} \cdot t} = 2 \sqrt{1.5 \times 10^{-12} \times 6.31 \times 10^{8}}
$$
$$
= 2 \sqrt{9.465 \times 10^{-4}} = 2 \times 0.03077 = 0.06154\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Argument of error function:**

$$
\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{D_{cl} \cdot t}} = \dfrac{0.075}{0.06154} = 1.2187
$$

**Step 3 — From standard erf table / approximation (Taylor series or numerical lookup):**

$$
\text{erf}(1.2187) \approx 0.9127
$$

(Interp: erf(1.20) = 0.9103, erf(1.25) = 0.9229 → linear interpolation = 0.9103 + 0.187×(0.9229−0.9103) = 0.9103 + 0.0024 = 0.9127 ✓)

**Step 4 — Compute $C_{x,t}$:**

$$
C_{0.075, 20\text{yr}} = 0.60 - (0.60 - 0.05) \times 0.9127
$$
$$
= 0.60 - 0.55 \times 0.9127
$$
$$
= 0.60 - 0.5020
$$
$$
\boxed{\,C_{x,t} = 0.098\% \text{ by weight of cement}\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** The chloride level at 75 mm cover after 20 years is approximately **0.10%**, which is **below the threshold** of 0.15–0.20% typically required to initiate rebar corrosion. Hence, the design is **safe** for a 20-year service life. Beyond 20 years, the design should be re-evaluated.

**(b) Rebar Protection Methods (7 marks):**

1. **Adequate Cover (50–100 mm)** — physical barrier; IS 456 mandates 75 mm in splash.
2. **Low w/c + SCMs** — densifies matrix, reduces Cl⁻ diffusion.
3. **Epoxy-Coated Rebars (ECR)** — fusion-bonded epoxy (ASTM A775); protection lost if cut/damaged.
4. **Galvanized Rebars** — zinc coating, sacrificial protection.
5. **Stainless Steel Cladding / Solid SS Rebars** — e.g., 316L; expensive but 100+ year life.
6. **Fibre-Reinforced Polymer (FRP) Rebars** — GFRP, CFRP; non-corroding, but brittle.
7. **Corrosion Inhibitors** — calcium nitrite admixture; delays initiation.
8. **Cathodic Protection (CP)** — impressed current or sacrificial anode (zinc, Al-Zn-In).
9. **Surface Sealers & Coatings** — silane/siloxane, epoxy-polyurethane top coats.
10. **Use of corrosion-resistant steel fibres** in the cover zone (SFRC).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Splash and Atmospheric zones** — splash is the MOST severe; students often incorrectly mark atmospheric as worst. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting units in Fick's Law numericals** — $D_{cl}$ is in **m²/s**, time in **seconds** (NOT years). Converting 20 years to seconds is mandatory. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 3. **Abrams' Law misuse** — the formula $f_c = A/B^{w/c}$ is empirical; students must use **calibrated** A, B values, not arbitrary. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Missing the `erf` function** — chloride diffusion without the error function gets **zero** in that part. Loss: **3–5 marks**.
> 5. **No mention of cover thickness** when asked about durability — examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for "minimum cover as per IS 456".
> 6. **Confusing w/c with w/b (water-binder ratio)** — in mixes with silica fume or GGBS, the denominator includes SCMs. Loss: **1 mark**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Marine Concrete**

- ☐ **Definition:** Specially engineered concrete resisting seawater aggression (chemical, physical, biological).
- ☐ **Four exposure zones:** Submerged < Tidal < Splash (most severe) < Atmospheric.
- ☐ **Cement choice:** SRPC (low C₃A < 5%), PPC, or blends with GGBS/silica fume.
- ☐ **Min. strength:** $f_{ck} \geq 40$ MPa; **Min. cement:** 400 kg/m³; **Max. w/c:** 0.40.
- ☐ **Cover:** 50 mm (atmospheric), 65 mm (tidal), **75–100 mm (splash)**, 65 mm (submerged).
- ☐ **Abrams' Law:** $f_c = A/B^{w/c}$ — exponential relationship.
- ☐ **Powers' Porosity:** $\phi_{cap} = 1 - 0.32/(w/c)$ — proves low w/c = low porosity.
- ☐ **Fick's 2nd Law:** $C_{x,t} = C_s - (C_s-C_i)\,\text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$ — chloride profile.
- ☐ **Admixtures:** Superplasticizers, silica fume (8%), fly ash (25%), GGBS (50%), Ca(NO₂)₂ inhibitor, AEA (4–6%).
- ☐ **Rebar protection:** Cover + ECR/SS/FRP + inhibitors + CP.
- ☐ **Key standards:** IS 456, IS 10262, IS 12330, ACI 357R, ACI 318, BS 6349, FIP Recommendations.
- ☐ **KTU-favoured numericals:** Abrams' calibration, Powers' porosity, Fick's diffusion with `erf`.
- ☐ **Application areas in shipbuilding:** Dry docks, slipways, caissons, jetties, quay walls, floating pontoons, PCV lighters.
- ☐ **Service life target:** 50–100 years with proper marine mix design.
- ☐ **Historical reference:** Mulberry Harbour Phoenix caissons (WWII) — marine concrete mass production.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: History of Concrete Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** History of Concrete Ships # MODULE 4 — HISTORY OF CONCRETE SHIPS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **concrete ship** is a watercraft whose hull is constructed primarily from **Portland cement concrete**, typically reinforced with **steel bars (rebar)** or **ferrocement** (a matrix of cement mortar reinforced with closely spaced wire mesh). The ship may also utilize **non-ferrous metallic inserts** (bronze, brass, or copper alloy) for fittings, valves, propeller shaft bearings, and seacocks where corrosion resistance to seawater is critical. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Concrete ships are marine vessels in which the principal structural hull material is reinforced or pre-stressed concrete, supplemented by carefully selected non-ferrous alloys for components exposed to galvanic corrosion in marine environments.* ### 1.2 Historical Classification of Concrete Marine Vessels | Class | Description | Period | |---|---|---| | **Ferro-cement boats** | Thin mortar shell over wire mesh; small craft only | 1848 – 1890s | | **Plain reinforced concrete ships** | Conventional rebar-reinforced hulls | 1900s – 1920s | | **Wartime emergency concrete ships** | Mass-produced for steel conservation | 1917 – 1919, 1942 – 1945 | | **Pre-stressed concrete hulls** | Post-tensioned tendons for tensile strength | 1950s onwards | | **Floating concrete structures** | Barges, docks, oil platforms | 1960s – present | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of a concrete ship like a floating eggshell — but the eggshell is reinforced with steel wire.** > > Just as a chicken's egg is incredibly strong when pressure is applied evenly across its curved surface, a concrete hull distributes hydrostatic pressure through its curved, monolithic geometry. However, like an egg, concrete is **strong in compression but weak in tension** — which is exactly why steel reinforcement (and later, pre-stressing tendons) was added. The "non-ferrous" angle in our KTU course refers to the bronze propellers, brass fittings, and copper-nickel piping that resist the highly corrosive seawater attacking a porous cement surface. ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Engineering Metrics - **Density of reinforced concrete:** $\rho_c \approx 2400 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (≈ **1.5× denser** than steel hulls at $\rho_s = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$) - **Compressive strength of structural marine concrete:** $f'_c \approx 30 \text{ to } 50 \text{ MPa}$ - **Modulus of elasticity:** $E_c \approx 25 \text{ to } 35 \text{ GPa}$ - **Standard seawater density:** $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - **Design life of a properly built concrete vessel:** 50 – 100 years > [!IMPORTANT] > The high density of concrete means a concrete ship must displace a *larger* volume of water than an equivalent steel ship to support the same cargo — but the **lower cost and abundant availability of cement and aggregate** historically made it a strategic substitute when steel was rationed. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Evolution of Concrete in Naval Architecture The development of concrete for shipbuilding progressed through **five distinct engineering phases**, each driven by a specific material limitation or wartime necessity: #### Phase 1 — The Lambot Ferro-Cement Innovation (1848) - **Who:** French gardener **Joseph-Louis Lambot** (1814–1897) - **What:** Built the world's first known concrete boat — a 4.2 m rowing punt reinforced with iron wire mesh. - **Why it matters:** Proved that *ferro-cement* (iron + cement) could be **waterproof** and **structurally viable** for marine use. - **The "Why" explained:** Lambot needed a boat that would not rot in the swampy vineyards of southern France. He used his own patented material called *"Ferciment"* — a mortar of sand, cement, and iron-wire mesh. #### Phase 2 — The European Theoretical Foundations (1896 – 1910) - **Carlo Gabellini (Italy, 1896):** Built a 3-ton concrete launch. - **G. A. Wayss (Germany, 1908):** Purchased Lambot's patent and popularized ferro-cement construction in continental Europe. - **Engineering insight:** Researchers discovered that **thin ferro-cement shells** behaved like **monocoque stressed-skin structures** — a radical departure from the heavy rib-and-plating system of steel shipbuilding. #### Phase 3 — Wartime Emergency Construction: World War I (1917 – 1919) - **Strategic context:** U-boat attacks had sunk ~25% of Allied merchant tonnage. **Steel was rationed** for guns, tanks, and submarines. - **United States Shipping Board (USSB) and British Emergency Fleet Corporation (EFC)** commissioned concrete ships as emergency cargo carriers. - **Key vessels built:** - **SS *Faith*** (1918, San Francisco) — the **first seagoing reinforced-concrete steamer** (3,200 GRT). - **SS *Palo Alto*** (1919, California) — 6,000-ton oil tanker, later converted into a dance hall/breakwater. - **Polias and Cretensis** (UK, 1919) — first British seagoing concrete steamers. #### Phase 4 — Inter-War Decline and Wartime Revival: World War II (1939 – 1945) - **Initial decline (1920s–1930s):** Concrete ships were declared uneconomical in peacetime. - **WWII revival:** - **United States (1942–1945):** Built **24 reinforced-concrete cargo ships and 80 concrete barges** at six shipyards. - **United Kingdom:** Constructed ~12 concrete vessels, including 10 lighters. - **Notorious design flaw:** Most WWII concrete ships suffered **severe cracking** due to *concrete shrinkage*, *thermal stress*, and *vibration fatigue* during trans-Atlantic crossings. - **The most famous survivor:** The **SS *Atlantus*** (1918), whose wreck still lies off Cape May, New Jersey. #### Phase 5 — Pre-Stressed Concrete and Modern Era (1950 – Present) - **Engineering breakthrough:** **Eugène Freyssinet's** post-tensioning system allowed concrete hulls to remain in *permanent compression*, eliminating tensile cracks. - **Modern applications:** Concrete **oil and gas platforms** (e.g., the **Ninian Central Platform**, 1978 — 600,000 t), floating docks, LNG terminals. - **Why concrete is making a comeback:** Superior fire resistance, **lower life-cycle cost**, and zero corrosion of the bulk hull. > [!NOTE] > **The "Non-Ferrous" Link in KTU Context:** The KTU course *PENST523* focuses on the **non-ferrous alloys** used in conjunction with concrete hulls — typically **aluminium-bronze** propellers, **copper-nickel (Cu-Ni 90/10 and 70/30)** piping, and **brass** valve fittings. These materials are chosen because **seawater is an aggressive electrolyte** that would otherwise corrode both the steel rebar and any ferrous fittings embedded in the porous concrete. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relationship | Physical Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V_{disp} = \dfrac{m_{ship}}{\rho_{sw}}$ | Displaced volume needed for buoyancy (Archimedes) | m³ | | 2 | $f'_c = 0.8 \cdot f_{cu}$ | Cylinder compressive strength from cube test | MPa | | 3 | $E_c = 4700 \sqrt{f'_c}$ | ACI code modulus of concrete | MPa | | 4 | $\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{f'_c}{\gamma_c \cdot \gamma_m}$ | Allowable stress with safety factors | MPa | | 5 | $F_{buoy} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$ | Buoyancy force (Archimedes' principle) | N | | 6 | $\Delta_{steel} \approx 1.6 \cdot \Delta_{conc}$ | Concrete hull weight is ~60% greater than steel for same displacement | t | | 7 | $D / t = \dfrac{p \cdot r}{\sigma_{allow}}$ | Thin-wall pressure hull hoop stress ratio | dimensionless | | 8 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2 \cdot E_c \cdot t^2}{r \cdot \sqrt{3(1 - \nu^2)}}$ | Critical buckling pressure of cylindrical concrete hull | Pa | | 9 | $\sigma_{tendon} = \dfrac{P_{jacking}}{A_{tendon}}$ | Prestress in post-tensioned tendons | MPa | | 10 | $\tau_{bond} = \dfrac{V}{u \cdot d}$ | Bond stress between rebar and concrete | MPa | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Board Exam Pitfall:** The *vertical bar* symbol `|` is **forbidden** inside KTU answer-sheet markdown tables. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. Always write ratios as $\dfrac{a}{b}$ or $a \mid b$ when needed. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Strategic reserve capacity** — Nations can maintain shipbuilding capability even when steel imports are embargoed. 2. **Fire-resistant naval bases** — Modern submarine pens are made of pre-stressed concrete (e.g., **Brest, Saint-Nazaire**). 3. **Offshore oil & gas** — The **Hibernia platform** (Canada) and **Troll A platform** (Norway) used pre-stressed concrete. 4. **Maritime durability** — Properly built concrete barges still operate **70+ years** after construction. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Buoyancy vs. Weight of a Concrete Hull **Problem Statement:** A reinforced-concrete ship has a hull mass of **4,800 tonnes**. The average hull density is $\rho_c = 2400$ kg/m³. Seawater density is $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³. Determine the **minimum displaced volume** needed to keep the vessel afloat and the **buoyancy reserve factor**. #### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify the physical principle.** The vessel floats when buoyant force equals weight: $$ F_{buoy} = W_{ship} $$ $$ \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{disp} = m_{ship} \cdot g $$ **Step 2 — Cancel gravitational acceleration** $g$ on both sides: $$ \rho_{sw} \cdot V_{disp} = m_{ship} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute the given values.** Note that **1 tonne = 1000 kg**, so $m_{ship} = 4{,}800 \times 10^3$ kg. $$ 1025 \cdot V_{disp} = 4{,}800 \times 10^3 $$ **Step 4 — Solve for displaced volume:** $$ V_{disp} = \dfrac{4{,}800 \times 10^3}{1025} $$ $$ V_{disp} = 4682.93 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 5 — Compute the hull's own volume** (for context, not buoyancy): $$ V_{hull} = \dfrac{m_{ship}}{\rho_c} = \dfrac{4{,}800 \times 10^3}{2400} = 2000 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 6 — Calculate the buoyancy reserve factor (BRF):** $$ \text{BRF} = \dfrac{V_{disp}}{V_{hull}} = \dfrac{4682.93}{2000} = 2.34 $$ **Interpretation:** The concrete hull must displace **2.34× its own geometric volume** in seawater — i.e., the ship must be **hollow enough** that the integrated volume of the underwater shape (including internal voids) gives that displacement. > [!NOTE] > **Insight for KTU exam:** A high BRF means a large underwater hull volume, which translates into a **shorter, fatter** hull form compared to steel ships — this is a defining characteristic visible in photographs of historical concrete ships like the *SS Atlantus*. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hoop Stress in a Cylindrical Concrete Pressure Hull **Problem Statement:** A cylindrical concrete pressure hull of **radius** $r = 2.5$ m and **wall thickness** $t = 0.20$ m is submerged to a depth of **30 m**. Compute the hoop stress $\sigma_h$ in the hull. Take $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s². #### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Calculate hydrostatic pressure** at depth $h$: $$ p = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h $$ **Step 2 — Substitute values:** $$ p = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 30 $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate:** $$ p = 301{,}657.5 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.302 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 4 — Apply the thin-wall cylinder formula** for hoop stress: $$ \sigma_h = \dfrac{p \cdot r}{t} $$ **Step 5 — Substitute the known quantities:** $$ \sigma_h = \dfrac{0.302 \times 10^6 \times 2.5}{0.20} $$ **Step 6 — Evaluate:** $$ \sigma_h = 3.775 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 7 — Compare with allowable compressive stress** of marine concrete ($f'_c = 35$ MPa): $$ \dfrac{\sigma_h}{f'_c} = \dfrac{3.775}{35} = 0.108 = 10.8\% $$ **Interpretation:** Hoop compressive stress uses only **~10.8% of the concrete's strength capacity**, so the cylindrical concrete pressure hull is **comfortably safe** at this depth. This is *why* concrete was attractive for **submarine hulls and undersea oil storage tanks** — the material is naturally suited to compressive loading. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** The *limiting factor* in concrete pressure hulls is **buckling**, not material crushing. Use the formula $P_{cr}$ from the cheat sheet to verify stability. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Concrete and Steel Hulls | Property | Reinforced Concrete | Mild Steel | Engineering Implication | |---|---|---|---| | Density (kg/m³) | **2400** | **7850** | Concrete hulls ~3.3× heavier for equal thickness | | Yield/Compressive strength (MPa) | **30 – 50** | **250** (yield) | Steel is 5–8× stronger in pure strength | | Modulus of elasticity (GPa) | **30** | **200** | Steel is 6.7× stiffer; concrete flexes more | | Corrosion in seawater | **Excellent** (passive matrix) | **Poor** (rusts) | Concrete hull needs **no painting** | | Fire resistance | **Excellent** (4+ hrs) | **Poor** (loses strength at 550 °C) | Concrete preferred for military vessels | | Cost (per kg, historical) | **Lower** | **Higher (volatile)** | Concrete is attractive during steel shortages | | Repair difficulty | **High** (specialist work) | **Moderate** | Steel easier to patch in remote ports | | Embodied energy | **Low** | **High** | Concrete is more sustainable | | Magnetism | **Non-magnetic** | **Ferromagnetic** | Concrete hulls are **stealth-friendly** for mines | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Timeline — Evolution of Concrete Ships ```mermaid flowchart LR A["1848 LAMBOT PUNT
First ferro-cement boat
France"] B["1896 GABELLINI LAUNCH
3 ton Italian prototype"] C["1910 WAYSS PATENT
Ferro-cement popularised"] D["1918 SS FAITH USA
First seagoing concrete steamer"] E["1919 SS PALO ALTO USA
6000 ton oil tanker"] F["1942 US WWII PROGRAM
24 ships plus 80 barges"] G["1950s PRESTRESSED HULLS
Freyssinet post-tensioning"] H["1978 NINIAN PLATFORM UK
600000 t concrete"] I["2020s MODERN APPLICATIONS
LNG terminals and floating docks"] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G G --> H H --> I ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hull Material Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph OuterLayer["OUTER HULL SURFACE"] Paint["Anti-fouling epoxy paint"] Cover["40 mm concrete cover zone"] end subgraph Reinforcement["REINFORCEMENT SYSTEM"] Rebar["Steel rebar grid 16 mm at 150 mm"] Mesh["Welded wire mesh 6 mm"] Tendons["Pre-stressing tendons if applicable"] end subgraph CoreStructure["STRUCTURAL CONCRETE CORE"] Matrix["Portland cement plus aggregate"] Admixture["Silica fume and corrosion inhibitor"] end subgraph NonFerrous["NON FERROUS FITTINGS"] Prop["Aluminium-bronze propeller"] Valves["Brass seacocks and valves"] Piping["Copper-nickel 90/10 piping"] end Paint --> Cover Cover --> Rebar Rebar --> Mesh Mesh --> Tendons Tendons --> Matrix Matrix --> Admixture Admixture --> Prop Admixture --> Valves Admixture --> Piping ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this diagram is in a Non-Ferrous Materials course:** The outer layers are ferrous/concrete, but every component **in contact with seawater and dissimilar metals** must be **non-ferrous** to avoid galvanic corrosion between the steel rebar (cathode) and any ferrous fittings (anode). Bronze, brass, and Cu-Ni alloys are selected to match the corrosion potential of the rebar-cement matrix. ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Functions in Ship Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Buoyancy["BUOYANCY CONTROL"] HullForm["Hull geometry"] Compartments["Watertight bulkheads"] end subgraph Strength["STRUCTURAL STRENGTH"] Concrete["Concrete compressive shell"] RebarA["Rebar tensile layer"] end subgraph Propulsion["PROPULSION AND FITTINGS"] Shaft["Stainless or monel shaft"] BronzeProp["Bronze propeller"] end subgraph Service["SERVICE SYSTEMS"] CuNiPipe["Cu-Ni 90/10 piping"] BrassValve["Brass valves"] end HullForm --> Concrete Compartments --> Concrete Concrete --> RebarA RebarA --> Shaft Shaft --> BronzeProp Concrete --> CuNiPipe CuNiPipe --> BrassValve ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024, Module 4]** > *Define the term "ferro-cement" and state its relevance to the earliest concrete boats.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Ferro-cement is a composite material formed by a **cement-sand mortar** matrix reinforced with **closely spaced layers of wire mesh** and possibly small-diameter steel bars. - **Origin (1 mark):** Invented by **Joseph-Louis Lambot** in 1848 (France), who built the first known concrete boat (a 4.2 m punt) using this technique. - **Relevance to KTU syllabus (1 mark):** Ferro-cement is the **proto-typical non-ferrous-friendly** concrete marine material because the thin wire mesh permits the use of **non-ferrous inserts** (e.g., bronze, brass) without the galvanic incompatibility encountered in heavy rebar construction. > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2023, Module 4]** > *Mention any two advantages and one disadvantage of concrete ship construction.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Advantage 1 (1 mark):** **Steel conservation** — concrete was used as a strategic substitute during the World Wars when steel was rationed. - **Advantage 2 (1 mark):** **Superior fire resistance and corrosion immunity** — concrete hulls do not rust and can survive temperatures above 1000 °C. - **Disadvantage (1 mark):** **Hull is ~60% heavier** than an equivalent steel hull, reducing cargo payload and increasing draft. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice) > **Q3. (A) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Choice A]** > *With neat diagrams, describe the historical evolution of concrete ships from Lambot's punt to the modern pre-stressed concrete oil platforms. Highlight the engineering reasons for the rise and fall of concrete ship construction.* **Model Answer (14 marks):** **(a) Early Innovations (7 marks)** - **1848 — Lambot's punt:** French gardener J.-L. Lambot built a 4.2 m rowing boat using his patented *Ferciment* — a sand-cement mortar reinforced with iron wire mesh. This was the **first ferro-cement boat** in history and demonstrated that concrete could be made **waterproof** and **structurally adequate** for marine use. *(2 marks)* - **1896 — Gabellini's launch:** Italian engineer Carlo Gabellini built a 3-ton concrete launch in Rome, proving that **reinforced concrete** (RCC) with steel bars could displace small boat steel construction. He embedded a continuous reinforcement cage inside the concrete shell. *(2 marks)* - **1908 — Wayss acquires the patent:** German engineer G. A. Wayss purchased Lambot's patent and **industrialised ferro-cement construction** in Europe, leading to widespread use in barges and pontoons. *(1 mark)* - **Engineering reason for the rise:** Concrete was **cheap, abundant, and immune to rot** — making it attractive for small inland vessels in an era when steel was expensive. *(1 mark)* - **Diagram (sketch only — describe in writing):** A longitudinal section of Lambot's punt showing the iron mesh embedded in cement mortar. *(1 mark)* **(b) Wartime Emergency Construction and Modern Pre-stressed Era (7 marks)** - **WWI boom (1917–1919):** German U-boat campaigns created an Allied shipping crisis. The **US Shipping Board** and **British Emergency Fleet Corporation** commissioned concrete ships to conserve steel. Key vessels: - **SS *Faith* (1918)** — first seagoing reinforced concrete steamer (3,200 GRT). - **SS *Palo Alto* (1919)** — 6,000-ton oil tanker, later repurposed as a floating breakwater at Aptos, California. *(2 marks)* - **WWII revival (1942–1945):** The United States built **24 concrete cargo ships and 80 concrete barges** at six shipyards. However, many suffered **severe cracking** from concrete shrinkage, thermal cycling, and vibration fatigue. *(1 mark)* - **Decline in peacetime:** Concrete ships were **20–30% more expensive** than steel ships when steel was freely available. Their heavy weight (≈2400 kg/m³) reduced cargo capacity. *(1 mark)* - **Modern pre-stressed concrete era (1950s–today):** Eugène Freyssinet's **post-tensioning system** revolutionised concrete hulls by placing them in *permanent compression*, eliminating tensile cracks. This led to: - Concrete **oil platforms** (Ninian Central, 1978, 600,000 t). - **LNG terminals** in offshore fields. - Modern **submarine pens** (e.g., Brest, Saint-Nazaire) for naval bases. *(2 marks)* - **Engineering reason for the fall and rise:** The *fall* was due to cost and weight penalties when steel was available. The *rise* came when pre-stressed concrete solved the cracking problem, and modern applications demand **fire resistance, non-magnetic hulls (for mines), and lower life-cycle cost**. *(1 mark)* --- > **Q3. (B) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Choice B]** > *Explain the role of non-ferrous materials in concrete ship construction. Discuss at least three non-ferrous alloys commonly used and justify their selection over ferrous alternatives.* **Model Answer (14 marks):** **(a) The Corrosion Problem in Concrete Hulls (7 marks)** - Concrete hulls contain **steel rebar** (ferrous), cement mortar matrix, and seawater on the outside. When chlorides from seawater penetrate the concrete cover, the rebar begins to corrode, expanding and **spalling** the surrounding concrete. *(2 marks)* - To preserve the rebar, **all embedded fittings** (valves, propeller shafts, sea chests) must be made of **non-ferrous alloys** whose corrosion potential matches the rebar-cement system — preventing galvanic micro-cells from forming. *(2 marks)* - The cement matrix itself, being porous, allows ion exchange. Using **non-ferrous metals** like Cu-Ni avoids the formation of iron oxides that would otherwise crack the hull. *(1 mark)* - A second engineering argument: **anti-magnetic stealth** — non-ferrous components in the hull help reduce the **magnetic signature** that triggers naval mines. *(1 mark)* - **Diagram/Description (1 mark):** Sketch a galvanic corrosion cell showing the rebar (anode), concrete pore water (electrolyte), and a non-ferrous insert (cathode) — explain why this configuration is acceptable when the insert is Cu-Ni or bronze. **(b) Three Common Non-Ferrous Alloys (7 marks)** 1. **Aluminium Bronze (Cu-Al-Fe-Ni)** — used for **propellers and propeller shafts**: - **Justification:** Excellent **cavitation resistance** in seawater, high strength (~700 MPa UTS), and a corrosion rate of <0.025 mm/yr in seawater. The passivating **Al₂O₃** layer resists chloride attack. *(2 marks)* 2. **Copper-Nickel 90/10 (Cu-Ni 90/10) and 70/30** — used for **seawater piping and heat exchangers**: - **Justification:** Outstanding **biofouling resistance** (the copper ions discourage marine growth), low corrosion rate (~0.02 mm/yr), and excellent resistance to **impingement attack** at high flow velocities. *(2 marks)* 3. **Naval Brass (Cu-Zn-Sn)** and **Tin Bronze** — used for **valves, seacocks, and pump bodies**: - **Justification:** **Anti-galling** behaviour in mating surfaces, good machinability, and the tin content improves resistance to **dezincification** in marine environments. *(2 marks)* - **Synthesis (1 mark):** These three non-ferrous alloy families collectively protect concrete hulls from internal corrosion, ensure long service life, and align with the **KTU 2024 syllabus focus on non-ferrous marine materials**. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on "History of Concrete Ships":** > 1. **Forgetting the year 1848** — Lambot's punt is the *de facto* starting point; omitting it loses **at least 1 mark** in any long-answer. > 2. **Confusing "ferro-cement" with "reinforced concrete."** Ferro-cement uses **wire mesh**; reinforced concrete uses **rebar**. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this mix-up. > 3. **Ignoring the WWII ships** — the 24 US-built concrete ships and 80 barges are a **guaranteed question** in any Module 4 paper. > 4. **Not linking concrete ships to non-ferrous materials** — this is a *non-ferrous* course. Always mention **bronze propellers, Cu-Ni piping, and brass fittings** at least once. > 5. **Missing the displacement math** — examiners expect students to know that a concrete hull is **~60% heavier** than a steel hull of equal cargo capacity. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **1848 — Lambot builds the first ferro-cement punt** in southern France. - **1918 — SS *Faith*** is the first seagoing concrete steamer in the US. - **1919 — SS *Palo Alto*** is a 6,000-ton concrete oil tanker in California. - **1942–1945 — WWII US program** builds 24 concrete cargo ships and 80 concrete barges. - Concrete hulls are **~60% heavier** than steel hulls of equal strength. - **Reinforced concrete** = steel rebar + cement mortar. - **Ferro-cement** = closely spaced wire mesh + thin cement mortar shell. - **Pre-stressed concrete** = post-tensioned tendons, no tensile cracks. - Concrete density $\rho_c = 2400$ kg/m³ vs. steel $\rho_s = 7850$ kg/m³. - **Non-ferrous components** (Cu-Ni 90/10, aluminium bronze, naval brass) are mandatory in concrete hulls to prevent **galvanic corrosion** of the rebar. - Concrete hulls offer **fire resistance, corrosion immunity, non-magnetism (anti-mine), and lower life-cycle cost** — the four "killer" advantages in modern naval applications. - Famous surviving wreck: **SS *Atlantus*** lies off Cape May, NJ, USA.

topics.title: Prestressed Concrete Vessels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Prestressed Concrete Vessels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 4: PRESTRESSED CONCRETE VESSELS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Prestressed Concrete Vessel (PCV)** is a marine structural unit — such as a barge, pontoon, floating dry-dock, submersible, or liquid/gas carrier — whose hull and primary load-bearing shell are constructed from high-strength concrete that has been pre-compressed by tensioned high-tensile steel tendons (wires, strands, or bars). The deliberate pre-compression counteracts the tensile stresses generated under hydrostatic, hydrodynamic, cargo, and self-weight service loads, keeping the concrete section in a state of net compression under all working conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Statement (verbatim style):**
> "A prestressed concrete vessel is a monolithic, watertight marine structure in which internal tensile stresses induced by fluid pressure, bending, and external marine loading are neutralised by a system of permanent, pre-induced compressive stresses introduced through tensioned steel reinforcement."

The two principal methods recognised by FIP (Fédération Internationale du Béton) and adopted in KTU naval-architecture modules are:

| Method | Procedure | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Pre-tensioning** | Tendons are tensioned *before* concrete is cast; bond is established after curing | Mass-produced barges, pontoon sections |
| **Post-tensioning** | Concrete is cast with ducts; tendons are tensioned *after* curing, then anchored | Large ships, LNG tanks, floating dry-docks |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Squeezed Barrel"

Imagine trying to pull apart the two ends of a wooden barrel made of separate staves. The staves will simply fall apart because wood is weak in tension. Now, **before** filling the barrel, imagine a worker wrapping tight steel hoops around the outside, squeezing every stave inward. When water pressure pushes the staves outward, the inward squeeze of the hoops must first be overcome *before* the staves can separate.

This is exactly how a prestressed concrete vessel works:

- The **concrete** = the wooden staves (strong in compression, weak in tension).
- The **internal water/cargo pressure** = tries to pull the staves apart (induces tension).
- The **tensioned steel tendons** = the steel hoops (squeeze the concrete inward, pre-loading it in compression).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The concrete is never allowed to experience tension under service loads — it remains in compression. The steel tendons absorb all tensile forces, behaving like the "skeleton in armour" of the vessel.

### 1.3 Historical & Materials Context (Why "Non-Ferrous" Framing?)

Although the *reinforcement* in a PCV is ferrous steel, the **bulk structural mass (≈ 95 % by volume)** is concrete — an aggregate-cement composite. The course framing emphasises:

- **Cementitious matrix** (non-metallic binder).
- **Non-ferrous aggregate alternatives** such as lightweight pumice, laterite, slag, fly-ash cenosphere, and bauxite aggregates.
- **Historical impetus:** Steel shortages during WWI and WWII forced shipbuilders to use concrete (e.g., the 1917 *SS Faith*, the WWII *Liberty ships' concrete escort carriers*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material Efficiency Metric for KTU:**
> Concrete has a **density of ≈ 24 kN/m³** (≈ 2.4 t/m³) — only marginally heavier than water (**ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³ = 9.81 kN/m³**). For a sealed vessel, this means the cargo capacity is preserved while structural cost drops dramatically compared to a steel hull (ρ_steel ≈ 7850 kg/m³).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Philosophy — The Three Design States

A KTU-board examiner expects you to distinguish three states of stress that govern PCV design:

1. **Unstressed State (Before Prestressing):** Concrete carries only self-weight; tensile strength is near-zero (≈ 0.1 f_ck).
2. **Prestressed State (After Locking, No External Load):** Concrete is in pure circumferential and longitudinal compression.
3. **Service State (Under Working Pressure + Prestress):** The net tensile stress in concrete at the most critical fibre must remain **≤ 0** (no-tension serviceability) or **≤ 0.45 √f_ck** for limited-crack designs (per IS 1343 / ACI 318).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Pressure Containment

Let us analyse why a thin cylindrical shell under internal pressure is the controlling geometry for a liquid cargo (oil, LNG, water ballast) vessel.

- **Step 1 — Hoop Force Calculation:** Internal pressure $p$ acting on a diameter $D$ creates a circumferential (hoop) tension per unit length.
- **Step 2 — Longitudinal Force:** End-caps transmit pressure creating a longitudinal compression of half the magnitude.
- **Step 3 — Concrete Receives Both Forces:** Without prestressing, the hoop tension would crack the concrete within minutes.
- **Step 4 — Tendons Apply Opposing Compression:** The high-tensile steel wires, tensioned and anchored, induce an equal and opposite compressive hoop stress.
- **Step 5 — Equilibrium:** Net hoop stress = (Tensile from pressure) − (Compressive from prestress) ≈ 0 (design optimum).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Equations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table — it covers ≈ 70 % of KTU ESE numericals on PCV.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Applicability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Hoop stress in thin cylinder | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p D}{2 t}$ | $p$ in N/mm², $D$ in mm, $t$ in mm | Thin-walled cylindrical shell |
| 2 | Longitudinal stress | $\sigma_L = \dfrac{p D}{4 t}$ | Same as above | Cylindrical pressure vessel |
| 3 | Hoop force per unit length | $T_h = p \cdot r = \dfrac{p D}{2}$ | $T_h$ in N/mm, $r$ = radius | Force to be resisted by tendons |
| 4 | Required circumferential prestressing force | $P_0 = \dfrac{T_h}{\eta_{tendons}}$ | $P_0$ in N, $\eta$ = tendon efficiency (0.85–0.95) | After losses |
| 5 | Effective prestress after losses | $P_e = P_0 \,(1 - \text{losses})$ | Losses ≈ 15–25 % of $P_0$ | Long-term design |
| 6 | Equivalent concrete compressive stress | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{P_e}{A_c}$ | $A_c$ = concrete cross-section area | Service state check |
| 7 | Bursting pressure (thin cylinder) | $p_{burst} = \dfrac{2 \, f_{ck} \, t}{D}$ | $f_{ck}$ = concrete characteristic strength | Ultimate limit state |
| 8 | Required tendon area | $A_{st} = \dfrac{P_e}{0.87 \, f_y}$ | $f_y$ = tendon yield strength | ULS design |
| 9 | Minimum concrete grade (KTU) | $f_{ck} \geq 40$ N/mm² | M40 minimum for PCV | As per IS 1343 |
| 10 | Modulus ratio (transformed section) | $m = \dfrac{E_s}{E_c}$ | $E_s$ ≈ 200 GPa, $E_c$ ≈ 30 GPa | Stress check in service |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The vertical bar symbol `\|` (used for absolute value) is intentionally avoided in the table above — it is rendered as `\vert` or by enclosing in parentheses to keep the markdown parser intact.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The mathematics of PCV is not academic. It directly governs:

- **LNG Carriers (Moss-Rosenberg design):** The world's only commercial fleet of spherical concrete LNG tanks used prestressed concrete for cryogenic containment at **−163 °C**.
- **Floating Dry-Docks:** Indian Navy's *Pipavav* dry-dock uses post-tensioned concrete for the pontoon.
- **Submarine Pressure Hulls:** Proposed design for deep-submergence vessels using radial prestressing.
- **Immersed Tube Tunnels:** Prestressed concrete elements lowered into sea-beds (e.g., Hong Kong-Zhuhai tunnel).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hoop Stress in a Thin-Walled Cylindrical PCV

**Starting Principle:** Consider a half-cylinder of diameter $D$ and wall thickness $t$ subjected to internal pressure $p$. The pressure acts on the projected area and must be resisted by the hoop stress $\sigma_h$ developed in the two cut edges of the shell.

**Step 1 — Identify the Free Body**

Cut the cylinder longitudinally along its length $L$. The two cut faces are each of area $A = t \times L$.

**Step 2 — Identify the Projected Area on Which Pressure Acts**

The pressure $p$ (N/mm²) pushes outward against the projected rectangle of width $D$ and length $L$. Force exerted by pressure:

$$F_{pressure} = p \cdot D \cdot L$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Resisting Force from Hoop Stress**

The hoop stress $\sigma_h$ acts over the two cut faces (one on each side of the cut). Each face has area $t \cdot L$, so the total resisting area is $2 \cdot t \cdot L$. Resisting force:

$$F_{resist} = \sigma_h \cdot (2 \cdot t \cdot L)$$

**Step 4 — Apply Static Equilibrium**

For a quasi-static fluid (no acceleration), the net force must be zero:

$$\sigma_h \cdot (2 \cdot t \cdot L) = p \cdot D \cdot L$$

**Step 5 — Simplify**

Cancel $L$ from both sides (since the result is independent of length):

$$\sigma_h \cdot 2 \cdot t = p \cdot D$$

Rearranging to isolate $\sigma_h$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}}$$

**Verification by dimensional analysis:** $p$ has units N/mm², $D/t$ is dimensionless, so $\sigma_h$ has units N/mm². ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Required Prestressing Force for Hoop Equilibrium

**Given:**
- Vessel internal radius $r = D/2$
- Internal pressure $p$
- Wall thickness $t$
- Tendon efficiency $\eta$ (typically 0.85 for friction + anchorage losses)

**Step 1 — Calculate Hoop Tension per Unit Length**

The hoop force (force per unit axial length) that tries to split the cylinder apart:

$$T_h = p \cdot r = \frac{p \cdot D}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Account for Tendon Layout**

If $n$ tendons are spaced uniformly around the circumference, each tendon applies a force $F$ per unit length. The total circumferential compression induced is $n \cdot F$ per unit length.

**Step 3 — Equilibrium of Tendons with Pressure**

For the concrete to remain in **zero net hoop stress** (ideal service state):

$$n \cdot F = T_h$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the Prestressing Force per Tendon**

If a single tendon wraps the full circumference, the tendon force $P$ required is:

$$P = \frac{T_h \cdot \pi \cdot D}{\eta}$$

where $\eta$ accounts for losses during locking.

**Step 5 — Cross-Sectional Area of Each Tendon**

Using the design strength of high-tensile steel ($0.87 \, f_y$ is the permissible stress per IS 1343):

$$A_{tend} = \frac{P}{0.87 \cdot f_y}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Numerical Trick:** Examiners often ask you to verify whether a *given* tendon area is sufficient. Always compute the available $P$ and compare with the *required* $P$ above.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for PCV Design

The following aligned block represents the KTU-recommended decision flow that an examiner expects in a 14-mark design question:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{GIVEN} &: \; p, \; D, \; L, \; f_{ck}, \; f_y \\
\textbf{STEP 1} &: \; \text{Compute } \sigma_h = \frac{pD}{2t} \\
\textbf{STEP 2} &: \; \text{Compute hoop force per unit length } T_h = \frac{pD}{2} \\
\textbf{STEP 3} &: \; \text{Compute required prestress } P_0 = \frac{T_h \cdot \pi D}{\eta} \\
\textbf{STEP 4} &: \; \text{Compute tendon area } A_{st} = \frac{P_0}{0.87 \cdot f_y} \\
\textbf{STEP 5} &: \; \text{Verify: } \sigma_{c,actual} = \frac{P_0}{A_c} \geq \sigma_h \\
\textbf{STEP 6} &: \; \text{Add safety factor of 1.5 for ULS}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> A cylindrical PCV has internal diameter $D = 2.5$ m, wall thickness $t = 150$ mm, and is designed to carry a fluid at internal gauge pressure $p = 1.2$ N/mm². Concrete grade is **M40** ($f_{ck} = 40$ N/mm²) and high-tensile tendons have $f_y = 1500$ N/mm². Tendon efficiency $\eta = 0.85$. Compute the hoop stress and the required prestressing force.

**Solution Walk-Through:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_h &= \frac{p \cdot D}{2 \cdot t} \\
&= \frac{1.2 \times 2500}{2 \times 150} \quad \text{(D in mm)} \\
&= \frac{3000}{300} \\
&= 10 \; \text{N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_h &= \frac{p \cdot D}{2} = \frac{1.2 \times 2500}{2} = 1500 \; \text{N/mm} \\
P_0 &= \frac{T_h \cdot \pi \cdot D}{\eta} = \frac{1500 \times \pi \times 2500}{0.85} \\
&= \frac{11{,}780{,}972}{0.85} \\
&\approx 13{,}860{,}000 \; \text{N} = 13.86 \; \text{MN}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{st} &= \frac{P_0}{0.87 \cdot f_y} = \frac{13.86 \times 10^6}{0.87 \times 1500} \\
&= \frac{13.86 \times 10^6}{1305} \\
&\approx 10{,}620 \; \text{mm}^2 \;\;(\approx 10.62 \; \text{cm}^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The concrete alone would experience **10 N/mm²** of hoop tension — far above its tensile strength (≈ 0.35 × √40 ≈ 2.2 N/mm²). Therefore, **prestressing is mandatory** to keep the section crack-free in service.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Prestressed Concrete Vessel (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the structural logic of a PCV — the load flow, the prestress distribution, and the failure-mode monitoring — in the safe-syntax form required for KTU digital submissions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Internal Fluid Pressure P] --> B[Hoop Tension in Concrete Shell]
    A --> C[Longitudinal Compression]
    D[External Marine Loads] --> E[Bending Stress in Hull]
    F[Self Weight of Concrete] --> E
    B --> G{Net Tensile Stress Check}
    C --> G
    E --> G
    H[Post Tensioned Steel Tendons] --> I[Induced Circumferential Compression]
    I --> G
    G -->|Net tensile stress 0| J[Service Safe - No Cracking]
    G -->|Net tensile stress greater than 0| K[Service Failure - Cracking Risk]
    L[Pre Tensioned Wires] --> I
    M[Anchorage Zones] --> I
    N[Concrete Matrix fck 40 MPa] --> I
    O[Non Ferrous Aggregates Pumice Slag] --> N
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The two input streams (pressure + marine loads) create tensile and bending demands. The tendon system (pre + post-tensioned) injects a counter-compression. The diamond decision node checks whether the net effect is safe. Non-ferrous aggregates (relevant to PENST523) are highlighted as they influence $f_{ck}$ and $E_c$.

---

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Layout (Mermaid Block Representation)

The figure below shows a transverse cross-section of a typical PCV. Node IDs are alphanumeric-prefixed per the safe-syntax rule.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Inner Concrete Liner 25 mm] --> A2[Main Concrete Shell 150 mm]
    A2 --> A3[Outer Concrete Cover 50 mm]
    A4[Hoop Tendons at 100 mm Pitch] --- A2
    A5[Longitudinal Tendons at 300 mm Pitch] --- A2
    A6[High Tensile Wire Wrapping] --- A4
    A7[Anchorage Blisters at Quarter Points] --- A6
    A8[Non Ferrous Aggregate Core] --- A2
    A9[Bituminous Waterproofing] --> A3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Visualisation:** The above is a *functional block representation* of the cross-section, not a literal scaled drawing. In a KTU answer script, you would typically accompany this with a hand-drawn sketch (circumferential view) showing tendon paths, anchorage blisters, and concrete cover zones.

---

## 4.3 Construction Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Stage 1: Prepare Non Ferrous Aggregate Mix] --> B2[Stage 2: Cast Inner Liner]
    B2 --> B3[Stage 3: Place Ducts for Tendons]
    B3 --> B4[Stage 4: Cast Main Shell Concrete]
    B4 --> B5[Stage 5: Steam Cure to 80 Percent fck]
    B5 --> B6[Stage 6: Thread and Tension Tendons]
    B6 --> B7[Stage 7: Anchor and Grout Ducts]
    B7 --> B8[Stage 8: Apply Waterproofing Membrane]
    B8 --> B9[Stage 9: Hydrostatic Test at 1.5 Times Working Pressure]
    B9 --> B10[Stage 10: Launch and Fit Out]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

### Q1. Define a prestressed concrete vessel. List any two advantages over a steel vessel.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *prestressed concrete vessel* is a marine structure whose hull is built from high-strength concrete pre-compressed by tensioned steel tendons, so that the concrete remains in compression under all service loads. (2 marks)

**Two advantages:** (1 mark)
1. **Corrosion resistance** — the outer concrete cover protects internal tendons; minimal maintenance cost over a 50-year design life.
2. **Lower material cost** — concrete + tendons are cheaper than equivalent tonnage of marine-grade steel, especially when steel is imported.
3. *Bonus acceptable:* Better fire resistance, reduced magnetic signature (useful for naval vessels), higher damping of vibration.

---

### Q2. Distinguish between pre-tensioning and post-tensioning as applied to PCV construction.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Pre-tensioning | Post-tensioning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Tendon tensioned | *Before* concrete is cast | *After* concrete cures |
| Bond mechanism | Direct bond between tendon and concrete | Mechanical anchorages at ends |
| Typical use | Mass-produced small pontoons | Large barges, dry-docks, LNG tanks |
| Loss factors | Lower (≈ 10 %) | Higher (≈ 20 %) due to friction & anchorage slip |

(1.5 marks for the distinction table; 1.5 marks for the application examples)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper – Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Design-Oriented

**(a)** Derive the expression for hoop stress in a thin-walled cylindrical prestressed concrete vessel subjected to an internal pressure $p$. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical PCV has internal diameter $D = 3.0$ m, wall thickness $t = 200$ mm, and carries oil at internal gauge pressure $p = 0.8$ N/mm². Concrete grade is **M45** and high-tensile wire has $f_y = 1600$ N/mm² with tendon efficiency $\eta = 0.90$. Determine: (i) the hoop stress, (ii) the required circumferential prestressing force per metre length, and (iii) the cross-sectional area of steel tendons. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation** — *(Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation; key steps are):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Consider half cylinder of length } L &: \\
p \cdot D \cdot L &= 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L \\
\Rightarrow \sigma_h &= \frac{p D}{2 t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Assumptions** (1 mark each, any three):
1. Material is homogeneous and isotropic within the concrete shell.
2. Wall thickness $t \leq D/20$ (thin-walled approximation).
3. Plane sections remain plane after loading.
4. Internal pressure is uniform along the length.
5. Self-weight effects are neglected (or treated separately).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Hoop stress:**

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot D}{2 t} = \frac{0.8 \times 3000}{2 \times 200} = \frac{2400}{400} = 6.0 \; \text{N/mm}^2
$$

**(ii) Required prestressing force per metre length:**

$$
T_h = \frac{p \cdot D}{2} = \frac{0.8 \times 3000}{2} = 1200 \; \text{N/mm} = 1.2 \; \text{MN/m}
$$

Total prestressing force around the circumference:

$$
P_0 = \frac{T_h \cdot \pi \cdot D}{\eta} = \frac{1200 \times \pi \times 3000}{0.90} = 12{,}566{,}371 \; \text{N} \approx 12.57 \; \text{MN}
$$

**(iii) Tendon cross-sectional area:**

$$
A_{st} = \frac{P_0}{0.87 \cdot f_y} = \frac{12.57 \times 10^6}{0.87 \times 1600} = \frac{12.57 \times 10^6}{1392} \approx 9030 \; \text{mm}^2 \approx 90.3 \; \text{cm}^2
$$

**Conclusion:** Approximately **90 cm² of high-tensile steel** must be distributed around the 9.42 m circumference — equivalent to about **12 nos. of 12 mm diameter strands** or equivalent tendon arrangement.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Conceptual + Materials-Oriented

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the construction sequence of a post-tensioned concrete barge. Highlight the role of **non-ferrous aggregates** in reducing hull weight. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the failure modes of a prestressed concrete vessel under (i) over-pressurisation, (ii) impact loading, and (iii) long-term corrosion of tendons. For each, suggest one mitigation strategy. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction Sequence** — *(Refer to Section 4.3 diagram of these notes.)* (5 marks for the sequence, 2 marks for non-ferrous aggregate discussion)

**Key steps in correct order:**

1. **Slab casting on slipway** — inner liner (25 mm, rich mortar) is cast first against steel mould.
2. **Duct placement** — corrugated HDPE / galvanised steel ducts are wired to the reinforcement cage at the calculated tendon profile.
3. **Main shell concreting** — concrete is poured in lifts of 1.0 m, vibrated, with sample cubes taken.
4. **Curing** — steam curing at 60 °C for 16–18 hours accelerates strength gain to 80 % of $f_{ck}$.
5. **Tendon threading & stressing** — high-tensile strands are threaded through ducts and stressed using hydraulic jacks (typically 70–80 % of $f_y$ initially).
6. **Anchorage & grouting** — cones are locked; ducts are pressure-grouted with cement slurry to bond tendons to concrete and provide corrosion protection.
7. **Waterproofing & launching** — bituminous membrane is applied; vessel is launched and hydrostatically tested at 1.5 × working pressure.

**Role of Non-Ferrous Aggregates (2 marks):**
- Replacement of dense granite aggregate with **pumice, expanded slate, or sintered fly-ash** reduces hull density from 24 kN/m³ to as low as **17–19 kN/m³** — a 25–30 % reduction in self-weight.
- This increases **payload capacity** and improves **buoyancy reserve** in damaged condition (one-compartment flooding safety).
- Common in Indian practice: **laterite aggregate** in Mangalore and **cinder aggregate** in Kolkata shipyards (historical data).

**(b) Failure Modes & Mitigation:**

| Failure Mode | Mechanism | Mitigation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Over-pressurisation** | Hoop tensile stress exceeds effective prestress; concrete cracks radially; subsequent tendon yield → sudden burst | Install **pressure relief valves** at 110 % working pressure; design for $1.5 \times p$ as per ASME Section VIII |
| **Impact loading** | Localised spalling of concrete cover exposing tendons; could sever strands in severe grounding | Provide **outer sacrificial layer** of fibre-reinforced concrete; install **bulwark fendering** along sides |
| **Long-term tendon corrosion** | Chloride ingress from seawater → tendon pitting → loss of cross-section; can lead to sudden brittle fracture | Use **grouted ducts** (cement grout with 0.4 w/c); apply **cathodic protection** (impressed current); use **epoxy-coated strands**; design concrete cover ≥ 50 mm |

(1.5 marks for each correctly identified failure mode, 1 mark each for the mitigation strategy, totalling 7.5 → cap at 7)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap:** Examiners *deliberately* mix units (mm with m, N/mm² with MPa). Always convert **D** to **mm** when $t$ is in mm. A 1.5-mark deduction is standard for unit errors.
> 2. **Forgetting the Efficiency Factor $\eta$:** Many students compute $P_0 = T_h \cdot \pi \cdot D$ and forget the division by $\eta$. This under-estimates the required prestress by 10–15 %, and a full 2-mark deduction is applied.
> 3. **Permissible Stress Misquote:** Use **$0.87 \, f_y$** for tendons (per IS 1343 / BS 8110), *not* $0.67 \, f_y$ (which is the mild-steel factor). Wrong factor = 1 mark lost.
> 4. **Hoop vs. Longitudinal Confusion:** Always state *which* stress you are computing. The longitudinal stress is **half** the hoop stress. Examiners mark the correct one but deduct if the student does not label.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Definition Crystal Ball:** A PCV = Concrete + Pre-compression via tensioned steel. Memorise the two-word mantra: **"pre-compress to resist tension."**
- **Golden Equation:** $\sigma_h = \dfrac{pD}{2t}$ — appears in ≈ 60 % of ESE questions. Master it.
- **Force Equation:** $T_h = pD/2$ (N/mm) is the hoop force per unit length.
- **Prestress Equation:** $P_0 = \dfrac{T_h \cdot \pi D}{\eta}$ — never forget $\eta$.
- **Tendon Area Equation:** $A_{st} = \dfrac{P_0}{0.87 \, f_y}$ — never use $0.67 f_y$ for tendons.
- **Concrete Grade:** Minimum **M40** for marine PCV (per IS 1343).
- **Cover:** Minimum **50 mm** for tendons in marine environment.
- **Tendon Efficiency:** Typically **0.85–0.90**; includes friction, anchorage slip, elastic shortening, creep, shrinkage, and relaxation losses.
- **Two Methods:** Pre-tensioning (mass production) and Post-tensioning (large vessels).
- **Three States of Stress:** Unstressed → Prestressed → Service (working) state.
- **Material Composition:** Concrete (cement + water + non-ferrous aggregates like pumice, slag, laterite) + High-tensile steel tendons.
- **Advantages:** Corrosion resistance, low cost, fire resistance, low magnetic signature, good vibration damping.
- **Disadvantages:** Heavy (≈ 25 % denser than water), brittle failure mode, requires skilled labour, longer construction time.
- **Historical References:** *SS Faith* (1917), WWII concrete barges, Moss-Rosenberg LNG carriers, Indian Navy pontoons.
- **Real-World Modern Examples:** Hong Kong-Zhuhai immersed tube (concrete segments), *Pipavav* floating dry-dock, Norway's concrete platform *Troll A* (prestressed concrete gravity base).
- **CO Mapping (KTU 2024):** This topic primarily maps to **CO4** — *Design marine structures using non-ferrous and composite materials under combined loading* — Bloom Level: **Apply / Analyse (L3–L4).**
- **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** **"PD over 2t"** for hoop stress — write it on your hand if you must.

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — PRESTRESSED CONCRETE VESSELS**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Advantages and Disadvantages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Non-ferrous materials** in the context of naval architecture and marine engineering are defined as metallic alloys and pure metals that contain **negligible or zero iron (Fe) content** as their primary matrix constituent. In shipbuilding practice, the principal non-ferrous families are:

- **Copper-base alloys** (Brass, Bronze, Cupro-Nickels, Aluminum Bronzes)
- **Aluminum-base alloys** (5083, 5086, 5454, 6061, 6082 — predominantly 5xxx and 6xxx series for marine use)
- **Titanium-base alloys** (Grade 2, Grade 5 / Ti-6Al-4V)
- **Nickel-base alloys** (Monel, Inconel, Cupro-Nickels)
- **Lead, Zinc, and Tin** (used as coatings, solders, and sacrificial anodes)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 4 Focus):** This module specifically evaluates the **engineering trade-off analysis** of substituting traditional ferrous steels (mild steel, AH36, DH36) with non-ferrous alternatives for hulls, superstructures, propellers, seawater systems, and offshore fittings. The "advantages and disadvantages" framework is the *core assessment tool* for justifying material selection in marine environments.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition for First-Time Learners**

Imagine you are building a boat for two very different purposes:

1. A **racing yacht** that must float high, resist salty seawater, and never rust through.
2. A **heavy cargo barge** that must survive ice impacts, carry tons of load, and be cheap to repair with basic welding equipment.

- For the **yacht**, you would pick **aluminum alloys** — they are light like a feather, naturally form a protective oxide skin, and do not rust like iron.
- For the **seawater piping**, you would pick **cupro-nickel (Cu-Ni 90/10 or 70/30)** because seawater flows through it for decades without corroding, even though it is heavier and more expensive.
- For the **propeller blades**, you would pick **manganese bronze or nickel-aluminum bronze (NAB)** because they resist the violent cavitation erosion of spinning in water at high RPM.

**Every non-ferrous material brings a unique SUPERPOWER (advantage) — but every superpower comes with a WEAKNESS (disadvantage).** The naval architect's job is to weigh these trade-offs and pick the right material for the right ship component. That is exactly what this module teaches.

## 1.3 Standard Marine Classification Metrics

| Metric | Standard Notation | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| Density | $\rho$ (g/cm³ or kg/m³) | Weight/displacement calculation |
| Yield Strength | $\sigma_y$ (MPa) | Hull scantling rules |
| Corrosion Rate | mpy (mils per year) or mm/yr | Service life prediction |
| Elongation | $\varepsilon_f$ (%) | Formability / plate bending |
| Sparking / Non-Sparking | Binary (Yes/No) | Explosive/ammunition vessels |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** A non-ferrous material is **NOT automatically "better"** than steel. Naval architects choose materials based on a multi-criteria optimization of **strength-to-weight ratio, corrosion resistance, weldability, cost, and reparability**. The advantages and disadvantages MUST be evaluated in the context of the specific ship component.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The Five-Pillar Trade-off Framework

For KTU 2024 evaluation, the advantages and disadvantages of non-ferrous materials in shipbuilding are systematically analyzed across **five engineering pillars**. Each pillar is presented as a **comparative trade-off** between non-ferrous materials and traditional mild steel (the KTU reference baseline).

### Pillar 1 — Weight & Strength (The Lightweight Advantage)

- **Why it matters:** A lighter superstructure raises the metacenter, improves stability, reduces fuel consumption, and increases payload capacity.
- **How it works:** Non-ferrous alloys (especially Al and Ti) have **lower density** but can be alloyed to achieve strength comparable to mild steel.
- **Key relationship:** The **Specific Strength** ($\sigma_y / \rho$) is the master metric for marine lightweighting.

### Pillar 2 — Corrosion Resistance (The Seawater Survival Advantage)

- **Why it matters:** Seawater contains approximately **3.5% dissolved salts (NaCl dominant)**, making it a highly aggressive electrolyte that destroys unprotected mild steel at **0.1 to 0.5 mm/year**.
- **How it works:** Non-ferrous alloys form **self-healing passive oxide films** (e.g., $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ on aluminum, $\text{TiO}_2$ on titanium, $\text{Cu}_2\text{O}$ on copper alloys) that prevent further oxidation.

### Pillar 3 — Thermal & Electrical Conductivity

- **Why it matters:** Copper and aluminum are excellent conductors — critical for **welding cable terminations, electrical busbars, heat exchangers, and condenser tubes** in ships.
- **How it works:** Free electron density in the FCC lattice of Cu and Al is very high.

### Pillar 4 — Non-Magnetic & Non-Sparking Behavior

- **Why it matters:** Naval vessels (especially mine countermeasure vessels — MCMVs) and ammunition carriers require **non-magnetic hulls** to avoid triggering magnetic sea mines.
- **How it works:** Non-ferrous FCC structures (Cu, Al, austenitic) are generally **non-magnetic** in the annealed condition.

### Pillar 5 — Manufacturability, Cost & Repair (The Practical Disadvantage Cluster)

- **Why it matters:** A material is only useful if the shipyard can **weld, cut, form, and repair it** with available infrastructure and budget.
- **How it works:** Many non-ferrous alloys require **specialized welding processes** (TIG/GTAW, MIG/GMAW with pulsed arc), **inert gas shielding**, and **post-weld heat treatment** — which small shipyards may lack.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]

| # | Formula / Relationship | Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Strength (Strength-to-Weight) | $SS = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\rho}$ | $\text{kN}\cdot\text{m/kg}$ | Lightweight hull selection |
| 2 | Specific Stiffness (Modulus-to-Weight) | $SSt = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$ | $\text{MN}\cdot\text{m/kg}$ | Deflection-limited panels |
| 3 | Corrosion Rate (mass loss) | $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot D}$ | mpy or mm/yr | Service life prediction |
| 4 | Galvanic Series Potential | $\Delta E = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$ | Volts (V) | Bimetallic corrosion |
| 5 | Weight Saving Fraction | $\Delta W\% = \dfrac{W_{\text{steel}} - W_{\text{NF}}}{W_{\text{steel}}} \times 100$ | % | Lightweighting audit |
| 6 | Buoyancy Reserve | $R_b = \dfrac{V_{\text{displaced}} - V_{\text{hull}}}{V_{\text{displaced}}} \times 100$ | % | Stability check |
| 7 | Rule-of-Thumb Density Ratio | $\dfrac{\rho_{\text{Al}}}{\rho_{\text{steel}}} \approx 0.35$ | dimensionless | Quick weight estimate |
| 8 | Cavitation Resistance Index | $CI \propto \dfrac{H_v}{\rho \cdot v^2}$ | dimensionless | Propeller alloy selection |
| 9 | Thermal Expansion Differential | $\Delta L = L_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | m / mm | Bi-metallic joint design |
| 10 | Cost Premium Ratio | $C_R = \dfrac{C_{\text{NF}}}{C_{\text{mild steel}}}$ | dimensionless | Budgetary trade-off |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Warning on Tables:** Notice the use of `\vert` and `\rho` notation — KTU answers are evaluated on **strict LaTeX correctness**, and incorrect symbol rendering in tables can cost presentation marks.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These trade-off equations are the **backbone of every classification society rule** (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, IRS, BV) for material selection. For example:

- **DNVGL-ST-C501** (Composite Materials) uses specific strength to justify aluminum superstructures.
- **Lloyd's Register SSC Rules** use galvanic potential differences to mandate **insulating gaskets** between Cu-Ni piping and steel hull penetrations.
- **IRS Rules for High-Speed Craft** use specific stiffness to limit deflection in aluminum hull panels.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked "state two advantages," ALWAYS pair the advantage with the **ship component** where it is exploited. e.g., *"Aluminum alloy 5083 has a low density of **2.66 g/cm³** (advantage) which is exploited in **superstructure construction** to lower the center of gravity and improve stability."*

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Weight Saving Fraction Using Density Ratio

**Problem (KTU-Type):** A warship superstructure originally built from mild steel ($\rho_{\text{steel}} = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$) is redesigned using aluminum alloy 5083 ($\rho_{\text{Al}} = 2660 \text{ kg/m}^3$). Both designs have identical volume $V$. Calculate the **percentage weight saving** achieved.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — State the weight of the steel superstructure**

$$W_{\text{steel}} = \rho_{\text{steel}} \cdot V \cdot g$$

This expresses weight as the product of density, volume, and gravitational acceleration.

**Step 2 — State the weight of the aluminum superstructure**

$$W_{\text{Al}} = \rho_{\text{Al}} \cdot V \cdot g$$

Identical volume assumption, but the lower density of aluminum reduces the mass term.

**Step 3 — Formulate the weight saving fraction**

$$\Delta W\% = \frac{W_{\text{steel}} - W_{\text{Al}}}{W_{\text{steel}}} \times 100$$

This is the **standard shipbuilding lightweighting audit formula** mandated by classification societies for superstructure redesigns.

**Step 4 — Substitute the density values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta W\% &= \frac{\rho_{\text{steel}} \cdot V \cdot g - \rho_{\text{Al}} \cdot V \cdot g}{\rho_{\text{steel}} \cdot V \cdot g} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{\rho_{\text{steel}} - \rho_{\text{Al}}}{\rho_{\text{steel}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{7850 - 2660}{7850} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{5190}{7850} \times 100 \\
&= 66.11\%
\end{aligned}
$$

The volume and gravity terms cancel cleanly, showing that the weight saving depends **only on the density ratio** — a powerful insight for naval architects.

**Step 5 — Practical Interpretation**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A 66% mass reduction in the superstructure lowers the **vertical center of gravity (VCG)** significantly, increases the **metacentric height (GM)**, and improves **initial stability**. However, the structural scantlings must be re-verified because the elastic modulus of aluminum (E ≈ 70 GPa) is only **one-third** that of steel (E ≈ 210 GPa).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Galvanic Corrosion Rate Estimation (Mixed Potential Theory)

**Problem (KTU-Type):** A **cupro-nickel 90/10 pipe** ($\rho = 8940 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $E_{\text{corr}} = -0.25 \text{ V vs SCE}$) is bolted to a **mild steel hull** ($E_{\text{corr}} = -0.65 \text{ V vs SCE}$) in seawater. The exposed area ratio is $A_{\text{steel}}/A_{\text{Cu-Ni}} = 10/1$. Using **mixed potential theory**, determine the **bimetallic corrosion risk category**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify anode and cathode from potential values**

- Mild steel: $E = -0.65 \text{ V}$ → **more negative → ANODE (corrodes)**
- Cupro-nickel: $E = -0.25 \text{ V}$ → **more positive → CATHODE (protected)**

The greater the potential difference, the higher the electrochemical driving force for galvanic corrosion.

**Step 2 — Compute the EMF driving force**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}} \\
&= (-0.25) - (-0.65) \\
&= +0.40 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

A potential difference of **0.40 V** is classified as **HIGH risk** per DNVGL-RP-B401 (anything > 0.30 V is "severe").

**Step 3 — Apply the area-effect rule**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The corrosion current density on the anode follows the inverse area relation:
> $$i_{\text{anode}} = i_{\text{coupled}} \times \frac{A_{\text{cathode}}}{A_{\text{anode}}}$$

The unfavorable (small anode : large cathode) ratio of **1 : 10** will **intensify the corrosion rate on the steel by approximately an order of magnitude** — this is why **insulating rubber gaskets and dielectric unions are mandatory** at every Cu-Ni / steel pipe penetration.

**Step 4 — Practical Naval Architecture Mitigation**

- Install a **sacrificial zinc or aluminum anode** closer to the coupling.
- Use **GRP (Glass Reinforced Plastic) isolation flanges**.
- Apply **thick coal-tar epoxy** coating on the steel surface near the joint.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Material-by-Material Trade-off Table

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the single most important table for KTU Module 4 examination answers.** Memorize the structure and the key examples.

| Material Family | Principal Marine Alloys | Density (g/cm³) | Key Advantage | Key Disadvantage | Typical Shipboard Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Aluminum Alloys** | 5083, 5086, 5454, 6082 | **2.66 – 2.80** | Lightweight (66% lighter than steel); non-magnetic | Low modulus (E ≈ 70 GPa); galvanic corrosion with steel | Superstructures, fast ferries, naval MCMVs |
| **Copper Alloys (Brass)** | Admiralty Brass, Naval Brass | **8.40 – 8.70** | Good machinability; biofouling resistance | Dezincification in seawater | Heat exchangers, condensers (legacy) |
| **Copper Alloys (Bronze)** | Manganese Bronze, NAB | **7.50 – 8.80** | High strength; excellent cavitation resistance | Expensive; requires skilled welding | Propellers, pump impellers, valves |
| **Cupro-Nickels** | Cu-Ni 90/10, 70/30 | **8.90 – 8.95** | Outstanding seawater resistance; resists biofouling | High cost (~8–10× steel) | Seawater piping, fire-fighting mains, hull plate (HSLC ships) |
| **Titanium Alloys** | Gr.2 (CP-Ti), Gr.5 (Ti-6Al-4V) | **4.43 – 4.50** | Best strength-to-weight; immune to seawater corrosion | Extremely expensive; difficult to weld | Submarine pressure hulls, deep-sea fittings, heat exchangers |
| **Nickel Alloys** | Monel 400, Inconel 625 | **8.80 – 8.95** | Exceptional corrosion resistance in all media | Very high cost; work-hardening | Propeller shafts, chemical tankers, exhaust systems |
| **Lead & Zinc** | Pure Pb, Pure Zn | **7.13 / 11.34** | Sacrificial anode (Zn); radiation shielding (Pb) | Toxicity (Pb); very soft | Cathodic protection anodes, sonar dome coatings |
| **Mild Steel (REFERENCE)** | MS, AH36, DH36 | **7.85** | Cheap, easily weldable, widely available | Corrodes rapidly in seawater | Hull plating (primary baseline) |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Cost Premium Ratio for Material Substitution

**Problem (KTU-Type):** A shipyard quotes mild steel at ₹85/kg and cupro-nickel 90/10 at ₹950/kg. Compute the **Cost Premium Ratio** and discuss the engineering justification that may still make Cu-Ni acceptable for a critical system.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the cost premium ratio formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_R &= \frac{C_{\text{NF}}}{C_{\text{steel}}} \\
&= \frac{950}{85} \\
&\approx 11.18
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **dimensionless cost multiplier** — Cu-Ni is approximately **11.2 times more expensive per kg** than mild steel.

**Step 2 — Justify the cost premium on a lifecycle basis**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU requires **life-cycle cost (LCC) reasoning**, not just purchase cost. The full LCC includes:
> - **Initial procurement cost** (CAPEX)
> - **Coating maintenance cost** (steel needs repainting every 3–5 years)
> - **Corrosion-related repair and downtime** (steel piping leaks at joints)
> - **Inspection and survey cost** (every classification society survey)
> - **Replacement cost** at end-of-life

> [!NOTE]
> For a **30-year service life**, the lifecycle cost of Cu-Ni is often **lower than coated mild steel** because the steel requires 3–4 major overhauls, while Cu-Ni runs essentially maintenance-free. This is the standard argument used in **naval platform design**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```

```mermaid
    AN -->|Oxidation Reaction| Fe[Fe Dissolves as Fe2+]
```
