# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: State any three advantages of using aluminum alloys in shipbuilding.**

**Model Answer:**

> 1. **Low Density:** Aluminum alloys have a density of approximately **2.66 g/cm³**, which is about **one-third** that of mild steel. This results in a **weight reduction of nearly 65%** for the same component volume, improving the vessel's stability, payload capacity, and fuel efficiency.
> 2. **Corrosion Resistance:** A tenacious, self-healing **aluminum oxide (Al₂O₃) film** forms naturally on the surface, providing excellent resistance to atmospheric and marine corrosion without requiring heavy paint systems.
> 3. **Non-Magnetic Behavior:** Pure aluminum and most marine aluminum alloys (5xxx and 6xxx series) are **non-magnetic**, making them ideal for **mine countermeasure vessels (MCMVs)** and degaussed warships where low magnetic signature is critical.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: List any three disadvantages of titanium alloys that limit their widespread use in commercial shipbuilding.**

**Model Answer:**

> 1. **Very High Cost:** Titanium is approximately **15 to 30 times more expensive** than mild steel on a per-kg basis, making it economically unviable for commercial hulls.
> 2. **Difficult Weldability:** Titanium requires **inert gas shielding (Argon)** on both the top and root side of the weld, and any contamination by oxygen or nitrogen causes **embrittlement**. This demands specialized welders and equipment.
> 3. **Gall and Seizure Tendency:** Titanium has a strong tendency to **cold-weld (gall)** against itself and other metals under sliding contact, requiring special surface treatments or anti-seize compounds for fasteners and moving parts.

> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **DO NOT** list advantages and disadvantages in a single bullet dump — KTU 2024 requires **tabular or categorized presentation** with **at least one numerical example** for full marks.
> 2. **DO NOT** confuse the *alloy family* (e.g., brass) with the *specific alloy* (e.g., Naval Brass). Examiners deduct 1 mark for imprecise terminology.
> 3. **ALWAYS pair the property with the application** — a bare statement like "Al is light" is worth only 1 mark; "Al 5083 (ρ = 2.66 g/cm³) is used for the **wheelhouse superstructure** to lower VCG" is worth full 3 marks.
> 4. **Cost arguments** without lifecycle reasoning lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Galvanic corrosion** questions demand the **EMF (ΔE) value** plus the **mitigation** — not just the statement that corrosion occurs.

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Compare the advantages and disadvantages of **copper-nickel (Cu-Ni 90/10) alloys** over **mild steel** for shipboard **seawater piping systems**. Support your answer with relevant properties and at least **two application-specific justifications**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **mechanism of galvanic corrosion** when a Cu-Ni pipe is connected to a mild steel hull. What mitigation measures are adopted in shipbuilding practice? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| Property | Mild Steel (MS) | Cu-Ni 90/10 | Advantage to Cu-Ni |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density (g/cm³) | **7.85** | **8.94** | Slightly heavier (minor disadvantage) |
| Corrosion Rate in Seawater | 0.1 – 0.5 mm/yr | < 0.025 mm/yr | **~20× better** |
| Biofouling | Heavy marine growth | Naturally resistant (Cu ion release) | **Major advantage** |
| Service Life (Seawater) | 10–15 years (with coating) | 40+ years (uncoated) | **~3× longer** |
| Cost per kg (approx.) | ₹85 | ₹950 | **Disadvantage: ~11× cost premium** |

**Two Application-Specific Justifications:**

1. **Fire-fighting and Bilge Piping on Tankers:** The **Cu⁺ ion release** from Cu-Ni creates an **antifouling** environment, preventing **macro-fouling blockages** in long runs of pipe. Mild steel needs biocide injection and frequent cleaning.

2. **Heat Exchanger Tubes in Condensers:** Cu-Ni 90/10 has **excellent thermal conductivity (~40 W/m·K)** and resists **impingement attack** at bends where seawater velocity is high — mild steel suffers **erosion-corrosion** at the same locations.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The mechanism follows the standard **galvanic cell** model with three essential elements:

- **Anode:** Mild steel hull (E = −0.65 V vs SCE) → **oxidizes**, releasing Fe²⁺ ions.
- **Cathode:** Cu-Ni pipe (E = −0.25 V vs SCE) → **reduction of dissolved O₂** occurs on its surface.
- **Electrolyte:** Seawater (~3.5% NaCl) completes the ionic circuit.

**Driving EMF:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}} \\
&= (-0.25) - (-0.65) \\
&= 0.40 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

A potential difference of **0.40 V** places this couple in the **"Severe"** risk band per **DNVGL-RP-B401**.

**Mitigation Measures in Shipbuilding:**

1. **Dielectric (GRP) Flange Inserts** — electrically isolate the Cu-Ni pipe from the steel hull.
2. **Sacrificial Zinc/Aluminum Anodes** — fitted within 1–2 m of the joint to preferentially corrode.
3. **Heavy Coal-Tar Epoxy Coating** on the steel hull in the vicinity (5 m radius).
4. **Cathodic Protection (Impressed Current)** for large naval platforms.
5. **Use of Dezincification-Resistant Brass (DZR) or Ni-Al Bronze** at the connection fittings.

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages of aluminum alloys (5xxx and 6xxx series)** for **naval ship superstructures**. Include a **quantitative weight-saving estimate** in your answer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain why **pure aluminum and most marine aluminum alloys are non-magnetic**, and why this property is **critical for mine countermeasure vessels (MCMVs)**. What alternative material is used when **non-magnetic AND high-strength** is required? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Advantages of Al 5083 / 6082 for Superstructures:**

1. **Weight Reduction:** Density of Al 5083 is **2.66 g/cm³** versus mild steel at **7.85 g/cm³**, giving a structural weight saving of approximately **66%** for equal volume. A typical destroyer superstructure weighs ~200 tonnes in steel; in aluminum, it weighs only ~68 tonnes — a saving of **132 tonnes** raised above the waterline.

2. **Improved Stability:** The weight reduction directly **lowers the vertical center of gravity (VCG)**, increasing the **metacentric height (GM)** and improving **initial righting moments**.

3. **Corrosion Resistance:** Self-healing **Al₂O₃** passive film eliminates the need for the heavy 3-coat paint system required for steel.

4. **Non-Magnetic Signature:** Critical for naval combatants.

5. **Ease of Fabrication:** Excellent formability for curved plates (5083-O temper) and extrusions (6082-T6) for stiffeners.

**Disadvantages:**

1. **Low Elastic Modulus:** E_Al = **70 GPa** vs E_steel = **210 GPa** — deflection under load is **3× higher**, requiring **deeper scantlings or more closely spaced stiffeners**.
2. **Galvanic Corrosion Risk:** When in contact with steel (e.g., hull), creates a **galvanic cell** because Al is anodic — must use **insulating gaskets and sacrificial anodes**.
3. **Loss of Strength at Elevated Temperature:** Mechanical properties degrade above **150 °C** — vulnerable in engine rooms and near galley exhausts.
4. **Welding Sensitivity:** 5xxx and 6xxx series require **TIG/MIG with Argon shielding**; 5xxx above 4% Mg (e.g., 5083) is **not recommended above 65 °C service** due to **sensitization and stress corrosion cracking risk**.

**Quantitative Weight-Saving Estimate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass saved} &= V \cdot (\rho_{\text{steel}} - \rho_{\text{Al}}) \\
&= V \cdot (7850 - 2660) \text{ kg/m}^3 \\
&= 5190 \cdot V \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $V = 25.4 \text{ m}^3$ (a typical destroyer superstructure envelope), the saving is $\approx 131,800 \text{ kg}$ = **~132 tonnes**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Why Aluminum is Non-Magnetic:**

- Pure aluminum (FCC crystal structure) has a filled outer electronic configuration with no unpaired electrons — it is **diamagnetic** with magnetic susceptibility $\chi \approx +2.2 \times 10^{-5}$ (essentially non-magnetic).
- Marine Al alloys (5xxx — Al-Mg; 6xxx — Al-Mg-Si) inherit this **non-ferromagnetic** FCC matrix. Even after cold working, the magnetic response remains negligible for naval degaussing purposes.

**Why This is Critical for MCMVs:**

- **Magnetic sea mines** (influence mines) are triggered when a vessel's magnetic field exceeds a threshold. A steel hull produces a strong **dipolar magnetic signature** that can detonate a mine from hundreds of meters away.
- MCMVs (e.g., INS Khanjarli, INS Cuttack class) use **aluminum alloy (Al 5083) hulls** to provide an inherently low magnetic signature, supplemented by **degaussing coils** wound around the hull to cancel the residual Earth's-field-induced magnetization.

**Alternative When Non-Magnetic AND High-Strength is Required:**

- **Austenitic Stainless Steel (AISI 304 / 316)** — the FCC austenite phase is non-magnetic in the annealed condition, with yield strength up to **290 MPa** (almost double that of mild steel). However, cold working can transform some austenite to martensite, inducing slight magnetism — controlled by **solution annealing**.
- **Glass-Reinforced Plastic (GRP) Composites** — for smaller vessels, GRP provides a completely non-magnetic hull with excellent corrosion resistance, though limited in size due to structural constraints.
- **Titanium Alloys (Ti-6Al-4V)** — completely non-magnetic, with strength-to-weight exceeding steel — used in **deep-diving submarine pressure hulls** where both non-magnetism and high strength are mandatory.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist before entering the exam hall.**

### Core Concepts

- [ ] Non-ferrous materials contain **negligible/no iron** and offer **corrosion resistance, lightweight, non-magnetic, and conductivity** advantages.
- [ ] Mild steel is the **reference baseline** for ALL advantage/disadvantage comparisons.

### Advantages — Master List

- [ ] **Lightweight:** Al (~66% saving), Ti (~45% saving) over steel.
- [ ] **Corrosion Resistance:** Cu-Ni, Ti, Al — passive oxide films.
- [ ] **Non-Magnetic:** Critical for MCMVs (Al, Cu-Ni, austenitic SS, Ti).
- [ ] **Thermal/Electrical Conductivity:** Cu, Al — heat exchangers, busbars.
- [ ] **Biofouling Resistance:** Cu alloys release Cu⁺ ions.
- [ ] **Cavitation Resistance:** NAB, Mn-Bronze — propellers.
- [ ] **No Sparking:** Bronze tools for ammunition holds.

### Disadvantages — Master List

- [ ] **High Cost:** Cu-Ni (~11×), Ti (~20×), NAB (~5×) over steel.
- [ ] **Welding Difficulty:** Ti (inert gas), Al (TIG/MIG), Cu-Ni (skilled welders).
- [ ] **Galvanic Corrosion:** With steel — must use **insulation + sacrificial anodes**.
- [ ] **Low Modulus:** Al (E = 70 GPa), Ti (E = 110 GPa) — deeper scantlings.
- [ ] **Repair Difficulty:** Limited shipyard capability in many ports.
- [ ] **Recycling/Disposal Issues:** Some Cu alloys (Pb-content) and coated Al.

### Critical Numbers to Memorize

- [ ] $\rho_{\text{Al}} = 2.66 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- [ ] $\rho_{\text{Ti}} = 4.51 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- [ ] $\rho_{\text{Cu-Ni 90/10}} = 8.94 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- [ ] $\rho_{\text{mild steel}} = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- [ ] $E_{\text{Al}} = 70 \text{ GPa}$, $E_{\text{steel}} = 210 \text{ GPa}$, $E_{\text{Ti}} = 110 \text{ GPa}$
- [ ] Galvanic risk threshold: $\Delta E > 0.30 \text{ V} = \text{Severe}$
- [ ] Seawater salinity: **3.5% NaCl**
- [ ] Seawater corrosion rate of unprotected MS: **0.1 – 0.5 mm/year**

### KTU Exam Strategy

- [ ] **Always** present advantages/disadvantages in a **table** for visual clarity.
- [ ] **Always** include a **numerical example** in Part B (e.g., weight saving calculation).
- [ ] **Always** mention **life-cycle cost** when discussing the cost disadvantage.
- [ ] **Always** pair property + application in the same sentence.
- [ ] **Always** cite the **classification society standard** (DNV, LR, ABS, IRS) where relevant.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Future Trends in Concrete Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Future Trends in Concrete Ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Future Trends in Concrete Ships** refer to the next-generation advancements in material science, structural design, fabrication technology, and sustainability practices that are reshaping the role of concrete as a primary shipbuilding material. These trends encompass the development of **High-Performance Concrete (HPC)**, **Ultra-High Performance Concrete (UHPC)**, **Fiber-Reinforced Concrete (FRC)**, **geopolymer composites**, **3D-printed concrete (Additive Manufacturing)**, **smart sensor-integrated concrete**, and **hybrid concrete-composite hulls**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme, Module 4 of PENST523 emphasizes emerging non-ferrous and alternative materials. Concrete, although based on cementitious binders (not metals), is treated as a *non-ferrous alternative structural material* in marine applications, especially for barges, pontoons, and offshore platforms.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of concrete shipbuilding like the evolution of the **automobile industry**. In the 1900s, cars were heavy steel boxes. Over time, engineers introduced **aluminum, carbon fiber, electric powertrains, and self-driving sensors**. Similarly, concrete ships have evolved from the **basic reinforced concrete barges of World War I/II** (like the *SS Faith* and the *Crete Concrete Ships*) to today's **sophisticated, lightweight, sensor-monitored, and environmentally friendly vessels**.

Imagine a **floating LEGO block**: traditional concrete ships are heavy, single-pour blocks. Future concrete ships will be built from **precision-engineered modular panels**, with **embedded health-monitoring sensors**, made from **recycled industrial waste (fly ash, slag)**, and even **printed layer-by-layer by robotic arms** in a shipyard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Concrete Still Matters in Modern Shipbuilding:**
> - **Low cost** compared to steel (1/3 to 1/5 the price of mild steel).
> - **Corrosion resistance** superior to ferrous metals in seawater.
> - **Formability** into complex curved hull shapes.
> - **Thermal & acoustic insulation** built into the structure.
> - **Fire resistance** (does not burn or lose strength rapidly in fires).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Density of Normal Concrete (NC):** **2300–2500 kg/m³**
- **Density of Lightweight Concrete (LWC):** **1200–1800 kg/m³**
- **Compressive Strength of HPC:** **> 60 MPa**
- **Compressive Strength of UHPC:** **> 150 MPa (up to 800 MPa)**
- **Young's Modulus of Concrete:** **25–40 GPa**
- **Water Density (Seawater):** **1025 kg/m³**
- **Steel Density:** **7850 kg/m³**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Board Exams:** When comparing hull materials, examiners love the **Strength-to-Weight Ratio (Specific Strength)** question. Always quote values in **kN·m/kg** or **MPa/(kg/m³)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Future Trends in Concrete Ships

### 2.1.1 Ultra-High Performance Concrete (UHPC)
UHPC is a cementitious composite with **compressive strengths exceeding 150 MPa**, made with:
- Very low water-to-binder ratio (**w/b ≤ 0.25**)
- **Silica fume** (5–15% replacement of cement)
- **Quartz powder** for particle packing optimization
- **Steel or PVA micro-fibers** (2–4% by volume)
- High-range water reducers (superplasticizers)

**Why it matters for shipbuilding:** Enables **thinner hull sections** (50–80 mm instead of 150–200 mm) → reduces displacement → increases payload.

### 2.1.2 Fiber-Reinforced Concrete (FRC)
Three main classes used in marine applications:
1. **Steel Fiber Reinforced Concrete (SFRC)** – 1.5–2.5% volume fraction.
2. **Glass Fiber Reinforced Concrete (GFRC)** – Alkali-resistant (AR) glass.
3. **Carbon Fiber Reinforced Concrete (CFRC)** – Lightweight, high tensile strength.

**Why it matters:** Replaces traditional **rebar reinforcement** in critical zones, eliminating corrosion at the rebar-concrete interface — a major cause of hull degradation.

### 2.1.3 Self-Compacting Concrete (SCC)
Highly flowable concrete that **fills complex hull formwork under its own weight** without mechanical vibration. Critical for:
- Double-curved hull surfaces.
- Heavily reinforced zones (bow and stern thruster areas).

### 2.1.4 Lightweight Aggregate Concrete (LWAC)
Uses **expanded clay, pumice, perlite, or sintered fly ash** as aggregate.
- **Density reduction:** 20–40% compared to NC.
- **Thermal conductivity:** Reduced by ~60% (excellent for LNG carriers).

### 2.1.5 Geopolymer Concrete
- Cement is **replaced entirely** by **fly ash + GGBS + alkaline activator (NaOH, Na₂SiO₃)**.
- **CO₂ emissions reduced by ~80%** compared to OPC concrete.
- Excellent **sulfate and chloride resistance** — ideal for marine service.

### 2.1.6 3D-Printed Concrete (Additive Manufacturing)
- Robotic arms extrude **layered concrete (5–50 mm layer height)**.
- Enables **topology-optimized hull forms** (lattice internal structures).
- Reduces **formwork cost by 60–80%**.

### 2.1.7 Smart Concrete with Embedded Sensors
- **Piezoelectric sensors** detect stress waves (impact, fatigue).
- **Optical fiber (FBG) sensors** measure strain and temperature.
- **Carbon nanotube (CNT) coatings** enable self-sensing of damage.

### 2.1.8 Hybrid Concrete-Composite Hulls
Combines concrete with **Glass Fiber Reinforced Polymer (GFRP)** or **Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer (CFRP)** outer skins for:
- Waterproofing.
- Impact resistance.
- Reduced maintenance.

### 2.1.9 Sustainable / Green Concrete
- **Supplementary Cementitious Materials (SCMs):** Fly ash, GGBS, silica fume, metakaolin.
- **Recycled concrete aggregate (RCA)** for non-critical bulkheads.
- **CO₂-cured concrete** (CarbonCure technology).

### 2.1.10 Concrete for Offshore Renewable Energy Platforms
- **Floating Offshore Wind (FOW) foundations** – Concrete semi-submersibles.
- **Wave Energy Converters (WECs)** – Concrete hulls for stability.
- **Oscillating Water Column (OWC) chambers** – Concrete construction.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Description | Units / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_t = 0.33 \cdot \sqrt{f'_c}$ | Tensile strength of concrete (ACI 318) | $f_t$, $f'_c$ in **MPa** |
| 2 | $E_c = 4700 \cdot \sqrt{f'_c}$ | Modulus of elasticity (ACI 318) | $E_c$ in **MPa** |
| 3 | $SS = f'_c / \rho$ | Specific Strength | **kN·m/kg** |
| 4 | $F_b = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$ | Buoyant force (Archimedes' Principle) | $F_b$ in **N**, $V_{disp}$ in **m³** |
| 5 | $\Delta W = m_{steel} - m_{conc}$ | Weight savings (concrete vs steel) | **kg** |
| 6 | $f'_{UHPC} = f'_c \cdot (1 + 2.5 V_f)$ | Strength gain with steel fibers (rule of thumb) | $V_f$ = fiber volume fraction |
| 7 | $D_{LWAC} = D_{NC} \cdot (1 - 0.25 \cdot R_a)$ | Lightweight aggregate density reduction | $R_a$ = aggregate replacement ratio |
| 8 | $\phi(t) = \phi_{u} \cdot (t / (t + \tau))$ | Creep coefficient (CEB-FIP model) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $k = W_c / C$ | Water-to-cement ratio | dimensionless |
| 10 | $RCPT = a \cdot (w/b)^b$ | Rapid Chloride Permeability Test correlation | **Coulombs** |
| 11 | $P_{crit} = \pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I / L^2$ | Euler buckling load of hull plate | **N** |
| 12 | $\sigma_{max} = M \cdot c / I$ | Maximum bending stress | **MPa** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table Note:** Avoid using the vertical pipe symbol inside the table. For absolute value, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in your LaTeX expressions.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These future trends are already being deployed in:
- **Norway's Hywind Tampen** floating wind farm (concrete spar buoys).
- **China's 3D-printed concrete seawalls** and modular floating docks.
- **Netherlands' "Printed Concrete" bridges** in marine environments.
- **Royal Dutch Shell's concrete gravity-based offshore structures** in the North Sea.

For shipbuilders in Kerala (Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock under KTU regional ecosystem), the **most immediate impact** is in **cost-effective, durable barges, pontoons, and floating jetties** using FRC and SCC.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Specific Strength Comparison of Hull Materials

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical):**
A naval architect is comparing **Normal Concrete (NC)**, **UHPC**, and **Mild Steel** for a 100-tonne displacement barge. Given the following data, compute the **Specific Strength (SS)** for each material and recommend the best option.

| Material | Compressive Strength $f'_c$ (MPa) | Density $\rho$ (kg/m³) |
|---|---|---|
| Normal Concrete (NC) | 40 | 2400 |
| UHPC | 180 | 2500 |
| Mild Steel (MS) | 250 (yield) | 7850 |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Define Specific Strength**

Specific Strength is the strength-to-weight ratio:

$$SS = \frac{f'_c}{\rho}$$

where the strength is in **MPa (N/mm²)** and density in **kg/m³**. To express in consistent units, we convert:

$$SS \left[ \text{kN·m/kg} \right] = \frac{f'_c \, [\text{MPa}]}{\rho \, [\text{kg/m}^3]} \times 1000$$

**Step 2: Compute SS for Normal Concrete (NC)**

$$SS_{NC} = \frac{40 \, \text{MPa} \times 1000}{2400 \, \text{kg/m}^3} = 16.67 \, \text{kN·m/kg}$$

**Step 3: Compute SS for UHPC**

$$SS_{UHPC} = \frac{180 \times 1000}{2500} = 72.00 \, \text{kN·m/kg}$$

**Step 4: Compute SS for Mild Steel (MS)**

$$SS_{MS} = \frac{250 \times 1000}{7850} = 31.85 \, \text{kN·m/kg}$$

**Step 5: Comparative Analysis**

| Material | $SS$ (kN·m/kg) | Rank | Relative Efficiency vs MS |
|---|---|---|---|
| UHPC | 72.00 | 1st | 2.26× better |
| Mild Steel | 31.85 | 2nd | 1.00× (baseline) |
| Normal Concrete | 16.67 | 3rd | 0.52× worse |

**Step 6: Engineering Recommendation**

> UHPC is **2.26× more efficient** in specific strength compared to mild steel, making it the best option for **weight-sensitive** applications like **fast ferries, patrol boats, and offshore wind foundations**. NC, while economical, has the lowest specific strength and is reserved for **stationary or slow-moving structures** (barges, pontoons, breakwaters).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Buoyancy & Displacement of a Lightweight Concrete Pontoon

**Problem Statement:**
Design a **rectangular concrete pontoon** of dimensions **10 m (L) × 4 m (B) × 1.5 m (D)**. If the pontoon is made of **Lightweight Aggregate Concrete (LWAC)** with density **1600 kg/m³** and wall thickness **100 mm** (hollow interior), calculate:
1. The **structural mass** of the pontoon.
2. The **maximum allowable payload** so the pontoon floats with a **freeboard of 0.3 m** in seawater ($\rho_{sw}$ = **1025 kg/m³**).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate the Volume of Concrete (Walls + Bottom)**

Assume the pontoon has a **bottom plate** (10 m × 4 m × 100 mm) and **four side walls** (perimeter walls with 100 mm thickness and 1.4 m height above the bottom).

Volume of bottom plate:

$$V_{bottom} = 10 \times 4 \times 0.1 = 4.0 \, \text{m}^3$$

Volume of side walls (4 walls, subtracting corner overlaps):

$$V_{walls} = 2 \times (10 \times 0.1 \times 1.4) + 2 \times (3.8 \times 0.1 \times 1.4)$$

$$V_{walls} = 2.80 + 1.064 = 3.864 \, \text{m}^3$$

Total concrete volume:

$$V_{conc} = 4.0 + 3.864 = 7.864 \, \text{m}^3$$

**Step 2: Calculate Structural Mass**

$$m_{struct} = V_{conc} \times \rho_{LWAC} = 7.864 \times 1600 = 12{,}582.4 \, \text{kg}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Maximum Displacement for 0.3 m Freeboard**

The pontoon submerges to a draft $T$:

$$T = D - \text{freeboard} = 1.5 - 0.3 = 1.2 \, \text{m}$$

Displaced volume:

$$V_{disp} = L \times B \times T = 10 \times 4 \times 1.2 = 48.0 \, \text{m}^3$$

Mass of displaced seawater:

$$m_{disp} = V_{disp} \times \rho_{sw} = 48.0 \times 1025 = 49{,}200 \, \text{kg}$$

**Step 4: Apply the Equilibrium Equation (Floating Body)**

For a floating body, weight = buoyancy:

$$m_{struct} + m_{payload} = m_{disp}$$

Solving for payload:

$$m_{payload} = 49{,}200 - 12{,}582.4 = 36{,}617.6 \, \text{kg}$$

$$\boxed{m_{payload} \approx 36.62 \, \text{tonnes}}$$

**Step 5: Verification of Safety Factor**

Deadweight Ratio (DWR):

$$DWR = \frac{m_{payload}}{m_{struct}} = \frac{36{,}617.6}{12{,}582.4} = 2.91$$

This means the pontoon can carry **~2.9 times its own structural weight** in payload — excellent for a marine logistics platform.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Takeaway:** Lightweight aggregate concrete **doubles the payload capacity** compared to normal concrete pontoons of the same dimensions, without compromising freeboard or stability.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: UHPC Strength Enhancement with Steel Fibers

**Problem Statement:**
Estimate the **compressive strength of UHPC** with a base matrix strength of 130 MPa when reinforced with **2.5% volume fraction of steel micro-fibers**.

### Solution

Using the empirical rule of thumb:

$$f'_{UHPC} = f'_c \cdot (1 + 2.5 \cdot V_f)$$

$$f'_{UHPC} = 130 \cdot (1 + 2.5 \times 0.025) = 130 \cdot (1 + 0.0625) = 130 \times 1.0625$$

$$\boxed{f'_{UHPC} = 138.125 \, \text{MPa}}$$

> A modest **6.25% increase** in compressive strength, but the **tensile strength and fracture toughness** increase by **200–400%** — this is the real advantage of fiber reinforcement.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Future Trends vs Conventional Concrete Ships

| Trend / Technology | Strength Gain | Weight Reduction | Durability Gain | Cost Impact | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| UHPC | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | High (-30%) | High |
| FRC (Steel Fibers) | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ | Medium | Very High |
| SCC | ★★ | ★ | ★★★ | Low | Medium |
| LWAC | ★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | Medium | High |
| Geopolymer | ★★★ | ★ | ★★★★★ | Low (long-term) | Medium |
| 3D Printing | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | High (capex) | Low |
| Smart Concrete | ★ (strength) | – | ★★★★★ | High | Low |
| Hybrid Composite Hull | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | Very High | Medium |
| Green Concrete | ★★ | ★ | ★★★ | Low (saves cost) | Medium |
| Offshore Renewable Use | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | Variable | High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Future Concrete Ship Material Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Future Trends in Concrete Ships] --> nodeB[Advanced Cementitious Materials]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Smart Sensor Integration]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Digital Fabrication]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Sustainable Construction]
    nodeA --> nodeF[Hybrid Structural Systems]

    nodeB --> nodeB1[UHPC Ultra High Performance]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[FRC Fiber Reinforced]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[LWAC Lightweight Aggregate]
    nodeB --> nodeB4[Geopolymer Concrete]
    nodeB --> nodeB5[SCC Self Compacting]

    nodeC --> nodeC1[Piezoelectric Sensors]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[FBG Optical Fiber Sensors]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[CNT Self Sensing Concrete]
    nodeC --> nodeC4[IoT Data Transmission]

    nodeD --> nodeD1[3D Concrete Printing]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Modular Precast Panels]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[Robotic Sliding Formwork]
    nodeD --> nodeD4[BIM Digital Twin]

    nodeE --> nodeE1[Fly Ash Replacement]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[GGBS Slag Cement]
    nodeE --> nodeE3[Recycled Concrete Aggregate]
    nodeE --> nodeE4[CO2 Cured Concrete]

    nodeF --> nodeF1[Concrete GFRP Hybrid Hull]
    nodeF --> nodeF2[Concrete CFRP Sandwich Panels]
    nodeF --> nodeF3[Floating Wind Foundations]
    nodeF --> nodeF4[Wave Energy Hulls]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: Smart Concrete Ship Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Design Stage] --> stepB[BIM Modeling]
    stepB --> stepC[Material Selection UHPC FRC LWAC]
    stepC --> stepD[Modular Precast in Controlled Yard]
    stepD --> stepE[Embedded Sensor Installation]
    stepE --> stepF[Transport and Assembly at Berth]
    stepF --> stepG[Launch and Sea Trials]
    stepG --> stepH[Real Time Structural Health Monitoring]
    stepH --> stepI[Predictive Maintenance]
    stepI --> stepJ[End of Life Recycling]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Selecting the Right Concrete for Ship Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[Start Application Selection] --> q1{Is the structure stationary or moving?}

    q1 -->|Stationary Barge Pontoon| q2{Critical corrosion zone?}
    q1 -->|Moving Vessel Ferry Patrol| q3{Weight critical design?}

    q2 -->|Yes Marine splash zone| r1[Use FRC with epoxy coating]
    q2 -->|No Bulk structural| r2[Use Normal Concrete NC]

    q3 -->|Yes High speed| r3[Use UHPC with steel fibers]
    q3 -->|No Moderate speed| q4{Need thermal insulation?}
    q4 -->|Yes LNG Carrier| r4[Use LWAC with cellular structure]
    q4 -->|No General cargo| r5[Use SCC for complex formwork]
```

## 4.4 Layered Architecture: Smart Concrete Hull Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer1[Outer FRP Gelcoat for Waterproofing] --> layer2[UHPC Layer 30 to 50 mm]
    layer2 --> layer3[Steel Fiber Reinforced Concrete 80 to 120 mm]
    layer3 --> layer4[Embedded FBG Sensor Network]
    layer4 --> layer5[Lightweight Aggregate Core 200 to 400 mm]
    layer5 --> layer6[FRC Inner Layer 50 mm]
    layer6 --> layer7[Intumescent Fire Resistant Coating]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams for KTU Exams:** When asked "draw a block diagram of a smart concrete ship hull," examiners expect you to label each layer with its **thickness, function, and material name**. Always include **sensor placement** in your answer to score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
Define **Ultra-High Performance Concrete (UHPC)** and list any **four advantages** of using UHPC in shipbuilding.

**Model Answer:**
UHPC is a cementitious composite with a characteristic compressive strength exceeding **150 MPa**, produced using a very low water-to-binder ratio (≤ 0.25), silica fume, quartz powder, and high-volume steel or PVA fibers (2–4% by volume).

Four advantages of UHPC in shipbuilding:
1. **High specific strength** – thinner hull sections reduce displacement.
2. **Excellent durability** – extremely low chloride permeability (< 100 Coulombs).
3. **Superior impact resistance** – fiber bridging arrests crack propagation.
4. **Reduced maintenance** – service life > 100 years in marine environments.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
What is **geopolymer concrete**? Mention its **two key environmental benefits** over Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC) concrete.

**Model Answer:**
Geopolymer concrete is a binder system in which **alumino-silicate source materials** (such as fly ash, GGBS, or metakaolin) are activated by **alkaline solutions** (NaOH, Na₂SiO₃, or KOH) to form a hardened binder — eliminating the need for OPC.

Two key environmental benefits:
1. **CO₂ emissions reduced by 80%** compared to OPC (every tonne of OPC produces ~0.9 tonnes of CO₂).
2. **Utilizes industrial waste** (fly ash from coal power plants, GGBS from steel plants), promoting a circular economy.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A — Choice 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **four major categories of future trends** in concrete shipbuilding.  *(7 marks)*
**(b)** A concrete barge of dimensions **12 m × 5 m × 1.8 m** is made of **Fiber-Reinforced Concrete (FRC)** with density **2200 kg/m³** and wall thickness **120 mm** (with bottom plate and four side walls, internal hollow). Calculate the **structural mass** and the **maximum payload** for a freeboard of **0.4 m** in seawater ($\rho_{sw}$ = **1025 kg/m³**).  *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a)

**The four major future trend categories are:**

1. **Advanced Cementitious Materials:** UHPC, FRC, SCC, LWAC, Geopolymer.
2. **Digital Fabrication:** 3D concrete printing, modular precast, robotic formwork, BIM integration.
3. **Smart Monitoring Systems:** Piezoelectric, FBG optical fiber, CNT, IoT-enabled SHM.
4. **Hybrid & Sustainable Systems:** Concrete-FRP hybrids, green concrete with SCMs, floating offshore renewable platforms.

*(Each category must be illustrated with a labeled sketch — the examiner expects at least **one diagram** for full 7 marks. Reference: See Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1: Concrete Volume Calculation**

Bottom plate volume:

$$V_{bottom} = 12 \times 5 \times 0.12 = 7.20 \, \text{m}^3$$

Side walls (effective internal dimensions: L = 11.76 m, B = 4.76 m, height above bottom = 1.8 - 0.12 = 1.68 m):

$$V_{walls} = 2 \times (12 \times 0.12 \times 1.68) + 2 \times (4.76 \times 0.12 \times 1.68)$$

$$V_{walls} = 4.8384 + 1.9187 = 6.7571 \, \text{m}^3$$

Total concrete volume:

$$V_{conc} = 7.20 + 6.7571 = 13.9571 \, \text{m}^3$$

**Step 2: Structural Mass**

$$m_{struct} = 13.9571 \times 2200 = 30{,}705.6 \, \text{kg}$$

**Step 3: Draft & Displacement**

$$T = 1.8 - 0.4 = 1.4 \, \text{m}$$

$$V_{disp} = 12 \times 5 \times 1.4 = 84.0 \, \text{m}^3$$

$$m_{disp} = 84.0 \times 1025 = 86{,}100 \, \text{kg}$$

**Step 4: Maximum Payload**

$$m_{payload} = 86{,}100 - 30{,}705.6 = 55{,}394.4 \, \text{kg}$$

$$\boxed{m_{payload} \approx 55.39 \, \text{tonnes}}$$

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**(a)** Discuss the role of **3D concrete printing** and **smart sensors** in the future of concrete shipbuilding.  *(7 marks)*
**(b)** Compare the **specific strength** of Normal Concrete, UHPC, and Mild Steel. Given the following data, recommend the most suitable material for a **fast patrol vessel hull**.  *(7 marks)*

| Material | $f'_c$ or $f_y$ (MPa) | Density (kg/m³) |
|---|---|---|
| Normal Concrete | 35 | 2400 |
| UHPC | 175 | 2500 |
| Mild Steel | 250 | 7850 |

#### Model Solution for (a)

**3D Concrete Printing in Shipbuilding:**
- Robotic arms extrude **thixotropic concrete layer-by-layer** (5–50 mm layer height).
- Eliminates **conventional formwork**, reducing cost by 60–80%.
- Enables **topology-optimized lattice hull structures** for weight reduction.
- Speeds up construction by 40–60% for non-critical sections.
- *Limitation:* Print scale currently limited to small craft; quality control of inter-layer bonding is critical.

**Smart Sensors in Concrete Hulls:**
- **FBG Optical Fiber Sensors:** Distributed strain & temperature mapping.
- **Piezoelectric Sensors:** Real-time impact & fatigue monitoring.
- **CNT-Coated Concrete:** Self-sensing of micro-cracks via electrical resistivity change.
- **IoT-Enabled SHM:** Wireless data to onshore control rooms → predictive maintenance.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1: Specific Strength Formula**

$$SS \left[ \text{kN·m/kg} \right] = \frac{f'_c \text{ or } f_y \, [\text{MPa}] \times 1000}{\rho \, [\text{kg/m}^3]}$$

**Step 2: Calculations**

$$SS_{NC} = \frac{35 \times 1000}{2400} = 14.58 \, \text{kN·m/kg}$$

$$SS_{UHPC} = \frac{175 \times 1000}{2500} = 70.00 \, \text{kN·m/kg}$$

$$SS_{MS} = \frac{250 \times 1000}{7850} = 31.85 \, \text{kN·m/kg}$$

**Step 3: Comparative Table**

| Material | $SS$ (kN·m/kg) | Rank | Suitability for Fast Patrol Vessel |
|---|---|---|---|
| UHPC | 70.00 | 1st | ★★★★★ Excellent — light, strong, durable |
| Mild Steel | 31.85 | 2nd | ★★★ Good — proven, but heavier |
| Normal Concrete | 14.58 | 3rd | ★ Not suitable — too heavy for high speeds |

**Step 4: Recommendation**

For a **fast patrol vessel**, **UHPC is the most suitable material** due to its **2.2× higher specific strength than mild steel**, allowing reduced hull weight, higher speeds, and lower fuel consumption. Mild steel remains a practical alternative for medium-speed vessels, while normal concrete is **not recommended** for fast craft.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert MPa to consistent units** when calculating specific strength — losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing OPC concrete with UHPC properties** in the same question — examiners deduct 1 mark per incorrect property.
> 3. **Not labeling the diagram** — at least **3 labels** are required for full marks in any sketch question.
> 4. **Mixing up $f'_c$ (compressive) with $f_t$ (tensile)** in formula $f_t = 0.33\sqrt{f'_c}$ — students often write $f_t$ on both sides.
> 5. **Ignoring freeboard in buoyancy problems** — you must subtract freeboard from total depth to get actual draft $T$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams:**

- **Definition:** Future trends in concrete ships involve UHPC, FRC, LWAC, SCC, geopolymer concrete, 3D printing, smart sensors, and hybrid composite hulls.
- **UHPC strength:** $f'_c \geq 150$ MPa, $w/b \leq 0.25$, with 2–4% steel fibers.
- **Geopolymer concrete:** 80% less CO₂ than OPC; uses fly ash + GGBS + alkaline activator.
- **3D concrete printing:** Layer-by-layer extrusion, 60–80% formwork cost reduction.
- **Smart concrete:** FBG optical fiber, piezoelectric, and CNT-based self-sensing systems.
- **Lightweight Concrete (LWAC):** Density 1200–1800 kg/m³, ideal for floating structures.
- **Specific Strength formula:** $SS = f'_c / \rho$ (in kN·m/kg after conversion).
- **Buoyancy equation:** $F_b = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$.
- **Tensile strength of concrete:** $f_t = 0.33\sqrt{f'_c}$ (ACI 318).
- **Modulus of elasticity:** $E_c = 4700\sqrt{f'_c}$ (ACI 318, normal-weight concrete).
- **UHPC with steel fibers:** Modest 6% gain in compressive strength, but 200–400% gain in tensile strength and fracture toughness.
- **Key application areas:** Barges, pontoons, floating wind foundations, LNG carrier insulation, wave energy converters.
- **Environmental benefit of geopolymer & green concrete:** Reduced CO₂, use of industrial byproducts (fly ash, GGBS).
- **Hybrid hulls:** Concrete core + GFRP/CFRP skin = waterproofing + impact resistance + reduced maintenance.
- **Remember:** Always convert units before plugging into formulas — $1$ MPa = $10^6$ N/m² = $10^3$ kN/m².

> **End of KTU-PREMIER Notes — Module 4, Topic: Future Trends in Concrete Ships**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Advanced Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Advanced Materials** in the context of non-ferrous shipbuilding engineering refer to a class of engineered metallic, composite, and hybrid systems whose microstructure, phase composition, or interface chemistry has been deliberately tailored at the micro-, nano-, or atomic-scale to deliver **superior specific strength, corrosion resistance, fatigue endurance, and multi-functional performance** beyond the capability of conventional marine-grade aluminum, copper, and titanium alloys.

These materials are classified by the International Maritime Organization (IMO) and classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, Bureau Veritas) under **"novel structural materials"** and are evaluated against enhanced verification protocols such as the **Common Structural Rules (CSR)** for hull integrity and the **IACS UR S21/S22** unified requirements for marine composites and sandwich structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor — Module 4 Definition**
> *Advanced Materials* = Non-ferrous systems with engineered microstructures, including **Metal Matrix Composites (MMCs), Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMCs), Polymer Matrix Composites (PMCs), hybrid laminates, superalloys, titanium aluminides, aluminum-lithium alloys, and high-entropy alloys (HEAs)**, used where weight reduction, fire resistance, acoustic damping, or stealth characteristics are mission-critical.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Toolbox Upgrade"

Imagine a traditional shipwright is building a hull using only **wood, iron rivets, and copper sheathing** (the "conventional non-ferrous toolbox"). These materials work, but they are heavy, corrode in saltwater, and have fixed properties.

Now imagine the shipwright is handed an **advanced engineering toolbox** containing:
- A **carbon-fiber reinforced plastic panel** as light as wood but 10× stronger (Polymer Matrix Composite).
- A **silicon-carbide particle reinforced aluminum plate** that resists both saltwater corrosion and abrasion (Metal Matrix Composite).
- A **nickel-based superalloy turbine blade** that survives 1000 °C exhaust gases (Inconel/CMCs).
- A **shape-memory alloy pipe coupling** that "remembers" its shape after deformation (Nitinol).

This is precisely what *Advanced Materials* represent — a **tailored-property revolution** where engineers no longer select a material and design around its limitations; they instead **engineer the material to fit the design**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners**
> - *Conventional non-ferrous alloys* (Al 5083, Cu-Ni 90/10, Ti-6Al-4V) → properties fixed by composition + thermomechanical processing.
> - *Advanced materials* → properties engineered by **microstructural architecture** (reinforcement geometry, interface design, phase selection at the lattice level).

## 1.3 Classification of Advanced Materials (KTU Module 4 Scope)

Advanced non-ferrous materials in shipbuilding are broadly partitioned into **four functional families**:

| Family | Representative Systems | Primary Shipboard Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Metal Matrix Composites (MMCs)** | Al/SiC, Al/Al₂O₃, Ti/SiC | Superstructure panels, mast, propeller blades |
| **Polymer Matrix Composites (PMCs)** | CFRP, GFRP, Aramid/epoxy | Hull shells, radar-transparent enclosures |
| **Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMCs)** | SiC/SiC, Al₂O₃/Al₂O₃ | Exhaust ducts, heat exchangers, fire zones |
| **Engineered Bulk Alloys** | Ni-superalloys, γ-TiAl, Al-Li, HEAs, SMAs | Propulsors, fasteners, jet-engine internals |

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics Used in the Module

The following metrics are the **four "headline" KPIs** the KTU examiner expects you to know:

- **Specific Strength** $\sigma / \rho$ → strength-to-weight ratio (kN·m/kg).
- **Specific Stiffness** $E / \rho$ → stiffness-to-weight ratio (MN·m/kg).
- **Fatigue Endurance Limit** $\sigma_e$ → cyclic stress sustainable for $\geq 10^7$ cycles.
- **Corrosion Rate** $CR$ → mm/year in ASTM B117 salt-spray.

All four are highlighted in **bold** as they are guaranteed to appear in the KTU question paper.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of "Advancement" Over Conventional Alloys

Every advanced material covered in Module 4 derives its superiority from **one or more** of the following design levers:

### Pillar 1 — Reinforcement Mechanisms (Composite Materials)
- **Load transfer** from a soft matrix to a stiff reinforcement via **shear-lag** at the fiber/matrix interface.
- The rule-of-mixtures defines upper-bound performance:

$$\sigma_c = V_f \sigma_f + (1 - V_f) \sigma_m$$

- **Critical fiber length** $l_c$ governs whether the reinforcement can be loaded to its ultimate strength:

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$$

where $\sigma_f$ is the fiber strength, $d$ is the fiber diameter, and $\tau_i$ is the interfacial shear strength.

### Pillar 2 — Phase Engineering (Advanced Bulk Alloys)
- **γ′ (gamma-prime) precipitation** in Ni-superalloys (e.g., Inconel 718): coherent Ni₃(Al,Ti) precipitates impede dislocation motion at elevated temperatures.
- **α₂ + γ lamellae** in titanium aluminides (TiAl): alternate HCP/FCC platelets provide creep resistance up to ~750 °C.
- **High-Entropy Alloys (HEAs)** (e.g., AlCoCrFeNi): five or more principal elements in near-equiatomic ratios stabilize a single FCC/BCC solid solution rather than brittle intermetallics.

### Pillar 3 — Interface Engineering
- Weak interfaces (carbon/BN coatings on fibers) deflect cracks and grant **pseudo-ductility** to inherently brittle CMCs.
- Strong interfaces (in Al/SiC MMCs) maximize load transfer at the cost of fracture toughness.

### Pillar 4 — Functional Response (Smart Materials)
- **Shape Memory Alloys (SMAs)** like Nitinol (Ni-Ti) recover strain via the martensite↔austenite phase transformation.
- **Piezoelectric ceramics** (PZT) convert mechanical strain to electrical signals and vice versa — used in sonar transduction and vibration monitoring.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every equation you are likely to need to solve Module 4 problems. Memorize the **name → formula → unit** triplet.

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rule of mixtures — stiffness | $E_c = V_f E_f + (1 - V_f) E_m$ | $E$ in GPa, $V_f$ dimensionless |
| 2 | Rule of mixtures — strength | $\sigma_c = V_f \sigma_f + (1 - V_f) \sigma_m$ | $\sigma$ in MPa |
| 3 | Critical fiber length | $l_c = \sigma_f d / (2 \tau_i)$ | $l_c, d$ in m, $\sigma_f, \tau_i$ in Pa |
| 4 | Fiber efficiency factor | $\eta_l = 1 - (l_c / 2l)$ for $l \geq l_c$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Specific strength | $SS = \sigma_{uts} / \rho$ | kN·m/kg |
| 6 | Specific stiffness | $SSt = E / \rho$ | MN·m/kg |
| 7 | Corrosion rate (mass-loss) | $CR = (K \cdot W) / (A \cdot T \cdot D)$ | mm/yr, $K = 8.76 \times 10^4$ |
| 8 | Hall-Petch strengthening | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ | $\sigma_y$ in MPa, $d$ in m |
| 9 | Pilling-Bedworth ratio | $PBR = V_{oxide} / V_{metal}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | SMA recovery stress | $\sigma_{rec} = E \cdot \varepsilon_{rec}$ | MPa |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU LaTeX Pitfall** — never write $V_f \vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table. Use $V_f \cdot \lvert x \rvert$ or simply $V_f \cdot x$ to keep the table parser intact. (The vertical pipe `|` breaks GFM tables.)

## 2.3 Engineering Utility in Real Marine Applications

| Material | Shipboard Subsystem | Performance Gain Over Baseline |
|---|---|---|
| Al/SiC MMC (Duralcan F3S.20S) | Masts, sonar domes | 40% stiffness gain, 15% weight reduction |
| CFRP (T700/epoxy) | Hull shell of VISBY-class corvette | 50% weight reduction, magnetic signature ↓ |
| SiC/SiC CMC | Marine gas turbine combustor liner | Operates at 1200 °C without cooling air |
| Inconel 718 | Propeller shaft sleeves | Creep life 10× over Cu-Ni at 600 °C |
| Nitinol SMA | Pipe couplings on submarines | Self-tightening, vibration-resistant |
| Al-Li 2195 | Cryogenic LNG tanker secondary structure | Density 8% lower, modulus 10% higher than 2219 |

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Microstructural Engineering

A non-ferrous alloy such as **Al 5083-H116** is already an excellent marine alloy. Why, then, do we need advanced materials? The answer lies in **trade-off envelopes**:

- **Submarine pressure hulls** → need non-magnetic, high specific stiffness → CFRP outperforms steel by 5× in $E/\rho$.
- **Naval gas turbines** → need 1200 °C creep strength + saltwater resistance → SiC/SiC CMCs replace Inconel.
- **Fast patrol craft** → need radar-transparent superstructure → GFRP is transparent to X-band radar.

In each case, **conventional alloys are not eliminated** — they are *complemented* by advanced materials in weight-critical, signature-critical, or temperature-critical subsystems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rule of Mixtures for Composite Stiffness (Voigt Model)

We treat a unidirectional fiber-reinforced composite loaded **parallel** to the fibers. Both the fiber and matrix experience the **same strain** $\varepsilon$ (iso-strain assumption).

**Step 1 — Define the load sharing.**
Total load $P_c$ on the composite cross-section is the sum of loads carried by fiber and matrix:

$$P_c = P_f + P_m$$

**Step 2 — Convert loads to stresses using areas.**
With cross-sectional area $A_c$, fiber area $A_f = V_f A_c$, matrix area $A_m = (1 - V_f) A_c$:

$$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the volume fractions.**

$$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f V_f A_c + \sigma_m (1 - V_f) A_c$$

**Step 4 — Apply Hooke's law (iso-strain condition).**
Because $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$:

$$\sigma_c = V_f E_f \varepsilon + (1 - V_f) E_m \varepsilon$$

**Step 5 — Factor out the common strain $\varepsilon$.**

$$\sigma_c = \varepsilon \left[ V_f E_f + (1 - V_f) E_m \right]$$

**Step 6 — Recognize that $E_c = \sigma_c / \varepsilon$ and write the final rule of mixtures.**

$$E_c = V_f E_f + (1 - V_f) E_m$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The derivation hinges on the *iso-strain* assumption — valid when fibers are continuous, aligned, and bonded perfectly to the matrix. For transverse loading, the inverse Reuss (iso-stress) model applies: $1/E_c = V_f/E_f + (1 - V_f)/E_m$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Critical Fiber Length

We now ask: *what is the minimum fiber length $l_c$ required to develop the fiber's ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_f$ through interfacial shear?*

**Step 1 — Force balance on a single fiber of length $l$ and diameter $d$.**
The fiber is loaded by shear stress $\tau_i$ acting along the cylindrical interface area $\pi d l$:

$$F_{shear} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d l$$

**Step 2 — Force required to break the fiber in tension.**

$$F_{tensile} = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two forces (limiting condition for full fiber strength).**

$$\tau_i \cdot \pi d l_c = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $\pi d$ from both sides.**

$$\tau_i l_c = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{d}{4}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $l_c$.**

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_f d}{4 \tau_i}$$

> **Important Correction:** A common KTU textbook uses $l_c = \sigma_f d / (2 \tau_i)$ (full development from both ends — "tension from both sides" model). The factor of 2 or 4 depends on whether shear is transferred from **one** end or **both** ends. Always check the textbook convention; in Module 4 the **double-ended shear** form is preferred:

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Corrosion Rate (Mass-Loss Method, ASTM G1)

**Step 1 — Measure mass loss $\Delta m$ (grams) over exposure time $T$ (hours) for area $A$ (cm²) of a sample of density $D$ (g/cm³).**

**Step 2 — Convert mass loss to an equivalent uniform thickness loss using density.**

$$\Delta x = \frac{\Delta m}{A \cdot D} \quad \text{(in cm)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert from cm/hour to mm/year using the constant $K = 8.76 \times 10^4$.**

$$CR = \frac{8.76 \times 10^4 \cdot \Delta m}{A \cdot T \cdot D} \quad \text{(mm/year)}$$

**Step 4 — Worked example.**
A marine-grade Al/SiC MMC sample (density $D = 2.85$ g/cm³) loses $\Delta m = 0.45$ g over $T = 720$ h on an exposed area $A = 25$ cm². Compute the corrosion rate:

$$CR = \frac{8.76 \times 10^4 \times 0.45}{25 \times 720 \times 2.85}$$

$$CR = \frac{39420}{51300} = 0.768 \text{ mm/year}$$

**Step 5 — Classification (per ISO 9223).**
Since $CR < 0.1$ mm/year is required for ship hulls in continuous immersion, this MMC **fails** the criterion. The student would recommend a **barrier coating** or a **more corrosion-resistant matrix** (e.g., Al-Mg-Si 6xxx series).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Property Comparison of Module 4 Materials

The table below is the **single most-asked comparison** in KTU Module 4 viva and Part B questions. Memorize the trend, not the absolute numbers.

| Material | $\rho$ (g/cm³) | $E$ (GPa) | $\sigma_{uts}$ (MPa) | $E/\rho$ (MN·m/kg) | $\sigma/\rho$ (kN·m/kg) | Marine Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Al 5083-H116 (baseline) | 2.66 | 70 | 305 | 26.3 | 114.6 | Hull plates |
| Al/SiC MMC (20 vol%) | 2.85 | 100 | 480 | 35.1 | 168.4 | Masts, sonar domes |
| Al-Li 2195-T8 | 2.71 | 79 | 590 | 29.2 | 217.7 | Cryogenic structures |
| Ti-6Al-4V | 4.43 | 114 | 950 | 25.7 | 214.4 | Propeller blades |
| γ-TiAl (Ti-48Al-2Cr-2Nb) | 3.9 | 170 | 800 | 43.6 | 205.1 | Turbine blades |
| Inconel 718 | 8.19 | 200 | 1240 | 24.4 | 151.4 | High-temp fasteners |
| CFRP (T700/epoxy, 60% Vf) | 1.55 | 135 | 1500 | 87.1 | 967.7 | Hull shells |
| GFRP (E-glass/epoxy) | 1.90 | 38 | 1100 | 20.0 | 578.9 | Superstructure |
| SiC/SiC CMC | 2.50 | 200 | 400 | 80.0 | 160.0 | Combustor liners |
| AlCoCrFeNi HEA | 7.40 | 195 | 1450 | 26.4 | 195.9 | Experimental bearings |

> [!NOTE]
> **Trend Takeaway for Examiners:**
> 1. **CFRP dominates $E/\rho$** — the choice for stiffness-critical hulls.
> 2. **Al-Li and γ-TiAl dominate $\sigma/\rho$** for high-temperature structural parts.
> 3. **CFRP also dominates $\sigma/\rho$**, but its **failure mode is brittle**, so ductility-driven design uses Al-Li or Ti-6Al-4V.
> 4. **Inconel is heavy** — it is used only where temperature demands, not for weight savings.

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Shape Memory Recovery in Nitinol (Conceptual)

**Step 1 — Two phases.**
Nitinol exists in two crystallographic forms:
- **Martensite** (monoclinic, B19′) at low temperature.
- **Austenite** (cubic, B2) above the **Af** finish temperature.

**Step 2 — Loading in martensite.**
Below $M_f$, the material is deformed by **twinning** and **detwinning** — strains of up to 8% are fully recoverable.

**Step 3 — Heating above $A_f$.**
The crystal structure reverts to austenite, the "remembered" parent shape. The macroscopic strain $\varepsilon_{rec}$ recovers.

**Step 4 — Recovery stress if constrained.**
If the part is not free to move, a recovery stress develops:

$$\sigma_{rec} = E_{aust} \cdot \varepsilon_{rec}$$

For typical Nitinol, $E_{aust} \approx 75$ GPa, $\varepsilon_{rec} \approx 0.06$, giving:

$$\sigma_{rec} \approx 4.5 \text{ GPa}$$

This enormous stress is what makes **SMA couplings on submarine piping** self-tightening under thermal cycling.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Advanced Materials Selection Workflow

The diagram below maps the decision flow a naval architect follows when choosing an advanced material for a marine subsystem. Note the **Mermaid safeguard** rules: every node ID is alphanumeric with a letter prefix, and no reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) are used as node names.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Marine Design Brief]) --> A1{Is weight critical?}
    A1 -- YES --> A2{Is temperature > 600 C?}
    A1 -- NO --> A3[Use conventional non-ferrous alloy]
    A2 -- YES --> A4[Select Ni superalloy or SiC CMC]
    A2 -- NO --> A5{Is magnetic signature critical?}
    A4 --> A6[Verify IACS UR S21 compliance]
    A5 -- YES --> A7[Select CFRP or GFRP composite]
    A5 -- NO --> A8{Is corrosion in seawater the driver?}
    A7 --> A9[Apply IMO FTP Code fire test]
    A8 -- YES --> A10[Select Al SiC MMC or Al Li alloy]
    A8 -- NO --> A11[Use Ti 6Al 4V baseline]
    A10 --> A12[Conduct ASTM B117 salt spray]
    A11 --> A13[Final DNV GL approval]
    A9 --> A13
    A12 --> A13
    A6 --> A13
    A3 --> A13
    A13 --> A14([Material Released for Procurement])
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The **diamond nodes** are decision points — these are the questions you will be asked in the viva.
> - The **rectangle nodes** are material selections.
> - The **rounded-rectangle nodes** (`A0`, `A14`) are the start and end of the workflow.
> - **All compliance gates** (A6, A9, A12, A13) map to the regulatory/standards body that must approve the material for shipboard use.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — MMC Microstructural Design

A physical drawing of an MMC microstructure is impossible in plain Mermaid, so the block diagram below maps the **functional interactions** between the matrix, reinforcement, and interface.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M0[Al Alloy Matrix] --> M1[Load Transfer Zone]
    M2[SiC Particle Reinforcement] --> M1
    M3[Interface Mg2Si Reaction Layer] --> M1
    M1 --> M4[Macro Stress Field]
    M4 --> M5{Specific Strength KPI}
    M4 --> M6{Specific Stiffness KPI}
    M4 --> M7{Corrosion Rate KPI}
    M4 --> M8{Fatigue Endurance KPI}
    M5 --> M9[Final MMC Performance Envelope]
    M6 --> M9
    M7 --> M9
    M8 --> M9
    M3 -. Controls .-> M1
    M2 -. Dislocation pinning .-> M1
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The **Al matrix** is the load-bearing continuous phase.
> - The **SiC particles** are the stiff, strong reinforcement.
> - The **Mg₂Si reaction layer** is the *interface* — a chemical product formed during casting that determines how well load is transferred.
> - The **four KPI outputs** (M5–M8) feed the final performance envelope (M9), which is what the KTU examiner expects you to compare with the baseline alloy.

## 4.3 Process Flow for Composite Hull Manufacturing (Hand Layup → VARTM)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Mold Preparation] --> P2[Release Agent Application]
    P2 --> P3[Gelcoat Spray]
    P3 --> P4{Hand Layup or VARTM}
    P4 -- Hand Layup --> P5[Manual Resin Application]
    P4 -- VARTM --> P6[Vacuum Infusion Setup]
    P5 --> P7[Roller Compaction]
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Room Temp Cure 24 h]
    P8 --> P9[Post Cure 80 C 4 h]
    P9 --> P10[Demold and Trim]
    P10 --> P11[Ultrasonic NDT Inspection]
    P11 --> P12[Panel Approved for Hull Assembly]
```

> **Exam Hint:** If asked *"Why is VARTM preferred over hand layup for naval hulls?"*, the answer is **lower void content (<1% vs ~5%)**, **better fiber-volume-fraction control**, and **improved reproducibility** — all of which are required by **IACS UR S21**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper, Q.1(b)]**

### Question 1
**Differentiate between a Metal Matrix Composite (MMC) and a Polymer Matrix Composite (PMC) used in shipbuilding. Give one marine application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **MMC** = Metal matrix (typically Al, Ti, or Mg) reinforced with ceramic particles or fibers (SiC, Al₂O₃). *Properties:* high stiffness, high temperature capability, but heavier and harder to process.
- **PMC** = Polymer matrix (epoxy, polyester, vinylester) reinforced with carbon/glass/aramid fibers. *Properties:* extremely light, corrosion-proof, but temperature-limited to ~200 °C.
- **MMC application:** Al/SiC sonar dome or mast.
- **PMC application:** CFRP hull shell of a stealth corvette.

*(1 mark each for the differentiation, 1 mark for the application pair.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest Paper, Q.1(c)]**

### Question 2
**What is a Shape Memory Alloy (SMA)? Name the most common SMA used in marine applications and state one shipboard use.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. An SMA is a metal that "remembers" its original shape and returns to it upon heating, after being deformed at a lower temperature, via a reversible martensite↔austenite phase transformation. *(1 mark)*
2. The most common SMA is **Nitinol** (Nickel-Titanium, Ni-Ti). *(1 mark)*
3. Shipboard use: **self-tightening pipe couplings** in submarines and surface-ship seawater cooling lines. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, ESE Module 4, Q.11 (Choice A)]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the aid of a labeled schematic, explain the **microstructure and load-transfer mechanism** in a unidirectional **Al/SiC metal matrix composite**. Derive the **rule of mixtures for stiffness** and state the condition under which it is valid. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A unidirectional Al/SiC MMC contains **40 vol% SiC fibers** ($E_f = 410$ GPa, $\sigma_f = 3500$ MPa) in an aluminum matrix ($E_m = 70$ GPa, $\sigma_m = 90$ MPa). The fiber diameter is **140 µm** and the interfacial shear strength is **45 MPa**. Calculate: *(7 marks)*
- (i) The **longitudinal modulus** $E_c$.
- (ii) The **critical fiber length** $l_c$ and state whether a 5 mm long fiber is sufficient.
- (iii) The **composite strength** assuming both fiber and matrix reach their ultimate stress simultaneously.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Microstructure** (3 marks):
   - The MMC consists of a **continuous Al-alloy matrix** (often 6061 or 2124) and **discontinuous SiC reinforcement** in the form of whiskers, short fibers, or particles.
   - The interface is characterized by a thin reaction layer (MgAl₂O₄ or Mg₂Si depending on Mg content) that controls load transfer.
   - Load applied to the matrix is **transferred to the stiffer SiC** via **shear stresses at the interface** (shear-lag mechanism).

2. **Derivation of rule of mixtures for stiffness** (3 marks):
   The student must show the iso-strain derivation from Section 3.1, arriving at:

   $$E_c = V_f E_f + (1 - V_f) E_m$$

3. **Validity condition** (1 mark):
   The rule is valid when:
   - Fibers are **continuous, parallel, and aligned** with the load.
   - The **fiber-matrix bond is perfect** (no debonding).
   - Both phases experience **the same strain** (iso-strain, Voigt condition).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Longitudinal modulus** (2 marks):

$$E_c = V_f E_f + (1 - V_f) E_m$$

$$E_c = 0.40 \times 410 + 0.60 \times 70$$

$$E_c = 164 + 42 = 206 \text{ GPa}$$

**(ii) Critical fiber length** (3 marks):

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i}$$

$$l_c = \frac{3500 \times 10^6 \times 140 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 45 \times 10^6}$$

$$l_c = \frac{490}{90} = 5.44 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 5.44 \text{ mm}$$

Since the fiber length is **5 mm < 5.44 mm**, the fiber is **marginally insufficient** to develop its full strength. The fiber efficiency factor is:

$$\eta_l = 1 - \frac{l_c}{2l} = 1 - \frac{5.44}{2 \times 5.0} = 1 - 0.544 = 0.456$$

So only **45.6%** of the fiber strength is mobilized.

**(iii) Composite strength** (2 marks):

$$\sigma_c = V_f \sigma_f + (1 - V_f) \sigma_m$$

$$\sigma_c = 0.40 \times 3500 + 0.60 \times 90 = 1400 + 54 = 1454 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> - **Forgetting to convert MPa → Pa and µm → m** in the critical-length calculation → results in 1000× errors.
> - **Using $l_c = \sigma_f d / (4\tau_i)$** when the question implies double-ended shear → wrong by a factor of 2.
> - **Omitting the validity condition** for the rule of mixtures → lose 1 of 7 marks.
> - **Mixing up $E_c$** and $\sigma_c$ — stiffness is in GPa, strength in MPa.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, ESE Module 4, Q.11 (Choice B)]**

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Compare the **microstructure, density, specific strength, and temperature capability** of **three advanced non-ferrous materials** used in modern shipbuilding. Use tabulated data and select examples from MMCs, superalloys, and titanium aluminides. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A shipboard **gas turbine exhaust duct** is to be made from a SiC/SiC ceramic matrix composite. Justify the material selection over a nickel superalloy alternative, listing **four property advantages** and **two engineering challenges** of the CMC. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Comparison Table** (5 marks for the table, 2 marks for the discussion):

| Property | Al/SiC MMC (e.g., 20 vol%) | Inconel 718 (Ni-superalloy) | γ-TiAl (Ti-48Al-2Cr-2Nb) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Microstructure** | Al matrix + SiC particles; MgAl₂O₄ interface | γ-Ni matrix + γ′ (Ni₃Al,Ti) precipitates; δ, MC carbides | Alternating γ (FCC TiAl) + α₂ (HCP Ti₃Al) lamellae |
| **Density $\rho$ (g/cm³)** | ~2.85 | ~8.19 | ~3.9 |
| **Specific strength $\sigma/\rho$ (kN·m/kg)** | ~170 | ~150 | ~205 |
| **Max service T (°C)** | ~350 | ~700 | ~750 |

**Discussion** (2 marks):
- The **γ-TiAl** offers the best specific strength at the highest temperature, but it is brittle at room temperature.
- The **Inconel 718** has the highest absolute strength but the worst specific strength.
- The **Al/SiC MMC** is the lightest and easiest to fabricate but is limited to sub-400 °C service.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Four property advantages of SiC/SiC CMC over Inconel 718** (4 marks — 1 mark each):
1. **Density ~3× lower** (2.5 vs 8.2 g/cm³) → direct weight saving on a non-rotating duct.
2. **Higher service temperature** (1200 °C vs 700 °C) → no need for complex film-cooling passages.
3. **Excellent thermal-shock resistance** due to low coefficient of thermal expansion and high thermal conductivity.
4. **No oxidation embrittlement** — SiC forms a self-healing silica scale in combustion gases.

**Two engineering challenges** (2 marks — 1 mark each):
1. **Brittle fracture behavior** — damage tolerance is low, requiring rigorous NDT (e.g., infrared thermography) and large safety factors.
2. **High manufacturing cost** — chemical vapor infiltration (CVI) cycles can exceed 1000 hours per part.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often confuse **specific strength** with **specific stiffness** — both must be stated explicitly.
> - For the CMC question, do **not** state that the material is "stronger" than Inconel in absolute terms; emphasize the **specific** and **temperature** advantages.
> - Avoid saying "CMC is unbreakable" — it is brittle and **damage-tolerant design** is the central design philosophy.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Advanced Materials: The 15 Things You MUST Remember**

1. **Advanced materials = engineered microstructure**, not just new chemistry.
2. **Four families:** MMC, PMC, CMC, engineered bulk alloys (superalloys, TiAl, HEA, SMA).
3. **Rule of mixtures** (Voigt) for longitudinal stiffness: $E_c = V_f E_f + (1 - V_f) E_m$ — *iso-strain assumption*.
4. **Reuss model** for transverse stiffness: $1/E_c = V_f/E_f + (1 - V_f)/E_m$ — *iso-stress assumption*.
5. **Critical fiber length**: $l_c = \sigma_f d / (2 \tau_i)$ (double-ended shear convention).
6. **Specific strength $\sigma/\rho$** and **specific stiffness $E/\rho$** are the two key KPIs.
7. **CFRP** has the highest $E/\rho$ of all common shipbuilding materials; **Al-Li** has the best $\sigma/\rho$ among ductile alloys.
8. **Inconel 718** = precipitation-hardened Ni superalloy with γ′ Ni₃(Al,Ti); service limit ~700 °C.
9. **γ-TiAl** = intermetallic with γ + α₂ lamellae; service limit ~750 °C, brittle at 25 °C.
10. **HEAs** (e.g., AlCoCrFeNi) = 5+ elements, near-equiatomic, single solid-solution phase.
11. **Nitinol (Ni-Ti)** is the most common SMA; recovery strain ~8% via B2↔B19′ transformation.
12. **SiC/SiC CMC** is the temperature champion (1200 °C), used in marine turbine combustors.
13. **Corrosion rate formula**: $CR = 8.76 \times 10^4 \cdot \Delta m / (A \cdot T \cdot D)$.
14. **Standards to know:** IACS UR S21 (composites), IMO FTP Code (fire), DNV GL rules, ASTM B117 (salt spray), ASTM G1 (corrosion mass loss).
15. **Manufacturing route matters:** VARTM gives lower void content than hand layup; squeeze casting is the standard for Al/SiC MMCs.

> **One-Line Closing Maxim (favorite of KTU external examiners):**
> *In Module 4, the question is never "what is the strongest material?" — it is "**what is the strongest material per unit weight, per unit cost, per unit risk, in this specific marine environment?**"*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of Adhesive Bonding on Superstructures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST523 - NON FERROUS MATERIALS IN SHIPBUILDING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Use of Adhesive Bonding on Superstructures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Adhesive Bonding** is a material joining process in which a non-metallic polymeric substance (the *adhesive*) is used to create a structural connection between two or more adherends through surface attachment (adhesion) and internal mechanical interlocking (cohesion). In the context of shipbuilding, the use of adhesive bonding on superstructures refers specifically to the structural and semi-structural joining of non-ferrous materials — predominantly **aluminium alloys**, **fibre-reinforced polymers (FRP)**, and **stainless steel sandwich panels** — that form the deckhouses, bulkheads, masts, helicopter decks, and wheelhouse tops of a vessel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Board-Examiner Wording):**
> *Adhesive bonding of superstructures is a cold-joining technique whereby a load-bearing, chemically cured polymeric film is interposed between two prepared metallic or composite surfaces, transferring mechanical loads through shear across the bonded interface without the use of welding, riveting, or bolting.*

The technique is governed by classification society rules such as **DNV GL DNVGL-C501** (Composite Components), **Lloyd's Register SSC Rules for High Speed Craft**, and **ABS Rules for Materials and Welding — Part 2**, and is officially recognised as a primary structural joining method for aluminium superstructures in series production shipyards.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Sticky Sandwich"

Imagine you are building a toy house out of cardboard. You could either staple the walls together (mechanical fastening) or use craft glue to stick them (adhesive bonding). With staples, you can see the metal pins holding the walls; with glue, the connection is invisible, spreads the load across a larger area, and there are no holes. Now imagine the cardboard walls are replaced with aluminium sheets weighing several tonnes, and the glue is replaced with a special marine-grade structural adhesive. The principle is exactly the same — only the stakes, the engineering precision, and the materials are scaled up enormously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real Shipyard Analogy — The Visby-class Corvette:**
> The Swedish Navy's Visby-class corvette has an entire superstructure made of carbon-fibre composite sandwich panels, joined almost exclusively by adhesive bonding and co-curing. The hull is vacuum-infused and the superstructure panels are bonded using a toughened epoxy film adhesive — a textbook case of adhesive-bonded superstructure design in modern naval architecture.

## 1.3 Why Adhesives Are Used in Superstructures

The superstructure is the part of a ship above the main deck — including the bridge, accommodation block, funnels, and mast houses. Traditionally fabricated from mild steel, modern superstructures increasingly use **aluminium alloys (5083, 5086, 6082)** to reduce top weight and improve stability, and **FRP composites** to achieve corrosion-free, radar-friendly, and lightweight structures. Welding aluminium introduces distortion and HAZ softening; riveting introduces stress concentrations and creates leak paths. Adhesive bonding elegantly bypasses both problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Material Constants in Adhesive Bonding (Recall Values for Exams):**
> - **Elastic Modulus of Cured Epoxy Adhesive:** $E \approx 2\text{–}5 \text{ GPa}$
> - **Shear Strength of Structural Epoxy:** $\tau \approx 20\text{–}35 \text{ MPa}$
> - **Glass Transition Temperature of Marine Epoxy:** $T_g \approx 80\text{–}120\,^{\circ}\text{C}$
> - **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (Cured Adhesive):** $\alpha \approx 60\text{–}100 \times 10^{-6}\,/^{\circ}\text{C}$

## 1.4 Comparison with Other Joining Methods

| Property | Adhesive Bonding | Welding (MIG/TIG) | Mechanical Fastening (Riveting) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Heat Input** | None (cold process) | Very High | None |
| **Joint Stiffness** | High (continuous load path) | Highest | Discrete (point loads) |
| **Stress Concentration** | Low (distributed shear) | Moderate | High (at hole) |
| **Weight Penalty** | Negligible | Negligible | Moderate (fastener mass) |
| **Sealing Capability** | Excellent (bond IS the seal) | Poor | Poor (gaskets needed) |
| **Suitability for Al/FRP** | Excellent | Aluminium only | Both |
| **Fatigue Performance** | Very Good | Good | Moderate |
| **Disassembly** | Difficult | Very Difficult | Easy |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Adhesion–Cohesion Mechanism

The strength of an adhesive joint arises from **two simultaneous phenomena**:

1. **Adhesion** — the molecular forces (van der Waals, hydrogen bonding, and for some adhesives, covalent chemical bonds) between the adhesive and the substrate surface.
2. **Cohesion** — the internal strength of the cured adhesive film itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A well-designed adhesive joint should **fail cohesively (within the adhesive layer)** rather than **adhesively (at the interface)**. A cohesive failure indicates that the surface preparation and adhesive selection are correct; an adhesive failure indicates poor surface preparation or wrong adhesive chemistry.

## 2.2 The Six-Step Bonding Process

A KTU board examiner will expect a candidate to write the complete bonding sequence:

1. **Substrate Selection & Compatibility Check** — verify that the adhesive is chemically compatible with the adherend (e.g., epoxy with aluminium, methacrylate with FRP).
2. **Surface Preparation** — degrease, abrade, etch, and prime. For aluminium, the **chromate-free etch primer** or **anodising (e.g., phosphoric acid anodising — CAA or PAA)** is mandatory.
3. **Adhesive Application** — by hand roller, cartridge gun, or industrial robot. Film adhesives are preferred for critical joints.
4. **Assembly & Fixturing** — clamps, jigs, vacuum bags, or autoclave pressure to maintain uniform bondline thickness (**0.1–0.3 mm** typical).
5. **Curing** — at room temperature (24 hours) or elevated temperature (60–180 °C for 1–4 hours depending on chemistry). Heat-cured systems give superior mechanical and thermal properties.
6. **Inspection & Quality Control** — visual, tap-test, ultrasonic C-scan, thermography, or destructive coupon testing.

## 2.3 Common Adhesive Chemistries Used in Marine Superstructures

| Adhesive Type | Curing Mechanism | Typical Application | Marine Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Epoxy (toughened)** | Two-part (amine hardener) or heat-cured | Primary structural bonding of Al-Al and Al-FRP | Bonding wheelhouse tops, helicopter platform doublers |
| **Polyurethane (PU)** | Moisture-cured | Flexible bonds, dissimilar materials | Bonding FRP to aluminium deck panels |
| **Methacrylate (MMA)** | Two-part room-temp | Rapid field repairs, FRP-FRP | Emergency bonding of superstructure bulkheads |
| **Phenolic** | Heat & pressure | Fire-resistant sandwich panels | Bonding of fire-rated accommodation panels |
| **Silicone / MS Polymer** | Moisture-cured | Non-structural sealing | Perimeter sealing of bonded windows |
| **Cyanoacrylate** | Anionic (instant) | Temporary fixture only | NOT permitted for primary structure |

> [!NOTE]
> For aluminium superstructures, **toughened epoxy paste adhesives** such as those from the *3M Scotch-Weld DP-490*, *Huntsman Araldite 2015*, or *SikaPower® 4720* are industry benchmarks. They provide lap-shear strengths of **>30 MPa** on chromic-acid anodised aluminium 5083.

## 2.4 Joint Design Principles

### 2.4.1 The Single-Lap Joint — Most Common Configuration

A **single-lap joint** is the workhorse configuration. Two adherends overlap, and the adhesive transfers load by shear. The load path is not collinear — there is an **eccentricity** of $t/2$ on each side, where $t$ is the adherend thickness. This eccentricity produces a **peel stress** at the ends of the overlap, which is the primary cause of joint failure.

### 2.4.2 The Bondline Shear Stress Distribution

In an idealised, rigid-adherend joint, shear stress is uniform. In a real, elastic joint, the shear stress is **peaked at the ends** of the overlap and minimum at the centre. This was first analysed rigorously by **Volkersen (1938)** and later extended by **Goland & Reissner (1944)** to include the bending due to eccentricity.

### 2.4.3 Modes of Joint Loading

An adhesive joint can experience **four fundamental loading modes**:

- **Mode I — Tension (Cleavage):** Forces pull the adherends apart perpendicular to the bond plane.
- **Mode II — Shear (Sliding):** Forces slide the adherends parallel to the bond plane.
- **Mode III — Tearing (Peel):** A concentrated force acts at the edge of a flexible adherend, peeling it back.
- **Mode IV — Mixed Mode:** Combinations of the above.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Adhesives are very strong in **shear (Mode II)** but weak in **peel/cleavage (Modes I & III)**. Joint design must therefore aim to convert applied loads into shear and avoid peel stresses.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = \dfrac{P}{b \cdot L}$ | Average shear stress in a lap joint ($P$=load, $b$=width, $L$=overlap length) | MPa |
| 2 | $\tau_{max} = \tau_{avg} \cdot \left[\dfrac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right]$ | Volkersen's peak shear stress at the overlap ends; $\beta = \cosh(\omega L)$ correction factor | MPa |
| 3 | $\omega L = \sqrt{\dfrac{G \cdot L^2}{E \cdot t \cdot d}}$ | Dimensionless overlap parameter (Goland-Reissner); $G$=adhesive shear modulus, $d$=bondline thickness | — |
| 4 | $\sigma_{peel} = \dfrac{P \cdot t}{2 \cdot b \cdot d^2}$ | Peak peel stress at lap joint end due to bending eccentricity | MPa |
| 5 | $\tau_{design} = \dfrac{\tau_{ult}}{n}$ | Allowable shear stress with safety factor $n$ (typically $n = 3$–$6$ for marine) | MPa |
| 6 | $L_{opt} = 1.52 \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{E_{adh}}{G_{adh}}}$ | Optimum overlap length (Renton-Vinson) maximising joint efficiency | mm |
| 7 | $\Delta L = (\alpha_s - \alpha_a) \cdot \Delta T \cdot L$ | Thermal mismatch strain in bonded dissimilar materials | mm |
| 8 | $\tau_{thermal} = G_{adh} \cdot \Delta T \cdot (\alpha_s - \alpha_a)$ | Shear stress generated in the bondline by differential thermal expansion | MPa |
| 9 | $\sigma_{vM} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$ | Von Mises equivalent stress for plane stress in the adhesive | MPa |
| 10 | $T_g$ criterion | Marine structural adhesive: cure temperature $\geq T_g + 20\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ | °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Equation Notes for the Examiner:**
> - Formula (2) shows that as the overlap $L$ increases, $\beta \to 1$ and $\tau_{max}$ tends to infinity — the **diminishing returns** of long overlaps. This is why overlap length is capped at an optimum (Renton-Vinson).
> - Formula (8) is **critical for aluminium-FRP joints** because $\alpha_{Al} \approx 23 \times 10^{-6}/^{\circ}\text{C}$ while $\alpha_{FRP} \approx 5\text{–}10 \times 10^{-6}/^{\circ}\text{C}$. A 50 °C service swing (–10 °C to +40 °C) can induce $\tau_{thermal} \approx 2$–$4$ MPa in an unmodified joint — substantial compared to $\tau_{design} \approx 5$–$10$ MPa.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
